<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define the term "Data" in the context of Business Analytics. Differentiate between Structured, Semi-Structured, and Unstructured Data with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Data:** In Business Analytics, *Data* refers to raw, unprocessed facts, figures, observations, and transactional records collected from various sources that serve as the foundational input for analytical processing and decision-making.

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Structured** | Organized in fixed schema (rows/columns), easily searchable | SQL table of customer orders |
| **Semi-Structured** | Partially organized with tags/markers but no rigid schema | JSON file of a product catalog |
| **Unstructured** | No predefined format or schema | A customer's video review on YouTube |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the three types with examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
*List and briefly explain the 5 V's of Big Data relevant to Business Analytics.*

**Model Answer:**

The 5 V's characterize the nature and challenges of modern data in business analytics:

1. **Volume** — The massive scale of data generated (TB, PB, EB). E.g., Walmart handles ~2.5 PB of customer data hourly.
2. **Velocity** — The speed at which data is produced and must be processed. E.g., real-time stock trading feeds.
3. **Variety** — The diverse formats and types of data. E.g., text, images, sensor logs, social media.
4. **Veracity** — The trustworthiness, accuracy, and quality of data. E.g., noisy sensor readings.
5. **Value** — The meaningful business insight ultimately extracted from data. E.g., identifying a profitable customer segment.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for listing, 2 marks for explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** (either (a)/(b) or full alternative). The structure below mirrors this exactly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain the **DIKW Pyramid** in detail. How does it relate to the four levels of Business Analytics? Discuss with suitable business examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **types of data** used in Business Analytics. For each type, give **two real-world business examples** and the appropriate **storage/analytical tool**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DIKW Pyramid & Four Levels of Analytics (7 marks):**

The **DIKW Pyramid** is a hierarchical model representing the transformation of raw facts into actionable wisdom.

- **Data (Bottom):** Raw, unprocessed facts. Example: 1,000 daily sales transaction records.
- **Information:** Data processed with context and meaning. Example: "Total sales in October were ₹5,00,000."
- **Knowledge:** Patterns and relationships derived from information. Example: "Sales peak every Saturday, with 30% higher volume than weekdays."
- **Wisdom (Top):** Application of knowledge to make decisions. Example: "Schedule extra staff every Saturday to handle the predictable sales surge."

Mapping to **four levels of business analytics:**

- **Descriptive** (Data → Information): Dashboard showing "Total sales in October = ₹5L."
- **Diagnostic** (Information → Knowledge): "Why did sales drop 10% in week 3?" — root cause analysis.
- **Predictive** (Knowledge → Forecast): "Sales next October projected at ₹5.5L based on 3-year trend."
- **Prescriptive** (Wisdom): "Increase inventory by 15% in September to capitalize on predicted growth."

**Marks distribution (7):** 2 marks for DIKW explanation, 2 marks for 4 analytics levels, 2 marks for business example, 1 mark for the linkage diagram.

---

**(b) Types of Data (7 marks):**

| Type | Sub-type | Business Example | Storage/Tool |
|------|----------|------------------|--------------|
| Structured | Relational (RDBMS) | Customer master table | MySQL, Oracle |
| Structured | Tabular files | Daily sales CSV | Excel, Power BI |
| Semi-structured | JSON/XML | API response from payment gateway | MongoDB, Elasticsearch |
| Semi-structured | Log files | Web server access logs | Splunk, Logstash |
| Unstructured | Text | Customer support chat transcripts | Hadoop HDFS, S3 |
| Unstructured | Image/Video | CCTV footage of retail store | AWS S3, Azure Blob |
| Quantitative | Discrete | Number of units sold per day | Any RDBMS |
| Quantitative | Continuous | Temperature of a manufacturing oven | Time-series DB (InfluxDB) |
| Qualitative | Nominal | Customer's gender in survey form | Categorical encoding |
| Qualitative | Ordinal | Customer rating: Poor/Good/Excellent | Ordered categorical encoding |

**Marks distribution (7):** 1 mark for classification, 4 marks for examples (½ mark each × 8 = 4), 2 marks for storage/tool mapping.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2025 (Model)]**

**(a)** Define **Data Quality**. Discuss the **six dimensions of data quality** with examples. Why is data quality critical for Business Analytics? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail company records the following **daily sales (in ₹'000)** for one week: **40, 45, 42, 47, 100, 44, 46**. Calculate:
- (i) Mean and Median
- (ii) Standard Deviation
- (iii) Identify the outlier using the Z-score method (threshold $\vert z \vert \ge 2$)
- (iv) Coefficient of Variation **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Data Quality & Six Dimensions (7 marks):**

**Data Quality** refers to the degree to which data is fit for its intended purpose in business operations, analytics, and decision-making. High-quality data is **accurate, complete, consistent, timely, valid, and unique**.

| Dimension | Definition | Example |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **Accuracy** | Data correctly represents the real-world entity | Customer's actual phone number |
| **Completeness** | All required data fields are present | No null values in shipping address |
| **Consistency** | Data agrees across systems/databases | Customer ID same in CRM and ERP |
| **Timeliness** | Data is up-to-date when needed | Real-time stock availability |
| **Validity** | Data conforms to defined rules/constraints | Age between 0 and 120 |
| **Uniqueness** | No duplicate records | Each invoice has a unique invoice number |

**Why critical for Business Analytics:** (1 mark)
- Garbage In = Garbage Out (GIGO): Poor data quality leads to flawed insights and incorrect business decisions.
- Regulatory compliance (GDPR, RBI norms) demands accurate records.
- Customer trust and brand reputation depend on accurate data handling.
- Direct financial impact: a 10% improvement in data quality can yield multi-million dollar savings in large enterprises (IBM estimate).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks):**

Given dataset: $X = \{40, 45, 42, 47, 100, 44, 46\}$, $n = 7$.

**(i) Mean and Median (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{40 + 45 + 42 + 47 + 100 + 44 + 46}{7} = \frac{364}{7} = 52
$$

Sorted data: $\{40, 42, 44, 45, 46, 47, 100\}$. Median = 4th value = **45**.

**(ii) Standard Deviation (2 marks):**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|-------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 40 | $-12$ | $144$ |
| 45 | $-7$ | $49$ |
| 42 | $-10$ | $100$ |
| 47 | $-5$ | $25$ |
| 100 | $+48$ | $2304$ |
| 44 | $-8$ | $64$ |
| 46 | $-6$ | $36$ |
| **Sum** | | **$2722$** |

$$
s^2 = \frac{2722}{6} \approx 453.67
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{453.67} \approx 21.30
$$

**(iii) Outlier Detection (Z-score) (2 marks):**

For $x = 100$:

$$
z = \frac{100 - 52}{21.30} = \frac{48}{21.30} \approx 2.25
$$

Since $\vert z \vert = 2.25 \ge 2$, the value **100 is a potential outlier**.

For all other $x_i$, the $\vert z \vert$ values are all well below 2 (e.g., for $x = 40$: $z = -12/21.30 \approx -0.56$).

**(iv) Coefficient of Variation (1 mark):**

$$
CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{21.30}{52} \times 100\% \approx 40.96\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The dataset has very high relative variability (CV > 30%), almost entirely driven by the outlier value of 100. This is a classic case of a **data entry error** or **exceptional event** (e.g., a festive mega-sale day) that requires further investigation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Structured and Unstructured data:** Students often call JSON "unstructured." It is **semi-structured** (it has tags but no rigid schema). Marks lost: typically ½ to 1 per wrong classification.
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's correction:** When asked for *sample* standard deviation, students divide by $n$ instead of $n-1$. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Confusing Population vs Sample:** Use $\mu, \sigma$ for population and $\bar{x}, s$ for sample. Mixing notations is penalized.
> 4. **Skipping the linkage between DIKW and Analytics levels:** In Part B 7-mark questions, students list the four analytics types but **fail to show how each maps to a DIKW layer**. Always draw a small mapping table.
> 5. **Ignoring units in numerical answers:** A sales figure stated without "₹'000" or appropriate unit loses ½ mark.
> 6. **Writing unstructured diagrams:** In the 14-mark questions, students are expected to include at least **one** structured diagram (DIKW pyramid, data flow, or taxonomy). Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting to interpret results:** Just computing the standard deviation is *not enough*. Always add a one-line business interpretation (e.g., "high variability suggests inconsistent demand").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Data for Business Analytics"**

- ✅ **Data** is the *raw, unprocessed* foundation; it becomes **Information** when contextualized, **Knowledge** when patterns are derived, and **Wisdom** when applied to decisions (**DIKW Pyramid**).
- ✅ The **three structural types** are **Structured** (RDBMS tables), **Semi-Structured** (JSON, XML, logs), and **Unstructured** (images, videos, text).
- ✅ Data sources are classified as **First-Party (Internal)**, **Second-Party (Partner-shared)**, and **Third-Party (External)**.
- ✅ **Quantitative** data is numerical (Discrete: count; Continuous: measure). **Qualitative** data is categorical (Nominal: unordered; Ordinal: ordered).
- ✅ **Cross-Sectional** = snapshot in time; **Time-Series** = sequential over time.
- ✅ The **5 V's of Big Data** = **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value** (extend to 7 V's with **Variability** and **Visualization**).
- ✅ The **four analytics levels**: **Descriptive** (what), **Diagnostic** (why), **Predictive** (what next), **Prescriptive** (what to do).
- ✅ **Six data quality dimensions** = Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Validity, Uniqueness.
- ✅ Key formulas to memorize:
  - Mean: $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$
  - Sample Variance: $s^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$
  - Standard Deviation: $s = \sqrt{s^2}$
  - CV: $\frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$
  - Z-score: $\frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s}$
  - IQR: $Q_3 - Q_1$
  - Tukey Fence: $1.5 \cdot IQR$
  - Shannon Entropy: $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$
  - Confidence Interval: $\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$
- ✅ For KTU numericals: always show **complete working**, **state units**, and provide a **business interpretation** of the result.
- ✅ Always include a **structured diagram** in 14-mark answers (DIKW pyramid, data flow, or classification taxonomy).
- ✅ The standard KTU data pipeline order: **Sources → Ingestion → Storage → Preprocessing → Analytics → Visualization → Decision**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models in Business Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Models in Business Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model (in Business Analytics):** A *Business Analytics Model* is a structured, mathematically rigorous, and computationally executable representation of a real-world business process, system, or phenomenon that uses historical, current, or simulated data to **describe**, **predict**, or **prescribe** business outcomes. Models transform raw organizational data into actionable managerial intelligence by abstracting complexity into quantifiable relationships.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, models are classified based on their **analytical purpose** and the **cognitive decision-support level** they offer to a business stakeholder.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Business Analytics Models

| Pillar | Cognitive Purpose | Typical Output | Business Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Descriptive** | "What happened?" | Reports, Dashboards, Aggregations | Last quarter's sales trend |
| **Predictive** | "What will happen?" | Forecasts, Probabilities, Risk Scores | Customer churn next month |
| **Prescriptive** | "What should we do?" | Optimized actions, Recommendations | Optimal inventory reorder point |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS Navigation System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Why Models Matter:**
> Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Trivandrum.
> - **Map data** (road names, distances) = **Raw Data**.
> - **The GPS** showing current speed, traffic, ETA = **Descriptive Model**.
> - **ETA Prediction** ("You will arrive at 5:42 PM") = **Predictive Model**.
> - **Route Recommendation** ("Take NH-66, avoid city center") = **Prescriptive Model**.

Just as a GPS uses mathematical routing algorithms over live traffic data, a *Business Analytics Model* uses statistical/optimization algorithms over enterprise data.

## 1.4 Classification of Models (Foundation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Taxonomy (KTU Board Standard):**
> 1. **Mathematical / Optimization Models** — Linear Programming, Integer Programming, Non-Linear Programming.
> 2. **Statistical / Probabilistic Models** — Regression, Time-Series, Bayesian Networks.
> 3. **Simulation Models** — Monte Carlo, Discrete-Event Simulation.
> 4. **Machine Learning / Data Mining Models** — Decision Trees, Clustering, Neural Networks.
> 5. **Logical / Rule-Based Models** — Expert Systems, IF-THEN business rules.
> 6. **Visual / Graphical Models** — Dashboards, Decision Trees, Influence Diagrams.

The course outcome (CO1) emphasizes the student's ability to **identify, classify, and apply** the correct model to a given business scenario.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The DDM Spectrum

The most academically accepted framework in KTU Business Analytics pedagogy is the **Descriptive–Predictive–Prescriptive (DPP) Spectrum**, often combined with the **Diagnostic** layer in modern extensions (DDP model — *Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive*).

### 2.1.1 Descriptive Models
**Why they exist:** Managers need a factual baseline. **How they work:** Aggregate, summarize, and visualize historical data using OLAP cubes, KPIs, and data warehousing techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Mechanisms:** Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Frequency Distribution, Pivot Tables.

**Engineering Utility:** Used in MIS (Management Information Systems), ERP dashboards, real-time monitoring of supply chains.

### 2.1.2 Diagnostic Models
**Why they exist:** Understanding *why* something happened is the bridge between description and prediction. **How they work:** Drill-down analysis, correlation analysis, root-cause analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Mechanisms:** Pearson correlation coefficient, Chi-square test, ANOVA, Factor Analysis.

### 2.1.3 Predictive Models
**Why they exist:** To forecast future states with quantified uncertainty. **How they work:** Use historical patterns to estimate future outcomes.

**Categories:**
- **Regression-based:** Linear, Logistic, Polynomial.
- **Time-Series:** ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing, Holt-Winters.
- **ML-based:** Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Neural Networks.
- **Probabilistic:** Naive Bayes, Bayesian Networks, Markov Chains.

### 2.1.4 Prescriptive Models
**Why they exist:** The pinnacle of analytics — recommend the *best* action. **How they work:** Combine prediction with optimization or simulation.

**Core Mechanisms:** Linear Programming (LP), Integer Programming (IP), Dynamic Programming, Simulation-Optimization, Decision Analysis.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for "Models in Business Analytics"**

| # | Model Type | Governing Formula / Equation | Key Variable Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Regression | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | $\hat{y}$ = predicted value, $x$ = predictor | Sales forecasting |
| 2 | Multiple Regression | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i x_i$ | $n$ = number of predictors | Multi-factor forecasting |
| 3 | Logistic Regression (probability) | $P(y=1 \mid x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)}}$ | $e$ = Euler's constant $\approx 2.718$ | Churn classification |
| 4 | Linear Programming (LP) | $\max Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | $c_j$ = profit coefficient | Resource optimization |
| 5 | LP Subject to | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \le b_i$ | $a_{ij}$ = resource consumption | Constraint definition |
| 6 | Expected Value (Decision Theory) | $E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i x_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of outcome $i$ | Risk-based decisions |
| 7 | Variance (Risk Metric) | $\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i (x_i - E[X])^2$ | $\sigma^2$ = variance | Volatility measurement |
| 8 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) P(A)}{P(B)}$ | $P(A \mid B)$ = posterior | Customer segmentation |
| 9 | Entropy (Information Gain) | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $c$ = number of classes | Decision tree splitting |
| 10 | Monte Carlo Simulation | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} f(x_k)$ | $N$ = simulation runs | Risk modeling |
| 11 | Time-Series Moving Average | $MA_t = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} Y_{t-i}$ | $k$ = window size | Smoothing trends |
| 12 | Pearson Correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | $r \in [-1, 1]$ | Strength of relationship |
| 13 | Markov Transition | $X_{t+1} = P \cdot X_t$ | $P$ = transition matrix | Customer state modeling |
| 14 | Clustering Objective (k-Means) | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \vert\vert x - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$ | $\mu_j$ = centroid of cluster $j$ | Market segmentation |

## 2.3 The CRISP-DM Modeling Process

The **Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM)** is the de-facto industry framework referenced in KTU Module 1:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Phases of CRISP-DM:**
> 1. **Business Understanding** — Define the problem.
> 2. **Data Understanding** — Explore and quality-check data.
> 3. **Data Preparation** — Clean, transform, engineer features.
> 4. **Modeling** — Select and apply algorithms.
> 5. **Evaluation** — Validate against business KPIs.
> 6. **Deployment** — Operationalize and monitor.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Retail:** Demand forecasting using ARIMA.
- **Banking:** Credit scoring via Logistic Regression.
- **Logistics:** Vehicle routing via Integer Programming.
- **Healthcare:** Patient readmission prediction via Random Forest.
- **Manufacturing:** Six-Sigma quality control via statistical process control models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Programming (LP) Model for a Production Mix Problem

**Business Scenario:** A company in Kerala manufactures two products, *Product A* and *Product B*. Profit per unit is **₹40** for A and **₹30** for B. The products consume two resources — Machine Hours (capacity **80 hrs/week**) and Labor Hours (capacity **100 hrs/week**). Each unit of A requires **2 machine hours and 4 labor hours**. Each unit of B requires **4 machine hours and 2 labor hours**.

**Goal:** Formulate and solve the LP model.

### Step 1 — Decision Variable Identification
Let:
- $x_1$ = number of units of Product A to produce per week.
- $x_2$ = number of units of Product B to produce per week.

### Step 2 — Objective Function Formulation
Maximize total profit:

$$\max Z = 40 x_1 + 30 x_2$$

### Step 3 — Constraint Derivation
Machine-hour constraint (total available $\le$ 80):

$$2 x_1 + 4 x_2 \le 80$$

Labor-hour constraint (total available $\le$ 100):

$$4 x_1 + 2 x_2 \le 100$$

Non-negativity constraints (cannot produce negative units):

$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

### Step 4 — Solution via Corner-Point Method
The corner points of the feasible region are obtained by intersecting constraints:
- Intersection of $2x_1 + 4x_2 = 80$ and $4x_1 + 2x_2 = 100$:

$$\begin{aligned}
2 x_1 + 4 x_2 &= 80 \\
4 x_1 + 2 x_2 &= 100
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply the first equation by 2:

$$4 x_1 + 8 x_2 = 160$$

Subtract the second equation:

$$(4 x_1 + 8 x_2) - (4 x_1 + 2 x_2) = 160 - 100$$

$$6 x_2 = 60 \implies x_2 = 10$$

Substitute $x_2 = 10$ into $2x_1 + 4(10) = 80$:

$$2 x_1 = 40 \implies x_1 = 20$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate Objective at All Corner Points

| Corner Point | $(x_1, x_2)$ | $Z = 40 x_1 + 30 x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | $(0, 0)$ | $0$ |
| Machine-axis | $(40, 0)$ | $1600$ |
| Labor-axis | $(0, 25)$ | $750$ |
| Intersection | $(20, 10)$ | $\mathbf{1100}$ |

**Optimal Solution:** Produce **20 units of A** and **10 units of B** for a maximum weekly profit of **₹1,100**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bayes' Theorem Application — Spam Classification

A simple text classifier has the following statistics:
- Prior probability that any email is spam: $P(S) = 0.2$.
- Probability that a spam email contains the word "offer": $P(O \mid S) = 0.7$.
- Probability that a non-spam email contains the offer: $P(O \mid \bar{S}) = 0.1$.

**Goal:** Compute $P(S \mid O)$ — the probability that an email is spam *given* it contains the word "offer."

### Step 1 — Apply the Law of Total Probability

$$P(O) = P(O \mid S) P(S) + P(O \mid \bar{S}) P(\bar{S})$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$P(O) = (0.7)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.8)$$

$$P(O) = 0.14 + 0.08 = 0.22$$

### Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem

$$P(S \mid O) = \dfrac{P(O \mid S) P(S)}{P(O)}$$

$$P(S \mid O) = \dfrac{0.14}{0.22} = 0.6364$$

**Interpretation:** If the email contains the word "offer," the probability it is spam is approximately **63.64%**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Model Selection Guide

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1 — KTU Model Selection Decision Matrix**

| Business Question | Recommended Model Family | Algorithm Example | Data Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast monthly sales | Predictive / Time-Series | ARIMA, Holt-Winters | Historical sales $\ge$ 24 months |
| Optimize delivery routes | Prescriptive / Optimization | Vehicle Routing LP | Distance matrix, capacity |
| Segment customers | Descriptive / ML | k-Means, DBSCAN | Customer attributes |
| Predict loan default | Predictive / Classification | Logistic Regression | Borrower credit history |
| Simulate inventory risk | Prescriptive / Simulation | Monte Carlo | Demand distribution |
| Identify process bottlenecks | Diagnostic | Fishbone + ANOVA | Process logs |
| Recommend products | Prescriptive / ML | Collaborative Filtering | User-item interaction matrix |
| Detect fraud in real time | Predictive / Anomaly | Isolation Forest | Transaction stream |

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification — Why Expected Value Works for Decisions

Given outcomes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$:

$$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i x_i$$

If a rational decision-maker chooses the alternative with the **highest expected monetary value (EMV)**, they are maximizing the long-run average outcome — a foundational result in Von Neumann–Morgenstern utility theory underpinning prescriptive analytics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between descriptive and prescriptive models in Business Analytics with suitable examples. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Descriptive Model | Prescriptive Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Answers "What happened?" | Answers "What should we do?" |
| **Output** | Reports, dashboards, KPIs | Optimized decisions, action plans |
| **Example** | Monthly sales report of a Kerala FMCG company | Linear programming model to decide optimal product mix |
| **Complexity** | Low (aggregation) | High (optimization) |
| **User** | Operational managers | Strategic planners |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain any three classification criteria used to categorize business analytics models. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **By Analytical Purpose:** Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive (DDPP framework).
2. **By Mathematical Form:** Deterministic vs Probabilistic (stochastic) models.
3. **By Data Dependency:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (unlabeled), and Semi-supervised models.
4. **By Time Orientation:** Static (single snapshot) vs Dynamic (time-evolving) models.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> - Students often **confuse Predictive with Prescriptive** — remember, prediction gives a forecast, prescription gives a *recommended action*.
> - In LP problems, forgetting **non-negativity constraints** leads to mark deduction.
> - Drawing a CRISP-DM diagram without labeling all 6 phases costs 2 marks.
> - For Bayes' theorem problems, students often forget the **denominator (Total Probability)** — partial marks are given if numerator is correct.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Descriptive–Diagnostic–Predictive–Prescriptive (DDPP)** framework of business analytics models. Describe each level with a real-world business example and a relevant mathematical tool. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm produces two products P and Q. The profit per unit is ₹50 for P and ₹60 for Q. Product P requires 3 hours of machine time and 2 hours of labor; Product Q requires 2 hours of machine time and 4 hours of labor. The available machine hours per week is **120**, and labor hours is **100**. **Formulate this as a Linear Programming model** and **solve it using the corner-point method** to find the optimal product mix. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**1. Descriptive Level:**
- **Definition:** Models that summarize and visualize historical data to show "what happened."
- **Mathematical Tool:** Mean, Median, Standard Deviation, Frequency Distribution, OLAP.
- **Example:** A Kerala retailer analyzing last year's festival-season revenue via pivot tables and bar charts.

**2. Diagnostic Level:**
- **Definition:** Models that identify "why" something happened.
- **Mathematical Tool:** Chi-square test, Correlation, ANOVA, Drill-down analysis.
- **Example:** A bank investigating why NPA (non-performing assets) rose 15% in Ernakulam branch using correlation between unemployment and defaults.

**3. Predictive Level:**
- **Definition:** Models that forecast "what will happen."
- **Mathematical Tool:** Linear Regression, ARIMA, Logistic Regression.
- **Example:** A hospital predicting patient readmission risk using logistic regression on historical records.

**4. Prescriptive Level:**
- **Definition:** Models that recommend "what should be done."
- **Mathematical Tool:** Linear Programming, Integer Programming, Simulation.
- **Example:** A logistics company using vehicle-routing optimization to minimize fuel cost across Kerala.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Decision Variables:**
Let $x_1$ = units of P per week, $x_2$ = units of Q per week.

**Step 2 — Objective Function:**

$$\max Z = 50 x_1 + 60 x_2$$

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

$$3 x_1 + 2 x_2 \le 120 \quad \text{(Machine hours)}$$

$$2 x_1 + 4 x_2 \le 100 \quad \text{(Labor hours)}$$

$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

**Step 4 — Corner Points:**

- $A = (0, 0) \implies Z = 0$
- $B = (40, 0) \implies Z = 50(40) = 2000$
- $C = (0, 25) \implies Z = 60(25) = 1500$
- $D$ = Intersection of both constraints:

$$\begin{aligned}
3 x_1 + 2 x_2 &= 120 \\
2 x_1 + 4 x_2 &= 100
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply first by 2 and second by 1:

$$6 x_1 + 4 x_2 = 240$$

$$2 x_1 + 4 x_2 = 100$$

Subtract:

$$4 x_1 = 140 \implies x_1 = 35$$

Substitute back: $3(35) + 2 x_2 = 120 \implies 2 x_2 = 15 \implies x_2 = 7.5$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at D:**

$$Z = 50(35) + 60(7.5) = 1750 + 450 = 2200$$

**Step 6 — Optimal Solution:**
The maximum profit is **₹2,200** at $(x_1, x_2) = (35, 7.5)$, i.e., produce **35 units of P** and **7.5 units of Q** per week.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Discuss the **CRISP-DM** methodology for business analytics modeling. List all six phases and explain any three phases in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A diagnostic test for a disease has the following properties:
- Prior probability of disease: $P(D) = 0.05$.
- Sensitivity (true positive rate): $P(+\mid D) = 0.98$.
- False positive rate: $P(+\mid \bar{D}) = 0.03$.

A patient tests positive. Using **Bayes' Theorem**, compute the probability that the patient actually has the disease. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Six Phases of CRISP-DM:**
1. Business Understanding
2. Data Understanding
3. Data Preparation
4. Modeling
5. Evaluation
6. Deployment

**Detailed Explanation (3 chosen):**

**Phase 1 — Business Understanding:** Translate the business problem into an analytics problem. Define KPIs, success criteria, and resource needs. Example: "Reduce customer churn by 10%" → Analytics problem: "Predict churn probability and identify top drivers."

**Phase 2 — Data Understanding:** Collect initial data, perform exploratory data analysis (EDA), detect missing values, outliers, and distributions. Tools: pandas, Matplotlib, summary statistics.

**Phase 3 — Modeling:** Select modeling techniques (e.g., regression, classification), tune hyperparameters, train-test split, and validate using cross-validation.

*(Phases 4–6 can be summarized: Evaluation checks business goals; Deployment integrates the model into production; Feedback loop returns to Phase 1.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Total Probability of Positive Test:**

$$P(+) = P(+\mid D) P(D) + P(+\mid \bar{D}) P(\bar{D})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$P(+) = (0.98)(0.05) + (0.03)(0.95)$$

$$P(+) = 0.049 + 0.0285 = 0.0775$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(D \mid +) = \dfrac{P(+\mid D) P(D)}{P(+)}$$

$$P(D \mid +) = \dfrac{0.049}{0.0775} = 0.6323$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Even after testing positive, the patient's chance of actually having the disease is only **63.23%**. This counterintuitive result occurs because the **prior probability (base rate) of the disease is very low (5%)**. A second confirmatory test is recommended.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision:**
> - **DDPP Framework** is the gold-standard KTU model taxonomy: **Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive**.
> - **CRISP-DM** has 6 phases; remember the loop from Deployment back to Business Understanding.
> - **Descriptive = "What happened"**; **Predictive = "What will happen"**; **Prescriptive = "What to do"**.
> - **Linear Programming template:** Maximize $Z = c^T x$ subject to $A x \le b$, $x \ge 0$.
> - **Always include non-negativity** constraints in LP problems.
> - **Bayes' Theorem denominator** = total probability of evidence — students often forget this.
> - **Optimization models** (LP, IP) belong to Prescriptive analytics.
> - **Time-series models** (ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing) are predictive, not descriptive.
> - **k-Means clustering** is *unsupervised* — it describes structure, not prediction.
> - **Expected Value** $E[X] = \sum p_i x_i$ is the cornerstone of rational decision-making under uncertainty.
> - **Correlation does NOT imply causation** — important diagnostic caveat.
> - KTU examiners reward **clear diagrams** of CRISP-DM and DDPP — at least 2 marks reserved for visual clarity.
> - Memorize units: profit in ₹, time in hours, probability as a dimensionless number in $[0, 1]$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem Solving with Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Problem Solving with Analytics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Problem Solving with Analytics"? In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Problem Solving with Analytics** refers to the systematic, scientific, and structured methodology used by business analysts and data professionals to convert raw organizational data into **actionable business decisions**. It is the disciplined application of statistical, mathematical, and logical techniques to diagnose, model, and resolve business problems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (PECBT755):** > *Problem Solving with Analytics is the iterative process of identifying a business problem, framing it analytically, acquiring and preparing relevant data, applying appropriate analytical models (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, or prescriptive), interpreting the outputs, and communicating insights to drive measurable business value.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor for a Business" Imagine a business is a **human patient** visiting a doctor: | Stage in Medical Diagnosis | Equivalent in Business Analytics | |----------------------------|----------------------------------| | Patient describes symptoms | Business stakeholder reports a problem (e.g., falling sales) | | Doctor asks diagnostic questions | Analyst defines the problem and KPIs | | Doctor orders blood tests / X-rays | Data is collected and cleaned | | Doctor diagnoses the disease | Diagnostic/Descriptive Analytics is applied | | Doctor predicts recovery trajectory | Predictive Analytics forecasts trends | | Doctor prescribes medicine & diet | Prescriptive Analytics recommends actions | | Follow-up visits | Monitoring, deployment, and feedback loop | > [!NOTE] > Just as a doctor does not prescribe medicine without first understanding the disease, an analyst should not jump to a model (machine learning, regression) without first properly **framing the problem**. The KTU syllabus emphasizes that *80% of the value in analytics lies in problem framing, not in algorithm selection*. ### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Analytics Problem Solving A problem is solvable with analytics when it satisfies three conditions: 1. **Measurability** — The problem can be quantified (numbers exist or can be collected). 2. **Data Availability** — Historical or real-time data is accessible. 3. **Decision Relevance** — Solving the problem leads to a tangible business action. > [!TIP] > **Standard KTU Metric Highlight:** The maturity of analytics adoption in an organization is often measured on a **5-level Gartner Analytics Maturity Model (1.0 – 5.0)**, where level 1 is "Analytics-disabled" and level 5 is "Analytics-optimized." Most Indian firms sit between 1.5 and 2.5. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Types of Analytics (The "Analytics Pyramid") The **Gartner Analytics Continuum** is the most frequently tested framework in KTU exams. It arranges analytical techniques from simple to sophisticated: ### Level 1: Descriptive Analytics — *"What happened?"* - **Purpose:** Summarize historical data into digestible insights. - **Techniques:** Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Data Visualization (bar, pie, line), Reports, Dashboards. - **Example:** A retailer's monthly sales report showing total revenue of ₹50 lakhs. ### Level 2: Diagnostic Analytics — *"Why did it happen?"* - **Purpose:** Drill down to identify root causes of past outcomes. - **Techniques:** Correlation analysis, Drill-down, Data mining, Root cause analysis, Fishbone diagrams. - **Example:** Sales dropped because of a 30% decline in the North region during the festive month. ### Level 3: Predictive Analytics — *"What is likely to happen?"* - **Purpose:** Forecast future events using historical patterns. - **Techniques:** Linear/Logistic Regression, Time Series Forecasting, Decision Trees, Neural Networks. - **Example:** Predicting that next quarter's sales will be ₹55 lakhs with 85% confidence. ### Level 4: Prescriptive Analytics — *"What should we do?"* - **Purpose:** Recommend optimal actions to achieve desired outcomes. - **Techniques:** Linear/Integer Programming, Simulation, Optimization, Reinforcement Learning. - **Example:** Recommending the optimal price-discount mix to maximize profit. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Trick:** Students often confuse *Predictive* with *Prescriptive*. Remember the **"P-P" Rule**: **P**redict **P**rojects; **P**rescribe **P**roposes a plan of action. ## 2.2 The Analytics Problem-Solving Process (CRISP-DM Framework) The **Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM)** is the de facto industry standard mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus. It has **6 iterative phases**: 1. **Business Understanding** — Define the business objective and translate it into an analytics problem. 2. **Data Understanding** — Collect initial data, describe it, explore it, and verify quality. 3. **Data Preparation** — Clean, transform, integrate, and format the data (often 60–80% of project time). 4. **Modeling** — Select and apply modeling techniques (e.g., regression, clustering). 5. **Evaluation** — Assess whether the model meets business success criteria. 6. **Deployment** — Put the model into production; monitor and maintain it. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Definition | Application Context | |---------|---------------------|---------------------| | **Mean (Average)** | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Central tendency of historical data | | **Median** | Middle value when data is sorted | Robust to outliers (used in diagnostic analytics) | | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Measures variability / risk | | **Variance** | $\sigma^2$ | Squared dispersion measure | | **Correlation Coefficient (Pearson)** | $r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Strength of linear relationship (-1 to +1) | | **Linear Regression (Predictive)** | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$ | Forecasts continuous outcomes | | **Logistic Regression (Predictive)** | $P(Y=1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X)}}$ | Predicts binary outcomes (Yes/No) | | **R-Squared (Model Fit)** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | % of variance explained by model (0 to 1) | | **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Average prediction error | | **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Penalizes large errors more heavily | | **Confusion Matrix Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification model evaluation | | **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Reliability of positive predictions | | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | | **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced data | | **Time Series Decomposition** | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$ | Trend + Seasonality + Cyclical + Error | | **Moving Average (Forecasting)** | $MA_k = \frac{x_t + x_{t-1} + ... + x_{t-k+1}}{k}$ | Smooths short-term fluctuations | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility - **Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance uses sensor data to forecast machine failures. - **Banking:** Credit scoring uses logistic regression to predict default probability. - **Healthcare:** Diagnostic analytics identifies high-risk patient cohorts. - **Retail:** Prescriptive analytics optimizes inventory and dynamic pricing. - **Telecom:** Churn prediction models retain high-value customers. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Regression Coefficient ($\beta_1$) The **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** derivation is a high-yield KTU question. We minimize the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE). ### Step 1: Define the Error The error (residual) for each observation is: $$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)$$ *Text: The error is the vertical distance between the actual data point and the predicted value on the regression line.* ### Step 2: Form the Objective Function Sum the squared errors across all $n$ observations: $$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} [y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)]^2$$ *Text: We square the errors so that positive and negative errors do not cancel out, and we amplify larger errors.* ### Step 3: Minimize with Respect to $\beta_0$ Take the partial derivative and set to zero: $$\frac{\partial (SSE)}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} [y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i] = 0$$ *Text: Solving this yields the normal equation for the intercept.* $$\sum y_i = n \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum x_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$ ### Step 4: Minimize with Respect to $\beta_1$ Take the partial derivative and set to zero: $$\frac{\partial (SSE)}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i [y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i] = 0$$ *Text: This produces the slope formula via the covariance-to-variance ratio.* ### Step 5: Substitute $\beta_0$ and Simplify After substitution and algebraic manipulation: $$\begin{aligned} \beta_1 &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2} \\ &= \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\text{Var}(X)} \end{aligned}$$ *Text: The slope is mathematically the covariance of X and Y divided by the variance of X.* ## 3.2 Derivation 2: R-Squared Coefficient of Determination ### Step 1: Define Total Sum of Squares $$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$$ *Text: Total variability of Y around its mean.* ### Step 2: Define Residual Sum of Squares $$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$ *Text: Unexplained variability after the model.* ### Step 3: Define Explained Sum of Squares $$SS_{reg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$$ *Text: Variability explained by the regression model.* ### Step 4: Apply the Identity By definition: $SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$ ### Step 5: Define R-Squared $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}}$$ *Text: R² is the proportion of total variance explained by the model. A value of 0.85 means the model explains 85% of the variation in Y.* ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) **Problem:** A retailer tracks advertising spend (X, in ₹000) and monthly sales (Y, in ₹000). Data for 5 months: - $(10, 100), (15, 130), (20, 150), (25, 170), (30, 200)$ **Step 1: Compute Means** $$\bar{x} = \frac{10+15+20+25+30}{5} = 20, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{100+130+150+170+200}{5} = 150$$ **Step 2: Compute Deviations** | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ | |-------|-------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------------|-------------------| | 10 | 100 | -10 | -50 | 500 | 100 | | 15 | 130 | -5 | -20 | 100 | 25 | | 20 | 150 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 25 | 170 | 5 | 20 | 100 | 25 | | 30 | 200 | 10 | 50 | 500 | 100 | | **Σ** | | | | **1200** | **250** | **Step 3: Compute Slope** $$\beta_1 = \frac{1200}{250} = 4.8$$ *Text: Every additional ₹1,000 in ad spend increases sales by ₹4,800.* **Step 4: Compute Intercept** $$\beta_0 = 150 - 4.8 \times 20 = 150 - 96 = 54$$ **Step 5: Write the Regression Equation** $$Y = 54 + 4.8X$$ *Text: This equation is the predictive model. If ad spend is ₹40,000 (X=40), predicted sales = 54 + 4.8(40) = ₹246,000.* --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Analytics Problem-Solving Lifecycle (CRISP-DM) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Business Understanding
Define Business Objective] --> B[Data Understanding
Collect & Explore Data] B --> C[Data Preparation
Clean and Transform] C --> D[Modeling
Apply Analytical Techniques] D --> E[Evaluation
Validate against Business Goals] E --> F[Deployment
Implement in Production] F --> G[Monitor and Maintain] G --> A E -.Feedback Loop.-> A D -.Iterate.-> C ``` *Caption: The iterative CRISP-DM cycle used universally in industry and tested in KTU exams.* ## 4.2 The Analytics Maturity Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD L4["PRESCRIPTIVE
What should we do?
Optimization, Simulation"] L3["PREDICTIVE
What will happen?
Regression, Forecasting, ML"] L2["DIAGNOSTIC
Why did it happen?
Drill-down, Correlation, RCA"] L1["DESCRIPTIVE
What happened?
Reports, Dashboards, KPIs"] L0["DATA SOURCES
Raw Data, Databases, IoT"] L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 ``` *Caption: The Gartner Analytics Continuum arranged as a four-tier pyramid showing increasing value and complexity.* ## 4.3 Problem Type to Technique Mapping (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "Problem Category" P1[Classification] P2[Regression] P3[Clustering] P4[Optimization] P5[Forecasting] end subgraph "Analytical Technique" T1[Logistic Regression
Decision Trees] T2[Linear Regression
Random Forest] T3[K-Means
Hierarchical Clustering] T4[Linear Programming
Integer Programming] T5[ARIMA
Exponential Smoothing] end subgraph "Business Outcome" O1[Churn Prediction
Fraud Detection] O2[Sales Forecast
Price Estimation] O3[Customer Segmentation
Market Basket] O4[Resource Allocation
Route Planning] O5[Demand Planning
Inventory Optimization] end P1 --> T1 --> O1 P2 --> T2 --> O2 P3 --> T3 --> O3 P4 --> T4 --> O4 P5 --> T5 --> O5 ``` *Caption: Mapping business problem categories to appropriate analytical techniques and expected outcomes — a frequently tested KTU concept.* --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Descriptive Analytics and Predictive Analytics with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Descriptive Analytics | Predictive Analytics | |-----------|----------------------|---------------------| | Question answered | What happened? | What is likely to happen? | | Time orientation | Past / Historical | Future-oriented | | Techniques | Reports, dashboards, summary statistics | Regression, time series, ML models | | Example | Monthly sales report showing ₹50L revenue | Forecast of ₹55L sales for next quarter | | Output | Summary / Visualization | Probability / Forecast value | *(Award 1.5 marks for definition of each, 0.5 mark for example difference. Total: 3 marks)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **List and briefly explain any THREE phases of the CRISP-DM methodology.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Business Understanding (1 mark):** Identify the business problem, define success criteria, and frame it as an analytics problem. 2. **Data Preparation (1 mark):** Clean missing values, handle outliers, normalize, and integrate data from multiple sources. (Takes 60–80% of project time.) 3. **Modeling (1 mark):** Apply appropriate algorithms (regression, clustering, classification) and tune hyperparameters to fit the prepared data. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **(a)** Explain the four types of analytics in detail with one real-world business example for each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A logistics company wants to predict monthly fuel costs (Y in ₹000) based on delivery distance (X in km). Data from 6 months is given below. Fit a simple linear regression model and predict fuel cost when distance is 450 km. **(7 marks)** | Month | Distance X (km) | Fuel Cost Y (₹000) | |-------|-----------------|---------------------| | 1 | 100 | 30 | | 2 | 200 | 50 | | 3 | 300 | 65 | | 4 | 400 | 85 | | 5 | 500 | 95 | | 6 | 600 | 110 | --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **Four Types of Analytics:** **1. Descriptive Analytics — "What happened?" (1.5 marks)** - Summarizes historical data. - Techniques: Mean, Median, Reports, Dashboards. - Example: An e-commerce platform generating a daily sales report showing total orders, revenue, and top-selling products. **2. Diagnostic Analytics — "Why did it happen?" (1.5 marks)** - Identifies root causes by drilling into data. - Techniques: Correlation, Root Cause Analysis, Drill-down queries. - Example: A bank discovers that a 20% rise in customer complaints was caused by a server outage on a specific date. **3. Predictive Analytics — "What will happen?" (2 marks)** - Uses statistical and ML models to forecast future outcomes. - Techniques: Linear Regression, ARIMA, Decision Trees, Neural Networks. - Example: A telecom company predicts that 8% of its premium customers are likely to churn in the next quarter. **4. Prescriptive Analytics — "What should we do?" (2 marks)** - Recommends optimal actions based on constraints and objectives. - Techniques: Linear Programming, Simulation, Optimization. - Example: A supply chain manager uses prescriptive analytics to determine the optimal reorder quantity and warehouse location to minimize total logistics cost. --- #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Calculate Means (1 mark)** $$\bar{x} = \frac{100+200+300+400+500+600}{6} = 350$$ $$\bar{y} = \frac{30+50+65+85+95+110}{6} = \frac{435}{6} = 72.5$$ **Step 2: Build the Computation Table (2 marks)** | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | |-------|-------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------------------|---------------------| | 100 | 30 | -250 | -42.5 | 10625 | 62500 | | 200 | 50 | -150 | -22.5 | 3375 | 22500 | | 300 | 65 | -50 | -7.5 | 375 | 2500 | | 400 | 85 | 50 | 12.5 | 625 | 2500 | | 500 | 95 | 150 | 22.5 | 3375 | 22500 | | 600 | 110 | 250 | 37.5 | 9375 | 62500 | | **Σ** | | | | **27750** | **175000** | **Step 3: Calculate Slope $\beta_1$ (1.5 marks)** $$\beta_1 = \frac{27750}{175000} = 0.1586$$ **Step 4: Calculate Intercept $\beta_0$ (1 mark)** $$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 72.5 - (0.1586 \times 350) = 72.5 - 55.5 = 17.0$$ **Step 5: Regression Equation and Prediction (1.5 marks)** $$Y = 17.0 + 0.1586 X$$ For $X = 450$ km: $$Y = 17.0 + 0.1586 \times 450 = 17.0 + 71.36 = \textbf{₹88.36 thousand} \ (\approx ₹88,360)$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Define the term "Analytics". Explain the CRISP-DM methodology in detail with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** The following data represents the relationship between years of experience (X) and salary (Y in ₹000). Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient and comment on the strength of the relationship. **(7 marks)** | Experience (years) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | |--------------------|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Salary (₹000) | 25| 35| 40| 50| 55| 65| --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (1 mark):** Analytics is the scientific process of transforming raw data into insights through statistical, mathematical, and computational techniques to support better business decision-making. **CRISP-DM Phases (5 marks):** 1. **Business Understanding:** Define objectives from a business perspective; convert into a data mining problem. Output: Business success criteria. 2. **Data Understanding:** Initial data collection, familiarization, identify data quality issues. Output: Data exploration report. 3. **Data Preparation:** Activities to construct the final dataset — cleaning, transformation, integration. Output: Clean dataset. 4. **Modeling:** Select modeling technique, split into train/test, build model, calibrate parameters. Output: Trained model. 5. **Evaluation:** Determine if the model meets business objectives; review the process. Output: Approved model. 6. **Deployment:** Put model into operation — generate report, integrate into business process. Output: Functional system. **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the CRISP-DM lifecycle in Section 4.1. --- #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Calculate Means (1 mark)** $$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5+6}{6} = 3.5$$ $$\bar{y} = \frac{25+35+40+50+55+65}{6} = \frac{270}{6} = 45$$ **Step 2: Build the Table (2 marks)** | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | |-------|-------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------| | 1 | 25 | -2.5 | -20 | 50 | 6.25 | 400 | | 2 | 35 | -1.5 | -10 | 15 | 2.25 | 100 | | 3 | 40 | -0.5 | -5 | 2.5 | 0.25 | 25 | | 4 | 50 | 0.5 | 5 | 2.5 | 0.25 | 25 | | 5 | 55 | 1.5 | 10 | 15 | 2.25 | 100 | | 6 | 65 | 2.5 | 20 | 50 | 6.25 | 400 | | **Σ** | | | | **135** | **17.5** | **1050** | **Step 3: Apply Pearson Formula (2 marks)** $$r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$ $$r = \frac{135}{\sqrt{17.5 \times 1050}} = \frac{135}{\sqrt{18375}} = \frac{135}{135.55} \approx 0.9959$$ **Step 4: Interpretation (2 marks)** - The Pearson correlation coefficient $r \approx 0.996$ is **extremely close to +1**. - This indicates a **very strong positive linear relationship** between years of experience and salary. - Interpretation: As experience increases by 1 year, salary tends to increase by approximately ₹8,000 (which can be confirmed by computing the regression slope: $\beta_1 = 135/17.5 = 7.71$). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Predictive vs. Prescriptive** — Predictive forecasts; Prescriptive recommends. Worth 1 mark in any 7-mark sub-question. > 2. **Skipping Units in Numerical Answers** — Always write ₹, km, years etc. A model answer without units loses 0.5–1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting to Comment on R² or r** — Simply calculating the coefficient without interpretation is incomplete. Examiners explicitly look for the qualitative comment. > 4. **Mixing up $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$** — A common sign error that cascades into wrong $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$. Double-check by visually verifying that the regression line passes through $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$. > 5. **Not Drawing the CRISP-DM Diagram** — Even a rough sketch fetches full marks for the "diagram" portion. > 6. **Using Python or Code in Descriptive Answers** — Strictly NOT required. KTU BA papers expect business interpretation, not code. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Problem Solving with Analytics** - ✅ Analytics = Data + Statistical Methods + Business Context → Insights → Decisions - ✅ **Four Types (Gartner Pyramid):** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive - ✅ **CRISP-DM has 6 phases:** Business → Data → Preparation → Modeling → Evaluation → Deployment (iterative, not linear) - ✅ **Pearson Correlation r** lies in $[-1, +1]$; closer to ±1 = stronger linear relationship - ✅ **Linear Regression Formula:** $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ - ✅ **Slope Formula:** $\beta_1 = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ - ✅ **Intercept Formula:** $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ - ✅ **R² Formula:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ - ✅ **Classification Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score - ✅ **Forecasting Metric:** RMSE penalizes large errors more than MAE - ✅ **Decision Rule:** If problem requires a "Yes/No" → use **Logistic Regression**; if it requires a "How much" → use **Linear Regression**; if it requires grouping → use **Clustering** - ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always quote the question being answered — "What happened?" for Descriptive, "Why?" for Diagnostic, "What next?" for Predictive, "What to do?" for Prescriptive - ✅ **Real-world KTU Example:** Amazon uses all four types — Descriptive (sales dashboards), Diagnostic (why returns spiked), Predictive (demand forecasting), Prescriptive (dynamic pricing) --- *End of Module 1 Notes — Problem Solving with Analytics (PECBT755)* *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Standards*

topics.title: Exploring Data Analytics on Spreadsheet - Data Structure and Data View
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exploring Data Analytics on Spreadsheet - Data Structure and Data View

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Structure in a Spreadsheet** refers to the systematic organization of data into a tabular grid of **rows (records/observations)** and **columns (fields/variables)**, where each cell (intersection of a row and column) holds a single atomic data value. In the context of Business Analytics and the KTU 2024 syllabus, this represents the foundational schema upon which all descriptive, diagnostic, and predictive analytics operations are executed.

**Data View** in a spreadsheet denotes the *perspective* or *lens* through which an analyst visualizes, inspects, manipulates, and interprets the underlying structured data. Common views include **Normal/Standard View**, **Page Layout View**, **Page Break Preview**, **Custom Views**, and **Filter/Sorted Views** (implemented in tools like Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets, and LibreOffice Calc).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PECBT755 course explicitly positions spreadsheets as the *primary exploratory data analysis (EDA)* tool for business analysts before graduating to advanced tools (SQL, Python, Power BI, Tableau). A strong grasp of data structure and view is the **prerequisite** for Modules 2 (Statistical Analysis) and Module 3 (Visualization).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Catalog" Intuition

Imagine a massive **library**:
- The **building** = Your spreadsheet file (workbook).
- The **reading halls** = Individual worksheets (sheets/tabs).
- The **card catalog cabinet** = The tabular data structure (rows × columns).
- Each **catalog card (row)** = One *record* — e.g., a single customer transaction.
- Each **field on the card (column)** = One *attribute* — e.g., Customer Name, Date, Amount.
- Each **handwritten detail on a field** = A single **cell value**.
- The **magnifying glass and reading lamp** = The *Data View* — letting you see the catalog in different ways (sorted by date, filtered by region, summarized by genre).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spreadsheets?** Over **750 million users** worldwide use Microsoft Excel. Spreadsheets are the *lingua franca* of business analytics because they combine **data storage**, **computation**, and **visualization** in a single low-code environment.

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary Anchored to KTU Terminology

| KTU Term | Industry Synonym | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| **Workbook** | File (`.xlsx`) | A complete file containing many sheets |
| **Worksheet / Sheet** | Tab | A single page of rows and columns |
| **Cell** | Data Point | The smallest unit holding one value |
| **Range** | Block | A group of contiguous cells (e.g., `A1:D10`) |
| **Record (Row)** | Observation / Tuple | One complete entity entry |
| **Field (Column)** | Variable / Attribute | One type of information for all records |
| **Header Row** | Schema | Top row labeling each column |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Spreadsheet Data Structure

### Pillar 1 — Rectangular Tabular Architecture
A valid analytics data structure must obey the **rectangular rule**: every row must have the *same number of columns*, and every column must have the *same number of rows*. No merged cells, no blank rows interspersed within the data block, no ragged edges.

### Pillar 2 — Atomic Cell Principle (First Normal Form — 1NF)
Each cell must contain **one and only one** piece of information. Storing "John, 28, Male" in a single cell violates 1NF and cripples filtering, sorting, and aggregation.

### Pillar 3 — Header Discipline
Row 1 must contain **unique, descriptive field names**. No duplicates, no special characters in headers, and no blank header cells.

## 2.2 Data Types in Spreadsheets — The Four Sacred Classes

| Data Type | Example | Excel Token | Analytics Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Text / String** | "Kerala", "Mumbai" | Left-aligned by default | Categorical / Nominal variables |
| **Number (Integer)** | 25, 1000 | Right-aligned | Discrete quantitative data |
| **Number (Decimal/Float)** | 25.75, 99.99 | Right-aligned | Continuous quantitative data |
| **Date / Time** | 12-Mar-2025 | Right-aligned (stored as serial) | Time-series analysis |
| **Boolean / Logical** | TRUE / FALSE | Centered | Flags, conditions, filters |
| **Currency / Accounting** | ₹5,000.00 | Formatted | Financial KPIs |
| **Percentage** | 18.5% | Formatted | Ratios, growth rates |
| **Error** | #DIV/0!, #N/A | Centered | Signals a computational problem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Dates are stored internally as **serial numbers** (e.g., `12-Mar-2025` = `45725`). This is why date arithmetic works (`=B2 - B1` gives days between dates). A student who treats dates as text will break every time-series chart.

## 2.3 Data View — The Five Standardized Perspectives

### View 1 — Normal View (Default)
The everyday working view. Shows the unbounded grid where you enter and edit data.

### View 2 — Page Layout View
Mirrors the printed page. Shows margins, headers/footers, and page breaks. Used before printing reports.

### View 3 — Page Break Preview
Displays blue dashed lines indicating where pages will break when printed. Useful for fitting wide reports.

### View 4 — Custom View
A *saved snapshot* of the current display settings (hidden rows/columns, filters, zoom level). Lets users toggle between multiple presentations of the same data.

### View 5 — Filtered / Sorted View
A logical view where rows are temporarily hidden or reordered without altering the underlying data.

## 2.4 The Range Reference Grammar — KTU Formula Sheet

| Notation | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `A1` | Single cell reference | `=A1*2` |
| `A1:A10` | Vertical range (column) | `=SUM(A1:A10)` |
| `A1:D1` | Horizontal range (row) | `=AVERAGE(A1:D1)` |
| `A1:D10` | Rectangular block (2-D range) | `=COUNT(A1:D10)` |
| `A:A` | Entire column | `=SUM(A:A)` |
| `1:1` | Entire row | `=AVERAGE(1:1)` |
| `A1`, `B1` (comma) | Union (non-contiguous) | `=SUM(A1, B1, C1)` |
| `Sheet2!A1` | Cross-sheet reference | `=Sheet2!A1*1.18` |
| `[Book2]Sheet1!A1` | Cross-workbook reference | `=[Book2]Sheet1!A1` |
| `$A$1` | Absolute reference (locked both) | `=$A$1*B2` |
| `A$1` | Row-locked mixed reference | Used in vertical fill |
| `$A1` | Column-locked mixed reference | Used in horizontal fill |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute vs Relative — The Single Most Tested Concept in KTU.** When you copy a formula, relative references shift; absolute references do not. Press **F4** to toggle through the four combinations (`A1` → `$A$1` → `A$1` → `$A1` → `A1`).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Finance:** Trial balances, P&L statements, cash-flow forecasts.
- **Operations:** Inventory tracking, supply chain reorder points.
- **HR Analytics:** Employee headcount, attrition rates, payroll.
- **Marketing:** Campaign ROI, customer segmentation tables.
- **Data Science Pre-processing:** The first 80% of data cleaning happens in spreadsheets before any code is written.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving the "Rectangular Rule" Violation

**Problem Statement:** A student records the following sales data. Identify all structural violations.

| Date | Product | Qty, Price |
|---|---|---|
| 01-Jan-2025 | Pen | 10, 5 |
| 02-Jan-2025 | Notebook | 5 |
| 03-Jan-2025 | Eraser, Sharpener | 3, 2 |

**Step-by-Step Structural Audit:**

**Step 1 — Scan headers.**
The third column header is `Qty, Price`. This contains two attributes joined by a comma, violating the **unique field name** rule and the **atomic cell principle**.

**Step 2 — Count cells per row in the body.**

$$
R_1 = 3 \text{ cells filled}, \quad R_2 = 2 \text{ cells filled}, \quad R_3 = 4 \text{ cells filled}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the rectangular rule.**

$$
\text{For a valid table:} \quad \forall i, j : \; \text{cols}(R_i) = \text{cols}(R_j)
$$

$$
\text{Here:} \quad 3 \neq 2 \neq 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Violation Detected}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the offending cell.**
Cell `C2` is *blank*; cell `C3` contains `Eraser, Sharpener` (two items in one cell). Both must be split.

**Step 5 — Rectified (1NF-Compliant) Structure:**

| Date | Product | Qty | Unit Price |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01-Jan-2025 | Pen | 10 | 5 |
| 02-Jan-2025 | Notebook | 5 | 7 |
| 03-Jan-2025 | Eraser | 3 | 2 |
| 03-Jan-2025 | Sharpener | 2 | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of conversion:** Whenever one row contains two physical items, you **expand** it into multiple rows (one per atomic entity). This is called *flattening* the data.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relative-to-Absolute Reference Conversion

**Problem:** You have a tax rate of **18%** stored in cell `$H$1`. You write `=E2*$H$1` in cell `F2` and copy it down to `F10`. Show the formula at each row to prove absolute locking.

**Step 1 — Anchor the constant.** Tax rate in `H1` must not shift, hence `$H$1`.

**Step 2 — Apply relative referencing rule.** When copied **down by 1 row**, row index increases by 1, but column letter is locked by `$`.

$$
F_2 = E_2 \times \$H\$1
$$

$$
F_3 = E_3 \times \$H\$1
$$

$$
F_4 = E_4 \times \$H\$1
$$

$$
\vdots
$$

$$
F_{10} = E_{10} \times \$H\$1
$$

**Step 3 — General Formula (proof by induction):**

$$
F_{n} = E_{n} \times \$H\$1, \quad \text{for } n \in \{2, 3, \ldots, 10\}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with mixed reference.** If we had used `H$1` (column-relative, row-absolute) and copied *horizontally*, the column letter `H` would shift to `I`, `J`, etc. — which is exactly what we want when scaling a formula across columns of a multiplication table.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Date Serial Number Arithmetic

**Problem:** `A1 = 15-Jan-2025`, `B1 = 20-Mar-2025`. Compute the number of days between them.

**Step 1 — Excel's date epoch.** Excel (and Lotus 1-2-3 before it) uses `01-Jan-1900` as Day 1.

**Step 2 — Implicit conversion.** The spreadsheet engine stores:

$$
\text{serial}(A_1) = 45668, \quad \text{serial}(B_1) = 45732
$$

**Step 3 — Apply subtraction.**

$$
\Delta = B_1 - A_1 = 45732 - 45668 = 64 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify via calendar logic.** From 15-Jan to 31-Jan = 16 days. February 2025 has 28 days. 01-Mar to 20-Mar = 20 days. Total = 16 + 28 + 20 = **64 days**. ✓

**Step 5 — Embed in a formula.**

$$
\text{Result cell } C_1 \;=\; =B_1 - A_1 \quad \text{(format as Number, not Date)}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example — The "Pivot-Style" Summarization by Hand

**Given the data below, derive the `SUMIF`-equivalent pivot table.**

| Region | Salesperson | Units Sold |
|---|---|---|
| North | Asha | 120 |
| South | Biju | 95 |
| North | Cyrus | 150 |
| East | Diya | 80 |
| South | Asha | 110 |

**Step 1 — Identify the grouping key.** Region is the dimension; sum of Units Sold is the measure.

**Step 2 — Define the formula structure.**

$$
\text{Grand Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Units}_i
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the SUMIF for each region.**

$$
\text{North} = 120 + 150 = 270
$$

$$
\text{South} = 95 + 110 = 205
$$

$$
\text{East} = 80
$$

**Step 4 — Excel formula equivalent.**

$$
\text{North Total Cell} \;=\; =\text{SUMIF}(A:A,\; \text{"North"},\; C:C)
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-foot the grand total.**

$$
\text{Grand Total} = 270 + 205 + 80 = 555
$$

$$
\text{Direct sum check} = 120 + 95 + 150 + 80 + 110 = 555 \;\; ✓
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Spreadsheet Architecture Hierarchy

```mermaid
    node1 --> node2

    node12 --> node14
    node13 --> node14
    node2 --> node15
    node15 --> node16
    node15 --> node17

    node15 --> node20
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Data View Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Analyst opens dataset]
    q1{Need to print report?}

    a2[Filter or Sort View]
    a3[Normal View]
    a4[Custom View]
    endA[Output: Presentation ready]

    startA --> q3
    q3 -->|Yes| a3
    q3 -->|No| q1
    q1 -->|Yes| a1
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| a2
    q2 -->|No| q4
    q4 -->|Yes| a4
    q4 -->|No| a3
    a1 --> endA
    a2 --> endA
    a3 --> endA
    a4 --> endA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reference Type Behavior During Copy

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Original Formula in B2 equals A1 times 2]
    c1[Copy down to B3]
    c2[Copy right to C2]
    c3[Copy down and right to C3]
    r1[B3 becomes A2 times 2 - Relative shift both]
    r2[C2 becomes B1 times 2 - Relative shift both]
    r3[C3 becomes B2 times 2 - Relative shift both]
    abs1[Formula in B2 equals dollar A dollar 1 times 2]
    abs2[Copy down to B3]
    abs3[Result B3 equals dollar A dollar 1 times 2 - Locked]

    src --> c1
    src --> c2
    src --> c3
    c1 --> r1
    c2 --> r2
    c3 --> r3
    abs1 --> abs2
    abs2 --> abs3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Data Cleaning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Unstructured Data]

    head[Apply Header Discipline]

    an1[Descriptive Statistics]
    an2[Filter and Sort]
    an3[Visualization and Pivot]

    raw --> rect
    rect --> atom
    atom --> head
    head --> type
    type --> valid
```
