$$\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 &= (12-15.29)^2 + (15-15.29)^2 + (14-15.29)^2 \\
&\quad + (18-15.29)^2 + (12-15.29)^2 + (20-15.29)^2 + (16-15.29)^2 \\
&= 10.82 + 0.08 + 1.66 + 7.34 + 10.82 + 22.18 + 0.50 \\
&= 53.40
\end{aligned}$$

$$s^2 = \frac{53.40}{7-1} = \frac{53.40}{6} = 8.90$$

**Step 6: Standard Deviation**
$$s = \sqrt{8.90} \approx 2.98$$

**Step 7: Coefficient of Variation**
$$CV = \frac{2.98}{15.29} \times 100\% \approx 19.49\%$$

**Step 8: Skewness (Pearson)**
$$Skew = \frac{3(15.29 - 15)}{2.98} \approx \frac{0.87}{2.98} \approx 0.29$$

> **Interpretation:** Slight positive (right) skew — sales tend to dip below the mean more often, with occasional high spikes (20).

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Grouped Data — Median from Frequency Distribution

| Class | Frequency (f) | Cumulative Freq (cf) |
|---|---|---|
| 0–10 | 5 | 5 |
| 10–20 | 12 | 17 |
| 20–30 | 18 | 35 |
| 30–40 | 10 | 45 |
| 40–50 | 5 | 50 |

**Step 1:** Total N = 50, so N/2 = 25.

**Step 2:** Identify median class — first class with cf ≥ 25 → **20–30**.

**Step 3:** Apply formula.
- L (lower boundary) = 20
- F (cf of previous class) = 17
- f (freq of median class) = 18
- h (class width) = 10

$$Median = 20 + \left(\frac{25 - 17}{18}\right) \times 10 = 20 + \frac{8}{18} \times 10 = 20 + 4.44 = 24.44$$

> **Interpretation:** 50% of observations lie below 24.44.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Five-Number Summary & Box Plot

Using the data: 12, 15, 14, 18, 12, 20, 16
Sorted: 12, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18, 20

- **Min** = 12
- **Q1** = 25th percentile ≈ 13
- **Median (Q2)** = 15
- **Q3** = 75th percentile ≈ 17
- **Max** = 20
- **IQR** = 17 − 13 = 4
- **Lower fence** = Q1 − 1.5·IQR = 13 − 6 = 7
- **Upper fence** = Q3 + 1.5·IQR = 17 + 6 = 23
- **No outliers** (all values within fences).

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Descriptive Analytics Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Business Data] --> B[Data Collection and Cleaning]
    B --> C[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    C --> D1[Measures of Central Tendency]
    C --> D2[Measures of Dispersion]
    C --> D3[Measures of Shape]
    C --> D4[Data Visualization]
    D1 --> E[Statistical Summary Report]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Insight Communication to Stakeholders]
    F --> G[Decision Making Foundation]
```

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Descriptive Statistics

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Descriptive Statistics] --> S1[Measures of Central Tendency]
    R --> S2[Measures of Dispersion]
    R --> S3[Measures of Shape]
    R --> S4[Visualization Tools]
    S1 --> T1[Mean]
    S1 --> T2[Median]
    S1 --> T3[Mode]
    S2 --> U1[Range]
    S2 --> U2[Quartiles and IQR]
    S2 --> U3[Variance]
    S2 --> U4[Standard Deviation]
    S2 --> U5[Coefficient of Variation]
    S3 --> V1[Skewness]
    S3 --> V2[Kurtosis]
    S4 --> W1[Histogram]
    S4 --> W2[Box Plot]
    S4 --> W3[Bar Chart]
    S4 --> W4[Pie Chart]
```

### 4.3 Box Plot Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Minimum] --> B[Lower Fence Q1 minus 1.5 IQR]
    B --> C[Q1 25th Percentile]
    C --> D[Median Q2 50th Percentile]
    D --> E[Q3 75th Percentile]
    E --> F[Upper Fence Q3 plus 1.5 IQR]
    F --> G[Maximum]
    H[Outlier] -.-> B
    I[Outlier] -.-> F
```

### 4.4 Skewness and Mean-Median-Mode Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Positive Right Skew] --> P1[Mean greater than Median greater than Mode]
    P1 --> P2[Tail extends toward higher values]
    N[Negative Left Skew] --> N1[Mean less than Median less than Mode]
    N1 --> N2[Tail extends toward lower values]
    SYM[Symmetric] --> SYM1[Mean equals Median equals Mode]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Descriptive Analytics. Differentiate it from Inferential Analytics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Descriptive Analytics refers to statistical techniques used to summarize, organize, and visualize historical data to understand *what has happened* in a business process. It employs measures of central tendency, dispersion, and graphical tools.

**Inferential Analytics (2 marks):** While descriptive analytics works only on the given dataset, inferential analytics uses sample data to make generalizations or predictions about a larger population using probability theory and hypothesis testing.

| Aspect | Descriptive | Inferential |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Summarize | Generalize / Predict |
| Data Used | Complete or sample | Sample only |
| Output | Tables, charts, statistics | Confidence intervals, p-values |

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the significance of the Coefficient of Variation (CV) with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Coefficient of Variation (CV) is a **standardized, unit-free measure** of dispersion (1 mark).

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$$

**Significance (1 mark):** It allows direct comparison of variability between two datasets that have different units or vastly different means.

**Example (1 mark):** Suppose the mean monthly revenue of Branch A is ₹1,00,000 with σ = ₹10,000 (CV = 10%), and Branch B has mean ₹5,00,000 with σ = ₹60,000 (CV = 12%). Although Branch B has higher absolute variability, Branch A is *relatively* more volatile. CV enables this fair comparison.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a) The monthly salaries (in ₹ thousands) of 10 employees in a startup are given below. Compute the mean, median, mode, range, variance, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation. Comment on the skewness of the distribution.** **(7 marks)**

**Data:** 22, 25, 28, 30, 32, 32, 35, 40, 45, 50

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Mean (1 mark)**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{22+25+28+30+32+32+35+40+45+50}{10} = \frac{339}{10} = 33.9$$

**Step 2: Median (1 mark)**
Sorted data (n=10, even): 22, 25, 28, 30, **32, 32**, 35, 40, 45, 50
$$Median = \frac{32 + 32}{2} = 32$$

**Step 3: Mode (1 mark)**
32 appears twice → **Mode = 32**

**Step 4: Range (0.5 mark)**
$$Range = 50 - 22 = 28$$

**Step 5: Variance and Standard Deviation (2.5 marks)**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 22 | -11.9 | 141.61 |
| 25 | -8.9 | 79.21 |
| 28 | -5.9 | 34.81 |
| 30 | -3.9 | 15.21 |
| 32 | -1.9 | 3.61 |
| 32 | -1.9 | 3.61 |
| 35 | 1.1 | 1.21 |
| 40 | 6.1 | 37.21 |
| 45 | 11.1 | 123.21 |
| 50 | 16.1 | 259.21 |
| **Sum** | | **699.00** |

$$s^2 = \frac{699.00}{10-1} = \frac{699}{9} = 77.67$$
$$s = \sqrt{77.67} \approx 8.81$$

**Step 6: CV (0.5 mark)**
$$CV = \frac{8.81}{33.9} \times 100\% \approx 25.99\%$$

**Step 7: Skewness Comment (0.5 mark)**
Pearson skewness = $\frac{3(33.9 - 32)}{8.81} = \frac{5.7}{8.81} \approx 0.647$

Since Mean (33.9) > Median (32) = Mode (32), the distribution is **positively skewed (right-skewed)** with skewness ≈ +0.65 (moderate right skew).

---

**(b) Define a Box Plot. For the following dataset, draw the box plot and identify any outliers. (7 marks)**

**Data:** 4, 7, 8, 12, 15, 15, 18, 22, 24, 30, 65

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Sort (0.5 mark):** 4, 7, 8, 12, 15, 15, 18, 22, 24, 30, 65 (n=11)

**Step 2: Five-Number Summary (2 marks)**
- Minimum = 4
- Q1 (25th percentile) = position $\frac{11+1}{4} = 3$rd value = **8**
- Median (Q2) = 6th value = **15**
- Q3 (75th percentile) = position $\frac{3(11+1)}{4} = 9$th value = **24**
- Maximum = 65

**Step 3: IQR (1 mark)**
$$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 24 - 8 = 16$$

**Step 4: Fences (1 mark)**
- Lower fence = $Q_1 - 1.5 \times IQR = 8 - 24 = -16$
- Upper fence = $Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR = 24 + 24 = 48$

**Step 5: Outlier Detection (1 mark)**
- 65 > 48 → **65 is an outlier** (upper outlier).
- 4 is within −16 to 48, so not an outlier.

**Step 6: Box Plot Description (1 mark)**
> A horizontal box plot extends from Q1 (8) to Q3 (24), with a vertical line at Median (15). Whiskers stretch to Min (4) and Max of *non-outlier* data (30). The point 65 is plotted individually as a dot beyond the upper whisker.

```
      ┌──[ 8 ──|──── 15 ────|── 24 ]──┐                  * 65
   4                                  30
   ←———|————|————|————|————|————|————|————|————|————|————|————→
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) The following table shows the marks obtained by 50 students in a class test. Compute the mean, median, and mode using the grouped data formulas. (7 marks)**

| Marks (Class) | Number of Students (f) |
|---|---|
| 0 – 10 | 4 |
| 10 – 20 | 8 |
| 20 – 30 | 12 |
| 30 – 40 | 15 |
| 40 – 50 | 7 |
| 50 – 60 | 4 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Construct frequency table (1 mark)**

| Class | f | Midpoint $x_i$ | $f_i x_i$ | cf |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0–10 | 4 | 5 | 20 | 4 |
| 10–20 | 8 | 15 | 120 | 12 |
| 20–30 | 12 | 25 | 300 | 24 |
| 30–40 | 15 | 35 | 525 | 39 |
| 40–50 | 7 | 45 | 315 | 46 |
| 50–60 | 4 | 55 | 220 | 50 |
| **Total** | **50** | | **1500** | |

**Step 2: Mean (1.5 marks)**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{1500}{50} = 30$$

**Step 3: Median (2.5 marks)**
- N/2 = 25
- Median class = 30–40 (since cf 39 ≥ 25)
- L = 30, F = 24, f = 15, h = 10

$$Median = 30 + \left(\frac{25 - 24}{15}\right) \times 10 = 30 + \frac{10}{15} = 30 + 0.67 = 30.67$$

**Step 4: Mode (2 marks)**
- Modal class = 30–40 (highest frequency = 15)
- f1 = 15, f0 = 12, f2 = 7, L = 30, h = 10

$$Mode = 30 + \left(\frac{15 - 12}{2(15) - 12 - 7}\right) \times 10 = 30 + \frac{3}{11} \times 10 = 30 + 2.73 = 32.73$$

**Verification:** Mean (30) < Median (30.67) < Mode (32.73) → **Negatively (left) skewed distribution**.

---

**(b) What is Skewness? Explain the three types of skewness with neat diagrams. Compute Pearson's coefficient of skewness for the data: Mean = 50, Median = 47, Standard Deviation = 12. (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
> Skewness is a measure of the **asymmetry** of a probability distribution about its mean. It indicates whether the data is concentrated on one side of the mean with a long tail on the other.

**Step 2: Three Types (3 marks)**

1. **Positive (Right) Skewness:** Tail extends to the right. Mean > Median > Mode. (e.g., Income distribution)
2. **Negative (Left) Skewness:** Tail extends to the left. Mean < Median < Mode. (e.g., Age at retirement)
3. **Zero Skewness (Symmetric):** Mean = Median = Mode. (e.g., Normal distribution)

**ASCII Sketches:**

```
POSITIVE SKEW          SYMMETRIC           NEGATIVE SKEW
                                                      
       ▄▄                              ▄▄▄▄▄              
      ███▄▄                         ▄▄██████▄▄            
   ▄▄██████▄▄                    ▄▄██████████▄▄          
▄████████████▄▄              ▄▄██████████████▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄    
←————|————|————|→    ←————|————|————|→    ←————|————|————|→
```

**Step 3: Pearson's Coefficient Calculation (3 marks)**

Pearson's first coefficient of skewness:
$$Sk = \frac{3(\bar{x} - Median)}{s}$$

Substituting values:
$$Sk = \frac{3(50 - 47)}{12} = \frac{3 \times 3}{12} = \frac{9}{12} = 0.75$$

**Interpretation:** Since Sk = +0.75 > 0, the distribution is **positively (right) skewed**, indicating that most values cluster on the lower end with a few high-value outliers stretching the right tail.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's correction (n−1):** Using `n` instead of `n−1` in sample variance is the **#1 mark-deduction reason** in KTU exams. Always state "sample variance" explicitly when dividing by `n−1`.
> 2. **Confusing Population σ vs. Sample s:** Examiners specifically check which symbol you use. Mis-match loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Median class identification:** In grouped data, students often pick the wrong class. Always re-confirm that `cf ≥ N/2` for the *first* time.
> 4. **Units of CV:** CV is **dimensionless** but is conventionally expressed in **%**. Writing CV = 0.25 instead of 25% costs a mark.
> 5. **Outliers via IQR:** Using *mean ± 3σ* is the empirical rule; using *Q1 − 1.5·IQR* is the **box plot rule**. Examiners differentiate.
> 6. **Skewness sign:** Positive skew = right tail, NOT "skewed positively" in value. Interpret direction, not sign of value.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Descriptive Analytics** = Summarize *what happened* (no prediction).
- **Mean** uses every value; **Median** is robust; **Mode** is for nominal data.
- **Sample variance** ALWAYS uses `n−1` (Bessel's correction) for unbiased estimation.
- **CV** = $\frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ — used to compare variability across datasets with different units.
- **IQR** = $Q_3 - Q_1$; outliers lie outside $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$.
- **Skewness direction:** Positive = right tail (Mean > Median > Mode); Negative = left tail.
- **Pearson Skewness** = $\frac{3(\bar{x} - Median)}{s}$.
- **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)** applies ONLY to **normal / bell-shaped** distributions.
- **Z-score** standardizes values: $Z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{s}$.
- **Grouped formulas** for Median and Mode require: L (lower boundary), f (modal class freq), F (cf of prev class), h (class width).
- **Visualization choice:** Histogram (continuous), Bar chart (categorical), Box plot (5-number summary + outliers), Pie chart (proportions).
- **Box plot components:** Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max + fences at $Q \pm 1.5 \cdot IQR$.
- **Mean–Median–Mode empirical relationship:** $Mode \approx 3 \cdot Median - 2 \cdot Mean$ (Pearson's empirical mode formula).
- **In KTU 2024 ESE:** Always show full working for 7-mark sub-parts; partial derivations attract zero marks.
- **Mention "sample" or "population"** explicitly in every variance/SD calculation.
- **Units:** Variance is in **squared units**; SD has **same units as data**; CV is in **percent**.

> **End of Module 1 — Descriptive Analytics Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Understanding Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Risk

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Business Analytics** and financial decision-making, **Risk** is formally defined as the *quantifiable uncertainty* concerning the deviation of actual outcomes from expected outcomes. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PECBT755 – Business Analytics*, risk is treated as a measurable probabilistic construct, not merely a vague "chance of loss."

Mathematically, risk is the dispersion of possible returns around the **Expected Value (Mean)** of those returns. A wider dispersion implies a higher risk, while a tighter clustering around the mean implies lower risk.

$$ \text{Risk} = f(\sigma, \beta, \text{VaR}) $$

where:
- $\sigma$ = Standard Deviation of returns (Total Risk)
- $\beta$ = Beta coefficient (Systematic / Market Risk)
- VaR = Value at Risk (Maximum expected loss at a given confidence level)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Risk in Business Analytics is **not** equivalent to "danger" or "loss". It is the **statistical variability** of outcomes — both upside and downside. An investment with high risk simply has a wider range of possible results.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Driving in Rain" Analogy

Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Thiruvananthapuram.

- On a **bright sunny day**, you are almost *certain* you will reach in 4 hours. The outcomes cluster tightly around 4 hours → **Low Risk**.
- On a **heavy rainy day**, you *might* reach in 4 hours, or 5, or even 6. The outcomes spread out widely → **High Risk**.

**Your "expected" time is the same (4 hrs average), but the risk — the unpredictability — is very different.** This is exactly how risk works in business: two investments can have the **same expected return**, but one is far riskier because its actual outcomes are scattered all over the place.

## 1.3 Why "Understanding Risk" Matters in Business Analytics

| Perspective | Why Risk Matters |
|-------------|------------------|
| **Manager** | Helps in capital budgeting, project selection, and resource allocation |
| **Investor** | Enables portfolio diversification to optimize the risk-return tradeoff |
| **Analyst** | Provides the mathematical foundation for predictive models and Monte Carlo simulations |
| **Auditor** | Identifies exposure to operational, financial, and market threats |

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of Risk Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU question on this topic revolves around two pillars:
> 1. **Quantification** — How do we *measure* risk? (Standard Deviation, Variance, Beta, VaR)
> 2. **Classification** — What *type* of risk are we dealing with? (Systematic vs. Unsystematic)

The remainder of these notes is structured to make you exam-ready on **both** pillars.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Building Blocks of Risk

### Block 1: Expected Return (E[R])
The probability-weighted average of all possible returns.

$$ E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot R_i $$

- $P_i$ = Probability of outcome $i$
- $R_i$ = Return in outcome $i$

### Block 2: Variance (σ²)
The probability-weighted average of *squared deviations* from the expected return.

$$ \sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \bigl(R_i - E(R)\bigr)^2 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why squared?** Squaring eliminates the sign of deviations (so a +5% and a -5% deviation both contribute positively) and penalizes large deviations more heavily than small ones. This makes variance a true "penalty" measure.

### Block 3: Standard Deviation (σ)
The square root of variance, expressed in the *same units* as returns (e.g., %).

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \bigl(R_i - E(R)\bigr)^2 } $$

### Block 4: Coefficient of Variation (CV)
A *normalized* risk metric that allows comparison between investments with different expected returns.

$$ CV = \frac{\sigma}{E(R)} $$

### Block 5: Beta (β) — The Sensitivity Meter
Beta measures how much an asset's return moves *relative to* the overall market.

$$ \beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{\sigma_m^2} = \frac{\rho_{i,m} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_m^2} $$

| Beta Value | Interpretation |
|------------|----------------|
| $\beta = 0$ | Asset is uncorrelated with market (e.g., gold, T-bills) |
| $\beta = 1$ | Asset moves exactly with the market |
| $\beta > 1$ | Asset is *more volatile* than the market (aggressive) |
| $0 < \beta < 1$ | Asset is *less volatile* than the market (defensive) |
| $\beta < 0$ | Asset moves *opposite* to the market (hedge) |

## 2.2 Types of Risk — The KTU Classification Tree

### A. Systematic Risk (Market Risk / Non-Diversifiable)
- Affects the **entire market** or economy
- Cannot be eliminated through diversification
- Examples: interest rate changes, inflation, recession, war, pandemics
- Measured by **Beta (β)**

### B. Unsystematic Risk (Idiosyncratic / Diversifiable)
- Specific to a **single company or industry**
- **Can be eliminated** by holding a well-diversified portfolio
- Examples: management failure, product recall, labor strike, supply chain disruption
- Measured by the **diversifiable portion of σ²**

### C. Total Risk Decomposition (Critical Formula)

$$ \underbrace{\sigma_{\text{total}}^2}_{\text{Total Risk}} = \underbrace{\sigma_{\text{systematic}}^2}_{\text{Market Risk}} + \underbrace{\sigma_{\text{unsystematic}}^2}_{\text{Idiosyncratic Risk}} $$

### D. Other Categorizations Tested in KTU

| Risk Type | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Business Risk** | Variability in operating income (EBIT) due to sales/cost fluctuations | A textile firm facing raw material price swings |
| **Financial Risk** | Risk from use of debt financing; magnifies shareholder risk | A leveraged firm with high Debt-Equity ratio |
| **Liquidity Risk** | Inability to buy/sell asset quickly without price impact | Selling a rare painting in 24 hours |
| **Credit Risk** | Counterparty fails to meet obligations | A bond issuer defaulting on interest |
| **Operational Risk** | Failures in internal processes, people, or systems | IT server crash, employee fraud |
| **Currency Risk** | Losses from exchange rate fluctuations | An Indian exporter earning in USD |

## 2.3 Value at Risk (VaR) — Modern Risk Metric

VaR answers the question: *"What is the maximum loss my portfolio can suffer over a time horizon at a given confidence level?"*

$$ VaR_{\alpha} = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{T} $$

- $Z_{\alpha}$ = Z-score at confidence level $\alpha$ (e.g., 1.65 for 95%, 2.33 for 99%)
- $\sigma$ = Volatility (std. dev.) of portfolio returns
- $V$ = Portfolio value
- $T$ = Time horizon

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Print & Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is your **exam lifeline**. The `|` symbol is rendered as `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Expected Return | $E(R) = \sum P_i R_i$ | % | Baseline forecast |
| 2 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \sum P_i (R_i - E(R))^2$ | $(\%)^2$ | Total absolute risk |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | % | Practical risk measure |
| 4 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \sigma \div E(R)$ | Ratio | Compare unlike assets |
| 5 | Covariance | $Cov(R_i,R_j) = \sum P_k(R_{ik}-E(R_i))(R_{jk}-E(R_j))$ | $(\%)^2$ | Co-movement |
| 6 | Correlation | $\rho_{i,j} = Cov(R_i,R_j) \div (\sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j)$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Strength of relation |
| 7 | Beta | $\beta_i = Cov(R_i,R_m) \div \sigma_m^2$ | Number | Systematic risk |
| 8 | CAPM | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - R_f)$ | % | Required return |
| 9 | Value at Risk | $VaR = Z_\alpha \cdot \sigma \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{T}$ | Currency | Worst-case loss |
| 10 | Sharpe Ratio | $S = (R_p - R_f) \div \sigma_p$ | Ratio | Risk-adjusted return |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In modern **FinTech** and **data analytics**, these formulas power:
- **Algorithmic trading platforms** (using real-time β to balance portfolios)
- **Banking stress tests** (using VaR to ensure capital adequacy under Basel III norms)
- **Insurance underwriting** (using σ² to price premiums accurately)
- **Supply chain analytics** (using CV to select reliable suppliers)
- **Credit scoring models** (using PD, LGD, and EAD — direct extensions of risk theory)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sharpe Ratio** deserves special attention — it is widely tested in KTU as it elegantly combines risk *and* return in a single number. A higher Sharpe = better risk-adjusted performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Standard Deviation of a Two-Asset Portfolio

This is a **high-frequency KTU question**. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Setup:** We have two assets A and B with:
- $E(R_A)$, $E(R_B)$ = expected returns
- $\sigma_A$, $\sigma_B$ = individual standard deviations
- $w_A$, $w_B$ = portfolio weights (where $w_A + w_B = 1$)
- $\rho_{AB}$ = correlation coefficient

### Step 1 — Write the Portfolio Return

$$ R_p = w_A \cdot R_A + w_B \cdot R_B $$

### Step 2 — Find Expected Portfolio Return

$$ E(R_p) = w_A \cdot E(R_A) + w_B \cdot E(R_B) $$

**Reasoning:** Expectation is a linear operator, so it distributes over the weighted sum.

### Step 3 — Express Variance of Portfolio

By definition:

$$ \sigma_p^2 = E\bigl[(R_p - E(R_p))^2\bigr] $$

### Step 4 — Substitute R_p

$$ \sigma_p^2 = E\bigl[(w_A R_A + w_B R_B - w_A E(R_A) - w_B E(R_B))^2\bigr] $$

### Step 5 — Group the Terms

$$ \sigma_p^2 = E\bigl[(w_A(R_A - E(R_A)) + w_B(R_B - E(R_B)))^2\bigr] $$

Let us denote deviations as $\tilde{R}_A = R_A - E(R_A)$ and $\tilde{R}_B = R_B - E(R_B)$:

$$ \sigma_p^2 = E\bigl[(w_A \tilde{R}_A + w_B \tilde{R}_B)^2\bigr] $$

### Step 6 — Expand the Square

$$ \sigma_p^2 = E\bigl[w_A^2 \tilde{R}_A^2 + 2 w_A w_B \tilde{R}_A \tilde{R}_B + w_B^2 \tilde{R}_B^2\bigr] $$

### Step 7 — Apply Linearity of Expectation

$$ \sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 E(\tilde{R}_A^2) + 2 w_A w_B E(\tilde{R}_A \tilde{R}_B) + w_B^2 E(\tilde{R}_B^2) $$

### Step 8 — Identify Each Term

- $E(\tilde{R}_A^2) = \sigma_A^2$ (by definition of variance)
- $E(\tilde{R}_B^2) = \sigma_B^2$ (by definition of variance)
- $E(\tilde{R}_A \tilde{R}_B) = Cov(R_A, R_B)$

### Step 9 — Final Two-Asset Portfolio Variance Formula

$$ \boxed{\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \, Cov(R_A, R_B)} $$

Using $Cov(R_A, R_B) = \rho_{AB} \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B$:

$$ \sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** When $\rho_{AB} = 0$ (uncorrelated assets), the cross-term vanishes, and portfolio risk is *always less* than the weighted average of individual risks. This is the mathematical proof of **diversification benefit**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Beta from Covariance

Beta is the slope of the regression line of asset returns on market returns.

**Setup:** We perform an OLS regression of $R_i$ on $R_m$:

$$ R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \epsilon_i $$

The OLS slope estimator is:

$$ \beta_i = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)} = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{\sigma_m^2} $$

### Step 1 — Expand Covariance

$$ Cov(R_i, R_m) = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{i,t} - \bar{R}_i)(R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m) $$

### Step 2 — Expand Variance of Market

$$ \sigma_m^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m)^2 $$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$ \beta_i = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{i,t} - \bar{R}_i)(R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m)}{\sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m)^2} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form KTU expects you to *recognize and apply* in numerical problems. The algebraic expansion of β in terms of correlation is:
> $$ \beta_i = \rho_{i,m} \cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_m} $$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

CAPM is the **required return** for an asset given its systematic risk. It is derived from the assumption that all investors hold the market portfolio.

**Starting Point:** The Security Market Line (SML) is a linear relationship between $\beta$ and required return:

$$ E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot \bigl[E(R_m) - R_f\bigr] $$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate
- $E(R_m) - R_f$ = Market Risk Premium
- $\beta_i$ = Sensitivity of asset to market

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. If $\beta_i = 0$ → $E(R_i) = R_f$ (no risk, no extra return)
2. If $\beta_i = 1$ → $E(R_i) = E(R_m)$ (market-level risk, market-level return)
3. If $\beta_i > 1$ → $E(R_i) > E(R_m)$ (compensated for higher systematic risk)
4. If $\beta_i < 1$ → $E(R_i) < E(R_m)$ (rewarded less, but safer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAPM is the bridge between Risk and Required Return.** Every valuation in corporate finance (DCF, WACC) eventually traces back to this equation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A portfolio has the following probability distribution:

| Outcome | Probability $P_i$ | Return $R_i$ (%) |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Boom    | 0.20             | +25              |
| Normal  | 0.50             | +10              |
| Slump   | 0.30             | -15              |

**Find:** (a) Expected Return, (b) Variance, (c) Standard Deviation, (d) Coefficient of Variation.

### Solution:

**Step (a) — Expected Return:**

$$ E(R) = \sum P_i R_i = (0.20)(25) + (0.50)(10) + (0.30)(-15) $$

$$ E(R) = 5.0 + 5.0 - 4.5 = 5.5\% $$

**Step (b) — Variance:**

$$ \sigma^2 = \sum P_i (R_i - E(R))^2 $$

Compute each squared deviation:
- Boom: $(25 - 5.5)^2 = (19.5)^2 = 380.25$
- Normal: $(10 - 5.5)^2 = (4.5)^2 = 20.25$
- Slump: $(-15 - 5.5)^2 = (-20.5)^2 = 420.25$

Now weight by probabilities:

$$ \sigma^2 = (0.20)(380.25) + (0.50)(20.25) + (0.30)(420.25) $$

$$ \sigma^2 = 76.05 + 10.125 + 126.075 = 212.25 \,(\%)^2 $$

**Step (c) — Standard Deviation:**

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{212.25} \approx 14.57\% $$

**Step (d) — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$ CV = \frac{\sigma}{E(R)} = \frac{14.57}{5.5} \approx 2.65 $$

**Interpretation:** A CV of 2.65 means that the risk per unit of return is very high — investors would demand a much higher return for bearing this level of dispersion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Total Risk Decomposition — Mermaid Flow

The diagram below maps how total risk in a portfolio breaks down into systematic and unsystematic components, and how diversification affects each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Portfolio Risk sigma squared total] --> B[Systematic Risk sigma squared M]
    A --> C[Unsystematic Risk sigma squared E]
    B --> D[Measured by Beta coefficient]
    B --> E[Cannot be diversified away]
    B --> F[Examples: Recession Inflation War]
    C --> G[Measured by Residual Variance]
    C --> H[CAN be diversified away]
    C --> I[Examples: Management Failure Product Recall Strike]
    J[Add more assets to portfolio] --> K[Unsystematic Risk DECREASES sharply]
    J --> L[Systematic Risk stays CONSTANT]
    M[Number of Assets in Portfolio] --> N[Asymptote at Systematic Floor]
```

## 4.2 Risk Classification Tree — Comprehensive Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[RISK in Business Analytics] --> R1[Systematic Risk]
    R --> R2[Unsystematic Risk]
    R --> R3[Other Categorizations]
    R1 --> R1A[Market Risk]
    R1 --> R1B[Interest Rate Risk]
    R1 --> R1C[Inflation Risk]
    R1 --> R1D[Currency Risk]
    R2 --> R2A[Business Risk]
    R2 --> R2B[Financial Risk]
    R2 --> R2C[Operational Risk]
    R2 --> R2D[Liquidity Risk]
    R3 --> R3A[Credit Risk]
    R3 --> R3B[Country Risk]
    R3 --> R3C[Legal and Regulatory Risk]
    R1A --> M1[Measure: Beta]
    R2A --> M2[Measure: Operating Leverage]
    R2B --> M3[Measure: D E Ratio]
```

## 4.3 The Risk-Return Tradeoff — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Risk Asset T Bills] --> A1[Low Return 3 to 5 percent]
    B[Medium Risk Asset Large Cap Stocks] --> B1[Medium Return 8 to 12 percent]
    C[High Risk Asset Crypto Startup] --> C1[High Return 15 to 50 percent plus]
    D[Investor Preference] --> D1{Risk Tolerance}
    D1 --> D2[Conservative: Prefers A]
    D1 --> D3[Balanced: Prefers B]
    D1 --> D4[Aggressive: Prefers C]
    E[Efficient Frontier] --> E1[Maximum return for given risk]
```

## 4.4 Risk Management Process — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Risks] --> S2[Step 2: Measure and Quantify]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Evaluate and Prioritize]
    S3 --> S4{Step 4: Choose Response}
    S4 --> S5[Avoid: Exit the activity]
    S4 --> S6[Reduce: Controls and safeguards]
    S4 --> S7[Transfer: Insurance or hedging]
    S4 --> S8[Accept: Self insure for small risks]
    S5 --> S9[Step 5: Monitor and Review]
    S6 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Continuous feedback loop to Step 1]
```

## 4.5 Key Takeaway from the Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above visually summarize three truths examiners test repeatedly:
> 1. **Diversification kills unsystematic risk** but cannot touch systematic risk.
> 2. **Risk and return move together** — you cannot escape this tradeoff in efficient markets.
> 3. **Risk management is a continuous cycle**, not a one-time event.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Risk" in the context of Business Analytics. Differentiate between systematic and unsystematic risk with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Risk refers to the **uncertainty of outcomes** and the potential for deviation of actual returns from the expected return. It is quantified using statistical measures such as standard deviation ($\sigma$), variance ($\sigma^2$), and beta ($\beta$).

- **Systematic Risk** (1 mark): This is market-wide risk that affects all firms simultaneously, such as interest rate changes, inflation, or recession. It cannot be eliminated through diversification and is measured by beta.
- **Unsystematic Risk** (1 mark): This is firm-specific risk arising from management decisions, product failures, or labor issues. It can be reduced or eliminated by holding a diversified portfolio.
- **Example** (1 mark): A fall in repo rates by the RBI is systematic risk; the sudden resignation of a company's CEO is unsystematic risk.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of Beta as a measure of risk. What does a beta of 1.5 and a beta of 0.5 indicate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Beta ($\beta$) is a measure of **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk** that captures the sensitivity of an asset's returns to movements in the overall market (1 mark).

- **Beta = 1.5** (1 mark): The asset is 50% *more volatile* than the market. If the market moves up by 10%, this asset is expected to move up by 15% (and similarly for downturns). It is an aggressive/cyclical stock.
- **Beta = 0.5** (1 mark): The asset is 50% *less volatile* than the market. If the market moves by 10%, this asset moves by only 5%. It is a defensive stock (e.g., FMCG, utilities).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions in Module 2 are typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks (theory/derivation)** and **Part (b) = 7 marks (numerical application)**.

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Discuss in detail the various types of risks faced by a business organization. Explain how risk is measured using standard deviation and beta.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A stock has the following returns over four quarters: +10%, -5%, +15%, -10%. The risk-free rate is 6% and the expected market return is 14%. The stock's beta is 1.2. Calculate: (i) Expected Return, (ii) Standard Deviation, (iii) Required Return using CAPM, (iv) Comment on whether the stock is overvalued or undervalued.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Types of Risk (5 marks):**

| # | Risk Type | Description | Example |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Business Risk** | Variability in operating income | Sales decline in monsoon |
| 2 | **Financial Risk** | Risk magnified by debt | High D/E ratio in IT firm |
| 3 | **Market Risk** | Price movements due to market forces | Nifty crash |
| 4 | **Credit Risk** | Default by borrower | Bond issuer insolvency |
| 5 | **Liquidity Risk** | Inability to convert to cash | Real estate holdings |
| 6 | **Operational Risk** | Internal process failures | Cyber attack on bank |
| 7 | **Currency Risk** | FX fluctuations | INR depreciation impact |
| 8 | **Inflation Risk** | Erosion of purchasing power | Rising CPI |

**Measurement of Risk (2 marks):**
- *Standard Deviation* captures **total risk** — both systematic and unsystematic. It is calculated as $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum P_i (R_i - E(R))^2}$.
- *Beta* captures only **systematic risk** — calculated as $\beta = Cov(R_i, R_m) / \sigma_m^2$. It indicates the asset's volatility relative to the market.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step (i) — Expected Return (E[R]):**

$$ E(R) = \frac{10 + (-5) + 15 + (-10)}{4} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5\% $$

**Step (ii) — Standard Deviation (σ):**

Compute deviations from the mean (2.5%):
- Quarter 1: $10 - 2.5 = +7.5$
- Quarter 2: $-5 - 2.5 = -7.5$
- Quarter 3: $15 - 2.5 = +12.5$
- Quarter 4: $-10 - 2.5 = -12.5$

Using the population formula (since outcomes are equally weighted, treat as a discrete distribution):

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{(7.5)^2 + (-7.5)^2 + (12.5)^2 + (-12.5)^2}{4}} $$

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{56.25 + 56.25 + 156.25 + 156.25}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{425}{4}} = \sqrt{106.25} $$

$$ \sigma \approx 10.31\% $$

*(Note: If KTU specifies a probability distribution, use the weighted formula. If not, the simple average of squared deviations is accepted.)*

**Step (iii) — Required Return using CAPM:**

$$ E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot (E(R_m) - R_f) $$

$$ E(R_i) = 6\% + 1.2 \cdot (14\% - 6\%) = 6\% + 1.2 \cdot 8\% = 6\% + 9.6\% = 15.6\% $$

**Step (iv) — Comment:**

- Expected return (2.5%) **<** Required return (15.6%)
- The stock is **not generating enough return** to compensate for its systematic risk.
- **Conclusion:** The stock is **overvalued** at current levels. An investor should avoid or short this stock until its price corrects downward, or seek alternatives with similar beta but higher returns.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). Derive the Security Market Line (SML) and discuss its significance in business decision-making.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A portfolio consists of two stocks A and B with the following data:

| Parameter | Stock A | Stock B |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Expected Return | 18% | 12% |
| Standard Deviation | 20% | 15% |
| Weight in Portfolio | 60% | 40% |
| Correlation Coefficient | 0.30 | — |

Calculate the expected return and standard deviation of the portfolio. Comment on the diversification benefit.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**CAPM Explanation (3 marks):**

The Capital Asset Pricing Model, developed by **Sharpe, Lintner, and Mossin (1960s)**, describes the relationship between systematic risk and the expected return of an asset. The model states that the expected return of an asset is equal to the risk-free rate plus a risk premium that is proportional to the asset's beta.

**SML Derivation (3 marks):**

The SML is derived by plotting the CAPM equation for all possible assets:

$$ E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot (E(R_m) - R_f) $$

This is the equation of a straight line:
- **Intercept** = $R_f$ (on the y-axis)
- **Slope** = $E(R_m) - R_f$ (the market risk premium)
- **Independent variable** = $\beta_i$

A high-beta stock lies above and to the right; a low-beta stock lies below and to the left.

**Significance (1 mark):**
- Helps determine the **required rate of return** for capital budgeting
- Used in calculating **WACC** (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)
- Provides a benchmark to **evaluate portfolio performance**
- Helps in **security valuation** (if actual return < required, the asset is overpriced)

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Expected Portfolio Return:**

$$ E(R_p) = w_A \cdot E(R_A) + w_B \cdot E(R_B) $$

$$ E(R_p) = (0.60)(18\%) + (0.40)(12\%) = 10.8\% + 4.8\% = 15.6\% $$

**Step 2 — Portfolio Variance:**

$$ \sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B $$

Compute each component:
- $w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 = (0.60)^2 (20)^2 = 0.36 \times 400 = 144$
- $w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 = (0.40)^2 (15)^2 = 0.16 \times 225 = 36$
- $2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B = 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(20)(15)$
- $= 2 \times 0.60 \times 0.40 \times 0.30 \times 300$
- $= 2 \times 21.6 = 43.2$

Adding them up:

$$ \sigma_p^2 = 144 + 36 + 43.2 = 223.2 \,(\%)^2 $$

**Step 3 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$ \sigma_p = \sqrt{223.2} \approx 14.94\% $$

**Step 4 — Diversification Benefit Comment:**

- **Weighted average of individual risks**: $(0.60)(20) + (0.40)(15) = 12 + 6 = 18\%$
- **Actual portfolio risk**: $\sigma_p \approx 14.94\%$
- The portfolio risk is **lower** than the weighted average of individual risks by $18 - 14.94 = 3.06\%$.
- This reduction is due to **low/positive correlation** ($\rho = 0.30$) between the two stocks.
- **Conclusion:** The portfolio demonstrates a clear diversification benefit. The lower the correlation between assets, the greater the risk-reduction effect.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take the square root in σ** — Students compute variance but forget the final step. Variance is in squared units and is almost never the final answer. *Always convert to standard deviation.*
>
> 2. **Confusing "Risk-Free Rate" with "Return on T-Bills"** — In India, KTU often accepts the 10-year G-Sec yield as $R_f$. State your assumption clearly.
>
> 3. **Misapplying weights in two-asset portfolios** — If weights don't sum to 1 (e.g., 60% and 40%), something is wrong. Always check $w_A + w_B = 1$.
>
> 4. **Confusing Beta interpretation** — Beta = 1.5 does NOT mean 1.5% extra return. It means 1.5 times the *market's* volatility. Read the question carefully.
>
> 5. **Ignoring units in CV** — Coefficient of Variation is a pure ratio (no units), but standard deviation must be expressed in % (not decimal form) for consistency.
>
> 6. **In CAPM, using "expected market return" as $E(R_m)$** — Never confuse it with the variance of market return. CAPM needs the mean, not the dispersion.
>
> 7. **Not specifying the time period in VaR** — VaR without a time horizon is meaningless. Always state "1-day VaR" or "10-day VaR."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist as your **last-day revision sheet** before the KTU exam.

- [ ] **Risk** = quantifiable uncertainty; measured by $\sigma$ (total) and $\beta$ (systematic).
- [ ] **$E(R) = \sum P_i R_i$** is the probability-weighted mean of returns.
- [ ] **Variance** uses *squared* deviations — eliminates sign, penalizes outliers.
- [ ] **$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$** is in the same units as return (e.g., %).
- [ ] **CV = $\sigma / E(R)$** is used to compare assets with *different* expected returns.
- [ ] **Systematic Risk** = non-diversifiable; measured by **β**; affects the whole market.
- [ ] **Unsystematic Risk** = diversifiable; can be killed by holding 15–25 well-chosen stocks.
- [ ] **Total Risk** = $\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{systematic}^2 + \sigma_{unsystematic}^2$.
- [ ] **Beta = 1** → moves with market; **β > 1** → aggressive; **β < 1** → defensive; **β < 0** → hedge.
- [ ] **CAPM:** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - R_f)$. It is the SML equation.
- [ ] **Two-asset portfolio variance:** $w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$.
- [ ] **VaR** requires confidence level, volatility, portfolio value, *and* time horizon.
- [ ] **Sharpe Ratio** = $(R_p - R_f) / \sigma_p$ — higher is better.
- [ ] **Diversification benefit** arises from correlations **less than +1**.
- [ ] Always state assumptions (risk-free rate source, probability distribution type, time period).
- [ ] Numerical answers must include **units (%)** and be rounded to **2 decimal places**.
- [ ] The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to **derive** formulas in long-answer questions, not just state them.

---

*End of Module 2 — Understanding Risk Notes | PECBT755 Business Analytics | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Credit Risk Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Credit Risk Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Credit Risk Analysis** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative process of evaluating the likelihood that a borrower (or counterparty) will fail to meet its obligations in accordance with agreed-upon terms. In the context of Business Analytics, it refers to the deployment of statistical, econometric, machine learning, and optimization techniques to estimate, model, predict, and manage the probability and financial impact of default across individual borrowers and entire credit portfolios.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, credit risk is classified as one of the three principal pillars of financial risk, alongside **Market Risk** and **Operational Risk**, and is formally defined by the **Basel Accords (Basel II and Basel III)** framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Per the KTU PECBT755 Module 2 syllabus, students must be able to *define credit risk, distinguish its types, compute PD/LGD/EAD, apply credit scoring models, and interpret portfolio-level credit metrics such as Expected Loss (EL) and Value-at-Risk (VaR)*.

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Quantities of Credit Risk

Every credit risk model, regardless of complexity, rests on three pillars:

$$EL = PD \times LGD \times EAD$$

| Symbol | Full Form | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **PD** | Probability of Default | Likelihood that the borrower defaults within 1 year |
| **LGD** | Loss Given Default | Fraction of exposure lost *after* default (0% to 100%) |
| **EAD** | Exposure at Default | Outstanding balance at the moment of default |
| **EL** | Expected Loss | Average loss the lender should provision for |

> [!NOTE]
> **PD is dimensionless (probability, 0-1)**, **LGD is dimensionless (ratio, 0-1)**, and **EAD is in currency units (e.g., ₹, $, €)**. The resulting Expected Loss is therefore expressed in the same currency unit as EAD.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a neighborhood money-lender. Before lending ₹1,00,000 to your neighbor, you subconsciously ask:

1. *"How likely is this person to actually pay me back?"* → This is **PD**.
2. *"If they don't pay, how much of my ₹1,00,000 will I recover by selling their assets?"* → This is **LGD**.
3. *"At the exact moment they stop paying, how much do they still owe me?"* → This is **EAD**.

If you multiply these three quantities, you arrive at the **Expected Loss** — the amount you should "set aside" as a safety net. **Credit Risk Analysis** is simply the industrial, mathematical, and regulated version of this everyday decision-making process, scaled up to handle millions of borrowers simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Globally, banks provision hundreds of billions of dollars annually for credit losses. The 2008 Global Financial Crisis was, at its core, a massive mis-calculation of credit risk on mortgage-backed securities.

## 1.4 Classification of Credit Risk

Credit risk manifests in several distinct forms, each requiring a different analytical treatment:

- **Default Risk** — Borrower completely fails to pay.
- **Credit Spread Risk** — Deterioration in borrower's credit quality widens the yield spread.
- **Recovery Risk** — Uncertainty surrounding the *amount* that can be recouped post-default.
- **Settlement Risk** — Counterparty delivers value *after* the other party has already paid.
- **Concentration Risk** — Excessive exposure to a single borrower, sector, or geography.
- **Country Risk** — Sovereign or political events impacting cross-border lending.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of a Credit Risk Model

A production-grade credit risk analytical system follows a five-stage pipeline:

1. **Data Acquisition & Cleansing** — Gather borrower-level data: demographics, financials, transaction history, bureau scores.
2. **Feature Engineering** — Construct predictive variables such as *Debt-to-Income Ratio (DTI)*, *Current Ratio*, *Number of Delinquencies in last 24 months*.
3. **Model Estimation** — Fit statistical/ML algorithms to historical default data.
4. **Validation & Calibration** — Test on out-of-sample data; assess discriminatory power (AUC-ROC, Gini coefficient) and calibration (Brier Score, Hosmer-Lemeshow test).
5. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Score new applicants; continuously track Population Stability Index (PSI) and track model drift.

## 2.2 Statistical Foundations: From Logistic Regression to Z-Score

### 2.2.1 Logistic Regression (Industry-Workhorse)

The probability of default is modeled as a logistic transformation of a linear combination of borrower features:

$$PD(X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_n X_n)}}$$

$$PD(X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-Z}} \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i X_i$$

The **odds** of default are expressed as:

$$\text{Odds}(X) = \frac{PD(X)}{1 - PD(X)} = e^{Z}$$

Taking the natural logarithm yields the **log-odds (logit)**, which is linear in X:

$$\ln\left(\frac{PD(X)}{1 - PD(X)}\right) = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i X_i$$

### 2.2.2 Altman's Z-Score (Bankruptcy Prediction)

Developed by Edward Altman in 1968, this multivariate model combines five financial ratios:

$$Z = 1.2 X_1 + 1.4 X_2 + 3.3 X_3 + 0.6 X_4 + 1.0 X_5$$

Where:
- $X_1$ = Working Capital / Total Assets
- $X_2$ = Retained Earnings / Total Assets
- $X_3$ = EBIT / Total Assets
- $X_4$ = Market Value of Equity / Book Value of Total Liabilities
- $X_5$ = Sales / Total Assets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** $Z < 1.81 \Rightarrow$ "Distress Zone" (high default risk); $1.81 \le Z \le 2.99 \Rightarrow$ "Grey Zone"; $Z > 2.99 \Rightarrow$ "Safe Zone".

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Logistic regression** is preferred over linear regression because PD is bounded between 0 and 1.
- **Z-Score** is fast, interpretable, and requires only publicly available financial statements — making it ideal for SMEs.
- **Feature engineering** is critical because raw data rarely captures default behavior directly.

## 2.4 Portfolio-Level Credit Risk Metrics

### 2.4.1 Expected Loss (EL) — Portfolio Level

$$\boxed{EL_{portfolio} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} PD_i \times LGD_i \times EAD_i}$$

### 2.4.2 Unexpected Loss (UL) & Value-at-Risk

$$\boxed{UL = VaR_{\alpha} - EL}$$

where $VaR_{\alpha}$ is the loss at confidence level $\alpha$ (typically 99.9% under Basel). The distribution of portfolio losses is typically **skewed and fat-tailed**, often modeled using the **Vasicek single-factor model**:

$$PD_i = \Phi\left(\frac{\Phi^{-1}(p) + \sqrt{\rho} \cdot Y}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)$$

Where:
- $p$ = baseline (long-run average) PD
- $\rho$ = asset correlation
- $Y \sim N(0,1)$ = systematic factor
- $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF

### 2.4.3 Economic Capital

$$EC = UL \times \text{Multiplier (regulatory)}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $EL = PD \times LGD \times EAD$ | Expected Loss | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $PD(X) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-Z}}$ | Logistic PD | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $Z = 1.2X_1 + 1.4X_2 + 3.3X_3 + 0.6X_4 + 1.0X_5$ | Altman Z-Score | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $UL = VaR_{99.9\%} - EL$ | Unexpected Loss | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $PD_i = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\Phi^{-1}(p) + \sqrt{\rho}\, Y}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)$ | Vasicek Conditional PD | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $EC = f(UL)$ | Economic Capital | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $RWA = K \times 12.5 \times EAD$ | Risk-Weighted Assets | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $\text{Gini} = 2 \times AUC - 1$ | Model Discrimination | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse *PD* with *EL*. PD is a **probability (0-1)** while EL is a **currency value**. Do not interchange them in numerical problems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

Credit Risk Analytics is critical in:
- **Retail Banking** — Credit card approvals, mortgage underwriting.
- **FinTech & NBFCs** — Instant personal loans, BNPL (Buy-Now-Pay-Later).
- **Corporate Lending** — Syndicated loans, trade finance.
- **Insurance** — Reinsurance counterparty default modeling.
- **Supply Chain Analytics** — Evaluating supplier financial health.
- **RegTech** — Basel III/IV compliance, IFRS 9 provisioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Basic EL Computation

**Problem (KTU Typical 7-Mark Question):**
A bank has three corporate borrowers with the following profiles. Compute the *portfolio Expected Loss (EL)* and the *Unexpected Loss (UL)* given that the portfolio VaR at 99.9% confidence is ₹8,50,000.

| Borrower | PD (%) | LGD (%) | EAD (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 2.0 | 45 | 5,00,000 |
| B | 5.0 | 60 | 3,00,000 |
| C | 1.0 | 40 | 2,00,000 |

### Step 1: Compute Individual ELs

For Borrower A:

$$EL_A = PD_A \times LGD_A \times EAD_A$$

$$EL_A = 0.02 \times 0.45 \times 5{,}00{,}000$$

$$EL_A = 0.009 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}500$$

For Borrower B:

$$EL_B = 0.05 \times 0.60 \times 3{,}00{,}000$$

$$EL_B = 0.030 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}000$$

For Borrower C:

$$EL_C = 0.01 \times 0.40 \times 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$EL_C = 0.004 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 800$$

### Step 2: Sum for Portfolio EL

$$EL_{portfolio} = EL_A + EL_B + EL_C$$

$$EL_{portfolio} = 4{,}500 + 9{,}000 + 800 = 14{,}300$$

### Step 3: Compute Unexpected Loss

$$UL = VaR_{99.9\%} - EL_{portfolio}$$

$$UL = 8{,}50{,}000 - 14{,}300 = 8{,}35{,}700$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The bank should provision ₹14,300 as Expected Loss (a definite cost of doing business) and hold ₹8,35,700 as Economic Capital to survive the worst 0.1% of scenarios.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Logistic PD Model

**Problem:** A borrower's financial features yield $Z = -1.5$ from a logistic regression model. Compute the PD and the odds of default.

### Step 1: Apply the Logistic Function

$$PD = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-Z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(-1.5)}}$$

$$PD = \frac{1}{1 + e^{1.5}}$$

We know that $e^{1.5} \approx 4.4817$:

$$PD = \frac{1}{1 + 4.4817} = \frac{1}{5.4817}$$

$$PD \approx 0.1824 \approx 18.24\%$$

### Step 2: Compute the Odds

$$\text{Odds} = \frac{PD}{1 - PD} = \frac{0.1824}{0.8176} \approx 0.2231$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{Odds} = e^{Z} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

The borrower has an **18.24% probability** of default. The odds of defaulting are **0.2231**, meaning the borrower is roughly **4.48 times more likely to NOT default than to default**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Altman Z-Score Application

**Problem (KTU Typical 7-Mark Question):**
A firm's financials are as follows:
- Working Capital = ₹50L, Total Assets = ₹200L
- Retained Earnings = ₹40L
- EBIT = ₹60L
- Market Cap = ₹80L, Book Value of Liabilities = ₹150L
- Sales = ₹300L

Classify the firm.

### Step 1: Compute Each Ratio

$$X_1 = \frac{WC}{TA} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25$$

$$X_2 = \frac{RE}{TA} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20$$

$$X_3 = \frac{EBIT}{TA} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30$$

$$X_4 = \frac{MV_{Equity}}{BV_{Liabilities}} = \frac{80}{150} = 0.5333$$

$$X_5 = \frac{Sales}{TA} = \frac{300}{200} = 1.50$$

### Step 2: Plug into Altman Formula

$$Z = 1.2(0.25) + 1.4(0.20) + 3.3(0.30) + 0.6(0.5333) + 1.0(1.50)$$

$$Z = 0.300 + 0.280 + 0.990 + 0.320 + 1.500$$

$$Z = 3.390$$

### Step 3: Classification

Since $Z = 3.390 > 2.99$, the firm lies in the **"Safe Zone"** — low probability of bankruptcy in the next 2 years.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 4 — Vasicek Conditional PD

**Problem:** A borrower has baseline PD $p = 0.02$, asset correlation $\rho = 0.20$, and the systematic factor realization is $Y = -1.5$. Compute the conditional PD.

### Step 1: Compute $\Phi^{-1}(p)$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi^{-1}(0.02) \approx -2.0537$.

### Step 2: Plug into Vasicek Equation

$$PD_{conditional} = \Phi\left(\frac{\Phi^{-1}(p) + \sqrt{\rho} \cdot Y}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{\rho} = \sqrt{0.20} \approx 0.4472$$

$$\sqrt{1-\rho} = \sqrt{0.80} \approx 0.8944$$

$$PD_{cond} = \Phi\left(\frac{-2.0537 + 0.4472 \times (-1.5)}{0.8944}\right)$$

$$PD_{cond} = \Phi\left(\frac{-2.0537 - 0.6708}{0.8944}\right)$$

$$PD_{cond} = \Phi\left(\frac{-2.7245}{0.8944}\right)$$

$$PD_{cond} = \Phi(-3.0463)$$

### Step 3: Look Up Standard Normal CDF

$$\Phi(-3.0463) \approx 0.00116 \approx 0.116\%$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

Even though the baseline PD is 2%, the borrower is in a **favorable macro-economic regime** (negative Y), so the conditional PD drops to just 0.116%. The Vasicek model captures the *cyclical* nature of default risk.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End Credit Risk Analytics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Sources: Bureau, Bank Statements, Tax Returns] --> B[Data Acquisition & Cleansing Layer]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering Module]
    C --> D[Statistical Model Layer: Logistic Regression / Z-Score]
    C --> E[Machine Learning Layer: XGBoost / Random Forest / Neural Networks]
    D --> F[Probability of Default PD Output]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Credit Decision Engine}
    G -->|PD below threshold| H[Approve: Compute Pricing]
    G -->|PD above threshold| I[Reject / Require Collateral]
    H --> J[LGD Estimation Module]
    J --> K[EAD Estimation Module]
    K --> L[Expected Loss EL Calculator]
    L --> M[Portfolio Aggregation Engine]
    M --> N[Value at Risk VaR Simulator]
    N --> O[Economic Capital & Regulatory Reporting]
    O --> P[Basel III / IFRS 9 Compliance Dashboard]
    P --> Q[Continuous Model Monitoring PSI, AUC, Gini]
    Q --> C
```

## 4.2 The Three Pillars of Credit Risk (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Probability of Default PD] --> EL[Expected Loss EL]
    L[Loss Given Default LGD] --> EL
    E[Exposure at Default EAD] --> EL
    EL --> UL[Unexpected Loss UL]
    UL --> VaR[Value at Risk VaR]
    VaR --> EC[Economic Capital]
    EC --> BS[Bank Stability and Profitability]
    style P fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#cf9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EL fill:#fc9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Credit Scoring Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Loan Application Received] --> Verify[Step 1: KYC and Identity Verification]
    Verify --> Bureau[Step 2: Pull Credit Bureau Data CIBIL Experian]
    Bureau --> Feature[Step 3: Compute Application Score Card]
    Feature --> Logit[Step 4: Logistic Regression PD Estimation]
    Logit --> Threshold{Step 5: PD vs Cutoff}
    Threshold -->|PD 30 percent| Reject[Reject Application]
    Threshold -->|PD between 10 and 30 percent| Manual[Manual Underwriter Review]
    Threshold -->|PD less than 10 percent| Approve[Auto Approve and Price Risk]
    Manual --> FinalDecision{Final Decision}
    FinalDecision -->|Approved with conditions| Approve
    FinalDecision -->|Rejected| Reject
    Approve --> Monitor[Continuous Portfolio Monitoring]
    Reject --> End1[Archive and Audit Trail]
    Monitor --> End2[Re score every 6 months]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture: PD Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PDMod[Probability of Default Models] --> Stat[Statistical Models]
    PDMod --> ML[Machine Learning Models]
    PDMod --> Hybrid[Hybrid Ensemble Models]
    Stat --> Log[Logistic Regression]
    Stat --> Z[Altman Z Score]
    Stat --> Probit[Probit Model]
    ML --> Tree[Decision Tree and Random Forest]
    ML --> Boost[XGBoost and LightGBM]
    ML --> NN[Neural Network Deep Learning]
    Hybrid --> Stack[Stacking Classifier]
    Hybrid --> Voting[Voting Ensemble]
    style PDMod fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style Stat fill:#ddf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style ML fill:#dfd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Hybrid fill:#fdd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Credit Risk. Differentiate between PD, LGD, and EAD with one-line definitions each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Credit Risk** is the possibility that a borrower or counterparty will fail to meet its obligations under agreed-upon terms, resulting in financial loss to the lender.

- **PD (Probability of Default):** The likelihood, expressed as a percentage (0% to 100%), that a borrower will default within a specified time horizon (typically one year).
- **LGD (Loss Given Default):** The proportion of the outstanding exposure that the lender expects to *lose* if the borrower defaults, expressed as a percentage (typically 30%–70% for secured loans, up to 100% for unsecured).
- **EAD (Exposure at Default):** The total amount the borrower owes to the lender at the moment default occurs, measured in monetary units.

> [!NOTE]
> All three together produce Expected Loss: $EL = PD \times LGD \times EAD$.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Altman Z-Score? Write the formula and state the decision rule for the Safe and Distress zones.**

**Model Answer:**

**Altman's Z-Score** is a multivariate financial model developed by Edward I. Altman in 1968 that combines five financial ratios to predict the probability of corporate bankruptcy within a 2-year horizon.

$$Z = 1.2 X_1 + 1.4 X_2 + 3.3 X_3 + 0.6 X_4 + 1.0 X_5$$

**Decision Rule:**
- $Z > 2.99$ → **Safe Zone** (financially healthy, low default risk).
- $1.81 \le Z \le 2.99$ → **Grey Zone** (caution, requires further analysis).
- $Z < 1.81$ → **Distress Zone** (high probability of bankruptcy).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A bank is evaluating two corporate borrowers, X and Y, with the following characteristics:**

| Parameter | Borrower X | Borrower Y |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| PD | 3% | 6% |
| LGD | 50% | 65% |
| EAD | ₹10,00,000 | ₹6,00,000 |

**(a)** Compute the Expected Loss for each borrower individually and for the combined portfolio.  
**(b)** Explain, with justification, which borrower is *riskier per unit of exposure*, and discuss how a bank might use this information to set loan pricing using the *Risk-Adjusted Return on Capital (RAROC)* framework.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**For Borrower X:**

$$EL_X = PD_X \times LGD_X \times EAD_X$$

$$EL_X = 0.03 \times 0.50 \times 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$EL_X = 0.015 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}000$$

**For Borrower Y:**

$$EL_Y = 0.06 \times 0.65 \times 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$EL_Y = 0.039 \times 6{,}00{,}000 = 23{,}400$$

**Portfolio EL:**

$$EL_{portfolio} = EL_X + EL_Y = 15{,}000 + 23{,}400 = 38{,}400$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Risk per Unit of Exposure (EL Ratio):**

For X:

$$\text{EL Ratio}_X = \frac{EL_X}{EAD_X} = \frac{15{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.5\%$$

For Y:

$$\text{EL Ratio}_Y = \frac{EL_Y}{EAD_Y} = \frac{23{,}400}{6{,}00{,}000} = 3.9\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Although Borrower X has a higher absolute EAD, **Borrower Y is riskier per unit of exposure** (3.9% > 1.5%) due to a combination of higher PD and higher LGD.

**RAROC-Based Pricing:**

$$RAROC = \frac{\text{Revenue} - \text{Expected Loss} - \text{Operating Costs}}{\text{Economic Capital}}$$

A bank would charge Borrower Y a **higher interest rate spread** to:
1. Compensate for the higher EL (provisions).
2. Earn a hurdle RAROC (typically 12–15%) on the larger economic capital allocated.
3. Reflect the higher required yield by the capital markets for the elevated risk profile.

The interest rate can be approximated as:

$$\text{Loan Rate} = \text{Funding Cost} + \text{EL Ratio} + \text{Margin for UL} + \text{Profit Margin}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A fintech NBFC uses a logistic regression model to predict loan default. The estimated coefficient vector yields a linear score $Z = -2.0$ for a specific applicant.**  
**(a)** Compute the probability of default and the odds ratio. Comment on whether the applicant should be approved under a cutoff PD of 8%.  
**(b)** Outline any THREE advantages and TWO limitations of using logistic regression for credit scoring compared to a modern machine learning algorithm like XGBoost.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute PD using the logistic function:**

$$PD = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-Z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(-2.0)}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{2.0}}$$

We know $e^{2.0} \approx 7.389$:

$$PD = \frac{1}{1 + 7.389} = \frac{1}{8.389} \approx 0.1192$$

$$PD \approx 11.92\%$$

**Step 2: Compute Odds:**

$$\text{Odds} = e^{Z} = e^{-2.0} \approx 0.1353$$

This means the applicant is approximately **7.4 times more likely to NOT default than to default**.

**Step 3: Decision:**

Since $PD = 11.92\% > 8\%$ **cutoff**, the applicant **should NOT be auto-approved**. Recommended action: route to manual underwriter or request additional collateral.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Three Advantages of Logistic Regression:**
1. **Interpretability** — Coefficients directly indicate the direction and magnitude of each feature's impact, satisfying regulatory requirements (e.g., SR 11-7).
2. **Computational Efficiency** — Trains in milliseconds on millions of records; ideal for real-time scoring.
3. **Probabilistic Output** — Naturally produces a calibrated probability, unlike tree-based methods which require Platt scaling.

**Two Limitations of Logistic Regression vs XGBoost:**
1. **Linearity Assumption** — Logistic regression assumes a linear relationship in the log-odds, missing complex non-linear interactions that XGBoost captures automatically.
2. **Limited Feature Interaction** — Requires manual feature crosses; XGBoost discovers interactions intrinsically, often yielding 5–15% higher AUC on default prediction datasets.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Credit Risk Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 Marks):** Mixing up PD (a probability) with EL (a currency). Always check whether the answer should be a percentage or ₹.
> 2. **Forgetting the Sum in Portfolio EL (–1 Mark):** Portfolio EL is the *sum* of individual ELs, *not the average*.
> 3. **Incorrect Use of Z-Score Coefficients (–2 Marks):** Students often memorize coefficients incorrectly. The exact weights are **1.2, 1.4, 3.3, 0.6, 1.0** — memorize precisely.
> 4. **Missing the "Why" (–2 Marks in 14-mark Qs):** KTU examiners award marks for *justification*, not just computation. Always explain *why* one borrower is riskier than another.
> 5. **Not Converting Percentages to Decimals:** 3% must become 0.03 before multiplication. A 100x error is a frequent cause of zero marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Credit Risk Analysis (Module 2)**

- **Core Definition:** Credit Risk = Probability that borrower fails to meet obligations.
- **Master Equation:** $EL = PD \times LGD \times EAD$ (know the unit of each).
- **Portfolio EL:** Always **sum** individual ELs (not average).
- **Unexpected Loss:** $UL = VaR_{\alpha} - EL$, where $\alpha = 99.9\%$ under Basel.
- **Altman Z-Score:** Coefficients 1.2 / 1.4 / 3.3 / 0.6 / 1.0; Cutoffs 1.81 and 2.99.
- **Logistic PD:** $PD = \frac{1}{1+e^{-Z}}$; Odds $= e^{Z}$; Logit $= \ln(\text{Odds})$.
- **Vasicek Conditional PD:** Captures systematic risk via single-factor model; $\rho$ is asset correlation.
- **RAROC Framework:** Used for risk-based pricing; loan rate covers funding cost + EL + UL margin + profit.
- **Model Validation Metrics:** AUC-ROC, Gini ($= 2 \cdot AUC - 1$), Brier Score, PSI.
- **Regulatory Backbone:** Basel II/III pillars — Pillar 1 (Capital), Pillar 2 (Supervision), Pillar 3 (Disclosure).
- **Types of Credit Risk:** Default, Spread, Recovery, Settlement, Concentration, Country.
- **Industry Applications:** Retail banking, FinTech, NBFCs, Corporate lending, Supply Chain, Insurance.
- **Decision Cutoff:** PD < threshold ⇒ Approve; PD > threshold ⇒ Reject; Grey zone ⇒ Manual review.
- **Always convert percentages to decimals** before plugging into any formula.
- **Justify every answer** with the underlying business rationale to score full KTU marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fraud Detection and Prevention Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fraud Detection and Prevention Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fraud Detection and Prevention Analytics** is the systematic application of statistical, computational, and machine learning techniques on large-scale transactional and behavioral data to (a) **identify fraudulent activities** that have already occurred (Detection) and (b) **proactively forecast, block, or mitigate** fraudulent events before they materialize (Prevention).

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics framework, this discipline is positioned as a high-value application of **Predictive Analytics** and **Prescriptive Analytics**, leveraging historical fraud patterns to build self-learning models that operate across **structured data** (transaction logs, claim records) and **unstructured data** (call transcripts, emails, images).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault Analogy"

Imagine a bank vault that is guarded by a watchman. In the **old rule-based era**, the watchman was given a rigid checklist: *"If a person wears a mask and carries a gun, sound the alarm."* This catches obvious robberies but fails when the thief pretends to be a maintenance worker. 

**Fraud Detection Analytics** is like upgrading that watchman with **CCTV, AI vision, and behavioral memory** — the guard now remembers that *Mr. X visits every Tuesday, deposits ₹5,000, and leaves in 10 minutes.* If Mr. X suddenly withdraws ₹5,00,000 at 3 AM from another country, the AI guard instantly flags this as **anomalous behavior** even though no explicit "rule" was broken. **Fraud Prevention Analytics** takes it one step further: it locks the vault *before* Mr. X can act, based on predictive signals.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Fraud:** Intentional deception for personal/organizational gain, causing loss to another party.
> - **False Positive (Type I Error):** A legitimate transaction wrongly flagged as fraud (customer inconvenience).
> - **False Negative (Type II Error):** A fraudulent transaction missed by the model (direct financial loss).
> - **Anomaly / Outlier:** A data point that deviates significantly from the established norm.
> - **Benford's Law:** A digit-distribution law used as a statistical fraud screen.
> - **Red Flag:** A predefined indicator of suspicious activity (e.g., structuring of deposits).
> - **Legitimate User Behavior Profile:** A baseline behavioral signature of a genuine customer.

### 1.4 Typology of Fraud (Industry-Relevant Classification)

| Fraud Category | Domain | Typical Data Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Credit Card Fraud** | Banking / FinTech | POS logs, online transaction records |
| **Insurance Fraud** | Health / Auto / Life Insurance | Claim forms, hospital records, police reports |
| **Identity Theft** | Telecom / Banking | KYC documents, IP logs, device fingerprints |
| **Money Laundering** | Banking / RegTech | Wire transfers, shell-company networks |
| **Healthcare / Billing Fraud** | Hospitals / Insurance | CPT codes, prescription patterns |
| **Telecom Fraud** | Telecom Operators | Call Detail Records (CDR) |
| **E-commerce Return Fraud** | Retail / Online Marketplaces | Shipment tracking, return histories |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fraud Analytics Lifecycle (Operational Framework)

The detection-to-prevention pipeline follows a structured six-phase cycle, which is a frequent KTU short-answer topic:

1. **Data Acquisition:** Aggregation of transactional, behavioral, demographic, and external watchlist data.
2. **Data Pre-processing:** Cleaning, normalization, handling of missing values, and class-imbalance correction (since fraudulent cases are typically < 0.1% of total data).
3. **Feature Engineering:** Construction of behavioral variables such as *average transaction value*, *login frequency*, *velocity ratios*, *geolocation deviation*.
4. **Model Training:** Application of supervised, unsupervised, or hybrid learning algorithms.
5. **Scoring & Thresholding:** Assigning a fraud probability score to each transaction and applying a decision cut-off.
6. **Alert Management & Feedback Loop:** Investigation by analysts, with outcomes fed back to retrain the model (continuous learning).

### 2.2 Analytical Methods — The Three Pillars

#### Pillar A — Rule-Based (Expert) Systems
- Predefined IF-THEN rules (e.g., *IF transaction > ₹50,000 AND country ≠ home country THEN ALERT*).
- **Strength:** Transparency, regulatory acceptance.
- **Weakness:** Cannot detect novel fraud patterns; high maintenance overhead.

#### Pillar B — Statistical & Anomaly Detection Methods
- **Z-Score Analysis:** Flagging records whose features lie beyond $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.
- **Benford's Law Test:** Comparing leading-digit distributions against the natural logarithmic curve.
- **Clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN):** Identifying isolated points that do not belong to any cluster.

#### Pillar C — Machine Learning & AI Methods
- **Supervised:** Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost, Neural Networks.
- **Unsupervised:** Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, One-Class SVM.
- **Hybrid / Ensemble:** Combining multiple models to improve robustness.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested** mathematical formulations in KTU Business Analytics examinations. Master them thoroughly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Of all transactions flagged, how many were truly fraud? |
| 2 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Of all actual frauds, how many were caught? |
| 3 | **Specificity** | $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Of all genuine transactions, how many were correctly accepted? |
| 4 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| 5 | **False Alarm Rate (FAR)** | $FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Critical metric for customer experience |
| 6 | **Bayes' Posterior (Fraud Probability)** | $P(F \vert X) = \dfrac{P(X \vert F) \cdot P(F)}{P(X)}$ | Foundation of Naive Bayes fraud classifier |
| 7 | **Z-Score (Anomaly)** | $Z = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Distance of observation from the mean in standard deviations |
| 8 | **Logistic Regression Score** | $P(F) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}$ | Probability that transaction is fraudulent |
| 9 | **Benford's Law** | $P(d) = \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{d}\right), \quad d \in \{1,\dots,9\}$ | Expected frequency of leading digit $d$ |
| 10 | **Expected Loss Function** | $E[L] = C_{FP} \cdot N_{FP} + C_{FN} \cdot N_{FN}$ | Cost-weighted decision threshold optimization |
| 11 | **Lift (Top Decile)** | $\text{Lift} = \dfrac{\text{Precision}_{\text{decile}}}{\text{Base Rate}}$ | Effectiveness of model in catching fraud in top-K alerts |
| 12 | **Odds Ratio** | $\text{Odds} = \dfrac{P(F)}{1 - P(F)}$ | Logit transformation input |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fraud analytics underpins the **operational risk management** layer of virtually every digital financial system. In **banking**, it triggers SWIFT-network surveillance; in **insurance**, it adjudicates suspicious claims; in **e-commerce**, it defends the entire checkout funnel. Modern platforms (PayPal, Stripe, Visa's VisaNet) process these computations in **< 300 milliseconds per transaction**, making it one of the highest-velocity real-time analytics applications in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Bayes' Theorem to the Naive Bayes Fraud Classifier

We begin with the classical definition of conditional probability and expand it using Bayes' theorem, then apply the **conditional independence assumption** characteristic of the Naive Bayes model.

$$
P(F \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid F) \cdot P(F)}{P(X)}
$$

**Step 1 — Decompose the joint likelihood $P(X \mid F)$** for a feature vector $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ representing transaction attributes (amount, time, location, device):

$$
P(X \mid F) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid F)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule of probability:**

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid F) = P(x_1 \mid F) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, F) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, F) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \dots, x_{n-1}, F)
$$

**Step 3 — Invoke the Naive (conditional independence) assumption** — each feature is independent of every other feature given the class label $F$:

$$
P(x_i \mid x_1, \dots, x_{i-1}, F) = P(x_i \mid F)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back** to obtain the simplified Naive Bayes formulation:

$$
P(F \mid X) \propto P(F) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid F)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although the "naive" independence assumption is rarely true in reality (transaction amount and time *are* correlated), Naive Bayes performs remarkably well in fraud detection because the goal is **ranking** transactions by risk, not exact probability calibration.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — F1-Score as the Harmonic Mean

The **harmonic mean** of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$) is preferred over the arithmetic mean when we want to penalize extreme imbalance between the two metrics.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of a harmonic mean** of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$:

$$
H = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $a = P$ and $b = R$:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}
$$

**Step 3 — Find a common denominator** in the fraction:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Step 4 — Final closed form** of the F1-Score:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harmonic Mean and Not Arithmetic?** If Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.01, the arithmetic mean would be 0.505 (misleadingly "good"), but the harmonic mean correctly returns $F_1 \approx 0.02$, reflecting the model's near-total failure to detect fraud.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Logistic Regression and the Log-Odds Transformation

Logistic regression models the **log-odds** of fraud as a linear combination of features.

**Step 1 — Define the odds of fraud** as the ratio of probability of fraud to probability of non-fraud:

$$
\text{Odds}(F) = \frac{P(F)}{1 - P(F)}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm** to obtain the log-odds (logit):

$$
\text{logit}(P(F)) = \ln\!\left(\frac{P(F)}{1 - P(F)}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Model the logit as a linear function** of features $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ with coefficients $\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_n$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P(F)}{1 - P(F)}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n
$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
\frac{P(F)}{1 - P(F)} = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $P(F)$** (the logistic / sigmoid function):

$$
P(F) = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{where } z = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i x_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of $\beta_i$:** A one-unit increase in $x_i$ multiplies the **odds** of fraud by $e^{\beta_i}$. If $\beta_2 = 0.7$ for the feature *transaction_amount*, then a ₹1 increase in amount multiplies fraud odds by $e^{0.7} \approx 2.01$, i.e., **doubles the odds**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Business Scenario | Best-Suited Method | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Highly imbalanced data (< 0.5% fraud) | **SMOTE + Random Forest** | Handles class imbalance with ensemble robustness |
