| Real-time streaming transactions | **Online Logistic Regression / Hoeffding Trees** | Sub-millisecond scoring |
| Unknown / emerging fraud patterns | **Isolation Forest / Autoencoder** | Unsupervised anomaly detection |
| Regulatory auditability required | **Rule-based + Decision Tree** | Transparent decision logic |
| Large-scale graph-based money flows | **Graph Neural Networks (GNN)** | Detects money-mule networks |
| Small dataset with strong labels | **Naive Bayes** | Robust with limited data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Fraud Analytics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Transaction Stream] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Preprocessing and Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Hybrid Detection Engine]
    D --> E1[Rule Based Module]
    D --> E2[Statistical Anomaly Module]
    D --> E3[Machine Learning Classifier]
    E1 --> F[Risk Score Aggregator]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Threshold Decision}
    G -->|Score Above Cutoff| H[Alert Generation]
    G -->|Score Below Cutoff| I[Approve Transaction]
    H --> J[Analyst Investigation]
    J --> K[Feedback Loop]
    K --> C
    I --> L[Real Time Monitoring Dashboard]
    H --> L
```

### 4.2 Supervised vs Unsupervised Detection Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Historical Labeled Data] --> B[Supervised Branch]
    A1[Unlabeled Live Stream] --> C[Unsupervised Branch]
    B --> B1[Logistic Regression]
    B --> B2[Random Forest]
    B --> B3[XGBoost]
    C --> C1[Isolation Forest]
    C --> C2[Autoencoder]
    C --> C3[One Class SVM]
    B1 --> D[Score Fusion Layer]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Final Fraud Score]
    E --> F[Decision Engine]
```

### 4.3 Prevention vs Detection — Functional Contrast Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DET[Detection Layer]
        D1[Transaction Occurs] --> D2[Post Event Analysis]
        D2 --> D3[Flag and Investigate]
    end
    subgraph PREV[Prevention Layer]
        P1[User Behavior Profiling] --> P2[Predictive Risk Scoring]
        P2 --> P3[Pre Transaction Block]
    end
    DET --> OUT[Loss Minimization]
    PREV --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Confusion-Matrix Block Diagram (KTU Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Total Population] --> B[Predicted Fraud]
    A --> C[Predicted Legitimate]
    B --> B1[True Positive TP]
    B --> B2[False Positive FP]
    C --> C1[False Negative FN]
    C --> C2[True Negative TN]
    B1 --> M1[Recall TP over TP plus FN]
    B1 --> M2[Precision TP over TP plus FP]
    B2 --> M3[FAR FP over FP plus TN]
    C1 --> M1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define fraud detection analytics. List any four types of fraud commonly detected using analytics.*

**Model Answer:**
Fraud detection analytics is the use of statistical, computational, and machine-learning techniques on large datasets to identify fraudulent activities that have already occurred by recognizing anomalous patterns, red flags, and deviations from normal behavior.

Four common types of fraud:
1. **Credit card fraud** — unauthorized use of card details.
2. **Insurance fraud** — false or inflated claims.
3. **Identity theft** — impersonation using stolen personal data.
4. **Money laundering** — disguising illicit funds as legitimate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between fraud detection and fraud prevention analytics with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Detection | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Post-event (after fraud has occurred) | Pre-event (before fraud occurs) |
| **Goal** | Identify and flag the fraud | Block or deter the fraud |
| **Method** | Anomaly detection, classification | Predictive scoring, risk-based authentication |
| **Example** | Flagging a suspicious insurance claim after submission | Blocking a high-risk transaction before authorization |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a fraud detection analytics system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **data pre-processing** and **feature engineering** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With suitable formulas, explain **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and False Alarm Rate** for a fraud detection model. Why is the F1-Score preferred over simple accuracy? **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The architecture of a fraud detection analytics system consists of the following sequential layers:

1. **Data Ingestion Layer:** Collects transactional, behavioral, and external data from heterogeneous sources (databases, logs, APIs, third-party watchlists).
2. **Data Pre-processing Layer:** Handles missing values, removes duplicates, normalizes numerical fields, encodes categorical variables, and applies class-imbalance techniques (SMOTE, undersampling) since fraud cases are typically < 1% of data.
3. **Feature Engineering Layer:** Derives informative variables such as *transaction velocity* (transactions per hour), *amount deviation* (current amount vs. 30-day average), *location mismatch*, and *device fingerprint deviation*.
4. **Modeling Layer:** Applies supervised (Random Forest, XGBoost), unsupervised (Isolation Forest), or hybrid models.
5. **Scoring & Decision Layer:** Generates a fraud probability score and compares it against a threshold.
6. **Alert & Feedback Layer:** Routes alerts to investigators and feeds confirmed outcomes back to retrain the model.

**Role of Pre-processing & Feature Engineering:**
- Pre-processing ensures **data quality** and **model compatibility** (e.g., scaling for logistic regression).
- Feature engineering injects **domain knowledge**, dramatically improving model accuracy. For example, a raw "timestamp" is useless, but a derived feature *"time-since-last-transaction"* is highly predictive.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

The four core metrics are derived from the **confusion matrix**:

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

$$
\text{FAR (False Alarm Rate)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

**Why F1-Score is preferred over Accuracy:**
In fraud datasets, accuracy is misleading. If 99.9% of transactions are legitimate, a naive model that predicts *"all legitimate"* achieves 99.9% accuracy while catching **zero fraud**. The F1-Score balances Precision and Recall, making it robust to severe class imbalance and reflecting the model's true ability to detect rare fraud events.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes classifier** for fraud detection with mathematical formulation. State the **conditional independence assumption** clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Benford's Law**? Explain how it is used as a fraud detection tool in financial auditing. Provide the mathematical expression and an example. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Naive Bayes classifier applies **Bayes' theorem** to compute the posterior probability that a transaction is fraudulent given its features $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$.

$$
P(F \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid F) \cdot P(F)}{P(X)}
$$

**Conditional Independence Assumption:**
Given the class label $F$, every feature $x_i$ is assumed to be **statistically independent** of every other feature $x_j$. Formally:

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid F) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid F)
$$

Substituting, the classification rule becomes:

$$
\hat{F} = \arg\max_{F} \; P(F) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid F)
$$

Despite its simplicity, Naive Bayes works effectively for fraud screening because it requires only a small training set and provides fast, probabilistic ranking of suspicious transactions.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Benford's Law** (also called the **First-Digit Law**) states that in many naturally occurring numerical datasets, the leading digit $d$ is **not uniformly distributed**. The digit 1 appears as the leading digit in approximately 30.1% of cases, while 9 appears in only about 4.6%.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
P(d) = \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{d}\right), \quad d \in \{1, 2, \dots, 9\}
$$

**Example values:**

| $d$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(d)$ | 0.301 | 0.176 | 0.125 | 0.097 | 0.079 | 0.067 | 0.058 | 0.051 | 0.046 |

**Application in Fraud Detection:**
Auditors compare the leading-digit distribution of a company's reported financial figures against Benford's expected distribution. Significant deviations (e.g., an over-representation of digit 5) raise **red flags** for manipulated data, fabricated invoices, or fraudulent expense claims. This is a non-invasive, statistical first-pass screening tool widely used in forensic accounting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall:** Students frequently swap the two. Memorize: *Recall* = fraud caught out of **all actual fraud** (rows); *Precision* = fraud caught out of **all flagged** (columns).
> 2. **Forgetting the Class-Imbalance Context:** Simply stating "accuracy = 95%" without mentioning the imbalance issue will lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the Conditional Independence Assumption:** The word "naive" *means* the assumption — examiners expect you to state it explicitly.
> 4. **Drawing Benford's Law without a formula:** Always provide the closed-form expression; do not just describe it verbally.
> 5. **Ignoring the Feedback Loop:** When asked to "explain the architecture," students often omit the continuous-learning loop, which is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Fraud** is intentional deception causing loss; analytics detects it via data-driven models.
- **Detection = post-event flagging; Prevention = pre-event blocking.**
- The **fraud analytics lifecycle** has six phases: ingest → pre-process → engineer features → model → score → feedback.
- Three **method pillars**: rule-based, statistical/anomaly, and machine learning.
- **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation: TP, FP, FN, TN.
- **Precision** answers *"Of those flagged, how many were truly fraud?"*
- **Recall** answers *"Of all actual frauds, how many did we catch?"*
- **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean — robust to class imbalance.
- **False Alarm Rate** measures customer inconvenience from false positives.
- **Naive Bayes** applies Bayes' theorem with the **conditional independence** assumption.
- **Logistic Regression** models the log-odds of fraud as a linear function of features.
- **Z-Score** flags observations beyond $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.
- **Benford's Law** detects digit-distribution anomalies in financial data.
- **Class imbalance** is the central challenge — address via SMOTE, undersampling, or cost-sensitive learning.
- **Real-time systems** (e.g., payment gateways) require sub-second scoring using online learning.
- **Feedback loop** is essential: confirmed frauds and false positives must retrain the model continuously.
- Always compute **cost-weighted metrics** ($E[L] = C_{FP} \cdot N_{FP} + C_{FN} \cdot N_{FN}$) — fraud-detection thresholds should optimize business loss, not statistical accuracy.
- Top KTU-expected terminology: anomaly, red flag, baseline profile, drift, alert triage, lift, AUC-ROC, cost-sensitive classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytics in Banking and Financial Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Banking and Financial Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Analytics in Banking and Financial Services** is the systematic application of statistical, mathematical, computational, and machine-learning techniques to financial data — including transactions, customer records, market feeds, and regulatory reports — with the objective of generating actionable insights for **risk management, fraud prevention, customer relationship enhancement, regulatory compliance, and revenue optimization**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim-Equivalent Concept):**
> *Analytics in BFSI refers to the data-driven transformation of raw financial data into predictive, prescriptive, and descriptive intelligence that supports strategic, tactical, and operational decisions across retail banking, corporate banking, capital markets, insurance, and fintech ecosystems.*

The discipline operates across four cognitive levels, as defined by the Gartner Analytics Maturity Model:

| Level | Name | Core Question |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Descriptive Analytics** | *What happened?* |
| 2 | **Diagnostic Analytics** | *Why did it happen?* |
| 3 | **Predictive Analytics** | *What will happen?* |
| 4 | **Prescriptive Analytics** | *What should we do?* |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hospital Analogy**
> Imagine a bank is a **multi-specialty hospital** that serves millions of "patients" (customers) every day. The hospital cannot rely on guesswork.
> - **Descriptive Analytics** = Patient's past medical history file.
> - **Diagnostic Analytics** = The doctor's diagnosis of *why* the patient is sick today.
> - **Predictive Analytics** = The genetic test that forecasts *which diseases* the patient is likely to develop.
> - **Prescriptive Analytics** = The treatment plan that says *exactly which medicines, in what dosage, for how long* will maximize recovery and minimize side effects.
>
> In the same way, a modern bank uses analytics not just to record transactions, but to *diagnose* customer behavior, *predict* loan defaults, and *prescribe* personalized offers.

## 1.3 Why This Domain Matters for KTU

Banking and Financial Services is one of the **earliest and largest adopters of analytics** globally. According to industry estimates (NITI Aayog / NASSCOM reports on Indian BFSI), the sector invests **roughly 10–15% of its total IT budget** into analytics platforms, making it a high-priority topic in the PECBT755 syllabus.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Industry Metrics to Remember (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **RBI (Reserve Bank of India)** mandates analytics-driven reporting for all scheduled commercial banks.
> - **Basel III Norms** require banks to maintain minimum capital ratios computed through credit-risk models.
> - **SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)** enforces real-time surveillance analytics in capital markets.

## 1.4 The BFSI Sub-Domains Where Analytics Operates

The BFSI sector is not monolithic. Analytics is applied in:

1. **Retail Banking** — savings, loans, mortgages, credit cards.
2. **Corporate & Investment Banking** — mergers, IPOs, syndicated loans.
3. **Capital Markets** — equities, derivatives, commodities, forex.
4. **Insurance** — underwriting, claims, actuarial analytics.
5. **Fintech & Digital Payments** — UPI, mobile wallets, neo-banks.
6. **Wealth & Asset Management** — portfolio optimization, robo-advisory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of BFSI Analytics

### Pillar 1: Credit Risk Analytics
Credit risk is the probability that a borrower will **fail to meet contractual obligations** on a debt. Analytics is used to:
- **Score** loan applicants.
- **Segment** borrowers into risk buckets.
- **Price** loans dynamically based on risk.
- **Monitor** the portfolio for early-warning signs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Expected Loss (EL) Formula** is the cornerstone of credit-risk analytics under the **Basel III Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) Approach**:
> 
> $$\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$$
> 
> Where:
> - **PD** = Probability of Default
> - **LGD** = Loss Given Default
> - **EAD** = Exposure at Default
> 
> For example, a loan of ₹10,00,000 with PD = 3%, LGD = 45% has EL = ₹13,500.

### Pillar 2: Fraud Detection & Anti-Money Laundering (AML)
Banks lose **billions of dollars** annually to fraud. Analytics solves this through:
- **Rule-based systems** (e.g., flag transactions > ₹10 lakh in cash).
- **Anomaly detection** (statistical outliers, Isolation Forest, Autoencoders).
- **Graph analytics** (to find money-mule rings and shell-company networks).
- **Network analytics** (Neo4j, TigerGraph for transaction graphs).

### Pillar 3: Customer Analytics
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** — the total net profit a customer is expected to generate over their relationship with the bank.
- **Churn Prediction** — modelling the probability a customer will leave for a competitor.
- **RFM Segmentation** — Recency, Frequency, Monetary.
- **Next-Best-Action (NBA)** — prescriptive recommendation engines.
- **Sentiment Analytics** — NLP on call-centre transcripts, social media, reviews.

### Pillar 4: Operational & Regulatory Analytics (RegTech)
- **Stress Testing** — simulating economic shocks (2008-style crises, COVID-19).
- **Capital Adequacy Modelling** — ensuring CAR ≥ 9% under RBI norms.
- **Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR)** monitoring.
- **Automated regulatory reporting** (RBI, SEBI, FATF compliance).
- **Algorithmic trading surveillance** to detect market manipulation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **one-page cheat sheet** for the most important formulas tested in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for this topic. Memorize them.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Loss (EL) | $\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$ | PD %, LGD %, EAD ₹ | Basel III IRB standard |
| 2 | Customer Lifetime Value | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) \times r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | $R_t$ revenue, $C_t$ cost, $r_t$ retention, $d$ discount | Used in CRM analytics |
| 3 | Value at Risk (VaR), Parametric | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = \mu - z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma$ | $\mu$ mean return, $\sigma$ std dev, $z_{\alpha}$ confidence | Normality assumption |
| 4 | Sharpe Ratio | $S = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | $R_p$ portfolio return, $R_f$ risk-free, $\sigma_p$ std dev | Measures risk-adjusted return |
| 5 | Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) | $\text{CAR} = \frac{\text{Tier 1} + \text{Tier 2 Capital}}{\text{Risk-Weighted Assets}}$ | % | RBI minimum: 9% (with buffer 11.5%) |
| 6 | RFM Score | $\text{RFM}_{\text{Score}} = w_R \cdot R + w_F \cdot F + w_M \cdot M$ | Weights sum to 1 | Used for segmentation |
| 7 | Default Correlation | $\rho_{D} = \frac{\text{Cov}(D_1, D_2)}{\sigma_{D_1} \cdot \sigma_{D_2}}$ | Range [-1, 1] | Critical in portfolio credit risk |
| 8 | Logit Credit Score | $\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i$ | $p$ = prob of default, $x_i$ = features | Foundation of FICO scoring |
| 9 | Liquidity Coverage Ratio | $\text{LCR} = \frac{\text{HQLA}}{\text{Net Cash Outflows over 30 days}}$ | % | Must be ≥ 100% (Basel III) |
| 10 | Z-Score (Fraud Anomaly) | $Z = \frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$ | Z > 3 typically fraud | Standard anomaly detector |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Rendering Tip:** When copying these into your answer sheet, always wrap the formula in `$$ ... $$` for display. Do **NOT** use vertical pipes inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` for absolute value expressions.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

While this is a management course, BFSI analytics intersects with **computer science engineering** in the following ways:
- **Big Data Engineering** — Apache Spark, Hadoop for processing petabytes of transaction logs.
- **AI/ML Engineering** — TensorFlow, PyTorch models for fraud detection.
- **Cybersecurity** — SOC (Security Operations Center) analytics.
- **Cloud Computing** — AWS Financial Services, Azure for banking.
- **Blockchain Analytics** — Chainalysis for crypto transactions.

This interdisciplinary nature is precisely **why PECBT755 is a Professional Elective** — it bridges management theory with engineering practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Expected Loss (EL) — Worked Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU-Style):**
> A retail bank disburses a personal loan of **₹5,00,000** to Mr. Arun. Internal models estimate:
> - Probability of Default (PD) = **4%**
> - Loss Given Default (LGD) = **60%**
> - Exposure at Default (EAD) = **₹5,00,000**
>
> Compute the **Expected Loss** and interpret it.

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula
From the formula sheet, the EL equation is:

$$
\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\text{EL} = 0.04 \times 0.60 \times 5{,}00{,}000
$$

> Here, **PD = 0.04** (because 4% = 0.04 as a decimal) and **LGD = 0.60** (because 60% = 0.60).

### Step 3 — Multiply Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EL} &= 0.04 \times 0.60 \times 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 0.024 \times 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{12{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The bank should provision **₹12,000** as an expected loss reserve for this single loan. Across a portfolio of 10,000 similar loans (with identical risk parameters), the bank must provision **₹12,00,00,000 (₹1.2 Crore)** in capital reserves under Basel III norms.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Always convert percentages to decimals before substituting. Many students lose 1 mark by writing "4 × 60 × 5,00,000".

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Value at Risk (VaR) — 1-Day, 95% Confidence

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A trader's daily portfolio return has a mean **μ = 0.1%** and standard deviation **σ = 1.5%**. Compute the **1-day 95% VaR** for a portfolio of value **₹10,00,000 (₹10 Lakh)**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula
The parametric (variance-covariance) VaR formula is:

$$
\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = (\mu - z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma) \times V
$$

Where $V$ is the portfolio value and $z_{\alpha}$ is the z-score for the chosen confidence level.

### Step 2 — Find the Z-Score
For **95% confidence**, the one-tailed z-score is **1.645**.

### Step 3 — Substitute and Compute the Percentage Loss

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VaR}_{\%} &= \mu - z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 0.001 - 1.645 \times 0.015 \\
&= 0.001 - 0.024675 \\
&= -0.023675
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates a *loss* of 2.3675% of the portfolio.

### Step 4 — Convert to Monetary Value

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VaR}_{₹} &= 0.023675 \times 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{₹23{,}675}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
> The bank is **95% confident** that the daily loss on this ₹10 Lakh portfolio will **not exceed ₹23,675**. In other words, there is only a 5% chance of losing more than this amount on any given trading day.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Caveat:** VaR assumes **normal distribution** of returns, which underestimates **tail risk** (extreme losses during crises like 2008 or COVID-19). This is why **Conditional VaR (CVaR) / Expected Shortfall** is preferred in modern risk practice.

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — 3-Year Horizon

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A credit-card customer yields **₹4,000** annual profit, with an annual churn probability of **20%** (retention r = 0.80). The discount rate is **10%**. Compute the **3-year CLV**.

### Step 1 — Retention Rate
Churn = 20% ⟹ Retention $r_t$ for each year:
- Year 1: 1.00
- Year 2: 0.80
- Year 3: 0.64

### Step 2 — Apply the CLV Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} &= \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{r_t \cdot \text{Profit}_t}{(1+d)^t} \\
&= \frac{1.00 \times 4000}{(1.10)^1} + \frac{0.80 \times 4000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{0.64 \times 4000}{(1.10)^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1} &= \frac{4000}{1.10} = 3636.36 \\
\text{Term 2} &= \frac{3200}{1.21} = 2644.63 \\
\text{Term 3} &= \frac{2560}{1.331} = 1923.37
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum All Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} &= 3636.36 + 2644.63 + 1923.37 \\
&= \mathbf{₹8{,}204.36}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
> This customer is worth **₹8,204.36** over 3 years. The bank can justify spending up to this amount on acquisition cost, marketing, and retention incentives without destroying value.

## 3.4 The Complete Analytical Matrix — BFSI Use Cases

| Use Case | Data Source | Analytics Technique | Business Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Credit Scoring** | Loan application forms, bureau data (CIBIL) | Logistic Regression, XGBoost, Random Forest | Approve/Decline, set interest rate |
| **Transaction Fraud** | Real-time payment stream | Anomaly Detection, Graph Neural Networks | Block suspicious transaction |
| **Anti-Money Laundering** | KYC, transaction monitoring | Network analysis, NLP on alerts | File STR with FIU-IND |
| **Customer Churn** | CRM, transaction history | Survival analysis, classification | Targeted retention campaign |
| **Algorithmic Trading** | Market tick data | Time-series, reinforcement learning | Generate buy/sell signals |
| **Loan Portfolio Risk** | Internal loan ledger | Monte Carlo simulation | Stress-test capital adequacy |
| **Wealth Advisory** | Customer holdings, market data | Markowitz portfolio optimization | Robo-advisor recommendations |
| **Insurance Claims** | Claim forms, hospital records | OCR + NLP + predictive models | Auto-adjudicate claims, detect fraud |
| **Marketing** | Web/app logs, social media | RFM, recommendation engine, NLP | Personalized cross-sell offers |
| **Cyber Threat** | Network logs, SIEM | UEBA, deep learning | Real-time intrusion detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** The same customer dataset can power *multiple* use cases. For example, a credit-card transaction stream simultaneously feeds fraud detection, churn prediction, cross-selling, and credit-limit-revision models. A complete answer should mention this **convergence of analytics on a single data asset**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Architecture of a BFSI Analytics System

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how raw data flows from the operational banking systems through analytics layers to deliver business value. This diagram is frequently asked as "Explain the architecture of analytics in banking" in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1[Core Banking System CBS] --> lake[Data Lake and Data Warehouse]
    src2[Payment Switch UPI NEFT IMPS] --> lake
    src3[CRM and Mobile App Logs] --> lake
    src4[External Bureau CIBIL Experian] --> lake
    src5[Market Data Feeds NSE BSE] --> lake
    src6[Regulatory Filings RBI SEBI] --> lake
    lake --> ingest[Ingestion Layer Kafka Sqoop]
    ingest --> process[Processing Layer Spark Hadoop]
    process --> clean[Data Cleansing and Feature Engineering]
    clean --> store[Analytical Store HDFS Redshift Snowflake]
    store --> layer1[Descriptive Layer Dashboards MIS Reports]
    store --> layer2[Diagnostic Layer Root Cause OLAP Cubes]
    store --> layer3[Predictive Layer ML Models Risk Scores]
    store --> layer4[Prescriptive Layer Optimization Reinforcement Learning]
    layer1 --> exec[Senior Management and Board]
    layer2 --> mgr[Branch and Regional Managers]
    layer3 --> anlst[Risk and Fraud Analysts]
    layer4 --> strat[Strategy and Treasury Teams]
    exec --> value1[Improved CAR and Compliance]
    mgr --> value2[Branch Level KPIs and Targets]
    anlst --> value3[Reduced NPA and Fraud Losses]
    strat --> value4[Higher ROA and Customer Lifetime Value]
```

## 4.2 The Credit Risk Analytics Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram maps the **end-to-end credit risk decision workflow** — from data collection to portfolio monitoring. It is a high-yield KTU diagram for 7-mark sub-questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Loan Application] --> B[KYC and Document Verification]
    B --> C[Data Enrichment CIBIL Bureau Pull]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering Income Age Debt Ratio]
    D --> E[Credit Scoring Model Logistic Regression XGBoost]
    E --> F{Score Threshold Check}
    F -->|Score Above Cutoff| G[Approve and Set Risk Based Pricing]
    F -->|Score Below Cutoff| H[Decline or Refer to Manual Underwriter]
    G --> I[Loan Disbursement]
    H --> J[Rejection Letter and Adverse Action Code]
    I --> K[Ongoing Behavioral Monitoring]
    K --> L[Early Warning System Score Deterioration]
    L --> M[Restructuring or Recovery Action]
    K --> N[Portfolio Stress Testing Monte Carlo]
    N --> O[Capital Adequacy and Provisioning]
```

## 4.3 The Fraud Detection Real-Time Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates **real-time fraud scoring** during a transaction. In KTU, you may be asked to draw a "fraud detection architecture" — this is the model answer template.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tx[Incoming Transaction] --> enrich[Enrich with Customer Profile Device ID Location]
    enrich --> rules[Rule Based Filters Velocity Blacklist Geo]
    rules -->|Pass| ml[ML Fraud Score Model XGBoost Neural Net]
    rules -->|Fail| block1[Auto Block and Alert]
    ml --> threshold{Score Above Fraud Threshold}
    threshold -->|Yes| review[Hold Transaction Send to Manual Review]
    threshold -->|No| approve[Approve and Process Payment]
    review --> analyst[Fraud Analyst Decision]
    analyst --> release[Release Transaction]
    analyst --> confirm[Confirm Fraud Update Model]
    confirm --> feedback[Feedback Loop to Retrain ML Model]
    block1 --> feedback
```

## 4.4 Customer Churn Analytics Lifecycle

> [!TIP]
> Often tested in KTU as "Explain the customer analytics process in banking." The diagram below shows the **CRISP-DM-like** lifecycle applied specifically to BFSI churn modelling.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Business Understanding Define Churn Definition] --> p2[Data Understanding CRM Transactions Demographics]
    p2 --> p3[Data Preparation Clean Aggregate Label Churn]
    p3 --> p4[Modelling Logistic Regression Random Forest Survival Analysis]
    p4 --> p5[Evaluation AUC Precision Recall F1 Score]
    p5 --> p6[Deployment API into CRM Real Time Scoring]
    p6 --> p7[Targeted Retention Offers Discounts Loyalty Rewards]
    p7 --> p8[Monitor Model Drift and Recalibrate Quarterly]
    p8 --> p2
```

## 4.5 Risk Management Framework — Three Lines of Defence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Three Lines of Defence** model is a regulatory expectation from **RBI, SEBI, and Basel Committee**. KTU often asks this as a 7-marker theory question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1A[Line 1 Business Units Own Risk Management] --> L2A[Line 2 Independent Risk Management and Compliance]
    L1A --> L3A[Line 3 Internal Audit Assurance]
    L2A --> L3A
    L1A --> board[Board and Risk Committee]
    L2A --> board
    L3A --> board
    board --> regulator[Regulators RBI SEBI PMLA]
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**
- **Line 1** — The business itself owns and manages day-to-day risk (e.g., branch managers checking KYC compliance).
- **Line 2** — Risk Management and Compliance functions provide independent oversight.
- **Line 3** — Internal Audit provides independent assurance to the Board.
- The **Board** supervises all three and reports to **regulators**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions are **short-answer type**. The model answer should be **3 to 4 sentences** with one example.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
Define **Analytics in Banking and Financial Services**. List any **four application areas**.

**Model Answer:**
Analytics in Banking and Financial Services refers to the systematic use of statistical, machine-learning, and data-mining techniques on financial data to generate insights for better decision-making. The four major application areas are: **(i) Credit Risk Analytics** for loan default prediction, **(ii) Fraud Detection and Anti-Money Laundering (AML)** to identify suspicious transactions, **(iii) Customer Analytics** including churn modelling and lifetime value estimation, and **(iv) Regulatory and Operational Analytics** (RegTech) for capital adequacy, stress testing, and automated compliance reporting to RBI/SEBI.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
Explain the **Expected Loss (EL)** formula used in Basel III credit risk modelling. Mention its three components.

**Model Answer:**
The Expected Loss formula is $\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$. Its three components are:
- **PD (Probability of Default):** Likelihood that the borrower defaults within one year.
- **LGD (Loss Given Default):** Proportion of the exposure that is lost if default occurs.
- **EAD (Exposure at Default):** The total outstanding amount the bank is exposed to at the time of default.

This formula is used by banks to compute the minimum capital reserves required under the **Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) approach** of Basel III.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts. KTU follows an **internal choice** pattern — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Explain the **four types of analytics** (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) with one **banking example** for each. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss the role of **Fraud Detection Analytics** in banks. Describe **any three techniques** used, with suitable examples. (7 marks)

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (a)

The four types of analytics form a **maturity pyramid**, each level building on the previous one.

1. **Descriptive Analytics — "What happened?"**
   - Uses historical data to summarize past events.
   - **Banking Example:** A dashboard showing that loan disbursements grew by 12% in Q3 FY2024, but NPAs (Non-Performing Assets) in the personal-loan segment grew by 18%.

2. **Diagnostic Analytics — "Why did it happen?"**
   - Uses drill-down, correlation, and root-cause analysis.
   - **Banking Example:** An analyst investigates and finds that the 18% NPA spike in personal loans is concentrated in the 25–30 age group in Tier-2 cities, where unemployment rose 6%.

3. **Predictive Analytics — "What will happen?"**
   - Uses statistical models and ML to forecast future events.
   - **Banking Example:** A logistic-regression credit-scoring model predicts that 4.2% of the current loan portfolio is likely to default in the next 12 months, with a total expected loss of ₹45 Crore.

4. **Prescriptive Analytics — "What should we do?"**
   - Uses optimization and simulation to recommend actions.
   - **Banking Example:** The bank's optimization engine recommends tightening credit norms for the 25–30 age segment in Tier-2 cities (reduce loan-to-value ratio from 80% to 65%) and offering pre-approved credit-card upgrades to high-CLV customers instead of fresh loans.

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (b)

**Fraud Detection Analytics** is critical because global banks lose **tens of billions of dollars annually** to card fraud, account takeover, identity theft, and money laundering. Analytics enables **real-time** and **post-transactional** detection.

**Three Key Techniques:**

1. **Rule-Based Filtering (Heuristic Engine):**
   - Hard-coded rules like *"reject if 5 transactions > ₹50,000 occur within 10 minutes from the same card in different cities."*
   - Fast, interpretable, but generates high false positives.

2. **Statistical & ML Anomaly Detection:**
   - Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, and One-Class SVM flag transactions that deviate from a customer's historical pattern.
   - **Example:** If Mr. Sharma, who normally spends ₹2,000/day on his debit card, suddenly transacts ₹3,00,000 at 3 AM in a foreign country, an Isolation Forest model flags it with a high anomaly score.

3. **Graph & Network Analytics:**
   - Builds a transaction graph (nodes = accounts; edges = money flows).
   - **Example:** Detects a money-mule ring where 50 accounts receive small deposits from 1,000 sources and then forward 95% of the money to a single offshore account within hours — a classic layering pattern in money laundering.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students write only the *names* of techniques (e.g., "uses ML") without giving a **banking example** — KTU deducts 1–2 marks for this.
> - Always include a **realistic numerical example** or scenario in part (b) to earn full marks.
> - Do not confuse **fraud detection** with **credit risk** — these are *different* analytics problems, even though both use ML.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**. Derive the CLV formula and compute it for a 3-year horizon given the data: Annual profit = ₹5,000, retention = 80%, discount rate = 10%. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss the **regulatory analytics** landscape in Indian banking with reference to **RBI, SEBI, and Basel III norms**. (7 marks)

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (a)

**Concept of CLV:**
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is the **total present value of net profit** that a customer is expected to generate over the entire duration of their relationship with the bank. It is one of the most important metrics in CRM analytics because it tells the bank *how much to spend* on customer acquisition, retention, and cross-selling.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{r_t \cdot \text{Profit}_t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

**Given Data:**
- Annual profit = ₹5,000
- Retention $r_t$: Year 1 = 1.0, Year 2 = 0.80, Year 3 = 0.64
- Discount rate $d$ = 0.10

**Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} &= \frac{1.00 \times 5000}{(1.10)^1} + \frac{0.80 \times 5000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{0.64 \times 5000}{(1.10)^3} \\
&= \frac{5000}{1.10} + \frac{4000}{1.21} + \frac{3200}{1.331} \\
&= 4545.45 + 3305.79 + 2404.21 \\
&= \mathbf{₹10{,}255.45}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The bank can spend up to ₹10,255 on this customer over 3 years (in acquisition + retention cost) without destroying value. If customer acquisition cost (CAC) is ₹2,000, the customer is highly profitable.

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (b)

**Regulatory Analytics Landscape in Indian Banking:**

1. **RBI (Reserve Bank of India) Mandates:**
   - **Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR)** must be ≥ 9% (with buffer 11.5%).
   - **Priority Sector Lending (PSL)** — 40% of bank credit to agriculture, MSMEs, and weaker sections; tracked via analytics.
   - **Stressed Assets Framework** — banks must classify loans as SMA-0, SMA-1, SMA-2, and NPA based on days-past-due analytics.
   - **KYC / AML Compliance** — risk-based customer profiling.

2. **SEBI Mandates (Capital Markets):**
   - **Algorithmic Trading Surveillance** — exchanges must run real-time analytics to detect market manipulation (pump-and-dump, spoofing).
   - **Insider Trading Detection** — pattern analytics on unusual trading volumes before corporate announcements.
   - **Investor Grievance Analytics** — tracking complaints on SCORES (SEBI Complaint Redressal System).

3. **Basel III Norms (Global, Adopted by India):**
   - **Three Pillars:** Minimum Capital Requirements, Supervisory Review, Market Discipline.
   - **LCR (Liquidity Coverage Ratio)** ≥ 100%.
   - **NSFR (Net Stable Funding Ratio)** ≥ 100%.
   - **Counter-Cyclical Capital Buffer (CCCB)** during boom periods.
   - **Leverage Ratio** ≥ 4% as a backstop to risk-weighted models.
   - **Pillar 3 Disclosures** — banks must publish detailed risk metrics on their websites for market discipline.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> - Do not write only "Basel III" generically — name **at least two specific ratios** (CAR, LCR, NSFR) to earn full marks.
> - RBI vs. SEBI distinction is often poorly answered. **RBI = Banks**, **SEBI = Stock markets/commodities**. Confusing them costs 1 mark.
> - Always mention **at least one Indian-specific mandate** (PSL, SMA, UPI analytics) to demonstrate applied knowledge.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision Before KTU ESE:**

- [ ] **BFSI Analytics** = application of statistical/ML/optimization techniques to financial data for decision-making.
- [ ] **Four Types:** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (Gartner Maturity Model).
- [ ] **Major Use Cases:** Credit Risk, Fraud/AML, Customer Analytics (Churn/CLV/RFM), RegTech, Algo Trading, Wealth Management.
- [ ] **Expected Loss Formula:** $\text{EL} = \text{PD} \times \text{LGD} \times \text{EAD}$ — cornerstone of Basel III IRB.
- [ ] **VaR Formula:** $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = (\mu - z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma) \times V$ — assumes normal distribution; for tails use CVaR.
- [ ] **CLV Formula:** $\sum \frac{r_t \cdot \text{Profit}_t}{(1+d)^t}$ — guides customer acquisition spending.
- [ ] **Sharpe Ratio:** $\frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ — measures risk-adjusted portfolio performance.
- [ ] **CAR:** $\frac{\text{Tier 1} + \text{Tier 2}}{\text{RWA}} \ge 9\%$ — RBI minimum.
- [ ] **LCR:** $\frac{\text{HQLA}}{\text{Net Cash Outflows (30d)}} \ge 100\%$ — Basel III.
- [ ] **Three Lines of Defence:** Business → Risk/Compliance → Internal Audit → Board → Regulator.
- [ ] **RBI** regulates banks; **SEBI** regulates capital markets; **PMLA** mandates AML.
- [ ] **SMAs:** SMA-0 (1–30 DPD), SMA-1 (31–60 DPD), SMA-2 (61–90 DPD), NPA (>90 DPD).
- [ ] **RFM Segmentation:** Recency, Frequency, Monetary — used for customer targeting.
- [ ] **Z-Score Anomaly Detection:** $Z = \frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$; Z > 3 = potential fraud.
- [ ] **Fraud Techniques:** Rule-based, ML anomaly detection, Graph/Network analytics.
- [ ] **Key Data Sources:** CBS, UPI/NEFT/IMPS switch, CRM, CIBIL bureau, market feeds.
- [ ] **Tools Stack:** Hadoop, Spark, Kafka, Snowflake, Neo4j, TensorFlow, Python/R, SAS.
- [ ] **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exam:**
  - Converting percentages to decimals before substitution.
  - Confusing RBI with SEBI jurisdiction.
  - Writing only "uses ML" without naming the *specific* technique.
  - Forgetting to interpret numerical answers in business context.
  - Not providing banking examples in theoretical questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** Always **structure** your answer with **headings, formulas in $...$ or $$...$$, and at least one diagram (hand-drawn mermaid-style block diagram is acceptable in handwriting mode)**. Examiners reward *clarity of structure* as much as content correctness. A 7-mark answer that is well-structured with formulas and an example can outscore a 7-mark unstructured answer that is technically superior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytics in Retail Banking and Wealth Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Retail Banking and Wealth Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Analytics in Retail Banking and Wealth Management** refers to the systematic application of descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytical techniques — using statistical modelling, machine learning, optimization, and big-data platforms — to data generated across the **retail banking value chain** (acquisition, servicing, transactions, lending, risk, and retention) and the **wealth management value chain** (client profiling, portfolio construction, advisory, and performance reporting).

Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECBT755)** definition, the discipline is positioned at the intersection of **financial services domain knowledge** and **data-driven decision science**, supporting decisions such as loan approvals, credit-limit setting, fraud blocking, customer cross-selling, wealth advisory, and regulatory capital allocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PECBT755 explicitly places banking and wealth analytics as a *vertical application* of business analytics. The learner is expected to connect **analytical methods** (regression, classification, clustering, optimization) with **banking use-cases** (credit scoring, fraud, churn, portfolio construction).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large supermarket that knows, for every customer, exactly which items they buy, when they shop, and what they might want next week. Now replace the supermarket with a **bank** and the items with **financial products** (loans, credit cards, mutual funds, insurance, fixed deposits). That is precisely what retail banking analytics does.

A simpler way to picture it:

- **Retail Banking Analytics** = The bank's *"smart brain"* for millions of ordinary customers (savings, loans, cards, payments). It decides *who deserves a loan, who is likely to default, who will churn, and what product to pitch next*.
- **Wealth Management Analytics** = The bank's *"personal doctor"* for high-net-worth individuals (HNIs). It diagnoses the client's financial health, prescribes an investment portfolio, and continuously monitors the treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English takeaway:** If a bank were a hospital, retail banking would be the **general OPD** (volume-based, standardised treatment) and wealth management would be the **specialist clinic** (relationship-based, customised treatment). Analytics is the diagnostic equipment used in both.

---

## 1.3 Three Pillars that Anchor the Topic (KTU CO Mapping)

| Pillar | What it Means in Banking | Typical KTU Course Outcome Touched |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Analytics** | Segmentation, CLV, churn, Next-Best-Offer | CO2 — Apply analytics to domain functions |
| **Risk & Credit Analytics** | PD, LGD, EAD, fraud detection, AML | CO3 — Evaluate risk with analytical models |
| **Wealth / Portfolio Analytics** | Asset allocation, Sharpe, VaR, robo-advisory | CO4 — Prescriptive analytics for finance |

---

## 1.4 Core Metrics Used in the Industry (Bolded for High Recall)

The following standard metrics are universally used by banks and asset managers. They are part of the **high-yield KTU vocabulary** for this module:

- **AUM (Assets Under Management)** — total market value of assets a bank/wealth firm manages for clients.
- **NIM (Net Interest Margin)** — difference between interest earned and interest paid, expressed as a **% of interest-earning assets**.
- **NPA (Non-Performing Asset)** — a loan where repayment is overdue beyond **90 days**.
- **CAR (Capital Adequacy Ratio)** — bank's available capital expressed as a **% of risk-weighted assets** (regulatory minimum under Basel norms is typically **8%** under Pillar 1, **10.5%+** including buffers).
- **CLV (Customer Lifetime Value)** — present value of the future cash flows a customer is expected to generate.
- **PD / LGD / EAD** — Probability of Default, Loss Given Default, Exposure at Default.
- **Sharpe Ratio** — risk-adjusted return of an investment portfolio.
- **VaR (Value at Risk)** — maximum loss over a defined horizon at a given confidence level.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Analytics Maturity Used in Banks

Banks typically evolve their analytical capability in four stages. This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

1. **Descriptive Analytics** — "What happened?"  
   Examples: dashboard showing daily branch footfall, monthly NPA reports, MIS reports.  
   Techniques: SQL, dashboards, OLAP cubes, summary statistics.

2. **Diagnostic Analytics** — "Why did it happen?"  
   Examples: Why did NPAs rise in a particular region? Why did a wealth client's AUM drop?  
   Techniques: correlation analysis, drill-down, root-cause analysis, regression.

3. **Predictive Analytics** — "What is likely to happen?"  
   Examples: Will this applicant default? Will this customer churn? Will the market fall?  
   Techniques: logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, neural networks, time-series forecasting.

4. **Prescriptive Analytics** — "What should we do?"  
   Examples: What interest rate to offer? What asset allocation to recommend? How much capital to keep aside?  
   Techniques: optimisation (linear/quadratic programming), reinforcement learning, simulation, recommendation engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners love asking students to **classify a given banking use-case** into one of these four categories. For example, *"A model that recommends a portfolio rebalancing action to a wealth manager"* → **Prescriptive**.

---

## 2.2 Key Functional Areas Where Analytics is Deployed

### 2.2.1 Retail Banking Analytics Use-Cases

| Functional Area | Analytical Question | Typical Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Acquisition** | Which channel gives the most profitable customers? | Attribution modelling, propensity scoring |
| **Credit Scoring** | Will this loan applicant default in 12 months? | Logistic regression, gradient boosting |
| **Cross-Selling** | What is the *next-best-product* for this customer? | Recommendation engines, association rules |
| **Fraud Detection** | Is this transaction legitimate? | Anomaly detection, supervised classifiers |
| **Anti-Money Laundering (AML)** | Does this transaction pattern suggest layering? | Network analytics, graph algorithms |
| **Churn Management** | Which customers are about to leave? | Survival analysis, classification |
| **Customer Segmentation** | Who are my high-value vs. low-value customers? | K-means clustering, RFM analysis |
| **Channel Optimisation** | Should we route this query to app, call-centre, or branch? | Multi-armed bandit, NLP routing |

### 2.2.2 Wealth Management Analytics Use-Cases

| Functional Area | Analytical Question | Typical Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Client Risk Profiling** | What is the right asset allocation for this HNI? | Rule engines + ML hybrid |
| **Portfolio Construction** | What mix maximises return for given risk? | Mean-variance optimisation (Markowitz) |
| **Robo-Advisory** | Can an algorithm give sound advice at low cost? | Goal-based planning algorithms |
| **Performance Attribution** | Why did the portfolio underperform? | Brinson-Fachler model |
| **Tax-Loss Harvesting** | Which holdings should be sold to reduce tax? | Optimisation with tax constraints |
| **Client Churn in Wealth** | Is the HNI about to move to a competitor? | Behavioural analytics, NRI monitoring |
| **Market Risk Measurement** | What is the worst 1-day loss on this portfolio? | VaR, CVaR, Monte Carlo simulation |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable, **directly tested** in KTU Board examinations, and presented using `\vert` (not `|` ) to keep the markdown tables intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $$CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \cdot r_t}{(1+d)^t}$$ | $d$ = annual discount rate; $r_t$ = retention prob. in year $t$; $T$ = horizon in years; result in **currency units**. |
| 2 | **Churn Rate** | $$Churn\% = \frac{Customers\;Lost\;in\;Period}{Customers\;at\;Start\;of\;Period} \times 100$$ | Dimensionless **%**; period usually monthly/quarterly. |
| 3 | **Retention Rate** | $$Retention = 1 - Churn\%$$ | Decimal (0 to 1) or %. |
| 4 | **Expected Credit Loss (ECL)** | $$ECL = PD \times LGD \times EAD$$ | All three in decimals/%; result in **currency units**. |
| 5 | **Net Interest Margin (NIM)** | $$NIM\% = \frac{Interest\;Income - Interest\;Expense}{Average\;Interest\;Earning\;Assets} \times 100$$ | Expressed as a **%** of assets. |
| 6 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $$Sharpe = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$$ | $R_p$ = portfolio return; $R_f$ = risk-free return; $\sigma_p$ = portfolio std. dev.; dimensionless. |
| 7 | **Portfolio Variance (2 assets)** | $$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$$ | $w_i$ = weights, $\sigma_i$ = std. dev., $\rho$ = correlation. |
| 8 | **Value at Risk (Parametric, Normal)** | $$VaR_{\alpha} = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V - \mu \cdot V$$ | $Z_{\alpha}$ = standard-normal quantile (e.g., 1.645 for 95%); $V$ = portfolio value; loss in **currency units**. |
| 9 | **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** | $$CAGR = \left(\frac{Ending\;Value}{Beginning\;Value}\right)^{1/n} - 1$$ | $n$ = number of years; expressed as decimal or %. |
| 10 | **Net Present Value (NPV) of a wealth product** | $$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$ | $r$ = discount rate; $CF_t$ = cash flow; currency units. |
| 11 | **RFM Monetary Score (normalised)** | $$M_{norm} = \frac{M - M_{min}}{M_{max} - M_{min}}$$ | Ranged **[0, 1]**, dimensionless. |
| 12 | **RFM Composite Score** | $$RFM_{score} = \alpha R + \beta F + \gamma M$$ | $\alpha+\beta+\gamma = 1$ weights; dimensionless. |
| 13 | **Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR)** | $$CAR\% = \frac{Tier\;1\;Capital + Tier\;2\;Capital}{Risk\;Weighted\;Assets} \times 100$$ | Basel III minimum = **8%** (Pillar 1); result in **%**. |
| 14 | **Propensity-to-Buy Score (Logistic)** | $$P(Y=1 \mid X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_p X_p)}}$$ | Probability ∈ **[0, 1]**, dimensionless. |
| 15 | **Lift (Model Evaluation)** | $$Lift = \frac{Response\;Rate\;in\;Top\;Decile}{Overall\;Response\;Rate}$$ | Dimensionless ratio; > 1 means model is better than random. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Banking Industry** — Loss reduction (fraud, NPA), revenue growth (cross-sell), regulatory compliance (Basel, IFRS 9).
- **Fintech / Neo-Banks** — Instant credit decisions, embedded finance, dynamic risk pricing.
- **Wealth & Asset Management** — Algorithmic portfolio rebalancing, personalised advisory, performance attribution.
- **Insurance (cross-sell)** — Bundling analytics: a customer with home loan is a high-propensity buyer of home insurance.
- **RegTech** — Using analytics to monitor capital adequacy, stress-test balance sheets, and automate compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> The same CLV / churn / propensity formulas are *universal* — they appear in telecom, e-commerce, and subscription businesses too. KTU examiners **love** cross-domain reasoning in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

> **Problem (Typical 7-mark KTU style):**  
> A retail bank acquires a salaried customer. The expected annual revenue from the customer is **₹15,000** and the annual servicing cost is **₹4,000**. The retention probability in each of the next 4 years is **80%**. The bank's discount rate is **10% p.a.** Compute the CLV.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters**

$$
Revenue = 15{,}000,\quad Cost = 4{,}000,\quad Margin_t = R - C = 11{,}000
$$

$$
r_t = (0.8)^{t},\quad d = 0.10,\quad T = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Build the CLV summation**

$$
CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{4} \frac{Margin_t \cdot (0.8)^{t}}{(1.10)^{t}}
$$

Wait — note the conventional simplification. The retention probability $r_t$ is the **probability the customer is still present in year $t$**, and the discount factor $(1+d)^t$ deflates future cash to present. The two effects are usually combined:

$$
CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{4} \frac{11{,}000 \cdot (0.8)^{t}}{(1.10)^{t}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate year by year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t=0 &: \quad \frac{11{,}000 \cdot (0.8)^{0}}{(1.10)^{0}} = \frac{11{,}000 \cdot 1}{1} = 11{,}000 \\
t=1 &: \quad \frac{11{,}000 \cdot 0.8}{1.10} = \frac{8{,}800}{1.10} = 8{,}000.00 \\
t=2 &: \quad \frac{11{,}000 \cdot 0.64}{1.21} = \frac{7{,}040}{1.21} = 5{,}818.18 \\
t=3 &: \quad \frac{11{,}000 \cdot 0.512}{1.331} = \frac{5{,}632}{1.331} = 4{,}231.40 \\
t=4 &: \quad \frac{11{,}000 \cdot 0.4096}{1.4641} = \frac{4{,}505.6}{1.4641} = 3{,}077.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the discounted cash flows**

$$
CLV = 11{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 5{,}818.18 + 4{,}231.40 + 3{,}077.55 = 32{,}127.13
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Answer (Final):**  
> **CLV ≈ ₹32,127.13**  
> *Interpretation:* Acquiring this customer is profitable as long as the bank's customer-acquisition cost (CAC) is below **₹32,127**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Expected Credit Loss (ECL)

> **Problem:** A bank has a portfolio of **5,000 personal loans**. The average Exposure at Default (EAD) per loan is **₹2,00,000**. The historical Probability of Default (PD) is **3%** and the Loss Given Default (LGD) is **60%**. Compute the Expected Credit Loss.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters**

$$
PD = 0.03,\quad LGD = 0.60,\quad EAD = 2{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ECL formula per loan**

$$
ECL_{loan} = PD \times LGD \times EAD = 0.03 \times 0.60 \times 2{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the multiplication**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ECL_{loan} &= 0.018 \times 2{,}00{,}000 \\
           &= 3{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Scale to portfolio (5,000 loans)**

$$
ECL_{portfolio} = 5{,}000 \times 3{,}600 = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** **ECL = ₹1.8 crore** for the portfolio.  
> *Bank implication:* The bank must set aside at least **₹1.8 crore** as provision, in line with **IFRS 9 / Ind AS 109** expected-credit-loss framework.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Sharpe Ratio for a Wealth Portfolio

> **Problem:** A wealth manager's portfolio delivered a return of **14%** last year. The risk-free rate was **6%**. The portfolio's standard deviation of returns was **12%**. Compute the Sharpe Ratio and interpret.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters**

$$
R_p = 0.14,\quad R_f = 0.06,\quad \sigma_p = 0.12
$$

**Step 2 — Compute excess return**

$$
Excess\;Return = R_p - R_f = 0.14 - 0.06 = 0.08
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by volatility**

$$
Sharpe = \frac{0.08}{0.12} = 0.6667
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** **Sharpe Ratio ≈ 0.67**  
> *Interpretation:* For every 1% of volatility (risk) taken, the portfolio earned **0.67% of excess return** over the risk-free asset. A Sharpe > 1 is considered good; > 2 is excellent; < 0 is unacceptable.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Portfolio Variance (2 Assets)

> **Problem:** A wealth client has ₹60,000 in Asset A and ₹40,000 in Asset B. Standard deviations: $\sigma_A = 15\%$, $\sigma_B = 10\%$. Correlation $\rho_{AB} = 0.3$. Compute the portfolio variance and standard deviation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute weights**

$$
w_A = 0.60,\quad w_B = 0.40
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the two-asset variance formula**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= (0.60)^2(0.15)^2 + (0.40)^2(0.10)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(0.15)(0.10) \\
           &= (0.36)(0.0225) + (0.16)(0.0100) + (0.144)(0.0045) \\
           &= 0.008100 + 0.001600 + 0.000648 \\
           &= 0.010348
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root for standard deviation**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.010348} = 0.1017 = 10.17\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\sigma_p^2 = 0.01035$, $\sigma_p = 10.17\%$  
> *Insight:* Diversification brought the portfolio's risk from 15% (Asset A alone) down to **10.17%**, demonstrating the **core principle of modern portfolio theory** that KTU expects you to articulate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Banking Use-Case → Method → Outcome

| Use-Case | Data Required | Analytical Method | Business Outcome | KTU Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Credit card fraud | Transaction logs, device ID, geo-location | Isolation Forest, XGBoost classifier | Real-time block of suspicious txn | Apply / Analyse |
| Personal loan default | Demographics, bureau score, income | Logistic regression, decision tree | Approve / reject / price loan | Apply |
| Customer churn in savings | Tenure, balance trend, complaint count | Survival analysis, gradient boosting | Retention campaign targeting | Analyse |
| Wealth client risk profile | Age, income, goals, horizon | Rule engine + clustering | Suggested asset allocation | Apply |
| Portfolio rebalancing | Holdings, returns, volatility | Mean-variance optimisation | New weights mailed to advisor | Evaluate |
| AML monitoring | Transaction network, KYC data | Graph algorithms, anomaly detection | Suspicious-activity report | Evaluate |
| Branch staffing | Footfall, time-of-day, seasonality | Time-series ARIMA / Prophet | Optimal staff roster | Create |
| Robo-advisory | Goals, risk tolerance, horizon | Goal-based optimisation | Personalised investment plan | Create |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Bloom's Mapping:** The "Apply" / "Analyse" levels dominate Module 2. Students who can correctly *classify* a use-case into the right column above typically score full marks on the 14-mark "explain with example" questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
    exec1[Branch Manager]
    exec2[Credit Officer]

    src1 --> lake

    use5 --> exec1

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagram shows a typical four-layer analytics architecture used in retail banks. Data flows **up** from sources, gets refined in the data lake, processed through the four analytical layers, and finally delivered as **consumable insights** to the right business executive.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Wealth Management Analytics Workflow (Client Journey)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HNI Client Onboarding] --> B[KYC and Risk Profiling]
    B --> C[Goal Discovery - Retirement - Education - Legacy]
    C --> D[Analytical Asset Allocation]
    D --> E[Portfolio Construction - Markowitz Model]
    E --> F[Execution and Order Routing]
    F --> G[Continuous Monitoring - Performance and Risk]
    G --> H{Alerts Triggered?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Rebalancing Recommendation]
    H -- No --> J[Periodic Review]
    I --> E
    J --> G
    G --> K[Tax Optimisation and Reporting]
    K --> L[Client Reporting Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** This is a **closed-loop** workflow. The client does not get a static portfolio — the analytics engine constantly monitors, rebalances, and reports. The arrow **back to E** is the "feedback loop" that examiners love to mention as evidence of prescriptive analytics.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Flow for Credit Scoring (Predictive Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[New Loan Application Received] --> B[Data Extraction - Bureau - Income - Demographics]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Logistic Regression Probability]
    D --> E{Predicted PD}
    E -- Low PD Below 2% --> F[Auto Approve]
    E -- Medium PD 2 to 8% --> G[Manual Underwriter Review]
    E -- High PD Above 8% --> H[Decline or Reprice with Higher Rate]
    F --> I[Sanction Letter Generated]
    G --> J{Underwriter Decision}
    J -- Approve --> I
    J -- Decline --> H
    H --> K[Rejection Letter with Adverse Action Reason]
    I --> L[Disbursement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The thresholds (2% and 8%) are illustrative. Real banks calibrate these from historical default data using ROC analysis. KTU examiners often give such diagrams and ask students to *explain the role of analytics at each step*.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — RFM Segmentation Process for Retail Banking

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Transaction Data] --> B[Compute Recency - Days Since Last Transaction]
    A --> C[Compute Frequency - Number of Transactions per Quarter]
    A --> D[Compute Monetary - Average Ticket Size]
    B --> E[Score 1 to 5 using Quintiles]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Concatenate R F M Labels e.g. 5 4 3]
    F --> G{Cluster Membership}
    G -- Champions --> H[Premium Rewards and Wealth Pitch]
    G -- At Risk --> I[Win Back Campaign]
    G -- New Customers --> J[Activation and Cross Sell]
    G -- Hibernating --> K[Dormancy Communication]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** RFM is a **diagnostic-then-prescriptive** pattern. The clustering (diagnostic) feeds directly into the campaign selection (prescriptive). This is a very common 7-mark KTU descriptive question.
