<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Customer Lifetime Value (CLV). List any four components that drive CLV in a retail banking context.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** is the **present value of the future net cash flows** that a customer is expected to generate over the entire duration of their relationship with the bank.
>
> Four key drivers of CLV in retail banking are:
>
> 1. **Average revenue per customer** (interest earned, fees, FX margin).
> 2. **Servicing cost per customer** (branch, call-centre, digital costs).
> 3. **Retention probability** (1 minus churn rate) over each future year.
> 4. **Discount rate** (cost of funds / opportunity cost of capital).
>
> *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each × 4 components = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between descriptive and prescriptive analytics in the context of wealth management. Give one example of each.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Descriptive Analytics | Prescriptive Analytics |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Question Answered** | What happened / what is the current state? | What should we do to optimise an outcome? |
> | **Nature** | Backward-looking, summarising | Forward-looking, recommending actions |
> | **Example in Wealth** | A monthly dashboard showing portfolio returns over the last 12 months | An engine that recommends a rebalanced asset mix to maximise Sharpe Ratio for a client with a stated risk profile |
>
> *(1.5 marks for the differentiation + 1.5 marks for the examples = 3 marks)*
>
> > [!WARNING]
> > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *predictive* with *prescriptive*. Remember: *predicting* the S&P 500 will fall 5% next month is **predictive analytics**; deciding to *shift the portfolio to 70% bonds* in response is **prescriptive analytics**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail how analytics is applied in credit scoring for retail loans. Describe the data sources, the typical model architecture, and how the probability of default (PD) is operationalised in loan decisioning.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Credit scoring is the **canonical predictive-analytics use-case in retail banking**. It converts a loan application into a numeric probability of default that drives an automated decision.

**1. Data Sources (2 marks):**
- **Applicant demographics** — age, marital status, residential stability.
- **Financial data** — declared income, existing obligations, bank-statement aggregates.
- **Bureau data** — credit history, prior defaults, enquiry count, credit-utilisation ratio.
- **Behavioural data** — internal transaction patterns (UPI / debit-card usage), savings account vintage.
- **Macroeconomic context** — unemployment rate in the applicant's pin-code.

**2. Model Architecture (3 marks):**
- A **logistic regression** baseline because of regulatory interpretability (banks must often justify a rejection to the applicant).
- **Gradient boosting** (XGBoost / LightGBM) for higher accuracy, accepted with SHAP / LIME explainability.
- Feature engineering converts raw fields into **WOE (Weight of Evidence)** bins for logistic regression, and into **raw + interaction features** for tree models.
- The model is **trained on 24–36 months of historical loans labelled as default / non-default** and validated on a holdout set using AUC, KS statistic, and Gini coefficient.

**3. Operationalising PD (2 marks):**
- The output is a **probability score between 0 and 1**.
- Banks set **PD cut-offs (score bands)** for auto-approve, manual review, and decline.
- PD is multiplied with **LGD × EAD** to compute **Expected Credit Loss (ECL)**, which becomes the **provisioning number** under IFRS 9.
- PD also feeds into **risk-based pricing** — higher PD applicants are charged a higher interest rate to compensate for the higher expected loss.

---

**(b) A bank's portfolio has average PD = 4%, LGD = 55%, and EAD = ₹3,00,000 per loan. There are 8,000 such loans. Compute the total Expected Credit Loss. If the bank can reduce PD to 2% by spending ₹2 crore on a more rigorous verification process, should the bank invest? Justify with calculation.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute current ECL (2 marks)**

$$
ECL_{current} = PD \times LGD \times EAD \times N
$$

$$
= 0.04 \times 0.55 \times 3{,}00{,}000 \times 8{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply step-by-step (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ECL_{current} &= 0.022 \times 3{,}00{,}000 \times 8{,}000 \\
              &= 6{,}600 \times 8{,}000 \\
              &= 5{,}28{,}00{,}000 \\
              &= ₹5.28 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute new ECL after intervention (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ECL_{new} &= 0.02 \times 0.55 \times 3{,}00{,}000 \times 8{,}000 \\
          &= 0.011 \times 3{,}00{,}000 \times 8{,}000 \\
          &= 3{,}300 \times 8{,}000 \\
          &= 2{,}64{,}00{,}000 \\
          &= ₹2.64 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute net benefit and decide (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Loss\;Saved &= ECL_{current} - ECL_{new} \\
            &= 5.28 - 2.64 = ₹2.64 \text{ crore} \\
Net\;Benefit &= Loss\;Saved - Verification\;Cost \\
            &= 2.64 - 2.00 = ₹0.64 \text{ crore positive}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Yes, the bank should invest the **₹2 crore**, because the net expected loss reduction is **₹64 lakh positive**. The intervention not only pays for itself but also creates a **risk-adjusted return on capital** improvement.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the role of analytics in wealth management. Cover client profiling, portfolio construction, and performance attribution with suitable analytical techniques for each stage.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Wealth management is **relationship-driven, not volume-driven**. Analytics amplifies the relationship manager's capability.

**1. Client Profiling (2 marks):**
- **Inputs:** age, net worth, income, liabilities, family situation, investment experience, time horizon, liquidity needs, tax bracket, and stated risk tolerance.
- **Technique:** A **rule-engine** combined with **clustering (K-means)** to discover natural client archetypes (e.g., *Conservative Retiree*, *Aggressive Accumulator*, *Legacy Builder*).
- **Output:** A **risk-profile label** (Conservative / Moderate / Aggressive) and an **investment policy statement (IPS)** that drives all subsequent portfolio decisions.

**2. Portfolio Construction (3 marks):**
- **Inputs:** risk-profile, capital-market assumptions (expected returns, volatilities, correlations), constraints (liquidity, tax, ethical screens).
- **Technique:** **Mean-variance optimisation (Markowitz, 1952)** to find weights that maximise expected return for a given variance. Variants include **Black-Litterman** (incorporating the manager's views) and **risk-parity** (equal risk contribution).
- **Modern practice:** Use **Monte Carlo simulation** to test whether the recommended portfolio will likely meet the client's *goal* (e.g., ₹5 crore at age 60) under thousands of random market paths.
- **Output:** A **target asset allocation** (e.g., 50% equity, 30% debt, 15% gold, 5% alternatives) and a rebalancing policy.

**3. Performance Attribution (2 marks):**
- **Inputs:** portfolio returns, benchmark returns, sector/asset-class returns.
- **Technique:** **Brinson-Fachler model** decomposes active return into **allocation effect** (did we over-weight the right sectors?) and **selection effect** (did we pick the right securities within each sector?).
- **Output:** An attribution report the relationship manager uses in client meetings to explain *why* the portfolio beat or lagged the benchmark.

---

**(b) A portfolio delivered 18% return last year. The risk-free rate was 5%. The portfolio's standard deviation was 14%. The benchmark delivered 12% return with 10% standard deviation. Compute the Sharpe Ratio of the portfolio and the Information Ratio versus the benchmark. Comment on the quality of active management.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs (1 mark)**

$$
R_p = 0.18,\quad R_f = 0.05,\quad \sigma_p = 0.14
$$

$$
R_b = 0.12,\quad \sigma_b = 0.10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Sharpe Ratio (2 marks)**

$$
Sharpe_p = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p} = \frac{0.18 - 0.05}{0.14} = \frac{0.13}{0.14} = 0.929
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Tracking Error (2 marks)**

> Note: Since individual returns of the portfolio and benchmark over time are not given, we approximate the tracking-error-style deviation using the difference in standard deviation as a simplifying assumption. (In a KTU exam, students may use the formula below.)

$$
TE = \sqrt{\sigma_p^2 + \sigma_b^2 - 2\rho_{pb}\sigma_p\sigma_b}
$$

> Assuming correlation $\rho_{pb} = 0.9$ (typical for an active manager vs. benchmark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TE &= \sqrt{0.0196 + 0.0100 - 2(0.9)(0.14)(0.10)} \\
   &= \sqrt{0.0296 - 0.0252} \\
   &= \sqrt{0.0044} \\
   &= 0.0663
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Information Ratio (2 marks)**

$$
IR = \frac{R_p - R_b}{TE} = \frac{0.18 - 0.12}{0.0663} = \frac{0.06}{0.0663} = 0.905
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
>
> - **Sharpe ≈ 0.93** — the portfolio is providing roughly 0.93 units of excess return per unit of total risk. Modest but positive.
> - **Information Ratio ≈ 0.90** — the manager is generating 0.90 units of active return per unit of active risk. An IR > 0.5 is considered good, > 1.0 is excellent.
> - **Conclusion:** The active manager has delivered **meaningful risk-adjusted outperformance**, but the Sharpe is below 1.0, suggesting there is room to improve the risk efficiency of the portfolio.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing PD with ECL** — PD is a probability (dimensionless, 0–1). ECL is in **currency units**. Mixing them up costs full marks in credit-risk questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor in CLV** — A common error is to sum nominal cash flows without dividing by $(1+d)^t$. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Writing "predictive" instead of "prescriptive"** — "Predicts churn" ≠ "Recommends retention action". Vocabulary precision is heavily rewarded.
> 4. **Skipping the decision step in numericals** — A 7-mark numerical without a *concluding recommendation* (approve / reject / invest / don't invest) loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No unit / dimensionality mention** — Always write "₹5.28 crore" not just "5.28". Unit awareness is a soft skill examiners reward.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2, PECBT755**

- **Retail banking analytics** = applying the four-stage model (descriptive → diagnostic → predictive → prescriptive) to retail financial products.
- **Wealth management analytics** = same four-stage model applied to **client-specific portfolio decisions**.
- **CLV formula** combines margin × retention × discount factor — make sure to use the **discount factor $(1+d)^t$**.
- **ECL = PD × LGD × EAD** is the **single most-tested formula** in this module.
- **Sharpe Ratio** = excess return / volatility; used to evaluate a wealth manager's risk-adjusted performance.
- **Information Ratio** = active return / tracking error; used to evaluate active management *against a benchmark*.
- **RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)** is the simplest segmentation framework and a **favourite descriptive-analytics KTU question**.
- **Markowitz mean-variance optimisation** is the backbone of portfolio construction in wealth management.
- **Next-Best-Offer (NBO)** analytics drives cross-sell in retail banking — usually a propensity-classification model.
- **Fraud detection** uses **anomaly detection + supervised classification** — distinguish between *behavioural* and *identity* fraud.
- **AML** is a **graph / network analytics** problem, not just a classification problem.
- **Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR)** minimum under Basel is **8% Pillar 1**, with buffers taking it to **10.5%+**.
- **Bloom's levels** — Module 2 emphasises *Apply* and *Analyse*; Part B questions often reach *Evaluate*.
- **Always end numericals with a managerial decision** — *approve, reject, invest, rebalance, or target for retention*.
- **Vocabulary precision** — *predictive* ≠ *prescriptive*; *PD* ≠ *ECL*; *Sharpe* (absolute) ≠ *Information Ratio* (vs. benchmark).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Planning and Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Demand Planning and Forecasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Demand Planning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Demand Planning** is the supply chain management process of forecasting, planning, and managing demand for products and services to ensure that the supply chain is efficiently aligned to meet customer demand while minimizing costs and maximizing customer satisfaction.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Demand Planning is treated as a **business analytics discipline** that integrates:
- **Statistical forecasting models**
- **Market intelligence**
- **Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)**
- **Collaborative inputs from stakeholders** (suppliers, distributors, sales teams)

### 1.2 Forecasting — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Forecasting** is the process of making predictions about future demand based on historical data, market analysis, and statistical/qualitative modeling techniques. The unit of output is typically *units demanded per time period* (e.g., pieces/month).

Forecasting is the **analytical engine** that drives Demand Planning.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Weatherman vs. Shopkeeper"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Forecasting as predicting the weather and Demand Planning as planning a picnic.**
>
> - The **Weatherman (Forecaster)** says: *"There is an 80% chance of rain this Saturday."*
> - The **Picnic Organizer (Demand Planner)** then decides: *Should we book the indoor hall? Should we keep umbrellas ready? Should we order more food or less?*
>
> The forecast (data + model) is useless on its own. Demand Planning converts that forecast into **actionable business decisions** — inventory, workforce, production, and procurement plans.

### 1.4 Core Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Demand** | The quantity of a product/service customers are willing to buy at a given price and time |
| **Forecast** | A statement about predicted future demand |
| **Forecast Horizon** | The time period into the future for which the forecast is made |
| **Forecast Error** | The deviation between actual demand and forecasted demand |
| **S&OP** | Sales and Operations Planning — a monthly cross-functional planning process |
| **CPFR** | Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment |
| **Bias** | A systematic tendency to over-forecast or under-forecast |
| **Lead Time** | Time between placing an order and receiving it |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Forecasting Methods

Forecasting methods are broadly classified into **two super-categories**:

#### A. Qualitative Methods (Judgmental)
Used when **historical data is scarce or unavailable**, e.g., for new products.
- **Executive Opinions (Jury of Experts)**
- **Sales Force Composite** — aggregating estimates from the field sales team
- **Delphi Method** — iterative anonymous expert consensus
- **Market Research & Customer Surveys**
- **Focus Groups**

#### B. Quantitative Methods (Statistical)
Used when **sufficient historical numerical data exists**.

**B.1 Time-Series Models** — forecast based on patterns in past demand over time.
- **Naive Forecast:** $F_{t+1} = D_t$ (tomorrow's forecast = today's actual)
- **Simple Moving Average (SMA):**
- **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)**
- **Exponential Smoothing (Simple)**
- **Holt's Linear Trend Method (Double Exponential Smoothing)**
- **Winter's Method (Triple Exponential Smoothing) — for seasonality**

**B.2 Causal / Associative Models** — forecast based on cause-effect relationships.
- **Linear Regression (Least Squares)**
- **Multiple Regression**
- **Econometric Models**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE papers.

$$
F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2} + \cdots + D_{t-n+1}}{n}
$$

This is the **n-period Simple Moving Average (SMA)**. Each of the previous $n$ demand values is given **equal weight** $= \dfrac{1}{n}$.

$$
F_{t+1} = w_1 D_t + w_2 D_{t-1} + w_3 D_{t-2} + \cdots + w_n D_{t-n+1}
$$

This is the **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** where the sum of all weights must equal 1:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1
$$

The **Simple Exponential Smoothing** formula:

$$
F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t
$$

where $0 < \alpha < 1$ is the **smoothing constant**.

The **Holt's Linear Trend Model** (for data with trend, no seasonality):

$$
L_t = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})
$$
$$
T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta)T_{t-1}
$$
$$
F_{t+p} = L_t + p \cdot T_t
$$

The **Linear Trend Regression** (Least Squares):

$$
\hat{Y} = a + bX
$$
$$
b = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}
$$
$$
a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}
$$

### 2.3 Forecast Accuracy / Error Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The three metrics you must know for KTU 2024 are **MAD, MSE, and MAPE**.

$$
\text{MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation)} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert D_t - F_t \vert}{n}
$$

$$
\text{MSE (Mean Squared Error)} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} (D_t - F_t)^2}{n}
$$

$$
\text{MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{D_t - F_t}{D_t} \right\vert \times 100
$$

**Tracking Signal** — checks for bias in forecasts:

$$
\text{Tracking Signal} = \frac{\text{Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)}}{\text{MAD}}
$$

A tracking signal between **$-4$ and $+4$** indicates the forecast is in control (with the **MAD-based control limits**).

### 2.4 Quick Formula Reference Table

| # | Method | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naive | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | No trend, no seasonality, baseline |
| 2 | Simple Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}$ | Stable demand, no trend |
| 3 | Weighted Moving Avg | $F_{t+1} = \sum w_i D_{t-i+1},\ \sum w_i = 1$ | Recent data more relevant |
| 4 | Exponential Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ | Stable demand, give more weight to recent |
| 5 | Holt's Linear | $F_{t+p} = L_t + p \cdot T_t$ | Trend present, no seasonality |
| 6 | Linear Regression | $\hat{Y} = a + bX$ | Causal / trend relationship |
| 7 | MAD | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert D_t - F_t \vert$ | Error measurement |
| 8 | MAPE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{D_t - F_t}{D_t}\right\vert \times 100$ | Relative error % |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Retail (Amazon, Walmart):** Demand forecasting drives inventory levels in fulfillment centers.
- **Manufacturing (Toyota, Tata Steel):** Forecasts feed MRP (Material Requirements Planning) systems.
- **Banking & Finance:** Forecasting loan defaults, customer churn, deposit volumes.
- **Healthcare:** Forecasting patient admissions for hospital staffing.
- **Hospitality:** Forecasting occupancy to optimize pricing and staffing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Moving Average (3-Period)

**Problem:** The quarterly demand for a product over the last 5 quarters is given below. Compute the 3-period Simple Moving Average forecast and the MAD.

| Quarter ($t$) | Actual Demand $D_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 100 |
| 2 | 110 |
| 3 | 120 |
| 4 | 130 |
| 5 | 140 |

**Step 1:** Identify when the forecast can start. With a 3-period SMA, the first forecast is for $t = 4$ using $t = 1, 2, 3$.

**Step 2:** Calculate the forecast for $t = 4$:

$$
F_4 = \frac{D_1 + D_2 + D_3}{3} = \frac{100 + 110 + 120}{3} = \frac{330}{3} = 110
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the forecast for $t = 5$:

$$
F_5 = \frac{D_2 + D_3 + D_4}{3} = \frac{110 + 120 + 130}{3} = \frac{360}{3} = 120
$$

**Step 4:** Compute forecast errors $e_t = D_t - F_t$ and absolute errors:

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | Error $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 130 | 110 | 20 | 20 |
| 5 | 140 | 120 | 20 | 20 |

**Step 5:** Compute MAD:

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{20 + 20}{2} = 20
$$

**Step 6:** The next forecast (for $t=6$) will be:

$$
F_6 = \frac{D_3 + D_4 + D_5}{3} = \frac{120 + 130 + 140}{3} = 130
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Because demand is steadily increasing, SMA **lags** the trend — it always under-forecasts in an uptrend. This is a classic KTU conceptual question — *"Why is SMA poor for trending data?"* Answer: it gives equal weight to old data and smooths over the trend.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Simple Exponential Smoothing

**Problem:** Given the first forecast $F_1 = 50$, actual demand $D_1 = 60$, and smoothing constant $\alpha = 0.3$, compute forecasts for $t = 2, 3, 4$ given $D_2 = 58,\ D_3 = 62,\ D_4 = 65$.

**Step 1:** Update the forecast using:

$$
F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $F_2$:

$$
F_2 = 0.3 \times 60 + 0.7 \times 50 = 18 + 35 = 53
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $F_3$:

$$
F_3 = 0.3 \times 58 + 0.7 \times 53 = 17.4 + 37.1 = 54.5
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $F_4$:

$$
F_4 = 0.3 \times 62 + 0.7 \times 54.5 = 18.6 + 38.15 = 56.75
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $F_5$ (next forecast) using $D_4 = 65$:

$$
F_5 = 0.3 \times 65 + 0.7 \times 56.75 = 19.5 + 39.725 = 59.225
$$

**Verification Table:**

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 50.00 |
| 2 | 58 | 53.00 |
| 3 | 62 | 54.50 |
| 4 | 65 | 56.75 |
| 5 | — | 59.225 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choosing $\alpha$:** A high $\alpha$ (close to 1) makes the forecast react quickly to recent changes. A low $\alpha$ (close to 0) makes the forecast stable but slow. KTU students often confuse this — remember: **higher $\alpha$ = more responsive forecast**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Linear Trend Regression (Least Squares)

**Problem:** Given 5 years of sales data, find the linear trend equation and forecast for year 7.

| Year $X$ | Sales $Y$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 55 |
| 3 | 60 |
| 4 | 65 |
| 5 | 70 |

**Step 1:** Build the calculation table:

| $X$ | $Y$ | $XY$ | $X^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50 | 1 |
| 2 | 55 | 110 | 4 |
| 3 | 60 | 180 | 9 |
| 4 | 65 | 260 | 16 |
| 5 | 70 | 350 | 25 |
| $\sum X = 15$ | $\sum Y = 300$ | $\sum XY = 950$ | $\sum X^2 = 55$ |

**Step 2:** Compute $\bar{X}$ and $\bar{Y}$:

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{\sum X}{n} = \frac{15}{5} = 3
$$
$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{\sum Y}{n} = \frac{300}{5} = 60
$$

**Step 3:** Compute slope $b$:

$$
b = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2} = \frac{5(950) - (15)(300)}{5(55) - (15)^2}
$$
$$
b = \frac{4750 - 4500}{275 - 225} = \frac{250}{50} = 5
$$

**Step 4:** Compute intercept $a$:

$$
a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X} = 60 - (5)(3) = 60 - 15 = 45
$$

**Step 5:** Write the trend equation:

$$
\hat{Y} = 45 + 5X
$$

**Step 6:** Forecast for year 7:

$$
\hat{Y}_{X=7} = 45 + 5(7) = 45 + 35 = 80 \text{ units}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Computing MAPE

Given forecast errors: $e_1 = 10,\ e_2 = -5,\ e_3 = 8,\ e_4 = -3$ and actuals: $D_1 = 100,\ D_2 = 80,\ D_3 = 95,\ D_4 = 70$.

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{4} \left( \left\vert \frac{10}{100} \right\vert + \left\vert \frac{-5}{80} \right\vert + \left\vert \frac{8}{95} \right\vert + \left\vert \frac{-3}{70} \right\vert \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{4}(0.10 + 0.0625 + 0.0842 + 0.0429) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{4}(0.2896) \times 100 = 0.0724 \times 100 = 7.24\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark:** A MAPE below **10%** is considered excellent, 10–20% is good, 20–50% is acceptable, and above 50% is poor. KTU examiners love testing this interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Demand Planning Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Historical Sales Data] --> B1[Data Collection and Cleansing]
    A2[Market Intelligence] --> B1
    A3[Promotional Calendar] --> B1
    A4[Customer Inputs] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Select Forecasting Method]
    C1 --> C2[Qualitative Methods]
    C1 --> C3[Quantitative Methods]
    C2 --> D1[Generate Forecast]
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Validate with MAPE / MAD]
    E1 --> F1[Forecast Approved]
    F1 --> G1[Collaborate with S and OP]
    G1 --> H1[Procurement and Production Plans]
    H1 --> I1[Monitor Actual vs Forecast]
    I1 --> J1{Error in Control?}
    J1 -- No --> K1[Continue Process]
    J1 -- Yes --> L1[Refit Model and Recompute]
    L1 --> C1
```

### 4.2 Classification of Forecasting Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Forecasting Methods] --> nodeB[Qualitative Judgmental]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Quantitative Statistical]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Delphi Method]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Sales Force Composite]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Executive Opinions]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Market Research]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Time Series Models]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Causal Models]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Naive Forecast]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b[Moving Average]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c[Exponential Smoothing]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1d[Holt Winters]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a[Linear Regression]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b[Multiple Regression]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c[Econometric Models]
```

### 4.3 S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning) Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Demand Planning] --> nodeP2[Supply Planning]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Pre S and OP Meeting]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Executive S and OP Meeting]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Approved Plan Execution]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Monitor and Review]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When KTU asks *"Draw the S&OP cycle"*, the 5-step closed-loop above is the *board-exam gold standard*. Always close the loop with a feedback arrow from "Monitor & Review" back to "Demand Planning".

### 4.4 Forecast Error Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Compute Forecast Error] --> nodeS2{Is Error Systematic?}
    nodeS2 -- Yes --> nodeS3[Compute Tracking Signal]
    nodeS2 -- No --> nodeS4[Random Noise]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS5{TS between minus 4 and plus 4?}
    nodeS5 -- Yes --> nodeS6[Forecast is in Control]
    nodeS5 -- No --> nodeS7[Bias Detected]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Recalibrate Model]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS9[Accept Forecast]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Qualitative forecasting** is based on **judgment, opinion, intuition, and expert experience** rather than numerical data. It is used when historical data is unavailable, e.g., for new product introduction. *Example: Delphi Method, Market Research.* **(1.5 marks)**
- **Quantitative forecasting** uses **mathematical and statistical models applied to historical numerical data** to project future demand. *Example: Simple Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, Linear Regression.* **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the significance of the smoothing constant $\alpha$ in exponential smoothing? What happens when $\alpha$ approaches 0 and 1?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The smoothing constant $\alpha$ controls the **weight given to the most recent actual demand** versus past forecasted values in the smoothing equation. **(1 mark)**
- When $\alpha \to 1$: the forecast places **almost all weight on the most recent demand**, making it **highly responsive** to changes but also more volatile. **(1 mark)**
- When $\alpha \to 0$: the forecast places **very little weight on recent demand**, making it **very smooth and stable** but slow to react to changes. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

(a) *Explain the various steps involved in the demand planning process. How does Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP) integrate with demand planning?* **(7 Marks)**

**(b) The monthly demand for a product for the last 8 months is given below. Using a 3-period Moving Average, compute the forecast for months 6, 7, 8, and 9. Also compute the MAD.** **(7 Marks)**

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 200 | 220 | 210 | 230 | 240 | 250 | 235 | 245 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Steps in Demand Planning Process (7 Marks):**
1. **Data Collection:** Gather historical sales, market intelligence, customer orders, promotional plans. **(1 mark)**
2. **Demand Sensing:** Identify micro-trends in near-real-time using POS, social media, weather data. **(1 mark)**
3. **Statistical Forecasting:** Apply time-series or causal models to project baseline demand. **(1 mark)**
4. **Collaborative Forecasting:** Integrate forecasts from sales, marketing, customers (CPFR). **(1 mark)**
5. **Demand Shaping:** Adjust using pricing, promotions, and product mix decisions. **(1 mark)**
6. **S&OP Integration:** Reconcile the demand forecast with supply capacity in a cross-functional meeting to produce a unified operating plan. **(1 mark)**
7. **Execution and Monitoring:** Convert the plan into procurement, production, and inventory orders; continuously monitor forecast accuracy. **(1 mark)**

**S&OP Integration (carried within marks):** S&OP is a **monthly executive review** where the demand plan is balanced with the supply plan. Any gap (excess or shortfall) is resolved through inventory, outsourcing, or demand-shaping levers. It acts as the **governance bridge** between forecasting and execution.

---

**(b) 3-Period Moving Average (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Forecast for Month 6:

$$
F_6 = \frac{D_3 + D_4 + D_5}{3} = \frac{210 + 230 + 240}{3} = \frac{680}{3} \approx 226.67
$$

**Step 2:** Forecast for Month 7:

$$
F_7 = \frac{D_4 + D_5 + D_6}{3} = \frac{230 + 240 + 250}{3} = \frac{720}{3} = 240
$$

**Step 3:** Forecast for Month 8:

$$
F_8 = \frac{D_5 + D_6 + D_7}{3} = \frac{240 + 250 + 235}{3} = \frac{725}{3} \approx 241.67
$$

**Step 4:** Forecast for Month 9 (next period):

$$
F_9 = \frac{D_6 + D_7 + D_8}{3} = \frac{250 + 235 + 245}{3} = \frac{730}{3} \approx 243.33
$$

**Step 5:** Error Computation Table:

| Month $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert D_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | 250 | 226.67 | 23.33 |
| 7 | 235 | 240.00 | 5.00 |
| 8 | 245 | 241.67 | 3.33 |

**Step 6:** MAD:

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{23.33 + 5.00 + 3.33}{3} = \frac{31.66}{3} \approx 10.55
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students compute the forecast for month 5 (where SMA cannot be applied) and lose 1 mark. The first valid 3-period SMA forecast is **always for period $n+1$**, i.e., month 6. Always state the starting period explicitly.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

(a) *Explain the Simple Exponential Smoothing method. Why is it preferred over Moving Average for stable demand?* **(7 Marks)**

**(b) The actual demand for a product for the past 5 months is: 100, 120, 130, 125, 140. The initial forecast for month 1 was 95. Using $\alpha = 0.4$, compute the exponential smoothing forecast for months 2 to 6. Also compute the MAD.** **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Simple Exponential Smoothing — Explanation (7 Marks):**
- It is a forecasting method that updates the forecast for the next period as a **weighted average** of the **previous actual demand** and the **previous forecast**. **(2 marks)**
- Formula: $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ where $\alpha$ is the smoothing constant ($0 < \alpha < 1$). **(1 mark)**
- It requires **less historical data** — only the previous forecast and the previous actual. **(1 mark)**
- **Why preferred over Moving Average:** Older observations are not discarded but their influence **decays exponentially**, and the most recent data automatically gets higher weight. MA requires storing $n$ past values and gives them equal weight regardless of recency. **(2 marks)**
- Limitation: Cannot handle **trend or seasonality** — works only for **stationary demand** without systematic patterns. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Exponential Smoothing Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $F_1 = 95,\ \alpha = 0.4,\ (1-\alpha) = 0.6$. Data: $D_1 = 100,\ D_2 = 120,\ D_3 = 130,\ D_4 = 125,\ D_5 = 140$.

**Step 1:** Forecast for month 2:

$$
F_2 = 0.4(100) + 0.6(95) = 40 + 57 = 97
$$

**Step 2:** Forecast for month 3:

$$
F_3 = 0.4(120) + 0.6(97) = 48 + 58.2 = 106.2
$$

**Step 3:** Forecast for month 4:

$$
F_4 = 0.4(130) + 0.6(106.2) = 52 + 63.72 = 115.72
$$

**Step 4:** Forecast for month 5:

$$
F_5 = 0.4(125) + 0.6(115.72) = 50 + 69.432 = 119.432
$$

**Step 5:** Forecast for month 6:

$$
F_6 = 0.4(140) + 0.6(119.432) = 56 + 71.6592 = 127.6592
$$

**Step 6:** Compute MAD using $\vert D_t - F_t \vert$ for $t = 2,3,4,5$:

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert D_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 120 | 97.00 | 23.00 |
| 3 | 130 | 106.20 | 23.80 |
| 4 | 125 | 115.72 | 9.28 |
| 5 | 140 | 119.432 | 20.568 |

**Step 7:** MAD:

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{23.00 + 23.80 + 9.28 + 20.568}{4} = \frac{76.648}{4} = 19.162
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Two common mistakes: (i) Students often use $F_1$ and $D_1$ to compute $F_2$ correctly but **swap $\alpha$ and $(1-\alpha)$** — remember $\alpha$ multiplies the **actual** demand, not the previous forecast. (ii) Students sometimes include the initial forecast error in MAD — the **first forecast is a given, not a prediction**, so MAD starts from $t = 2$ in this case.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 10 Critical Points**

- **Demand Planning** = forecast + collaboration + execution. It is broader than forecasting.
- **Forecasting** uses past data + models to predict future demand; output is a number (units).
- **Qualitative methods** for new products, **Quantitative methods** for established products.
- **Time-series** uses only past demand values; **Causal models** use external independent variables (price, advertising, etc.).
- **Naive forecast** is $F_{t+1} = D_t$ — the simplest benchmark.
- **SMA formula:** $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum D_{t-i+1}$. **Lags trends** — never use for clearly increasing/decreasing data.
- **WMA formula:** weighted average where $\sum w_i = 1$. Recent data can be given higher weights.
- **Exponential Smoothing:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$. Higher $\alpha$ = more responsive.
- **MAD, MSE, MAPE** are the three key accuracy metrics. MAPE is in **percentage** — don't forget to multiply by 100.
- **Tracking Signal** between $\pm 4$ = forecast in control. Outside this range = bias — recalibrate the model.
- **Holt's model** = for trend. **Winter's model** = for trend + seasonality. **Simple ES** = neither.
- **S&OP** integrates demand plan with supply plan. It is the **governance layer** of Demand Planning.
- **CPFR** = collaborative forecasting shared with supply chain partners.
- In KTU numericals, **always show all intermediate substitution steps** — board examiners award step marks liberally.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Building (Analytical Modeling)** is the systematic, iterative process of selecting, training, validating, and deploying a mathematical or statistical representation of a real-world business phenomenon using historical and current data, with the objective of generating predictions, classifications, segmentations, or optimization decisions that support managerial decision-making.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, Model Building is positioned as the **core analytical engine** of the Business Analytics lifecycle. It sits between *Data Preparation* and *Model Deployment*, and it is the phase where raw, cleaned data is mathematically transformed into actionable business intelligence.

The **SAS (Statistical Analysis System) Institute's SEMMA methodology** and the industry-standard **CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** both highlight Model Building as the stage where the actual learning algorithm interacts with the prepared dataset to discover patterns, estimate parameters, and minimize a defined error function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Recipe Development" Model**
> 
> Imagine a Michelin-star chef who wants to build the *perfect* recipe for a signature dish.
> 
> - **The Recipe (Model)** is a structured set of rules: "If I add X grams of spice and cook for Y minutes at Z°C, the dish will taste a certain way."
> - **The Kitchen Experiments (Training Data)** are the hundreds of trials the chef runs, tweaking ingredients and timings.
> - **The Taste Testers (Validation Set)** are independent judges who confirm the recipe works on new, unseen occasions.
> - **The Menu Launch (Deployment)** is when the perfected recipe becomes part of the restaurant's offerings and is monitored for customer feedback.
> 
> Model building in business analytics is *exactly* this — it is the disciplined experimentation of mathematical "recipes" until the model reliably produces the right business outcomes on new, unseen data.

### 1.3 Core Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hypothesis Function** $\hat{y} = f(x, \theta)$ | The mathematical mapping the model learns from inputs to outputs |
| **Parameters** $\theta$ | The internal coefficients the model adjusts during training |
| **Training Set** | The portion of data used to fit the model parameters |
| **Test Set** | A held-out dataset used to evaluate the model's generalization capability |
| **Loss Function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ | The mathematical penalty the model minimizes during learning |
| **Hyperparameters** | Configuration values set *before* training (e.g., learning rate, tree depth) |
| **Generalization** | The ability of a model to perform well on unseen data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to clearly distinguish between *parameters* (learned from data) and *hyperparameters* (set by the analyst), as this distinction is a frequently tested concept in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Six-Phase Model Building Pipeline

Model Building is **not a single event** — it is an iterative pipeline of six tightly-coupled phases. Each phase has a clear input, output, and validation gate.

#### **Phase 1: Business Problem Translation**
The analyst converts a *business question* into a *mathematical objective*.

- **Classification Question** → "Which customers will churn?" → Predict a discrete label $y \in \{0, 1\}$
- **Regression Question** → "What will next quarter's revenue be?" → Predict a continuous value $y \in \mathbb{R}$
- **Clustering Question** → "What are our natural customer segments?" → Discover groups without labels
- **Optimization Question** → "How should we allocate the marketing budget?" → Maximize/minimize an objective function

#### **Phase 2: Model Selection**
The analyst chooses the candidate algorithm family based on the data type, volume, linearity, and interpretability requirement.

$$
\text{Model Family} = \arg\min_{M \in \mathcal{M}} \left[ \text{Interpretability}(M) - \alpha \cdot \text{Predictive Power}(M) \right]
$$

where $\alpha$ is a stakeholder-defined trade-off coefficient.

#### **Phase 3: Training (Parameter Estimation)**
The model parameters $\theta$ are learned by minimizing a loss function on the training data.

$$
\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}\left( y_i, \hat{y}_i \right)
$$

#### **Phase 4: Validation (Generalization Check)**
The model is evaluated on a held-out validation set using **k-Fold Cross-Validation** to estimate true out-of-sample performance.

#### **Phase 5: Hyperparameter Tuning**
External configuration values are optimized using Grid Search, Random Search, or Bayesian Optimization.

#### **Phase 6: Final Evaluation & Deployment Readiness**
The model is tested on a *completely untouched* test set, and metrics are reported to stakeholders.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all exam-critical mathematical formulas** for Model Building. Master these and you will secure the numerical/application marks.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hypothesis Function** | $\hat{y} = f(x, \theta)$ | The model's predicted output |
| 2 | **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Objective** | $\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2$ | Minimize sum of squared residuals |
| 3 | **OLS Closed-Form Solution** | $\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | Normal equation for linear regression |
| 4 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Squared error average; units² |
| 5 | **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same unit as $y$; primary KTU metric |
| 6 | **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers; same unit as $y$ |
| 7 | **Coefficient of Determination** $R^2$ | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Variance explained; range $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | **Adjusted $R^2$** | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$ | Penalizes extra predictors |
| 9 | **Logistic (Sigmoid) Function** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps real line to $(0, 1)$ |
| 10 | **Log-Loss (Cross-Entropy)** | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{p}_i) \right]$ | Loss for classification |
| 11 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Correct predictions ratio |
| 12 | **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| 13 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| 14 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 15 | **Bias-Variance Decomposition** | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{irred}}$ | Total expected error split |
| 16 | **Regularization (Ridge / L2)** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$ | Shrinks large coefficients |
| 17 | **Regularization (Lasso / L1)** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ | Drives coefficients to zero |
| 18 | **k-Fold CV Score** | $\text{CV}_k = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Metric}_i$ | Average across $k$ validation folds |
| 19 | **Information Gain (Entropy Reduction)** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Decision tree splitting criterion |
| 20 | **Gini Impurity** | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$ | CART splitting criterion |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Retail & E-Commerce:** Demand forecasting, dynamic pricing, recommendation engines
- **Banking & Finance:** Credit scoring, fraud detection, algorithmic trading
- **Healthcare:** Disease prediction, patient readmission risk, drug discovery
- **Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance, quality control, supply chain optimization
- **Telecommunications:** Customer churn prediction, network anomaly detection
- **Marketing:** Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) modeling, campaign response prediction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of model in industry is **not solely** based on accuracy — it is equally driven by **interpretability, computational cost, latency, and regulatory compliance** (e.g., GDPR, RBI guidelines).

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Form OLS Solution (Linear Regression)

This is a **high-frequency KTU derivation** — expect 7 marks from this in Part B.

**Starting Point:** The linear regression hypothesis is

$$
\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i
$$

**Step 1: Write the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE) as the objective function.**

The analyst wants to find $\beta_0, \beta_1$ that minimize

$$
S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right)^2
$$

**Step 2: Take the partial derivative of $S$ with respect to $\beta_0$ and set it to zero.**

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

This simplifies to the first **normal equation**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = n \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

Dividing by $n$, we obtain the *centroid relationship*:

$$
\bar{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{x}
$$

**Step 3: Take the partial derivative of $S$ with respect to $\beta_1$ and set it to zero.**

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

This simplifies to the second **normal equation**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = \beta_0 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2
$$

**Step 4: Solve the two normal equations simultaneously.**

From Step 2, substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ into Step 3:

$$
\sum x_i y_i = (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}) \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = \bar{y} \sum x_i - \beta_1 \bar{x} \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2
$$

Rearranging,

$$
\beta_1 \left( \sum x_i^2 - \bar{x} \sum x_i \right) = \sum x_i y_i - \bar{y} \sum x_i
$$

After algebraic manipulation (using $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ and $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}$):

$$
\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

**Step 5: The final closed-form expressions for the OLS estimators are:**

$$
\boxed{\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} \quad , \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}}
$$

**Step 6: Matrix Form Generalization (for multiple regression).**

For $p$ predictors, the OLS estimator in matrix notation is

$$
\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y
$$

**Logical Reasoning Behind Each Step:**

| Step | Mathematical Action | Business Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Define SSE as objective | Quantify total prediction error to minimize |
| 2 | Set $\partial S / \partial \beta_0 = 0$ | Find the intercept where the error slope is zero (optimum) |
| 3 | Set $\partial S / \partial \beta_1 = 0$ | Find the slope where the error slope is zero (optimum) |
| 4 | Solve simultaneously | The two unknowns require two independent equations |
| 5 | Express $\beta_1$ via deviations | Removes the need to know the intercept; cleaner formula |
| 6 | Matrix form | Scales to thousands of predictors efficiently |

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Expected Test Error)

This is the **conceptual cornerstone** of model building — a guaranteed KTU 14-mark question.

**Starting Point:** For any learned model $\hat{f}(x)$ on a test point $x_0$ with true target $y_0 = f(x_0) + \epsilon$ (where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$), the expected prediction error is:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 \right]
$$

**Step 1: Expand the squared error using the trick $(a + b + c)^2$ decomposition.**

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 \right] = \mathbb{E}\left[ \left( f(x_0) + \epsilon - \hat{f}(x_0) \right)^2 \right]
$$

$$
= \mathbb{E}\left[ \left( f(x_0) - \hat{f}(x_0) + \epsilon \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 2: Apply the variance-addition identity: $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2$.**

For the random variable $Z = f(x_0) - \hat{f}(x_0) + \epsilon$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[Z^2] = \text{Var}(Z) + (\mathbb{E}[Z])^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\text{Var}(Z)$.**

Since $\epsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$:

$$
\text{Var}(Z) = \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \sigma^2
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\mathbb{E}[Z]$.**

$$
\mathbb{E}[Z] = \mathbb{E}[f(x_0) - \hat{f}(x_0) + \epsilon] = f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)] + 0
$$

Define the **Bias** as:

$$
\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(x_0)) = f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)]
$$

**Step 5: Combine the components.**

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 \right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x_0))}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0))}_{\text{sensitivity to training data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}
$$

**Final Canonical Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Expected Test Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}}
$$

**Business Interpretation:**

| Term | Meaning | Management Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Bias}^2$ | Average error from wrong assumptions | A *linear* model trying to fit *non-linear* data |
| $\text{Variance}$ | Error from sensitivity to data | A *deep* tree that memorizes training noise |
| $\sigma^2$ | Inherent data randomness | Cannot be reduced; bound the achievable accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Examiner's Key Insight:** The fundamental modeling challenge is that *decreasing bias by increasing model complexity increases variance*. The optimal model minimizes the **sum** of the first two, not either one alone.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Logistic Regression Gradient (Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU occasionally asks for the *form* of the logistic regression loss and its interpretation. Full Newton-Raphson derivation is not required.

The logistic hypothesis is

$$
\hat{p}(x) = \sigma(\beta^T x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\beta^T x}}
$$

The loss function (cross-entropy) is

$$
\mathcal{L}(\beta) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log \hat{p}(x_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{p}(x_i)) \right]
$$

The gradient update rule (Gradient Descent) is

$$
\beta^{(t+1)} = \beta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}(\beta^{(t)})
$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Model Families

| Model Family | Best Use Case | Interpretability | Handles Non-Linearity | Handles Large $p$ | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Regression** | Continuous outcomes, linear relationships | Very High | No | Yes (with regularization) | Very High |
| **Logistic Regression** | Binary classification | High | No (linear decision boundary) | Yes | Very High |
| **Decision Tree (CART)** | Mixed data, business rules | High | Yes | Moderate | High |
| **Random Forest** | High-accuracy classification/regression | Low | Yes | Yes | Moderate |
| **K-Nearest Neighbors** | Small datasets, pattern matching | Medium | Yes | No (curse of dimensionality) | Moderate |
| **K-Means Clustering** | Customer segmentation | Medium | Yes (spherical clusters) | Moderate | High |
| **Naive Bayes** | Text classification, spam filtering | High | No (conditional independence) | Yes | Moderate |
| **ARIMA** | Time series forecasting | High | Partial (linear) | No | Moderate |

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 The CRISP-DM Model Building Process (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Business Understanding] --> B[Phase 2: Data Understanding]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Data Preparation]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Model Building]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Model Evaluation]
    E --> F{Performance Acceptable?}
    F -- No --> G[Phase 6a: Iterate and Retune]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Phase 6b: Deployment and Monitoring]
    H --> I[Continuous Feedback Loop]
    I --> C
```

**Explanation of Flow:**

- **Phase 1 (Business Understanding)** — Translate the business question into a quantifiable objective (e.g., "Reduce churn by 15%").
- **Phase 2 (Data Understanding)** — Perform Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), check distributions, missing values, correlations.
- **Phase 3 (Data Preparation)** — Clean, impute, encode, scale, and engineer features. This phase consumes **60–70%** of the analyst's time.
- **Phase 4 (Model Building)** — The heart of the process. Train candidate models.
- **Phase 5 (Model Evaluation)** — Validate using test set and business KPIs.
- **Phase 6 (Deployment)** — Two possible sub-paths: iterate OR deploy.
- **Continuous Loop** — Models degrade over time (concept drift); feedback is critical.

### 4.2 The Model Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the target variable labeled?}
    Q1 -- No --> C[Use Clustering Models: K-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the target continuous or categorical?}
    Q2 -- Continuous --> Q3{Is relationship linear?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R1[Linear Regression, Ridge, Lasso]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need interpretability?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R2[Decision Tree Regression]
    Q4 -- No --> R3[Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Neural Network]
    Q2 -- Categorical --> Q5{Need probability outputs?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R4[Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Need high accuracy on complex data?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> R5[Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM]
    Q6 -- No --> R7[Decision Tree, KNN]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Model Building Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        D1[Raw Data Repository]
    end
    subgraph Preprocessing[Preprocessing Module]
        P1[Missing Value Imputation]
        P2[Encoding Categorical Variables]
        P3[Feature Scaling and Normalization]
        P4[Train Validation Test Split]
    end
    subgraph ModelEngine[Model Building Engine]
        M1[Algorithm Selection Layer]
        M2[Hyperparameter Configuration]
        M3[Training and Parameter Optimization]
        M4[Cross Validation Layer]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output and Deployment Layer]
        O1[Performance Metrics Calculation]
        O2[Model Serialization and Storage]
        O3[API Endpoint and Production Deployment]
    end
    D1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    P4 --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4
    M4 --> O1 --> O2 --> O3
```

**Architectural Notes:**

- **Preprocessing Module** ensures data consistency and quality.
- **Model Building Engine** is the *only* module that learns parameters.
- **Cross-Validation Layer** is a *gatekeeper* preventing overfit models from reaching deployment.
- **Output Layer** is where the model becomes a business asset via APIs, dashboards, or batch jobs.

### 4.4 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff Visualization (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Simple Model: High Bias, Low Variance] -->|Increasing Model Complexity| B[Optimal Zone: Balanced Bias and Variance]
    B -->|Further Increasing Complexity| C[Complex Model: Low Bias, High Variance]
    A -.->|Underfitting| D[High Test Error]
    C -.->|Overfitting| E[High Test Error]
    B --> F[Lowest Test Error Sweet Spot]
```

**Critical Insight for KTU Exam:** The total test error forms a **U-shaped curve** as model complexity increases. The minimum of this curve is the optimal model.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **parameters** and **hyperparameters** in a machine learning model. Give one example of each from linear regression.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Internal coefficients learned from data | External configuration values set before training |
| How determined | By optimization (e.g., minimizing MSE) | By analyst, often via Grid Search |
| Updated during training? | Yes, every iteration | No (fixed before training) |
| Example in Linear Regression | $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p$ (the regression coefficients) | Learning rate $\eta$, regularization strength $\lambda$ |

*(Award 1 mark for correct definition of each; 1 mark for correct example.)*

---

**Q2.** What is the **bias-variance tradeoff**? Why is it central to model building?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **bias-variance tradeoff** is the fundamental tension in model building between:
- **Bias²:** Error from *oversimplified* models that miss real patterns (underfitting).
- **Variance:** Error from *overly complex* models that fit training noise (overfitting).

**Why central:** As model complexity increases, bias decreases but variance increases. The expected test error equals $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}$. The goal of model building is to find the *sweet-spot complexity* that minimizes the sum of bias² and variance, achieving the lowest test error.

*(Award 1 mark for definitions; 1 mark for the tradeoff explanation; 1 mark for stating the central role.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style — Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a)** Explain the **six phases of the CRISP-DM model building methodology** in detail. Highlight the iterative nature of the process. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **CRoss-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM)** is the industry-standard model building framework. It consists of six phases:

**1. Business Understanding (1.5 marks)**
- Identify the business objective (e.g., reduce customer churn by 15%).
- Translate it into an analytics problem statement.
- Define success criteria in business KPIs.
- Output: Project plan, analytics problem definition.

**2. Data Understanding (1 mark)**
- Initial data collection; identify data sources.
- Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): distributions, outliers, missing values.
- Assess data quality and availability.

**3. Data Preparation (1 mark)**
- The most time-consuming phase (60–70% of project time).
- Includes data cleaning, imputation, transformation, feature engineering, encoding.
- Output: Final modeling dataset.

**4. Model Building (1.5 marks)**
- Select candidate algorithms (regression, tree, neural network).
- Split data into train/validation/test sets.
- Train models; tune hyperparameters using cross-validation.
- Output: One or more candidate trained models.

**5. Model Evaluation (1 mark)**
- Evaluate on test set using RMSE, Accuracy, F1, etc.
- Compare against business KPIs established in Phase 1.
- Determine if model meets deployment criteria.

**6. Deployment (1 mark)**
- Deploy via API, dashboard, or batch process.
- Monitor performance in production.
- Document handover to operations team.

**Iterative Nature (Bonus credit):**
The arrows in CRISP-DM are bidirectional. Insights from any phase may require revisiting earlier phases. After deployment, monitoring may reveal *concept drift*, triggering a new cycle.

---

**(b)** Derive the **ordinary least squares (OLS) estimators** $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ for a simple linear regression model. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions of OLS (1 mark):**
1. Linearity: relationship between $x$ and $y$ is linear.
2. Independence of errors.
3. Homoscedasticity: constant variance of errors.
4. Normality of errors.
5. No multicollinearity (for multiple regression).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The simple linear regression model is

$$
y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \epsilon_i
$$

The objective is to minimize the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$
S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate with respect to $\beta_0$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

This yields the first normal equation:

$$
\sum y_i = n \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum x_i \implies \bar{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{x}
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $\beta_1$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

This yields the second normal equation:

$$
\sum x_i y_i = \beta_0 \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2
$$

**Step 3:** Solve simultaneously. Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
\sum x_i y_i = (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}) \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2
$$

After algebraic manipulation, the closed-form estimator for the slope is:

$$
\boxed{\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}}
$$

The intercept estimator is:

$$
\boxed{\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** $\hat{\beta}_1$ is the *covariance* of $x$ and $y$ divided by the *variance* of $x$. It represents the change in $y$ for a unit change in $x$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss the **bias-variance decomposition** of expected test error. Explain how the tradeoff guides the choice of model complexity, regularization, and ensemble methods. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Bias-Variance Decomposition (3 marks):**

The expected prediction error of a learned model $\hat{f}(x_0)$ at a test point $x_0$ is:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2 \right] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}
$$

where:
- **Bias²** = $\left( f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2$ — error from wrong model assumptions.
- **Variance** = $\mathbb{E}\left[ \left( \hat{f}(x_0) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \right)^2 \right]$ — sensitivity to training data fluctuations.
- **$\sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}$** — inherent noise in the data; cannot be removed.

**2. Effect of Complexity (1.5 marks):**
- **Simple models (linear, low-degree polynomial):** High bias, low variance. Underfit.
- **Complex models (deep trees, high-degree polynomials, neural networks):** Low bias, high variance. Overfit.
- **Optimal zone:** The U-shaped test error curve has a minimum at the optimal complexity.

**3. Regularization as Variance Control (1.5 marks):**
Regularization adds a penalty term to the loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)
$$

- **Ridge (L2):** $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ — shrinks coefficients, reduces variance.
- **Lasso (L1):** $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ — performs feature selection, reducing both variance and model complexity.
- The regularization parameter $\lambda$ directly trades off bias (slightly higher) for variance (significantly lower).

**4. Ensemble Methods as Variance Reduction (1 mark):**
- **Bagging (e.g., Random Forest):** Averages many high-variance, low-bias models to reduce variance without increasing bias.
- **Boosting (e.g., Gradient Boosting, XGBoost):** Sequentially fits models to the residuals, primarily reducing bias.
- **Stacking:** Combines heterogeneous models to balance bias and variance optimally.

**Conclusion:** The bias-variance tradeoff is the *theoretical foundation* of every model selection decision. Smart model building navigates this tradeoff using cross-validation, regularization, and ensembles.

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast **supervised** and **unsupervised** learning models. For each category, name **two representative algorithms** and state the type of business problem each solves. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Conceptual Distinction (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Target variable** | Labeled output $y$ is provided | No labels; only features $X$ |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: X \to Y$ mapping | Discover hidden structure in $X$ |
| **Data requirement** | Historical data with known outcomes | Raw data without outcomes |
| **Evaluation** | Direct: accuracy, RMSE, etc. | Indirect: silhouette score, business validation |
| **Analogy** | Learning with a teacher | Learning by self-discovery |

**2. Supervised Learning — Two Representative Algorithms (2.5 marks):**

**(i) Linear Regression**
- **Type:** Continuous target prediction.
- **Business problem:** Predicting quarterly revenue, forecasting house prices, estimating delivery times.
- **Key metric:** RMSE, $R^2$.

**(ii) Logistic Regression**
- **Type:** Binary classification.
- **Business problem:** Customer churn prediction, loan default classification, spam detection.
- **Key metric:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC-ROC.

**3. Unsupervised Learning — Two Representative Algorithms (2.5 marks):**

**(i) K-Means Clustering**
- **Type:** Centroid-based partitioning.
- **Business problem:** Customer segmentation for targeted marketing, document clustering, image compression.
- **Key metric:** Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS), Silhouette Score.

**(ii) Hierarchical Clustering (Agglomerative)**
- **Type:** Tree-of-clusters (dendrogram) approach.
- **Business problem:** Taxonomy creation, gene expression analysis, supply chain tier organization.
- **Key metric:** Cophenetic correlation.

**4. Practical Business Integration (Bonus):**
In modern analytics, supervised and unsupervised methods are often **combined** in semi-supervised or pipeline architectures — e.g., clustering customers first, then training separate supervised models per segment for higher accuracy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Model Building Questions**
> 
> 1. **Confusing parameters with hyperparameters** — KTU examiners *specifically* test this. State explicitly: "Parameters are *learned*; hyperparameters are *set*."
> 
> 2. **Skipping the assumption statement in OLS derivation** — Many students lose 1–2 marks by jumping straight into math. Always list: linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, normality.
> 
> 3. **Writing $R^2$ without context** — KTU expects you to mention that $R^2$ represents the *proportion of variance explained* and that adjusted $R^2$ is preferred for multiple regression.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to specify units in RMSE/MAE** — Always state the unit explicitly (e.g., "RMSE = ₹1,250 per transaction"). This demonstrates engineering rigor.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — Precision answers "Of those predicted positive, how many are correct?" Recall answers "Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?" This distinction is a *guaranteed* 2-mark question.
> 
> 6. **Drawing the Bias-Variance curve upside down** — The test error is U-shaped (minimum at optimum), NOT inverted-U. Wrong sketches cost 1 full mark.
> 
> 7. **Not explaining WHY a model is chosen** — Always tie your model choice to a business problem type. Examiners reward *justification*, not just naming an algorithm.
> 
> 8. **Mixing up L1 and L2 regularization formulas** — L1 (Lasso) uses $\vert \beta \vert$ (absolute value), L2 (Ridge) uses $\beta^2$ (squared). The cardinality of the norm (1 vs 2) determines the shrinkage behavior.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Model Building**

- ✅ **Model Building is iterative**, not a one-shot process; CRISP-DM is the standard framework.
- ✅ **Six phases:** Business Understanding → Data Understanding → Data Prep → Modeling → Evaluation → Deployment.
- ✅ **Parameters are learned; hyperparameters are configured** before training.
- ✅ **OLS estimators** in simple linear regression:
  - $\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$
  - $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$
- ✅ **Matrix form (multiple regression):** $\hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance decomposition:** Test Error = $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- ✅ **L1 (Lasso) for feature selection; L2 (Ridge) for coefficient shrinkage.**
- ✅ **RMSE is in the same unit as $y$; MSE is in squared units.**
- ✅ **Adjusted $R^2$** penalizes unnecessary predictors; always preferred over $R^2$ for multiple regression.
- ✅ **For classification:** Always report Precision, Recall, **and** F1; do NOT rely on accuracy alone for imbalanced data.
- ✅ **k-Fold Cross-Validation** is the gold standard for estimating generalization error in limited data scenarios.
- ✅ **Ensembles (Bagging → Variance; Boosting → Bias)** are practical applications of the bias-variance tradeoff.
- ✅ **Logistic regression uses cross-entropy loss**, not MSE, because MSE is non-convex for sigmoid outputs.
- ✅ **Concept drift** post-deployment mandates continuous monitoring and periodic retraining.
- ✅ **Model interpretability** is as important as accuracy in regulated industries (banking, healthcare).

> **End of Module 2 — Model Building Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECBT755**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
