topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supply Chain Analytics - Supply Planning - Procurement and Strategic Sourcing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Supply Chain Analytics - Supply Planning, Procurement & Strategic Sourcing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Supply Chain Analytics (SCA)

**Supply Chain Analytics (SCA)** is the application of data-driven analytical techniques — including descriptive, predictive, prescriptive, and cognitive models — to optimize every node and link of the supply chain, from raw material supplier to end customer. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, SCA is positioned as a decision-support discipline that converts large volumes of supply chain data (transactional, sensor, and external) into actionable insights for planning, execution, and strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Supply Chain Analytics refers to the systematic use of data, statistical models, optimization algorithms, and visualization tools to measure, manage, and improve supply chain performance across procurement, production, distribution, and returns.*

**The Four Pillars of Supply Chain Analytics:**

| Pillar | Question Answered | Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Descriptive** | *What happened?* | Dashboards, KPI reports, OLAP |
| **Predictive** | *What will happen?* | Time-series forecasting, regression, ML |
| **Prescriptive** | *What should we do?* | Linear/Integer programming, simulation |
| **Cognitive** | *Why did it happen & how to adapt?* | AI, NLP, digital twins |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Supply Chain as a Human Body"**
> Imagine a car as the supply chain. **Descriptive analytics** is the rear-view mirror (you know what speed you drove at). **Predictive analytics** is the GPS that says you will reach the destination in 30 minutes. **Prescriptive analytics** is the GPS suggesting the *fastest* route by considering traffic. **Cognitive analytics** is the self-driving system that learns from your driving style and adapts.

---

### 1.2 Supply Planning

**Supply Planning** is the mid-term and short-term decision process that determines *how*, *when*, and *where* the firm will source, produce, and deliver products to meet forecasted demand while respecting capacity, inventory, and cost constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** *Supply planning is the function of balancing supply (production + procurement + inventory) against demand at the SKU, plant, and distribution-center level using master planning, MRP, and capacity-planning models.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of supply planning as a chef's prep list. Before the dinner rush (demand), the chef (planner) decides how much rice to cook, how many vegetables to chop, and how much to keep in the fridge (inventory). The prep list must match the number of expected diners, with a buffer (safety stock) for surprise guests.

**Key Outputs of Supply Planning:**
- **Production Plan** (aggregate, master, and detailed schedule)
- **Inventory Plan** (target stock levels by period)
- **Procurement Plan** (purchase orders, quantities, dates)
- **Distribution Plan** (allocation to DCs and customers)

---

### 1.3 Procurement

**Procurement** is the operational function of acquiring goods, services, and works from external suppliers at the best possible total cost of ownership, in the required quality, quantity, and time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** *Procurement encompasses the end-to-end process of identifying needs, sourcing suppliers, negotiating contracts, raising purchase orders, receiving goods, inspecting quality, and processing payments.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Procurement is *what* you buy and *how* you buy it. It is the act of going to the market with your shopping list. The procurement officer is the family member who goes to the wholesale market — comparing prices, haggling, checking quality, and arranging delivery.

**Procurement Process (P2P Cycle):**
1. **Plan** → Identify need (MRP-driven)
2. **Source** → Select supplier (RFQ/RFP)
3. **Negotiate** → Finalize price, terms, SLA
4. **Order** → Raise Purchase Order (PO)
5. **Receive** → GRN (Goods Receipt Note)
6. **Pay** → 3-way match (PO + GRN + Invoice)

---

### 1.4 Strategic Sourcing

**Strategic Sourcing** is the *analytical and long-term* approach to procurement. It is a data-driven, continuous, and proactive process of evaluating and re-evaluating the supplier base to maximize value, mitigate risk, and align with business strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** *Strategic sourcing is the systematic, analytics-driven process of analyzing spend data, segmenting the supply market, evaluating suppliers, and developing long-term sourcing strategies that minimize total cost and risk while maximizing value creation.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If **procurement** is *shopping* (transactional), **strategic sourcing** is *real estate investment* (portfolio-level). A family will shop for groceries weekly, but they will also strategically decide — *should we buy a house in this neighborhood for long-term value?* Strategic sourcing asks: *Which suppliers are our "properties"? Which "neighborhoods" (markets) should we invest in? Which "houses" should we exit?*

**7-Step Strategic Sourcing Process (Coupa/CAPS framework):**
1. **Profile the Category** — what are we buying?
2. **Analyze the Spend** — how much and on whom?
3. **Analyze the Market** — who supplies, what forces?
4. **Develop Strategy** — Kraljic matrix position
5. **Select Suppliers** — TCO + scoring model
6. **Negotiate & Contract** — SLAs, win-win
7. **Implement & Track** — KPIs, continuous improvement

---

### 1.5 Quick Comparison Table

| Aspect | Procurement | Strategic Sourcing |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term (PO to PO) | Long-term (3–5 years) |
| **Focus** | Transactional efficiency | Value & risk optimization |
| **Approach** | Reactive | Proactive & data-driven |
| **Key Output** | Purchase Order | Sourcing Strategy & Contract |
| **Decision Driver** | Price | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) |
| **Supplier Relation** | Adversarial/Arm's length | Partnership/Strategic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Analytical Architecture of Supply Chain Analytics

The discipline of supply chain analytics is built on a **layered architecture** that maps business questions to the correct analytical technique.

**Layer 1 — Data Layer:** ERP (SAP, Oracle), WMS, TMS, IoT sensors, supplier portals.
**Layer 2 — Descriptive Layer:** Dashboards (Power BI, Tableau), KPI scorecards (OTIF, fill rate, inventory turnover).
**Layer 3 — Predictive Layer:** Demand forecasting, supplier risk scoring, lead-time prediction.
**Layer 4 — Prescriptive Layer:** Linear programming for network design, EOQ for inventory, simulation for capacity.
**Layer 5 — Cognitive Layer:** Generative AI for contracts, digital twins for scenario planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The examiner expects you to *map a given business problem* to the *correct layer* and the *correct technique*. For instance, "predicting next quarter's demand" → Layer 3, "deciding optimal order quantity" → Layer 4.

---

## 2.2 The Inventory Control Models (Most-Tested in KTU)

### 2.2.1 Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — The Wilson Formula

The **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** is the order quantity that minimizes the **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)**, which is the sum of **Ordering Cost** and **Carrying (Holding) Cost**.

**Assumptions of the Basic EOQ Model:**
- Demand is **deterministic and constant** (D units/year)
- Order quantity is **constant** (Q)
- Lead time is **known and constant** (L)
- **No stockouts** are allowed
- Ordering cost is **fixed per order** (S)
- Holding cost is **proportional to average inventory** (H = h × C, where h is holding rate, C is unit cost)

**Total Cost Function:**

$$\text{TIC}(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding Cost}}$$

**Optimal Order Quantity (EOQ):**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

**Number of Orders per Year:**

$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{DS}{2H}}$$

**Time Between Orders (Cycle Time):**

$$T^{*} = \frac{1}{N^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{2S}{DH}}$$

**Minimum Total Cost:**

$$\text{TIC}^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At the EOQ point, Ordering Cost = Holding Cost.** This is a "magical" property of the EOQ that the KTU examiner loves to test. If you are asked to verify whether a given Q is optimal, compute both costs and check if they are equal.

---

### 2.2.2 EOQ with Quantity Discounts (All-Units Discount)

When suppliers offer **tiered price breaks** (c₁ > c₂ > c₃ for higher volumes), the optimal Q is computed as:

1. Calculate EOQ for the **lowest price bracket** (smallest unit cost, since H = h·c, lower c → lower H → higher EOQ).
2. If EOQ falls in that bracket → optimal.
3. If not, **adjust upward** to the price-break quantity of that bracket and recompute total cost.
4. **Compare** the adjusted total cost of each feasible price bracket and pick the **minimum**.

**Total Cost with Discounted Unit Cost (c):**

$$\text{TIC}(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot h \cdot c + D \cdot c$$

---

### 2.2.3 Reorder Point (ROP) and Safety Stock

The **Reorder Point (ROP)** is the inventory level at which a new order must be placed to avoid stockout during the lead time.

$$ROP = d \cdot L + SS$$

where:
- $d$ = average daily demand
- $L$ = lead time in days
- $SS$ = safety stock

**Safety Stock Formula (Variable Demand / Variable Lead Time):**

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

where:
- $z$ = z-score for desired service level (e.g., 95% → 1.65, 99% → 2.33)
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = standard deviation of demand during lead time
- $L$ = lead time

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Intuition:** Safety stock is the "pillow" you keep on the sofa to absorb shocks (demand surges or late deliveries). The higher the variability, the thicker the pillow. The z-score is the "comfort level" of the pillow (95% comfort vs 99% comfort).

---

## 2.3 ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)

**ABC Analysis** is a Pareto-based inventory classification technique that segments SKUs based on annual consumption value.

- **Class A** (~20% of SKUs, ~80% of consumption value) — *tight control, frequent review*
- **Class B** (~30% of SKUs, ~15% of value) — *moderate control*
- **Class C** (~50% of SKUs, ~5% of value) — *loose control, bulk review*

**Consumption Value (CV):**

$$CV_i = \text{Annual Demand}_i \times \text{Unit Cost}_i$$

---

## 2.4 The Kraljic Matrix (Strategic Sourcing Core Model)

The **Kraljic Matrix** segments purchased items along two dimensions:
- **Supply Risk** (x-axis): scarcity, switching cost, number of suppliers
- **Profit Impact** (y-axis): volume, value, importance to product

| | Low Supply Risk | High Supply Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **High Profit Impact** | **Strategic Items** | **Bottleneck Items** |
| **Low Profit Impact** | **Leverage Items** | **Non-Critical Items** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sourcing Strategies by Quadrant (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
> - **Strategic** → Long-term partnerships, joint development, dual sourcing, volume guarantees
> - **Bottleneck** → Secure supply, large safety stock, develop alternatives, accept high price
> - **Leverage** → Competitive bidding, aggressive negotiation, multiple suppliers
> - **Non-Critical** → Standardize, e-procurement, low-touch transactions

---

## 2.5 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) in Sourcing

**TCO** is the sum of all costs over the supplier relationship lifecycle — not just the purchase price.

$$TCO = C_{\text{price}} + C_{\text{ordering}} + C_{\text{holding}} + C_{\text{quality}} + C_{\text{transport}} + C_{\text{admin}} + C_{\text{risk}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A cheap Chinese supplier with 10% defects and 6-week lead time is *more expensive* than a domestic supplier with 0.5% defects. The purchase price is the *visible* cost; TCO captures the *hidden* costs of poor quality, delays, and rework.

---

## 2.6 Supplier Scoring & Selection (Weighted Point Model)

Suppliers are evaluated on multiple criteria with weights summing to 1:

$$\text{Score}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$$

where:
- $w_i$ = weight of criterion $i$
- $r_{ij}$ = normalized rating of supplier $j$ on criterion $i$ (typically 0–10 scale)

**Normalization of rating:**

$$r_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{r_{ij} - \min(r_{ij})}{\max(r_{ij}) - \min(r_{ij})}$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Memorize this table.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units |
| 2 | **Total Inventory Cost** | $TIC(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | ₹/year |
| 3 | **Optimal Number of Orders** | $N^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DS}{2H}}$ | orders/yr |
| 4 | **Cycle Time** | $T^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | years |
| 5 | **Reorder Point** | $ROP = dL + SS$ | units |
| 6 | **Safety Stock** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | units |
| 7 | **Holding Cost** | $H = h \cdot c$ | ₹/unit/year |
| 8 | **TIC with Quantity Discount** | $TIC = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{Q}{2}hc + Dc$ | ₹/year |
| 9 | **ABC Classification** | Sort by $CV_i = D_i \times c_i$, apply Pareto | — |
| 10 | **TCO** | Sum of price + ordering + holding + quality + transport + admin + risk | ₹/year |
| 11 | **Supplier Score** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ with $\sum w_i = 1$ | score |
| 12 | **Service Level → z-score** | 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 99% → 2.33 | — |
| 13 | **Kraljic Quadrants** | Strategic / Leverage / Bottleneck / Non-Critical | — |

**Boundary Conditions for EOQ validity:**
- Demand must be **uniform and deterministic**.
- Lead time must be **constant**.
- **No stockouts permitted**.
- Unit cost must be **constant** (except in discount models).

**Real-world engineering utility:** The EOQ model is the foundation of MRP-II and ERP systems (SAP MM, Oracle EBS) that drive procurement orders in industries ranging from automotive to FMCG to e-commerce warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EOQ Formula (Complete, Exam-Ready)

We derive the Economic Order Quantity from first principles.

**Step 1 — Set up the Total Cost Function.**

The total relevant cost of an inventory system consists of two components:
- The **Ordering Cost** = (Number of orders per year) × (Cost per order)
- The **Holding Cost** = (Average inventory) × (Holding cost per unit per year)

If annual demand is $D$ units and each order is $Q$ units:

$$\text{Number of orders per year} = \frac{D}{Q}$$

$$\text{Ordering Cost per year} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

The average inventory under a saw-tooth cycle (instantaneous replenishment, no safety stock) is exactly half the order quantity:

$$\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

$$\text{Holding Cost per year} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

Therefore the Total Inventory Cost is:

$$\text{TIC}(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Minimize TIC with respect to Q using calculus.**

To find the minimum, take the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{TIC})}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the resulting equation.**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^{2}}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify it is a minimum (second derivative test).**

$$\frac{d^{2}(\text{TIC})}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive for all positive $Q$, the critical point is indeed a **global minimum**.

**Step 5 — Verify the EOQ Optimality Condition (Ordering Cost = Holding Cost).**

At $Q = Q^{*}$:

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} S = \frac{D \cdot S}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DHS}{2}}$$

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{Q^{*}}{2} H = \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DHS}{2}}$$

Both are **equal**, confirming the optimality condition.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — EOQ (Full KTU Board-Style Solution)

**Problem:** A manufacturing firm uses 18,000 units/year of a raw material. The cost of placing one order is **₹450**, and the holding cost is **25% of the unit cost per year**. The unit cost is **₹80**. Find:
- (a) The EOQ
- (b) The number of orders per year
- (c) The cycle time
- (d) The total inventory cost per year
- (e) Verify the optimality condition.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 18{,}000$ units/year
- $S = \text{₹}450$ per order
- $h = 25\% = 0.25$
- $c = \text{₹}80$ per unit
- $H = h \cdot c = 0.25 \times 80 = \text{₹}20$ per unit per year

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — EOQ:**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 450}{20}}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{16{,}200{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{810{,}000}$$

$$Q^{*} = 900 \text{ units}$$

**Part (b) — Number of Orders per Year:**

$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{18{,}000}{900} = 20 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Part (c) — Cycle Time:**

$$T^{*} = \frac{1}{N^{*}} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ years} = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.6 \text{ months}$$

Equivalently, $T^{*} = 18$ days per cycle.

**Part (d) — Total Inventory Cost:**

$$\text{TIC}^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 450 \times 20}$$

$$\text{TIC}^{*} = \sqrt{324{,}000{,}000} = \text{₹}18{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Part (e) — Optimality Check:**

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \cdot S = \frac{18{,}000}{900} \times 450 = 20 \times 450 = \text{₹}9{,}000$$

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{Q^{*}}{2} \cdot H = \frac{900}{2} \times 20 = 450 \times 20 = \text{₹}9{,}000$$

Both costs are equal at **₹9,000**, confirming the EOQ is optimal. ✅

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Quantity Discount Model

**Problem:** A buyer places orders for an item with the following price schedule:

| Quantity Bracket | Unit Price (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 – 499 | 20 |
| 500 – 999 | 18 |
| 1,000 and above | 16 |

Annual demand $D = 5{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S = \text{₹}200$, holding rate $h = 20\%$. Determine the optimal order quantity.

**Given:**
- $D = 5{,}000$, $S = 200$, $h = 0.20$

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ for the lowest price bracket (₹20).**

$$H_1 = 0.20 \times 20 = \text{₹}4$$

$$Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 200}{4}} = \sqrt{500{,}000} \approx 707 \text{ units}$$

Since $Q_1 = 707$ is in the 500–999 bracket (not in the 1–499 bracket for ₹20), the **₹20 price is infeasible**. The Q for the ₹20 price would need to be at least 707, but at 707 units the price is ₹18. We must adjust.

**Step 2 — Compute EOQ for the next bracket (₹18).**

$$H_2 = 0.20 \times 18 = \text{₹}3.6$$

$$Q_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 200}{3.6}} = \sqrt{555{,}555.56} \approx 745 \text{ units}$$

$Q_2 = 745$ falls within the 500–999 bracket, so the ₹18 price is **feasible** and this is a candidate.

**Step 3 — Compute EOQ for the lowest bracket (₹16).**

$$H_3 = 0.20 \times 16 = \text{₹}3.2$$

$$Q_3 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 200}{3.2}} = \sqrt{625{,}000} \approx 791 \text{ units}$$

$Q_3 = 791$ does **not** fall in the 1,000-and-above bracket. We must adjust upward to the price-break point of that bracket: $Q_{\text{adj}} = 1{,}000$ units.

**Step 4 — Compute total cost at each feasible candidate.**

For $Q = 745$ at ₹18:

$$\text{TIC}_{745} = \frac{5{,}000 \times 200}{745} + \frac{745}{2} \times 3.6 + 5{,}000 \times 18$$

$$= 1{,}342.28 + 1{,}341 + 90{,}000 = \text{₹}92{,}683.28$$

For $Q = 1{,}000$ at ₹16:

$$\text{TIC}_{1000} = \frac{5{,}000 \times 200}{1{,}000} + \frac{1{,}000}{2} \times 3.2 + 5{,}000 \times 16$$

$$= 1{,}000 + 1{,}600 + 80{,}000 = \text{₹}82{,}600$$

**Step 5 — Select the minimum.**

$$\text{TIC}_{1000} = \text{₹}82{,}600 < \text{TIC}_{745} = \text{₹}92{,}683$$

**Optimal Order Quantity: $Q^{*} = 1{,}000$ units at a unit price of ₹16.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — Supplier Scoring (Strategic Sourcing)

**Problem:** A firm wants to select a supplier among three (A, B, C) using five criteria. Compute the weighted score and recommend a supplier.

| Criterion | Weight (w) | Supplier A | Supplier B | Supplier C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Price (low is good) | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| Quality (high is good) | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Delivery (high is good) | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Capacity (high is good) | 0.15 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Service (high is good) | 0.10 | 6 | 8 | 7 |

*(Note: For "Price, low is good", the rating is already inverted by the evaluator — higher rating = lower price, which is consistent with the "higher is better" convention used here.)*

**Step 1 — Compute weighted scores.**

$$\text{Score}_A = (0.30)(8) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(6)$$

$$= 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.60 = 7.85$$

$$\text{Score}_B = (0.30)(6) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(8)$$

$$= 1.80 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.80 = 7.45$$

$$\text{Score}_C = (0.30)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(9) + (0.10)(7)$$

$$= 2.70 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.70 = 7.70$$

**Step 2 — Rank the suppliers.**

| Rank | Supplier | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | **7.85** ← BEST |
| 2 | C | 7.70 |
| 3 | B | 7.45 |

**Decision:** **Select Supplier A** as the strategic sourcing partner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Intuition:** Notice how a low price is a strong lever (30% weight) — Supplier C's low price gives it a 2.70 contribution, but A wins because of its superior quality (2.25) and balanced service. This illustrates the *TCO logic* of strategic sourcing: low price does not always win; the **total weighted value** does.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Supply Chain Analytics Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Data Sources] --> B[Data Integration Layer]
    B --> C[Analytics Engine]
    C --> D1[Descriptive]
    C --> D2[Predictive]
    C --> D3[Prescriptive]
    C --> D4[Cognitive]
    D1 --> E[Decision Support Dashboard]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F1[Supply Planning]
    E --> F2[Procurement]
    E --> F3[Strategic Sourcing]
    E --> F4[Logistics]
    F1 --> G[Optimized Supply Chain]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    A1[ERP / SAP] --> A
    A2[WMS / IoT Sensors] --> A
    A3[Supplier Portals] --> A
    A4[Market Data Feeds] --> A
    G --> H[KPIs - OTIF - Fill Rate - Inventory Turnover - Cost Reduction]
```

## 4.2 The 7-Step Strategic Sourcing Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 - Profile the Category] --> S2[Step 2 - Analyze Spend Data]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 - Analyze the Supply Market]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 - Develop Sourcing Strategy]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 - Select Suppliers]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 - Negotiate and Contract]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 - Implement and Track KPIs]
    S7 -. Continuous Improvement .-> S1
    S4 -. Kraljic Matrix .-> S4
    S5 -. TCO Model .-> S5
```

## 4.3 Kraljic Matrix — Strategic Sourcing Positioning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1["High Profit Impact + Low Supply Risk = LEVERAGE ITEMS"]
        K1[Competitive Bidding]
        K2[Multiple Suppliers]
        K3[Volume Aggregation]
        K4[Spot Purchase]
    end
    subgraph Q2["High Profit Impact + High Supply Risk = STRATEGIC ITEMS"]
        K5[Long-term Partnership]
        K6[Joint Development]
        K7[Dual Sourcing]
        K8[Volume Guarantees]
    end
    subgraph Q3["Low Profit Impact + High Supply Risk = BOTTLENECK ITEMS"]
        K9[Secure Supply]
        K10[Large Safety Stock]
        K11[Develop Alternatives]
        K12[Accept High Price]
    end
    subgraph Q4["Low Profit Impact + Low Supply Risk = NON-CRITICAL ITEMS"]
        K13[Standardize]
        K14[E-Procurement]
        K15[Catalog Buying]
        K16[Minimize Admin]
    end
```

## 4.4 The P2P (Procure-to-Pay) Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Identify Need] --> P2[Source Supplier RFQ RFP]
    P2 --> P3[Negotiate Terms]
    P3 --> P4[Raise Purchase Order]
    P4 --> P5[Receive Goods - GRN]
    P5 --> P6[Quality Inspection]
    P6 --> P7[3-Way Match - PO + GRN + Invoice]
    P7 --> P8[Payment Processing]
    P8 --> P9[Supplier Performance Review]
    P9 -. Feedback Loop .-> P2
```

## 4.5 EOQ Cost Behavior & Optimality (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Order Quantity Q] --> B[Ordering Cost = DS/Q]
    A --> C[Holding Cost = QH/2]
    B --> D[Total Inventory Cost TIC]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Cost Balance Check}
    E -- OC greater than HC --> F[Reduce Q]
    E -- HC greater than OC --> G[Increase Q]
    E -- OC equals HC --> H[OPTIMAL EOQ Achieved]
    F --> I[Recalculate TIC]
    G --> I
    I --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each diagram maps to a high-yield KTU concept. The P2P cycle frequently appears in 7-mark sub-questions; the Kraljic matrix is the *go-to* 7-mark framework question; the EOQ cost-balance flow justifies the optimality condition that examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Modeled after KTU 2024 Scheme University Examination — direct, definition/conceptual questions that test Course Outcomes CO2 and CO3.*

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between procurement and strategic sourcing. Mention any four points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Procurement | Strategic Sourcing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transactional, short-term, PO-level | Analytical, long-term, category-level |
| 2 | Focus on getting the right price | Focus on minimizing Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) |
| 3 | Reactive — issues a PO when need arises | Proactive — plans the supplier portfolio in advance |
| 4 | Arm's-length, often adversarial relationship | Partnership, joint development, win-win |
| 5 | Single-event decision | Continuous, data-driven process |
| 6 | Operational (tactical) | Strategic |

**Conclusion:** Strategic sourcing is the *upstream, analytical, and long-term* version of procurement. While procurement closes the deal, strategic sourcing decides *whether* and *with whom* to deal.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)? State its formula and one key assumption.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) is the fixed order quantity that minimizes the sum of ordering cost and holding (carrying) cost in an inventory system, assuming deterministic and constant demand.

**Formula:**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

where $D$ = annual demand (units), $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹), and $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹).

**Key Assumption (any one):**
- Demand is constant and deterministic
- Lead time is known and fixed
- No stockouts are permitted
- Replenishment is instantaneous (entire order arrives at once)
- Unit cost is independent of order quantity

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *Modeled after KTU 2024 Scheme ESE — Part B questions with 7+7 sub-parts. Choose either OR.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the four types of supply chain analytics with one example of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A retail company sells 10,000 units of a product per year. The cost of placing an order is ₹300 and the holding cost per unit per year is ₹15.
- (i) Calculate the EOQ. (3 marks)
- (ii) Calculate the number of orders per year and the time between orders. (2 marks)
- (iii) What is the total inventory cost at the EOQ? (2 marks)

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Four Types of Supply Chain Analytics (7 marks)**

**1. Descriptive Analytics — "What happened?"**
- *Definition:* Uses historical data, KPIs, and dashboards to summarize past supply chain performance.
- *Example:* A monthly dashboard showing the On-Time-In-Full (OTIF) delivery rate, inventory turnover, and supplier defect rates for the last quarter.

**2. Predictive Analytics — "What will happen?"**
- *Definition:* Uses statistical and machine-learning models to forecast future outcomes such as demand, lead times, and supplier risk.
- *Example:* Using ARIMA or Prophet models to forecast next quarter's demand for 5,000 SKUs with 90% accuracy.

**3. Prescriptive Analytics — "What should we do?"**
- *Definition:* Uses optimization and simulation to recommend the *best* action given constraints and objectives.
- *Example:* Running a linear-programming model to determine the optimal plant-to-customer assignment that minimizes total logistics cost subject to capacity constraints.

**4. Cognitive Analytics — "How do we adapt intelligently?"**
- *Definition:* Uses AI, NLP, and digital twins to learn, reason, and adapt in real-time.
- *Example:* A digital twin of the supply chain that simulates the impact of a typhoon in Taiwan on semiconductor supply, recommends alternate routes, and auto-triggers a re-planning event.

| Type | Question | Technique | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Descriptive | What happened? | Dashboards, OLAP | OTIF KPI report |
| Predictive | What will happen? | Forecasting, regression | Demand forecast |
| Prescriptive | What should we do? | Optimization, simulation | LP-based network design |
| Cognitive | How to adapt? | AI, digital twin | Real-time disruption response |

---

**Part (b) — EOQ Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D = 10{,}000$ units/year, $S = \text{₹}300$ per order, $H = \text{₹}15$ per unit per year.

**Part (b)(i) — EOQ Calculation (3 marks):**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 300}{15}}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}000{,}000}{15}} = \sqrt{400{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = 632.46 \approx 632 \text{ units}}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Number of Orders and Cycle Time (2 marks):**

$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} \approx 15.81 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$T^{*} = \frac{1}{N^{*}} = \frac{1}{15.81} \approx 0.0633 \text{ years} \approx 23.1 \text{ days}$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Total Inventory Cost (2 marks):**

$$\text{TIC}^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 300 \times 15}$$

$$\text{TIC}^{*} = \sqrt{90{,}000{,}000} = \text{₹}9{,}486.83 \text{ per year}$$

**Verification of Optimality:**
- Ordering Cost = $\frac{D}{Q^{*}} \times S = 15.81 \times 300 = \text{₹}4{,}743.4$
- Holding Cost = $\frac{Q^{*}}{2} \times H = \frac{632.46}{2} \times 15 = \text{₹}4{,}743.4$ ✓ (Equal — optimal!)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Kraljic Matrix. For each quadrant, suggest an appropriate sourcing strategy. (7 marks)

**(b)** A company is evaluating three suppliers (X, Y, Z) for a critical component using four criteria. Compute the weighted score and recommend the best supplier. (7 marks)

| Criterion | Weight | Supplier X | Supplier Y | Supplier Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Price | 0.40 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Quality | 0.30 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Delivery | 0.20 | 6 | 8 | 9 |
| Service | 0.10 | 8 | 7 | 7 |

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Kraljic Matrix (7 marks)**

**Definition:** The Kraljic Matrix, developed by Peter Kraljic (1983), is a 2×2 strategic sourcing tool that classifies purchased items along two dimensions — **Profit Impact** (y-axis) and **Supply Risk** (x-axis) — to derive differentiated sourcing strategies.

**The Four Quadrants and Their Strategies:**

| Quadrant | Characteristics | Recommended Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Strategic Items** | High profit impact, high supply risk (e.g., proprietary chips) | **Partnerships, joint development, long-term contracts, dual sourcing, volume guarantees** |
| **2. Leverage Items** | High profit impact, low supply risk (e.g., commodity chemicals) | **Aggressive negotiation, multiple suppliers, competitive bidding, exploit buying power** |
| **3. Bottleneck Items** | Low profit impact, high supply risk (e.g., rare-earth minerals) | **Secure supply, large safety stock, develop alternatives, accept high price** |
| **4. Non-Critical Items** | Low profit impact, low supply risk (e.g., office stationery) | **Standardize, e-procurement, catalog buying, low administrative effort** |

**Conclusion:** The Kraljic Matrix prevents the "one-size-fits-all" trap in procurement. By segmenting purchases, firms can allocate their strategic effort where it matters most — typically in the **Strategic** and **Bottleneck** quadrants.

---

**Part (b) — Supplier Scoring (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the weighted score of each supplier.**

For Supplier X:

$$\text{Score}_X = (0.40)(7) + (0.30)(9) + (0.20)(6) + (0.10)(8)$$

$$= 2.80 + 2.70 + 1.20 + 0.80 = \mathbf{7.50}$$

For Supplier Y:

$$\text{Score}_Y = (0.40)(9) + (0.30)(7) + (0.20)(8) + (0.10)(7)$$

$$= 3.60 + 2.10 + 1.60 + 0.70 = \mathbf{8.00}$$

For Supplier Z:

$$\text{Score}_Z = (0.40)(6) + (0.30)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.10)(7)$$

$$= 2.40 + 2.40 + 1.80 + 0.70 = \mathbf{7.30}$$

**Step 2 — Rank the suppliers.**

| Rank | Supplier | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Y** | **8.00** ← BEST |
| 2 | X | 7.50 |
| 3 | Z | 7.30 |

**Step 3 — Decision.**

**Select Supplier Y** as the strategic sourcing partner. Despite Supplier X's higher quality rating (9 vs 7), Y's superior price rating (9 vs 7) combined with strong quality (7) and delivery (8) yields the highest total weighted value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity Insight:** If the weight on "Price" were reduced from 0.40 to 0.20, Supplier X (7.50) would tie or beat Y. This shows that *weighting decisions* are themselves strategic and must reflect the firm's current priorities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Module — Read Before the Exam**
> 
> **1. EOQ Numerical Errors (–2 to –3 marks):**
> - Forgetting to convert holding rate $h$ to $H = h \times c$ when only the *rate* is given.
> - Mixing up units: if $D$ is annual and lead time is in days, convert $L$ to years or $D$ to daily demand.
> - Writing $\sqrt{2DS/H}$ but plugging in wrong units of $S$ or $H$ (e.g., using per-order cost with a per-day demand without conversion).
> 
> **2. Kraljic Matrix Mistakes (–2 marks):**
> - Confusing the axes (Profit Impact vs. Supply Risk). Practice sketching the matrix in the answer sheet — it earns you +1 mark for presentation.
> - Giving generic "negotiate hard" for all quadrants. The examiner wants *differentiated* strategies per quadrant.
> 
> **3. Strategic Sourcing vs. Procurement Confusion (–1 mark):**
> - In Part A "differentiate" questions, students often write vague points. Use a *table* — examiners give 1 extra mark for clear tabular differentiation.
> 
> **4. Supplier Scoring (–2 marks):**
> - Not normalizing the ratings when they are on different scales (e.g., some criteria on 0–5, some on 0–10).
> - Forgetting to verify that weights sum to 1.0 (showing a sanity check earns you 1 mark).
> 
> **5. Safety Stock / ROP (–2 marks):**
> - Using the wrong z-score for the service level. Memorize: 90%→1.28, 95%→1.65, 97.5%→1.96, 99%→2.33.
> - Using $\sigma_{d}$ (daily demand std) instead of $\sigma_{LT}$ (lead-time demand std). The formula $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ assumes $\sigma_{LT}$ is the *lead-time demand* standard deviation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Walking Into the Exam Hall**
> 
> **Conceptual Anchors:**
> - ☐ **Supply Chain Analytics** has 4 layers: Descriptive → Predictive → Prescriptive → Cognitive.
> - ☐ **Supply Planning** = balancing supply (production + procurement + inventory) against demand at SKU/plant/DC level.
> - ☐ **Procurement** = transactional, short-term, PO-level "buying" function.
> - ☐ **Strategic Sourcing** = analytical, long-term, category-level "deciding whom to buy from" function.
> - ☐ The 7-step strategic sourcing process: Profile → Analyze Spend → Analyze Market → Develop Strategy → Select Suppliers → Negotiate → Implement & Track.
> 
> **Mathematical Anchors (Must-Know):**
> - ☐ **EOQ:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ where $H = h \times c$.
> - ☐ **TIC at EOQ:** $\sqrt{2DSH}$ — and at this point, **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost**.
> - ☐ **ROP:** $dL + SS$.
> - ☐ **Safety Stock:** $z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$.
> - ☐ **Service-level z-scores:** 90%→1.28, 95%→1.65, 99%→2.33.
> - ☐ **Supplier Score:** $\sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
> - ☐ **TCO** = Price + Ordering + Holding + Quality + Transport + Admin + Risk (always more than sticker price).
> 
> **Strategic Anchors:**
> - ☐ **Kraljic Matrix** has 4 quadrants: Strategic / Leverage / Bottleneck / Non-Critical.
> - ☐ **Leverage items** → competitive bidding; **Strategic** → partnership; **Bottleneck** → secure supply; **Non-Critical** → e-procurement.
> - ☐ **ABC Analysis** = 80/20 Pareto: 20% of SKUs account for 80% of value.
> - ☐ **P2P cycle** ends with a 3-way match (PO + GRN + Invoice).
> 
> **Examiner's Pet Topics (Highest Probability in 2024 ESE):**
> - ☐ EOQ numerical with or without quantity discount.
> - ☐ Kraljic Matrix quadrant identification + strategy.
> - ☐ Differentiate: Procurement vs. Strategic Sourcing (table format).
> - ☐ Supplier scoring with weighted criteria.
> - ☐ 4 types of supply chain analytics with examples.
> 
> **One-Line Memory Hook:**
> *"Procurement buys the goods. Strategic sourcing decides the right goods to buy, from the right supplier, for the right TCO, at the right risk level."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Modeling - Aggregate Planning and Resource Allocation Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Modeling - Aggregate Planning and Resource Allocation Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Aggregate Planning?

**Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a medium-range (3–18 months) operational planning activity that translates long-term strategic plans into a blueprint for determining overall production quantities, workforce levels, inventory holdings, backlogs, and subcontracting levels to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Aggregate planning is a process by which a company determines levels of capacity, production, subcontracting, inventory, stockouts, and pricing over a specified planning horizon to meet forecasted demand while minimizing costs.*

---

## 1.2 What is Inventory Modeling?

**Inventory Modeling** is the mathematical and analytical representation of stock-keeping behavior in a supply chain. It uses probabilistic and deterministic equations to determine optimal order quantities, reorder points, safety stock, and replenishment schedules that minimize the sum of ordering, holding, and shortage costs.

---

## 1.3 What are Resource Allocation Decisions?

**Resource Allocation Decisions** in the aggregate planning context refer to the strategic trade-offs a manager makes between *discretionary fixed resources* (workforce, machine capacity) and *variable resources* (overtime, subcontracting, inventory build-up) to balance supply with demand.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Imagine you run a busy restaurant. Each week, you must decide:
> - **How many chefs to employ** (workforce level),
> - **How much raw material (rice, vegetables) to pre-stock** (inventory),
> - **Whether to pay overtime** during weekend rushes (overtime),
> - **Or to outsource some orders to a catering partner** (subcontracting).
>
> You do NOT plan each dish separately — you plan in *aggregate*: total meals, total labor hours, total ingredient tons.
>
> This is **Aggregate Planning**. The *recipe for the optimal ingredient order size* is **Inventory Modeling**. And the *decision to buy more pans vs. hire another chef* is **Resource Allocation**.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Demand Forecast** | Estimated future customer demand over planning horizon |
| **Planning Horizon** | The time period (usually 3–18 months) over which AP is executed |
| **Capacity** | Maximum output a facility can produce per unit time |
| **Backlog** | Customer demand that is unmet but promised for later delivery |
| **Chase Strategy** | Matching production rate exactly to demand by varying workforce |
| **Level Strategy** | Maintaining constant production rate; using inventory as buffer |
| **Mixed/Hybrid Strategy** | Combination of chase and level strategies |
| **Subcontracting** | Outsourcing production to third-party suppliers |
| **Overtime/Undertime** | Working beyond/less than regular hours at premium/discount rates |

---

## 1.6 The Three Fundamental Planning Hierarchies

Aggregate planning sits **between** long-range strategic planning and short-range scheduling:

- **Long-Range (Strategic):** Capacity, facility location, technology (1–5 years)
- **Medium-Range (Aggregate):** Production rates, workforce, inventory (3–18 months) ← *THIS MODULE*
- **Short-Range (Operational):** Daily/weekly scheduling, dispatching (days–weeks)

> [!TIP]
> **Why does Aggregate Planning matter?**
> It is the **financial hinge** of operations. Poor aggregate planning inflates costs by 10–25% of total production cost — KTU examiners love questions on cost minimization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Aggregate Planning Process — Step-by-Step Logic

Aggregate planning follows a rigorous 7-step process. Each step must be logically completed before moving to the next.

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Planning Horizon:** Determine the time window (e.g., 6 months, 12 months).
2. **Step 2 — Demand Forecasting:** Aggregate demand into time-bucket totals (e.g., monthly demand units).
3. **Step 3 — Determine Current Capacity:** Assess regular-time, overtime, and subcontracting capacities.
4. **Step 4 — Identify Alternative Strategies:** Develop chase, level, and mixed strategies.
5. **Step 5:** **Cost Evaluation** — Compute total cost for each alternative (labor, hiring, firing, overtime, inventory, backorder, subcontracting).
6. **Step 6 — Iterative Comparison:** Compare costs across strategies and select the minimum-cost plan.
7. **Step 7 — Implementation and Control:** Convert the aggregate plan into master production schedule (MPS).

---

## 2.2 Cost Components in Aggregate Planning

Aggregate planning involves **eight** distinct cost categories. Every KTU question will test at least 3–4 of these.

### 2.2.1 Regular-Time Labor Cost
$$C_{RT} = R \times D_t$$

where $R$ = regular-time labor rate (₹/unit), $D_t$ = units produced in period $t$.

### 2.2.2 Overtime Cost
$$C_{OT} = O \times P_t$$

where $O$ = overtime premium rate, $P_t$ = overtime units in period $t$.

### 2.2.3 Hiring Cost
$$C_{H} = H \times (W_t - W_{t-1})^+$$

### 2.2.4 Firing (Layoff) Cost
$$C_{F} = F \times (W_{t-1} - W_t)^+$$

### 2.2.5 Inventory Holding Cost
$$C_{I} = h \times I_t$$

where $h$ = holding cost per unit per period, $I_t$ = ending inventory in period $t$.

### 2.2.6 Backorder (Shortage) Cost
$$C_{B} = b \times B_t$$

### 2.2.7 Subcontracting Cost
$$C_{SC} = S \times Q_t^{SC}$$

### 2.2.8 Material/Production Cost
$$C_{M} = M \times P_t$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $D$ = annual demand (units), $S$ = ordering cost (₹/order), $H$ = holding cost (₹/unit/year) |
| 2 | $TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | **Minimum Total Cost (EOQ)** | Total annual cost at optimal order size |
| 3 | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H} \cdot \dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | **Production Order Quantity (EPQ)** | $p$ = production rate (units/day), $d$ = demand rate (units/day) |
| 4 | $ROP = d \times L + SS$ | **Reorder Point** | $L$ = lead time (days), $SS$ = safety stock |
| 5 | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_L$ | **Safety Stock** | $z$ = z-score for service level, $\sigma_L$ = std. dev. of lead-time demand |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{Q^*}{D}$ | **Cycle Time / Order Interval** | Time between successive orders (years) |
| 7 | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q^*}$ | **Number of Orders per Year** | Optimal number of orders |
| 8 | $I_{max} = Q \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$ | **Maximum Inventory (EPQ)** | Peak inventory under finite production rate |
| 9 | $\sum_{t=1}^{T} (P_t + I_{t-1} - I_t) = \sum D_t$ | **Production-Inventory Balance** | Aggregate production equals cumulative demand |
| 10 | $TC_{AP} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(C_{RT} + C_{OT} + C_H + C_F + C_I + C_B + C_{SC})$ | **Total Aggregate Plan Cost** | Sum of all cost components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Note for KTU:**
> If holding cost $H$ is given as a **percentage** $i$ of item cost $c$, then $H = i \cdot c$. KTU questions frequently use this conversion — losing marks here is common.

---

## 2.4 Aggregate Planning Strategies — Detailed Analysis

### 2.4.1 Chase Strategy
- **Mechanism:** Production in each period exactly matches demand. Workforce is hired/fired as needed.
- **Inventory Level:** Near zero (no inventory buildup).
- **Backlog:** Zero (demand is always met on time).
- **Cost Profile:** Low holding cost, high hiring/firing cost.
- **Best When:** Hiring/firing costs are low and skilled labor is readily available.

### 2.4.2 Level Strategy
- **Mechanism:** Constant production rate throughout the horizon. Workforce is held constant.
- **Inventory Level:** Builds up in low-demand periods, depletes in high-demand periods.
- **Cost Profile:** High holding cost, low hiring/firing cost.
- **Best When:** Holding cost is low and workforce adjustment is expensive.

### 2.4.3 Mixed/Hybrid Strategy
- **Mechanism:** Combines elements of chase and level. Uses overtime, subcontracting, part-time workers, and moderate inventory.
- **Cost Profile:** Balanced across all cost categories.
- **Best When:** Realistic operations with moderate cost trade-offs.

---

## 2.5 Lot-Sizing Heuristics for Resource Allocation

When demand varies by period, KTU requires the **lot-sizing decision**: *how much to produce in each period?*

| Method | Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | Produce exactly what is demanded | Zero inventory holding; high setup |
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | Order Q* each period regardless of demand | Stable demand; ignores period variation |
| **Period Order Quantity (POQ)** | Order to cover $\frac{Q^*}{\bar{d}}$ periods | Average demand heuristic |
| **Part Period Balancing (PPB)** | Keep adding periods until cumulative PPB ≈ EOQ | Balances setup vs. holding |
| **Least Unit Cost (LUC)** | Add periods until incremental cost/order = min | Stepwise cost minimization |
| **Silver-Meal** | Minimize average cost per period | Penalizes long coverage |
| **Wagner-Whitin (WW)** | Dynamic programming — OPTIMAL solution | Exam reference benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wagner-Whitin Algorithm:**
> This dynamic programming method produces the mathematically optimal lot-sizing solution but is computationally intensive. In KTU exams, you may be asked to explain its logic, not execute it fully.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Aggregate Planning Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Quarterly production mix balancing inventory of parts and finished cars |
| **FMCG / Beverages** | Seasonal demand smoothing via inventory build before festivals |
| **Electronics** | Component allocation across product lines during chip shortages |
| **Apparel** | Workforce sizing for peak vs. off-season collections |
| **Healthcare** | Staffing allocation across flu season vs. off-peak periods |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

The **EOQ model** minimizes the sum of ordering cost and holding cost. We derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

- Demand rate $D$ (units/year) is **constant and known**.
- Order quantity $Q$ is **fixed** and arrives instantaneously.
- Ordering cost $S$ (₹/order) is **fixed per order**.
- Holding cost $H$ (₹/unit/year) is **linear** in inventory.
- No shortages, no lead time, infinite planning horizon.

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Cost Functions

**Annual Ordering Cost:**
Number of orders per year = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$.

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \left(\dfrac{D}{Q}\right) \cdot S$$

**Annual Holding Cost:**
Average inventory under instantaneous replenishment = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$.

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \left(\dfrac{Q}{2}\right) \cdot H$$

### 3.1.3 Total Annual Cost Function

$$
TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{DS}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{HQ}{2}}_{\text{Holding}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Minimization via Calculus

To minimize $TC(Q)$, take the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$
\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Second-Order Condition (Concavity Check)

$$
\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is **positive**, $TC(Q)$ is convex and $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum**. ✓

### 3.1.6 Minimum Total Cost

Substitute $Q^*$ back into $TC(Q)$:

$$
TC(Q^*) = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

$$
TC(Q^*) = \sqrt{\frac{DS \cdot H}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DS \cdot H}{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** At the EOQ, ordering cost **exactly equals** holding cost. This is the **principle of balance** — KTU examiners test this property frequently.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)

When production is **gradual** (not instantaneous) at rate $p$, inventory builds up during production and depletes during non-production.

### 3.2.1 Modified Setup

- Production rate = $p$ units/day.
- Demand rate = $d$ units/day, with $p > d$.
- Setup cost $S$, holding cost $H$.
- Production run time = $Q/p$ days.

### 3.2.2 Maximum Inventory

Inventory rises at rate $(p - d)$ during production:

$$
I_{max} = Q - d \cdot \frac{Q}{p} = Q \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)
$$

Average inventory = $\dfrac{I_{max}}{2}$.

### 3.2.3 Total Cost Function

$$
TC(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{H \cdot Q}{2} \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)
$$

### 3.2.4 Optimization

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 0
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)} = \frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p-d}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p-d}}}
$$

### 3.2.5 Special Case Verification

If $p \to \infty$, then $\dfrac{p}{p-d} \to 1$, and $Q^*_{EPQ} \to Q^*_{EOQ}$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aggregate Planning Cost Function (Worked Numerical Format)

Consider a planning horizon with $T$ periods. Let $P_t$ = production in period $t$, $D_t$ = demand in period $t$, $I_t$ = ending inventory in period $t$, $W_t$ = workforce in period $t$.

### 3.3.1 Inventory Balance Equation

$$
I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t \quad \forall t \in \{1, 2, \dots, T\}
$$

### 3.3.2 Production Constraint

$$
P_t = r \cdot W_t + O_t
$$

where $r$ = productivity per worker per period, $O_t$ = overtime units.

### 3.3.3 Total Cost Function

$$
TC = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ R \cdot P_t + O \cdot O_t + H \cdot \Delta W_t^+ + F \cdot \Delta W_t^- + h \cdot I_t + b \cdot B_t + S \cdot Q_t^{SC} \right]
$$

where:
- $R$ = regular production cost/unit
- $O$ = overtime cost/unit
- $H$ = hiring cost/worker, $F$ = firing cost/worker
- $h$ = holding cost/unit/period
- $b$ = backorder cost/unit/period
- $S$ = subcontracting cost/unit

### 3.3.4 Solution Approaches

| Approach | Method | Suitable For |
|---|---|---|
| **Graphical / Charting** | Cumulative production vs. demand chart | Manual, small problems |
| **Spreadsheet (Trial & Error)** | Try alternative plans, compute cost | Medium complexity |
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Formulate and solve with simplex | Large-scale, multi-product |
| **Transportation Method** | Treat periods as sources, demand as destinations | Production-smoothing |
| **Simulation** | Monte Carlo / discrete-event | Stochastic demand |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Numericals:** Master the *cumulative production graph* method. Plot cumulative demand vs. cumulative production; the **vertical distance** = inventory, **horizontal distance** = time to produce.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Comparison Table

| Decision Variable | Chase Strategy | Level Strategy | Mixed Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Rate** | Varies with demand | Constant | Partial variation |
| **Workforce** | Hires/fires each period | Fixed | Occasional adjustment |
| **Inventory** | Near zero | Builds up & depletes | Moderate |
| **Backlog** | Usually zero | Possible | Possible |
| **Subcontracting** | Rare | Common for peaks | Used selectively |
| **Overtime** | Limited | Used for peaks | Moderate |
| **Total Cost (typical)** | High if hiring costly | High if holding costly | **Lowest (optimal)** |
| **Implementation** | Simple operations | Easy workforce | Complex coordination |
| **Risk** | Quality/morale issues | Obsolescence risk | Balanced |

---

## 3.5 Cumulative Production Graph — Logic

For a level strategy:
- **Slope of production line** = production rate per period.
- **Vertical gap** between production line and demand line at any period = cumulative inventory.
- **Steep production line** = faster production → inventory build-up.
- **Flat production line** = no production → inventory depletion.

The slope is chosen such that **ending inventory = beginning inventory** over the horizon (cyclic plan), or equals desired ending inventory.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define Aggregate Planning. List any four cost components considered in aggregate planning. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Aggregate Planning is a medium-range (3–18 months) operational planning process that determines the optimal levels of production, workforce, inventory, backlogs, and subcontracting to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost.

**Four Cost Components (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
1. Regular-time labor/production cost
2. Overtime cost
3. Hiring and firing (layoff) cost
4. Inventory holding cost and backorder cost

---

### Question 2: Write the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) formula and state the relationship between ordering cost and holding cost at the optimal order quantity. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**EOQ Formula (2 marks):**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.

**Relationship at Optimum (1 mark):**
At the optimal EOQ, **Annual Ordering Cost = Annual Holding Cost**.

$$
\frac{DS}{Q^*} = \frac{HQ^*}{2}
$$

This is known as the *principle of balance* in inventory theory.

---

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

### OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

> A manufacturing firm forecasts the following monthly demand for the next 6 months:
>
> | Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Demand (units) | 500 | 700 | 800 | 600 | 400 | 900 |
>
> **Cost Data:**
> - Regular production cost = ₹40/unit
> - Overtime cost = ₹55/unit
> - Hiring cost = ₹300/worker
> - Firing cost = ₹500/worker
> - Holding cost = ₹5/unit/month
> - Backorder cost = ₹10/unit/month
> - Productivity = 100 units/worker/month
> - Beginning workforce = 6 workers, Beginning inventory = 100 units
>
> **Required:**
> **(a)** Develop a **Level Production Strategy** plan and compute the total cost. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Develop a **Chase Production Strategy** plan and compute the total cost. Compare with the level strategy. (7 marks)

---

#### PART (a) — Level Strategy Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute average demand per month (1 mark)**

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{500 + 700 + 800 + 600 + 400 + 900}{6} = \frac{3900}{6} = 650 \text{ units/month}
$$

**Step 2: Compute average production (Level) (1 mark)**

To avoid ending inventory build-up, set production = average demand = 650 units/month.

Workers required = $\dfrac{650}{100} = 6.5 \approx 7$ workers (rounded up to avoid shortage). We use **7 workers** producing 700 units/month (assume fractional workers not allowed).

Adjust: Production = 700 units/month.

**Step 3: Compute workforce changes (1 mark)**

Beginning workforce = 6 → End = 7. Hiring = 1 worker.
Hiring cost = $1 \times 300 = ₹300$. No firing.

**Step 4: Build the inventory balance table (3 marks)**

$$
I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t
$$

| Month | Demand $D_t$ | Production $P_t$ | Begin Inv. | End Inv. $I_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 700 | 100 | 100 + 700 − 500 = **300** |
| 2 | 700 | 700 | 300 | 300 + 700 − 700 = **300** |
| 3 | 800 | 700 | 300 | 300 + 700 − 800 = **200** |
| 4 | 600 | 700 | 200 | 200 + 700 − 600 = **300** |
| 5 | 400 | 700 | 300 | 300 + 700 − 400 = **600** |
| 6 | 900 | 700 | 600 | 600 + 700 − 900 = **400** |

**Step 5: Compute Total Cost (1 mark)**

| Cost Component | Calculation | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Regular Production | $700 \times 6 \times 40$ | 168,000 |
| Hiring | $1 \times 300$ | 300 |
| Holding | $(300+300+200+300+600+400) \times 5$ | 10,500 |
| **Total** | | **₹1,78,800** |

---

#### PART (b) — Chase Strategy Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Production = Demand in each month (1 mark)**

| Month | Demand | Workers Required | Production |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 5 | 500 |
| 2 | 700 | 7 | 700 |
| 3 | 800 | 8 | 800 |
| 4 | 600 | 6 | 600 |
| 5 | 400 | 4 | 400 |
| 6 | 900 | 9 | 900 |

**Step 2: Compute hiring and firing (2 marks)**

| Transition | Workers Change | Action | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Month 0 → 1 | 6 → 5 | Fire 1 | $1 \times 500 = ₹500$ |
| Month 1 → 2 | 5 → 7 | Hire 2 | $2 \times 300 = ₹600$ |
| Month 2 → 3 | 7 → 8 | Hire 1 | $1 \times 300 = ₹300$ |
| Month 3 → 4 | 8 → 6 | Fire 2 | $2 \times 500 = ₹1000$ |
| Month 4 → 5 | 6 → 4 | Fire 2 | $2 \times 500 = ₹1000$ |
| Month 5 → 6 | 4 → 9 | Hire 5 | $5 \times 300 = ₹1500$ |

Total hiring = ₹2400; Total firing = ₹2500.

**Step 3: Inventory & backorder handling (2 marks)**

Beginning inventory = 100 units. Use inventory before any production.

| Month | Begin Inv. | Demand | Production | Used from Inv. | End Inv. | Backlog |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 500 | 400 | 100 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 700 | 700 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

(For simplicity, assume chase produces exactly demand, so no inventory holding beyond initial use.)

**Step 4: Total Cost (2 marks)**

| Component | Calculation | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Production | $(500+700+800+600+400+900) \times 40 = 3900 \times 40$ | 156,000 |
| Hiring | | 2,400 |
| Firing | | 2,500 |
| Holding | Minimal | 0 |
| **Total** | | **₹1,60,900** |

**Comparison:** Chase Strategy (₹1,60,900) < Level Strategy (₹1,78,800).

**Chase is preferred** in this scenario because hiring/firing costs are low relative to holding costs.

---

### OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> A factory uses **20,000 units/year** of a raw material. The ordering cost is **₹500 per order** and the holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. Lead time is **6 days**, and the firm operates **250 days/year**.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, the **number of orders per year**, the **cycle time**, and the **total annual cost**. (7 marks)
> **(b)** If the supplier offers a **quantity discount** of 2% on unit price for orders ≥ 1500 units, compute the **effective EOQ** under this discount scheme and determine whether the discount should be accepted. Assume unit price = ₹100. (7 marks)

---

#### PART (a) — EOQ Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $D = 20{,}000$ units/year
- $S = ₹500$/order
- $H = ₹10$/unit/year
- Operating days = 250

**Step 1: EOQ (2 marks)**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20000 \times 500}{10}} = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000} = 1414.21 \approx 1414 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Number of orders per year (1 mark)**

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{20000}{1414.21} = 14.14 \approx 14 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 3: Cycle Time (1 mark)**

$$
T = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{1414.21}{20000} \times 250 = 17.68 \approx 17.68 \text{ days}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
T = \frac{250}{14.14} = 17.68 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4: Total Annual Cost (2 marks)**

$$
TC = \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{HQ^*}{2} + P \cdot D
$$

$$
TC = \frac{20000 \times 500}{1414.21} + \frac{10 \times 1414.21}{2} + 100 \times 20000
$$

$$
TC = 7071.07 + 7071.07 + 2{,}000{,}000 = ₹20{,}14{,}142.14
$$

Or using the shortcut:

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH} + P \cdot D = \sqrt{2 \times 20000 \times 500 \times 10} + 2{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{200{,}000{,}000} + 2{,}000{,}000 = 14142.14 + 2{,}000{,}000 = ₹20{,}14{,}142.14
$$

**Total ordering cost = Total holding cost = ₹7,071.07** (verifies balance principle ✓).

---

#### PART (b) — Quantity Discount Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1: Discount Scenario Setup (1 mark)**

- Discount = 2% on unit price of ₹100 → Discounted price = ₹98.
- Discounted holding cost = $i \cdot P_{disc} = 0.10 \times 98 = ₹9.80$/unit/year (assuming $i = 10\%$).
- Discount break quantity = $Q_b = 1500$ units.

**Step 2: Compute Adjusted EOQ (1 mark)**

$$
Q^*_{disc} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20000 \times 500}{9.80}} = \sqrt{2040816.33} \approx 1428.71 \text{ units}
$$

Since $Q^*_{disc} = 1428.71 < 1500$ (break quantity), we **cannot** simply order the adjusted EOQ. We must order the **break quantity** $Q_b = 1500$.

**Step 3: Compute Total Cost at $Q = 1500$ with discount (2 marks)**

$$
TC_{1500} = \frac{20000 \times 500}{1500} + \frac{9.80 \times 1500}{2} + 98 \times 20000
$$

$$
TC_{1500} = 6666.67 + 7350 + 1{,}960{,}000 = ₹19{,}74{,}016.67
$$

**Step 4: Compute Total Cost at $Q^* = 1414$ without discount (1 mark)**

$$
TC_{1414} = 7071.07 + 7071.07 + 2{,}000{,}000 = ₹20{,}14{,}142.14
$$

**Step 5: Decision (2 marks)**

Savings with discount = $20{,}14{,}142.14 - 19{,}74{,}016.67 = ₹40{,}125.47$ per year.

**Since savings > 0, ACCEPT the quantity discount** and order $Q = 1500$ units per cycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Holding Cost:** If $H$ is given as a percentage of unit price, students often forget to convert. Always write $H = i \times c$ explicitly.
> 2. **EOQ ≠ Always Optimal Under Discounts:** If the EOQ falls below the discount break, students must compare $TC$ at break quantity, not at EOQ. KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 3. **Inventory Balance Sign Errors:** Aggregate planning problems: $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$. Reversing this loses 1–2 marks per error.
> 4. **Forgetting Workforce Productivity:** Workers needed = Demand ÷ Productivity per worker. Missing this gives wrong workforce counts.
> 5. **Level Strategy ≠ Constant Inventory:** Inventory fluctuates; only *production* and *workforce* are constant. Many students incorrectly state inventory is constant.
> 6. **Forgetting Reorder Point:** $ROP = d \times L + SS$. Students often compute EOQ but ignore lead time and safety stock.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Aggregate Planning** = Medium-range (3–18 months) planning of production, workforce, and inventory to meet demand at minimum cost.
- ☐ **Inventory Modeling** = Mathematical optimization of stock levels using EOQ/EPQ frameworks.
- ☐ **Resource Allocation** = Trade-off between fixed (workforce) and variable (overtime, subcontract) resources.

### Key Formulas (Memorize with Units)
- ☐ $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — **EOQ**
- ☐ $TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ — **Minimum cost**
- ☐ $Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{(2DS/H) \cdot (p/(p-d))}$ — **EPQ**
- ☐ $N = D/Q^*$, $T = Q^*/D$ — **Orders per year, cycle time**
- ☐ $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ — **Inventory balance**
- ☐ At EOQ: **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** ← *Balance principle*

### Strategy Comparison
- ☐ **Chase:** Variable production, low inventory, high hiring/firing cost.
- ☐ **Level:** Constant production, high inventory, low hiring/firing cost.
- ☐ **Mixed:** Uses overtime/subcontract; usually lowest cost.

### Lot-Sizing Heuristics (in order of complexity)
- ☐ **L4L** (simplest) → **EOQ** → **POQ** → **PPB** → **LUC** → **Silver-Meal** → **Wagner-Whitin** (optimal)

### Cost Components (Eight total)
- ☐ Regular production, Overtime, Hiring, Firing, Holding, Backorder, Subcontracting, Material.

### Aggregate Planning Solution Methods
- ☐ Graphical/Cumulative chart → Spreadsheet trial-and-error → Linear Programming → Transportation method → Simulation.

### Exam-Day Golden Rules
- ☐ Always state assumptions clearly.
- ☐ Show cost breakdown table, not lump sum.
- ☐ Verify EOQ using second derivative (mention $d^2TC/dQ^2 > 0$).
- ☐ For discount problems: compare $TC$ at break quantity, not at adjusted EOQ.
- ☐ Convert holding-cost percentage to absolute value before substitution.
- ☐ Mention safety stock and reorder point for full-credit inventory answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
