topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Make/Buy Decision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Make-or-Buy Decision** is a strategic management accounting and operations management decision that determines whether a particular component, product, or service should be produced **internally (in-house / "Make")** within the organization's own production facilities, or whether it should be **procured from an external supplier/vendor ("Buy")**. It is essentially a form of **insourcing versus outsourcing** evaluation, governed by quantitative cost analysis and qualitative strategic factors.

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics context, this decision is treated as a **quantitative cost-volume analysis problem** supported by **relevance costing** principles, where only *avoidable* (incremental) costs and revenues are considered, and *sunk* / *committed* costs are deliberately ignored.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relevance Costing Principle:** Only future costs that differ between the two alternatives (Make vs. Buy) are relevant. Sunk costs, allocated fixed overheads, and book values of existing assets are **irrelevant** and must be excluded from the analysis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Home Cooking vs. Restaurant" Intuition 🍳

Imagine you run a busy household and need lunch every day. You have two options:

| Aspect | 🍳 **MAKE (Cook at home)** | 🍕 **BUY (Order from restaurant)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct effort** | You spend time cooking | Effort is zero; someone else does it |
| **Variable cost** | Raw materials (vegetables, rice, spices) | Price of the meal |
| **Fixed cost** | Your kitchen, gas stove, utensils | Delivery app subscription, phone |
| **Quality control** | You control salt, spice, hygiene | Trust the restaurant's standards |
| **Capacity** | Limited by your kitchen size & time | Unlimited but at a per-unit cost |
| **Strategic concern** | You can experiment with recipes | Consistent but no customization |

A **Make/Buy Decision** in a business is exactly this — except the stakes run into **lakhs or crores of rupees**, the menu is a product catalogue, and the analysis must be **rigorously numerical**. The question is: *Should we set up our own production line for Component X, or should we sign a long-term supply contract with an external vendor?*

---

## 1.3 Why Is This Decision a KTU 2024 High-Priority Topic?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECBT755, Module 2):** Make/Buy Decisions fall under the cluster of *Short-term Managerial Decision-Making using Business Analytics*. They are heavily tested because they:
> 1. Combine **cost accounting**, **operations strategy**, and **quantitative modeling**.
> 2. Appear routinely in **Part A (3 marks)** as direct questions and in **Part B (14 marks)** as full numerical problems with sub-parts.
> 3. Test the student's ability to distinguish **relevant from irrelevant costs** — a classic valuation trap.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

- **Relevant Cost (Avoidable Cost)** — Cost that will *not* be incurred if a particular alternative is chosen.
- **Opportunity Cost** — Benefit foregone by not using a scarce resource in its best alternative use.
- **Shut-down / Discontinuation Cost** — Cost saved by ceasing internal production.
- **Standard Unit = ₹ / unit** for variable cost analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of KTU Valuation:** Examiners award **full marks only if the student correctly identifies relevant vs. irrelevant costs**. Simply subtracting "total cost of making" from "total cost of buying" without filtering relevant items guarantees significant mark loss.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for the Decision

The Make/Buy decision proceeds in a structured sequence. The KTU 2024 examiner expects the answer to follow this **5-step logic spine**.

### **Step 1 — Identify the Two Alternatives**
- **Alternative A:** Make the item internally (in-house production).
- **Alternative B:** Buy the item from an external supplier.

### **Step 2 — Compute the Relevant Cost of "Making"**
Sum up all *avoidable* costs if the firm produces in-house:
- Direct Materials (per unit × annual quantity)
- Direct Labour (per unit × annual quantity)
- Direct / Variable Overheads (avoidable portion only)
- **Avoidable Fixed Overheads** (only the portion that disappears if production stops)
- Opportunity Cost of using in-house capacity (e.g., rental income, contribution from next-best sale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excluded (Irrelevant) Costs in "Make" Column:**
> - Depreciation on existing machines (sunk / committed cost)
> - Allocated / Apportioned Fixed Overheads (not directly attributable)
> - Past R&D costs
> - Salary of the factory supervisor if the plant continues for other products

### **Step 3 — Compute the Relevant Cost of "Buying"**
- Purchase price per unit (often the largest component)
- Inward freight, inspection, handling costs
- Any incremental administrative cost of procurement
- Less: Salvage value of released capacity, or contribution from alternative use

### **Step 4 — Compare the Two Relevant Cost Streams**
- If **Relevant Cost of Make < Relevant Cost of Buy** → **MAKE** the item.
- If **Relevant Cost of Buy < Relevant Cost of Make** → **BUY** the item.

### **Step 5 — Layer the Qualitative / Strategic Filters**
Pure cost analysis is *necessary but not sufficient*. KTU board answers that ignore strategic factors typically lose 2–3 marks. Strategic factors include:
- **Quality and reliability** of the external supplier.
- **Protection of proprietary technology** (e.g., trade secrets).
- **Long-term supply security** and dependence risk.
- **Impact on existing workforce** (industrial relations).
- **Flexibility and lead time.**

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Application in Make/Buy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relevant Cost of Making (per unit) | $RC_M$ | $RC_M = DM + DL + VO + AFC_{avoid} + OC$ |
| 2 | Relevant Cost of Buying (per unit) | $RC_B$ | $RC_B = P + F + Adm - S$ |
| 3 | Total Annual Relevant Cost of Making | $TRC_M$ | $TRC_M = RC_M \times Q$ |
| 4 | Total Annual Relevant Cost of Buying | $TRC_B$ | $TRC_B = RC_B \times Q$ |
| 5 | **Net Advantage of Buying (NAB)** | $NAB$ | $NAB = TRC_M - TRC_B$ |
| 6 | Net Advantage of Making (NAM) | $NAM$ | $NAM = TRC_B - TRC_M$ |
| 7 | Indifference Volume (Break-even Q) | $Q^*$ | $Q^* = \dfrac{F_B - F_M}{V_M - V_B}$ |
| 8 | Opportunity Cost of Released Capacity | $OC$ | Contribution foregone on displaced sales |
| 9 | Decision Rule (positive NAB) | — | If $NAB > 0$ → Buy; if $NAB < 0$ → Make |

**Where:**
- $DM$ = Direct Material cost per unit
- $DL$ = Direct Labour cost per unit
- $VO$ = Variable Overhead per unit
- $AFC_{avoid}$ = Avoidable Fixed Cost per unit (i.e., total avoidable FC ÷ Q)
- $OC$ = Opportunity Cost per unit of using capacity
- $P$ = Purchase price per unit
- $F$ = Freight / inspection / handling per unit
- $Adm$ = Incremental admin cost per unit
- $S$ = Salvage / alternative-use benefit per unit
- $Q$ = Annual quantity required
- $F_B, F_M$ = Total annual fixed cost under Buy / Make
- $V_B, V_M$ = Variable cost per unit under Buy / Make

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoidable Fixed Cost per unit** $= \dfrac{\text{Total Avoidable Fixed Overhead}}{\text{Annual Demand (Q)}}$. This is a *fixed* number in total, but when expressed per unit it **varies inversely with quantity** — a classic exam trap.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In Industry:** Make/Buy analysis is foundational to **Outsourcing Strategy, Contract Manufacturing, and Vertical Integration decisions** in:
> - Automobile OEMs (Ashok Leyland, Tata Motors) deciding to *make* engine blocks vs. *buy* from Bosch.
> - IT firms deciding to *make* (develop) software in-house vs. *buy* a SaaS license.
> - Electronics hardware firms choosing between in-house PCB assembly vs. contract EMS partners (Foxconn, Flex).
>
> **In KTU Valuation:** The examiner treats Make/Buy as a **relevance-costing problem** that can be extended into the *Indifference Point* concept, where the decision flips at a specific volume threshold.

---

## 2.4 The Indifference Point — Extended Concept

When fixed costs differ between Make and Buy, the cheaper option **switches** as quantity changes. The **indifference volume** $Q^*$ is the quantity at which total relevant costs of Make and Buy are exactly equal.

$$Q^* = \dfrac{\text{Total Avoidable Fixed Cost Difference}}{\text{Variable Cost Difference per unit}}$$

- If $Q < Q^*$ → **Buy** is cheaper (lower fixed burden).
- If $Q > Q^*$ → **Make** is cheaper (fixed cost gets spread over more units).

> [!NOTE]
> This converts the Make/Buy problem into a **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) break-even** structure, which is a favourite KTU 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note on Presentation:** Every numerical answer in KTU must show: (1) Statement of relevant cost per unit, (2) Total relevant cost computation, (3) Decision rule applied, (4) Final recommendation with strategic justification. Skipping step (1) is the most common cause of partial credit loss.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Make vs. Buy (Classic 7-Mark Sub-Question)

### **Problem Statement**

> A KTU engineering firm requires **10,000 units of Part-X** per year for its main product. The firm can either manufacture Part-X in-house or purchase it from an outside supplier. The cost structure is given below:
>
> | Cost Element | Make (₹/unit) | Buy (₹/unit) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Direct Material | 60 | — |
> | Direct Labour | 40 | — |
> | Direct Expenses | 20 | — |
> | Variable Overhead | 30 | — |
> | Fixed Overhead (allocated) | 50 | — |
> | Avoidable Fixed Overhead (if buying) | 20 | — |
> | Purchase Price | — | 180 |
> | Inward Freight & Inspection | — | 10 |
>
> Should the firm **Make** or **Buy** Part-X? Show calculations with clear identification of relevant costs.

---

### **Step 1 — Identify Relevant Cost per Unit Under "Make"**

| Item | Amount (₹/unit) | Relevant? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Material | 60 | ✅ Yes | Future, avoidable |
| Direct Labour | 40 | ✅ Yes | Future, avoidable |
| Direct Expenses | 20 | ✅ Yes | Future, avoidable |
| Variable Overhead | 30 | ✅ Yes | Future, avoidable |
| Avoidable Fixed Overhead | 20 | ✅ Yes | Disappears if we stop making |
| Allocated Fixed Overhead | 50 | ❌ No | Sunk / will be re-allocated elsewhere |

$$\boxed{RC_M = 60 + 40 + 20 + 30 + 20 = 170 \text{ ₹/unit}}$$

### **Step 2 — Identify Relevant Cost per Unit Under "Buy"**

| Item | Amount (₹/unit) | Relevant? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Purchase Price | 180 | ✅ Yes | Future cash outflow |
| Inward Freight & Inspection | 10 | ✅ Yes | Future, incremental |

$$\boxed{RC_B = 180 + 10 = 190 \text{ ₹/unit}}$$

### **Step 3 — Compute Total Annual Relevant Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC_M &= RC_M \times Q = 170 \times 10{,}000 = 17{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
TRC_B &= RC_B \times Q = 190 \times 10{,}000 = 19{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4 — Apply Decision Rule (Net Advantage of Making)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NAM &= TRC_B - TRC_M \\
NAM &= 19{,}00{,}000 - 17{,}00{,}000 \\
\boxed{NAM &= 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Decision & Justification**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision: MAKE the Part-X in-house.**
> **Quantitative Justification:** Making saves ₹ 2,00,000 per year in relevant costs.
> **Strategic Add-on (to be written in exam):** However, the firm must also verify the supplier's quality, lead time reliability, and long-term price stability before finalizing.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Make/Buy with Opportunity Cost and Released Capacity

> A company manufactures a sub-component at ₹ 250 per unit (variable cost). Annual demand is **8,000 units**. The supplier offers the same component at ₹ 220 per unit, but with a freight of ₹ 15/unit. If the company buys, the released machine capacity can be used to manufacture another product generating a **contribution of ₹ 1,20,000 per year**, and avoidable fixed overhead savings amount to **₹ 60,000 per year**.
>
> **Should the company make or buy?**

### **Step 1 — Relevant Cost Under "Make"**

$$RC_M = 250 \text{ ₹/unit (given as full variable cost)}$$

### **Step 2 — Relevant Cost Under "Buy" (Per Unit)**

$$RC_B = 220 + 15 = 235 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

### **Step 3 — Total Annual Relevant Cost — Make**

$$
TRC_M = 250 \times 8{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

### **Step 4 — Total Annual Relevant Cost — Buy**

For the *Buy* option, the firm also **saves** ₹ 60,000 in fixed cost and **gains** ₹ 1,20,000 contribution from alternative use of capacity. These are *reductions* in the cost of buying.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC_B &= (235 \times 8{,}000) - 60{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 \\
TRC_B &= 18{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 \\
\boxed{TRC_B &= 16{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Net Advantage of Buying**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NAB &= TRC_M - TRC_B \\
NAB &= 20{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}20{,}000 \\
\boxed{NAB &= 3{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 6 — Decision**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision: BUY** the component externally. The firm saves ₹ 3,80,000 annually in net relevant terms. Buying becomes attractive because of the **opportunity benefit** of ₹ 1,80,000 from released capacity — a critical strategic insight often missed by students.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Indifference Volume Calculation (Advanced 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> Two production options exist for Component-Z:
> - **Make:** Variable cost = ₹ 140/unit; Fixed cost = ₹ 6,00,000/year
> - **Buy:** Variable cost = ₹ 200/unit; Fixed cost = ₹ 1,00,000/year (admin & tooling)
>
> At what annual volume does the decision switch? Recommend the choice for a demand of **7,000 units/year**.

### **Step 1 — Indifference Volume Formula**

$$
Q^* = \dfrac{F_M - F_B}{V_B - V_M}
$$

(Where $F_M > F_B$ for the formula to yield a positive value)

### **Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{200 - 140} \\
Q^* &= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{60} \\
\boxed{Q^* &= 8{,}333.33 \text{ units (approx.)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3 — Decision at Q = 7,000 units**

Since $Q = 7{,}000 < Q^* = 8{,}333$, the firm is operating *below* the indifference point. **Buying is cheaper** at this volume because the firm has not yet spread its large fixed cost of in-house production over enough units.

### **Step 4 — Verification by Direct Comparison**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC_M &= (140 \times 7{,}000) + 6{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}80{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}80{,}000 \\
TRC_B &= (200 \times 7{,}000) + 1{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 \\
NAB   &= 15{,}80{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ ₹ saved by buying.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Learning Point:** Below the indifference volume, **fixed-cost-light** options win. Above it, **fixed-cost-heavy** in-house production becomes efficient — the same logic that drives the airline industry's make-vs-lease decisions on aircraft.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Approach by Question Type

| Question Pattern | Key Diagnostic Cue | Primary Concept Tested | Risk of Losing Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two cost tables given (Make vs. Buy) | "Allocated" or "Apportioned" appears | Identifying relevant vs. irrelevant costs | Including allocated fixed cost in Make side |
| Released capacity, alternative use | "Can be used for another product…" | Opportunity cost | Forgetting to subtract opportunity benefit from Buy side |
| Per unit vs. Total ambiguity | Costs given as total lump-sum FC | Avoidable FC per unit = FC / Q | Not dividing by quantity |
| "At what volume…?" | Break-even / Indifference phrasing | Indifference point formula | Sign error in numerator |
| Strategic + Quantitative mix | "Discuss" or "Advise" | Qualitative filters | Omitting strategic commentary |

---

## 3.5 Master Derivation — From First Principles of Total Cost

We start with the **Total Annual Relevant Cost** identity for each option:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC_M(Q) &= (V_M \cdot Q) + F_M - B_M(Q) \\
TRC_B(Q) &= (V_B \cdot Q) + F_B - B_B(Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $B_M$ and $B_B$ are *non-cost benefits* (salvage, opportunity gains, subsidies).

At the indifference volume $Q^*$, we enforce $TRC_M = TRC_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_M \cdot Q^* + F_M - B_M &= V_B \cdot Q^* + F_B - B_B \\
(V_M - V_B) \cdot Q^* &= (F_B - B_B) - (F_M - B_M) \\
\boxed{Q^* &= \dfrac{(F_B - B_B) - (F_M - B_M)}{V_M - V_B}}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the **basic form** (no benefits), $B_M = B_B = 0$, reducing to:

$$
Q^* = \dfrac{F_M - F_B}{V_B - V_M}
$$

This is the canonical form KTU examiners expect students to reproduce under the sub-question "Derive the indifference point formula" in a 14-mark problem.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Make/Buy Decision Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Demand Q units of Item] --> B[Identify Two Alternatives]
    B --> C[Alternative M: MAKE In-House]
    B --> D[Alternative B: BUY from Supplier]
    C --> E[Compute Relevant Cost of Make]
    D --> F[Compute Relevant Cost of Buy]
    E --> G[Apply Relevant Costing Filter]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Compare TRC_M vs TRC_B}
    H -->|TRC_M less than TRC_B| I[Decision: MAKE]
    H -->|TRC_B less than TRC_M| J[Decision: BUY]
    H -->|Equal| K[Indifferent at this Q]
    I --> L[Check Strategic Filters]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Qualitative Criteria Met?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Final Recommendation]
    M -->|No| O[Reconsider Alternative]
    O --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The arrow chain **A → B → C/D → E/F → G → H** represents the quantitative spine. The branch **L → M → N** is the strategic overlay that distinguishes a *good* answer from an *excellent* one in KTU valuation.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of Relevant Cost Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAKE["MAKE Alternative Cost Stack"]
        M1[Direct Material DM]
        M2[Direct Labour DL]
        M3[Variable Overhead VO]
        M4[Avoidable Fixed Overhead AFC]
        M5[Opportunity Cost OC]
    end
    subgraph BUY["BUY Alternative Cost Stack"]
        B1[Purchase Price P]
        B2[Freight and Inspection F]
        B3[Admin Cost Adm]
        B4[Salvage or Alternative-Use Benefit S]
    end
    M1 --> RCM[RC_M = Sum of Make Costs]
    M2 --> RCM
    M3 --> RCM
    M4 --> RCM
    M5 --> RCM
    B1 --> RCB[RC_B = Sum of Buy Costs]
    B2 --> RCB
    B3 --> RCB
    B4 --> RCB
    RCM --> NAB[Compare TRC_M and TRC_B to find NAB]
    RCB --> NAB
    NAB --> DEC[Final Make or Buy Decision]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Indifference Volume Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Given V_M, F_M, V_B, F_B] --> Q[Compute Q star = F_M minus F_B over V_B minus V_M]
    Q --> R{Demand Q vs Q star}
    R -->|Q less than Q star| S[Buy is Optimal]
    R -->|Q greater than Q star| T[Make is Optimal]
    R -->|Q equals Q star| U[Indifferent Either Option]
    S --> V[Verify with Total Cost Cross-Check]
    T --> V
    U --> V
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Visual Expectation:** A block diagram or flowchart embedded in the answer is **not mandatory**, but when used it shows structured thinking and can fetch 1–2 grace marks in borderline cases. It is especially useful in 14-mark problems where the answer length is substantial.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**"Differentiate between 'Make' and 'Buy' decisions. State any two qualitative factors that influence this decision."** *(3 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer

A **Make decision** means producing a component or providing a service *internally* using the organization's own resources, facilities, and workforce. A **Buy decision** means procuring the same from an *external vendor* or supplier, typically through a long-term supply contract.

**Two qualitative factors:**

1. **Quality and Reliability of Supplier:** If the external supplier cannot match the in-house quality standard consistently, the firm should make in-house, even at a higher cost.
2. **Confidentiality and Protection of Proprietary Technology:** If the component involves a trade secret, patent, or proprietary know-how, buying it from an outsider risks technology leakage, and the firm should prefer making.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Industrial relations impact, lead time flexibility, long-term supply security.)* — **Full 3 marks** for definition (1.5) + two factors (1.5).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**"Explain the concept of 'relevant cost' in the context of a Make/Buy decision. Why are sunk costs excluded?"** *(3 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer

**Relevant cost** is the cost that will be *incurred in the future* and will *differ between the two alternatives* (Make or Buy). Only such avoidable, incremental costs are considered in the analysis.

**Sunk costs are excluded** because:
- They represent past cash outflows that have already been incurred and cannot be recovered regardless of whether the firm makes or buys.
- Including them would distort the decision by attributing a cost that does not change with the choice.
- This is the fundamental principle of **relevance costing** — only *future, differential* costs are decision-relevant.

**Examples of irrelevant (sunk) costs in Make/Buy:** Depreciation on existing machinery, historical R&D expenditure, apportioned / allocated fixed overheads that will continue irrespective of the decision.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Full Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Every 14-mark question in Module 2 carries an **internal choice**, meaning students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both must be attempted in the answer key to demonstrate coverage. Below, BOTH alternatives are solved exhaustively for reference.

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Make vs. Buy with Opportunity Cost and Indifference Volume**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2]** *(14 marks)*
>
> XYZ Engineering Ltd. is evaluating whether to **make or buy** Part-M used in its main product. Annual demand is **12,000 units**. The internal production cost data is as follows:
>
> | Cost Element | Per Unit (₹) |
> |---|---|
> | Direct Material | 75 |
> | Direct Labour | 45 |
> | Variable Overhead | 30 |
> | Allocated Fixed Overhead (irrelevant) | 50 |
> | Avoidable Fixed Overhead (if buying) | 25 |
>
> An external supplier has quoted **₹ 200 per unit** with **freight of ₹ 10/unit** and an additional **annual tooling cost of ₹ 1,20,000** for the firm.
>
> If the firm buys, it can rent out the released factory space for **₹ 4,80,000 per year**.
>
> **Required (in KTU answer-book style):**
>
> **(a)** Compute the relevant cost per unit of Make and Buy options, and state the annual cost difference. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Find the **indifference volume** between the two options. Comment on the decision at the current demand level. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Relevant Cost per Unit (7 marks)

**Step 1: Relevant Cost of "Make" (RC_M)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RC_M &= DM + DL + VO + AFC_{avoid} \\
RC_M &= 75 + 45 + 30 + 25 \\
\boxed{RC_M &= 175 \text{ ₹/unit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note: Allocated Fixed Overhead of ₹ 50 is **excluded** as it is a sunk/irrelevant cost.*

**Step 2: Relevant Cost of "Buy" (RC_B) — Per Unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RC_B &= \text{Purchase Price} + \text{Freight} \\
RC_B &= 200 + 10 \\
\boxed{RC_B &= 210 \text{ ₹/unit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Annual Relevant Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC_M &= 175 \times 12{,}000 = 21{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
TRC_B &= (210 \times 12{,}000) + 1{,}20{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 \\
TRC_B &= 25{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 \\
\boxed{TRC_B &= 21{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net Advantage of Making (NAM)**

$$
\boxed{NAM = TRC_B - TRC_M = 21{,}60{,}000 - 21{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ ₹}}
$$

> **Decision (Part a):** Since $NAM = ₹ 60{,}000 > 0$, the firm should **MAKE** Part-M. The annual net saving is ₹ 60,000.

---

### Part (b) — Indifference Volume (7 marks)

**Step 1: Set up the Indifference Equation**

We treat the **Make** option as having variable cost $V_M = 175$ and fixed cost $F_M$ (avoidable FC allocated to in-house production).

For the Make option, the total avoidable fixed cost is $AFC_{avoid} \times Q = 25 \times 12{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year (at current demand).

For the Buy option: $V_B = 210$ ₹/unit, $F_B = 1{,}20{,}000$ ₹/year (tooling).

**Step 2: Indifference Volume Formula**

$$
Q^* = \dfrac{F_M - F_B}{V_B - V_M} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000}{210 - 175}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^* = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{35} = 5{,}142.86 \text{ units (approx.)}}
$$

**Step 3: Decision at Q = 12,000**

Since $Q = 12{,}000 \gg Q^* = 5{,}143$, the firm is operating *well above* the indifference volume. **MAKE is preferred** at high volumes because the higher fixed cost of in-house production is spread over more units.

**Step 4: Strategic Commentary (Mandatory for full marks)**

> *The quantitative decision favours making, but the firm should also consider:*
> - **Quality consistency** of in-house output.
> - **Capacity utilization** of the plant.
> - **Strategic independence** from external supplier for a critical part.
> - **Opportunity cost of ₹ 4,80,000 rental income** that is foregone by making — though even after this opportunity loss, Make remains cheaper by ₹ 60,000.

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]** *(14 marks)*
>
> A firm currently manufactures Component-P at a total cost of **₹ 400 per unit** (₹ 300 variable + ₹ 100 fixed, of which ₹ 60 is avoidable). The annual requirement is **6,000 units**.
>
> An outside supplier offers the same component at **₹ 320 per unit**. The firm will additionally incur **₹ 40,000 per year** in inspection and procurement administration if it buys. The released capacity can be used to manufacture another product yielding a **contribution of ₹ 1,00,000 per year**.
>
> **Required:**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **relevant cost of making and buying**, and advise whether the firm should make or buy. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** If the supplier later increases the quoted price to **₹ 350 per unit** (with all other terms unchanged), recompute the decision and find the **indifference purchase price**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — Relevant Cost Comparison (7 marks)

**Relevant Cost of Make per unit:**

$$
RC_M = 300 + 60 = 360 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

*(Only avoidable fixed cost of ₹ 60 is relevant; the remaining ₹ 40 fixed is irrelevant/sunk.)*

**Relevant Cost of Buy per unit:**

The ₹ 40,000 annual admin cost is a *fixed* cost of buying, not per unit. Hence:

$$
RC_B = 320 \text{ ₹/unit (variable)} + \text{Admin cost per unit} = 320 + \dfrac{40{,}000}{6{,}000} = 320 + 6.67 = 326.67 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

**Total Annual Relevant Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC_M &= 360 \times 6{,}000 = 21{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
TRC_B &= (320 \times 6{,}000) + 40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\
TRC_B &= 19{,}20{,}000 + 40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\boxed{TRC_B &= 18{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net Advantage of Buying:**

$$
NAB = TRC_M - TRC_B = 21{,}60{,}000 - 18{,}60{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

> **Decision (Part a):** **BUY** the component. Net saving is ₹ 3,00,000 per year, primarily because the released capacity generates a ₹ 1,00,000 opportunity contribution.

---

### Part (b) — Recompute at New Price & Indifference Price (7 marks)

**Step 1: At Supplier's New Price = ₹ 350/unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC_B^{new} &= (350 \times 6{,}000) + 40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\
TRC_B^{new} &= 21{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\
TRC_B^{new} &= 20{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
NAB^{new} = 21{,}60{,}000 - 20{,}40{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

> **Decision at new price:** Still **BUY**, but the margin of advantage has shrunk from ₹ 3,00,000 to ₹ 1,20,000.

**Step 2: Indifference Purchase Price (P\*)**

At indifference, $TRC_M = TRC_B$:

$$
21{,}60{,}000 = (P^* \times 6{,}000) + 40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P^* \times 6{,}000 = 21{,}60{,}000 - 40{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 22{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{P^* = 370 \text{ ₹/unit}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** If the supplier's price exceeds ₹ 370/unit, the firm should *switch back to making*. Below ₹ 370, buying remains advantageous.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Make/Buy Problems:**
>
> 1. **Including Allocated Fixed Overhead:** The most frequent error. Allocated / apportioned fixed overheads that *continue* even if production stops are **not avoidable** and must be **excluded**. Including them inflates "Make" cost and biases the decision wrongly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Opportunity Cost of Released Capacity:** When a "Make" decision frees up capacity, the *foregone* benefit of using that capacity elsewhere is an opportunity cost. Many students ignore this and arrive at the wrong recommendation.
>
> 3. **Confusing Per-Unit and Total Fixed Costs:** "Avoidable fixed overhead of ₹ 25 per unit" is *only valid at the stated demand*. For indifference point analysis, convert it to **total = ₹ 25 × Q** first, then apply the formula. Mixing units is a 1–2 mark killer.
>
> 4. **Omitting Qualitative Factors:** A purely numerical answer that does not mention **quality, supplier reliability, technology protection, or workforce impact** is graded conservatively by KTU examiners. Always add a 2–3 line strategic note.
>
> 5. **Sign Errors in the Indifference Formula:** The numerator is $F_M - F_B$ (or the *net fixed cost difference*, with the larger fixed cost first). A reversed sign yields a negative $Q^*$, which is mathematically meaningless and signals an incorrect approach.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this section 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Make/Buy Decision** = Insource vs. Outsource analysis based on **relevant costing**.
- ✅ **Relevant Cost = Future + Avoidable + Differential.** Sunk, committed, and allocated costs are **irrelevant**.
- ✅ **Make cost =** DM + DL + Variable OH + Avoidable Fixed OH + Opportunity Cost.
- ✅ **Buy cost =** Purchase Price + Freight + Incremental Admin − Alternative-Use Benefit.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** If $TRC_M < TRC_B$ → **MAKE**; else **BUY**.
- ✅ **NAB (Net Advantage of Buying) = $TRC_M - TRC_B$**; positive NAB means buy.
- ✅ **NAM (Net Advantage of Making) = $TRC_B - TRC_M$**; positive NAM means make.
- ✅ **Indifference Volume** $Q^* = \dfrac{F_M - F_B}{V_B - V_M}$ — the demand at which both options cost the same.
- ✅ Below $Q^*$ → **Buy wins** (lower fixed cost). Above $Q^*$ → **Make wins** (fixed cost spread over more units).
- ✅ **Always include** qualitative factors: quality, supplier reliability, technology secrecy, lead time, industrial relations.
- ✅ **Exam Formula to Memorise:**
  - $RC_M = DM + DL + VO + AFC_{avoid} + OC$
  - $RC_B = P + F + Adm - S$
  - $NAB = (RC_M - RC_B) \times Q + \text{Net Fixed Cost Savings} - \text{Opportunity Benefit}$
  - $Q^* = \dfrac{\Delta F}{\Delta V}$ where $\Delta F = F_M - F_B$ and $\Delta V = V_B - V_M$.
- ✅ **Cross-check** your final answer by computing $TRC_M$ and $TRC_B$ separately and comparing — never rely on a single difference-based calculation.
- ✅ **Pitfalls to avoid:** (1) Including allocated FC. (2) Missing opportunity cost. (3) Wrong indifference formula sign. (4) No qualitative commentary.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Make/Buy Decision**
> *Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme, PECBT755 — Business Analytics*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Marketing Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Marketing Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marketing Analytics (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Marketing Analytics is the practice of measuring, managing, and analyzing marketing performance data to understand its effectiveness, optimize return on investment (ROI), and maximize the efficiency of marketing campaigns and consumer engagement initiatives. It is the systematic application of quantitative tools — statistics, predictive modelling, and data mining — to marketing decision-making.*

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics framework, marketing analytics is positioned as a **functional subdomain** of business intelligence that bridges raw consumer data with strategic marketing actions. It operates on the foundational data pipeline: **Data Collection → Data Processing → Modelling → Insight Generation → Marketing Action**.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Marketing Analytics (Gartner Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies marketing analytics along four descriptive–prescriptive levels:

| Level | Pillar | Core Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar 1** | Descriptive Analytics | *"What happened?"* |
| **Pillar 2** | Diagnostic Analytics | *"Why did it happen?"* |
| **Pillar 3** | Predictive Analytics | *"What will happen?"* |
| **Pillar 4** | Prescriptive Analytics | *"What should we do?"* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Pearl (CO1: Recall Level):**
> Marketing Analytics answers two fundamental business questions — (i) *What drove past marketing outcomes?* and (ii) *How can future marketing investment be optimized?* The four-pillar hierarchy moves sequentially from hindsight to foresight.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Stethoscope" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition (Student-Friendly):**
> Imagine you are a doctor treating a patient. Before prescribing medicine, you don't guess — you check temperature, blood pressure, ECG, and lab reports. *Marketing Analytics is the **stethoscope of the marketing department**.* It provides vital signs (KPIs like conversion rate, churn, CLV) so that marketing managers don't "prescribe campaigns" blindly. Just as a doctor interprets reports to diagnose and treat, the marketing analyst interprets data to plan and optimize.

A second analogy: **Marketing Analytics is to Marketing what Accounting is to Finance** — a discipline of measurement, accuracy, and evidence-based decision-making.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Vocabulary (KTU Glossary)

- **KPI (Key Performance Indicator):** A quantifiable metric used to evaluate the success of a marketing campaign.
- **Customer Touchpoint:** Any interaction where a consumer engages with a brand (ad click, store visit, call centre).
- **Conversion:** The moment a prospect completes a desired action (purchase, signup, download).
- **Funnel:** The staged journey from awareness → consideration → purchase → loyalty.
- **Segmentation:** Dividing the heterogeneous market into homogeneous subgroups.
- **Attribution:** Assigning credit for a conversion to specific marketing channels/touchpoints.

## 1.5 Learning Outcomes Mapped to KTU COs

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (CO Mapping):**
> - **CO2 (Apply):** Apply descriptive and predictive metrics to evaluate marketing campaigns.
> - **CO3 (Analyze):** Analyze customer behaviour using segmentation and RFM models.
> - **CO4 (Evaluate):** Evaluate marketing ROI, CLV, and attribution models.
> - **CO5 (Create):** Design a basic dashboard of marketing KPIs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Marketing Analytics Process (MAP) — 6-Stage Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the standard MAP lifecycle. The analytical depth of every marketing decision rests on these six operational stages:

1. **Defining the Marketing Problem:** E.g., "Why did Q3 sales drop 15%?"
2. **Data Collection:** First-party (CRM, website logs), Second-party (partner data), Third-party (industry reports).
3. **Data Cleaning & Integration:** Removing duplicates, handling missing values, unifying taxonomies.
4. **Analytical Modelling:** Statistical tests, regression, clustering, classification.
5. **Insight Generation & Visualization:** Translating model output into plain-language findings via dashboards.
6. **Action & Optimization:** Implementing findings (re-allocating ad budget, retargeting) and looping back to Stage 1.

## 2.2 The Marketing Mix (4Ps / 7Ps) — Analytical Lens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Henry Borden's Marketing Mix (Foundational Concept — KTU Frequently Asked):**
> The 4Ps framework — *Product, Price, Place, Promotion* — is the structural skeleton upon which marketing analytics is applied. Each "P" is a distinct data domain requiring separate KPIs.

| Marketing P | Typical KPIs | Data Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Product** | Return Rate, Feature Adoption, NPS | CRM, Product Analytics |
| **Price** | Price Elasticity, Margin %, Discount Penetration | Sales, Finance Systems |
| **Place (Distribution)** | Inventory Turnover, Channel Share, Sell-Through | ERP, Retail Audit |
| **Promotion** | CTR, Conversion Rate, Reach, Impressions | Ad Platforms, Web Analytics |

## 2.3 Customer Segmentation — Conceptual Models

Segmentation analytics answers: *"Who are our most valuable customers?"* Four classical bases are tested in KTU papers:

- **Demographic Segmentation:** Age, Income, Gender, Education.
- **Geographic Segmentation:** Region, Climate, Urban/Rural.
- **Psychographic Segmentation:** Lifestyle, Values, Personality.
- **Behavioural Segmentation:** Usage rate, loyalty status, purchase occasion.

## 2.4 RFM Analysis (Recency–Frequency–Monetary)

> [!NOTE]
> **RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) Model — KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> RFM is a behavioural segmentation technique that scores each customer on three dimensions, typically on a 1–5 scale. The composite RFM score ($R \times F \times M$) is used to identify *Champions, Loyal Customers, At-Risk, and Hibernating* segments.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Metrics)

The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** tested in KTU 2024 Marketing Analytics questions. **All pipe symbols use `\vert` to preserve markdown syntax.**

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conversion Rate (CR) | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Total Visitors}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 2 | Click-Through Rate (CTR) | $CTR = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 3 | Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) | $CAC = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{Number of New Customers}}$ | Currency unit |
| 4 | Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) | $CLV = \overline{AV} \times \overline{APF} \times \overline{ACL}$ | Currency unit |
| 5 | CLV: Alternative (Discounted) | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ | Discount rate $d$ applied |
| 6 | Marketing ROI | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit from Marketing}}{\text{Marketing Cost}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 7 | Return on Ad Spend (ROAS) | $ROAS = \dfrac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}}$ | Ratio |
| 8 | Market Share | $MS = \dfrac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 9 | Relative Market Share | $RMS = \dfrac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Largest Competitor Sales}}$ | Ratio |
| 10 | Cart Abandonment Rate | $CAR = \dfrac{\text{Carts Created} - \text{Carts Completed}}{\text{Carts Created}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 11 | Bounce Rate | $BR = \dfrac{\text{Single Page Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 12 | Churn Rate | $Churn = \dfrac{\text{Customers Lost}}{\text{Customers at Start}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 13 | Net Promoter Score (NPS) | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | Range $-100$ to $+100$ |
| 14 | RFM Composite Score | $S_{RFM} = R \cdot F \cdot M$ | Each in $\vert 1, 5 \vert$ |
| 15 | Lift (Market Basket Analysis) | $Lift(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A) \cdot P(B)}$ | $\vert 0, \infty \vert$ |
| 16 | Confidence (Association Rule) | $Confidence(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$ | $\vert 0, 1 \vert$ |
| 17 | Support (Association Rule) | $Support(A \Rightarrow B) = P(A \cap B)$ | $\vert 0, 1 \vert$ |
| 18 | Cost Per Lead (CPL) | $CPL = \dfrac{\text{Total Campaign Cost}}{\text{Number of Leads}}$ | Currency unit |
| 19 | CPL-to-CAC Ratio | $Ratio = \dfrac{CPL}{CAC}$ | Used to assess funnel efficiency |
| 20 | Engagement Rate | $ER = \dfrac{\text{Interactions}}{\text{Reach}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility (Why KTU Teaches This)

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Relevance Callout:**
> - **E-Commerce (Flipkart/Amazon):** Uses Market Basket Analysis for "Frequently Bought Together."
> - **Telecom (Jio/Airtel):** Uses CLV and Churn models to retain high-value subscribers.
> - **Banking (HDFC):** Uses RFM scoring for credit card cross-selling.
> - **Digital Marketing (Google/Meta Ads):** CTR, ROAS, and Attribution are the daily bread.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: CLV (Customer Lifetime Value) — Discounted Cash Flow Form

The Discounted CLV formula accounts for the **time value of money**, making it the most academically rigorous form tested in KTU 14-mark questions.

### Step 1: Define Gross Margin per Period

For a customer acquired in period $0$, the gross margin (revenue $-$ cost of goods) generated in period $t$ is:

$$
GM_t = R_t - C_t
$$

where $R_t$ is revenue at period $t$ and $C_t$ is the direct cost of serving the customer at period $t$.

### Step 2: Apply the Retention Probability Factor

A customer is retained into period $t$ with probability $r_t$. The expected contribution in period $t$ becomes:

$$
E_t = GM_t \cdot r_t
$$

### Step 3: Discount to Present Value

Using a constant discount rate $d$, the present value of expected contribution at period $t$ is:

$$
PV_t = \dfrac{E_t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

### Step 4: Sum Across the Customer's Lifetime $T$

The total Customer Lifetime Value is the sum of all discounted future contributions minus the acquisition cost $CAC$:

$$
CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t} - CAC
$$

### Step 5: Simplified Average CLV (Tested Frequently)

For a stationary customer (constant margin, constant retention), the geometric series converges to:

$$
CLV = \overline{GM} \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1 - r)(1 + d)} - CAC
$$

This is the formula students must memorize for KTU 3-mark short-answer questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numeric Example (CLV):**
> Suppose $GM = \text{Rs. }500$/month, retention $r = 0.85$, discount $d = 0.01$/month, $CAC = \text{Rs. }2000$. Compute CLV.
>
> First, compute the multiplier:
> $$\dfrac{r}{(1 - r)(1 + d)} = \dfrac{0.85}{(1 - 0.85)(1 + 0.01)} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.15 \times 1.01} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.1515} \approx 5.6106$$
>
> Next, multiply by gross margin:
> $$\overline{GM} \times 5.6106 = 500 \times 5.6106 = \text{Rs. }2805.30$$
>
> Finally, subtract the acquisition cost:
> $$CLV = 2805.30 - 2000 = \text{Rs. }805.30$$
>
> **Decision Rule (KTU CO4):** A customer is profitable if $CLV > CAC$ and the **CLV : CAC ratio exceeds 3 : 1**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Marketing ROI (with Attribution)

Marketing ROI is the canonical performance metric. The KTU version requires **attribution** to allocate revenue correctly across multi-channel campaigns.

### Step 1: Compute Gross Revenue from Marketing

If a campaign drives $N$ conversions at average order value $AOV$:

$$
GR = N \times AOV
$$

### Step 2: Compute Net Profit

$$
NP = GR - COGS - Marketing\:Cost
$$

where $COGS$ is the Cost of Goods Sold attributable to those $N$ conversions.

### Step 3: Apply the ROI Formula

$$
ROI = \dfrac{NP}{Marketing\:Cost} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 4: Multi-Touch Attribution Adjustment (Advanced)

For a customer journey touching $k$ channels, revenue $R_i$ is allocated using a weighted attribution model:

$$
R_i = w_i \cdot R_{total}, \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = 1
$$

Three classical models:

| Attribution Model | Weight Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **First-Touch** | $w_1 = 1$, rest $= 0$ | Brand awareness campaigns |
| **Last-Touch** | $w_k = 1$, rest $= 0$ | Direct-response / bottom-funnel |
| **Linear** | $w_i = \dfrac{1}{k}$ for all $i$ | Balanced multi-channel |

### Step 5: Worked Example — ROI with Attribution

A campaign receives 100 conversions, $AOV = \text{Rs. }1500$, $COGS = 60\%$ of $GR$, total ad spend $= \text{Rs. }20{,}000$. Compute ROI under the linear attribution model across 4 channels.

**Step (a):** Compute Gross Revenue:
$$GR = 100 \times 1500 = \text{Rs. }150{,}000$$

**Step (b):** Compute COGS (60% of GR):
$$COGS = 0.60 \times 150{,}000 = \text{Rs. }90{,}000$$

**Step (c):** Compute Net Profit:
$$NP = 150{,}000 - 90{,}000 - 20{,}000 = \text{Rs. }40{,}000$$

**Step (d):** Compute ROI:
$$ROI = \dfrac{40{,}000}{20{,}000} \times 100\% = 200\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Rule (KTU CO4):** An ROI above 100% means the campaign returned twice the marketing investment — a benchmark of strong performance.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right KPI

The KTU 2024 exam often gives a scenario (e.g., a startup, a SaaS firm, a retail chain) and asks students to choose KPIs. The analytical matrix below is the high-yield decision tool.

| Business Scenario | Primary KPI | Secondary KPI | Avoid (Misleading) |
|---|---|---|---|
| E-Commerce Startup | Conversion Rate | CAC, AOV | Page Views alone |
| Subscription SaaS | CLV / CAC Ratio | Churn Rate | New Signups |
| FMCG Brand | Market Share | NPS, Reach | CTR (online-only) |
| Luxury Brand | Average Order Value | Repeat Purchase | Conversion Volume |
| B2B Services | Pipeline Velocity | CPL, Win Rate | Impressions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Insight (CO5):** The choice of KPI must align with the **stage of the marketing funnel** — awareness-stage campaigns are measured on *Reach* and *Impressions*, while conversion-stage campaigns are measured on *Conversion Rate* and *ROAS*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Marketing Analytics Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following block-level functional flow diagram maps the complete lifecycle of a marketing analytics engagement, from problem definition to optimization loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    problemA[Stage 1: Define Marketing Problem] --> dataA[Stage 2: Data Collection First Party CRM Web Logs Social Media]
    dataA --> cleanA[Stage 3: Data Cleaning and Integration Remove Duplicates Handle Missing Values]
    cleanA --> modelA[Stage 4: Analytical Modelling Statistical Tests Regression Clustering Classification]
    modelA --> insightA[Stage 5: Insight Generation Dashboards Reports Visualizations]
    insightA --> actionA[Stage 6: Action and Optimization Reallocate Budget Retarget Campaign]
    actionA -. Feedback Loop .-> problemA
```

## 4.2 The Four Pillars Architecture (Hierarchical Mermaid)

This diagram illustrates the hierarchy from Descriptive (hindsight) to Prescriptive (foresight) — a high-frequency KTU diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    descA[Pillar 1: Descriptive Analytics WHAT HAPPENED] --> diagA[Pillar 2: Diagnostic Analytics WHY DID IT HAPPEN]
    diagA --> predA[Pillar 3: Predictive Analytics WHAT WILL HAPPEN]
    predA --> prescA[Pillar 4: Prescriptive Analytics WHAT SHOULD WE DO]
    prescA --> decisionA[Strategic Marketing Decision]
    descA --> toolsA[Tools: Dashboards, KPIs, Reports]
    diagA --> toolsB[Tools: Drill Down, Correlation, Root Cause]
    predA --> toolsC[Tools: Regression, Time Series, ML]
    prescA --> toolsD[Tools: Optimization, Simulation]
```

## 4.3 RFM Segmentation Matrix (Customer Grid)

The RFM model is presented as a 5x5 grid; the diagram below shows the strategic segment cells.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    champA[Champions High R High F High M] --> loyalA[Loyal Customers High F High M]
    loyalA --> potA[Potential Loyalists Low R High F]
    potA --> newA[New Customers High R Low F]
    newA --> hibernA[Hibernating Low R Low F Low M]
    hibernA --> atRiskA[At Risk Low R High M]
    atRiskA --> cantA[Cannot Lose Them Low R Very High M]
    cantA --> champA
```

## 4.4 The Marketing Funnel — Analytics View (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    awareA[Awareness Stage Metric Impressions Reach] --> interestA[Interest Stage Metric CTR Time on Site]
    interestA --> considerA[Consideration Stage Metric Add to Cart Wishlist]
    considerA --> intentA[Intent Stage Metric Checkout Initiated]
    intentA --> purchaseA[Purchase Stage Metric Conversion Rate Revenue]
    purchaseA --> loyalA[Loyalty Stage Metric Repeat Purchase NPS]
```

## 4.5 KPI Hierarchy Map (4Ps Analytics Coverage)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mktA[Marketing Analytics] --> p1A[Product Analytics Returns Feature Adoption NPS]
    mktA --> p2A[Price Analytics Elasticity Margin Discount Penetration]
    mktA --> p3A[Place Analytics Channel Share Sell Through]
    mktA --> p4A[Promotion Analytics CTR Conversion Rate ROAS Reach]
    p1A --> kpiA[KPI Dashboard for Manager]
    p2A --> kpiA
    p3A --> kpiA
    p4A --> kpiA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Marketing Analytics. List the four pillars of marketing analytics.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Marketing Analytics** is the practice of measuring, managing, and analyzing marketing performance data to maximize its effectiveness and optimize return on investment. It uses quantitative tools — statistics, data mining, and predictive modelling — to support evidence-based marketing decisions.

The **four pillars** of marketing analytics are:

1. **Descriptive Analytics** — *What happened?* (Reports, dashboards, KPIs).
2. **Diagnostic Analytics** — *Why did it happen?* (Drill-down, correlation, root-cause analysis).
3. **Predictive Analytics** — *What will happen?* (Regression, forecasting, machine learning).
4. **Prescriptive Analytics** — *What should we do?* (Optimization, simulation, recommendation engines).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) and Customer Lifetime Value (CLV). Why is the CLV/CAC ratio important?**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | CAC | CLV |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Cost incurred to acquire one new customer | Total revenue/profit a customer generates over their lifetime |
> | **Formula** | $\dfrac{\text{Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers}}$ | $\sum \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ |
> | **Perspective** | Short-term cost | Long-term value |
> | **Goal** | Minimize | Maximize |

The **CLV/CAC ratio** measures the *return efficiency* of customer acquisition spend.
- Ratio **< 1** → Loss-making acquisition.
- Ratio **= 3** → Industry benchmark for healthy SaaS businesses.
- Ratio **> 5** → Under-investing in growth; safe to scale up marketing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):**
> Students frequently write *CAC is the profit and CLV is the cost* — this is **incorrect**. Memorize the formula direction explicitly: CAC = Spend ÷ Customers, CLV = Revenue/Customer × Lifespan.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester – Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> *Answer ANY ONE full question from the Module 3 choice block. Maximum 14 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: A digital retailer in Kerala launches an online campaign with the following data:**
- Total impressions: **500,000**
- Clicks: **12,500**
- Conversions: **625**
- Total ad spend: **Rs. 2,50,000**
- Average order value (AOV): **Rs. 1,600**
- Cost of goods sold (COGS) per order: **60% of AOV**

**Part (a) (7 Marks):** Compute the (i) CTR, (ii) Conversion Rate, (iii) CPC, and (iv) ROAS.

**Part (b) (7 Marks):** If the company retains each acquired customer for an average of 4 future purchases at Rs. 1,200 each, with a retention probability of 0.80 and discount rate of 5% per period, calculate the CLV. Comment on profitability using the CLV : CAC benchmark.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Given:** Impressions $= 500{,}000$; Clicks $= 12{,}500$; Conversions $= 625$; Ad Spend $= \text{Rs. }2{,}50{,}000$; $AOV = \text{Rs. }1{,}600$.

**(i) Click-Through Rate (CTR):**
$$CTR = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100 = \dfrac{12{,}500}{500{,}000} \times 100 = 2.5\%$$

**(ii) Conversion Rate (CR):**
$$CR = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Clicks}} \times 100 = \dfrac{625}{12{,}500} \times 100 = 5\%$$

**(iii) Cost Per Click (CPC):**
$$CPC = \dfrac{\text{Ad Spend}}{\text{Clicks}} = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{12{,}500} = \text{Rs. }20 \text{ per click}$$

**(iv) Return on Ad Spend (ROAS):**

Total Revenue:
$$GR = \text{Conversions} \times AOV = 625 \times 1600 = \text{Rs. }10{,}00{,}000$$

ROAS:
$$ROAS = \dfrac{GR}{\text{Ad Spend}} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 4.0$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** ROAS of 4.0 means every ₹1 spent returned ₹4 — a *strong* campaign performance.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute CAC.**
$$CAC = \dfrac{\text{Ad Spend}}{\text{New Customers}} = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{625} = \text{Rs. }400$$

**Step 2: Compute CLV (simplified discounted form).**

Given: $\overline{GM} = 1200$, $r = 0.80$, $d = 0.05$, $T = 4$.

Using the geometric-series closed form:
$$CLV = \overline{GM} \cdot \dfrac{r}{(1 - r)(1 + d)} - CAC$$
$$CLV = 1200 \times \dfrac{0.80}{(1 - 0.80)(1 + 0.05)} - 400$$
$$CLV = 1200 \times \dfrac{0.80}{0.20 \times 1.05} - 400$$
$$CLV = 1200 \times \dfrac{0.80}{0.21} - 400$$
$$CLV = 1200 \times 3.8095 - 400$$
$$CLV = 4571.43 - 400 = \text{Rs. }4171.43$$

**Step 3: Compute CLV : CAC ratio.**
$$\dfrac{CLV}{CAC} = \dfrac{4171.43}{400} \approx 10.43$$

**Step 4: Comment on Profitability.**

The CLV : CAC ratio of **10.43** significantly exceeds the industry benchmark of **3 : 1**. This indicates that the company is **under-investing in customer acquisition** — it could safely scale up marketing spend by 2–3× and still maintain profitability. The campaign is **highly profitable** in the long run.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - Many students forget to **convert discount rate** to per-period (here, 5% per period, not annual).
> - Some forget to **subtract CAC** at the end. Both are **2-mark deductions** each.
> - The interpretation comment must use the **CLV/CAC ratio rule** — a 2-mark allocation is for the strategic comment, not just the math.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: A retail bank in Kochi wants to perform RFM segmentation on its 10,000 credit card customers.**

**Part (a) (7 Marks):** Explain the RFM model. Describe how you would score customers on Recency, Frequency, and Monetary dimensions and how the composite score identifies segments like *Champions*, *At Risk*, and *Hibernating*.

**Part (b) (7 Marks):** For a sample of 5 customers given below, compute the RFM composite score and classify each into a strategic segment. State the marketing action recommended for each segment.

| Customer | Days Since Last Purchase | Purchases Last 6 Months | Total Spend (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 5 | 12 | 95,000 |
| C2 | 90 | 2 | 8,000 |
| C3 | 200 | 6 | 60,000 |
| C4 | 30 | 8 | 45,000 |
| C5 | 350 | 1 | 3,000 |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

> **RFM Model Definition (2 marks):**
> RFM is a customer segmentation technique that scores each customer on three behavioural dimensions:
> - **R (Recency):** How recently did the customer purchase? Lower days = higher score.
> - **F (Frequency):** How often do they purchase? Higher count = higher score.
> - **M (Monetary):** How much do they spend? Higher value = higher score.

**Scoring Methodology (3 marks):**
Each dimension is scored on a **1–5 scale** using quintile segmentation:
- Sort customers by each dimension, divide into 5 equal groups (or use percentile thresholds).
- Assign score 5 (best) to top 20%, score 1 (worst) to bottom 20%.

**Composite Score (1 mark):**
$$S_{RFM} = R + F + M \quad \text{(sum)} \quad \text{or} \quad R \times F \times M \quad \text{(product)}$$

**Segment Classification Logic (1 mark):**
| Segment | R | F | M | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Champions** | 5 | 5 | 5 | Reward, early access |
| **Loyal** | 4–5 | 4–5 | 4–5 | Cross-sell, loyalty programs |
| **At Risk** | 1–2 | 4–5 | 4–5 | Win-back, personalized offers |
| **Hibernating** | 1–2 | 1–2 | 1–2 | Reactivation campaigns or ignore |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Score Each Customer.**

Define quintile thresholds based on the sample:

| Dimension | Score 5 | Score 4 | Score 3 | Score 2 | Score 1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Recency (days, *lower = better*) | $\leq 30$ | $31\text{–}60$ | $61\text{–}120$ | $121\text{–}250$ | $> 250$ |
| Frequency (purchases) | $\geq 10$ | $7\text{–}9$ | $4\text{–}6$ | $2\text{–}3$ | $1$ |
| Monetary (₹) | $\geq 80{,}000$ | $50{,}000\text{–}79{,}999$ | $25{,}000\text{–}49{,}999$ | $10{,}000\text{–}24{,}999$ | $< 10{,}000$ |

**Step 2: Apply Scoring to Each Customer.**

| Customer | Recency (days) | R Score | Frequency | F Score | Monetary (₹) | M Score | Composite (Sum) | Segment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **C1** | 5 | **5** | 12 | **5** | 95,000 | **5** | **15** | Champion |
| **C2** | 90 | **3** | 2 | **2** | 8,000 | **1** | **6** | Hibernating |
| **C3** | 200 | **2** | 6 | **3** | 60,000 | **4** | **9** | At Risk |
| **C4** | 30 | **5** | 8 | **4** | 45,000 | **3** | **12** | Loyal |
| **C5** | 350 | **1** | 1 | **1** | 3,000 | **1** | **3** | Lost |

**Step 3: Recommended Marketing Actions.**

- **C1 (Champion, 15):** Reward with VIP privileges, invite to beta-test new products, request referrals.
- **C2 (Hibernating, 6):** Send reactivation email with deep discount; if no response in 60 days, move to dormant list.
- **C3 (At Risk, 9):** Send personalized "We miss you" campaign + cashback offer + dedicated relationship manager call.
- **C4 (Loyal, 12):** Cross-sell premium card or loan products; enroll in loyalty rewards program.
- **C5 (Lost, 3):** Low-cost reactivation test; if unresponsive, deprioritize to reduce marketing spend waste.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (RFM Question):**
> - Students often assign **higher scores to higher days for Recency** — this is *inverted*. **Lower recency days = higher R score.**
> - Do **not** multiply R × F × M without thinking — if any dimension is 1, the product becomes 1, obscuring differences. The **sum** is more interpretable for 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Basics of Marketing Analytics**

- ✅ Marketing Analytics = **measurement + analysis + optimization** of marketing activity using data.
- ✅ Four pillars: **Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive** (sequence is sequential, not optional).
- ✅ Marketing Mix (4Ps): **Product, Price, Place, Promotion** — each has distinct KPIs.
- ✅ **Conversion Rate** is a *funnel-bottom* metric; **CTR** is a *funnel-middle* metric.
- ✅ **CAC** = cost focus; **CLV** = value focus; their ratio drives acquisition strategy.
- ✅ Healthy **CLV : CAC ≈ 3 : 1**; < 1 = burning cash; > 5 = under-investing.
- ✅ **Market Share** uses *industry sales* as denominator; **Relative Market Share** uses *largest competitor* as denominator.
- ✅ **RFM** stands for Recency–Frequency–Monetary; scores 1–5 each; composite = sum or product.
- ✅ **Recency is inverse** — lower days = higher score (most common exam mistake).
- ✅ **Lift (Market Basket Analysis)** > 1 indicates *positive association* between products A and B.
- ✅ **Attribution models:** First-touch, Last-touch, Linear, Time-decay, Position-based — each tells a different story.
- ✅ **Churn Rate** measures customer loss; its complement (1 − churn) is the **retention rate**.
- ✅ **NPS** ranges from **−100 to +100**; above 50 is excellent; below 0 indicates brand crisis.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers (₹, %, ratio) — examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.
- ✅ The **6-stage MAP process** is the operational backbone of every marketing analytics project.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marketing Decisions Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marketing Decisions Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marketing Decision Models** are quantitative, mathematical, and statistical frameworks used by managers and analysts to **describe, predict, and optimize** decisions related to the marketing mix (Product, Price, Place, Promotion). They convert qualitative marketing strategy into measurable, data-driven decision rules.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** context for **Business Analytics (PECBT755)**, marketing decision models form the analytical bridge between raw customer data and strategic marketing actions. They include:
- **Response Models** (how sales react to advertising/promotion)
- **Diffusion Models** (how new products spread in a market)
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** (long-run value of a customer)
- **Conjoint & Choice Models** (consumer preference structure)
- **Linear Programming Models** (optimal media/marketing-mix allocation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core idea:** Replace "gut-feeling" marketing with **measurable, optimizable, testable** decision rules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a **Marketing Decision Model** as a **"GPS for a Marketing Manager"**.

- A **GPS** takes your current location (current sales), destination (target revenue), and the road network (customer behavior) and computes the **best route** (optimal marketing mix).
- If traffic (competition) changes, the GPS recalculates.
- A **Marketing Decision Model** takes your current customer base, product adoption rate, and budget, and computes the **best mix of price, ad spend, and channel investment** to reach the revenue target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key benefit:** It tells the manager **"why"** a decision works and **"what happens if"** conditions change — exactly the kind of evidence-based reasoning KTU examiners reward.

## 1.3 Classification of Marketing Decision Models

Marketing models can be classified by **decision type** and **analytical technique**:

| Class | Example Models | Managerial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Market Response Models** | Linear, Multiplicative, Adbudg | Sales vs. Ad spend |
| **Diffusion Models** | Bass, Logistic | New product forecasting |
| **Customer-Level Models** | CLV, RFM, Churn | Customer portfolio mgmt. |
| **Choice Models** | Logit, Probit, Conjoint | Product feature design |
| **Allocation Models** | LP, Goal Programming | Budget/ media mix |
| **Segmentation Models** | Clustering, LTV-based | Targeted campaigns |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus places strong weight on **Bass Diffusion, CLV, Conjoint, and Linear Programming** models. These are the **high-yield** topics for the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Marketing Mix (4Ps) — The Decision Variables

The controllable decision variables in any marketing decision model are the famous **4Ps**:
1. **Product** — features, quality, branding
2. **Price** — list price, discounts, credit terms
3. **Place** — distribution channels, retail coverage
4. **Promotion** — advertising, sales force, digital

A marketing decision model explicitly **quantifies the relationship** between one or more of these variables and the **outcome variable** (sales, profit, market share).

---

## 2.2 Market Response Models

A **market response function** links marketing effort to sales. The two most common forms are:

### (a) Linear Response Model
$$S = a + b \cdot M$$

where $S$ = sales, $M$ = marketing effort, $a$ = baseline sales, $b$ = response coefficient.

### (b) Multiplicative (Constant-Elasticity) Model
$$S = a \cdot M^{b}$$

Here $b$ is the **elasticity of sales with respect to marketing effort**. This is the most widely used in practice because it gives diminishing returns naturally.

### (c) Adbudg Model (Little's Model)
$$S = b_0 + b_1 \cdot \sqrt{A_x} + b_2 \cdot \sqrt{A_y} - b_3 \cdot A_x^{2} - b_4 \cdot A_y^{2}$$

A **second-order (concave)** response to two advertising media, $A_x$ and $A_y$, capturing diminishing returns and saturation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Adbudg model is concave — there is a **unique profit-maximizing ad spend** (set $\partial \text{Profit} / \partial A_x = 0$). Students often miss the square-root transformation.

---

## 2.3 Bass Diffusion Model (New-Product Forecasting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Bass Diffusion Model** is the single most important marketing forecasting model in the world. It is named after **Frank Bass (1969)** and describes the **timing of first purchases** of a new product.

### Core Idea
Two effects drive adoption:
- **Innovation effect (p):** people adopt because of mass-media influence (external).
- **Imitation effect (q):** people adopt because others have (word-of-mouth, internal).

### The Hazard (Instantaneous Adoption Probability)
$$\frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)} = p + q \cdot F(t)$$

where:
- $F(t)$ = cumulative fraction of the market that has adopted by time $t$
- $f(t)$ = derivative, the **density** of adoptions at time $t$
- $p$ = **coefficient of innovation** (typically 0.001 – 0.03)
- $q$ = **coefficient of imitation** (typically 0.3 – 0.5)

### Closed-Form Cumulative Adoption
$$F(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p}\, e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

### Cumulative Number of Adopters
$$N(t) = m \cdot F(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p}\, e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

where $m$ = **market potential** (ultimate total adopters).

### Peak Adoption Time
$$t^{*} = \frac{1}{p+q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)$$

This is the time at which the **rate of new adoptions is maximum** — a key managerial insight for production planning and cash-flow forecasting.

---

## 2.4 Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLV** is the **present value of all future profits** generated by a customer over the entire duration of the relationship.

### Discounted CLV Formula
$$\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1 + d)^{t}}$$

where:
- $R_t$ = revenue from customer in period $t$
- $C_t$ = cost of serving customer in period $t$
- $d$ = discount rate (cost of capital)
- $T$ = expected lifetime of the customer (in periods)

### CLV with Constant Margin and Constant Retention
If revenue per period is $R$, cost per period is $C$, retention probability is $r$, and discount rate is $d$:

$$\text{CLV} = \frac{R - C}{1 + d - r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** As $r \to 1+d$, CLV $\to \infty$ — a customer who never churns is infinitely valuable. **Retention is the single most powerful CLV lever.**

---

## 2.5 Linear Programming in Marketing (Media Selection)

Marketing managers must allocate a **fixed budget** across media (TV, print, digital, etc.) to **maximize reach, frequency, or sales**.

### Standard LP Form
$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \quad \text{(cost of media } j\text{)}$$

Subject to:
- $\sum_{j} a_{ij} x_j \;\geq\; R_i$ (reach target segment $i$ at least $R_i$ times)
- $x_j \geq 0$
- (Optionally) $x_j \leq U_j$ (upper bound per medium)

where $x_j$ = number of insertions in medium $j$, $c_j$ = cost per insertion, $a_{ij}$ = reach of medium $j$ in segment $i$.

---

## 2.6 Conjoint Analysis (Consumer Preference Modeling)

**Conjoint Analysis** decomposes a customer's overall preference for a product into **part-worth utilities** for each attribute level.

### Utility Model
$$U(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{l=1}^{L_k} \beta_{kl} \cdot x_{kl}$$

where:
- $x_{kl}$ = 1 if attribute $k$ at level $l$ is present, 0 otherwise
- $\beta_{kl}$ = part-worth utility of that level
- $U(x)$ = total utility of the product profile $x$

The chosen model is estimated from **rating or ranking data** via OLS, Logit, or HB (Hierarchical Bayes).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Model | Key Formula | Decision Variable | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Response | $S = a + bM$ | Marketing spend $M$ | Sales |
| Multiplicative | $S = a M^{b}$ | Marketing spend $M$ | Sales (elasticity $b$) |
| Adbudg | $S = b_0 + b_1 \sqrt{A_x} - b_3 A_x^{2}$ | Ad spend $A$ | Sales |
| Bass Hazard | $f/(1-F) = p + qF$ | Time $t$ | Adoption prob. |
| Bass Cumulative | $F(t) = (1 - e^{-(p+q)t})/(1 + (q/p)e^{-(p+q)t})$ | Time | Fraction adopted |
| Peak Time | $t^* = \ln(q/p)/(p+q)$ | — | Peak adoption time |
| CLV | $\sum (R_t - C_t)/(1+d)^t$ | Retention $r$ | Long-run profit |
| CLV (constant) | $(R-C)/(1+d-r)$ | $r$ | Long-run profit |
| Media LP | $\min \sum c_j x_j$ s.t. reach $\geq R_i$ | Media $x_j$ | Optimal allocation |
| Conjoint | $U(x) = \sum \beta_{kl} x_{kl}$ | Attribute level $x_{kl}$ | Utility / choice |
| Logit Choice | $P_i = e^{V_i}/\sum e^{V_j}$ | Utility $V$ | Choice probability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These models drive **Google Ads bidding, Amazon recommendation engines, telecom churn prevention, pharmaceutical launch planning, and FMCG trade promotions** — multi-billion-dollar applications.

---

## 2.8 Engineering / Managerial Utility

| Field | Model Used | Real Application |
|---|---|---|
| **FMCG** | Bass + LP | New product launch budgeting |
| **Telecom** | CLV + Churn | Customer retention offers |
| **E-commerce** | Logit / Choice | Recommendation & pricing |
| **Auto industry** | Conjoint | Feature mix for new car |
| **Pharma** | Diffusion | Drug adoption forecasting |
| **Banking** | CLV + Segmentation | Credit-card cross-sell |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bass Diffusion Model

We derive the closed-form $F(t)$ from the hazard equation.

### Step 1 — Write the Hazard Function
The hazard (probability of adoption at time $t$, given not yet adopted) is assumed linear in cumulative adoption:
$$\frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)} = p + q F(t) \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $f(t) = dF/dt$
Because $F(t)$ is the cumulative, its derivative is the density:
$$\frac{dF/dt}{1 - F} = p + q F \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

### Step 3 — Separate Variables
$$dF = (p + qF)(1 - F)\, dt = \left[p + (q - p)F - q F^{2}\right] dt$$

So,
$$\frac{dF}{p + (q-p)F - qF^{2}} = dt \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}$$

### Step 4 — Factor the Denominator
The denominator is a quadratic in $F$ with roots:
$$F_{\pm} = \frac{-(q-p) \pm \sqrt{(q-p)^{2} + 4pq}}{-2q} = \frac{(q-p) \mp (p+q)}{2q}$$

The two roots are:
$$F_{+} = -\frac{p}{q} \;\;,\;\; F_{-} = 1$$

### Step 5 — Partial Fractions
Using $F_{+} = -p/q$ and $F_{-} = 1$:
$$p + (q-p)F - qF^{2} = -q(F - 1)\!\left(F + \frac{p}{q}\right)$$

So Equation 3 becomes:
$$\frac{dF}{-q(F-1)(F + p/q)} = dt$$

Applying partial fractions:
$$\frac{1}{(1-F)(F + p/q)} = \frac{1}{1 + p/q} \left[\frac{1}{1-F} + \frac{1}{F + p/q}\right]$$

Let $A = 1 + p/q = (p+q)/q$. Then:
$$\frac{dF}{-q} \cdot \frac{1}{A}\left[\frac{1}{1-F} + \frac{1}{F + p/q}\right] = dt$$

### Step 6 — Integrate Both Sides
Integrating from $0$ to $F(t)$ and $0$ to $t$:
$$\frac{1}{p+q}\left[-\ln(1-F) + \ln\!\left(F + \frac{p}{q}\right)\right]_{0}^{F(t)} = t$$

### Step 7 — Apply Boundary Condition
At $t=0$, $F(0) = 0$. Evaluating the bracket at the lower limit gives $\ln(p/q)$. After algebra (collecting logs and exponentiating):

$$1 - F(t) = \frac{(p+q)/q \cdot p/q \cdot e^{-(p+q)t}}{1}$$

After simplification:
$$\boxed{\,F(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p}\, e^{-(p+q)t}}\,}$$

This is the **cumulative fraction of adopters at time $t$** in the Bass Model.

### Step 8 — Peak Adoption Time
Set $f'(t) = 0$. Since $f(t) = (p + qF(t))(1 - F(t))$, by symmetry the maximum occurs at $F = p/q \cdot (1 - F)$ ... solving gives:
$$F(t^{*}) = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{p}{2q} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t^{*} = \frac{1}{p+q}\ln\!\frac{q}{p}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of CLV (Constant-Retention, Constant-Margin Case)

### Step 1 — Set Up the Recursion
Let $CLV_0$ = value at the start, $M$ = margin per period (revenue − cost), $r$ = retention probability, $d$ = discount rate.

A customer continues into next period with probability $r$. The expected discounted value:
$$CLV_0 = M + \frac{r}{1+d} \cdot CLV_0$$

### Step 2 — Solve Algebraically
$$CLV_0 - \frac{r}{1+d} CLV_0 = M$$
$$CLV_0 \left(1 - \frac{r}{1+d}\right) = M$$
$$CLV_0 \cdot \frac{1 + d - r}{1 + d} = M$$

### Step 3 — Final Closed Form
$$\boxed{\,CLV_0 = \frac{M(1+d)}{1 + d - r} = \frac{R - C}{1 + d - r}\,}$$

(If we treat $M$ as the *undiscounted* period margin, the formula collapses to the simpler form $\frac{R-C}{1+d-r}$.)

### Step 4 — Interpretation
- If $r > 1 + d$ → **denominator is negative** → CLV is **infinite** (mathematical artifact of the geometric series when retention exceeds discount + 1). Practically, CLV becomes very large.
- Higher $r$ → higher CLV.
- Higher $d$ → lower CLV.

---

## 3.3 Marketing Mix LP — Worked Numerical Setup

A firm has **₹10,00,000** to spend on three media. Reach and cost are:

| Medium $j$ | Cost/insertion $c_j$ (₹) | Reach in Segment 1 $a_{1j}$ | Reach in Segment 2 $a_{2j}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| TV | 50,000 | 60 (thousand) | 30 |
| Print | 20,000 | 20 | 25 |
| Digital | 10,000 | 15 | 10 |

Targets: Reach at least **300 (k)** in Seg 1 and **200 (k)** in Seg 2.

**LP Formulation:**
$$\min Z = 50{,}000\, x_1 + 20{,}000\, x_2 + 10{,}000\, x_3$$
$$\text{s.t. } 60x_1 + 20x_2 + 15x_3 \geq 300$$
$$30x_1 + 25x_2 + 10x_3 \geq 200$$
$$x_1, x_2, x_3 \geq 0$$

**Solve at optimum** (e.g., via Simplex). Optimal solution typically lies at the intersection of the two binding constraints. Substituting $x_1, x_2$ in terms of $x_3$ and minimizing, or simply iterating feasible corner points, yields the **cost-minimizing media plan**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Marketing Decision Model — Master Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[KTU Marketing Decision Framework]:::top
    A0 --> B1[Market Response Models]
    A0 --> B2[Diffusion Models]
    A0 --> B3[Customer Value Models]
    A0 --> B4[Allocation LP Models]
    A0 --> B5[Choice and Conjoint Models]
    
    B1 --> C1[Linear S = a + bM]
    B1 --> C2[Multiplicative S = aMb]
    B1 --> C3[Adbudg concave]
    
    B2 --> D1[Bass Diffusion]
    D1 --> D2[F of t closed form]
    D1 --> D3[Peak time t star]
    
    B3 --> E1[CLV discounted sum]
    B3 --> E2[CLV constant formula]
    B3 --> E3[Churn and retention]
    
    B4 --> F1[Media selection LP]
    B4 --> F2[Sales force allocation]
    B4 --> F3[Goal programming]
    
    B5 --> G1[Logit choice]
    B5 --> G2[Probit choice]
    B5 --> G3[Conjoint part worth]
    
    C1 --> OUT1[Decision: optimal ad spend]
    C2 --> OUT1
    C3 --> OUT1
    D2 --> OUT2[Decision: launch plan]
    D3 --> OUT2
    E1 --> OUT3[Decision: customer targeting]
    E2 --> OUT3
    E3 --> OUT3
    F1 --> OUT4[Decision: budget mix]
    F2 --> OUT4
    F3 --> OUT4
    G1 --> OUT5[Decision: product design]
    G2 --> OUT5
    G3 --> OUT5
    
    classDef top fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold
```

## 4.2 Bass Diffusion — Adoption Curve Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Time t = 0]:::node --> S1[Innovation p drives first adopters]
    S1 --> S2[F of t grows slowly]
    S2 --> S3[Imitation q takes over]
    S3 --> S4[F of t grows fast]
    S4 --> S5[Market saturates F of t approaches 1]
    S5 --> S6[Time t star = peak adoption]
    S6 --> S7[Production and cash flow planning]
    
    classDef node fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
```

## 4.3 CLV Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Customer Transaction History]:::node
    IN --> ST1[Step 1: Estimate revenue R per period]
    IN --> ST2[Step 2: Estimate cost C per period]
    IN --> ST3[Step 3: Estimate retention r]
    IN --> ST4[Step 4: Discount rate d]
    
    ST1 --> CALC[CLV Computation]
    ST2 --> CALC
    ST3 --> CALC
    ST4 --> CALC
    
    CALC --> FORM{Use constant or variable model?}
    FORM -->|Constant| F1[CLV = R minus C over 1 plus d minus r]
    FORM -->|Variable| F2[CLV = sum of discounted R minus C]
    
    F1 --> OUT[Segment customers by CLV]
    F2 --> OUT
    OUT --> DEC{Decision}
    DEC -->|High CLV| D1[Retain with offers]
    DEC -->|Low CLV| D2[Reduce marketing spend]
    DEC -->|Negative CLV| D3[Win back or let go]
    
    classDef node fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OBJ[Objective: Minimize total cost or Maximize reach]:::node
    OBJ --> C1[Decision variables x sub j number of insertions in medium j]
    C1 --> C2[Constraints: reach targets per segment]
    C2 --> C3[Constraints: budget ceiling]
    C3 --> C4[Constraints: nonnegativity x j greater than zero]
    C4 --> SOLVE[Apply Simplex or Solver]

```
