# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Market Intelligence in Action

> **Scenario (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> A KTU-graduate entrepreneur is launching a new cloud-kitchen brand in **Kochi** focused on South-Indian breakfast. The business analyst must compute key Market Intelligence metrics.

**Given Data:**
- Target population of working professionals in Kochi: **$N = 250{,}000$**
- Average monthly spend on breakfast (out-of-home): **$\bar{R} = ₹1{,}800$**
- Current 3 major competitors' combined market share: **75%**
- Expected entrepreneur's market share in Year 1: **5%**
- Number of customer surveys returned: **$2{,}000$**
  - Promoters: **$1{,}100$**
  - Detractors: **$300$**
  - Passives: **$600$**

### Step 1: Compute Total Addressable Market (TAM)

$$
\text{TAM} = N \times \bar{R} \times 12 \text{ months}
$$

$$
\text{TAM} = 250{,}000 \times 1{,}800 \times 12
$$

$$
\text{TAM} = 5{,}40{,}00{,}00{,}000 = ₹540 \text{ Crores annually}
$$

**Interpretation:** The total breakfast out-of-home market in Kochi is ₹540 Cr per year.

### Step 2: Compute Serviceable Available Market (SAM)

$$
\text{SAM} = \text{TAM} \times \text{Cloud-Kitchen Penetration \%} = 540 \times 0.30 = ₹162 \text{ Cr}
$$

(Assuming 30% of breakfast market is online/cloud-kitchen-served.)

### Step 3: Compute Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM) — Year 1

$$
\text{SOM} = \text{SAM} \times \text{Expected Market Share \%}
$$

$$
\text{SOM} = 162 \times 0.05 = ₹8.1 \text{ Cr}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Net Promoter Score (NPS)

$$
\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}
$$

$$
\%\text{Promoters} = \dfrac{1{,}100}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 55\%
$$

$$
\%\text{Detractors} = \dfrac{300}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

$$
\text{NPS} = 55 - 15 = \mathbf{+40}
$$

**Interpretation:** An NPS of **+40** is **excellent** (industry average for cloud-kitchens is +25). This indicates strong word-of-mouth potential and validates market acceptance.

### Step 5: Compute 3-Year Market Growth Rate (CAGR)

**Given:** Market value grew from **₹400 Cr** (Year 0) to **₹540 Cr** (Year 3).

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left( \dfrac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left( \dfrac{540}{400} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} = (1.35)^{0.3333} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} = 1.1055 - 1 = 0.1055 = \mathbf{10.55\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Kochi breakfast market is growing at **~10.55% per annum**, indicating a healthy, expanding market — favorable for entry.

### Step 6: Compute Competitor Strength Score (Weighted Benchmarking)

| **Attribute (i)** | **Weight ($w_i$)** | **Our Score ($s_i$)** | **Weighted ($w_i \times s_i$)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Price Competitiveness | 0.25 | 7 | 1.75 |
| Menu Variety | 0.20 | 8 | 1.60 |
| Delivery Time | 0.20 | 6 | 1.20 |
| Brand Recognition | 0.15 | 4 | 0.60 |
| Customer Service | 0.20 | 9 | 1.80 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — | **6.95 / 10** |

$$
\text{Strength Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i = 6.95
$$

**Interpretation:** We are **moderately strong** (6.95/10) versus competitors. Priority improvement areas: **Brand Recognition (0.60)** and **Delivery Time (1.20)**.

### Step 7: Compute Intelligence ROI

**Assumed:** Decisions enabled by MI generated **₹1.2 Cr additional revenue**; MI program cost **₹15 Lakh**.

$$
\text{ROI}_{\text{MI}} = \dfrac{1.2 \text{ Cr} - 0.15 \text{ Cr}}{0.15 \text{ Cr}} = \dfrac{1.05}{0.15} = 7.0 = \mathbf{700\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every ₹1 invested in Market Intelligence returns ₹7 — highly justified investment.

## 3.2 SWOT Analysis Matrix (Analytical Tool Used in MI)

> **Applied to the cloud-kitchen scenario:**

| | **Helpful** | **Harmful** |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Origin** | **STRENGTHS:** Authentic South-Indian menu, strong supply-chain tie-ups, founder's IIT/KTU pedigree | **WEAKNESSES:** Limited brand awareness, no offline presence, single city operation |
| **External Origin** | **OPPORTUNITIES:** Growing working-professional segment, UPI payment adoption, health-conscious eating trend | **THREATS:** Aggressive Zomato/Swiggy discounting, price wars, food regulatory changes |

> [!NOTE]
> The SWOT matrix is **the most frequently asked analytical tool** in KTU Market Intelligence questions. Memorize the 2x2 structure and the Internal/External × Helpful/Harmful logic.

## 3.3 Porter's Five Forces — Analytical Framework

| **Force** | **Intensity (1–5)** | **Implication for the cloud-kitchen** |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat of New Entrants** | 4 (High) | Low entry barriers, capital-light model invites competition |
| **Bargaining Power of Suppliers** | 2 (Low) | Many raw-material vendors, easily switchable |
| **Bargaining Power of Buyers** | 5 (Very High) | Customers can switch apps with one tap; high price sensitivity |
| **Threat of Substitutes** | 4 (High) | Home-cooked meals, dhabas, traditional tiffin centers |
| **Industry Rivalry** | 5 (Very High) | Intense Zomato-Swiggy competition, price wars |

> **Strategic Insight:** Forces 4 and 5 are dominant → Focus on **differentiation (unique recipes, hyper-local taste, strong delivery network)** rather than price competition.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Market Intelligence. How is it different from Market Research?**

**Model Answer:**

**Market Intelligence (MI)** is the continuous and systematic process of gathering, analyzing, and disseminating information about the external business environment — covering customers, competitors, products, partners, and market trends — to support strategic and tactical decision-making.

**Key Differences:**

| **Aspect** | **Market Intelligence** | **Market Research** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broad, covers entire market environment | Narrow, focused on a specific question |
| **Duration** | Continuous, ongoing | Episodic, project-based |
| **Objective** | Long-term strategic awareness | Solve a specific current problem |
| **Output** | Ongoing intelligence reports and alerts | One-time research findings |
| **Example** | Monitoring competitor pricing daily | "Should we launch Product X in City Y?" |

> **Conclusion:** Market Research is a **subset/tool** of the broader Market Intelligence function. MI is the **continuous radar**; Research is the **periodic deep-dive**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any THREE components of Market Intelligence.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Customer Intelligence:** Information about customers' demographics, psychographics, buying behavior, satisfaction levels (NPS, CSAT), and lifetime value. *Example:* Tracking churn rate in a telecom company.

2. **Competitor Intelligence:** Strategic and tactical data on direct and indirect competitors, including their pricing, product launches, marketing campaigns, strengths, and weaknesses. *Example:* Monitoring Flipkart's Big Billion Days offers.

3. **Product Intelligence:** Data on product performance, customer perception, feature gaps, and lifecycle stage. *Example:* Analyzing online reviews to identify which smartphone features users value most.

4. *(Optional fourth for completeness)* **Brand Intelligence:** Measures of brand awareness, perception, and equity through surveys and social media sentiment.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **PRIMARY CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five-stage Market Intelligence process** with a suitable real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Porter's Five Forces model** and apply it to analyze the competitive structure of the **Indian EV (Electric Vehicle) industry**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Market Intelligence Process

The Market Intelligence process comprises **five interlinked stages**, often visualized as a cycle:

**Stage 1 — Planning & Direction:**
- Define business objectives and Key Intelligence Questions (KIQs).
- Example: Tata Motors asks, *"What is the global EV adoption rate and what are Tesla's pricing strategies?"*
- Assign a cross-functional intelligence team and set the budget/timeline.

**Stage 2 — Data Collection:**
- **Primary:** Customer surveys on EV preferences, dealer interviews, focus groups in metro cities.
- **Secondary:** BloombergNEF reports, NITI Aayog EV policy documents, SIAM statistics, competitor press releases.
- **Internal:** Tata's own CRM data, sales records of Nexon EV, website analytics.

**Stage 3 — Data Analysis:**
- Apply **SWOT** to compare Tata's EV strengths with BYD/MG.
- Run **PESTLE** to understand FAME-II subsidies, fuel price trends, charging infrastructure status.
- Conduct **competitor benchmarking** on range, price, charging time.
- Apply **sentiment analysis** on Twitter/LinkedIn posts about Tata EVs.

**Stage 4 — Reporting & Dissemination:**
- Produce an **executive dashboard** showing competitor price movements.
- Issue **real-time alerts** when a competitor launches a new model.
- Distribute **monthly intelligence briefs** to the Strategy and Product teams.

**Stage 5 — Decision-Making & Action:**
- Based on intelligence, Tata decides to **reduce Nexon EV price by ₹50,000** to counter MG ZS EV.
- Launches a **new long-range variant** to address range-anxiety feedback.
- The outcomes of these decisions feed back into Stage 1 (closing the loop).

**Real-World Anchor:** Tesla's 2023 price-cut decision was driven by **competitor and demand intelligence** showing slowing demand and BYD's aggressive expansion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Porter's Five Forces — Indian EV Industry

| **Force** | **Intensity** | **Analysis** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Threat of New Entrants** | **High (4/5)** | Low capital for 2-wheelers; many startups (Ola Electric, Ather, Simple Energy); government PLI scheme encourages new players |
| **2. Bargaining Power of Suppliers** | **High (4/5)** | Battery cells dominated by few players (CATL, LG); lithium and cobalt supply concentrated in few countries; import dependency |
| **3. Bargaining Power of Buyers** | **Medium-High (3.5/5)** | Price-sensitive Indian market; multiple brand options; increasing awareness, but total options still limited |
| **4. Threat of Substitutes** | **Medium (3/5)** | ICE vehicles still dominate; public transport; CNG/bi-fuel alternatives; hybrids gaining traction |
| **5. Industry Rivalry** | **High (4.5/5)** | Intense competition among Tata, Mahindra, MG, BYD, Hyundai, Kia, Ola, and upcoming Tesla; price wars, feature wars |

**Strategic Implications:**
- **Suppliers (batteries)** are the most critical bottleneck → Localize battery manufacturing (Tata's Agratas plant).
- **Buyer power** can be reduced via **brand differentiation, after-sales service, financing options**.
- **Substitutes** demand focus on **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) messaging** to highlight EV savings.
- **High rivalry** rewards **scale players** like Tata and Mahindra with cost advantages.

**Conclusion:** The Indian EV industry's **attractiveness is moderate-to-high** in the long term, with **supplier and rivalry forces being the most critical** to monitor continuously through Market Intelligence.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is **SWOT Analysis**? Prepare a **SWOT matrix for a startup launching an AI-powered EdTech platform in Kerala**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **sources of Market Intelligence data** and explain the difference between **primary and secondary data** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): SWOT Analysis of Kerala AI-EdTech Startup

**SWOT Analysis** is a strategic planning tool that evaluates an organization's **Strengths** (internal-positive), **Weaknesses** (internal-negative), **Opportunities** (external-positive), and **Threats** (external-negative). It synthesizes Market Intelligence into a 2x2 actionable matrix.

**Applied SWOT Matrix — "EduAI Kerala" Startup:**

| | **Helpful (To Achieve Objective)** | **Harmful (To Achieve Objective)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Origin** | **STRENGTHS:** <br>• AI/ML expertise from KTU/Cochin University talent pool <br>• Localized Malayalam-language content <br>• Low operating cost (Kerala's high digital literacy) | **WEAKNESSES:** <br>• Limited brand recognition <br>• Small initial funding (bootstrapped) <br>• Dependence on internet penetration in rural Kerala <br>• No offline coaching backup |
| **External Origin** | **OPPORTUNITIES:** <br>• Kerala's 100% literacy + digital literacy push <br>• NEP 2020 emphasis on technology-based learning <br>• Growing demand for upskilling (AI, data science) <br>• Government of Kerala's "KITE" infrastructure | **THREATS:** <br>• Aggressive competition from Byju's, Unacademy, Coursera <br>• Data privacy regulations (DPDP Act 2023) <br>• Resistance from traditional tuition centers <br>• AI-hallucination risks damaging credibility |

**Strategic Insights from SWOT:**
- Convert **Strength × Opportunity (SO strategy):** Use Malayalam content + Kerala's digital literacy to dominate regional market.
- Address **Weakness × Threat (WT strategy):** Build offline hybrid model to counter traditional players.
- Leverage **Strength × Threat (ST strategy):** Use AI accuracy to compete with Byju's content quality.
- Exploit **Opportunity × Weakness (WO strategy):** Partner with KITE/government schools to overcome brand-recognition weakness.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Sources of Market Intelligence Data

Market Intelligence draws from **three primary source categories**:

**1. Primary Data Sources (Field-sourced, first-hand):**
- Data collected directly by the firm for a specific intelligence need.
- **Methods:** Surveys, in-depth interviews, focus groups, field observation, experiments.
- **Examples:**
  - Surveying 1,000 customers about their EV purchase intent.
  - Conducting focus groups to test a new shampoo fragrance.
- **Strength:** Highly specific, current, tailored.
- **Weakness:** Expensive, time-consuming, may have bias.

**2. Secondary Data Sources (Desk-research, second-hand):**
- Data that already exists, collected by others for different purposes.
- **Categories:**
  - **Published sources:** Industry reports (Nielsen, Gartner, McKinsey), trade journals, newspapers, magazines.
  - **Government sources:** Census of India, RBI reports, Ministry of Statistics, NITI Aayog publications.
  - **Online sources:** Company websites, social media, Google Trends, Statista, SEMrush.
  - **Internal databases:** CRM, ERP, sales records, web analytics.
- **Examples:**
  - Reading the **Annual Report of Maruti Suzuki** to understand their strategy.
  - Using **Google Trends** to track regional interest in a product category.
- **Strength:** Cheap, fast, large volume available.
- **Weakness:** May be outdated, irrelevant, or inaccurate.

**3. Tertiary Sources (Compiled secondary data):**
- Indexes, bibliographies, directories that point to primary and secondary sources.

**Key Distinction Table for KTU Exam:**

| **Aspect** | **Primary Data** | **Secondary Data** |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Collected first-hand by researcher | Already collected by others |
| **Cost** | High | Low |
| **Time** | Long | Short |
| **Specificity** | Tailored to exact need | General purpose |
| **Currency** | Very current | May be outdated |
| **Bias** | Researcher bias possible | Source-publisher bias possible |
| **Example** | Customer survey on AI-EdTech preferences | NITI Aayog report on Kerala's digital literacy |

> **Conclusion:** Effective Market Intelligence programs **blend both primary and secondary sources** — secondary data for breadth and context, primary data for depth and current validation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Market Intelligence Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Market Research with Market Intelligence** — These are NOT the same. State the difference explicitly. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Listing tools without explaining application** — Don't just say "SWOT." Explain each quadrant with examples. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 3. **Skipping the cyclical/feedback nature** — Always mention the **feedback loop** in the MI process. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring ethics** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes ethical intelligence gathering. Mention **SCIP (Society of Competitive Intelligence Professionals) Code of Ethics** if asked about CI. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the framework to a context** — Generic answers lose marks. Always **apply** SWOT/PESTLE/Porter to a specific industry/scenario. (Loss: 3–4 marks)
> 6. **Calculation errors in CAGR / TAM / NPS** — Practice numerical problems. The CAGR exponent is **1/n**, not **n**. NPS uses **only % Promoters − % Detractors**, ignoring Passives. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 7. **No mention of continuous monitoring** — MI is **continuous**, not one-time. Reinforce this in every answer. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Market Intelligence (Module 3)**

- 🔑 **Definition:** Continuous, systematic process of gathering → analyzing → disseminating external market information for strategic decisions.

- 🔑 **Three Source Categories:** Primary (first-hand), Secondary (existing data), Tertiary (compilations).

- 🔑 **Five-Stage Process:** Planning → Collection → Analysis → Reporting → Decision (with feedback loop).

- 🔑 **Seven Key Components:** Customer, Competitor, Product, Market/Industry, Partner, Brand, Sales Intelligence.

- 🔑 **Key Analytical Tools (must memorize):**
  - **SWOT** (Internal × External × Helpful × Harmful)
  - **PESTLE** (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental)
  - **Porter's Five Forces** (Entry, Suppliers, Buyers, Substitutes, Rivalry)
  - **Competitor Profiling, Benchmarking, Win/Loss Analysis**

- 🔑 **Critical Quantitative Formulas:**
  - $\text{TAM} = N \times \bar{R}$
  - $\text{Market Share \%} = \dfrac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$
  - $\text{CAGR} = \left( \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$
  - $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$

- 🔑 **MR vs. MI Distinction:** MR = episodic, specific question | MI = continuous, broad monitoring.

- 🔑 **Ethics:** Always mention **SCIP Code of Ethics** in competitor intelligence discussions.

- 🔑 **Feedback Loop:** Every answer about the MI process must end by mentioning the **cyclical nature and feedback loop**.

- 🔑 **Application is Mandatory:** Always **apply** frameworks (SWOT, Porter, PESTLE) to a **real industry or company** — generic answers are penalized.

- 🔑 **Key KPIs to Memorize:** TAM, SAM, SOM, CAGR, NPS, CSAT, Market Share, Win Rate, Sentiment Score, MI ROI.

- 🔑 **Continuous vs. Episodic:** Reinforce in every answer that MI is **continuous** and MR is **episodic** — this single distinction often earns or loses 2 marks.

---

Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Text Analysis (Text Mining / Text Analytics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Text Analysis* (also called *Text Mining* or *Text Analytics*) is the process of deriving **high-quality, structured information from unstructured textual data** through the application of statistical, linguistic, Machine Learning, and Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques. It converts raw text into quantifiable, actionable business intelligence. In simple terms — businesses sit on a mountain of unstructured text: customer reviews, tweets, emails, support tickets, survey responses, news articles, legal contracts, and medical records. A human cannot read **millions of documents**, but algorithms can. Text Analytics is the *automated reading machine* of business. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (The "Smart Library" Analogy):** > Imagine a library containing **10 million books** written in 50 different languages, and a customer asks: *"What do people feel about our new electric car?"* A human librarian would take **years**. Text Analytics is like giving that librarian **supernatural speed, perfect memory, and pattern-recognition glasses**. It opens every book, reads every sentence, detects the customer's name, identifies positive/negative tone, and delivers a one-page report — all in seconds. ### Sub-fields of Text Analysis (Syllabus Highlights) | Sub-field | What it Does | Business Use | |---|---|---| | **Information Extraction (IE)** | Pulls entities (names, dates, money) from text | KYC, invoice processing | | **Sentiment Analysis / Opinion Mining** | Detects polarity (positive/negative/neutral) | Brand monitoring, review scoring | | **Text Classification** | Assigns predefined categories to documents | Spam filtering, ticket routing | | **Topic Modeling** | Discovers hidden themes in a corpus | Trend discovery, content strategy | | **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Identifies persons, organizations, locations | News intelligence, CRM enrichment | | **Text Summarization** | Compresses long documents into key points | Executive briefings, legal review | | **Keyword / Key-Phrase Extraction** | Finds most informative terms | SEO, tagging | --- ## 1.2 Search Analysis (Search Engine Analytics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Search Analysis* is the systematic study of the **search behaviour of users** (queries entered, clicks made, dwell time, bounce rate) and the **performance of search systems** (relevance, ranking, click-through rate) to optimize information retrieval, content visibility, and conversion outcomes. In other words — Search Analysis asks two questions: 1. *What are people searching for?* (Demand side) 2. *How well does my content satisfy that search?* (Supply side) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (The "Restaurant Menu" Analogy):** > A restaurant owner observes that **70% of customers order Biriyani** but it appears at the bottom of the menu. By simply **moving Biriyani to the top**, sales rise 35%. Search Analysis is identical: Google/Bing observe that **users type "best noise-cancelling headphones under ₹5000"** but most websites bury that answer 10 paragraphs deep. Search engines **re-rank content** so the best, most relevant answer sits at position #1. Search Analytics measures *how often*, *how long*, and *where* users click. ### Pillars of Search Analysis - **Search Engine Optimization (SEO)** — improving organic ranking - **Search Engine Marketing (SEM)** — paid search advertising (Google Ads) - **Query Log Analysis** — mining user search histories - **Click-Through Rate (CTR) Analysis** — snippet attractiveness - **Information Retrieval (IR) Models** — Boolean, Vector Space, Probabilistic - **Ranking Algorithms** — TF-IDF, BM25, PageRank # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Text Analytics Pipeline (Operational Logic) Text Analytics is **not a single algorithm** — it is a **pipeline** of stages. Each stage refines the data. 1. **Text Collection (Corpus Assembly)** — gather raw text from APIs, websites, databases, sensors. 2. **Text Pre-processing / Cleaning** — remove noise so the algorithm sees *signal*. 3. **Text Representation / Vectorization** — convert words into numbers (computers understand only numbers). 4. **Feature Engineering** — extract statistical or semantic features. 5. **Model Application** — run classification, clustering, sentiment, topic models. 6. **Visualization & Insights** — word clouds, dashboards, trend lines. ### Step 2 — Text Pre-processing Operations - **Tokenization** — splitting `"I love KTU exams!"` → `["I", "love", "KTU", "exams", "!"]` - **Lowercasing** — `Hello` → `hello` - **Stop-word Removal** — delete `the, is, at, which, on` (no semantic value). - **Stemming** — `running, ran, runs` → `run` (crude rule-based chopping). - **Lemmatization** — `better` → `good` (dictionary-based, more accurate). - **Punctuation & Special Character Removal**. - **Normalization** — handling emojis, hashtags (`#BiriyaniLovers`), abbreviations (`gr8` → `great`). - **N-Gram Generation** — combine adjacent tokens (`New York` as one bigram). ### Step 3 — Text Representation Models | Model | Description | Equation / Notation | |---|---|---| | **Bag of Words (BoW)** | Counts word frequencies; ignores order | $\text{BoW}(d) = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n]$ | | **TF-IDF** | Weights rare-but-important words higher | See Formula Sheet | | **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe)** | Dense vectors capturing semantic meaning | $\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | | **Bag of N-Grams** | Extends BoW to phrase-level tokens | N-gram counts | | **One-Hot Encoding** | Binary vector per word | $w_i \in \{0,1\}^n$ | --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > These are the **exam-critical formulas** for the module. Master the table — KTU loves 7- or 14-mark derivations on these. | # | Concept | Formula | Symbols / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Term Frequency (TF)** | $\text{TF}(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = raw count of term $t$ in doc $d$ | | 2 | **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ | $N$ = total docs, $\text{df}(t)$ = docs containing $t$ | | 3 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $\text{TF-IDF}(t,d) = \text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)$ | Dimensionless score | | 4 | **Smoothed IDF (avoids div-by-zero)** | $\text{IDF}_{\text{smooth}}(t) = \log\left(\dfrac{N+1}{\text{df}(t)+1}\right) + 1$ | Used by scikit-learn | | 5 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\lVert \vec{A} \rVert \cdot \lVert \vec{B} \rVert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$; for TF-IDF, $[0, 1]$ | | 6 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}}$ | High $P$ = few false alarms | | 7 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}}$ | High $R$ = few missed positives | | 8 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ | | 9 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{\text{TP}+\text{TN}}{\text{TP}+\text{TN}+\text{FP}+\text{FN}}$ | Overall correctness | | 10 | **BM25 (Okapi Ranking)** | $\text{BM25}(t,d) = \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{f_{t,d}(k_1+1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1(1-b+b\cdot \frac{\vert d\vert}{\text{avgdl}})}$ | $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b \approx 0.75$ | | 11 | **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ | | 12 | **Bounce Rate** | $\text{Bounce} = \dfrac{\text{Single-page sessions}}{\text{Total sessions}}$ | User experience metric | | 13 | **Polarity Score (Sentiment)** | $S = \dfrac{P - N}{P + N + 2}$ | $P$ = positive words, $N$ = negative words | | 14 | **Jaccard Similarity (Query-Doc)** | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Set-based similarity | | 15 | **PageRank (Simplified)** | $\text{PR}(p) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \dfrac{\text{PR}(q)}{L(q)}$ | $d$ = damping factor $\approx 0.85$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > In all KTU 2024 paper derivations, when you write a set notation like $\vert A \cap B \vert$, use **`\vert`** (not `|`) inside LaTeX to avoid markdown breakage. The same rule applies in the exam — write neatly with clear absolute-value bars. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Text & Search Analytics | Industry | Application | Outcome | |---|---|---| | **E-Commerce** | Review sentiment + product search ranking | Amazon increases sales 20-30% via ranking | | **Banking** | Fraud detection in transaction narratives | Reduces false positives by 40% | | **Healthcare** | Mining Electronic Medical Records (EMR) | Early disease outbreak detection | | **Government** | Public-policy opinion mining on Twitter | Real-time citizen feedback | | **HR / Recruitment** | Resume parsing + JD matching | Faster, unbiased shortlisting | | **Legal** | Contract clause extraction & risk tagging | Saves 70% review time | | **Hospitality** | TripAdvisor / Google review analysis | Hotel dynamic pricing | ### Why TF-IDF matters in Search A search engine receives **billions of queries daily**. The simple question it must answer: *"Does document $d$ match query $q$?"* Pure word-count fails because the word `the` appears in every document. TF-IDF fixes this by **demoting common words and promoting discriminative ones**. That is why a search for *"machine learning algorithms"* returns the AI textbook, not the dictionary. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — TF-IDF Computation (Classic KTU Derivation) > **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** > Consider a mini-corpus of 3 documents: > - $D_1$ = *"the cat sat on the mat"* > - $D_2$ = *"the dog sat on the log"* > - $D_3$ = *"cats and dogs are pets"* > > Compute the **TF-IDF vector** for the term `"cat"` with respect to $D_1$ and the term `"dog"` with respect to $D_2$. Assume natural log. ### Step A — Term Frequency (TF) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TF}(\text{"cat"}, D_1) &= \dfrac{\text{Count of "cat" in } D_1}{\text{Total words in } D_1} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{6} \quad \text{(since "the cat sat on the mat" has 6 tokens)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TF}(\text{"dog"}, D_2) &= \dfrac{\text{Count of "dog" in } D_2}{\text{Total words in } D_2} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{6} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### Step B — Document Frequency (DF) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{df}(\text{"cat"}) &= \text{Number of docs containing "cat"} = 1 \text{ (only } D_1\text{)} \\ \text{df}(\text{"dog"}) &= 1 \text{ (only } D_2\text{)} \\ N &= 3 \text{ documents} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step C — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IDF}(\text{"cat"}) &= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(\text{"cat"}) + 1}\right) + 1 \\ &= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1+1}\right) + 1 \\ &= \ln(1.5) + 1 \\ &= 0.4055 + 1 \\ &= 1.4055 \end{aligned} $$ (Using smoothed IDF to handle the case where a term is missing in a document — KTU examiners expect this form.) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IDF}(\text{"dog"}) &= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{3}{1+1}\right) + 1 = 1.4055 \quad \text{(same as above)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### Step D — TF-IDF Weight $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TF-IDF}(\text{"cat"}, D_1) &= \text{TF}(\text{"cat"}, D_1) \cdot \text{IDF}(\text{"cat"}) \\ &= 0.1667 \times 1.4055 \\ &= 0.2343 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TF-IDF}(\text{"dog"}, D_2) &= 0.1667 \times 1.4055 = 0.2343 \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is this useful?** The high TF-IDF score (0.2343) tells the search engine that "cat" is **discriminative** in $D_1$ — meaning $D_1$ is *the* document to return for a query about cats. Common words like `the` would receive an IDF close to 0, so they get filtered out. **This is the soul of Information Retrieval.** --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Cosine Similarity Between Two Documents > **Problem:** Two documents have TF-IDF vectors. > $A = (0.5, \, 0.0, \, 0.7)$ > $B = (0.0, \, 0.9, \, 0.4)$ > Find the cosine similarity. State whether the documents are semantically similar. ### Step A — Dot Product $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} &= (0.5)(0.0) + (0.0)(0.9) + (0.7)(0.4) \\ &= 0 + 0 + 0.28 \\ &= 0.28 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step B — Magnitudes $$ \begin{aligned} \lVert \vec{A} \rVert &= \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.0^2 + 0.7^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0 + 0.49} = \sqrt{0.74} = 0.8602 \\ \lVert \vec{B} \rVert &= \sqrt{0.0^2 + 0.9^2 + 0.4^2} = \sqrt{0 + 0.81 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.97} = 0.9849 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step C — Cosine Similarity $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sim}(A,B) &= \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\lVert \vec{A} \rVert \cdot \lVert \vec{B} \rVert} \\ &= \dfrac{0.28}{0.8602 \times 0.9849} \\ &= \dfrac{0.28}{0.8473} \\ &= 0.3305 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** > $\text{sim} = 0.3305$ is **closer to 0 than 1**, meaning the documents are **weakly related**. They share little vocabulary in the TF-IDF space. If it were $\geq 0.7$, the documents would be near-duplicates. KTU loves this kind of geometric interpretation. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Sentiment Polarity Score > **Problem:** A product review contains $P = 8$ positive words, $N = 3$ negative words. Compute the polarity score $S$ and classify the sentiment. ### Solution $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \dfrac{P - N}{P + N + 2} \\ &= \dfrac{8 - 3}{8 + 3 + 2} \\ &= \dfrac{5}{13} \\ &= 0.3846 \end{aligned} $$ > **Classification (KTU convention):** > - $S \geq 0.3$ → **Positive** ✓ (here $S = 0.3846$) > - $-0.3 < S < 0.3$ → Neutral > - $S \leq -0.3$ → Negative The review is classified as **positive sentiment**, with a moderate confidence. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Search Engine Performance Metrics (CTR & Quality) > **Problem:** A webpage received **12,000 impressions** in Google Search and **840 clicks**. Compute CTR. If the average position is 4.2, comment on the quality. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CTR} &= \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} = \dfrac{840}{12000} = 0.07 = 7\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Industry benchmark (Kerala / Indian e-commerce, 2024):** > - Position 1: 25-30% CTR > - Position 5: 6-7% CTR > > At position 4.2, a CTR of 7% is **on-target**, indicating the title-tag and meta-description are well-crafted. Below 4% at this position = needs SEO improvement. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Text Analytics Pipeline (End-to-End Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Raw Text Sources

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define Text Mining. List any four major applications of Text Mining in Business Analytics.** *(3 marks)* ### **Model Answer:** Text Mining (Text Analytics) is the process of extracting **meaningful patterns, sentiments, entities, and topics** from large volumes of unstructured textual data using NLP, statistics, and Machine Learning. **Four applications:** 1. **Sentiment Analysis of Reviews** — Amazon, Flipkart mine customer feedback to detect product issues early. 2. **Spam Detection in Emails** — Gmail classifies 99.9% of spam using text classification models (Naive Bayes, BERT). 3. **Customer Support Ticket Routing** — Companies like Freshworks use text classification to auto-assign tickets to the right team. 4. **Brand Reputation Monitoring** — Social listening tools (Brand24, Sprout Social) track brand mentions across Twitter, Reddit, and news for crisis management. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Explain with an example how TF-IDF is used to weight terms in a document collection.** *(3 marks)* ### **Model Answer:** **TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency)** is a numerical statistic that reflects how important a word is to a document within a corpus. - **TF** measures how frequently a term appears in a single document. - **IDF** reduces the weight of terms that appear in *many* documents (like `the`, `is`) because they are less discriminative. **Example:** Suppose we have 1000 documents and the word `algorithm` appears in only 10. Its IDF will be **high**, so when a user searches for `algorithm`, documents containing that term get ranked higher. $$ \text{TF-IDF}(t,d) = \text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right) $$ --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** of the following. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **architecture of a typical search engine** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **crawler, indexer, and ranker** in detail. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **TF-IDF computation** for a small text corpus of 3 documents. Also compute the **cosine similarity** between two documents using the obtained vectors. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution to (a):** A search engine is a software system designed to search for information on the World Wide Web. The three core components are: 1. **Web Crawler (Spider / Bot):** - An automated program (e.g., Googlebot) that browses the web by following hyperlinks. - Starts from a seed URL, fetches pages, extracts new links, and recursively visits them. - Stores fetched pages in a raw page repository. - Must obey the `robots.txt` protocol and respect crawl-delay directives. 2. **Indexer:** - Takes the raw HTML, removes tags, extracts textual content, tokenizes it, and builds an **Inverted Index**. - **Inverted Index** structure: `term → [list of documents containing that term]` - Example entry: `("Biriyani", [Doc12, Doc47, Doc89, ...])` - Also computes metadata: TF, IDF, document length, PageRank. 3. **Ranker (Ranking Engine):** - Given a user query, retrieves candidate documents from the inverted index. - Computes a relevance score using **TF-IDF, BM25, PageRank, freshness, user location, personalization signals**. - Returns the **top-K (usually 10)** results as the SERP. - Modern rankers use **Learning-to-Rank (LTR)** and **Neural IR (BERT, MUM)** for semantic understanding. ``` User Query → Query Processor → Index Lookup → Ranking → SERP (Inverted Index) (BM25+PR) ``` #### **Model Solution to (b):** *(Working already shown in Section 3.1 and 3.2 — reproduce the same step-by-step on the answer sheet.)* **Recap of expected steps:** 1. List the corpus: $D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$. 2. Compute TF for each term in each document. 3. Compute DF (document frequency) for each term. 4. Compute IDF using $\ln(N / \text{df}(t))$. 5. Multiply TF × IDF to get the TF-IDF vector. 6. For cosine similarity, use the TF-IDF vectors of two documents as $A$ and $B$. 7. Apply $\text{sim}(A,B) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\lVert \vec{A} \rVert \, \lVert \vec{B} \rVert}$. 8. Interpret the result in the range $[0, 1]$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Discuss the concept of **Sentiment Analysis**. Explain with a real-world example how a business can use sentiment analysis to improve decision-making. Differentiate between **lexicon-based** and **machine-learning-based** approaches. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy** with formulas. A search engine returned 50 documents for a query. Of these, 40 are relevant, and out of 80 actually relevant documents in the database, 60 were not retrieved. Compute Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution to (a):** **Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)** is a Natural Language Processing task that identifies, extracts, and quantifies the emotional tone (positive, negative, neutral) expressed in text. **Real-world example:** *Café Coffee Day (CCD)* wants to monitor customer sentiment on Twitter after launching a new cold-coffee variant. - They collect **50,000 tweets** mentioning the product using Twitter API. - Lexicon-based scoring classifies each tweet as Positive / Negative / Neutral. - Result: **62% Positive, 28% Neutral, 10% Negative.** - The **10% negative cluster** is drilled into — complaints about *"too sweet"* are dominant. CCD reformulates the recipe → next quarter sentiment rises to **78% Positive** and sales grow **15%**. **Lexicon-based Approach:** - Uses a predefined dictionary (e.g., VADER, Bing Liu Lexicon) mapping words to sentiment scores. - Aggregates word scores to get a sentence/document polarity. - **Pros:** Simple, fast, no training data needed. **Cons:** Cannot handle sarcasm, context, domain-specific terms. **Machine-Learning-based Approach:** - Treats sentiment as a **classification problem** (binary or multi-class). - Features: TF-IDF, word embeddings, n-grams. - Models: Naive Bayes, SVM, LSTM, BERT. - **Pros:** Handles context, sarcasm (with enough data). **Cons:** Needs labelled training corpus, compute-intensive. #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Formulas (already in the cheat sheet):** $$ P = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \qquad R = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} \qquad F_1 = \dfrac{2 P R}{P + R} \qquad \text{Acc} = \dfrac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{Total}} $$ **Given:** - Retrieved = 50 - Relevant in retrieved (TP) = 40 - Relevant in DB but not retrieved (FN) = 60 - Not relevant and retrieved (FP) = 50 − 40 = **10** - Total relevant in DB = TP + FN = 40 + 60 = 100 **Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= \dfrac{40}{40 + 10} = \dfrac{40}{50} = 0.80 \;(80\%) \\ R &= \dfrac{40}{40 + 60} = \dfrac{40}{100} = 0.40 \;(40\%) \\ F_1 &= \dfrac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.40}{0.80 + 0.40} = \dfrac{0.64}{1.20} = 0.5333 \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** High precision (80%) means most retrieved docs are relevant. Low recall (40%) means many relevant docs were missed. The F1 of 0.53 reflects the imbalance — common in search engines that prioritize precision (relevance) over recall (coverage) to keep the SERP clean. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT write the formula `|A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B|` with bare `|` symbols on the answer sheet** — the evaluator may misread it. Use clear, large absolute-value bars: $|A \cap B| / |A \cup B|$ written with full-size parentheses or "card(A∩B) / card(A∪B)". > 2. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy** is the most common loss-of-marks mistake. Accuracy measures correctness over *all classes*; Precision is a per-class metric focused on *predicted positives*. > 3. **Forgetting to take $\log$ in IDF** — students often write TF-IDF as $N/\text{df}(t)$ directly, missing the logarithm. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in any KTU paper. > 4. **In cosine similarity**, students frequently write the dot product in the numerator but forget the denominator ($\lVert A \rVert \cdot \lVert B \rVert$) — that is a **conceptual error, not a typo**. > 5. **In sentiment classification**, the threshold is *not always 0*. KTU follows the convention: $\geq 0.3$ positive, $\leq -0.3$ negative, else neutral. State the threshold explicitly in your answer. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist) - **Text Mining vs Text Analytics vs NLP:** Text Mining is the *extraction* of knowledge; Text Analytics is the *measurement and interpretation*; NLP is the *underlying language technology* enabling both. - **TF-IDF = TF × IDF**; TF captures importance *within* a document; IDF captures rarity *across* the corpus. - **Cosine similarity** is the **standard metric** for measuring document similarity in the Vector Space Model; it ignores magnitude, focuses on angle between vectors. - **Sentiment Analysis** classifies text as Positive / Negative / Neutral using lexicon or ML-based approaches. - **Precision vs Recall trade-off:** Higher precision = fewer false positives; higher recall = fewer false negatives. F1 balances both. - **Search Engine** = Crawler + Indexer + Ranker. Ranking uses **TF-IDF, BM25, PageRank, and modern Neural IR models** (BERT, MUM). - **SEO On-Page:** Title tag, meta description, H1/H2 headings, internal linking, keyword density. - **SEO Off-Page:** Backlinks from high-DA domains, social signals, brand mentions. - **CTR** is a critical search metric; Position-1 typically gets 25-30% CTR. - **Bounce Rate** > 70% = poor user experience; aim for 40-60%. - **Jaccard similarity** is set-based, **Cosine similarity** is vector-based — KTU often asks for the distinction. - **PageRank** treats the web as a Markov chain; a page's rank is the steady-state probability a random surfer lands on it. - **BM25** improves on TF-IDF by normalizing for document length (parameter $b$) and saturating TF (parameter $k_1$). - Always state **assumptions** (e.g., natural log vs log base 2, smoothed vs unsmoothed IDF) before KTU derivations. - **For 14-mark answers**, KTU expects: definition → diagram → formula → numerical example → interpretation. - **Avoid bare `|` in math** — use `\vert` or full-size notation in both the exam and your handwritten copies. > **Best of luck with your KTU ESE preparation! 🌟**

topics.title: Customer Profiling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Customer Profiling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CUSTOMER PROFILING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Profiling** is the systematic process of collecting, analysing, and synthesising quantitative and qualitative data about an organisation's existing or prospective customers to construct a comprehensive, multi-dimensional archetype (or *persona*) that represents the behavioural, demographic, psychographic, and transactional characteristics of a defined market segment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Business Analytics terminology, customer profiling is positioned as a **descriptive analytics** technique that bridges raw Customer Relationship Management (CRM) data with **prescriptive strategies** such as targeted marketing, churn prevention, and lifetime value optimisation. It is the *foundation block* upon which segmentation, targeting, and positioning (STP) decisions are operationalised.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Shop Customer ID Card"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine you own a small café in Kochi. You notice three distinct groups walk in:
> - *Group A:* 25-year-old tech professionals who order oat-milk lattes between 8 AM–10 AM and pay via UPI.
> - *Group B:* 45-year-old homemakers who visit on weekends with family and prefer filter coffee and snacks.
> - *Group C:* College students who come at night, study for hours, and order only one item repeatedly.
>
> **Customer Profiling** is the act of writing a detailed "identity card" for each group — their age, income, visit time, spend pattern, loyalty, and motivation. Once these cards exist, you can decide *what* to offer, *when*, and *to whom* — moving from guesswork to data-driven decisions.

### 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are universally used in customer profiling and are **KTU 2024 high-yield**:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Lifetime Value** | CLV | Currency (₹, $, €) |
| **Recency** | R | Days |
| **Frequency** | F | Transactions / period |
| **Monetary Value** | M | Currency / transaction |
| **Churn Rate** | $C_{r}$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Average Purchase Value** | APV | Currency |
| **Purchase Frequency** | PF | Transactions / year |

### 1.4 Position in the Business Analytics Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> Customer Profiling operates at the intersection of:
> 1. **Data Collection Layer** — POS, CRM, web logs, surveys
> 2. **Analytical Layer** — clustering, classification, RFM modelling
> 3. **Decision Layer** — personalised campaigns, dynamic pricing
>
> It transforms *unknown visitors* into *known entities* with predictable behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars (DGPB Framework) of Customer Profiling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus mandates mastery of the **DGPB dimensions**. Every profile must be described along these axes:

**1. Demographic Profiling (D)**
- Age, gender, income, education, occupation, marital status, family size.
- **Why it matters:** Demographic attributes are the most stable predictors of consumption patterns and are easily obtainable from CRM forms, loyalty cards, and census overlays.

**2. Geographic Profiling (G)**
- Country, region, climate, urban/rural classification, population density.
- **Why it matters:** Geographic clustering informs distribution logistics, regional pricing, and culturally tailored messaging (e.g., Onam offers in Kerala vs. Pongal offers in Tamil Nadu).

**3. Psychographic Profiling (P)**
- Lifestyle, values, personality, attitudes, social class, interests (VALS framework).
- **Why it matters:** Two customers with identical demographics can have radically different purchase triggers — psychographics explain *why* they buy.

**4. Behavioural Profiling (B)**
- Purchase history, brand loyalty, benefit sought, usage rate, readiness stage, occasion-based buying.
- **Why it matters:** Behavioural data is the *most actionable* — it captures actual decisions, not just stated intent.

### 2.2 The Customer Profiling Workflow (Six-Stage Pipeline)

| Stage | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Data Acquisition** | Collect from CRM, ERP, web, social | Raw dataset |
| **2. Data Cleansing** | Handle nulls, duplicates, outliers | Refined dataset |
| **3. Variable Selection** | Choose DGPB variables | Feature matrix |
| **4. Profiling Technique** | RFM, clustering, regression | Customer archetype |
| **5. Validation** | Test profile against holdout sample | Confidence score |
| **6. Activation** | Push to marketing / sales systems | Personalised campaign |

### 2.3 Core Profiling Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Focus:** Expect a 14-mark question contrasting at least two of the following techniques.

**(a) RFM Analysis (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)**
- Each customer receives a score (typically 1–5) on three dimensions.
- High R + High F + High M = **Champions**.
- Low R + Low F + Low M = **Hibernating / Lost**.

**(b) K-Means Clustering**
- Unsupervised learning algorithm that partitions customers into *k* groups based on Euclidean distance similarity.
- Optimal *k* determined via the **Elbow Method** or **Silhouette Score**.

**(c) Decision Tree Classification**
- Supervised learning; predicts profile category from labelled training data.
- Highly interpretable — output is a flowchart of decision rules.

**(d) Logistic Regression Profiling**
- Estimates the probability that a customer belongs to a particular segment (e.g., "churner vs. retainer").

**(e) Persona Synthesis**
- Qualitative approach: combines analytics with ethnographic interviews to create 3–5 fictional but data-grounded personas.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all examinable formulas**. Memorise these — they appear in both Part A (3-mark) derivations and Part B (14-mark) problems.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Lifetime Value (Basic)** | $\text{CLV} = \text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG} \times \text{AC}$ | Estimate total revenue from a customer |
| 2 | **Average Purchase Value** | $\text{APV} = \dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Number of Orders}}$ | Normalise spend per transaction |
| 3 | **Purchase Frequency (Annual)** | $\text{PF} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Orders}}{\text{Number of Unique Customers} \times \text{Years}}$ | Measure repeat behaviour |
| 4 | **Customer Gross Margin** | $\text{CG} = \dfrac{\text{Total Gross Margin}}{\text{Total Revenue}}$ | Profitability per customer |
| 5 | **Average Customer Lifespan** | $\text{AC} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Churn Rate}}$ | Expected duration of relationship |
| 6 | **Churn Rate** | $C_{r} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Lost}}{\text{Customers at Start}} \times 100$ | Attrition measurement |
| 7 | **RFM Composite Score** | $\text{RFM Score} = w_{R} \cdot R + w_{F} \cdot F + w_{M} \cdot M$ | Weighted segmentation |
| 8 | **Silhouette Score** | $s(i) = \dfrac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$ | Validate cluster quality |
| 9 | **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares** | $\text{WCSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \vert x - \mu_{i} \vert^{2}$ | Elbow method objective |
| 10 | **Z-Score Normalisation** | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Feature scaling pre-clustering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Reminder:** APV = Average Purchase Value, PF = Purchase Frequency, CG = Customer Gross Margin (as a decimal), AC = Average Customer Lifespan (in years), $w_{R}$, $w_{F}$, $w_{M}$ are weights such that $w_{R} + w_{F} + w_{M} = 1$, $a(i)$ = mean distance to points in same cluster, $b(i)$ = mean distance to points in next nearest cluster.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Retail (Amazon, Flipkart):** RFM-based personalisation drives the "Recommended for You" carousel.
- **Telecom (Jio, Airtel):** Behavioural profiling flags high-churn-risk prepaid users 30 days before disconnection.
- **Banking (HDFC, SBI):** Demographic + transactional profiling supports credit-limit decisions and fraud detection.
- **Streaming (Netflix, Hotstar):** Psychographic + behavioural profiling drives content recommendations and original production decisions.
- **Healthcare:** Patient profiling enables predictive admission models and personalised wellness programmes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** Derive the CLV formula starting from revenue-per-customer-per-year logic, then generalise to multi-year horizons. This is a **frequently repeated 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE papers.

**Step 1 — Define Annual Revenue Contribution per Customer.**

The revenue generated by a single customer in one year is the product of how much they spend per transaction and how often they transact:

$$R_{\text{annual}} = \text{APV} \times \text{PF}$$

**Step 2 — Adjust for Profit Margin.**

Not all revenue is profit. Multiply by the gross margin ratio (CG, expressed as a decimal, e.g., 0.25 for 25%):

$$P_{\text{annual}} = \text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the Customer's Lifespan.**

The total expected profit over the relationship lifetime is the annual profit multiplied by the average number of years the customer stays (AC):

$$\text{CLV} = \text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG} \times \text{AC}$$

**Step 4 — Express AC in Terms of Churn Rate.**

If a constant fraction $C_{r}$ (decimal) of customers churn each year, the expected lifespan follows a geometric distribution:

$$\text{AC} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} t \cdot (1 - C_{r})^{t-1} \cdot C_{r} = \dfrac{1}{C_{r}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Back.**

$$\text{CLV} = \dfrac{\text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG}}{C_{r}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A *lower* churn rate causes a *non-linear* explosion in CLV. Halving churn from 20% to 10% **doubles** the customer lifespan and therefore doubles the lifetime value — assuming all other factors remain constant. This is the strategic rationale behind every loyalty programme.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: RFM Score Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A retail dataset of 1,000 customers has been scored on R, F, M each on a 1–5 scale (5 = best). Construct the composite RFM score using equal weights and classify into tiers.

**Step 1 — Assign Component Scores.**

Each customer $i$ receives $(R_{i}, F_{i}, M_{i})$ where $R_{i} \in [1,5]$, $F_{i} \in [1,5]$, $M_{i} \in [1,5]$.

**Step 2 — Apply Equal Weights.**

For equal-weight RFM, set $w_{R} = w_{F} = w_{M} = \dfrac{1}{3}$:

$$\text{RFM}_{i} = \dfrac{1}{3} R_{i} + \dfrac{1}{3} F_{i} + \dfrac{1}{3} M_{i}$$

**Step 3 — Normalise the Output Range.**

Minimum value: $\dfrac{1+1+1}{3} = 1.000$.
Maximum value: $\dfrac{5+5+5}{3} = 5.000$.
Range: $[1.000,\, 5.000]$.

**Step 4 — Define Tier Thresholds.**

| Tier | RFM Score Range | Strategic Label |
|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 | $[4.5,\, 5.0]$ | **Champions** — reward & retain |
| Tier 2 | $[4.0,\, 4.5)$ | **Loyal Customers** — upsell |
| Tier 3 | $[3.0,\, 4.0)$ | **Potential Loyalists** — engage |
| Tier 4 | $[2.0,\, 3.0)$ | **At-Risk** — win back |
| Tier 5 | $[1.0,\, 2.0)$ | **Hibernating** — reactivation |

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.**

Customer A: $R = 5, F = 4, M = 5$.

$$\text{RFM}_{A} = \dfrac{5 + 4 + 5}{3} = \dfrac{14}{3} = 4.667$$

Tier 1 → **Champion**.

Customer B: $R = 1, F = 2, M = 1$.

$$\text{RFM}_{B} = \dfrac{1 + 2 + 1}{3} = \dfrac{4}{3} = 1.333$$

Tier 5 → **Hibernating**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Profiling Technique

| Scenario | Best Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 10,000+ customers, no labels | **K-Means Clustering** | Unsupervised; reveals natural groups |
| 1,000 labelled churn/not-churn records | **Logistic Regression** | Supervised; interpretable coefficients |
| Need explainable rules for non-technical managers | **Decision Tree** | Produces human-readable IF-THEN rules |
| Quick segmentation with limited variables | **RFM Analysis** | Simple, fast, widely accepted in marketing |
| Qualitative insights + small sample | **Persona Synthesis** | Ethnographic depth; rich narrative output |
| Mixed numeric + categorical features | **Hierarchical Clustering** | Handles heterogeneous data; dendrogram visualisation |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: End-to-End CLV Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Style Problem (KTU Module 3 typical 7-mark):**
> An online fashion retailer observes the following over the past year:
> - Total Revenue = ₹5,00,00,000
> - Number of Orders = 2,00,000
> - Number of Unique Customers = 50,000
> - Total Gross Margin = ₹1,50,00,000
> - Annual Churn Rate = 25%

**Step 1 — Compute Average Purchase Value (APV).**

$$\text{APV} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = ₹2{,}500$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Purchase Frequency (PF).**

$$\text{PF} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000 \times 1} = 4 \text{ orders / customer / year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Customer Gross Margin (CG).**

$$\text{CG} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 0.30$$

**Step 4 — Compute Average Customer Lifespan (AC).**

$$\text{AC} = \dfrac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5 — Compute CLV.**

$$\text{CLV} = 2{,}500 \times 4 \times 0.30 \times 4 = ₹12{,}000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Managerial Interpretation:** Each customer is worth ₹12,000 over their lifetime. The maximum allowable Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) is approximately **30% of CLV** (i.e., ₹3,600). Spending more on acquisition than this destroys value.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 3: Optimal Number of Clusters via the Elbow Method

**Step 1 — Define the WCSS objective.**

$$\text{WCSS}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \vert x - \mu_{i} \vert^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Plot WCSS against k.**

As $k$ increases, WCSS strictly decreases (each point eventually becomes its own cluster with WCSS = 0).

**Step 3 — Identify the Elbow.**

The optimal $k$ is at the point where the marginal reduction in WCSS sharply diminishes — the visual "bend" in the curve. Beyond this point, adding clusters yields diminishing returns and overfitting risk.

**Step 4 — Validate with Silhouette Score.**

$$s(i) = \dfrac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$$

The global silhouette score is the mean of $s(i)$ across all points. Values close to **+1** indicate well-clustered data; values near **0** indicate overlapping clusters; negative values suggest misclassification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Customer Profiling End-to-End Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Data Sources: CRM, POS, Web Logs, Social Media] --> B1[Stage 1: Data Acquisition]
    B1 --> B2[Stage 2: Data Cleansing and Validation]
    B2 --> C1[Stage 3: Feature Engineering]
    C1 --> D1[Demographic Variables]
    C1 --> D2[Geographic Variables]
    C1 --> D3[Psychographic Variables]
    C1 --> D4[Behavioural Variables]
    D1 --> E1[Stage 4: Profiling Engine]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[RFM Scoring Module]
    E1 --> F2[K-Means Clustering Module]
    E1 --> F3[Decision Tree Classifier]
    E1 --> F4[Persona Synthesis Module]
    F1 --> G1[Stage 5: Profile Validation]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{Silhouette Score and Holdout Accuracy Above Threshold?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Stage 6: Profile Activation]
    H1 -- No --> B2
    I1 --> J1[Targeted Marketing Campaigns]
    I1 --> J2[Churn Prevention System]
    I1 --> J3[Personalised Recommendations]
    I1 --> J4[Dynamic Pricing Engine]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The pipeline begins with multi-source data ingestion, passes through cleansing and the DGPB feature split, enters the multi-technique profiling engine, is validated against a quality threshold, and finally activates business actions. The feedback loop (validation failure → re-cleanse) is critical and is often missed in student answers.

---

### 4.2 RFM Segmentation Matrix (Strategic Mapping)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title RFM Segmentation Quadrant
    x-axis "Low Frequency" --> "High Frequency"
    y-axis "Low Monetary" --> "High Monetary"
    quadrant-1 "Champions"
    quadrant-2 "Big Spenders, New Arrivals"
    quadrant-3 "Hibernating Low Value"
    quadrant-4 "Loyal but Light Spenders"
    CustomerA[0.9, 0.85]: [0.9, 0.85]
    CustomerB[0.7, 0.4]: [0.7, 0.4]
    CustomerC[0.2, 0.15]: [0.2, 0.15]
    CustomerD[0.35, 0.75]: [0.35, 0.75]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Quadrant:** Plot Frequency (x-axis) against Monetary Value (y-axis). Top-right customers are **Champions** requiring retention focus. Top-left are **Big Spenders with Low Frequency** — reactivation candidates. Bottom-right are **Frequent but Low Spenders** — upsell opportunities. Bottom-left are **Hibernating Low Value** — minimum marketing investment.

---

### 4.3 DGPB Profiling Framework (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Dem[Demographic Dimension]
        D1[Age]
        D2[Gender]
        D3[Income]
        D4[Education]
    end
    subgraph Geo[Geographic Dimension]
        G1[Region]
        G2[Climate Zone]
        G3[Urban versus Rural]
    end
    subgraph Psy[Psychographic Dimension]
        P1[Lifestyle]
        P2[Values]
        P3[Personality Traits]
    end
    subgraph Beh[Behavioural Dimension]
        B1[Purchase History]
        B2[Loyalty Status]
        B3[Usage Rate]
    end
    Dem --> X[Integrated Customer Profile]
    Geo --> X
    Psy --> X
    Beh --> X
    X --> Y[Marketing Action]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> Define customer profiling. List and briefly explain any four dimensions used for customer profiling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Customer profiling is the process of constructing a detailed, multi-dimensional description of customers using demographic, geographic, psychographic, and behavioural data to support targeted business decisions.

**Dimensions (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Demographic** — age, gender, income, education, occupation; describes *who* the customer is.
2. **Geographic** — region, climate, urban/rural setting; describes *where* the customer is located.
3. **Psychographic** — lifestyle, values, attitudes, personality; describes *why* the customer buys.
4. **Behavioural** — purchase history, loyalty, usage rate, benefit sought; describes *how* the customer acts.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
> What is RFM analysis? Explain the meaning of R, F, and M with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

RFM analysis is a quantitative customer profiling technique that scores each customer on three behavioural dimensions to enable segmentation (1 mark).

- **R (Recency):** How recently did the customer make a purchase? *Example: A customer who bought 5 days ago scores higher on R than one who bought 60 days ago.* (0.67 mark)
- **F (Frequency):** How often does the customer purchase within a defined period? *Example: A customer with 12 annual orders scores higher on F than one with 2 orders.* (0.67 mark)
- **M (Monetary):** How much does the customer spend per transaction or in total? *Example: A customer spending ₹10,000 per order scores higher on M than one spending ₹500.* (0.67 mark)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the six-stage customer profiling workflow with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Discuss any two customer profiling techniques with suitable business examples. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (14 marks):**

**(a) Six-Stage Workflow (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (1 mark):** Collect data from CRM systems, point-of-sale terminals, website analytics, social media APIs, and customer surveys. Ensure multi-source coverage along DGPB dimensions.

**Stage 2 — Data Cleansing (1 mark):** Remove duplicates, handle nulls (imputation or exclusion), detect outliers (IQR or Z-score method), and standardise formats (dates, currencies).

**Stage 3 — Variable Selection (1 mark):** Choose the most relevant DGPB variables. Apply feature engineering — derive new variables like "days since last purchase" or "average basket size."

**Stage 4 — Profiling Technique (2 marks):** Apply one or more techniques — RFM scoring, K-Means clustering, decision trees, or persona synthesis. The output is a set of customer archetypes or segments.

**Stage 5 — Validation (1 mark):** Test the profile against a holdout sample. Use Silhouette Score (for clustering) or accuracy metrics (for classification). Reject profiles failing quality thresholds.

**Stage 6 — Activation (1 mark):** Deploy profiles into business systems — CRM, marketing automation, recommendation engines. Monitor campaign performance and refresh profiles periodically.

---

**(b) Two Techniques with Examples (7 marks):**

**Technique 1 — RFM Analysis (3.5 marks):**
RFM analysis segments customers by Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value, each scored on a 1–5 scale. The composite RFM score (equal or weighted) classifies customers into tiers such as Champions, Loyal, At-Risk, and Hibernating.
*Business Example:* An e-commerce platform like Flipkart uses RFM to identify Champions (R=5, F=5, M=5) and offers them early access to festive sales. At-Risk customers (R=2, F=3, M=3) receive personalised win-back coupons.

**Technique 2 — K-Means Clustering (3.5 marks):**
K-Means partitions customers into *k* clusters by minimising within-cluster sum of squared distances. The optimal *k* is chosen via the Elbow Method or Silhouette Score.
*Business Example:* A telecom operator clusters 10 million prepaid users into 5 segments: Heavy Data Users, Voice-Only, Budget Users, Travellers, and Dormant. Each segment receives a tailored recharge plan, improving average revenue per user (ARPU) by 12–18%.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main — Alternative Module Choice]**
> **(a)** Derive the Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) formula from first principles and explain each component with a numerical example. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Explain the role of churn rate in customer profiling. How does a 50% reduction in churn affect CLV? (7 marks)

**Model Answer (14 marks):**

**(a) CLV Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Annual revenue per customer = $\text{APV} \times \text{PF}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Annual profit = $\text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Lifetime profit = $\text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG} \times \text{AC}$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Derive $\text{AC} = 1 / C_{r}$ using the geometric series of churn probabilities:

$$\text{AC} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} t \cdot (1 - C_{r})^{t-1} \cdot C_{r} = \dfrac{1}{C_{r}}$$

**Step 5 (0.5 mark):** Final CLV formula:

$$\text{CLV} = \dfrac{\text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG}}{C_{r}}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks — Numerical Example):**
Given APV = ₹2,500, PF = 4, CG = 0.30, Churn = 25%:

$$\text{CLV} = \dfrac{2{,}500 \times 4 \times 0.30}{0.25} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{0.25} = ₹12{,}000$$

---

**(b) Churn and CLV Impact (7 marks):**

**Definition of Churn (1.5 marks):** Churn rate ($C_{r}$) is the percentage of customers who stop transacting with the business during a given period. It is the inverse of customer retention and directly determines average customer lifespan.

**Mathematical Relationship (2.5 marks):** Since $\text{CLV} = \dfrac{\text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG}}{C_{r}}$, CLV is **inversely proportional** to churn rate. A 50% reduction in churn means $C_{r,\text{new}} = 0.5 \times C_{r,\text{old}}$.

**Numerical Impact (3 marks):**
If $C_{r,\text{old}} = 0.20$ and $C_{r,\text{new}} = 0.10$, and APV = ₹2,000, PF = 5, CG = 0.25:

$$\text{CLV}_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{2{,}000 \times 5 \times 0.25}{0.20} = ₹12{,}500$$

$$\text{CLV}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{2{,}000 \times 5 \times 0.25}{0.10} = ₹25{,}000$$

**CLV Doubles (₹12,500 → ₹25,000)** — a 100% increase from a 50% churn reduction. This non-linear effect is why retention is the single most leveraged investment in customer analytics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing profiling with segmentation.** Profiling describes the *characteristics* of customers; segmentation is the act of *grouping* them. Many students write the same definition for both — this loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Omitting units in CLV problems.** Always state ₹, $ or years. A derivation without units is treated as incomplete.
> 3. **Forgetting the churn-to-lifespan conversion.** A surprising number of students write $\text{CLV} = \text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG} \times \text{AC}$ and stop there, missing the crucial step of expressing AC as $1/C_{r}$.
> 4. **Writing demographic = profiling.** The DGPB framework requires *all four* dimensions; partial answers are penalised.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark workflow question.** KTU mandates a block diagram for full marks on process questions.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Customer Profiling (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Construction of multi-dimensional customer archetypes using data.
- **DGPB Framework:** Demographic, Geographic, Psychographic, Behavioural — all four are mandatory.
- **Six-Stage Workflow:** Acquisition → Cleansing → Variable Selection → Profiling → Validation → Activation.
- **RFM Analysis:** Recency, Frequency, Monetary — scored 1–5, composite = $\frac{R+F+M}{3}$ (equal weights).
- **CLV Formula:** $\text{CLV} = \dfrac{\text{APV} \times \text{PF} \times \text{CG}}{C_{r}}$.
- **Churn–Lifespan Link:** $\text{AC} = \dfrac{1}{C_{r}}$ (geometric series derivation).
- **K-Means Objective:** Minimise WCSS; choose *k* via Elbow Method; validate with Silhouette Score $\in [-1, +1]$.
- **CAC Rule:** Maximum allowable Customer Acquisition Cost ≈ 30% of CLV.
- **Profiling vs. Segmentation:** Profiling = *what is the customer like*; Segmentation = *grouping similar customers*.
- **High-Yield Techniques:** RFM, K-Means, Decision Tree, Logistic Regression, Persona Synthesis.
- **Real-World Applications:** Amazon recommendations, Jio churn modelling, HDFC credit decisions, Netflix content strategy.
- **Always include:** Units (₹, days, %), a diagram for workflow questions, and a numerical example for derivation questions.
- **Exam Pattern:** Expect 1–2 questions per paper on this topic — usually one 3-mark definition/dimensions question and one 14-mark derivation/case study.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Statistical Programming # Statistical Programming — Core Foundation & Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic sits at the intersection of **computational statistics** and **data-driven decision making**. It equips the B.Tech student with the practical skill of translating statistical theory into executable, reproducible code — a graduate attribute directly aligned with **CO3: Apply statistical and computational tools for business analytics.** --- ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Statistical Programming** is the disciplined application of high-level programming languages and environments (primarily **R**, **Python**, and **SAS**) to perform the entire pipeline of statistical analysis — from data ingestion and cleaning, through descriptive/inferential statistics, to the construction of predictive models and visual reporting. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *Business Analytics*, the syllabus treats **R (and its IDE RStudio)** as the canonical reference environment because R was designed *by statisticians, for statisticians*, and contains built-in primitives for virtually every distribution, test, and model a B.Tech analytics student will encounter. $$ \text{Statistical Programming} \equiv \big\{ \text{Syntax} + \text{Data Structures} + \text{Statistical Functions} + \text{Reproducibility} \big\} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction the Examiner Tests:** > *Statistical Computing* = the *mathematics* behind the algorithm (e.g., Newton-Raphson for MLE). > *Statistical Programming* = the *engineering* of putting that algorithm into code, handling data I/O, edge cases, and producing readable output. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Statistical Kitchen" Imagine you are a **chef in a research kitchen**: | Kitchen Element | Statistical Equivalent | |---|---| | The **kitchen itself** (stove, oven, counters) | The R/Python interpreter & IDE | | **Ingredients** (raw vegetables, spices) | Raw data (CSV, Excel, JSON, DB) | | **Knives, mixers, pans** | Data structures (vectors, data frames) | | **Recipes** (precise instructions) | Statistical functions (`t.test`, `lm`) | | **The plated dish** | Insight, chart, model output | | **Recipe book (so others can cook it)** | Reproducible R-Markdown / Jupyter script | Just as a chef cannot serve a dish without knowing how to **clean**, **chop**, **measure**, and **cook** ingredients, a Business Analyst cannot extract insight without knowing how to **import**, **wrangle**, **compute**, and **visualize** data programmatically. --- ## 1.3 Why Statistical Programming? The Three Pillars 1. **Reproducibility** — A script documents *exactly* how a number was produced. Hand-typed Excel formulas do not. 2. **Scalability** — A loop or `apply()` family handles millions of rows; a spreadsheet crashes. 3. **Reproducibility + Auditability** — In regulated industries (BFSI, Pharma), the *code* itself is the audit trail. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Favourite Opening Line:** *"Statistical programming enables the analyst to move from one-time manual calculation to automated, reproducible, and scalable analytical workflows."* --- ## 1.4 The R Ecosystem at a Glance | Component | Role | Example | |---|---|---| | **Base R** | Core interpreter | `mean(x)`, `sd(x)` | | **RStudio IDE** | Development environment | 4-pane layout: Script, Console, Environment, Plots | | **CRAN Packages** | Add-on libraries | `dplyr`, `ggplot2`, `caret` | | **R Markdown** | Literate programming | `.Rmd` files weaving code + prose | | **Shiny** | Interactive web apps | Business dashboards | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** > **`apply()` family** is a *vectorized* alternative to loops — *faster*, *more idiomatic*, and *preferred* in board answers. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Stage Statistical Programming Workflow The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the student to recognize and *execute* the following canonical pipeline. Every Part-B question maps to one or more of these stages. ``` Stage 1 → Stage 2 → Stage 3 → Stage 4 → Stage 5 INGEST → WRANGLE → COMPUTE → MODEL → COMMUNICATE ``` ### Stage 1 — Data Ingestion (I/O) Reading structured data into the working environment. - **CSV / Text:** `read.csv("file.csv", header=TRUE, sep=",")` - **Excel:** `readxl::read_excel("file.xlsx", sheet=1)` - **Web / API:** `jsonlite::fromJSON()`, `httr::GET()` - **Database:** `DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT ...")` ### Stage 2 — Data Wrangling (Cleaning & Transformation) Using base R and **`dplyr`** verbs: - `filter()` — row subsetting - `select()` — column selection - `mutate()` — create new variables - `arrange()` — sorting - `group_by() %>% summarise()` — split-apply-combine - `merge()` or `*join*` family — combining datasets ### Stage 3 — Descriptive & Inferential Computation - Central tendency: `mean`, `median`, `Mode` (custom) - Dispersion: `sd`, `var`, `IQR`, `range` - Distribution: `dnorm`, `pnorm`, `qnorm`, `rnorm` - Tests: `t.test`, `chisq.test`, `prop.test` ### Stage 4 — Modelling - Linear Model: `model <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2, data=df)` - Logistic: `glm(y ~ x, family=binomial)` - ANOVA: `aov(yield ~ fertilizer, data=farm)` - Extract coefficients: `summary(model)$coefficients` ### Stage 5 — Communication - `plot()`, `hist()`, `boxplot()` - `ggplot2::ggplot()` for grammar-of-graphics - `ggtitle()`, `xlab()`, `ylab()`, `theme_minimal()` --- ## 2.2 R Data Structures — The "KTU Big Four" The examiner **loves** asking: *"Differentiate between vector, matrix, list, and data frame."* Memorize this table. | Structure | Dimensions | Homogeneous? | Indexing Example | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Vector** | 1-D | Yes (single type) | `x[3]` | Numeric series, character list | | **Matrix** | 2-D | Yes | `m[2,3]` | Numeric tabular data, linear algebra | | **List** | n-D | **No** (heterogeneous) | `lst[["name"]]` | Model outputs, mixed-type containers | | **Data Frame** | 2-D | Column-wise heterogeneous | `df$col` or `df[1,]` | The **workhorse** for analytics | > [!NOTE] > **Why `[[ ]]` vs `[ ]` for lists?** > `[ ]` returns a *sub-list*; `[[ ]]` extracts the *actual element*. In an exam, drawing this distinction earns full marks. --- ## 2.3 The `apply()` Family — Functional Programming in R | Function | Input | Output | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | `apply(X, MARGIN, FUN)` | Matrix / Array | Vector / Matrix | Row/column summaries | | `lapply(X, FUN)` | List / Vector | **List** | Apply, return list | | `sapply(X, FUN)` | List / Vector | **Simplified** (vector/matrix) | User-friendly `lapply` | | `mapply(FUN, ...)` | Multiple lists | Vector / List | Multiple-argument iteration | | `tapply(X, INDEX, FUN)` | Vector + Factor | Array | Group-wise stats | **Why it matters:** Inferential statistics in R is built on these primitives — e.g., `tapply(sales, region, mean)` gives regional means without an explicit loop. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Statistical Tests Implemented in Code) | Test | R Function | Statistic Formula | Decision Rule | |---|---|---|---| | **One-sample t** | `t.test(x, mu=μ₀)` | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}$ | Reject $H_0$ if $\vert t \vert > t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}$ | | **Two-sample t** | `t.test(x, y)` | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}}$ | df $= n_1+n_2-2$ | | **Paired t** | `t.test(x, y, paired=T)` | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d/\sqrt{n}}$ | df $= n-1$ | | **Chi-square (independence)** | `chisq.test(table)` | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | df $=(r-1)(c-1)$ | | **One-way ANOVA** | `aov(y ~ group)` | $F = \dfrac{MS_{\text{between}}}{MS_{\text{within}}}$ | df $=(k-1,\, N-k)$ | | **Simple Linear Reg.** | `lm(y ~ x)` | $\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | $t = \hat{\beta}_1 / SE(\hat{\beta}_1)$ | | **Correlation** | `cor(x, y)` | $r = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(x,y)}{s_x \, s_y}$ | Test via `cor.test()` | | **Z-test (proportion)** | `prop.test()` | $z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}$ | Asymptotic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pipeline Logic the Examiner Awards Marks For:** > After running any test, the *next* line should **always** be: > 1. `result <- t.test(...)` > 2. `result$p.value` — for decision > 3. `result$conf.int` — for confidence interval > Showing all three earns full marks on a 7-mark sub-question. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility -
