<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Convention:** Part A questions test direct recall, definitions, and short explanations. Each carries **3 marks**. Model answers should be 3–4 sentences plus a diagram if applicable.

### Question 1: Define Data Abstraction. List and briefly explain the three levels of data abstraction.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Data Abstraction is the process of hiding the complex, low-level implementation details of data storage and exposing only the essential, relevant features to the user. It is a fundamental property of DBMS that allows different users to view data at different levels of detail.

**Three Levels (2 marks):**

1. **Physical (Internal) Level:** The lowest level describing *how* data is physically stored on disk — file structures, indexes, access paths, storage blocks.

2. **Logical (Conceptual) Level:** The middle level describing *what* data is stored — entities, attributes, relationships, and constraints — independent of physical storage.

3. **View (External) Level:** The highest level describing only the *portion* of data relevant to a specific user or application. Multiple external views can exist for one conceptual schema.

> [!TIP]
> **How to score full marks:** Always include a one-line definition first, then enumerate ALL three levels with a *what* and *who uses it* statement for each.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Schema and Instance. Give a suitable example.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Schema | Instance |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | The structure / design of the database (intension) | The actual data stored at a given moment (extension) |
| **Nature** | Static — changes rarely | Dynamic — changes frequently |
| **Analogy** | Blueprint of a building | Building itself at a point in time |
| **Operations** | Affected by DDL (CREATE, ALTER, DROP) | Affected by DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) |
| **Stability** | Stable over long periods | Changes with every data modification |

**Example (1 mark):**

- **Schema:** `Student(roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50), marks FLOAT)` — the structure definition.
- **Instance:** The set of tuples currently in the table — e.g., `{(1, 'Anu', 89.5), (2, 'Rahul', 76.0)}` at 10:00 AM today.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The most common error is confusing "schema" with "instance". Remember: **Schema = Structure, Instance = Snapshot**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Convention:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each has sub-parts (typically a = 7 marks, b = 7 marks). Provide diagrams wherever possible.

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the three-level architecture of a database system in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of mappings between levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define data independence. Differentiate between logical and physical data independence with examples. Explain why physical data independence is easier to achieve than logical data independence. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Three-Level Architecture (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The three-level architecture (also called the ANSI/SPARC architecture) is a framework for separating the user view of a database from the physical storage of data. It was proposed by the ANSI/SPARC study group in 1975 to provide a clear separation of concerns.

**Three Levels (4 marks):**

1. **External Level (Highest)** — The user view. Each user has access to a specific portion of the data, customized to their needs. For example, the Sales department might see only `Customer Name` and `Order Amount`, while hiding sensitive fields like `Credit Card Number`.

2. **Conceptual (Logical) Level (Middle)** — The community logical view. It represents the entire database in terms of entities, attributes, relationships, and constraints, independent of any physical consideration. It is defined using a conceptual data model such as the ER model or relational schema.

3. **Internal (Physical) Level (Lowest)** — The physical storage view. It describes how data is stored on disk — file organization (heap, hashed, B+ tree), indexing strategies, data compression, and block allocation.

**Mappings (2 marks):**
- **External/Conceptual Mapping:** Translates requests from the external view to the conceptual schema. If the conceptual schema changes, this mapping is updated without affecting external views.
- **Conceptual/Internal Mapping:** Translates conceptual-level operations into physical-level operations on storage. Allows the physical schema to change without affecting the conceptual schema.

**Diagram (must include):** The Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes is the perfect reference.

**Part (b) — Data Independence (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Data Independence is the capacity to change the schema at one level of the database system without having to change the schema at the next higher level.

**Logical Data Independence (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** The ability to modify the conceptual schema without affecting external schemas or application programs.
- **Example:** Adding a new attribute `email_id` to the `Customer` table — applications that do not reference this column continue to work without modification.
- **Difficulty:** Hard to achieve because logical changes may affect application queries.

**Physical Data Independence (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** The ability to modify the physical schema without affecting the conceptual schema.
- **Example:** Reorganizing a table from a heap file to a B+ tree index, or moving a database from one disk to another, or splitting a file across multiple storage devices.
- **Difficulty:** Easier to achieve.

**Why Physical is Easier (1 mark):** Physical data independence is easier because the physical layer is well-encapsulated by the storage manager. The conceptual schema abstracts away physical details, so changes in storage structures, indexes, or file organization do not propagate upward. Logical changes, however, are semantically meaningful and may affect application logic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write "logical data independence = changing logical schema" without mentioning that external schemas remain unchanged. The key point is **non-propagation** of changes.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to provide a **concrete example** for EACH type of independence. The examiner awards 1.5 marks for the example alone.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Skipping the diagram in part (a). A 7-mark question without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing schema and instance in the context of independence. Independence applies to **schemas** (structure), not instances (data).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** What is a database system? Explain the various components of a database system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the database administrator (DBA). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a schema. Explain the different types of schemas in a DBMS. Discuss the data dictionary / metadata repository. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Database System & Components (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A database system is a combination of the database (the actual stored data) and the software (DBMS) that manages, retrieves, and manipulates that data, along with the hardware, users, and procedures that support it.

**Components (4 marks):**

1. **Hardware** — Physical devices (servers, disks, memory, network) on which the database resides.
2. **Software** — The DBMS software that acts as an intermediary between users and the database (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB).
3. **Data** — The raw facts stored in the database — both operational and metadata.
4. **Procedures** — The set of rules, policies, and instructions governing the design, use, and security of the database.
5. **Database Access Language** — SQL or similar language used to interact with the database.
6. **People (Users)** — End users, application programmers, DBAs, system analysts.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** A hierarchical diagram showing:
- Users (Top) → Application Programs / Query Tools → DBMS Software → Database (Bottom)
- Side branches: DBA, System Analyst, Naive Users, Sophisticated Users

**Role of DBA (1 mark):**
- Schema definition and modification
- Storage structure and access method definition
- Granting user authorization and security
- Backup, recovery, and performance tuning
- Monitoring and maintaining the database

**Part (b) — Schemas & Data Dictionary (7 marks):**

**Schema Definition (1 mark):** A schema is the structural description of the database — the logical blueprint that defines tables, fields, relationships, views, indexes, and constraints at a particular abstraction level.

**Types of Schemas (4 marks):**

1. **Physical Schema (Internal Schema):** Describes the physical storage structure of the database. Defined in the data definition language and manipulated by the storage manager.

2. **Conceptual Schema (Logical Schema):** Describes the logical structure of the entire database — entities, attributes, relationships, and integrity constraints. Independent of physical considerations.

3. **External Schema (Subschema / User View):** Describes the portion of the database relevant to a specific user or application. Multiple external schemas can coexist, each providing a tailored view.

**Data Dictionary / Metadata Repository (2 marks):**
- A **data dictionary** (or system catalog) is a centralized repository of metadata — data *about* the data.
- It stores information about: schemas, table definitions, column types, constraints, indexes, user privileges, and statistics.
- The DBMS consults the data dictionary before executing any query or DDL operation.
- Itself acts as a database, often queried by the DBA and system tools.

**Example:** In MySQL, `INFORMATION_SCHEMA` is the built-in data dictionary that contains metadata about all databases, tables, columns, and privileges.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "schema = table" — this is **wrong**. A schema is the *design* of the entire database, not a single table.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the difference between a **schema** (structure/intension) and an **instance** (data/extension). Examiners frequently test this.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not distinguishing between a **data dictionary** and a **database**. The data dictionary *contains* metadata; the database contains actual data.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Missing a **block diagram** in part (a). 7-mark questions demand visualization.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [x] **Data Abstraction** = Hiding complexity, exposing essentials. Foundation of DBMS architecture.
- [x] **Three Levels:** Physical (Internal) → Logical (Conceptual) → View (External). Top is simplest, bottom is most detailed.
- [x] **ANSI/SPARC Architecture** is the standard 3-level model — know its name for KTU exams.
- [x] **External Schema** = User-specific view. Multiple views per database.
- [x] **Conceptual Schema** = Community view of the entire database. One per database.
- [x] **Internal Schema** = Physical storage details. One per database.
- [x] **Schema** = Structure/Design (Intension) → Stable, changes via DDL.
- [x] **Instance** = Actual Data (Extension) → Changes frequently via DML.
- [x] **Logical Data Independence** = Conceptual schema can change without affecting external schemas/applications. **Hard to achieve.**
- [x] **Physical Data Independence** = Physical schema can change without affecting the conceptual schema. **Easier to achieve.**
- [x] **DDL** (Data Definition Language) — operates on **schemas**. Examples: `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`.
- [x] **DML** (Data Manipulation Language) — operates on **instances**. Examples: `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`.
- [x] **DCL** (Data Control Language) — handles authorization. Examples: `GRANT`, `REVOKE`.
- [x] **TCL** (Transaction Control Language) — manages transactions. Examples: `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`.
- [x] **DBA** (Database Administrator) = has access to ALL three levels; responsible for security, performance, backup, schema.
- [x] **Naive Users** = interact only with the **External Level** through pre-built applications.
- [x] **Sophisticated Users** = interact with the **Logical Level** using ad-hoc SQL queries.
- [x] **Application Programmers** = interact with **Logical + External** levels.
- [x] **Data Dictionary** = Centralized metadata repository. Stores info about schemas, constraints, indexes, privileges.
- [x] **Mappings:** External→Conceptual and Conceptual→Internal. They enable data independence.
- [x] **Cardinality Constraint:** $|S_{ext}| \leq |S_{con}| \leq |S_{phy}|$ — higher level views are subsets of lower levels.
- [x] **Real-world analogy:** Car dashboard (View) → Engine mechanics (Logical) → Piston dynamics (Physical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:**
> 1. Always draw the **3-level architecture diagram** for 7-mark questions on abstraction.
> 2. Always give **concrete examples** for logical and physical data independence.
> 3. Always distinguish **schema (intension)** from **instance (extension)**.
> 4. Memorize the **mapping functions** $M_{E \to C}$ and $M_{C \to I}$.
> 5. Remember: **DDL changes Schema; DML changes Instance** — this single line has appeared in KTU papers 5+ times.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sentiment Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sentiment Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)** is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, evaluations, appraisals, attitudes, and emotions of people toward entities, products, services, organizations, individuals, issues, events, topics, and their attributes expressed in written text.

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics framework, Sentiment Analysis is formally classified as a **sub-field of Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Text Analytics** that systematically transforms unstructured textual data into structured, machine-interpretable polarity and emotion scores. The discipline intersects **Data Mining**, **Computational Linguistics**, and **Machine Learning**.

### 1.1.1 The Three Pillars of Sentiment (Subjectivity Triad)

| Pillar | Formal Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Entity** | Target / Object | The product, person, event, or topic being discussed |
| **Aspect** | Feature / Attribute | The specific property of the entity under evaluation |
| **Holder** | Opinion Source | The person or agent expressing the sentiment |

> [!NOTE]
> A sentiment expression is formally represented as the quadruple: **(Holder, Entity, Aspect, Polarity)**. This is the canonical representation used in KTU university exam questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Shop Judge"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine 10,000 customers tweeting about a new coffee blend. Reading each tweet manually is impossible. **Sentiment Analysis is like hiring an ultra-fast digital judge** who skims each review, weighs the words (e.g., "amazing", "bitter", "okay"), and renders a verdict: **Positive**, **Negative**, or **Neutral** — often with a confidence percentage. The judge can also detect finer emotions like **joy, anger, or surprise**, just like a human reading tone.

## 1.3 Levels of Granularity in Sentiment Analysis

1. **Document Level** — Classifies the sentiment of an entire document/review as a single unit.
2. **Sentence Level** — Identifies polarity of individual sentences (assumes one opinion per sentence).
3. **Aspect Level (Feature-Based)** — Extracts specific aspects and their corresponding sentiments (e.g., "battery life" → positive, "screen" → negative).

## 1.4 Core Categories of Sentiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sentiment Polarity Classes (Standard KTU Taxonomy):**
> - **Positive** — Favorable opinion (e.g., "Excellent product!")
> - **Negative** — Unfavorable opinion (e.g., "Terrible service.")
> - **Neutral** — Factual or non-opinionated text (e.g., "The phone was released in 2024.")
> - **Conflict / Mixed** — Both positive and negative in the same unit (advanced category).

## 1.5 Fundamental Challenges

- **Sarcasm & Irony** — "Oh great, another update that broke everything."
- **Context Dependency** — "Unpredictable" is positive for a thriller, negative for a car.
- **Negation Handling** — "Not good" reverses polarity.
- **Domain Specificity** — A lexicon trained on movie reviews fails on medical texts.
- **Multilingual & Code-Mixed Text** — Common in Indian e-commerce reviews.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (Operational Concept Breakdown)

The sentiment classification system operates through **four sequential stages**:

1. **Text Acquisition & Preprocessing**
   - Tokenization → Stopword Removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → Lowercasing
   - **Why:** Reduces noise, normalizes vocabulary, decreases feature dimensionality.

2. **Feature Engineering / Representation**
   - **Bag of Words (BoW)**, **TF-IDF**, **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe)**, or **Transformer Embeddings (BERT)**.
   - **Why:** Machines require numerical vectors — text must be converted to a mathematical form.

3. **Sentiment Classification Engine**
   - **Lexicon-Based**, **Machine Learning**, or **Hybrid** models.
   - **Why:** The algorithm applies the learned function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ where $X$ is the feature vector and $Y$ is the sentiment label.

4. **Post-Processing & Evaluation**
   - Polarity aggregation, aspect extraction, and **metric computation** (Precision, Recall, F1).

## 2.2 The Three Major Methodological Approaches

### Approach A: Lexicon (Dictionary) Based
- Uses a pre-built dictionary of words tagged with sentiment scores.
- Score of a text = sum of scores of constituent words (often weighted).
- **Examples:** VADER, SentiWordNet, Bing Liu Lexicon.

### Approach B: Machine Learning Based
- Treats sentiment analysis as a **supervised classification** problem.
- Algorithms: Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Deep Neural Networks.
- Requires a labeled training corpus (e.g., IMDB, Amazon reviews).

### Approach C: Hybrid / Ensemble
- Combines lexicon scores as features for ML classifiers.
- Often yields state-of-the-art accuracy in KTU project evaluations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are direct, exam-ready equations expected in KTU university examinations.**

| Concept | Formula | Description / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Term Frequency** | $tf(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Frequency of term $t$ in document $d$ (normalized) |
| **Inverse Document Frequency** | $idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right)$ | $N$ = total documents, $df(t)$ = docs containing $t$ |
| **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ | Combined weight for feature extraction |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness of the classifier |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | How many predicted positives are correct |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | How many actual positives were captured |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| **Macro F1** | $F_1^{macro} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{1,i}$ | Unweighted average across $n$ classes |
| **Weighted F1** | $F_1^{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot F_{1,i}$ | Class-frequency-weighted F1 |
| **Lexicon Polarity Score** | $S_{doc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{score}(w_i)$ | Sum of sentiment lexicon scores in a document |
| **PMI (Lexicon Expansion)** | $PMI(w_1, w_2) = \log\left(\frac{P(w_1 \wedge w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)}\right)$ | Pointwise Mutual Information for SO-PMI method |
| **Confusion Matrix Total** | $N = TP + TN + FP + FN$ | Sum of all classification outcomes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Always remember to use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the vertical pipe symbol `|` when writing these formulas in your answer sheets to avoid markdown formatting errors in typed submissions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Brand Reputation Management** — Real-time Twitter/Reddit monitoring dashboards.
- **Voice of Customer (VoC) Programs** — Analyzing call center transcripts.
- **Political Campaign Analytics** — Predicting election outcomes from social media.
- **Financial Forecasting** — Sentiment-derived stock market prediction (e.g., sentiment-driven ETFs).
- **Product Recommendation Engines** — Adjusting recommendations based on review polarity.
- **Human Resources** — Analyzing employee Glassdoor reviews for retention insights.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: TF-IDF Weight from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** Given a corpus of $N = 3$ documents:
- $D_1$ = "the phone is great"
- $D_2$ = "the battery is bad"
- $D_3$ = "great camera and great battery"

Compute the TF-IDF weight of the word **"great"** in document $D_1$.

### Step 1 — Compute Term Frequency (TF) of "great" in $D_1$

The word "great" appears **1 time** in $D_1$, and $D_1$ has **4 total words**.

$$tf(\text{great}, D_1) = \frac{f_{\text{great}, D_1}}{\sum_{t' \in D_1} f_{t',D_1}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Logic:** Normalization divides raw count by document length to prevent bias toward longer documents.

### Step 2 — Compute Document Frequency (DF) of "great"

"Great" appears in $D_1$ and $D_3$ → $df(\text{great}) = 2$.

### Step 3 — Compute Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

$$idf(\text{great}) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df(\text{great})}\right) = \log\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \log(1.5)$$

$$\log(1.5) \approx 0.1761$$

**Logic:** IDF reduces the weight of common words. Since "great" appears in 2 of 3 documents, its IDF is moderate.

### Step 4 — Compute Final TF-IDF Weight

$$w_{\text{great}, D_1} = tf(\text{great}, D_1) \times idf(\text{great})$$

$$w_{\text{great}, D_1} = 0.25 \times 0.1761 \approx 0.0440$$

**Interpretation:** A low weight, because "great" is fairly common across the corpus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lexicon-Based Sentiment Scoring (VADER Style)

**Problem:** Score the sentence — *"The movie was not good, but the cinematography was absolutely amazing."*

**Lexicon Scores (Hypothetical):** good = +0.7, amazing = +1.8, not = -1.0 (negation multiplier)

### Step 1 — Tokenize and Apply Lexicon

| Word | Base Score | Negation-Adjusted Score |
|---|---|---|
| good | +0.7 | -0.7 (negated) |
| amazing | +1.8 | +1.8 |

### Step 2 — Aggregate the Scores

$$S_{sentence} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{score}(w_i) = (-0.7) + (+1.8) = +1.1$$

### Step 3 — Apply Threshold Rule (VADER Convention)

$$\text{Polarity} = \begin{cases} \text{Positive} & \text{if } S \geq 0.05 \\ \text{Neutral} & \text{if } -0.05 < S < 0.05 \\ \text{Negative} & \text{if } S \leq -0.05 \end{cases}$$

Since $S = +1.1 \geq 0.05$, the sentence is classified as **Positive**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: F1-Score from Confusion Matrix

**Confusion Matrix for a 2-Class Sentiment Classifier (Positive vs. Negative):**

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP = 80 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Negative** | FP = 10 | TN = 90 |

### Step 1 — Compute Precision

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} \approx 0.8889$$

### Step 2 — Compute Recall

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

### Step 3 — Compute F1-Score

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8889 \times 0.80}{0.8889 + 0.80}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7111}{1.6889} = 2 \cdot 0.4211 \approx 0.8421$$

### Step 4 — Compute Accuracy (for completeness)

$$Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{80+90}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU valuations, when classes are imbalanced (e.g., 90% positive reviews), **Accuracy is misleading** — F1 is the preferred metric.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: SO-PMI (Semantic Orientation via PMI) for Lexicon Expansion

The Turney & Littman (2003) method computes the semantic orientation of a phrase $p$ as:

$$SO(p) = PMI(p, \text{"excellent"}) - PMI(p, \text{"poor"})$$

Where:

$$PMI(p, w) = \log\left(\frac{\text{hits}(p \text{ NEAR } w)}{\text{hits}(p) \cdot \text{hits}(w)}\right)$$

**Example Computation for phrase "unpredictable plot":**

$$PMI(\text{unpredictable plot}, \text{excellent}) = \log\left(\frac{1200}{4000 \cdot 50000}\right) = \log(6 \times 10^{-6}) \approx -12.02$$

$$PMI(\text{unpredictable plot}, \text{poor}) = \log\left(\frac{50}{4000 \cdot 50000}\right) = \log(2.5 \times 10^{-7}) \approx -15.20$$

$$SO = -12.02 - (-15.20) = +3.18$$

Since $SO > 0$, the phrase is classified as **Positive**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Sentiment Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Text Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B1 --> B2[Stopword Removal]
    B2 --> B3[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    B3 --> C[Feature Extraction]
    C --> C1[Bag of Words]
    C --> C2[TF IDF Vectorization]
    C --> C3[Word Embeddings]
    C3 --> D[Sentiment Classification Engine]
    D --> D1[Lexicon Based Method]
    D --> D2[Machine Learning Model]
    D --> D3[Deep Learning Transformer]
    D1 --> E[Polarity Output]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Positive]
    E --> E2[Negative]
    E --> E3[Neutral]
    E --> F[Performance Evaluation]
    F --> F1[Precision]
    F --> F2[Recall]
    F --> F3[F1 Score]
    F --> F4[Accuracy]
```

## 4.2 Lexicon-Based Sentiment Scoring Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RawText[Input Review Text] --> Tokenize[Token Splitter]
    Tokenize --> Lookup{Sentiment Lexicon Lookup}
    Lookup -->|Word Found| Score1[Retrieve Polarity Score]
    Lookup -->|Word Not Found| Score2[Apply PMI Expansion]
    Score1 --> Negation[Check Negation Context]
    Score2 --> Negation
    Negation --> Aggregator[Score Aggregator]
    Aggregator --> Threshold{Threshold Rule Check}
    Threshold -->|Score Positive| OutPos[Output Positive Label]
    Threshold -->|Score Neutral| OutNeu[Output Neutral Label]
    Threshold -->|Score Negative| OutNeg[Output Negative Label]
```

## 4.3 Machine Learning Classification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Corpus[Labeled Corpus of Reviews] --> TrainSplit[Train Test Split 80 20]
    TrainSplit --> FeatEng[Feature Engineering TF IDF]
    FeatEng --> TrainModel[Train Classifier]
    TrainModel --> ModelPick{Choose Algorithm}
    ModelPick -->|Option 1| NB[Naive Bayes]
    ModelPick -->|Option 2| SVM[Support Vector Machine]
    ModelPick -->|Option 3| RF[Random Forest]
    ModelPick -->|Option 4| LR[Logistic Regression]
    NB --> Predict1[Predict Test Labels]
    SVM --> Predict1
    RF --> Predict1
    LR --> Predict1
    Predict1 --> Eval[Confusion Matrix Generation]
    Eval --> Metrics[Compute Precision Recall F1]
    Metrics --> Deploy[Deploy Best Model]
```

## 4.4 Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Review[Customer Review Document] --> Step1[Step A Aspect Extraction]
    Step1 --> AspectList[List of Aspects e.g. Battery, Camera, Price]
    AspectList --> Step2[Step B Opinion Word Extraction]
    Step2 --> OpinionList[Associated Opinion Words]
    OpinionList --> Step3[Step C Sentiment Polarity Determination]
    Step3 --> Step3a[Lexicon Score Lookup]
    Step3 --> Step3b[ML Classifier per Aspect]
    Step3a --> Step4[Step D Aggregation per Aspect]
    Step3b --> Step4
    Step4 --> Output[Structured Output e.g. Battery Positive, Camera Negative]
```

## 4.5 Evaluation Metrics Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CM[Confusion Matrix] --> TP[True Positives]
    CM --> FP[False Positives]
    CM --> FN[False Negatives]
    CM --> TN[True Negatives]
    TP --> P[Precision]
    FP --> P
    TP --> R[Recall]
    FN --> R
    P --> F1[F1 Score]
    R --> F1
    TP --> Acc[Accuracy]
    TN --> Acc
    FP --> Acc
    FN --> Acc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Sentiment Analysis. List its three main polarity classes.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)** is a Natural Language Processing task that computationally identifies, extracts, and classifies opinions, emotions, and attitudes expressed in text toward entities such as products, services, or events.

**Three main polarity classes:**
1. **Positive** — Favorable opinion (e.g., "Loved the experience!")
2. **Negative** — Unfavorable opinion (e.g., "Worst purchase ever.")
3. **Neutral** — Factual or absence of opinion (e.g., "The product weighs 200g.")

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Document Level and Aspect Level Sentiment Analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Document Level | Aspect Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Entire document treated as one unit | Specific features/aspects analyzed |
| **Output** | Single polarity label for the whole text | Multiple polarity labels, one per aspect |
| **Complexity** | Low | High — requires aspect extraction |
| **Example** | "The phone is great." → Positive | "Battery" → Positive, "Camera" → Negative |
| **Use Case** | Quick review classification | Fine-grained product feedback analysis |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the different levels of sentiment analysis in detail with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Sentiment Analysis operates at three hierarchical levels of granularity:

1. **Document Level Sentiment Analysis**
   - Treats the entire document as a single unit of opinion.
   - Assumes the document contains opinions on a **single entity**.
   - **Example:** A movie review like *"The film was a masterpiece with stunning visuals and a compelling storyline"* is classified as **Positive** as a whole.
   - **Limitation:** Fails when multiple entities or mixed opinions are present.

2. **Sentence Level Sentiment Analysis**
   - Classifies each sentence independently.
   - **Sub-task 1:** First determines whether a sentence is subjective or objective.
   - **Sub-task 2:** Classifies subjective sentences as Positive or Negative.
   - **Example:** *"The phone is expensive."* (Objective — no sentiment) vs. *"The phone is overpriced."* (Subjective — Negative).
   - **Limitation:** A single sentence can contain two opinions (e.g., *"Battery is great but camera is bad."*).

3. **Aspect Level (Feature-Based) Sentiment Analysis**
   - The **finest granularity** — extracts specific aspects/features and their corresponding sentiments.
   - **Example:** From *"The battery life is amazing, but the camera is poor"*, we extract:
     - Aspect: **battery life** → Sentiment: **Positive**
     - Aspect: **camera** → Sentiment: **Negative**
   - **Process:** Aspect Extraction → Opinion Word Identification → Sentiment Classification per Aspect.
   - **Used in:** E-commerce product reviews, hotel feedback analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to clearly state the **quadruple representation (Entity, Aspect, Holder, Polarity)** in aspect-level questions.

---

**(b)** Describe the Lexicon-Based approach to sentiment analysis. Explain how the SO-PMI method works. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Lexicon-Based Approach:**

A sentiment lexicon is a **pre-compiled dictionary** of words and phrases each tagged with a polarity score. The overall sentiment of a text is computed by aggregating individual word scores.

**Steps:**
1. Preprocess the text (tokenize, normalize).
2. For each token, retrieve its polarity score from the lexicon.
3. Apply **negation handling** (e.g., "not good" inverts the score).
4. Aggregate scores (sum, average, or weighted average).
5. Apply threshold rules to assign final polarity.

**Popular Lexicons:** VADER, SentiWordNet, Bing Liu Opinion Lexicon, AFINN.

**SO-PMI (Semantic Orientation via Pointwise Mutual Information):**

Proposed by Turney & Littman, SO-PMI is a **corpus-based lexicon expansion technique** that determines the semantic orientation of a phrase without a pre-existing dictionary.

**Formulas:**

$$PMI(w_1, w_2) = \log\left(\frac{P(w_1 \wedge w_2)}{P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2)}\right)$$

$$SO(p) = PMI(p, \text{"excellent"}) - PMI(p, \text{"poor"})$$

- $P(w_1, w_2)$ is the probability of words $w_1$ and $w_2$ co-occurring.
- If $SO(p) > 0$ → phrase is **Positive**.
- If $SO(p) < 0$ → phrase is **Negative**.
- If $SO(p) \approx 0$ → phrase is **Neutral**.

**Example:** For "unpredictable plot" in a movie corpus, if it co-occurs frequently with "excellent" and rarely with "poor", SO is positive.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the process of sentiment analysis using a Machine Learning approach. Discuss the role of TF-IDF in feature extraction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ML-Based Sentiment Analysis Workflow:**

The ML approach treats sentiment classification as a **supervised text classification problem**.

**Block Diagram (Verbal Description):**
1. **Dataset Collection** — Labeled corpus (e.g., IMDB, Amazon reviews).
2. **Text Preprocessing** — Tokenization, stopword removal, stemming.
3. **Train-Test Split** — Typically 80:20 ratio.
4. **Feature Extraction** — TF-IDF vectorization.
5. **Model Training** — Classifier (Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression).
6. **Prediction** — Polarity label for unseen text.
7. **Evaluation** — Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Accuracy.

**Role of TF-IDF in Feature Extraction:**

TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) converts text into numerical feature vectors.

$$w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \times idf(t) = tf(t,d) \times \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right)$$

- **TF** captures the importance of a word **within a document**.
- **IDF** penalizes words that are **common across all documents** (e.g., "the", "is").
- The product gives **high weight to discriminative words** and low weight to common words.
- **Why it matters:** Without TF-IDF, classifiers get confused by frequent but uninformative words. TF-IDF creates a sparse but meaningful feature matrix for the ML algorithm.

**Example:** In a corpus of 1000 reviews, the word "excellent" appearing 3 times in one review and 50 times across all docs will get a meaningful TF-IDF weight that helps the classifier identify it as a positive indicator.

---

**(b)** A sentiment classifier was tested on 200 reviews. The confusion matrix is as follows:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 90 | 10 |
| **Actual Negative** | 20 | 80 |

**Calculate: (i) Accuracy (ii) Precision (iii) Recall (iv) F1-Score.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** TP = 90, FN = 10, FP = 20, TN = 80, N = 200.

**(i) Accuracy:**

$$Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{90+80}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85 \text{ (85\%)}$$

**(ii) Precision:**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{90}{90+20} = \frac{90}{110} \approx 0.8182 \text{ (81.82\%)}$$

**Interpretation:** When the model predicts "Positive", it is correct 81.82% of the time.

**(iii) Recall:**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{90}{90+10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 \text{ (90\%)}$$

**Interpretation:** The model successfully captures 90% of all actual positive reviews.

**(iv) F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8182 \times 0.90}{0.8182 + 0.90}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7364}{1.7182} = 2 \times 0.4286 \approx 0.8571 \text{ (85.71\%)}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | 0.8500 |
| Precision | 0.8182 |
| Recall | 0.9000 |
| **F1-Score** | **0.8571** |

> [!NOTE]
> Since the dataset is balanced (110 positive vs. 90 negative), accuracy and F1 are similar. In imbalanced scenarios, F1 is the **superior** metric.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — Precision = "Of those I predicted positive, how many are right?" Recall = "Of all actual positives, how many did I find?"
> 2. **Forgetting Negation Handling** — Lexicon-based systems fail badly on "not good" unless explicitly handled. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for omitting this.
> 3. **Mixing Up Levels** — Confusing sentence level with aspect level is a frequent error.
> 4. **Not Writing Formula Units/Variables** — Always define $N$, $df(t)$, $TP$, etc., in your answer.
> 5. **Skipping Block Diagrams in ML Questions** — KTU awards 1-2 marks specifically for the diagram.
> 6. **Computing Macro F1 vs. Weighted F1 Wrong** — Macro = simple average; Weighted = class-frequency-weighted.
> 7. **Ignoring Sarcasm & Context** — Mentioning these as challenges scores bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Sentiment Analysis:**

- **Definition:** NLP task to identify and classify opinions in text as Positive / Negative / Neutral.
- **Levels:** Document → Sentence → Aspect (finest).
- **Polarity Classes:** Positive, Negative, Neutral (+ Mixed/Conflict in advanced).
- **Approaches:** Lexicon-based, Machine Learning, Hybrid, Deep Learning.
- **Key Lexicons:** VADER, SentiWordNet, Bing Liu, AFINN.
- **ML Algorithms:** Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest.
- **Feature Extraction:** BoW, TF-IDF, Word2Vec, BERT embeddings.
- **Critical Formula — TF-IDF:** $w = tf \times \log(N/df)$
- **Critical Formula — F1:** $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$
- **SO-PMI Method:** $SO(p) = PMI(p, \text{excellent}) - PMI(p, \text{poor})$
- **Major Challenges:** Sarcasm, Negation, Context, Domain Specificity, Multilingualism.
- **Applications:** Brand monitoring, VoC analytics, political forecasting, finance, HR.
- **Preferred Metric:** F1-Score (especially for imbalanced data).
- **Aspect Quadruple:** (Holder, Entity, Aspect, Polarity) — remember this exact format.
- **Hybrid Approach = Lexicon + ML** = often state-of-the-art in practical deployments.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytics in Digital - Decoding Consumer Intent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Digital - Decoding Consumer Intent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Consumer Intent** in the digital analytics domain refers to the underlying purpose, goal, or desired outcome that drives a user's online behavior, expressed explicitly through search queries, navigation patterns, and interaction signals, or inferred implicitly through behavioral data such as clickstream, dwell time, scroll depth, and micro-conversion events.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for Business Analytics, *Decoding Consumer Intent* is positioned as the analytical process of transforming raw, multi-source digital behavioral data into structured intent signals that can be used for **real-time personalization, predictive targeting, conversion optimization**, and **strategic marketing interventions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECBT755, Module 3):**
> Decoding consumer intent involves identifying the *latent motivation* behind a digital interaction by combining **explicit data** (search keywords, form inputs, declared preferences) with **implicit data** (mouse movement, session duration, content engagement metrics) to predict the probability of a downstream action (purchase, signup, churn).

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Consumer Intent in Digital Analytics

| Pillar | Description | Data Source Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Declared Intent** | Stated goals of the user | Search queries, form fields, survey responses |
| **Observed Intent** | Inferred from digital behavior | Page views, clicks, scroll depth, time on page |
| **Derived Intent** | Modeled from patterns across sessions | ML-based intent scores, lookalike audiences |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Shop Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The "Detective in the Coffee Shop" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you own a coffee shop. A customer walks in and says, *"I'd like a strong espresso, no sugar"* — that is **Declared Intent** (they told you what they want).
>
> Another customer walks in, glances at the dessert counter, picks up a menu, lingers on the breakfast section, and then asks, *"What's in the cappuccino?"* — this is **Observed Intent**. You, as the owner, can decode that they are probably hungry and leaning toward a creamy, food-pairing drink.
>
> A third customer walks in every Tuesday at 8 AM, orders a latte, and sits by the window for 40 minutes. From this **Derived Intent** pattern, you can predict: *"This customer will be here next Tuesday for the same latte."* That is **Predicted Intent** built from longitudinal behavior.
>
> **Digital Analytics does exactly this — but at scale, across millions of users, in milliseconds.**

## 1.4 Why Consumer Intent Matters — The Business Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Commercial Outcomes of Decoding Intent:**
> 1. **Higher Conversion Rate:** Intent-targeted campaigns convert **2× to 5× better** than demographic-based campaigns (industry benchmark: Google, Meta, Adobe Analytics case studies).
> 2. **Lower Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC):** Right-message-right-time reduces wasted impressions.
> 3. **Improved Customer Lifetime Value (CLV):** Intent-aware personalization increases repeat-purchase probability.

## 1.5 The Intent Decoding Pipeline (High-Level)

The end-to-end process of decoding consumer intent follows a structured **5-stage pipeline**:

1. **Data Collection** → Multi-source capture (web, mobile, CRM, social)
2. **Signal Extraction** → Feature engineering on raw events
3. **Intent Classification** → Mapping signals to intent categories
4. **Intent Scoring** → Assigning probability/strength values
5. **Activation** → Routing the intent to channels (ad servers, CRM, sales)

This pipeline forms the backbone of every modern **Customer Data Platform (CDP)** and **Real-Time Bidding (RTB)** system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Intent Categories

According to the foundational taxonomy by **Broder (2002)** and refined by **Jansen, Booth, & Spink (2008)** in the field of Information Retrieval, digital search/consumer intent is classified into four canonical categories. These appear repeatedly in KTU exam questions.

| Intent Type | User Goal | Example Query | Commercial Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Informational** | To learn, find knowledge | *"What is machine learning?"* | Low (top-of-funnel) |
| **Navigational** | To reach a specific site/page | *"Facebook login"* | Brand-direct |
| **Transactional** | To complete an action (buy, download) | *"Buy iPhone 15 online"* | Highest (BOFU) |
| **Commercial Investigation** | To compare before purchase | *"iPhone 15 vs Samsung S24"* | High (MOFU) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "INT" Mnemonic:**
> **I** — Informational
> **N** — Navigational
> **T** — Transactional
> (Commercial Investigation is the *hidden fourth*, often tested as a 3-mark "list and explain" question.)

## 2.2 The Two Classes of Intent Signals

### 2.2.1 Explicit Signals (Stated by the User)
- Search query text
- Form-field inputs (newsletter signup, preference centers)
- Declared demographics
- Cart additions, wishlists
- Email subscription actions

### 2.2.2 Implicit Signals (Observed Behavior)
- **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** on ads or recommendations
- **Dwell Time (DT):** Time spent on a page
- **Scroll Depth (SD):** Percentage of page scrolled
- **Bounce Rate (BR):** Single-page sessions
- **Return Frequency (RF):** Revisit count in a window
- **Micro-conversions:** Video plays, PDF downloads, button hovers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Explicit signals are sparse but high-confidence. Implicit signals are abundant but noisy. Effective intent decoding requires **weighted fusion** of both.

## 2.3 The Intent Score Model

The **Intent Score (IS)** is a composite metric that quantifies the strength of a user's purchase/conversion intent at a given moment. It is computed as a weighted sum of normalized behavioral signals.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Decoding Consumer Intent

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Intent Score (Weighted Sum)** | $IS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | $w_i$ = weight of signal $i$ (unitless, $\sum w_i = 1$); $s_i$ = normalized score of signal $i$ in $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | **Normalized Dwell Time** | $s_{DT} = \dfrac{DT_{user}}{DT_{max}}$ | $DT_{user}$ = observed dwell time (seconds); $DT_{max}$ = max benchmark (seconds) |
| 3 | **Click-Through Rate** | $CTR = \dfrac{C_{clicks}}{C_{impressions}}$ | $C_{clicks}$ = total clicks; $C_{impressions}$ = total impressions (dimensionless) |
| 4 | **Bounce Rate** | $BR = \dfrac{S_{single}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $S_{single}$ = single-page sessions; $S_{total}$ = total sessions |
| 5 | **Conversion Rate** | $CR = \dfrac{N_{conv}}{N_{visitors}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{conv}$ = conversions; $N_{visitors}$ = unique visitors |
| 6 | **Bayesian Intent Update** | $P(I \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(E)}$ | $P(I)$ = prior intent; $P(E \mid I)$ = likelihood; $P(E)$ = evidence normalizer |
| 7 | **Engagement Rate (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{C_{engaged}}{C_{reach}} \times 100\%$ | $C_{engaged}$ = users who interacted; $C_{reach}$ = unique reached users |
| 8 | **Scroll Depth Score** | $s_{SD} = \dfrac{P_{scrolled}}{P_{total}}$ | $P_{scrolled}$ = pixels scrolled; $P_{total}$ = total page pixels (dimensionless ratio) |
| 9 | **Recency-Frequency-Monetary (RFM) Composite** | $RFM = \alpha R + \beta F + \gamma M$ | $R$ = recency; $F$ = frequency; $M$ = monetary; $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weights |
| 10 | **Attribution Weight (Linear)** | $w_{touch_i} = \dfrac{1}{N_{touches}}$ | $N_{touches}$ = total touchpoints in journey (equal credit) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** All vertical bars in formulas (e.g., conditional probability notation $P(A \vert B)$) are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Theoretical Frameworks for Intent Decoding

### Framework 1: The Micro-Moments Model (Google, 2015)

Google's framework identifies four **Micro-Moments** that capture intent in real time:
1. **I-want-to-know** moments (Informational intent)
2. **I-want-to-go** moments (Navigational/local intent)
3. **I-want-to-do** moments (Task-oriented intent)
4. **I-want-to-buy** moments (Transactional intent)

Each moment is a **decision-making pinch point** where consumer intent is at its peak crystallinity.

### Framework 2: The Intent Funnel (TOFU → MOFU → BOFU)

$$Intent_{strength} \propto f(depth, recency, specificity)$$

- **TOFU (Top-of-Funnel):** Broad, informational, low commercial intent
- **MOFU (Middle-of-Funnel):** Comparative, commercial-investigation intent
- **BOFU (Bottom-of-Funnel):** Transactional, high purchase intent

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Intent Decoding is Applied in Industry:**
> - **Google Search Ads:** Bid prices vary by keyword intent category ($CPC_{transactional} \gg CPC_{informational}$)
> - **E-commerce Personalization:** Amazon, Flipkart use real-time intent scoring for "Recommended for You"
> - **Banking & FinTech:** Loan product targeting based on browsing signals
> - **SaaS Lead Scoring:** HubSpot, Salesforce assign intent scores to inbound leads
> - **Dynamic Pricing:** Uber, airlines use intent signals to modulate prices

## 2.6 Bayesian Updating of Intent (Conceptual)

When a new behavior is observed, the prior belief about a user's intent is updated using **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(I_{k+1} \mid E_{new}) = \dfrac{P(E_{new} \mid I_{k+1}) \cdot P(I_{k})}{P(E_{new})}$$

This forms the **probabilistic engine** behind real-time personalization systems. Every new click, page view, or scroll event nudges the intent probability distribution.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations — From Raw Events to Intent Score

### Derivation 1: Normalized Intent Score (Min-Max Normalization)

**Problem Setup:** A user exhibits four raw behavioral signals:
- Dwell Time: $DT_{user} = 120$ seconds
- Page Depth: $D = 5$ pages
- Scroll Depth: $P_{scrolled} = 2400$ px, $P_{total} = 3000$ px
- Repeat Visits: $V = 3$ in 7 days

Industry benchmarks (maximum observed values):
- $DT_{max} = 300$ s, $D_{max} = 10$ pages, $P_{total}$ is given, $V_{max} = 10$

**Weights (assigned by domain expert):** $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.2$, $w_3 = 0.2$, $w_4 = 0.2$

**Step 1: Normalize each signal to the range [0, 1].**

The Min-Max normalization formula is:

$$s_i = \dfrac{x_i - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$

For our signals (assuming $x_{min} = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{DT} &= \dfrac{DT_{user}}{DT_{max}} = \dfrac{120}{300} = 0.400 \\
s_{D} &= \dfrac{D}{D_{max}} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.500 \\
s_{SD} &= \dfrac{P_{scrolled}}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{2400}{3000} = 0.800 \\
s_{V} &= \dfrac{V}{V_{max}} = \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the weighted sum formula.**

$$IS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IS &= (0.4 \times 0.400) + (0.2 \times 0.500) + (0.2 \times 0.800) + (0.2 \times 0.300) \\
  &= 0.160 + 0.100 + 0.160 + 0.060 \\
  &= 0.480
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

$IS = 0.480$ falls in the **[0.0 – 0.5)** band, which corresponds to **"Browsing / Considering"** intent — the user is engaged but not yet at purchase readiness. A retargeting ad with a soft call-to-action (e.g., "Learn more about our new arrivals") is appropriate.

---

### Derivation 2: Bayesian Intent Update

**Problem Setup:** Prior knowledge suggests that among users in the "Electronics" category, 30% have high purchase intent. A new signal arrives: the user has added an item to their cart. Historically, 75% of users who add-to-cart go on to purchase, and only 25% of those with high intent add-to-cart.

**Given:**
- Prior: $P(I) = 0.30$ (probability of high intent)
- $P(E \mid I) = 0.75$ (likelihood of cart-add given high intent)
- $P(E \mid \neg I) = 0.25$ (likelihood of cart-add given low intent)

**Step 1: Compute total evidence $P(E)$.**

Using the law of total probability:

$$P(E) = P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I) + P(E \mid \neg I) \cdot P(\neg I)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(E) &= (0.75 \times 0.30) + (0.25 \times 0.70) \\
     &= 0.225 + 0.175 \\
     &= 0.400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$P(I \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(E)}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(I \mid E) &= \dfrac{0.75 \times 0.30}{0.400} \\
            &= \dfrac{0.225}{0.400} \\
            &= 0.5625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret.**

The prior probability of high intent (30%) was updated to **56.25%** after observing the cart-add event. This is a **27.75 percentage-point lift** — the system now believes the user is more likely than not to be a high-intent prospect, triggering premium ad-creative or sales-outreach automation.

---

### Derivation 3: RFM-Based Intent Segmentation

**Problem Setup:** Compute the RFM composite for two customers with the following data:

| Customer | Recency (days) | Frequency (orders) | Monetary (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 8 | 12,000 |
| B | 30 | 2 | 3,000 |

Use the standard RFM scoring (1 = worst, 5 = best) with weights $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.3$, $\gamma = 0.4$.

**Step 1: Convert raw values to quintile scores (1–5).**

For recency (lower is better), for frequency and monetary (higher is better):

| Customer | $R$ (score) | $F$ (score) | $M$ (score) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 (very recent) | 5 (high freq) | 5 (high monetary) |
| B | 2 (old) | 2 (low freq) | 2 (low monetary) |

**Step 2: Compute the weighted RFM composite.**

$$RFM = \alpha R + \beta F + \gamma M$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
RFM_A &= (0.3 \times 5) + (0.3 \times 5) + (0.4 \times 5) = 1.5 + 1.5 + 2.0 = 5.0 \\
RFM_B &= (0.3 \times 2) + (0.3 \times 2) + (0.4 \times 2) = 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.8 = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Map to intent segment.**

| RFM Score | Segment | Marketing Action |
|---|---|---|
| 4.0 – 5.0 | **Champions** (High Intent) | Loyalty rewards, early access |
| 3.0 – 3.9 | **Potential Loyalists** | Personalized recommendations |
| 2.0 – 2.9 | **At Risk / Low Intent** | Re-engagement campaigns, discounts |
| 1.0 – 1.9 | **Hibernating** | Suppress or win-back |

Customer A → Champions. Customer B → Low Intent / At Risk.

---

| Long-tail query | ✅ Strong | ❌ Weak | ⚠️ Medium | ❌ Weak |
| Branded query | ❌ Weak | ✅ Strong | ❌ Weak | ⚠️ Medium |
| Comparison keyword (*"vs"*) | ⚠️ Medium | ❌ Weak | ✅ Strong | ❌ Weak |

## 5.1 KTU University Exam Pattern Reference

## 5.2 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: Define "Consumer Intent" in the context of digital analytics. List the four canonical types of consumer intent.**

**Model Answer:**

Consumer Intent is the underlying purpose that drives a user's online behavior, expressed through search queries, navigation, and engagement signals. The four canonical types are:

1. **Informational** — seeking knowledge (e.g., "how does solar panel work?")
2. **Navigational** — reaching a specific site (e.g., "Amazon login")
3. **Transactional** — completing a purchase (e.g., "buy shoes online")
4. **Commercial Investigation** — comparing options (e.g., "iPhone vs Samsung review")

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *omit Commercial Investigation* and list only 3 types, losing 1 full mark. The fourth type is the most-tested nuance in KTU 2024 papers.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Set]
**Q: Differentiate between Explicit and Implicit intent signals with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Explicit Signal | Implicit Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Stated by user | Observed by system |
| Confidence | High | Moderate (requires inference) |
| Example | Search query "buy DSLR camera" | Spending 8 minutes on a camera review page, scrolling to specs section |

Explicit signals are direct declarations of intent; implicit signals are behavioral inferences that may or may not correlate with actual intent.

---

## 5.3 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series A]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Micro-Moments framework for decoding consumer intent. Describe each of the four micro-moments with a digital example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Micro-Moments framework**, popularized by Google (2015), describes the intent-rich decision points where users turn to a device to act on a need. It comprises four critical micro-moments:

1. **I-want-to-know moments:** Users seek information spontaneously.
   *Example:* A user Googles "symptoms of vitamin D deficiency" — informational intent.
2. **I-want-to-go moments:** Users look for a local business or place.
   *Example:* Searching "coffee shop near me open now" — navigational/local intent.
3. **I-want-to-do moments:** Users want help completing a task or trying something new.
   *Example:* Watching a YouTube tutorial on "how to tie a Windsor knot" — task-oriented intent.
4. **I-want-to-buy moments:** Users are ready to purchase and need the right offer.
   *Example:* Searching "best deal on OnePlus Nord" — transactional/commercial intent.

In each micro-moment, the user's intent is at its **highest crystallinity**, making it the optimal time for brands to deliver relevant content, ads, or product recommendations. Marketers who win these micro-moments capture loyalty and conversion.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compute the Intent Score for a user with the following normalized signals and weights. Interpret the result.**

| Signal | Normalized Value ($s_i$) | Weight ($w_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| Dwell Time | 0.70 | 0.30 |
| Pages Visited | 0.50 | 0.20 |
| Add to Cart | 1.00 | 0.35 |
| Repeat Visit | 0.40 | 0.15 |

**Model Answer:**

Using the weighted-sum formula:

$$IS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IS &= (0.30 \times 0.70) + (0.20 \times 0.50) + (0.35 \times 1.00) + (0.15 \times 0.40) \\
  &= 0.210 + 0.100 + 0.350 + 0.060 \\
  &= 0.720
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An Intent Score of **0.720** falls in the **[0.6, 0.8)** band, indicating **High Purchase Intent / MOFU-BOFU transition**. The user should be served a personalized offer, dynamic discount, or sales-assist chat to accelerate conversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *forget to show the intermediate products* ($0.30 \times 0.70 = 0.210$, etc.). KTU valuators allocate 1 mark specifically for **each correct intermediate product**. Skipping steps costs you 2–3 marks even if the final answer is right.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series B]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] Describe the architecture of a real-time intent-decoding system. List and explain the five layers with their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

A real-time intent-decoding system is structured in **five architectural layers**:

1. **Data Sources Layer:** Aggregates events from web logs, mobile SDKs, CRM, social media, and third-party data partners. Example: Google Analytics 4 stream + Salesforce CRM + Meta Pixel.

2. **Signal Extraction Layer:** Performs sessionization, clickstream parsing, NLP tokenization of search queries, and feature engineering. Example: Extracting "dwell time on pricing page" as a feature.

3. **Intent Classification Layer:** Categorizes users into intent buckets using rule-based filters (e.g., query contains "buy") and ML models (e.g., logistic regression, gradient-boosted trees, transformer-based classifiers).

4. **Intent Scoring Layer:** Assigns a numerical probability/score (0 to 1) to each user, updated in real time using Bayesian inference or gradient-boosted scoring.

5. **Activation Layer:** Routes the intent output to action channels — ad servers (Google Ads, Meta), CRM (Salesforce, HubSpot), sales notifications, or on-site personalization engines.

This architecture enables **sub-second latency** for real-time bidding (RTB) and **batch processing** for downstream segmentation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A bank has observed that among website visitors, 20% have high loan-intent. The likelihood of a visitor requesting a callback is 0.60 if they have high intent, and 0.15 if they have low intent. If a visitor requests a callback, what is the posterior probability that they have high loan intent? Use Bayes' Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Prior: $P(I) = 0.20$
- $P(E \mid I) = 0.60$ (callback request given high intent)
- $P(E \mid \neg I) = 0.15$ (callback request given low intent)

**Step 1: Compute total evidence $P(E)$ using the law of total probability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(E) &= P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I) + P(E \mid \neg I) \cdot P(\neg I) \\
     &= (0.60 \times 0.20) + (0.15 \times 0.80) \\
     &= 0.120 + 0.120 \\
     &= 0.240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(I \mid E) &= \dfrac{P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(E)} \\
            &= \dfrac{0.60 \times 0.20}{0.240} \\
            &= \dfrac{0.120}{0.240} \\
            &= 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

The posterior probability of high loan intent, given a callback request, is **0.50 (50%)**. This is a **30 percentage-point lift** over the prior (20% → 50%), making the callback event a strong leading indicator. The bank should prioritize routing such users to its loan-officer team for follow-up.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly compute the numerator as $0.60 \times 0.20 = 0.12$ and *forget to divide by $P(E) = 0.24$*, reporting the final answer as **0.12** instead of **0.50**. This is the most common KTU 2024 Baye's-theorem error — 2-mark penalty applies for missing the denominator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
