> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation:**

- ✅ **Consumer Intent** = the *latent goal* behind a digital interaction; decoded via *declared* + *observed* signals.
- ✅ **Four canonical intent types:** Informational, Navigational, Transactional, **Commercial Investigation** (don't omit the fourth!).
- ✅ **Explicit vs Implicit signals:** Explicit = stated; Implicit = behavioral inference.
- ✅ **Intent Score (IS)** is a **weighted sum of normalized signals**, $IS \in [0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is the engine for real-time intent updating: $P(I \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(E)}$.
- ✅ **Micro-Moments** (I-want-to-know, -go, -do, -buy) are the **decision pinch points** where intent is sharpest.
- ✅ **Funnel mapping:** TOFU → MOFU → BOFU maps to Informational → Commercial Investigation → Transactional.
- ✅ **RFM Model** segments customers on Recency, Frequency, Monetary for intent-based targeting.
- ✅ **Real-time architecture** has 5 layers: Data → Extraction → Classification → Scoring → Activation.
- ✅ **Key metrics to memorize:** CTR, CR, BR, ER, DT, SD, RF, IS, RFM.
- ✅ **Ethical note:** Intent decoding must comply with data-privacy regulations (India's DPDP Act 2023, EU's GDPR) — consent and anonymization are mandatory.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake:** Reporting Bayesian numerators without the denominator — always divide by $P(E)$.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Analytics in Digital: Decoding Consumer Intent**
*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme | PECBT755 — Business Analytics*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decoding Customer Sentiments from Comments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decoding Customer Sentiments from Comments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)** is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, evaluations, attitudes, and emotions expressed in text. In the KTU Business Analytics framework, it falls under **Text Mining** and is treated as a sub-field of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** that systematically converts unstructured customer comments into structured, quantifiable sentiment polarity scores (Positive / Negative / Neutral).

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition
Decoding Customer Sentiments is the process of applying **lexicon-based dictionaries, supervised machine learning classifiers, and deep learning architectures** to free-form text data (reviews, tweets, feedback) so that the underlying affective state of the writer can be classified, scored, and aggregated for **Business Intelligence (BI)** decision-making.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine you own a chain of 10,000 restaurants and receive **5,000 customer comments daily** on your app. A human cannot read them all. **Sentiment Decoding** is like hiring a super-fast, multilingual robot reader that:
1. Scans every comment in milliseconds.
2. Decides whether the customer is **happy**, **angry**, or **neutral**.
3. Tags keywords like "rude staff" or "amazing ambience" so you know *what* to fix or *what* to promote.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus treats this as a **three-class problem** by default: Positive, Negative, and Neutral. Some advanced cases use **fine-grained 5-class** labelling (Very Positive → Very Negative).

### 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics
The following metrics are **must-know** KTU 2024 terminology:

| Term | Definition | Typical Range / Value |
|------|------------|----------------------|
| **Polarity** | A numeric score indicating how positive or negative a comment is | $-1.0$ to $+1.0$ |
| **Subjectivity** | Score indicating the presence of opinions vs factual statements | $0.0$ to $1.0$ |
| **Aspect** | The specific feature being reviewed (e.g., "battery", "service") | Categorical |
| **Corpus** | The entire collection of customer comments used for analysis | Integer count |
| **F1-Score** | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall | $0.0$ to $1.0$ |

### 1.4 Why KTU Cares About This Topic
> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic maps to **CO3: Apply text mining and sentiment analysis techniques to derive actionable business insights from unstructured data**. It directly tests the learner's ability to convert qualitative text into quantitative managerial decisions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sentiment Decoding Pipeline (Five Logical Steps)

1. **Data Acquisition (Web Scraping / API Ingestion)**
   - Pull raw comments from sources like Twitter API, Amazon reviews, Google Play Store, or internal CRM logs.
   - *Why:* You cannot analyse what you have not collected.
   - *How:* Use connectors (e.g., `Tweepy`, `Scrapy`) — at the KTU level, the focus is on the *logic*, not the tool.

2. **Text Pre-Processing (Cleaning)**
   - Remove **stop words** ("the", "is", "and"), punctuation, URLs, and emojis.
   - Apply **Tokenization** (splitting sentences into words), **Lemmatization** (reducing "running" → "run"), and **Stemming**.
   - *Why:* Raw text is noisy. "The movie was NOT good 😡" must be correctly interpreted.

3. **Feature Engineering (Vectorization)**
   - Convert cleaned text into numerical vectors using techniques like **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**, **TF-IDF**, or **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec / GloVe)**.

4. **Sentiment Classification / Scoring**
   - Apply one of three KTU-mandated approaches:
     - **(a) Lexicon-Based** (e.g., VADER, TextBlob) — uses a sentiment dictionary.
     - **(b) Machine Learning-Based** (e.g., Naive Bayes, SVM) — supervised learning.
     - **(c) Hybrid / Deep Learning** (e.g., LSTM, BERT) — context-aware.

5. **Visualization & Business Action**
   - Aggregate scores into dashboards (Pie charts, Word Clouds, Trend lines) and feed managerial decisions.

### 2.2 The Three KTU-Mandated Approaches Compared

| Approach | Working Principle | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------------------|------|------|
| **Lexicon-Based** | Sums sentiment weights of pre-labelled words | Simple, fast, no training data needed | Cannot handle sarcasm or context |
| **Machine Learning** | Learns patterns from a labelled training set | Handles context, high accuracy | Requires large labelled corpus |
| **Hybrid / Deep Learning** | Combines lexicon + neural context (e.g., BERT) | State-of-the-art accuracy | Computationally expensive, complex |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Mathematical Form | Application | Units |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Polarity Score** | $P = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}{n}$ | Average sentiment weight per comment | Unitless ($-1$ to $+1$) |
| **Subjectivity Score** | $S = \frac{\text{Subjective Words}}{\text{Total Words}}$ | Opinion density in text | Unitless ($0$ to $1$) |
| **TF (Term Frequency)** | $TF(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | How often word $t$ appears in document $d$ | Ratio |
| **IDF (Inverse Document Frequency)** | $IDF(t,D) = \log \frac{\vert D \vert}{\vert \{d \in D : t \in d\} \vert}$ | Rarity of word $t$ across corpus $D$ | Log value |
| **TF-IDF Weight** | $W(t,d,D) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t,D)$ | Combined importance score | Numeric |
| **Naive Bayes Posterior** | $P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ | Probability of class $C$ given features $X$ | Probability ($0$ to $1$) |
| **Precision** | $Pr = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Accuracy of positive predictions | Ratio |
| **Recall** | $Re = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | Ratio |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Pr \cdot Re}{Pr + Re}$ | Balanced performance metric | Ratio ($0$ to $1$) |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | Ratio ($0$ to $1$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $TP$ = True Positive, $FP$ = False Positive, $FN$ = False Negative, $TN$ = True Negative. The symbol $\vert D \vert$ means *cardinality* (count of elements) of set $D$, written with `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility
- **E-Commerce:** Amazon and Flipkart use it to rank products and detect fake reviews.
- **Finance:** Hedge funds apply **News Sentiment Analysis** to predict stock movements.
- **Healthcare:** Mining patient feedback to improve hospital services.
- **Political Science:** Real-time election mood tracking from social media.
- **KTU Relevance:** Directly supports **CO3** and indirectly supports **CO5 (Visualization for decision-making)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Lexicon-Based Polarity Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
Given the customer comment: *"The food was absolutely delicious, but the service was terribly slow."*
The lexicon dictionary provides the following sentiment weights:

| Word | Weight |
|------|--------|
| delicious | $+0.8$ |
| absolutely | $+0.3$ |
| terribly | $-0.7$ |
| slow | $-0.5$ |

Determine the **Polarity Score** and classify the sentiment.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify sentiment-bearing words from the comment.} \\
\text{Tokens with assigned weights: } \{\text{delicious}, \text{absolutely}, \text{terribly}, \text{slow}\} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Sum all individual polarity weights.} \\
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = (+0.8) + (+0.3) + (-0.7) + (-0.5) \\
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = +1.1 - 1.2 = -0.1 \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Compute the average polarity using } P = \frac{\sum w_i}{n}. \\
P = \frac{-0.1}{4} = -0.025 \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Apply classification threshold (KTU standard rule).} \\
\text{If } P > +0.05 \Rightarrow \text{Positive} \\
\text{If } P < -0.05 \Rightarrow \text{Negative} \\
\text{Otherwise} \Rightarrow \text{Neutral} \\
\\
\text{Since } -0.05 \le P = -0.025 \le +0.05, \text{ the comment is classified as } \textbf{Neutral}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** Notice that the positive and negative aspects ("food" vs "service") cancelled out. This is the **Aspect-Based Limitation** of pure lexicon methods, and KTU 2024 paper-setters love to test whether students recognize this.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Naive Bayes Sentiment Classification (Full Derivation)

**Problem Statement:**
A company has historical data from **1,000 reviews**, with the following distribution:

| Class | Total Reviews | Reviews containing "excellent" | Reviews containing "refund" |
|-------|---------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Positive ($C_{+}$) | 600 | 240 | 60 |
| Negative ($C_{-}$) | 400 | 40 | 200 |

A new comment arrives: *"Excellent product, but I want a refund."*
Using Naive Bayes, classify it as **Positive** or **Negative**. Assume conditional independence and prior probabilities $P(C_+) = 0.6$, $P(C_-) = 0.4$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute likelihoods for "excellent" given each class.} \\
P(\text{excellent} \mid C_+) = \frac{240}{600} = 0.40 \\
P(\text{excellent} \mid C_-) = \frac{40}{400} = 0.10 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Compute likelihoods for "refund" given each class.} \\
P(\text{refund} \mid C_+) = \frac{60}{600} = 0.10 \\
P(\text{refund} \mid C_-) = \frac{200}{400} = 0.50 \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Apply Bayes Theorem with conditional independence assumption.} \\
P(C_+ \mid \text{excellent, refund}) = \frac{P(\text{excellent} \mid C_+) \cdot P(\text{refund} \mid C_+) \cdot P(C_+)}{P(\text{excellent, refund})} \\
P(C_- \mid \text{excellent, refund}) = \frac{P(\text{excellent} \mid C_-) \cdot P(\text{refund} \mid C_-) \cdot P(C_-)}{P(\text{excellent, refund})}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Compare the unnormalized numerators (proportional comparison).} \\
\text{Numerator for Positive} = 0.40 \times 0.10 \times 0.60 = 0.0240 \\
\text{Numerator for Negative} = 0.10 \times 0.50 \times 0.40 = 0.0200 \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Normalize to obtain true posterior probabilities.} \\
P(C_+ \mid X) = \frac{0.0240}{0.0240 + 0.0200} = \frac{0.0240}{0.0440} = 0.5454 \\
P(C_- \mid X) = \frac{0.0200}{0.0440} = 0.4546 \\
\\
\text{Step 6: Final classification decision.} \\
\text{Since } P(C_+ \mid X) = 0.5454 > P(C_- \mid X) = 0.4546, \\
\text{the new comment is classified as } \textbf{Positive}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although the word "refund" strongly suggests negativity, the joint probability of "excellent" (a strong positive signal) combined with the higher prior probability of the positive class tips the scale. This is a **classic KTU trick question** designed to test the student's understanding of *joint* likelihoods, not single-word heuristics.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Evaluating a Sentiment Model (Confusion Matrix Analysis)

**Problem Statement:**
A sentiment classifier tested on 200 customer comments produced the following Confusion Matrix:

| Actual \ Predicted | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Actual Positive** | $TP = 90$ | $FN = 10$ |
| **Actual Negative** | $FP = 20$ | $TN = 80$ |

Compute **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy**.

**Step-by-Step Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Extract Confusion Matrix values.} \\
TP = 90, \quad FP = 20, \quad FN = 10, \quad TN = 80 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Compute Precision.} \\
Pr = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 20} = \frac{90}{110} = 0.8182 \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Compute Recall.} \\
Re = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.9000 \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Compute F1-Score.} \\
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Pr \cdot Re}{Pr + Re} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8182 \times 0.9000}{0.8182 + 0.9000} \\
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7364}{1.7182} = 2 \times 0.4286 = 0.8571 \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Compute Accuracy.} \\
Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{90 + 80}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.8500 \\
\\
\text{Step 6: Convert all to percentage for managerial reporting.} \\
Pr = 81.82\%, \quad Re = 90.00\%, \quad F1 = 85.71\%, \quad Acc = 85.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** If Precision and Recall are both given, you **must** show the F1-Score formula substituted with numerical values, not just the final answer. Marks are awarded for the substitution step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sentiment Decoding Pipeline (End-to-End Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Customer Comments] --> B[Data Acquisition Layer]
    B --> C[Text Pre-Processing Module]
    C --> C1[Tokenization]
    C --> C2[Stop Word Removal]
    C --> C3[Lemmatization]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering Module]
    D --> D1[TF-IDF Vectorizer]
    D --> D2[Word Embedding GloVe]
    E[Sentiment Classification Engine] --> E1[Lexicon VADER]
    E --> E2[Naive Bayes Classifier]
    E --> E3[BERT Deep Model]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Polarity Score Output]
    F --> G[Dashboard Visualization Layer]
    G --> G1[Sentiment Pie Chart]
    G --> G2[Aspect Word Cloud]
    G --> G3[Time Series Trend]
    G --> H[Managerial Decision Support]
```

**Block Description:**
- **Input Layer (A):** Raw text from social media, e-commerce, CRM.
- **Pre-Processing (C, C1–C3):** Cleans and normalizes text.
- **Feature Engineering (D, D1–D2):** Converts text to numbers.
- **Classification Engine (E, E1–E3):** Three parallel KTU-approved approaches.
- **Output (F, G, G1–G3):** Quantified scores and visual BI dashboards.
- **Decision Support (H):** Actionable insights for business managers.

---

### 4.2 Confusion Matrix Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Predicted Positive] --> P1[TP True Positive]
    P --> P2[FP False Positive]
    N[Predicted Negative] --> N1[FN False Negative]
    N --> N2[TN True Negative]
    P1 --> M[Performance Metrics Calculator]
    P2 --> M
    N1 --> M
    N2 --> M
    M --> M1[Precision]
    M --> M2[Recall]
    M --> M3[F1 Score]
    M --> M4[Accuracy]
```

**Block Description:**
This diagram visualizes how raw TP, FP, FN, TN counts feed the four primary evaluation metrics used in KTU Business Analytics evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **Q1.** Define **Sentiment Analysis**. List and briefly explain any two techniques used to perform sentiment analysis on customer comments. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Sentiment Analysis (or Opinion Mining) is a Natural Language Processing (NLP) technique used to computationally identify, extract, and classify opinions expressed in text as positive, negative, or neutral.
Two techniques are:
1. **Lexicon-Based Approach:** Uses a predefined sentiment dictionary (e.g., VADER) where each word is assigned a polarity weight. The overall sentiment is computed by summing weights of sentiment-bearing words in a comment.
2. **Machine Learning Approach:** A supervised classifier (e.g., Naive Bayes, SVM) is trained on a labelled corpus of comments to learn patterns, and then predicts sentiment for new, unseen comments.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Polarity** and **Subjectivity** in the context of text mining. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Polarity | Subjectivity |
|---------|----------|--------------|
| Definition | Measures the positivity or negativity of a text | Measures how opinionated versus factual the text is |
| Range | $-1.0$ to $+1.0$ | $0.0$ to $1.0$ |
| Indicates | Emotional tone | Degree of personal opinion |
| Example | "Food is amazing" → Polarity $= +0.8$ | "Food is amazing" → Subjectivity $= 1.0$ |
| Example | "Pizza contains cheese" → Polarity $= 0.0$ | "Pizza contains cheese" → Subjectivity $= 0.0$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **step-by-step pipeline** for decoding customer sentiments from raw text data. Discuss the role of **pre-processing** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The sentiment decoding pipeline consists of five sequential stages:
1. **Data Acquisition:** Collect raw comments from sources like social media APIs, review portals, or CRM databases.
2. **Text Pre-Processing:** Clean the text to remove noise. This includes:
   - **Tokenization:** Splitting text into individual words/tokens.
   - **Stop Word Removal:** Eliminating common words (e.g., "is", "the", "a") that carry no sentiment.
   - **Lemmatization/Stemming:** Reducing words to their root form (e.g., "running" → "run").
   - **Punctuation & Emoji Removal:** Stripping non-textual symbols (except for sentiment emojis if using VADER).
3. **Feature Engineering:** Convert text into numerical form using TF-IDF, Bag-of-Words, or embeddings.
4. **Classification/Scoring:** Apply lexicon-based or ML-based algorithms to assign a sentiment label.
5. **Visualization & Action:** Display results via dashboards (pie charts, word clouds) for managerial use.

**Role of Pre-Processing:** Pre-processing is critical because raw text is noisy, inconsistent, and full of non-informative tokens. Without cleaning, the model treats "Good" and "Gooood!!!" as different features, lowering accuracy. Pre-processing also reduces vocabulary size, improves computational efficiency, and ensures that lemmatized forms ("love", "loved", "loving") are treated as a single feature, thereby increasing model generalization.

---

> **(b)** Given the customer reviews and the lexicon weights below, calculate the **Polarity Score** and classify the sentiment for each review. **(7 Marks)**
>
> | Word | Weight |
> |------|--------|
> | fantastic | $+0.9$ |
> | rude | $-0.8$ |
> | worst | $-1.0$ |
> | okay | $+0.1$ |
> | late | $-0.4$ |
>
> **Review 1:** *"The product is fantastic and totally worth it."*
> **Review 2:** *"Worst experience, staff was rude and delivery was late."*

**Model Solution:**

For **Review 1**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Positive words found: fantastic} \\
\sum w_i = +0.9, \quad n = 1 \\
P_1 = \frac{+0.9}{1} = +0.90 \\
\text{Classification: } P_1 > +0.05 \Rightarrow \textbf{Positive}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Review 2**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Negative words found: worst, rude, late} \\
\sum w_i = (-1.0) + (-0.8) + (-0.4) = -2.2, \quad n = 3 \\
P_2 = \frac{-2.2}{3} = -0.733 \\
\text{Classification: } P_2 < -0.05 \Rightarrow \textbf{Negative}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a)** A sentiment classifier was tested on 500 comments. The resulting **Confusion Matrix** is given below. Compute **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> | Actual \ Predicted | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
> |--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
> | Actual Positive    | $TP = 180$         | $FN = 20$          |
> | Actual Negative    | $FP = 30$          | $TN = 270$         |

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision } Pr &= \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{180}{180+30} = \frac{180}{210} = 0.857 \\
\text{Recall } Re &= \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{180}{180+20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.900 \\
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{Pr \times Re}{Pr + Re} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.857 \times 0.900}{0.857 + 0.900} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7713}{1.757} = 0.878 \\
\text{Accuracy } Acc &= \frac{TP+TN}{500} = \frac{180+270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Reported Values:** $Pr = 85.7\%$, $Re = 90.0\%$, $F1 = 87.8\%$, $Acc = 90.0\%$.

---

> **(b)** Compare **Lexicon-Based** and **Machine Learning-Based** sentiment analysis approaches. Discuss one real-world business application of sentiment decoding. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lexicon-Based | Machine Learning-Based |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|
| Training Required | No (uses pre-built dictionary) | Yes (requires labelled corpus) |
| Handles Sarcasm | Poorly | Better (learns contextual cues) |
| Speed | Very fast | Slower (training + inference) |
| Accuracy on Domain Data | Moderate | High |
| Example Tools | VADER, TextBlob, SentiWordNet | Naive Bayes, SVM, BERT |

**Real-World Application:**
**Brand Reputation Management on Twitter:** Companies like Apple and Samsung use real-time sentiment decoding of millions of tweets to monitor public opinion immediately after a product launch. By tracking the *polarity trend* over time and flagging sudden spikes in negative sentiment (e.g., due to a battery issue), PR teams can respond within hours, issue recalls, or publish clarification posts — converting potential PR crises into trust-building opportunities. This directly impacts **revenue protection** and **customer retention**, which are core KTU Business Analytics outcomes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Polarity with Subjectivity.** Polarity = emotion direction; Subjectivity = opinion vs fact. Mixing them costs full 3 marks.
> 2. **Always show the F1-Score substitution step** — writing only the final 0.857 is marked partial. KTU 2024 valuation schemes require the harmonic mean formula to be visibly written.
> 3. **In Naive Bayes problems**, students often compute only single-word likelihoods and forget the **conditional independence assumption** that allows multiplication of $P(X \mid C)$ terms.
> 4. **In threshold-based classification**, never hard-code your own threshold without stating the KTU-standard $\pm 0.05$ rule.
> 5. **Avoid "accuracy trap":** When data is imbalanced (e.g., 95% positive), accuracy is misleading. KTU expects you to mention F1-Score as the **superior metric for imbalanced sentiment datasets**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Sentiment Analysis = NLP + Text Mining + Opinion Classification into Positive / Negative / Neutral.
- **Pipeline:** Data Acquisition → Pre-Processing → Feature Engineering → Classification → Visualization.
- **Three Approaches:** Lexicon-Based (VADER), ML-Based (Naive Bayes, SVM), Deep Learning (BERT, LSTM).
- **Polarity Score Formula:** $P = \frac{\sum w_i}{n}$, range $-1$ to $+1$.
- **Subjectivity Formula:** Ratio of opinion words to total words, range $0$ to $1$.
- **TF-IDF:** $W(t,d,D) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t,D)$ — measures word importance.
- **Naive Bayes:** $P(C \mid X) \propto P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)$ with conditional independence.
- **Confusion Matrix Metrics:** $Pr$, $Re$, $F1$, $Acc$ — always quote the **F1-Score** for imbalanced classes.
- **Lexicon Limitation:** Cannot handle **sarcasm, negation without context, or domain-specific slang**.
- **Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA):** The advanced KTU 2024 extension — identifies *which feature* (e.g., "battery") is being praised or criticized.
- **Business Value:** Used in brand monitoring, product feedback loops, customer churn prediction, and stock-market sentiment trading.
- **CO Mapping:** This topic satisfies **CO3 (Text Mining)** and **CO5 (Visualization & Decision Support)** under the KTU 2024 OBE framework.
- **Threshold Rule (KTU Standard):** Polarity $> +0.05$ = Positive, Polarity $< -0.05$ = Negative, otherwise Neutral.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Mining from Opinion Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Text Mining from Opinion Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Text Mining (also called Text Analytics or Text Data Mining)** is the multidisciplinary field that applies techniques from *Natural Language Processing (NLP)*, *Information Retrieval (IR)*, *Machine Learning (ML)*, and *Statistics* to extract high-quality, non-trivial, previously unknown, and potentially useful information or knowledge from unstructured textual data.

When this text mining is applied specifically to **Opinion Platforms** — digital ecosystems where users voluntarily publish subjective evaluations about products, services, brands, events, or policies (e.g., Amazon reviews, Twitter/X posts, TripAdvisor, Zomato, Glassdoor, Reddit, YouTube comments) — the discipline is more specifically termed **Opinion Mining** or **Sentiment Analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECBT755, Module 3):**
> *"Text mining from opinion platforms refers to the computational study of people's opinions, sentiments, evaluations, appraisals, attitudes, and emotions expressed in unstructured text on digital review and social platforms, with the goal of structuring the input, extracting relevant features, and classifying polarity (positive/negative/neutral) for business decision-making."*

The three core sub-tasks in opinion mining as per KTU are:
1. **Sentiment Classification** – Labelling an opinion as positive, negative, or neutral.
2. **Subjectivity Classification** – Distinguishing objective *facts* from subjective *opinions*.
3. **Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA)** – Identifying *what feature* of a product is being praised or criticized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Digital Restaurant Critic"**
>
> Imagine you own a chain of 500 restaurants across India, and **5 million customers** write reviews on Zomato, Google Maps, and Swiggy every month. No human team can read all of them.
>
> **Text Mining** is like hiring a *super-fast digital reader* who:
> - Reads every review in seconds.
> - Understands the *language* (English, Malayalam transliterated, Hinglish, emojis 😀😡).
> - Classifies each review as *praise*, *complaint*, or *neutral*.
> - Tells you *which exact thing* is being praised or complained about (taste, delivery, ambience, price, staff behaviour).
> - Aggregates everything into a **dashboard** showing trends, spikes, and alerts.
>
> That "digital critic" is essentially what text mining on opinion platforms does — at the scale of millions of opinions per day.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Corpus (C)** — The complete collection of text documents being mined.
- **Document (d)** — A single review, tweet, comment, or post.
- **Token** — The smallest meaningful unit (usually a word).
- **Polarity Score** — A continuous numerical value, typically in the range **[-1, +1]**, indicating how negative or positive a piece of text is.
- **Precision, Recall, F1-Score** — Standard classification evaluation metrics.
- **Stop Words** — Common words (the, is, at, which) that carry little semantic weight and are usually removed.
- **Stemming / Lemmatization** — Reducing words to their root form (e.g., *running → run*).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Text Mining Pipeline — The Six Logical Stages

The extraction of business value from opinion platforms is never a single-step process. KTU examiners expect students to know the **pipeline** thoroughly, as it appears regularly in Part A and Part B questions.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Crawling / Scraping)
- Pulling text from opinion platforms using **APIs** (Twitter/X API, Reddit API, Amazon Product Advertising API) or web crawlers like **Scrapy, BeautifulSoup, Octoparse**.
- Ethical considerations: Terms of Service, GDPR, rate limiting.

### Stage 2 — Text Pre-processing
Raw text is noisy. Pre-processing cleans it so that algorithms can interpret it.

- **Tokenization** — Splitting `"I loved the biryani!"` → `[I, loved, the, biryani]`.
- **Lowercasing** — `Biryani` and `biryani` treated as the same token.
- **Stop-word Removal** — Removing `the, a, an, is, of, at`.
- **Stemming** (Porter Stemmer) — `loved → love`, `running → run`.
- **Lemmatization** (WordNet) — `better → good` (dictionary-based, smarter than stemming).
- **Punctuation, number, and emoji handling** — Sometimes emoji are converted to sentiment labels (e.g., `😡` → negative weight).
- **Normalization** — Handling chat language: `gr8 → great`, `luv → love`.

### Stage 3 — Feature Representation / Extraction
Computers do not understand words; they understand numbers. Text must be converted into a numerical vector.

- **Bag of Words (BoW)** — Counts the frequency of each word. Loses word order.
- **Term Frequency (TF)** — Frequency of a term $t$ in document $d$.
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** — How *rare* a word is across the corpus. Common words get low weights, rare words get high weights.
- **TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)** — The product of TF and IDF. The most common statistical feature in opinion mining.
- **Word Embeddings** — Dense vector representations (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT) where semantically similar words sit close in vector space.

### Stage 4 — Opinion / Sentiment Classification
- **Lexicon-based approach** — Uses sentiment dictionaries (e.g., VADER, AFINN, Bing Liu lexicon). Words are pre-scored. Final polarity = sum of lexicon scores.
- **Machine Learning approach** — Supervised models: **Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest** trained on labelled reviews.
- **Deep Learning approach** — **LSTM, BERT, RoBERTa** — state-of-the-art contextual models.
- **Hybrid approach** — Combines lexicon + ML for better accuracy on informal text.

### Stage 5 — Aspect Extraction (for ABSA)
- Identifying *what* feature is being discussed: *battery life, delivery time, customer service, taste, packaging.*
- Techniques: **Rule-based (frequent noun phrases), Topic Modelling (LDA), or DL sequence labelling (NER).**

### Stage 6 — Visualization & Business Reporting
- **Word Clouds**, **Sentiment Trend Graphs**, **Aspect-wise Polarity Bar Charts**, **Geo-sentiment Heat Maps**, **Alert Dashboards** (Power BI, Tableau).

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

| Industry | Application of Opinion Text Mining |
|---|---|
| E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart) | Product review summarization, fake review detection |
| Hospitality (OYO, Airbnb) | Guest experience monitoring, location-based feedback |
| Finance | News sentiment for stock movement prediction (FinBERT) |
| Politics | Election opinion tracking, policy feedback analysis |
| Healthcare | Patient feedback analysis, drug review mining |
| Manufacturing | Warranty claim text mining, defect categorization |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Cheat Sheet — Memorise This Block Before the Exam.**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Term Frequency | $tf(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = raw count of term $t$ in document $d$ |
| Inverse Document Frequency | $idf(t,C) = \log \dfrac{\vert C \vert}{1 + \vert \{ d \in C : t \in d \} \vert}$ | $\vert C \vert$ = total docs, denominator = docs containing $t$ |
| TF-IDF Weight | $w(t,d,C) = tf(t,d) \times idf(t,C)$ | Scalar weight per term-document pair |
| Naive Bayes Posterior | $P(c \vert D) = \dfrac{P(D \vert c) \cdot P(c)}{P(D)}$ | $c$ = class, $D$ = document features |
| NB Likelihood (Multinomial) | $P(D \vert c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert c)^{count(w_i)}$ | $w_i$ = word, $P(w_i \vert c)$ = smoothed prob |
| Laplace Smoothing | $P(w \vert c) = \dfrac{count(w,c) + 1}{count(c) + \vert V \vert}$ | $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size |
| Polarity Score (Lexicon) | $S_{doc} = \dfrac{\sum_{w \in d} score(w)}{\vert d \vert}$ | Range: $[-1, +1]$ |
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $TP$ = True Positive, $FP$ = False Positive |
| Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $FN$ = False Negative |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ |
| Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $TN$ = True Negative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note on Formula Symbols:**
> In the KTU answer script, always **explicitly define every variable** before substituting. Examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark for un-defined variables in derivations.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Computing TF-IDF Weights from Scratch

Suppose we have a **mini-corpus** of 3 customer reviews (documents) for a newly launched smartphone:

- $d_1$ = *"battery is excellent battery is long lasting"*
- $d_2$ = *"camera quality is poor and battery drains fast"*
- $d_3$ = *"screen is excellent but price is too high"*

**Vocabulary** (unique words, after lowercasing & stop-word removal):
$V = \{ battery, excellent, long, lasting, camera, quality, poor, drains, fast, screen, price, high \}$

So $\vert V \vert = 12$.

We will compute the **TF-IDF weight for the word "battery"** in document $d_1$.

### Step 1 — Compute Term Frequency $tf(battery, d_1)$

We look at the raw text of $d_1$: *"battery is excellent battery is long lasting"*.

After removing stop words (`is`): *"battery excellent battery long lasting"*.

- Total non-stop words in $d_1$ = **5**.
- Occurrences of *battery* in $d_1$ = **2**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
tf(battery, d_1) &= \frac{f_{battery, d_1}}{\sum_{t' \in d_1} f_{t', d_1}} \\
&= \frac{2}{5} \\
&= 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* TF normalizes the raw count by the document length so that long and short documents can be compared fairly.

### Step 2 — Compute Inverse Document Frequency $idf(battery, C)$

We count in how many of the **3 documents** the word *battery* appears:

- $d_1$ → contains *battery* ✓
- $d_2$ → contains *battery* ✓
- $d_3$ → does NOT contain *battery* ✗

So $\vert \{ d \in C : battery \in d \} \vert = 2$.

Total documents $\vert C \vert = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf(battery, C) &= \log \frac{\vert C \vert}{1 + \vert \{ d \in C : battery \in d \} \vert} \\
&= \log \frac{3}{1 + 2} \\
&= \log \frac{3}{3} \\
&= \log(1) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The `+1` in the denominator is the **smoothing term** that prevents division by zero when a word appears in every document. Because *battery* appears in 2 out of 3 documents (a common word), its IDF is **0**, meaning it carries **no discriminative power** — it does not help us distinguish reviews from each other.

### Step 3 — Compute TF-IDF Weight

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(battery, d_1, C) &= tf(battery, d_1) \times idf(battery, C) \\
&= 0.4 \times 0 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* This is correct — *battery* is mentioned too often across the corpus (2 of 3 docs) to be a *distinguishing* keyword.

### Step 4 — Repeat for the Word "lasting" in $d_1$

- $tf(lasting, d_1) = 1/5 = 0.2$
- *lasting* appears in $d_1$ only → denominator = $1 + 1 = 2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf(lasting, C) &= \log \frac{3}{1+1} = \log(1.5) \approx 0.176 \\
w(lasting, d_1, C) &= 0.2 \times 0.176 = 0.0352
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* *lasting* is rare, so it gets a higher TF-IDF, meaning it is more useful in distinguishing $d_1$ from the others.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Naive Bayes Sentiment Classification with Laplace Smoothing

**Setup:** We have a tiny training corpus of 6 product reviews (3 positive, 3 negative) and want to classify a new review: *"great product but slow delivery"*.

| # | Review | Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | great product love it | POS |
| 2 | amazing quality fast | POS |
| 3 | excellent value great | POS |
| 4 | terrible quality slow | NEG |
| 5 | poor service bad | NEG |
| 6 | worst product slow delivery | NEG |

**Vocabulary $V$** (unique words): *great, product, love, amazing, quality, fast, excellent, value, terrible, slow, service, poor, bad, worst, delivery* → $\vert V \vert = 15$.

### Step 1 — Calculate Prior Probabilities

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(POS) &= \frac{3}{6} = 0.5 \\
P(NEG) &= \frac{3}{6} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing

For class POS, total words (with repetition) = 3+3+3 = 9 words.
For class NEG, total words = 3+3+3 = 9 words.

Using the Laplace smoothing formula:

$$
P(w \mid c) = \frac{count(w,c) + 1}{count(c) + \vert V \vert}
$$

- $P(great \mid POS) = (1+1)/(9+15) = 2/24 = 1/12$
- $P(product \mid POS) = (1+1)/(9+15) = 1/12$
- $P(slow \mid POS) = (0+1)/(9+15) = 1/24$
- $P(delivery \mid POS) = (0+1)/(9+15) = 1/24$

- $P(great \mid NEG) = (0+1)/(9+15) = 1/24$
- $P(product \mid NEG) = (1+1)/(9+15) = 1/12$
- $P(slow \mid NEG) = (2+1)/(9+15) = 3/24 = 1/8$
- $P(delivery \mid NEG) = (1+1)/(9+15) = 1/12$

### Step 3 — Compute Posterior Probabilities for the New Review *"great product but slow delivery"*

We ignore *but* as a stop word. The four content words are: *great, product, slow, delivery*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D \mid POS) &= P(great \mid POS) \cdot P(product \mid POS) \cdot P(slow \mid POS) \cdot P(delivery \mid POS) \\
&= \frac{1}{12} \times \frac{1}{12} \times \frac{1}{24} \times \frac{1}{24} \\
&= \frac{1}{82944} \approx 1.205 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D \mid NEG) &= P(great \mid NEG) \cdot P(product \mid NEG) \cdot P(slow \mid NEG) \cdot P(delivery \mid NEG) \\
&= \frac{1}{24} \times \frac{1}{12} \times \frac{1}{8} \times \frac{1}{12} \\
&= \frac{1}{27648} \approx 3.617 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiply the conditional word probabilities, since Naive Bayes assumes conditional independence between words given the class.

### Step 4 — Final Normalized Posterior (Class Decision)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(POS \mid D) &\propto P(D \mid POS) \cdot P(POS) = 1.205 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5 = 6.03 \times 10^{-6} \\
P(NEG \mid D) &\propto P(D \mid NEG) \cdot P(NEG) = 3.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5 = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P(NEG \mid D) > P(POS \mid D)$, the new review *"great product but slow delivery"* is classified as **NEGATIVE**.

*Conversion logic:* Even though the word *great* is positive, the *slow delivery* carries more weight in the training data, swinging the classification to NEGATIVE. This illustrates how aspect-specific words dominate.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Confusion Matrix & F1-Score

A Naive Bayes model is tested on **200 reviews**. The resulting confusion matrix is:

| Actual \ Predicted | Predicted POS | Predicted NEG |
|---|---|---|
| Actual POS | **TP = 80** | FN = 20 |
| Actual NEG | FP = 30 | **TN = 70** |

### Precision

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.727
$$

### Recall

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80
$$

### F1-Score

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.727 \times 0.80}{0.727 + 0.80} = \frac{1.1632}{1.527} \approx 0.762
$$

### Accuracy

$$
Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 70}{200} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75
$$

*Conversion logic:* Accuracy alone can be misleading on imbalanced datasets; F1 gives a balanced view of the classifier's reliability.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define text mining. How is it different from data mining?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Text mining* is the process of extracting meaningful patterns, knowledge, and insights from unstructured textual data using techniques from NLP, information retrieval, statistics, and machine learning.

*Difference from data mining:*
- **Data mining** operates on **structured, tabular data** (e.g., relational databases, spreadsheets) and uses mathematical/statistical models on numerical/categorical fields.
- **Text mining** operates on **unstructured, free-form text** (reviews, tweets, articles) and must first convert text into structured numerical form (via BoW, TF-IDF, embeddings) before applying any analysis.
- Text mining adds linguistic steps (tokenization, POS tagging, parsing) that data mining does not require.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clear distinction.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE pre-processing steps in text mining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Tokenization** — Splitting a sentence into individual words or sub-word units called tokens. *Example:* `"The phone is great"` → `[The, phone, is, great]`.
2. **Stop-word Removal** — Eliminating frequently occurring but semantically weak words like *the, a, an, is, of, in* to reduce noise and dimensionality.
3. **Stemming / Lemmatization** — Reducing words to their root form. Stemming is rule-based (e.g., *running → runn*), while lemmatization is dictionary-based and context-aware (e.g., *better → good*).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an opinion mining system with a neat block diagram. Discuss each component in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An opinion mining system consists of five interconnected layers, as described in Section 4.2 of these notes:

1. **Source Layer** — Opinion platforms (Amazon, Twitter, Zomato, Glassdoor, Reddit) generate raw unstructured text continuously.
2. **Ingestion Layer** — Streaming tools like **Apache Kafka** and **REST APIs** collect data in real time and push it into a staging database.
3. **Pre-processing Layer** — Tokenization, stop-word removal, lemmatization, and normalization (handling chat abbreviations like *gr8, lol*).
4. **Feature Extraction Layer** — Conversion to vectors using **BoW, TF-IDF, Word2Vec, or BERT embeddings**.
5. **Classification / Analytics Layer** — Lexicon-based, ML, or DL models label sentiment. Aspect extraction identifies *which feature* is being talked about.
6. **Visualization Layer** — Dashboards in Power BI/Tableau, alert systems, and word clouds communicate findings to decision-makers.

*(Diagrammatic representation: 2 marks; Layer-by-layer explanation: 5 marks = 7 total.)*

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **TF-IDF** technique. Compute the TF-IDF weight of the word *"delivery"* in the following mini-corpus: **(7 marks)**

| Doc | Content |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | fast delivery good packaging |
| $d_2$ | slow delivery bad packaging |
| $d_3$ | excellent taste and fast service |

**Model Solution:**

TF-IDF combines two statistics:
- **Term Frequency (TF):** How often a word appears in a document.
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** How rare a word is across the corpus.

The intuition: a word that appears often in one document *and* rarely in others is a strong indicator of what that document is about.

**Step 1 — Vocabulary and corpus size:**
Unique words (after stop-word removal): *fast, delivery, good, packaging, slow, bad, excellent, taste, service*.
$\vert V \vert = 9$, $\vert C \vert = 3$.

**Step 2 — Term Frequency of *delivery* in $d_1$:**
In $d_1$, total non-stop words = 4. Occurrences of *delivery* = 1.

$$
tf(delivery, d_1) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — IDF of *delivery*:**
*delivery* appears in $d_1$ and $d_2$ → 2 documents.

$$
idf(delivery, C) = \log \frac{3}{1 + 2} = \log(1) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — TF-IDF Weight:**

$$
w(delivery, d_1, C) = 0.25 \times 0 = 0
$$

*Interpretation:* Even though *delivery* is mentioned in $d_1$, it is too common across the corpus to carry discriminative weight — hence TF-IDF is 0.

*(Formula: 2 marks, Computation: 4 marks, Interpretation: 1 mark = 7 total.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes classifier** for sentiment analysis. Derive the classification rule and discuss **Laplace smoothing** with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem** with a *naive* assumption of conditional independence between features (words) given the class.

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(c \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(D)}
$$

where $c$ is the class (POS or NEG) and $D$ is the document (the bag of words).

**Likelihood expansion** (assuming word independence):

$$
P(D \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)
$$

**Laplace Smoothing** is added to handle words that did not appear in training:

$$
P(w \mid c) = \frac{count(w,c) + 1}{count(c) + \vert V \vert}
$$

The `+1` in the numerator and `+ |V|` in the denominator ensure no probability is ever zero, even for unseen words.

**Example:** If the training corpus for NEG class has 100 total words across all negative reviews, and the word *sarcastic* never appeared, then without smoothing $P(sarcastic \mid NEG) = 0$, which would zero out the entire posterior. With smoothing, $P(sarcastic \mid NEG) = (0+1)/(100 + \vert V \vert) = 1/(100 + \vert V \vert) > 0$.

**Classification Rule:** Choose the class $\hat{c}$ that maximizes the posterior:

$$
\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)
$$

*(Theorem: 2 marks, Smoothing explanation: 3 marks, Example: 2 marks = 7 total.)*

---

**(b)** A sentiment classifier is tested on **500 reviews**. The confusion matrix is:

| Actual \ Predicted | POS | NEG |
|---|---|---|
| POS | 180 | 20 |
| NEG | 30 | 270 |

Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** for the POSITIVE class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

From the matrix: $TP = 180$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 270$.

**Accuracy:**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90
$$

**Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} = 0.857
$$

**Recall:**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.857 \times 0.90}{0.857 + 0.90} = \frac{1.5426}{1.757} = 0.878
$$

*Interpretation:* The classifier has a balanced and high performance — F1 of 0.878 indicates robust handling of both false positives and false negatives.

*(Each metric: 1.5 marks + 0.5 interpretation = 7 total.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Forgetting to define variables** — In derivation questions, examiners check the first line. Always write *"Let $D$ be the document, $c$ the class, $w_i$ the i-th word..."* before substituting.
> 2. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — Precision answers *"Of all reviews I called positive, how many actually were?"* Recall answers *"Of all actual positives, how many did I catch?"*
> 3. **Skipping Laplace smoothing in NB** — A common KTU trick question: if the training set doesn't contain a word from the test set, the naive Bayes posterior becomes zero. Always mention smoothing.
> 4. **Writing the TF formula with raw counts only** — TF must be **normalized by document length**; otherwise long documents dominate.
> 5. **Listing algorithms without a block diagram** — In 7-mark architecture questions, marks are split ~2 for diagram and ~5 for explanation. Never submit text alone.
> 6. **Ignoring aspect-based analysis** — When asked for *opinion mining*, do not stop at document-level polarity. Mention **ABSA** (Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis) for full marks.
> 7. **Missing ethical/legal note** — In any question about scraping opinion platforms, add a one-line note on **GDPR, IT Act 2000 (India), and platform Terms of Service** — examiners reward this awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Text mining extracts patterns from unstructured text; opinion mining is a specialized sub-field focusing on sentiment.
- **Three Core Tasks:** Sentiment classification, subjectivity classification, aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA).
- **Pipeline (6 stages):** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Classification → Aspect Mining → Visualization.
- **Pre-processing steps:** Tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming, lemmatization, normalization.
- **Feature Methods:** BoW (simple), TF-IDF (weighted), Word2Vec/GloVe (static embeddings), BERT (contextual embeddings).
- **Classification Approaches:** Lexicon-based (VADER, AFINN), Classical ML (Naive Bayes, SVM), Deep Learning (LSTM, BERT).
- **Key Formulas:** TF, IDF, TF-IDF, Naive Bayes Posterior with Laplace Smoothing, Polarity Score, Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy.
- **Naive Bayes Assumption:** Conditional independence of words given the class — a strong but empirically effective assumption.
- **Laplace Smoothing Formula:** $(count + 1) / (total + \vert V \vert)$ — never write a zero probability.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Always use **F1-Score** for imbalanced datasets; accuracy alone is misleading.
- **Visualization Tools:** Power BI, Tableau, Word Clouds, Geo-sentiment Heat Maps.
- **Real-World Use Cases:** Brand monitoring, product review summarization, election analysis, stock prediction, patient feedback.
- **Ethics:** Respect platform APIs, GDPR, anonymization, and consent.
- **Diagram must-knows:** Pipeline flow, ABSA architecture, sentiment classification process.
- **High-Yield Exam Triggers:** "Explain TF-IDF with example", "Compare lexicon vs ML approach", "Naive Bayes for sentiment", "Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis", "Compute F1 from confusion matrix".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** If the question gives you a confusion matrix, always compute **all four** metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1) — even if only one is asked. Examiners award bonus marks for completeness, and it serves as a verification check (e.g., F1 should be between Precision and Recall).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic Driven Predictive Models - Single-Period Purchase Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Logic Driven Predictive Models - Single-Period Purchase Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Period Purchase Decision (Newsvendor Problem):** A classic stochastic inventory model in which a decision-maker must place exactly **one** purchase order for a perishable or time-sensitive product before observing the actual random demand $D$, with the objective of maximizing the **expected profit** for that single selling period. The optimal order quantity $Q^{*}$ is derived using a *logic-driven predictive model* — a deterministic analytical expression that converts the probability distribution of demand into a single decision rule (the Critical Ratio).

A **Logic-Driven Predictive Model** in this context is *not* a black-box machine-learning algorithm. It is an **if–then logic rule** built from first principles of probability, cost structure, and demand distribution. The canonical rule is:

> *Order the smallest quantity $Q$ such that the probability that demand meets or exceeds $Q$ is at least equal to the Critical Ratio.*

Formally, the optimal $Q^{*}$ satisfies:

$$P(D \leq Q^{*}) \geq \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}}$$

where $C_{u}$ is the **cost of understocking** (lost margin per shortage unit) and $C_{o}$ is the **cost of overstocking** (loss per leftover unit).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Newspaper Vendor Analogy**
> Imagine a street vendor who must decide — *every morning, before knowing how many readers will pass by* — how many copies of the day's newspaper to buy from the publisher. Each unsold copy at night is worthless (recycled). Each missed customer is a lost sale. There is **no second chance**: no re-ordering at noon.
>
> * Buy too few → lost profit (underage cost).
> * Buy too many → wasted money on leftovers (overage cost).
>
> The vendor uses a **logic rule**: *"I will keep ordering additional copies as long as the chance of selling the next copy exceeds the chance of losing money on it."* That single rule, expressed as the Critical Ratio, is the essence of a logic-driven predictive model for a single-period purchase decision.

**Real-world KTU examples** (perishable / seasonal / one-shot):
- **Fashion retailer** ordering winter coats in October.
- **Bakery** baking Valentine's Day special cakes.
- **Airline** selling seats close to departure on a single flight leg.
- **Pharmacy** stocking vaccines with a fixed expiry date.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Order Quantity | $Q$ | Units purchased before demand is realized |
| Random Demand | $D$ | Stochastic demand for the period |
| Purchase Cost | $c$ | Cost paid per unit ordered |
| Selling Price | $p$ | Revenue per unit sold |
| Salvage Value | $s$ | Recovery value of one leftover unit ($s \leq c$) |
| Underage Cost | $C_{u}$ | Profit lost per unit of unmet demand |
| Overage Cost | $C_{o}$ | Loss incurred per unit of unsold inventory |
| Critical Ratio | $CR$ | $C_{u} / (C_{u} + C_{o})$ |
| Service Level | $SL$ | Probability of not stocking out: $P(D \leq Q)$ |
| Expected Profit | $E[\pi]$ | Mean profit across all demand outcomes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain — How a Logic-Driven Predictive Model Works

The model proceeds through **five deterministic logic steps**:

1. **Identify the cost structure.** Compute the underage cost (lost margin) and overage cost (loss on leftovers).
2. **Specify the predictive distribution.** Choose or estimate the demand distribution $f(d)$ or $F(d)$ from historical data or judgment.
3. **Compute the Critical Ratio (CR).** This is the *decision threshold* — the smallest acceptable probability of "covering" demand.
4. **Apply the inverse CDF (quantile rule).** Solve for $Q^{*} = F^{-1}(CR)$ in continuous cases, or the smallest integer $Q$ with $F(Q) \geq CR$ in discrete cases.
5. **Validate by marginal analysis.** Confirm that the *next* unit ordered has positive expected marginal profit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the Critical Ratio work?**
> For each *additional* unit beyond $Q$, the chance you can sell it equals $P(D > Q)$. Selling it yields $C_{u}$ (extra profit). The chance you cannot sell it equals $P(D \leq Q)$, costing $C_{o}$. The unit is worth ordering only if its expected marginal contribution is non-negative:
>
> $$C_{u} \cdot P(D > Q) - C_{o} \cdot P(D \leq Q) \geq 0$$
>
> Rearranging gives $P(D \leq Q) \geq C_{u} / (C_{u} + C_{o})$. This single inequality is the **logic-driven predictive model**.

## 2.2 Cost Formulas (Standard Identities)

$$C_{u} \;=\; p - c \quad \text{(lost margin per shortage unit)}$$

$$C_{o} \;=\; c - s \quad \text{(loss per leftover unit)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case — no salvage value ($s = 0$):** The overage cost collapses to $C_{o} = c$, i.e., the entire purchase cost is written off as a loss for any unsold unit. This is the classical newspaper vendor case.

## 2.3 Continuous-Demand Optimal Order Quantity

For a continuous demand distribution with CDF $F(\cdot)$:

$$Q^{*} \;=\; F^{-1}\!\left(\frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}}\right)$$

For the frequently tested **Normal distribution** $D \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$:

$$Q^{*} \;=\; \mu + z_{\alpha}\,\sigma, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}} \text{ and } z_{\alpha} = \Phi^{-1}(\alpha)$$

For the **Poisson distribution** $D \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, use the smallest integer $Q$ satisfying:

$$\sum_{d=0}^{Q} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{d}}{d!} \;\geq\; \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}}$$

## 2.4 Discrete-Demand Optimal Order Quantity

For a discrete demand distribution with PMF $P(D = d)$, the **marginal-analysis table** approach is used:

| Unit # $n$ | $P(D \geq n)$ | Marginal Profit $C_{u}$ | Marginal Loss $C_{o}$ | Order? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(D \geq 1)$ | $C_{u}$ | $C_{o}$ | Yes if $P(D \geq 1) > C_{o}/(C_{u}+C_{o})$ |
| 2 | $P(D \geq 2)$ | $C_{u}$ | $C_{o}$ | Yes if $P(D \geq 2) > C_{o}/(C_{u}+C_{o})$ |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Rule:** Order unit $n$ if and only if $P(D \geq n) \geq C_{o} / (C_{u} + C_{o})$.

## 2.5 Expected Profit Function

For discrete demand $\{d_{1}, d_{2}, \dots, d_{k}\}$ with probabilities $p_{i}$:

$$E[\pi(Q)] \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{i}\,\bigl[\,p\,\min(Q, d_{i}) \;+\; s\,(Q - d_{i})^{+} \;-\; c\,Q\,\bigr]$$

For continuous demand:

$$E[\pi(Q)] \;=\; p\!\int_{0}^{Q}\! d\,f(d)\,dd \;+\; p\,Q\!\int_{Q}^{\infty}\! f(d)\,dd \;+\; s\!\int_{0}^{Q}\!(Q-d)\,f(d)\,dd \;-\; c\,Q$$

A more compact equivalent form uses the **expected shortage** $L(Q) = E[(D-Q)^{+}]$:

$$E[\pi(Q)] \;=\; (p - s)\,\mathbb{E}[\min(D, Q)] \;+\; s\,\mathbb{E}[D] \;-\; c\,Q \;=\; (p-c)\,Q \;-\; (p-s)\,L(Q)$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{u} = p - c$ | Underage cost per unit |
| 2 | $C_{o} = c - s$ | Overage cost per unit |
| 3 | $CR = C_{u}/(C_{u} + C_{o})$ | Critical Ratio — decision threshold |
| 4 | $Q^{*} = F^{-1}(CR)$ | Optimal order qty (continuous) |
| 5 | $Q^{*} = \mu + z_{CR}\,\sigma$ | Optimal order qty (Normal demand) |
| 6 | $\min\{Q : P(D \leq Q) \geq CR\}$ | Optimal order qty (discrete) |
| 7 | Order unit $n$ iff $P(D \geq n) \geq C_{o}/(C_{u}+C_{o})$ | Marginal rule |
| 8 | $E[\pi(Q)] = (p-c)Q - (p-s)L(Q)$ | Expected profit using expected shortage |
| 9 | $SL = P(D \leq Q) = F(Q)$ | Type-1 service level (fill rate proxy) |
| 10 | Fill Rate $= 1 - L(Q)/\mathbb{E}[D]$ | Fraction of demand met from stock |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility beyond inventory:** This exact same logic-driven framework is used in **reliability engineering** (optimal preventive replacement), **machine learning** (cost-sensitive classification thresholds), and **capacity planning** (cloud server provisioning). Always write the Critical Ratio first — it is the universal logic gate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Critical Ratio

**Step 1 — Expected marginal profit of the $n$-th unit ordered.**

Suppose we currently hold $Q-1$ units and consider ordering one more. Two mutually exclusive outcomes occur in the period:

- With probability $P(D \geq Q)$, demand absorbs the unit → we earn an extra $C_{u} = p - c$.
- With probability $P(D < Q)$, the unit remains unsold → we lose $C_{o} = c - s$.

Therefore the expected marginal profit (EMP) of the $n$-th unit is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMP}(Q) &\;=\; C_{u} \cdot P(D \geq Q) \;-\; C_{o} \cdot P(D < Q) \\
&\;=\; C_{u} \cdot \bigl[\,1 - P(D < Q)\,\bigr] \;-\; C_{o} \cdot P(D < Q) \\
&\;=\; C_{u} \;-\; (C_{u} + C_{o})\,P(D < Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Optimality condition.**

Order the $n$-th unit only if $\text{EMP}(Q) \geq 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{u} - (C_{u} + C_{o})\,P(D < Q) &\;\geq\; 0 \\
(C_{u} + C_{o})\,P(D < Q) &\;\leq\; C_{u} \\
P(D < Q) &\;\leq\; \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}} \;=\; CR
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final logic rule (canonical form).**

$$
P(D \leq Q^{*}) \;\geq\; \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad Q^{*} \;=\; \min\bigl\{Q : F(Q) \geq CR\bigr\}
$$

Equivalently, the **marginal form**:

$$
P(D \geq n) \;\geq\; \frac{C_{o}}{C_{u} + C_{o}} \quad \text{for each unit } n \text{ to be ordered}
$$

This is the **logic-driven predictive rule** the KTU examiner expects.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Normal Demand

**Problem:** A KTU-campus canteen must decide how many fresh juice bottles to order for one day. Data:

- Purchase cost $c = 20$ INR / bottle
- Selling price $p = 50$ INR / bottle
- Salvage value $s = 5$ INR / bottle (returnable bottle)
- Demand $D \sim N(\mu = 200, \sigma = 30)$

**Step 1 — Compute costs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{u} &\;=\; p - c \;=\; 50 - 20 \;=\; 30 \text{ INR/unit} \\
C_{o} &\;=\; c - s \;=\; 20 - 5 \;=\; 15 \text{ INR/unit} \\
CR &\;=\; \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}} \;=\; \frac{30}{30 + 15} \;=\; \frac{30}{45} \;=\; 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Find $z_{CR}$.**

From the standard normal table, $P(Z \leq 0.43) \approx 0.6664$, so $z_{0.6667} \approx 0.4307$.

> *For KTU exam purposes, accepting $z_{CR} \approx 0.43$ earns full marks.*

**Step 3 — Compute $Q^{*}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*} &\;=\; \mu + z_{CR}\,\sigma \\
&\;=\; 200 + 0.43 \times 30 \\
&\;=\; 200 + 12.9 \\
&\;=\; 212.9 \;\approx\; 213 \text{ bottles (round up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute expected profit at $Q^{*} = 213$.**

Expected shortage $L(Q) = \sigma\bigl[\varphi(z) - z\,(1 - \Phi(z))\bigr]$ where $z = (Q-\mu)/\sigma = 13/30 = 0.4333$.

Using the standard normal loss function $L(z) = \varphi(z) - z\,(1 - \Phi(z))$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(0.43) &\;\approx\; 0.3637 - 0.43 \times (1 - 0.6664) \\
&\;\approx\; 0.3637 - 0.43 \times 0.3336 \\
&\;\approx\; 0.3637 - 0.1434 \\
&\;\approx\; 0.2203
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $L(Q) = \sigma \times L(z) = 30 \times 0.2203 \approx 6.61$ bottles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\pi] &\;=\; (p - c)\,Q \;-\; (p - s)\,L(Q) \\
&\;=\; (50 - 20)(213) \;-\; (50 - 5)(6.61) \\
&\;=\; 30 \times 213 \;-\; 45 \times 6.61 \\
&\;=\; 6390 - 297.45 \\
&\;\approx\; 6092.55 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Discrete Demand (Marginal Analysis)

**Problem:** A bookstore must order copies of a KTU reference book. Demand distribution:

| Demand $d$ | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 | 90 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(D=d)$ | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.35 | 0.25 | 0.10 |

- Purchase cost $c = 200$ INR, Selling price $p = 350$ INR, Salvage $s = 100$ INR.

**Step 1 — Costs and threshold.**

$$
C_{u} = 350 - 200 = 150, \quad C_{o} = 200 - 100 = 100, \quad \frac{C_{o}}{C_{u}+C_{o}} = \frac{100}{250} = 0.40
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(D \geq n)$ for each candidate order level.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D \geq 50) &\;=\; 1.00, \quad P(D \geq 60) = 0.90, \quad P(D \geq 70) = 0.70 \\
P(D \geq 80) &\;=\; 0.35, \quad P(D \geq 90) = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Marginal rule: order unit $n$ while $P(D \geq n) \geq 0.40$.**

| Unit from $Q$ | $P(D \geq \cdot)$ | $\geq 0.40$? | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1–50 (50 units) | $\geq 0.40$ (all $\geq 0.40$) | ✓ | Order |
| 51–70 | $\geq 0.70$ | ✓ | Order |
| 71–80 | $0.35$ | ✗ | **Stop** |

**Result:** $Q^{*} = 70$ copies.

**Step 4 — Compute expected profit at $Q^{*}=70$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\pi(70)] &\;=\; \sum_{d} P(d)\,\bigl[\,p\,\min(70,d) + s\,(70-d)^{+} - c\cdot 70\,\bigr] \\
&\;=\; 0.10\,(50\cdot350 - 70\cdot200 + 20\cdot100) \\
&\;\;\;+\; 0.20\,(60\cdot350 - 70\cdot200 + 10\cdot100) \\
&\;\;\;+\; 0.35\,(70\cdot350 - 70\cdot200) \\
&\;\;\;+\; 0.25\,(70\cdot350 + 0 - 70\cdot200) \\
&\;\;\;+\; 0.10\,(70\cdot350 + 0 - 70\cdot200)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term } d=50&: 0.10 \times (17500 - 14000 + 2000) = 0.10 \times 5500 = 550 \\
\text{Term } d=60&: 0.20 \times (21000 - 14000 + 1000) = 0.20 \times 8000 = 1600 \\
\text{Term } d=70&: 0.35 \times (24500 - 14000) = 0.35 \times 10500 = 3675 \\
\text{Term } d=80&: 0.25 \times 10500 = 2625 \\
\text{Term } d=90&: 0.10 \times 10500 = 1050
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E[\pi(70)] \;=\; 550 + 1600 + 3675 + 2625 + 1050 \;=\; 9500 \text{ INR}
$$

## 3.4 Expected-Profit Verification Across $Q$

To confirm $Q^{*}=70$ is optimum, tabulate $E[\pi(Q)]$ for $Q \in \{60, 70, 80\}$:

| $Q$ | $E[\pi]$ (INR) |
|---|---|
| 60 | 8 800 |
| **70** | **9 500** ← max |
| 80 | 8 750 |

Optimal confirmed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision-Flow Architecture of the Logic-Driven Predictive Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Single-Period Decision] --> B[Step 1: Gather Inputs]
    B --> B1[Purchase cost c]
    B --> B2[Selling price p]
    B --> B3[Salvage value s]
    B --> B4[Demand distribution F of D]
    B1 --> C[Step 2: Compute Cost Structure]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Cu = p minus c]
    C --> C2[Co = c minus s]
    C1 --> D[Step 3: Compute Critical Ratio]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[CR = Cu divided by Cu plus Co]
    D1 --> E[Step 4: Apply Predictive Rule]
    E --> E1{Is Demand Continuous?}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Q star equals F inverse of CR]
    E1 -- No --> F2[Find smallest Q with F of Q greater or equal CR]
    F1 --> G[Step 5: Compute Expected Profit]
    F2 --> G
    G --> G1[E of pi of Q equals p minus c times Q minus p minus s times L of Q]
    G1 --> H[Step 6: Sensitivity Check]
    H --> H1[Vary CR by plus or minus 10 percent]
    H --> H2[Re-evaluate Q star and E of pi]
    H1 --> I[OUTPUT: Q star and Expected Profit]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[END]
```

## 4.2 Marginal-Analysis Decision Tree (Per-Unit Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Consider ordering unit n] --> Q1{Is n less than or equal to Q current?}
    Q1 -- No --> STOP1[Stop: Do not order more]
    Q1 -- Yes --> C1[Compute P of D greater than or equal to n]
    C1 --> C2[Compute threshold T equals Co divided by Cu plus Co]
    C2 --> D1{Is P of D greater than or equal to n greater than or equal to T?}
    D1 -- Yes --> ORDER1[Order unit n: expected marginal profit is non-negative]
    D1 -- No --> STOP2[Reject unit n: expected marginal loss dominates]
    ORDER1 --> NEXT[Increment n by 1]
    NEXT --> N1
    STOP1 --> DONE[OUTPUT: Q star equals n minus 1]
    STOP2 --> DONE
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Inputs → Model → Outputs)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        I1[Cost Parameters: c, p, s]
        I2[Demand Data: Historical Sales]
        I3[Distribution Fit: F of d]
    end
    subgraph MODEL[LOGIC-DRIVEN PREDICTIVE MODEL]
        M1[Cost Engine: Cu and Co]
        M2[Critical Ratio Generator]
        M3[Quantile Solver: F inverse of CR]
        M4[Marginal Analyzer]
        M5[Expected Profit Calculator]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        O1[Optimal Order Quantity Q star]
        O2[Expected Profit E of pi]
        O3[Service Level SL]
        O4[Fill Rate FR]
    end
    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> M3
    I3 --> M4
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M2 --> M4
    M3 --> M5
    M4 --> M5
    M5 --> O1
    M5 --> O2
    M3 --> O3
    M5 --> O4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the **Critical Ratio** in the context of a single-period purchase decision. State its formula and interpret each term.  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Critical Ratio (CR) is the probability threshold used in a logic-driven predictive model to determine the optimal order quantity in a single-period (newsvendor) decision.
>
> $$CR \;=\; \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}}$$
>
> where $C_{u} = p - c$ is the **underage cost** (lost margin per shortage unit) and $C_{o} = c - s$ is the **overage cost** (loss per leftover unit). The optimal order quantity $Q^{*}$ is the smallest integer satisfying $P(D \leq Q^{*}) \geq CR$. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 interpretation)*

### Q2. Distinguish between **underage cost** and **overage cost** in inventory decisions. Give one real-world example of each.  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Underage cost ($C_{u}$):** The opportunity loss incurred when a unit of demand cannot be met because inventory is insufficient. Example: A baker loses ₹80 margin when a customer cannot be served because the cake is sold out.
>
> **Overage cost ($C_{o}$):** The actual loss incurred when an unsold unit remains in inventory at the end of the period. Example: The same baker loses ₹50 on each unsold cake that must be discarded.
>
> In formula terms, $C_{u} = p - c$ and $C_{o} = c - s$. *(3 marks: 1.5 each concept)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Continuous Normal-Demand Newsvendor `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

A retail store must order a single batch of seasonal umbrellas before the monsoon. Estimated demand is **Normal with mean $\mu = 500$ units and standard deviation $\sigma = 80$ units**.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Purchase cost ($c$) | ₹ 150 |
| Selling price ($p$) | ₹ 280 |
| Salvage value ($s$) | ₹ 50 |

**(a)** Compute the underage cost, overage cost, and the Critical Ratio. Determine the optimal order quantity $Q^{*}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the expected profit at $Q^{*}$ and the achieved service level. If management demands a 95 % service level, recompute the new $Q$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Cost structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{u} &\;=\; p - c \;=\; 280 - 150 \;=\; \text{₹ }130 \\
C_{o} &\;=\; c - s \;=\; 150 - 50 \;=\; \text{₹ }100 \\
CR &\;=\; \frac{C_{u}}{C_{u} + C_{o}} \;=\; \frac{130}{130 + 100} \;=\; \frac{130}{230} \;\approx\; 0.5652
\end{aligned}
$$

From the standard normal table, $z_{0.5652} \approx 0.166$.

$$
Q^{*} \;=\; \mu + z_{CR}\,\sigma \;=\; 500 + 0.166 \times 80 \;=\; 500 + 13.28 \;\approx\; 514 \text{ units}
$$

**(b)** Expected profit:

With $z = (514 - 500)/80 = 0.175$, the standard normal loss function $L(z) = \varphi(z) - z(1 - \Phi(z)) \approx 0.3925 - 0.175 \times 0.4306 \approx 0.3171$.

$$
L(Q) \;=\; \sigma \cdot L(z) \;=\; 80 \times 0.3171 \;\approx\; 25.37 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\pi] &\;=\; (p - c)\,Q \;-\; (p - s)\,L(Q) \\
&\;=\; 130 \times 514 \;-\; 230 \times 25.37 \\
&\;=\; 66\,820 - 5\,835 \\
&\;\approx\; \text{₹ }60\,985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Service Level:** $SL = P(D \leq Q^{*}) = \Phi(z_{CR}) = CR \approx 0.5652$ (≈ **56.5 %**).

**For 95 % service level:** $z_{0.95} = 1.645$.

$$
Q_{0.95} \;=\; 500 + 1.645 \times 80 \;=\; 500 + 131.6 \;\approx\; 632 \text{ units}
$$

> *Trade-off:* Increasing the service level from 56.5 % to 95 % raises the order from 514 to 632 units (≈ +23 %), illustrating the cost of higher customer satisfaction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Discrete Demand with Marginal Analysis `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

A campus bookstore orders KTU-scheme textbooks. The discrete demand distribution is:

| Demand $d$ | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(D=d)$ | 0.15 | 0.25 | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.10 |

Cost data: $c = ₹ 250$, $p = ₹ 400$, $s = ₹ 100$.

**(a)** Using the marginal-analysis method, determine the optimal order quantity $Q^{*}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the expected profit at $Q^{*}$ and verify it is the maximum by comparing with $Q = 200$ and $Q = 250$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Cost structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{u} &\;=\; 400 - 250 \;=\; \text{₹ }150 \\
C_{o} &\;=\; 250 - 100 \;=\; \text{₹ }150 \\
\frac{C_{o}}{C_{u} + C_{o}} &\;=\; \frac{150}{300} \;=\; 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute cumulative probabilities:

| $Q$ | $F(Q) = P(D \leq Q)$ | $P(D \geq Q+1)$ | $P(D \geq Q+1) \geq 0.50$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0.15 | 0.85 | ✓ |
| 150 | 0.40 | 0.60 | ✓ |
| 200 | 0.70 | 0.30 | ✗ |

Order units while $P(D \geq n) \geq 0.50$. The 201st unit onward has $P(D \geq 201) = 0.30 < 0.50$. Hence $Q^{*} = 200$ books.

**(b)** Expected profit at $Q^{*} = 200$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\pi(200)] &\;=\; \sum_{d} P(d)\,\bigl[\,p\,\min(200,d) + s\,(200-d)^{+} - 200c\,\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

| $d$ | $P(d)$ | Sales | Salvage | Cost | Profit (₹) | $P \times$ Profit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0.15 | $100 \times 400 = 40\,000$ | $100 \times 100 = 10\,000$ | $200 \times 250 = 50\,000$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 150 | 0.25 | $150 \times 400 = 60\,000$ | $50 \times 100 = 5\,000$ | $50\,000$ | $15\,000$ | $3\,750$ |
| 200 | 0.30 | $80\,000$ | $0$ | $50\,000$ | $30\,000$ | $9\,000$ |
| 250 | 0.20 | $80\,000$ | $0$ | $50\,000$ | $30\,000$ | $6\,000$ |
| 300 | 0.10 | $80\,000$ | $0$ | $50\,000$ | $30\,000$ | $3\,000$ |

$$
E[\pi(200)] \;=\; 0 + 3\,750 + 9\,000 + 6\,000 + 3\,000 \;=\; \text{₹ }21\,750
$$

For comparison:

- $E[\pi(150)] = 0 \cdot 0.15 + 15\,000 \cdot 0.40 + 30\,000 \cdot 0.45 = 0 + 6\,000 + 13\,500 = \text{₹ }19\,500$
- $E[\pi(250)] = 0 \cdot 0.15 + 15\,000 \cdot 0.25 + 30\,000 \cdot 0.30 + 30\,000 \cdot 0.30 = 0 + 3\,750 + 9\,000 + 9\,000 = \text{₹ }21\,750$

So $Q^{*}=200$ ties with $Q=250$. In case of tie, the lower order quantity (less working capital tied up, less risk) is preferred — a standard KTU-evaluation convention. **Optimal $Q^{*} = 200$ books.**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize (–2 to –3 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing $C_{u}$ and $C_{o}$.** Underage = lost *opportunity* (selling price minus cost). Overage = actual *loss* on leftovers (cost minus salvage). Mixing them inverts the Critical Ratio and produces a wildly wrong $Q^{*}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to round $Q$ up.** Demand must be met; examiners expect the ceiling (smallest integer $\geq$ continuous optimum).
> 3. **Using the wrong tail probability.** $CR$ corresponds to $P(D \leq Q)$, NOT $P(D \geq Q)$. Students often flip this and order twice as much.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step.** Always tabulate $E[\pi(Q-1)]$, $E[\pi(Q)]$, $E[\pi(Q+1)]$ to prove optimality — KTU awards marks for this.
> 5. **Service-level trap.** A 95 % service level is NOT the same as the Critical Ratio solution. They differ unless $C_{u}=C_{o}$.
> 6. **No units in the answer.** Every numerical value must carry its unit (INR, units, %).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Single-period purchase decision** = newsvendor problem = one-shot ordering under stochastic demand.
- **Two cost types:** Underage $C_{u} = p - c$ (opportunity loss) and Overage $C_{o} = c - s$ (realized loss).
- **Critical Ratio $CR = C_{u}/(C_{u}+C_{o})$** is the decision threshold — the *single number* that converts the demand distribution into a logic rule.
- **Continuous demand:** $Q^{*} = F^{-1}(CR)$; for Normal demand $Q^{*} = \mu + z_{CR}\sigma$.
- **Discrete demand:** $Q^{*} = \min\{Q : F(Q) \geq CR\}$ OR equivalent marginal rule — order unit $n$ iff $P(D \geq n) \geq C_{o}/(C_{u}+C_{o})$.
- **Expected profit identity:** $E[\pi(Q)] = (p - c)Q - (p - s)L(Q)$ where $L(Q)$ is the expected normal loss.
- **Service level** $SL = P(D \leq Q) = F(Q)$; **Fill rate** $= 1 - L(Q)/\mathbb{E}[D]$.
- **Always verify** optimality by computing $E[\pi]$ at neighbouring $Q$ values.
- **Decision tree view:** for each additional unit, accept if expected marginal profit $\geq 0$.
- **KTU-favourite extensions:** sensitivity analysis, salvage-value variations, demand-distribution fitting, multi-product extensions, service-level constraints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Time Period Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multiple Time Period Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Time Period Models** are a class of quantitative forecasting and analytical techniques used in Business Analytics that explicitly model, decompose, and project a variable of interest across **more than one future time horizon**, while accounting for the temporal dependencies, trends, and seasonal patterns that exist across the observed historical periods.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these models fall under **Time Series Forecasting** and include:
- **Naïve** and **Average** methods
- **Moving Averages** (Simple and Weighted)
- **Exponential Smoothing** — Simple, Holt's Linear, and Holt-Winters
- **Decomposition Models** (Additive and Multiplicative)
- **Autoregressive (AR), Moving Average (MA), and ARIMA** models
- **Seasonal Index** based forecasting

The defining feature is the explicit treatment of **autocorrelation** — the idea that observations in period *t* are statistically dependent on observations in periods *t-1*, *t-2*, …, *t-k*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine you run a small tea stall in Kerala. You notice that during *Onam* and *Vishu*, your sales triple, while in the *monsoon months of June-July*, they drop by 30%. If a customer asks, "How many cups of tea will you sell over the **next 12 months**?" — you cannot answer by just looking at yesterday's sale. You need to look at the **pattern of the last 3-4 years** to project the **next multiple periods** correctly.
>
> **Multiple Time Period Models** are exactly this: instead of asking "what happens tomorrow?", we ask "what happens over the next *k* periods?" and we use **mathematical machinery** to capture trend (are sales growing year on year?), seasonality (peaks every December), and noise (random fluctuations).

## 1.3 Core Components of a Time Series

A univariate time series $Y_t$ is mathematically decomposed as:

$$Y_t = f(T_t, S_t, C_t, I_t)$$

Where:
- $T_t$ = **Trend** component (long-term direction)
- $S_t$ = **Seasonal** component (regular periodic fluctuations)
- $C_t$ = **Cyclical** component (long-term oscillations, e.g., business cycles)
- $I_t$ = **Irregular / Random** component (unexplained noise)

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction:** Seasonality is **predictable** (fixed period, e.g., 12 months), while Cyclical is **unpredictable in length** (recession/boom cycles). Always remember this — examiners love this distinction.

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Mapping

This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4**: *Apply time series and forecasting models to solve business decision problems.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Multiple Time Period Models

The models are classified into **four families**:

| Family | Methods | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
