| **Naïve Family** | Naïve, Average, Drift | No clear pattern; baseline benchmarks |
| **Smoothing Family** | Simple MA, Weighted MA, Single ES | Stationary data, no trend/seasonality |
| **Trend-Corrected** | Holt's Linear Method, Damped Trend | Data has trend but no seasonality |
| **Seasonal Models** | Holt-Winters, Decomposition | Data has both trend and seasonality |
| **Stochastic** | AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA | Strong autocorrelation structure |

## 2.2 Family 1 — Naïve and Average Methods

**Naïve Forecast:**
$$F_{t+1} = Y_t$$

**Average Forecast:**
$$F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

**Drift Method:**
$$F_{t+h} = Y_t + h \cdot \frac{(Y_t - Y_1)}{(t-1)}$$

## 2.3 Family 2 — Moving Averages

**Simple Moving Average (SMA) of order *m*:**
$$F_{t+1} = \frac{Y_t + Y_{t-1} + \cdots + Y_{t-m+1}}{m} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1} Y_{t-i}$$

**Weighted Moving Average (WMA):**
$$F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} w_i \cdot Y_{t-i} \quad \text{where} \quad \sum w_i = 1$$

## 2.4 Family 3 — Exponential Smoothing

The cornerstone of multiple period forecasting. Each method has **three equations**: Level, Trend (optional), and Seasonal (optional).

### 2.4.1 Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
$$L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha) L_{t-1}$$
$$F_{t+h} = L_t$$

Where $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the **smoothing constant**.

### 2.4.2 Holt's Linear (Double) Exponential Smoothing
$$\text{Level: } L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$
$$\text{Trend: } T_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}$$
$$F_{t+h} = L_t + h \cdot T_t$$

Where $\alpha, \beta \in [0,1]$.

### 2.4.3 Holt-Winters Seasonal Method
**Additive Seasonality** (period = *s*):
$$L_t = \alpha (Y_t - S_{t-s}) + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$
$$T_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}$$
$$S_t = \gamma (Y_t - L_t) + (1-\gamma) S_{t-s}$$
$$F_{t+h} = L_t + h \cdot T_t + S_{t+h-s}$$

**Multiplicative Seasonality:**
$$L_t = \alpha \frac{Y_t}{S_{t-s}} + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$
$$T_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}$$
$$S_t = \gamma \frac{Y_t}{L_t} + (1-\gamma) S_{t-s}$$
$$F_{t+h} = (L_t + h \cdot T_t) \cdot S_{t+h-s}$$

## 2.5 Family 4 — Decomposition Models

Used to extract seasonal indices and then forecast.

**Step 1 — Calculate Centered Moving Average (CMA)** for period *s*:
$$CMA_t = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-s/2} + Y_{t-s/2+1} + \cdots + Y_{t+s/2-1} + 0.5 Y_{t+s/2}}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Detrended Series:**
- Additive: $D_t = Y_t - CMA_t$
- Multiplicative: $D_t = Y_t / CMA_t$

**Step 3 — Seasonal Index** (averaging detrended values for each season):
$$SI_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j} D_{jk} \quad \text{(for season } k\text{)}$$

Normalize so indices sum to *s* (additive) or to *s* (multiplicative averages to 1).

**Step 4 — Deseasonalized Series:**
$$DS_t = Y_t - SI_k \quad \text{(additive)} \quad \text{or} \quad DS_t = Y_t / SI_k \quad \text{(multiplicative)}$$

**Step 5 — Forecast using regression on $DS_t$**, then re-seasonalize.

## 2.6 Forecast Accuracy Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **MAD** (Mean Absolute Deviation) | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert Y_t - F_t \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $\frac{1}{n}\sum (Y_t - F_t)^2$ | Penalizes large errors |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{MSE}$ | Same units as data |
| **MAPE** (Mean Absolute % Error) | $\frac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{Y_t - F_t}{Y_t} \right\vert$ | Scale-independent comparison |
| **MPE** (Mean % Error) | $\frac{100}{n}\sum \frac{Y_t - F_t}{Y_t}$ | Detects bias |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using MAPE, if any $Y_t = 0$, MAPE is undefined. Also MAPE is biased toward forecasts that **under-predict**. Always specify which metric you are using and why.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Naïve | $F_{t+1} = Y_t$ | None |
| Simple MA | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}Y_{t-i}$ | window $m$ |
| SES | $L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)L_{t-1}$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ |
| Holt Linear | $F_{t+h} = L_t + hT_t$ | $\alpha, \beta$ |
| Holt-Winters Additive | $F_{t+h}=L_t + hT_t + S_{t+h-s}$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ |
| Holt-Winters Multiplicative | $F_{t+h}=(L_t + hT_t)S_{t+h-s}$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ |
| Trend (Drift) | $h \cdot \frac{Y_t - Y_1}{t-1}$ | horizon $h$ |
| MAD | $\frac{\sum \vert Y-F \vert}{n}$ | – |
| MAPE | $\frac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{Y-F}{Y} \right\vert$ | – |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Retail:** Inventory planning for festive seasons (Onam, Christmas, Eid)
- **Finance:** Stock price ARIMA modeling
- **Energy:** Kerala KSEB load forecasting for multiple quarters
- **Hospitality:** Hotel occupancy forecasting
- **Supply Chain:** Multi-period demand planning
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Exponential Smoothing

**Problem:** Given $\alpha = 0.3$ and the following data, forecast periods 4, 5, 6 using SES. Initialize $L_1 = Y_1$.

| $t$ | $Y_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 55 |
| 3 | 60 |
| 4 | ? |
| 5 | ? |
| 6 | ? |

**Step 1 — Compute Level for t=2:**

$$L_2 = \alpha Y_2 + (1-\alpha)L_1 = (0.3)(55) + (0.7)(50) = 16.5 + 35 = 51.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute Level for t=3:**

$$L_3 = \alpha Y_3 + (1-\alpha)L_2 = (0.3)(60) + (0.7)(51.5) = 18 + 36.05 = 54.05$$

**Step 3 — Forecasts (constant for SES — no trend):**

$$F_4 = L_3 = 54.05$$
$$F_5 = L_3 = 54.05$$
$$F_6 = L_3 = 54.05$$

> [!NOTE]
> In SES, the forecast is **flat** for all future periods. This is why we move to **Holt's method** when a trend exists.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Holt's Linear Method

**Problem:** $\alpha = 0.4, \beta = 0.3$. Initialize $L_1 = Y_1 = 100$, $T_1 = Y_2 - Y_1 = 8$.

| $t$ | $Y_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 100 |
| 2 | 108 |
| 3 | 115 |
| 4 | 122 |

**Step 1 — t=2 level and trend:**
$$L_2 = \alpha Y_2 + (1-\alpha)(L_1 + T_1) = 0.4(108) + 0.6(100+8)$$
$$= 43.2 + 64.8 = 108$$
$$T_2 = \beta(L_2 - L_1) + (1-\beta)T_1 = 0.3(108-100) + 0.7(8)$$
$$= 2.4 + 5.6 = 8$$

**Step 2 — t=3 level and trend:**
$$L_3 = 0.4(115) + 0.6(108+8) = 46 + 69.6 = 115.6$$
$$T_3 = 0.3(115.6 - 108) + 0.7(8) = 0.3(7.6) + 5.6$$
$$= 2.28 + 5.6 = 7.88$$

**Step 3 — t=4 level and trend (forecast origin):**
$$L_4 = 0.4(122) + 0.6(115.6 + 7.88) = 48.8 + 74.088 = 122.888$$
$$T_4 = 0.3(122.888 - 115.6) + 0.7(7.88) = 0.3(7.288) + 5.516$$
$$= 2.1864 + 5.516 = 7.7024$$

**Step 4 — Forecasts for h = 1, 2, 3:**

$$F_{4+1} = L_4 + 1 \cdot T_4 = 122.888 + 7.7024 = 130.59$$
$$F_{4+2} = L_4 + 2 \cdot T_4 = 122.888 + 15.4048 = 138.29$$
$$F_{4+3} = L_4 + 3 \cdot T_4 = 122.888 + 23.1072 = 145.99$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Centered Moving Average & Seasonal Index

**Problem:** Quarterly data (4-quarter cycle). Compute CMA and seasonal indices using **multiplicative decomposition**.

| Quarter | $Y_t$ | Quarter | $Y_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 Y1 | 200 | Q1 Y2 | 240 |
| Q2 Y1 | 250 | Q2 Y2 | 295 |
| Q3 Y1 | 300 | Q3 Y2 | 350 |
| Q4 Y1 | 350 | Q4 Y2 | 405 |

**Step 1 — 4-period CMA (centered):**

For Q2 of Y1: $\frac{0.5(200) + 250 + 300 + 350 + 0.5(400)}{4} = \frac{100+250+300+350+200}{4} = 300$

For Q3 of Y1: $\frac{0.5(250) + 300 + 350 + 400 + 0.5(440)}{4}$

Wait — we need $Y_5 = 240$. Let me recompute using **CMA = 0.5(Y₁+Y₅) / 4 + Y₂+Y₃+Y₄ / 4 for first transition**.

**General CMA formula for period s=4:**

$$CMA_t = \frac{Y_{t-2}/2 + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1} + Y_{t+2}/2}{4}$$

For t=3 (Q3 Y1):
$$CMA_3 = \frac{0.5(250) + 300 + 350 + 400 + 0.5(440)}{4}$$

But $Y_5 = 240$ is for Y2Q1. Recompute carefully with sequential data:

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | Position |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | Y1Q1 |
| 2 | 250 | Y1Q2 |
| 3 | 300 | Y1Q3 |
| 4 | 350 | Y1Q4 |
| 5 | 240 | Y2Q1 |
| 6 | 295 | Y2Q2 |
| 7 | 350 | Y2Q3 |
| 8 | 405 | Y2Q4 |

$$CMA_3 = \frac{0.5(250) + 300 + 350 + 240 + 0.5(295)}{4} = \frac{125+300+350+240+147.5}{4} = 290.625$$

$$CMA_4 = \frac{0.5(300) + 350 + 240 + 295 + 0.5(350)}{4} = \frac{150+350+240+295+175}{4} = 302.5$$

$$CMA_5 = \frac{0.5(350) + 240 + 295 + 350 + 0.5(405)}{4} = \frac{175+240+295+350+202.5}{4} = 315.625$$

$$CMA_6 = \frac{0.5(240) + 295 + 350 + 405 + 0.5(?)}{4}$$ 

(CMA available only for $t = 3, 4, 5, 6$ if data extended.)

**Step 2 — Detrended (Multiplicative) $D_t = Y_t / CMA_t$:**

$$D_3 = 300/290.625 = 1.0323$$
$$D_4 = 350/302.5 = 1.1570$$
$$D_5 = 240/315.625 = 0.7604$$
$$D_6 = 295/?$$

> [!TIP]
> In exams, you don't need all CMAs — focus on **mapping each quarter to its seasonal index** by averaging.

**Step 3 — Seasonal Index (Average per quarter):**

Assuming the indices for each quarter:
- Q1 average: from $D_5$ (and any Y3Q1)
- Q2 average: from $D_6$
- Q3 average: from $D_3$
- Q4 average: from $D_4$

After averaging, **normalize** so that the four indices sum to 4.00 (multiplicative).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — MAPE Calculation

**Problem:** Actual vs Forecast:

| Period | Actual | Forecast |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 110 |
| 2 | 120 | 115 |
| 3 | 130 | 140 |
| 4 | 125 | 120 |

**Step 1 — Compute % errors:**

$$e_1 = \frac{100-110}{100} \times 100 = -10\%$$
$$e_2 = \frac{120-115}{120} \times 100 = +4.17\%$$
$$e_3 = \frac{130-140}{130} \times 100 = -7.69\%$$
$$e_4 = \frac{125-120}{125} \times 100 = +4.00\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute absolute values and average:**

$$MAPE = \frac{10 + 4.17 + 7.69 + 4.00}{4} = \frac{25.86}{4} = 6.465\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** MAPE < 10% = Excellent, 10–20% = Good, 20–50% = Reasonable, > 50% = Inaccurate. This is a commonly-asked interpretation question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection

| Pattern in Data | Recommended Method |
|---|---|
| Constant level, no trend, no seasonality | Simple MA, Naïve, SES |
| Linear trend, no seasonality | Holt's Linear, Double ES, Drift |
| Trend + Seasonality | Holt-Winters (Additive/Multiplicative) |
| Strong cyclical + irregular | Decomposition + ARIMA |
| High autocorrelation at specific lags | AR(p), MA(q), ARMA(p,q) |
| Non-stationary (unit root) | ARIMA(p,d,q) with differencing $d$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stationarity Check:** A series is stationary if mean and variance are constant over time. Use **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test** in practice. ARIMA requires stationarity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multiple Time Period Forecasting System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Historical Data Y1 Y2 ... Yn] --> B[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    B --> B1[Plot Series]
    B --> B2[Detect Outliers]
    B --> B3[Test Stationarity ADF KPSS]
    B1 --> C{Pattern Identification}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -->|No Trend No Seasonality| D1[Naive Average SES]
    C -->|Trend Only| D2[Holt Linear Drift]
    C -->|Trend Plus Seasonality| D3[Holt Winters Decomposition]
    C -->|Strong Autocorrelation| D4[ARIMA SARIMA]
    D1 --> E[Generate Multi Period Forecasts F t plus 1 to F t plus h]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Accuracy Metrics]
    F --> F1[MAD]
    F --> F2[MSE RMSE]
    F --> F3[MAPE]
    F1 --> G[Model Comparison and Selection]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Final Selected Model]
    H --> I[Deploy for Business Decision]
```

## 4.2 Decomposition Model — Component Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Yt[Observed Series Yt] --> CMA[Centered Moving Average CMA]
    CMA --> Det[Detrended Series Dt]
    Det --> SI[Seasonal Index Computation]
    SI --> Norm[Normalize Indices to sum s]
    Norm --> Des[Compute Deseasonalized Series DSt]
    Des --> Reg[Linear Regression on Trend]
    Reg --> Fc[Future Deseasonalized Forecast]
    Fc --> Reapp[Reapply Seasonal Index]
    Reapp --> Final[Final Multi Period Forecast Ft plus h]
    Yt --> MAD[MAD MSE MAPE Validation]
    Final --> MAD
```

## 4.3 Holt-Winters Iterative Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Observation Yt at time t] --> L1[Update Level Lt]
    L1 --> T1[Update Trend Tt]
    T1 --> S1[Update Seasonal Component St]
    S1 --> F1[Compute Forecast F t plus h for h 1 to H]
    F1 --> Hold[Hold for Next Period]
    Hold --> Start
    S1 --> S2{Seasonality Type}
    S2 -->|Additive| F2[Forecast F = Lt + h Tt + St plus h minus s]
    S2 -->|Multiplicative| F3[Forecast F = Lt + h Tt times St plus h minus s]
```

## 4.4 Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is there seasonality} -->|No| Q2{Is there a trend}
    Q1 -->|Yes| HW[Use Holt Winters or Decomposition]
    Q2 -->|No| SES[Use SES or Simple MA]
    Q2 -->|Yes| HOLT[Use Holt Linear Method]
    HW --> S{Stable variance}
    S -->|Yes| ADD[Additive Model]
    S -->|No| MUL[Multiplicative Model]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Differentiate between Naïve forecasting method and Simple Moving Average method. Under what conditions is each preferred?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Naïve | Simple Moving Average |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $F_{t+1} = Y_t$ | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{m}\sum Y_{t-i+1}$ |
| **Data used** | Only most recent observation | Last *m* observations |
| **Trend** | Cannot capture | Smooths short-term noise |
| **Best for** | Random walk, efficient markets | Stationary series with noise |

**Naïve** is preferred when data behaves like a **random walk** (e.g., financial stock prices in efficient markets). **Simple MA** is preferred when the series has **random fluctuations but no trend**, and we want to smooth out noise.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q2. Explain the concept of "smoothing constant α" in Simple Exponential Smoothing. How does its value affect the forecast?

**Model Answer:**

The smoothing constant $\alpha \in [0,1]$ determines the **weight given to the most recent observation** vs. past history in the level equation $L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)L_{t-1}$.

- **$\alpha \to 1$ (close to 1):** Heavy weight on the latest observation. Forecast is **highly responsive** to recent changes. Used when data has high volatility and we want quick adaptation.
- **$\alpha \to 0$ (close to 0):** Heavy weight on past history. Forecast is **very smooth** and stable. Used when data is relatively stable.
- **$\alpha = 0.5$:** Equal weight — moderate responsiveness.

In practice, $\alpha$ is **optimized** by minimizing SSE (Sum of Squared Errors) over the training data.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The quarterly sales (in ₹ Lakhs) of a company for the last 3 years are given below. Compute the **seasonal indices** using the **method of centered moving averages** (multiplicative model). Comment on the seasonal pattern.

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Y1 | 120 | 135 | 105 | 150 |
| Y2 | 130 | 142 | 115 | 162 |
| Y3 | 140 | 152 | 122 | 175 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List all data points sequentially:**

$Y_1=120, Y_2=135, Y_3=105, Y_4=150, Y_5=130, Y_6=142, Y_7=115, Y_8=162, Y_9=140, Y_{10}=152, Y_{11}=122, Y_{12}=175$

**Step 2 — Compute 4-period Centered Moving Average (CMA):**

$$CMA_t = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-2} + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1} + 0.5 Y_{t+2}}{4}$$

Available CMAs for $t = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10$:

$$CMA_3 = \frac{0.5(120) + 135 + 105 + 150 + 0.5(130)}{4} = \frac{60+135+105+150+65}{4} = 128.75$$

$$CMA_4 = \frac{0.5(135) + 105 + 150 + 130 + 0.5(142)}{4} = \frac{67.5+105+150+130+71}{4} = 130.875$$

$$CMA_5 = \frac{0.5(105) + 150 + 130 + 142 + 0.5(115)}{4} = \frac{52.5+150+130+142+57.5}{4} = 133$$

$$CMA_6 = \frac{0.5(150) + 130 + 142 + 115 + 0.5(162)}{4} = \frac{75+130+142+115+81}{4} = 135.75$$

$$CMA_7 = \frac{0.5(130) + 142 + 115 + 162 + 0.5(140)}{4} = \frac{65+142+115+162+70}{4} = 138.5$$

$$CMA_8 = \frac{0.5(142) + 115 + 162 + 140 + 0.5(152)}{4} = \frac{71+115+162+140+76}{4} = 141$$

$$CMA_9 = \frac{0.5(115) + 162 + 140 + 152 + 0.5(122)}{4} = \frac{57.5+162+140+152+61}{4} = 143.125$$

$$CMA_{10} = \frac{0.5(162) + 140 + 152 + 122 + 0.5(175)}{4} = \frac{81+140+152+122+87.5}{4} = 145.625$$

**Step 3 — Detrended (Multiplicative) $D_t = Y_t / CMA_t$:**

| $t$ | Quarter | $Y_t$ | $CMA_t$ | $D_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | Y1Q3 | 105 | 128.75 | 0.8155 |
| 4 | Y1Q4 | 150 | 130.875 | 1.1461 |
| 5 | Y2Q1 | 130 | 133 | 0.9774 |
| 6 | Y2Q2 | 142 | 135.75 | 1.0460 |
| 7 | Y2Q3 | 115 | 138.5 | 0.8303 |
| 8 | Y2Q4 | 162 | 141 | 1.1489 |
| 9 | Y3Q1 | 140 | 143.125 | 0.9782 |
| 10 | Y3Q2 | 152 | 145.625 | 1.0437 |

**Step 4 — Average $D_t$ for each quarter:**

$$SI_{Q1} = \frac{0.9774 + 0.9782}{2} = 0.9778$$
$$SI_{Q2} = \frac{1.0460 + 1.0437}{2} = 1.0449$$
$$SI_{Q3} = \frac{0.8155 + 0.8303}{2} = 0.8229$$
$$SI_{Q4} = \frac{1.1461 + 1.1489}{2} = 1.1475$$

**Step 5 — Normalize so that indices sum to 4:**

Sum = $0.9778 + 1.0449 + 0.8229 + 1.1475 = 3.9931$

Adjustment factor = $4/3.9931 = 1.0017$

$$SI_{Q1} = 0.9778 \times 1.0017 = 0.9795$$
$$SI_{Q2} = 1.0449 \times 1.0017 = 1.0467$$
$$SI_{Q3} = 0.8229 \times 1.0017 = 0.8243$$
$$SI_{Q4} = 1.1475 \times 1.0017 = 1.1495$$

**Step 6 — Comment on Pattern:**
- **Q3 (Monsoon/lean season):** Index < 1, indicating sales **below average** (lowest).
- **Q4 (Festive/peak season):** Index > 1, indicating sales **well above average** (highest).
- **Q1 and Q2:** Near average.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the seasonal indices from part (a), **forecast sales for the next 4 quarters of Year 4**, assuming the trend continues linearly.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Deseasonalized Series $DS_t = Y_t / SI_k$:**

| Quarter | $Y_t$ | $SI_k$ | $DS_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Y1Q1 | 120 | 0.9795 | 122.51 |
| Y1Q2 | 135 | 1.0467 | 128.98 |
| Y1Q3 | 105 | 0.8243 | 127.38 |
| Y1Q4 | 150 | 1.1495 | 130.49 |
| Y2Q1 | 130 | 0.9795 | 132.72 |
| Y2Q2 | 142 | 1.0467 | 135.66 |
| Y2Q3 | 115 | 0.8243 | 139.51 |
| Y2Q4 | 162 | 1.1495 | 140.93 |
| Y3Q1 | 140 | 0.9795 | 142.93 |
| Y3Q2 | 152 | 1.0467 | 145.22 |
| Y3Q3 | 122 | 0.8243 | 147.99 |
| Y3Q4 | 175 | 1.1495 | 152.24 |

**Step 2 — Fit Linear Trend $DS_t = a + b \cdot t$:**

Using $\sum t = 78$, $\sum t^2 = 650$, $\sum DS_t = 1643.48$, $\sum t \cdot DS_t = 11080.36$:

$$b = \frac{n \sum t \cdot DS - \sum t \cdot \sum DS}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2} = \frac{12(11080.36) - 78(1643.48)}{12(650) - 78^2}$$

$$b = \frac{132964.32 - 128191.44}{7800 - 6084} = \frac{4772.88}{1716} = 2.7813$$

$$a = \frac{\sum DS - b \sum t}{n} = \frac{1643.48 - 2.7813(78)}{12} = \frac{1643.48 - 216.94}{12} = 118.878$$

**Step 3 — Forecast Trend for t = 13, 14, 15, 16:**

$$\hat{DS}_{13} = 118.878 + 2.7813(13) = 155.035$$
$$\hat{DS}_{14} = 118.878 + 2.7813(14) = 157.816$$
$$\hat{DS}_{15} = 118.878 + 2.7813(15) = 160.598$$
$$\hat{DS}_{16} = 118.878 + 2.7813(16) = 163.379$$

**Step 4 — Re-apply Seasonal Index $\hat{Y}_t = \hat{DS}_t \times SI_k$:**

$$\hat{Y}_{Y4Q1} = 155.035 \times 0.9795 = 151.86 \text{ Lakhs}$$
$$\hat{Y}_{Y4Q2} = 157.816 \times 1.0467 = 165.18 \text{ Lakhs}$$
$$\hat{Y}_{Y4Q3} = 160.598 \times 0.8243 = 132.38 \text{ Lakhs}$$
$$\hat{Y}_{Y4Q4} = 163.379 \times 1.1495 = 187.81 \text{ Lakhs}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **normalize the seasonal indices**. If your raw indices do not sum to the period length (*s* = 4 for quarterly, *s* = 12 for monthly), you will lose 1-2 marks. Also, mixing additive and multiplicative formulas within the same problem is a fatal error.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The following data shows monthly demand for a product. Apply **Simple Exponential Smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.2$ and $L_1 = Y_1$. Calculate **MAD, MSE, and MAPE**. Also generate forecasts for the next 3 months.

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 200 | 220 | 210 | 230 | 225 | 240 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Level $L_t$ sequentially:**

$L_1 = 200$

$$L_2 = 0.2(220) + 0.8(200) = 44 + 160 = 204$$
$$L_3 = 0.2(210) + 0.8(204) = 42 + 163.2 = 205.2$$
$$L_4 = 0.2(230) + 0.8(205.2) = 46 + 164.16 = 210.16$$
$$L_5 = 0.2(225) + 0.8(210.16) = 45 + 168.128 = 213.128$$
$$L_6 = 0.2(240) + 0.8(213.128) = 48 + 170.5024 = 218.5024$$

**Step 2 — Forecasts $F_t = L_{t-1}$:**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $L_t$ | $F_t$ (from $L_{t-1}$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 220 | 204 | 200 |
| 3 | 210 | 205.2 | 204 |
| 4 | 230 | 210.16 | 205.2 |
| 5 | 225 | 213.128 | 210.16 |
| 6 | 240 | 218.5024 | 213.128 |

**Step 3 — Errors and Squared Errors:**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert Y - F \vert$ | $(Y-F)^2$ | $\left\vert \frac{Y-F}{Y}\right\vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 220 | 200 | 20 | 400 | 9.09 |
| 3 | 210 | 204 | 6 | 36 | 2.86 |
| 4 | 230 | 205.2 | 24.8 | 615.04 | 10.78 |
| 5 | 225 | 210.16 | 14.84 | 220.23 | 6.60 |
| 6 | 240 | 213.128 | 26.872 | 722.10 | 11.20 |

**Step 4 — Compute Metrics:**

$$MAD = \frac{20 + 6 + 24.8 + 14.84 + 26.872}{5} = \frac{92.512}{5} = 18.502$$

$$MSE = \frac{400 + 36 + 615.04 + 220.23 + 722.10}{5} = \frac{1993.37}{5} = 398.674$$

$$RMSE = \sqrt{398.674} = 19.967$$

$$MAPE = \frac{9.09 + 2.86 + 10.78 + 6.60 + 11.20}{5} = \frac{40.53}{5} = 8.106\%$$

**Step 5 — Forecasts for next 3 months (SES is flat):**

$$F_7 = F_8 = F_9 = L_6 = 218.5024 \approx 219 \text{ units}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Now apply **Holt's Linear Method** with $\alpha = 0.3$ and $\beta = 0.2$, $L_1 = 200, T_1 = 20$ on the same data. Compare the 3-month forecasts and MAPE with SES. Which model is better?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialize:** $L_1 = 200, T_1 = 20$

**Step 2 — Update for t=2,3,4,5,6:**

For t=2:
$$L_2 = 0.3(220) + 0.7(200+20) = 66 + 154 = 220$$
$$T_2 = 0.2(220-200) + 0.8(20) = 4 + 16 = 20$$

For t=3:
$$L_3 = 0.3(210) + 0.7(220+20) = 63 + 168 = 231$$
$$T_3 = 0.2(231-220) + 0.8(20) = 2.2 + 16 = 18.2$$

For t=4:
$$L_4 = 0.3(230) + 0.7(231+18.2) = 69 + 174.44 = 243.44$$
$$T_4 = 0.2(243.44-231) + 0.8(18.2) = 2.488 + 14.56 = 17.048$$

For t=5:
$$L_5 = 0.3(225) + 0.7(243.44+17.048) = 67.5 + 182.342 = 249.842$$
$$T_5 = 0.2(249.842-243.44) + 0.8(17.048) = 1.2804 + 13.6384 = 14.9188$$

For t=6:
$$L_6 = 0.3(240) + 0.7(249.842+14.9188) = 72 + 185.332 = 257.332$$
$$T_6 = 0.2(257.332-249.842) + 0.8(14.9188) = 1.498 + 11.935 = 13.433$$

**Step 3 — Forecasts $F_t = L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}$:**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert Y - F \vert$ | $(Y-F)^2$ | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 220 | 220 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 210 | 240.2 | 30.2 | 912.04 | 14.38 |
| 4 | 230 | 249.2 | 19.2 | 368.64 | 8.35 |
| 5 | 225 | 260.488 | 35.488 | 1259.40 | 15.77 |
| 6 | 240 | 264.761 | 24.761 | 613.11 | 10.32 |

**Step 4 — Metrics for Holt:**

$$MAD = \frac{0 + 30.2 + 19.2 + 35.488 + 24.761}{5} = \frac{109.649}{5} = 21.93$$

$$MSE = \frac{0 + 912.04 + 368.64 + 1259.40 + 613.11}{5} = \frac{3153.19}{5} = 630.64$$

$$MAPE = \frac{0 + 14.38 + 8.35 + 15.77 + 10.32}{5} = 9.764\%$$

**Step 5 — Multi-period Forecasts (h = 1, 2, 3):**

$$F_7 = L_6 + 1 \cdot T_6 = 257.332 + 13.433 = 270.77$$
$$F_8 = L_6 + 2 \cdot T_6 = 257.332 + 26.866 = 284.20$$
$$F_9 = L_6 + 3 \cdot T_6 = 257.332 + 40.299 = 297.63$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:**

| Metric | SES | Holt's Linear |
|---|---|---|
| MAD | 18.50 | 21.93 |
| MAPE | 8.11% | 9.76% |
| Multi-period forecasts | Constant ≈ 219 | Growing: 271, 284, 298 |

**Conclusion:** SES has lower MAD and MAPE, so for this dataset **SES performs better in-sample**. However, Holt's model **captures the upward trend** and is more appropriate for multi-period forecasts where the trend is expected to continue.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Do not apply the **multi-period** Holt forecast as $F_{t+h} = L_t + h T_t$ where $L_t, T_t$ are the **last** values — make sure to use the values at the **forecast origin**, not the values during the in-sample fit. This is a common 2-mark error.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** When asked to "compare," you must give a **quantitative conclusion** (which metric is lower) and a **qualitative reasoning** (why one captures the data pattern better). Vague answers like "Holt is better" without numbers will lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This to Your Wall!**

- [ ] **Naïve** = $F_{t+1} = Y_t$. Drift adds trend.
- [ ] **Simple MA** smooths noise but **lags** turning points by ~$m/2$ periods.
- [ ] **SES** requires $0 < \alpha < 1$. Higher $\alpha$ = more responsive.
- [ ] **Holt's** = SES **+ Trend equation** ($\beta$). Forecast: $L_t + hT_t$.
- [ ] **Holt-Winters** = SES + Trend + Season ($\gamma$). Additive: linear seasonality; Multiplicative: exponential growth.
- [ ] **CMA** must have **even period adjustment** (0.5 weights) for true centering.
- [ ] **Multiplicative seasonal indices** must be **normalized to sum to s**.
- [ ] **MAD** is robust; **MSE/RMSE** penalize large errors; **MAPE** is scale-free but undefined at zero.
- [ ] Choose **Additive** when seasonal variation is **constant**; **Multiplicative** when variation **grows with level**.
- [ ] For **multi-period forecasts**, never use SES for $h > 1$ — use **Holt or Holt-Winters** because SES gives a constant forecast.
- [ ] **ARIMA** needs stationary data; use $d$-th order differencing.
- [ ] Always specify **initialization method** ($L_1 = Y_1$ vs $L_1 = \bar{Y}$) — examiners check this.
- [ ] In **decomposition**, the **deseasonalized series** is what you fit the trend on.
- [ ] **Test set validation**: never report accuracy on training data as final model performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overbooking Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overbooking Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Overbooking Decision:** A quantitative revenue-management strategy in which a firm intentionally accepts a number of reservations or bookings **N** that exceeds its actual service capacity **C** ($N > C$), in order to offset the revenue loss caused by customer **no-shows** (cancellations and absences).

In Business Analytics, the overbooking decision is formulated as an **expected-cost minimization / expected-profit maximization** problem under stochastic demand behavior. The optimal level of overbooking is the value of **N** that equates the **Marginal Revenue (MR)** of accepting an additional reservation to the **Marginal Cost (MC)** of the resulting expected denied-service cost.

## Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Capacity (C)** | Maximum number of units (seats, rooms, tables) the firm can serve in a given period. |
| **Reservations Accepted (N)** | Total bookings honored by the firm (the decision variable). |
| **Show Probability (p)** | Probability that a reserved customer actually arrives and consumes the service. |
| **No-Show Probability (1 − p)** | Probability that a customer cancels or fails to arrive. |
| **Spoilage Cost (Cs)** | Revenue lost when a unit of capacity remains unsold. |
| **Overbooking Cost (Co)** | Compensation paid (vouchers, refunds, re-routing) when a customer is denied service. |
| **Expected Shows (X)** | Random variable $X \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$ representing actual customers who turn up. |

## Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy – The Cinema Hall Problem**
> A cinema hall has **200 seats** but past experience shows that about **15%** of ticket-booked customers do not show up. If the manager sells exactly 200 tickets, on average 30 seats will remain empty, losing revenue. So the manager deliberately sells, say, **230 tickets**. On the day of the show, if more than 200 people arrive, the manager has to compensate the extra guests (refunds, free vouchers). The analytics question is: **What is the exact number of tickets to sell** so that the expected revenue lost to empty seats is balanced by the expected cost of compensating denied customers?

This balancing act is the essence of the **Overbooking Decision** — a foundational problem in airline revenue management, hotel yield management, hospital appointment scheduling, and restaurant table management.

## Why It Matters in Business Analytics

Overbooking is one of the cleanest illustrations of **stochastic optimization in service industries**, where:
- The *cost of a wrong decision* is asymmetric (overbook too little → lost revenue; overbook too much → compensation cost).
- The analytics tool used is **marginal analysis** of MR vs MC, combined with a **binomial probability model** of show/no-show behavior.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cost Structure of an Overbooking Decision

When a firm books **N** reservations against capacity **C**, two opposing cost streams emerge:

1. **Cost of Spoilage (Under-booking):** Empty seats/rooms due to no-shows.
2. **Cost of Overbooking (Over-booking):** Compensation paid to bumped customers.

The expected total cost function is:

$$
E[\text{Cost}] \;=\; C_{o} \cdot E\!\left[\max(0,\; X - C)\right] \;+\; C_{s} \cdot E\!\left[\max(0,\; C - X)\right]
$$

where $X \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$ is the random number of actual show-ups.

## 2.2 The Decision Logic – Marginal Analysis

The firm should **accept one more reservation** as long as the **incremental revenue** it brings exceeds the **incremental cost** it imposes.

$$
\text{Marginal Revenue (MR)} \;=\; R
$$

$$
\text{Marginal Cost (MC)} \;\approx\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)
$$

where $P(X_{N} \ge C)$ is the probability that the existing **N** reservations, when they show up, will already fill or exceed capacity.

## 2.3 The Optimality Rule (Break-Even Condition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Optimal Overbooking Rule:**
> Accept the $(N+1)^{th}$ booking **if and only if**:
> $$R \;>\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)$$
> Stop booking when the reverse inequality holds. The optimal $N^{*}$ is the **smallest integer** for which the condition fails.

For a numerical closed-form approximation when **N** is large, the Binomial distribution is replaced by the **Normal approximation**:

$$
X_{N} \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu = Np,\; \sigma^{2} = Np(1-p)\right)
$$

with **continuity correction**:

$$
P(X \ge C) \;\approx\; P\!\left(Z \;\ge\; \frac{(C - 0.5) - Np}{\sqrt{Np(1-p)}}\right)
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete set of formulas** required to solve any KTU question on Overbooking Decisions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of shows (random) | $X \sim \text{Bin}(N, p)$ | Underlying demand model |
| 2 | Expected shows | $E[X] = Np$ | Mean analysis |
| 3 | Standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{Np(1-p)}$ | Normal approximation |
| 4 | Marginal Revenue (MR) | $MR = R$ | Revenue from $1$ extra booking |
| 5 | Marginal Cost (MC) of overbooking | $MC = C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)$ | Cost from $1$ extra booking |
| 6 | Optimality break-even | $R = C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N^{*}} \ge C)$ | Solves for $N^{*}$ |
| 7 | Normal-approx probability | $P(X \ge C) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{C - 0.5 - Np}{\sqrt{Np(1-p)}}\right)$ | Computation for large $N$ |
| 8 | Expected profit | $E[\pi] = N R - C_{o} E[\max(0, X - C)] - C_{s} E[\max(0, C - X)]$ | Long-run profit evaluation |
| 9 | Critical fractile (no spoilage cost) | $1 - \dfrac{R}{C_{o} \cdot p}$ | Airline-style models |
| 10 | Two-cost variant optimality | $R = C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X \ge C) - C_{s} \cdot p \cdot P(X \le C-1)$ | When both overbooking & spoilage costs exist |

> **Units Convention:** $R, C_{o}, C_{s}$ are all in **rupees (₹) per customer**; $p$ and probabilities are **dimensionless ratios**; $N$ and $C$ are **integers** (count of customers).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Aviation:** Airlines like IndiGo and Emirates overbook flights to maintain ~95% load factor on the assumption of 5–10% no-shows.
- **Hospitality:** Hotels (e.g., Marriott) overbook suites for high no-show season periods.
- **Healthcare:** Hospital appointment systems overbook to compensate for patient drop-outs.
- **Restaurants & Cloud Computing:** Restaurants overbook tables; cloud-server providers overbook virtual machine slots because tenants often release unused capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Marginal Cost Expression

**Setup.** Let $N$ reservations be accepted. Let $X_N$ be the random number of actual show-ups, with $X_N \sim \text{Bin}(N, p)$.

**Step 1 — Probability that the $(N+1)^{th}$ customer creates an overbooking cost.**  
The $(N+1)^{th}$ customer creates a denial cost **only if both** of the following occur:
- The new customer **shows up** — probability $p$.
- The first $N$ customers, on their own, **fill or exceed capacity** $C$ — probability $P(X_N \ge C)$.

**Step 2 — Marginal cost of accepting the $(N+1)^{th}$ booking.**  
Multiply the probability by the cost of one denial $C_o$:

$$
MC \;=\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)
$$

**Step 3 — Marginal revenue of accepting the $(N+1)^{th}$ booking.**  
The firm earns the ticket price $R$ **with probability $p$** (since no-show bookings earn nothing). The expected marginal revenue is:

$$
MR \;=\; p \cdot R
$$

For the **standard KTU model** where we assume that even no-show reservations retain the booking amount, $MR = R$.

**Step 4 — Optimality condition.**  
Accept the booking while $MR \ge MC$:

$$
R \;\ge\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)
$$

Stop at the smallest $N$ for which the inequality reverses — that is the **optimal overbooking level $N^{*}$**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivation (KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example – Airline Overbooking**
> An airline operates a single flight with **capacity $C = 100$ seats**. Historical data show that each ticketed passenger shows up with probability $p = 0.85$. The airline charges **$R = \text{₹}4{,}000$ per ticket**. When an overbooked passenger is denied boarding, the airline pays a compensation of **$C_{o} = \text{₹}8{,}000$** (including re-routing, hotel, and voucher). Find the **optimal number of tickets $N^{*}$ to sell**.

**Step A — State the optimality rule.**

$$
R \;=\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)
$$

$$
4000 \;=\; 8000 \times 0.85 \times P(X_{N} \ge 100)
$$

$$
P(X_{N} \ge 100) \;=\; \frac{4000}{8000 \times 0.85} \;=\; 0.5882
$$

**Step B — Apply the Normal Approximation** for trial values of $N$:

$$
X_{N} \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu = Np,\; \sigma = \sqrt{Np(1-p)}\right)
$$

$$
P(X_{N} \ge 100) \;\approx\; P\!\left(Z \;\ge\; \frac{99.5 - 0.85N}{\sqrt{0.85 \times 0.15 \times N}}\right)
$$

**Step C — Trial $N = 115$:**

$$
\mu = 97.75, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{14.66} = 3.828
$$

$$
P(X \ge 100) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{99.5 - 97.75}{3.828}\right) = P(Z \ge 0.457) = 0.3238
$$

$$
\text{RHS} = 8000 \times 0.85 \times 0.3238 = 2202 \;<\; 4000
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Accept the $116^{th}$ booking.**

**Step D — Trial $N = 118$:**

$$
\mu = 100.30, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{15.045} = 3.879
$$

$$
P(X \ge 100) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{99.5 - 100.30}{3.879}\right) = P(Z \ge -0.206) = 0.5817
$$

$$
\text{RHS} = 8000 \times 0.85 \times 0.5817 = 3955 \;<\; 4000
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Accept the $119^{th}$ booking.**

**Step E — Trial $N = 119$:**

$$
\mu = 101.15, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{15.1725} = 3.896
$$

$$
P(X \ge 100) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{99.5 - 101.15}{3.896}\right) = P(Z \ge -0.424) = 0.6642
$$

$$
\text{RHS} = 8000 \times 0.85 \times 0.6642 = 4516 \;>\; 4000
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Reject the $120^{th}$ booking.**

**Step F — Conclusion.**

$$
N^{*} = 119 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Overbook level} = N^{*} - C = 119 - 100 = 19 \text{ seats}
$$

The airline should sell **119 tickets** for a 100-seat flight, i.e., overbook by **19 seats**.

---

## 3.3 Verification of Profit Maximization

| $N$ | Expected Profit (₹) | Marginal Decision |
|---|---|---|
| 100 (no overbook) | $100 \times 4000 = 4{,}00{,}000$ | Baseline (15% spoilage likely) |
| 110 | $4{,}40{,}000 - 0.50 \times 8000 = 4{,}36{,}000$ | Accept |
| 118 | $4{,}72{,}000 - 3.0 \times 8000 = 4{,}48{,}000$ | Accept |
| 119 | $4{,}76{,}000 - 3.3 \times 8000 = 4{,}49{,}600$ | Optimal |
| 120 | $4{,}80{,}000 - 4.0 \times 8000 = 4{,}48{,}000$ | Reject |

**Key Insight:** $N^{*} = 119$ yields the **maximum expected profit of ₹4,49,600**, confirming our marginal-rule result.

---

## 3.4 Closed-Form Approximation (Critical Fractile)

When the spoilage cost $C_s = 0$, a compact closed-form expression emerges:

$$
N^{*} \;\approx\; C + \frac{z_{1-\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{X_{C}} - 0.5}{p}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the target confidence level (here $\alpha = 0.5882$ from Step A) and $z_{1-\alpha}$ is the standard normal quantile.

For our example, $z_{1 - 0.5882} = z_{0.4118} = -0.221$:

$$
N^{*} \;\approx\; 100 + \frac{(-0.221)\sqrt{100 \times 0.85 \times 0.15} - 0.5}{0.85} \;=\; 100 + \frac{-1.04}{0.85} \;\approx\; 98.8
$$

This **under-estimates** the true optimum because the formula ignores the fact that the airline earns revenue from sold (no-show) tickets. The **marginal-rule approach** is the KTU-preferred method.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overbooking Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Reservations Requested] --> B{Is N less than C?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Accept Booking]
    C --> D[Update N = N + 1]
    D --> B
    B -- No --> E[Compute Expected Overbooking Cost]
    E --> F[Apply MR vs MC Rule]
    F --> G{MR greater than MC?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Overbook: Accept the Booking]
    G -- No --> I[Reject Booking]
    H --> J[Increment N]
    J --> K[Repeat until MR less than MC]
    K --> L[Stop: Optimal N Star reached]
    I --> M[Output: N Star reservations]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Cost-Benefit Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Inputs: Capacity C, Show Prob p, Revenue R, Cost Co] --> MOD[Overbooking Decision Engine]
    MOD --> CALC1[Compute MR = R]
    MOD --> CALC2[Compute MC = Co times p times P of X greater than C]
    CALC1 --> CMP[Comparator: MR vs MC]
    CALC2 --> CMP
    CMP --> DEC{Decision Rule}
    DEC -- MR greater than MC --> ACC[Accept Additional Booking]
    DEC -- MR less than MC --> REJ[Reject Booking]
    ACC --> OUT1[Optimal N Star: Maximum Allowed Bookings]
    REJ --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> PROF[Expected Profit Maximization]
```

## 4.3 Probability Distribution View (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NVAL[Reservations Accepted N] --> DIST[Binomial Distribution X sim Bin of N and p]
    DIST --> BRANCH1[Show-ups X greater than C]
    DIST --> BRANCH2[Show-ups X less than C]
    BRANCH1 --> COSTOV[Overbooking Cost Co incurred]
    BRANCH2 --> COSTSPL[Spoilage Cost Cs incurred]
    COSTOV --> NETC[Total Expected Cost]
    COSTSPL --> NETC
    NETC --> PROF2[Net Expected Profit NR minus Expected Cost]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define overbooking and state the two cost components involved. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
> **Overbooking** is the practice of accepting a number of reservations $N$ that exceeds the actual service capacity $C$ ($N > C$) to compensate for no-shows.  
> The two opposing cost components are:
> 1. **Spoilage Cost ($C_s$):** Revenue lost due to unused capacity (no-shows).
> 2. **Overbooking Cost ($C_o$):** Compensation paid to customers denied service (bumped).
>
> The optimal $N^{*}$ balances these two costs so as to **maximize expected profit**.

### Question 2: Write the marginal-revenue vs marginal-cost break-even condition for an optimal overbooking decision. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The break-even (optimality) condition is:

$$
R \;=\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)
$$

where $R$ is the marginal revenue per booking, $C_{o}$ is the per-customer denial cost, $p$ is the show probability, and $P(X_{N} \ge C)$ is the probability that the first $N$ bookings already fill or exceed capacity $C$. Accept one more booking while the inequality $R > C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C)$ holds.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A hotel has **150 rooms** available for a festival night. Based on historical data, the probability that a reserved guest shows up is **$p = 0.90$**. The room tariff is **$R = \text{₹}3{,}500$** per night. If an overbooked guest is denied a room, the hotel compensates with a free upgrade at a cost of **$C_{o} = \text{₹}6{,}000$** per guest.

**(a)** Formulate the marginal analysis condition for optimal overbooking and compute the target probability $P(X_{N} \ge C)$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the Normal approximation, find the **optimal number of reservations $N^{*}$** the hotel should accept. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

The marginal analysis rule for optimal overbooking is:

$$
R \;>\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C) \quad \text{(accept)}
$$

At the break-even (optimal) point:

$$
R \;=\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N^{*}} \ge C)
$$

Substituting the values $R = 3500$, $C_{o} = 6000$, $p = 0.90$, $C = 150$:

$$
3500 \;=\; 6000 \times 0.90 \times P(X_{N} \ge 150)
$$

$$
P(X_{N} \ge 150) \;=\; \frac{3500}{6000 \times 0.90} \;=\; \frac{3500}{5400} \;=\; 0.6481
$$

**Target probability** $P(X_{N} \ge 150) = 0.6481$.

**Model Solution (b):**

Using the Normal approximation $X_{N} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$:

$$
\mu = Np = 0.90N, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{Np(1-p)} = \sqrt{0.09N}
$$

With continuity correction:

$$
P(X \ge 150) \;=\; P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{149.5 - 0.90N}{\sqrt{0.09N}}\right) \;=\; 0.6481
$$

The corresponding z-value is $z_{1 - 0.6481} = z_{0.3519} = -0.385$.

Setting up the equation:

$$
\frac{149.5 - 0.90N}{\sqrt{0.09N}} \;=\; -0.385
$$

$$
149.5 - 0.90N \;=\; -0.385 \sqrt{0.09N} \;=\; -0.1155 \sqrt{N}
$$

Squaring and rearranging:

$$
(149.5 - 0.90N)^{2} \;=\; 0.01334 N
$$

Solving numerically (try $N = 168$):

$$
\mu = 151.2,\; \sigma = 3.889,\; P(X \ge 150) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{149.5 - 151.2}{3.889}\right) = P(Z \ge -0.437) = 0.669
$$

Slightly high. Try $N = 167$:

$$
\mu = 150.3,\; \sigma = 3.878,\; P(X \ge 150) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{149.5 - 150.3}{3.878}\right) = P(Z \ge -0.206) = 0.582
$$

Too low. Try $N = 168$ matches closer. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{N^{*} = 168 \text{ reservations}}
$$

The hotel should accept **168 reservations** against **150 rooms** — an overbook level of **18 rooms**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A restaurant has **40 tables**. The show probability for a reservation is **$p = 0.80$**. The average bill per table is **$R = \text{₹}1{,}200$**. The cost of refusing a confirmed reservation is **$C_{o} = \text{₹}2{,}500$** (voucher + reputation loss). The cost of an empty table is **$C_{s} = \text{₹}800$** (wasted setup + lost revenue).

**(a)** Write the two-cost marginal analysis condition and compute the **target $P(X \ge C)$** that yields the optimal $N^{*}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Apply the Normal approximation to determine the **optimal number of reservations** $N^{*}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

With both overbooking and spoilage costs, the marginal rule is:

$$
R \;=\; C_{o} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \ge C) \;-\; C_{s} \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N} \le C - 1)
$$

Substituting $R = 1200$, $C_{o} = 2500$, $C_{s} = 800$, $p = 0.80$, $C = 40$:

$$
1200 \;=\; 2500 \times 0.80 \times P(X \ge 40) - 800 \times 0.80 \times P(X \le 39)
$$

$$
1200 \;=\; 2000 \cdot P(X \ge 40) - 640 \cdot P(X \le 39)
$$

Since $P(X \le 39) = 1 - P(X \ge 40)$:

$$
1200 \;=\; 2000 \cdot P(X \ge 40) - 640 + 640 \cdot P(X \ge 40)
$$

$$
1840 \;=\; 2640 \cdot P(X \ge 40)
$$

$$
P(X \ge 40) \;=\; \frac{1840}{2640} \;=\; 0.6970
$$

**Target probability:** $P(X_{N} \ge 40) = 0.6970$, with corresponding $z_{0.3030} = -0.513$.

**Model Solution (b):**

Trial $N = 48$:

$$
\mu = 0.80 \times 48 = 38.4,\; \sigma = \sqrt{48 \times 0.16} = 2.771
$$

$$
P(X \ge 40) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{39.5 - 38.4}{2.771}\right) = P(Z \ge 0.397) = 0.346
$$

Too low. Trial $N = 52$:

$$
\mu = 41.6,\; \sigma = \sqrt{52 \times 0.16} = 2.884
$$

$$
P(X \ge 40) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{39.5 - 41.6}{2.884}\right) = P(Z \ge -0.728) = 0.767
$$

Slightly high. Try $N = 51$:

$$
\mu = 40.8,\; \sigma = \sqrt{51 \times 0.16} = 2.858
$$

$$
P(X \ge 40) = P\!\left(Z \ge \frac{39.5 - 40.8}{2.858}\right) = P(Z \ge -0.455) = 0.676
$$

Slightly low. Try $N = 52$ is the closest acceptable. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{N^{*} = 52 \text{ reservations}}
$$

The restaurant should accept **52 reservations** for **40 tables** — an overbook level of **12 tables**.

Expected profit:

$$
E[\pi] = 52 \times 1200 - E[\text{overbooking cost}] = 62{,}400 - 6400 = \text{₹}56{,}000
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the continuity correction** ($C - 0.5$ instead of $C$) — loses **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up MR and MC** — writing $MR = C_o$ or $MC = R$ — loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not specifying the optimization rule** — the model answer must explicitly say "accept while $R > C_o \cdot p \cdot P(X \ge C)$" — losing **1 mark** if missing.
> 4. **Using Poisson instead of Normal approximation** for large $N$ is acceptable but the formula form must be correctly stated.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute the overbook level** $N^{*} - C$ in the final answer — loses **1 mark** as it is a "complete-the-question" requirement.
> 6. **Carrying arithmetic errors in the z-table lookup** — always verify the continuity correction sign.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Overbooking Decisions**

- **Core idea:** Sell $N > C$ to recover lost capacity from no-shows.
- **Decision variable:** $N$ (number of reservations accepted).
- **Cost components:** $C_o$ (denial cost) and $C_s$ (spoilage cost).
- **Show probability:** $p$; no-show probability: $1 - p$.
- **Random variable:** $X \sim \text{Bin}(N, p)$ — number of actual show-ups.
- **MR formula:** $MR = R$ (revenue per booking).
- **MC formula (single-cost case):** $MC = C_o \cdot p \cdot P(X \ge C)$.
- **MC formula (two-cost case):** $MC = C_o \cdot p \cdot P(X \ge C) - C_s \cdot p \cdot P(X \le C - 1)$.
- **Optimality rule:** Accept booking while $MR > MC$; stop when $MR < MC$.
- **Break-even equation:** $R = C_o \cdot p \cdot P(X_{N^{*}} \ge C)$ (single-cost).
- **Normal approximation** (for large $N$): $\mu = Np$, $\sigma = \sqrt{Np(1-p)}$, use continuity correction $C - 0.5$.
- **Z-value lookup:** $P(X \ge C) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ where $z = (C - 0.5 - Np) / \sigma$.
- **Overbook level:** $N^{*} - C$ (the number of "extra" bookings accepted).
- **Expected profit formula:** $E[\pi] = N \cdot R - C_o \cdot E[\max(0, X - C)] - C_s \cdot E[\max(0, C - X)]$.
- **Real-world users:** Airlines, Hotels, Hospitals, Restaurants, Cloud-service providers.
- **Key risk:** Under-overbook → revenue loss; Over-overbook → compensation cost; the optimal $N$ **balances both**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Driven Predictive Models - Retail Pricing Markdowns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Driven Predictive Models - Retail Pricing Markdowns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data-Driven Predictive Models for Retail Pricing Markdowns** are quantitative analytical frameworks that leverage historical sales, inventory, competitor pricing, customer behaviour, and seasonal variables to forecast demand at discounted price points and recommend optimal markdown depths, timing, and duration. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, these models fall under the umbrella of *prescriptive and predictive analytics* applied to *revenue management* problems characteristic of the retail sector.

A **markdown** is defined as the deliberate reduction of the listed selling price of a product below its original (full) price, typically used to clear seasonal inventory, accelerate stock rotation, respond to competitive pressure, or stimulate demand in low-traffic periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes that a markdown is not a uniform discount — it is a *strategic lever* whose depth, duration, and timing must be predicted and optimized using data-driven models to maximize total contribution margin (revenue minus variable cost) over the product's remaining lifecycle.

Mathematically, the markdown problem is formulated as:

$$ \max_{p(t)} \quad \Pi = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ p(t) - c \right] \cdot D\!\left(p(t),\, \mathbf{x}(t)\right) $$

subject to the inventory constraint:

$$ \sum_{t=1}^{T} D\!\left(p(t),\, \mathbf{x}(t)\right) \;\le\; I_0 $$

where:
- $p(t)$ = price charged at period $t$
- $c$ = unit cost
- $D(\cdot)$ = predicted demand function
- $\mathbf{x}(t)$ = vector of contextual features (week, competitor price, weather, stockout flag, etc.)
- $I_0$ = initial inventory
- $T$ = markdown horizon

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fish market vendor at the end of the day. The freshest catch was sold at full price early in the morning. As the day progresses, ice melts, freshness decays, and unsold fish risk being thrown away. The vendor mentally (or now, with analytics) estimates:
- How many fish are likely to sell at full price tomorrow? (Forecast)
- If I lower the price by 20% today, how many *additional* customers will I attract? (Price elasticity)
- If I wait until the last hour and slash 50%, will I still have enough time to clear stock? (Markdown timing)

A *data-driven predictive model* does exactly this — but across **thousands of SKUs**, hundreds of stores, and millions of transactions — converting intuition into a repeatable, optimizable algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition (for first-time learners):** A data-driven predictive model for retail markdowns is a mathematical recipe that uses past sales data to predict *how many units* will sell at *which discounted price*, so the retailer can decide *when, how deep, and for how long* to discount a product to maximize profit while clearing inventory.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Markdown Models

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Price Elasticity of Demand | $\varepsilon$ | $-0.5$ to $-3.0$ | Measures % change in quantity for 1% change in price |
| Markdown Depth | $\delta$ | 5% – 70% | Percentage reduction from full price |
| Sell-Through Rate | $STR$ | 0% – 100% | Units sold ÷ Units received |
| Gross Margin Retention | $GMR$ | 30% – 95% | Realized revenue ÷ Full-price revenue |
| Weeks of Supply | $WOS$ | 1 – 52 | Inventory ÷ Average weekly sales |
| Cannibalization Rate | $\kappa$ | 0% – 30% | % of markdown sales pulled from full-price sales |
| Pull-Forward Effect | $\rho$ | 5% – 25% | % of future demand consumed early by current markdown |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** These metrics are *standardized* across retail analytics platforms (Oracle Retail, SAP CAR, SAS Markdown Optimization) — knowing their definitions is essential for KTU viva and interview questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Markdown Optimization Problem

A robust data-driven markdown model is built in **five logical layers**. Each layer is a building block that the upper layer depends upon.

### Layer 1 — Demand Forecasting Engine
- **Why:** Before deciding *how much* to discount, we must know *how much* would sell at the current price.
- **How:** Train supervised learning models (regression, gradient boosting, ARIMA, Prophet) on historical SKU-store-week sales with features such as price, promotions, holidays, weather, and lag sales.
- **Output:** $\hat{D}_0$ = baseline (no-markdown) demand forecast for the markdown horizon.

### Layer 2 — Price Elasticity Estimation
- **Why:** Without elasticity, a "10% off" is a guess; with elasticity, it is a controllable lever.
- **How:** Estimate $\varepsilon$ via log-log OLS regression:
$$ \ln(Q) = \alpha + \varepsilon \ln(P) + \mathbf{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X} + u $$
- **Output:** $\hat{\varepsilon}$ per SKU/store segment.

### Layer 3 — Cannibalization & Pull-Forward Correction
- **Why:** A discount today may simply shift sales from next week (pull-forward) or from neighboring SKUs (cannibalization). Net incremental demand is what matters.
- **How:** Apply correction factors $\kappa$ (cannibalization) and $\rho$ (pull-forward) calibrated via controlled A/B tests or difference-in-differences analysis.
- **Output:** *Incremental* demand $D^{inc} = D^{gross} \cdot (1 - \kappa - \rho)$.

### Layer 4 — Inventory-Aware Markdown Depth Selector
- **Why:** Markdowns are bounded by stock. Over-discounting wastes margin; under-discounting triggers end-of-season stockouts/leftovers.
- **How:** Solve the constrained optimization:
$$ p^*(t) = \arg\max_{p} \; \left[ (p-c)\cdot D^{inc}(p) \right] \quad \text{s.t. } \sum_t D^{inc}(p(t)) \ge I_0 - I_{target} $$
- **Output:** Optimal price trajectory $p^*(1), p^*(2), \dots, p^*(T)$.

### Layer 5 — Markdown Timing & Duration Engine
- **Why:** The same 30% discount in Week 2 vs Week 8 produces very different margins because the first discounts high-margin full-price sales (cannibalization), while late discounts salvage stranded inventory.
- **How:** Markov Decision Process (MDP) or dynamic programming over state $(t, I_t)$:
$$ V(t, I_t) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[ r(t,I_t,a) + \gamma\, V(t+1, I_{t+1}) \right] $$
- **Output:** Policy $\pi^*(t, I_t)$ mapping state → markdown action.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RENDERING RULE:** All vertical bars use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Price Elasticity | $\varepsilon \;=\; \dfrac{\%\,\Delta Q}{\%\,\Delta P} \;=\; \dfrac{dQ}{dP}\cdot\dfrac{P}{Q}$ | Sensitivity of demand to price change | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Constant-Elasticity Demand | $Q(p) \;=\; k \cdot p^{\varepsilon}$ | Power-law demand curve; basis of many markdown models | Units of Q |
| 3 | Log-Log OLS Estimator | $\ln Q_i \;=\; \alpha + \varepsilon \ln P_i + \mathbf{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}_i + u_i$ | Empirical estimation of $\varepsilon$ from transactions | — |
| 4 | Markdown Depth | $\delta \;=\; \dfrac{p_{full} - p_{md}}{p_{full}}$ | Fractional price reduction | Unitless (0–1) |
| 5 | Contribution Margin | $CM \;=\; (p - c)\cdot Q$ | Per-unit profit; KTU asks this in nearly every problem | Currency |
| 6 | Total Markdown Profit | $\Pi_{md} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} (p(t) - c)\, D^{inc}(p(t))$ | Objective function of the markdown model | Currency |
| 7 | Sell-Through Rate | $STR \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{sold}}{I_0} \cdot 100\%$ | Inventory clearance efficiency | % |
| 8 | Weeks of Supply | $WOS \;=\; \dfrac{I_t}{\bar{Q}_{weekly}}$ | How long current stock will last at current demand rate | Weeks |
| 9 | Cannibalization-Adj. Demand | $D^{inc} \;=\; D^{gross}\,(1 - \kappa - \rho)$ | Net new demand from a markdown | Units |
| 10 | Bell-Wright Reference Price | $p^{ref}_t \;=\; \alpha p^{ref}_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)\, p_{t-1}$ | Customer's internal price expectation; affects perceived discount | Currency |
| 11 | MDP Value Function | $V(s) \;=\; \max_a \left[ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\vert s,a)\, V(s') \right]$ | Optimal markdown policy | Currency |
| 12 | Newsvendor Markdown | $p^* \;:\; F\!\left(\dfrac{p^*-c}{p^*}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{I_0 - b}{c_f + c_o}$ | Single-period markdown under demand uncertainty | Currency |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fashion Retail (Zara, H&M):** Markdown models predict sell-through by SKU-week and trigger automated price reductions.
- **Grocery (Walmart, Tesco):** Perishable markdown models reduce food waste by 20–40%.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon):** Real-time dynamic markdown using reinforcement learning.
- **Automotive:** End-of-model-year clearance via multi-stage markdown ladders.
- **Hospitality (Hotels/Airlines):** Same mathematical framework underpins *revenue management systems* — a KTU-favourite interview discussion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** The course outcomes (COs) for Module 4 map to *Apply predictive modelling techniques to business decision-making* — markdown optimization is the canonical application.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Markdown Depth Under Constant Elasticity

**Setup:** A retailer has $I_0$ units of a seasonal SKU. Demand follows a constant-elasticity curve. Cost is $c$ per unit. Find the markdown price $p^*$ that maximizes total contribution margin.

**Step 1 — Write the demand function.**
With constant elasticity $\varepsilon < 0$:
$$ Q(p) = k \cdot p^{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 2 — Write the profit function.**
$$ \Pi(p) \;=\; (p - c)\, Q(p) \;=\; (p - c)\, k\, p^{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $p$ and set to zero.**
$$ \frac{d\Pi}{dp} \;=\; k\, p^{\varepsilon} + (p-c)\, k\, \varepsilon\, p^{\varepsilon-1} \;=\; 0 $$

Factor out $k\, p^{\varepsilon-1}$ (which is non-zero for $p>0$):
$$ k\, p^{\varepsilon-1}\,\bigl[\, p + (p-c)\,\varepsilon \,\bigr] \;=\; 0 $$

**Step 4 — Solve the bracketed term.**
$$ p + \varepsilon(p - c) \;=\; 0 $$
$$ p + \varepsilon p - \varepsilon c \;=\; 0 $$
$$ p\,(1 + \varepsilon) \;=\; \varepsilon c $$
$$ \boxed{\, p^* \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon\, c}{1 + \varepsilon} \,} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to markdown depth.**
$$ \delta^* \;=\; 1 - \frac{p^*}{p_{full}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\varepsilon c}{(1+\varepsilon)\,p_{full}} $$

**Step 6 — Sanity checks.**
- If $\varepsilon = -1$: $p^* = -\dfrac{-c}{0}$ → boundary (infinite price); interpret as "elastic enough to never discount."
- If $\varepsilon \to 0$: $p^* \to 0$; near-zero elasticity → discount is useless.
- If $\varepsilon = -2, c = 50$: $p^* = \dfrac{-2\cdot 50}{1-2} = \dfrac{-100}{-1} = 100$. So if full price is 150, markdown depth is 33.3%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The optimal price is the classic *monopoly markup* formula, expressed in elasticity form. The same derivation underlies airline and monopoly pricing theory — a unifying KTU concept.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Markdown Depth with Cannibalization Adjustment

**Setup:** Full-price demand is $Q_0$ per week. A fraction $\kappa$ of markdown sales would have occurred anyway at full price. The "incremental" lift from a markdown is reduced.

**Step 1 — Gross demand under markdown.**
$$ Q^{gross}(p) \;=\; Q_0 \left(\frac{p}{p_{full}}\right)^{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 2 — Subtract cannibalized sales.**
Sales that *would* have happened at full price = $\kappa \cdot Q^{gross}(p)$. So:
$$ Q^{inc}(p) \;=\; (1-\kappa)\, Q_0 \left(\frac{p}{p_{full}}\right)^{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 3 — Profit with cannibalization.**
$$ \Pi(p) \;=\; (p - c)\, (1-\kappa)\, Q_0 \left(\frac{p}{p_{full}}\right)^{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and solve (identical steps to Derivation 1).**
The factor $(1-\kappa)Q_0$ is a positive constant, so the FOC yields:
$$ p^* \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon\, c}{1 + \varepsilon} \quad \text{(unchanged!)} $$

**Step 5 — Markdown profit with vs without cannibalization.**
$$ \frac{\Pi^{inc}}{\Pi^{gross}} \;=\; (1-\kappa) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cannibalization does not change the *optimal price*, but it *reduces profit linearly* by factor $(1-\kappa)$. This is why retailers must measure $\kappa$ carefully via holdout tests.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Newsvendor-Based Markdown for Perishables

**Setup:** Single markdown period with uncertain demand $D \sim F(\cdot)$. Salvage value $= 0$. Under-stocking cost $= c_u = p - c$. Over-stocking cost (leftover) $= c_o = c$.

**Step 1 — Expected profit as a function of order/markdown quantity $Q$.**
$$ \mathbb{E}[\Pi(Q)] \;=\; (p - c)\, \mathbb{E}[\min(D, Q)] - c\,\mathbb{E}[(Q - D)^+] $$

**Step 2 — Use the standard Newsvendor identity.**
$$ \mathbb{E}[\min(D,Q)] \;=\; Q - \int_0^Q F(x)\,dx $$

So:
$$ \mathbb{E}[\Pi(Q)] \;=\; (p-c)\!\left[Q - \int_0^Q F(x)\,dx\right] - c\!\left[Q - \mathbb{E}[D] + \int_0^Q F(x)\,dx\right] $$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $Q$ and set to zero.**
$$ \frac{d\,\mathbb{E}[\Pi]}{dQ} \;=\; (p-c)\,[1 - F(Q)] - c\,F(Q) \;=\; 0 $$

**Step 4 — Solve for the critical fractile.**
$$ (p-c)\,[1 - F(Q^*)] \;=\; c\,F(Q^*) $$
$$ (p-c) - (p-c)\,F(Q^*) \;=\; c\,F(Q^*) $$
$$ (p-c) \;=\; F(Q^*)\,\bigl[(p-c) + c\bigr] \;=\; F(Q^*)\,p $$
$$ \boxed{\, F(Q^*) \;=\; \frac{p - c}{p} \,} $$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**
The optimal markdown should be deep enough to ensure a sell-through fractile equal to the *contribution margin ratio*. For $p = 80, c = 50$: $F(Q^*) = 30/80 = 0.375$ — so the markdown is justified only if historical data suggests 37.5% probability of selling out at that price.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Demand Assumption | Inventory | Output | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Constant-Elasticity | $Q = k p^{\varepsilon}$ | Infinite | Single $p^*$ | Conceptual / Part A |
| Cannibalization-Adjusted | $Q^{inc} = (1-\kappa)\,Q^{gross}$ | Infinite | $p^*$ + profit ratio | Part B derivation |
| Newsvendor Markdown | Stochastic $D \sim F$ | Single period | Critical fractile | Perishables / EoL |
| MDP / Dynamic Programming | State-dependent | Multi-period | Optimal policy | Fashion markdown |
| Regression-Based ML | Empirically estimated | Any horizon | Demand forecast | Modern retail |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual / Definition

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*"Define a markdown in the context of retail analytics. How does it differ from a discount or promotion?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A markdown is a *deliberate reduction* in the listed selling price of a product, typically applied to clear seasonal inventory, accelerate stock rotation, or respond to competitive pressure.
- **(1 mark)** A markdown is *permanent for the cycle* (a price reset) and is applied at the *retailer's discretion on a SKU–store–time basis*, unlike a *promotion* which is short-term and pre-scheduled.
- **(1 mark)** Unlike a *coupon-based discount* which is consumer-triggered, a markdown is retailer-triggered and is a strategic revenue-management lever optimized via predictive models.

### Question A2 — Short Numerical / Conceptual

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"A retailer observes that a 10% price reduction increased unit sales by 25%. Compute the price elasticity of demand. Is the demand elastic or inelastic?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$ \varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{+25\%}{-10\%} = -2.5 $$
- **(1 mark)** $\varepsilon = -2.5$
- **(1 mark)** Since $\vert \varepsilon \vert = 2.5 > 1$, demand is **elastic**.
- **(1 mark)** Implication: a markdown will *increase total revenue* (and possibly profit) — the retailer should discount.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Demand-Function Optimization

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

A retailer has $I_0 = 1000$ units of a seasonal apparel SKU. Unit cost $c = 400$. Full price $p_{full} = 800$. Estimated demand follows a constant-elasticity curve $Q(p) = 50{,}000 \cdot p^{-1.8}$.

**(a)** Derive the profit-maximizing markdown price $p^*$. Compute the markdown depth $\delta^*$.  
**(b)** If a controlled A/B test reveals that 20% of markdown sales are cannibalized from full-price demand, recompute the *effective* contribution margin and comment on whether the markdown should still be executed.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write profit function (1 mark).**
$$ \Pi(p) = (p - 400)\cdot 50{,}000\cdot p^{-1.8} $$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $p$ (2 marks).**
$$ \frac{d\Pi}{dp} = 50{,}000\,p^{-1.8} + (p-400)\cdot 50{,}000\cdot(-1.8)\,p^{-2.8} $$

Factor $50{,}000\,p^{-2.8}$:
$$ \frac{d\Pi}{dp} = 50{,}000\,p^{-2.8}\bigl[\,p - 1.8(p-400)\,\bigr] $$

**Step 3 — Set to zero and solve (2 marks).**
$$ p - 1.8p + 720 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -0.8p + 720 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; p^* = 900 $$

> Wait — verify: $p^* = \frac{\varepsilon c}{1+\varepsilon} = \frac{-1.8 \cdot 400}{1 - 1.8} = \frac{-720}{-0.8} = 900$.

But $p^* = 900 > p_{full} = 800$. This means **the model recommends a *price increase*, not a markdown!** The current full price is *below* the profit-maximizing price for this elasticity.

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks).**
Markdown depth is **negative** ($\delta^* = -12.5\%$), meaning the optimal action is to *raise* the price. The retailer should NOT mark down. If a markdown is mandatory (e.g., end-of-season), the optimal feasible markdown is $\delta = 0\%$ (no reduction).

**Optimal quantities (verification):**
$$ Q(800) = 50{,}000 \cdot 800^{-1.8} \approx 50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1}{800^{1.8}} \approx 0.19 \text{ units (degenerate)} $$

> For KTU credit, students should explicitly state the *direction* of the recommendation, not just plug numbers.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute base profit at $p = 800, c = 400$ (1 mark).**
Assume realized demand = baseline forecast $Q(800)$.
$$ \Pi^{gross} = (800 - 400)\cdot Q(800) $$

**Step 2 — Apply cannibalization (1 mark).**
Incremental demand is $80\%$ of gross:
$$ Q^{inc} = 0.8 \cdot Q(800) $$

**Step 3 — Compute incremental profit (1 mark).**
$$ \Pi^{inc} = 400 \cdot 0.8 \cdot Q(800) = 320 \cdot Q(800) $$

**Step 4 — Compare with no-markdown scenario (2 marks).**
Without the markdown, the same units would have been sold at full price with $Q(800)$ baseline and *no* cannibalization (since nothing was discounted). So the markdown *adds no net new demand*; it just reduces price:
$$ \Delta \Pi = \Pi^{inc} - \Pi^{no\,md} = 320\,Q(800) - 400\,Q(800) = -80\,Q(800) $$

**Step 5 — Recommendation (2 marks).**
The markdown **decreases profit by $80 \cdot Q(800)$** and should **not be executed**. With 20% cannibalization, the break-even incremental ratio is $1 - \kappa = 0.8$, meaning the price drop must be less than 20% to break even. Since 0% markdown is the (constrained) optimum, **hold full price** or reconsider the elasticity estimate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Reporting $p^* = 900$ as a markdown recommendation without checking whether it lies *above* $p_{full}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Failing to compute $\Delta \Pi$ and only computing $\Pi^{inc}$ in part (b).
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing cannibalization rate $\kappa$ with markdown depth $\delta$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Newsvendor-Style Markdown for Perishables

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Alternative Choice]**

A grocery chain has 500 kg of strawberries with a 1-day shelf life. Cost $= \text{₹}80/\text{kg}$. If marked down to $p$, weekly demand is $D \sim \text{Normal}(\mu = 600,\, \sigma = 150)$ truncated at zero. Unsold strawberries have salvage value $= 0$. Compute the markdown price that maximizes expected profit.

**(a)** Derive the critical fractile formula and compute the optimal markdown price numerically.  
**(b)** Suppose the retailer also has an alternative channel (donation) that yields salvage value $s = 30$. Recompute the optimal markdown price and comment on the trade-off.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall newsvendor critical fractile (1 mark).**
$$ F(Q^*) = \frac{p - c}{p} \quad \text{where } c \text{ is the over-stocking cost. With } s = 0,\ c_o = c. $$

**Step 2 — Set up the equation for $p$ given demand distribution (2 marks).**
We need the markdown $p$ such that the expected *sales* (i.e., $\mathbb{E}[\min(D, I_0)]$) equals $I_0 \cdot \dfrac{p-c}{p}$. For high $\mu$ relative to $I_0$, sales $\approx I_0$ when $p$ is very low.

We solve iteratively: try $p = 100$ → fractile needed = $(100-80)/100 = 0.20$. That is, sell 20% of stock expected. Since $I_0 = 500$, we need $\mathbb{E}[\min(D,500)] = 100$ kg, implying very high markdown required.

Try $p = 90$ → fractile $= 10/90 = 0.111$ → target sales = $55.5$ kg. Still very low.

Try $p = 85$ → fractile $= 5/85 = 0.0588$ → target = $29.4$ kg.

The required *expected sales* is much less than $I_0$ because the demand mean $\mu = 600$ is high but variance is also high. To meet a low fractile, $p$ must approach $c = 80$.

**Step 3 — Practical interpretation (2 marks).**
The *cost-to-price ratio* $c/p$ must equal the probability of selling out at price $p$. Since $c = 80$, the markdown price must satisfy:
$$ p \to \frac{c}{1 - F(Q^*)} $$
For $Q^* = 500$ and $F(\cdot)$ of Normal(600, 150), the $z$-score for $Q^* = 500$ is $(500-600)/150 = -0.667$, giving $F \approx 0.252$. So $p = 80/(1-0.252) = 80/0.748 \approx \text{₹}106.95$.

**Step 4 — Final answer with commentary (2 marks).**
$$ \boxed{\, p^* \;\approx\; \text{₹}\,107/\text{kg} \,} $$
This is a **markup** (not a markdown) over the $80$ cost — meaning the strawberries are *under-priced* at $80$. The retailer should *raise* the price, or, if markdown is mandatory (clearance), markdown only modestly to $\text{₹}85$–$\text{₹}90$ for a *targeted* subset of inventory.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Re-derive critical fractile with salvage value $s$ (2 marks).**
Now $c_o = c - s = 80 - 30 = 50$ (over-stocking cost is reduced by salvage).
$$ F(Q^*) = \frac{c_u}{c_u + c_o} = \frac{p - c}{(p - c) + (c - s)} = \frac{p - c}{p - s} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for $p$ (2 marks).**
$$ F(Q^*)\,(p - s) = p - c $$
$$ p\,F(Q^*) - s\,F(Q^*) = p - c $$
$$ p\,[F(Q^*) - 1] = s\,F(Q^*) - c $$
$$ p = \frac{c - s\,F(Q^*)}{1 - F(Q^*)} $$

**Step 3 — Plug in numbers using same $F(500) = 0.252$ (1 mark).**
$$ p = \frac{80 - 30\cdot 0.252}{1 - 0.252} = \frac{80 - 7.56}{0.748} = \frac{72.44}{0.748} \approx \text{₹}\,96.85 $$

**Step 4 — Interpret trade-off (2 marks).**
With salvage channel: $p^* = 96.85$ vs. without salvage: $p^* = 106.95$ — **a deeper markdown is justified** (lower price). The donation channel acts as an *insurance policy*, reducing the cost of leftover stock, so the retailer can afford to lower the price and capture more demand-side upside. The trade-off: deeper markdown → higher volume but lower margin; salvage value → partial recovery on leftovers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting that $c_o = c - s$, not $c$. Students frequently write $c_o = c$ even when salvage is mentioned.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Mixing up $c_u$ and $c_o$ symbols. Under-stocking cost $c_u = p - c$; over-stocking cost $c_o = c - s$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Not stating the units of $p^*$ (₹/kg).
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Reporting a number without showing the algebraic rearrangement from $F(Q^*)$ to $p$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Retail Pricing Markdowns**

- ☐ **Markdown ≠ Discount ≠ Promotion.** Markdown is a *retailer-initiated, lifecycle-based price reset* used for inventory clearance.
- ☐ The objective is to **maximize total contribution margin**, not just sell-through rate.
- ☐ **Price Elasticity $\varepsilon$** is the single most important parameter; it is negative and typically $-0.5$ to $-3.0$ for fashion/grocery.
- ☐ **Constant-elasticity demand** $Q = k p^{\varepsilon}$ is the canonical model for theoretical questions.
- ☐ **Optimal monopoly price** under constant elasticity: $p^* = \dfrac{\varepsilon\, c}{1+\varepsilon}$ — derived by FOC on profit.
- ☐ **Cannibalization rate $\kappa$** reduces *gross* demand to *incremental* demand: $Q^{inc} = (1-\kappa)\,Q^{gross}$. Cannibalization does not change $p^*$ but reduces profit by factor $(1-\kappa)$.
- ☐ **Pull-forward effect $\rho$** captures demand shifted from *future periods*, not from competitors. Combined with $\kappa$, the net incremental factor is $(1-\kappa-\rho)$.
- ☐ **Newsvendor markdown** for perishable/seasonal goods: critical fractile $F(Q^*) = \dfrac{p-c}{p}$ when salvage $s = 0$.
- ☐ With salvage value $s > 0$, the critical fractile becomes $F(Q^*) = \dfrac{p-c}{p-s}$, and the optimal price *decreases* (deeper markdown).
- ☐ **MDP / Dynamic Programming** is the gold standard for multi-period markdown; state = (time, inventory), action = markdown depth.
- ☐ **Markdown depth** $\delta = (p_{full} - p_{md})/p_{full}$ — a *negative* $\delta$ means price increase.
- ☐ **Sell-through rate** $STR$ and **Weeks of Supply** $WOS$ are operational KPIs that trigger markdown actions.
- ☐ Data-driven models *augment*, not replace, managerial judgement — always validate with A/B tests.
- ☐ The closed-loop architecture (forecast → optimize → execute → measure → retrain) is the engineering blueprint for any modern retail pricing system.
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark questions, *always* show: (i) profit function, (ii) FOC, (iii) algebraic solution, (iv) numerical substitution, (v) managerial interpretation.
- ☐ For KTU 3-mark questions, define the term *and* give a 1-sentence practical example — definitions alone earn only 1 mark.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Data Driven Predictive Models: Retail Pricing Markdowns**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling Relationships and Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modeling Relationships and Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modeling Relationships and Trends** in Business Analytics refers to the systematic application of statistical and mathematical techniques to identify, quantify, and forecast the inherent patterns that exist between business variables and across temporal dimensions. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic encompasses three fundamental pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Official KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Modeling Relationships and Trends is the analytical process of constructing **mathematical models** that capture the statistical association between two or more business variables (cross-sectional relationships) and the temporal evolution of a single variable over time (longitudinal trends). It is the quantitative backbone of **prescriptive and predictive analytics**.

The discipline is anchored on three measurable constructs:

1. **Correlation** ($\rho$ or $r$) — the *strength* and *direction* of a linear association.
2. **Regression** ($Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$) — the *functional form* of the relationship.
3. **Trend Analysis / Time Series Decomposition** — the *temporal structure* of a sequential dataset.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy — "The Weather Forecaster's Notebook"**
> Imagine a tea-shop owner in Kerala who wants to predict tomorrow's sales. He notices a pattern: **"When it rains heavily, fewer people walk in; when the temperature is mild, sales peak."** This is **modeling a relationship** (Rain vs. Sales). He also notices: **"Every December, sales spike regardless of weather."** This is **modeling a trend** (Seasonality + Long-term growth).
>
> The shopkeeper's *mental model* is exactly what we formalize statistically — a **Regression Equation** for the relationship and a **Time Series Decomposition** for the trend. Business analytics simply replaces his "gut feeling" with mathematically optimized coefficients that can be tested, validated, and deployed.

## 1.3 Core Constants, Notation & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE] > **Standard Business Analytics Notation Table**

| Symbol | Meaning | Business Context Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $Y$ | Dependent (Response) Variable | Sales Revenue, Customer Churn |
| $X$ | Independent (Predictor) Variable | Ad Spend, Temperature, Price |
| $r$ | Pearson Correlation Coefficient | Strength of Ad Spend vs. Sales |
| $\beta_0$ | Intercept (Baseline Value) | Sales when Ad Spend = 0 |
| $\beta_1$ | Slope (Marginal Effect) | ₹Sales gained per ₹1 Ad Spend |
| $\varepsilon$ | Error / Residual Term | Unobserved noise in business |
| $R^2$ | Coefficient of Determination | % of Sales variance explained |
| $T_t$ | Trend Component at time $t$ | Long-term sales growth |
| $S_t$ | Seasonal Component at time $t$ | Festive quarter sales boost |
| $C_t$ | Cyclical Component at time $t$ | Economic boom/bust effect |
| $I_t$ | Irregular / Random Component at time $t$ | One-off events, strikes |

The **standard business thresholds** used by KTU examiners are:

- $|r| < 0.3$ → **Weak** relationship
- $0.3 \leq |r| < 0.7$ → **Moderate** relationship
- $|r| \geq 0.7$ → **Strong** relationship
- $R^2 \geq 0.7$ → **Good** model fit
- Significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ (95% Confidence)
