# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars — A Structured Logic Breakdown

### Pillar A: Correlation Analysis (Measuring Association)

Correlation quantifies the **degree of co-movement** between two variables without implying causation. The most common metric is the **Pearson Product-Moment Correlation Coefficient**.

**Why Correlation First?**
Before building any predictive model, a business analyst *must* verify that the variables are statistically related. Building a regression on uncorrelated variables is a classic KTU examination pitfall.

**How It Works (Logic Steps):**
1. **Standardize** both variables: $Z_X = \frac{X - \bar{X}}{s_X}$, $Z_Y = \frac{Y - \bar{Y}}{s_Y}$.
2. **Multiply** the standardized pairs: $Z_{X_i} \cdot Z_{Y_i}$ for each observation.
3. **Average** the products across all $n$ observations.
4. This average *is* the Pearson correlation coefficient $r$.

> **Interpretation:** $r$ ranges from $-1$ (perfect negative) to $+1$ (perfect positive). A value of $0$ implies **no linear relationship**.

### Pillar B: Linear Regression (Modeling the Function)

Once correlation is confirmed, **regression** specifies the *exact functional form*. The **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method finds the line that minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances from the data points.

**Why OLS Specifically?**
The OLS estimator has a celebrated mathematical property — the **Gauss-Markov Theorem** — which states that among all *linear unbiased* estimators, OLS yields the one with the **minimum variance**. This makes it the gold standard in business analytics.

**How OLS Works (Logic Steps):**
1. Define the linear model: $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i$.
2. Define the **Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)**: $RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$.
3. Take partial derivatives of RSS with respect to $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.
4. Set the derivatives to zero (first-order optimality conditions).
5. Solve the resulting system of **Normal Equations** to obtain $\hat{\beta_0}$ and $\hat{\beta_1}$.

### Pillar C: Time Series Trend Analysis (Modeling Temporal Patterns)

A **time series** is a sequence of observations indexed by time. Business examples include monthly revenue, daily website traffic, and quarterly GDP. The classical **decomposition model** splits a series into systematic and random parts.

**Why Decompose?**
Different business drivers operate on different timescales. A retailer needs to know: *Is the sales drop a long-term decline (Trend) or just last month's Diwali effect wearing off (Seasonality)?* Decomposition answers precisely this.

**How Decomposition Works (Logic Steps):**
1. **Additive Model:** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ (used when seasonal variation is *constant*).
2. **Multiplicative Model:** $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$ (used when seasonal variation *grows* with trend).
3. **Isolate Trend** $T_t$: Apply moving averages of length $m$ (where $m$ = number of seasons).
4. **Detrend** the series: $Y_t - T_t$ (additive) or $Y_t / T_t$ (multiplicative).
5. **Isolate Seasonality** $S_t$: Average the detrended values for each season.
6. **Residual** $I_t$: Whatever remains after extracting $T$, $S$, and $C$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Formula Table — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Range | Unit |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Pearson Correlation | $r = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{\sqrt{[n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2][n \sum Y_i^2 - (\sum Y_i)^2]}}$ | $-1 \leq r \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Sample Mean | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | — | Same as $X$ |
| 3 | Sample Variance | $s_X^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | $s_X^2 \geq 0$ | $(unit)^2$ |
| 4 | OLS Slope | $\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ | All real numbers | $Y/X$ |
| 5 | OLS Intercept | $\hat{\beta_0} = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta_1} \bar{X}$ | All real numbers | Same as $Y$ |
| 6 | Regression Equation | $\hat{Y_i} = \hat{\beta_0} + \hat{\beta_1} X_i$ | — | Same as $Y$ |
| 7 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y_i})^2}{\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}$ | $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Standard Error of Estimate | $s_e = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y_i})^2}{n-2}}$ | $s_e \geq 0$ | Same as $Y$ |
| 9 | t-statistic for slope | $t = \frac{\hat{\beta_1}}{SE(\hat{\beta_1})}$ | Critical at $\alpha=0.05$: $\vert t \vert > 1.96$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Centered Moving Average (Trend) | $T_t = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-k} + Y_{t-k+1} + \dots + Y_{t+k-1} + 0.5 Y_{t+k}}{2k}$ | $m = 2k+1$ (odd) or $2k$ (even, centered) | Same as $Y_t$ |
| 11 | Additive Decomposition | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | $\sum S_t = 0$ over a period | Same as $Y_t$ |
| 12 | Multiplicative Decomposition | $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$ | $\sum S_t = m$ over a period | Same as $Y_t$ |
| 13 | Seasonal Index (Multiplicative) | $SI_j = \frac{\text{Avg of detrended values in season } j}{\text{Overall average}}$ | $SI_j \approx 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum \vert Y_i - \hat{Y_i} \vert$ | $MAE \geq 0$ | Same as $Y$ |
| 15 | Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y_i})^2}$ | $RMSE \geq 0$ | Same as $Y$ |

> [!WARNING] > **Critical Notation Rule:** In the markdown formula table, the absolute value symbol is rendered as `\vert \vert` instead of `\vert \vert` to avoid breaking the table's vertical-pipe syntax. Always write $\vert x \vert$ in your exam scripts.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Three-Test" Rule for Any Regression Model:**
> 1. **Significance Test** — Is the slope $\hat{\beta_1}$ statistically significant? (t-test, p-value $< 0.05$)
> 2. **Goodness-of-Fit Test** — Does the model explain enough variance? ($R^2 \geq 0.7$ preferred)
> 3. **Residual Test** — Are the errors random? (Check for patterns in residual plots)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the OLS Slope Coefficient $\hat{\beta_1}$

**Starting Premise:** We have $n$ paired observations $(X_1, Y_1), (X_2, Y_2), \dots, (X_n, Y_n)$ and we wish to fit a straight line $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ that minimizes the sum of squared vertical errors.

**Step 1 — Define the Residual for the $i^{th}$ observation:**

$$e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y_i} = Y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i)$$

**Explanation:** The residual $e_i$ is the vertical distance between the actual data point and the fitted regression line at $X_i$. The sign of $e_i$ indicates whether the model under-predicts ($e_i > 0$) or over-predicts ($e_i < 0$).

**Step 2 — Define the Residual Sum of Squares (RSS):**

$$RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2$$

**Explanation:** We square the residuals (a) to eliminate the sign, and (b) to penalize large errors more heavily. RSS is our objective function — the quantity we want to *minimize*.

**Step 3 — Differentiate RSS with respect to $\beta_0$:**

$$\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)$$

**Explanation:** We use the chain rule. The derivative of $(Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2$ with respect to $\beta_0$ is $2(Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) \cdot (-1)$.

**Step 4 — Set the partial derivative to zero (first-order condition):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the first normal equation:**

$$\sum Y_i = n \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum X_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by $n$ gives the averages. This is the **First Normal Equation**, which tells us the regression line always passes through the centroid $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$.

**Step 6 — Differentiate RSS with respect to $\beta_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)$$

**Explanation:** Now $\beta_1$ appears inside a linear term, so we multiply by $X_i$ via the chain rule.

**Step 7 — Set the partial derivative to zero (first-order condition):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

**Step 8 — Expand the second normal equation:**

$$\sum X_i Y_i = \beta_0 \sum X_i + \beta_1 \sum X_i^2$$

**Step 9 — Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$ from Step 5:**

$$\sum X_i Y_i = (\bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}) \sum X_i + \beta_1 \sum X_i^2$$

**Step 10 — Rearrange to isolate $\beta_1$:**

$$\sum X_i Y_i - \bar{Y} \sum X_i = \beta_1 \left( \sum X_i^2 - \bar{X} \sum X_i \right)$$

**Step 11 — Apply the algebraic identity:**

Using $\sum X_i = n \bar{X}$ and $\sum Y_i = n \bar{Y}$, we can rewrite:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum X_i Y_i - \bar{Y} \sum X_i &= \sum X_i Y_i - n \bar{X} \bar{Y} = \sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y}) \\ \sum X_i^2 - \bar{X} \sum X_i &= \sum X_i^2 - n \bar{X}^2 = \sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** This identity converts the sums into "centered" (deviation-from-mean) forms, which is the most computationally efficient representation.

**Step 12 — Final closed-form expression for $\hat{\beta_1}$:**

$$\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

**Final form equivalent** (using raw sums, which is more common in exam settings):

$$\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Proven:** The OLS estimator is derived purely from the calculus of minimizing RSS. This is the form KTU examiners expect in Part B 14-mark answers.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement [KTU Model Paper Style]:**
A retail chain in Kochi collects data on monthly advertising spend (₹ in '000s) and corresponding sales revenue (₹ in '000s) for 5 months:

| Month | Ad Spend $X$ | Sales $Y$ |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 10 | 30 |
| 2 | 20 | 45 |
| 3 | 30 | 50 |
| 4 | 40 | 65 |
| 5 | 50 | 80 |

**Compute the regression line, $R^2$, and predict sales when Ad Spend = ₹60,000.**

### Solution Table (Pre-computed Sums)

| $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $X_i^2$ | $Y_i^2$ | $X_i Y_i$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 10 | 30 | 100 | 900 | 300 |
| 20 | 45 | 400 | 2025 | 900 |
| 30 | 50 | 900 | 2500 | 1500 |
| 40 | 65 | 1600 | 4225 | 2600 |
| 50 | 80 | 2500 | 6400 | 4000 |
| $\sum X = 150$ | $\sum Y = 270$ | $\sum X^2 = 5500$ | $\sum Y^2 = 16050$ | $\sum XY = 9300$ |

With $n = 5$.

### Step 1: Compute the Slope $\hat{\beta_1}$

Using the raw-sums formula:

$$\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{n \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{5 \times 9300 - 150 \times 270}{5 \times 5500 - (150)^2}$$

**Numerator evaluation:**

$$5 \times 9300 = 46500 \quad ; \quad 150 \times 270 = 40500 \quad ; \quad 46500 - 40500 = 6000$$

**Denominator evaluation:**

$$5 \times 5500 = 27500 \quad ; \quad (150)^2 = 22500 \quad ; \quad 27500 - 22500 = 5000$$

**Final slope:**

$$\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{6000}{5000} = 1.2$$

**Interpretation:** Every additional ₹1,000 in advertising spend yields ₹1,200 in sales — a healthy positive ROI.

### Step 2: Compute the Intercept $\hat{\beta_0}$

$$\hat{\beta_0} = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta_1} \bar{X}$$

**Compute the means:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{150}{5} = 30 \quad ; \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{270}{5} = 54$$

**Substitution:**

$$\hat{\beta_0} = 54 - 1.2 \times 30 = 54 - 36 = 18$$

**Interpretation:** When ad spend is zero, baseline sales are ₹18,000 (organic revenue).

### Step 3: Write the Regression Equation

$$\hat{Y} = 18 + 1.2 X$$

### Step 4: Predict Sales when $X = 60$

$$\hat{Y} = 18 + 1.2 \times 60 = 18 + 72 = 90$$

**Interpretation:** When ad spend is ₹60,000, expected sales are ₹90,000.

### Step 5: Compute $R^2$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y_i})^2}{\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}$$

**Compute $\hat{Y_i}$ for each $X_i$:**

| $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $\hat{Y_i} = 18 + 1.2 X_i$ | $Y_i - \hat{Y_i}$ | $(Y_i - \hat{Y_i})^2$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | $(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 10 | 30 | 30 | 0 | 0 | -24 | 576 |
| 20 | 45 | 42 | 3 | 9 | -9 | 81 |
| 30 | 50 | 54 | -4 | 16 | -4 | 16 |
| 40 | 65 | 66 | -1 | 1 | 11 | 121 |
| 50 | 80 | 78 | 2 | 4 | 26 | 676 |
| | | | | **SSE = 30** | | **SST = 1470** |

**Final $R^2$ calculation:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{30}{1470} = 1 - 0.0204 = 0.9796 \approx 0.98$$

**Interpretation:** **97.96%** of the variation in sales is explained by ad spend — an **excellent** model fit for business analytics.

## 3.3 Time Series Trend Extraction — 4-Quarter Centered Moving Average

**Formula for even-period ($m=4$) centered moving average:**

$$T_t = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-2} + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1} + 0.5 Y_{t+2}}{4}$$

**Worked Example (Quarterly Sales, ₹ Lakhs):**

| Quarter $t$ | $Y_t$ | $T_t$ (Trend) | Seasonal + Irregular $Y_t - T_t$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Q1 | 100 | — | — |
| Q2 | 120 | 115.00 | +5.00 |
| Q3 | 140 | 125.00 | +15.00 |
| Q4 | 110 | 130.00 | -20.00 |
| Q1' | 120 | 135.00 | -15.00 |
| Q2' | 150 | 140.00 | +10.00 |
| Q3' | 160 | — | — |
| Q4' | 130 | — | — |

**Explanation of computation (e.g., $T_2$):**

$$T_2 = \frac{0.5 \times 100 + 120 + 140 + 110 + 0.5 \times 120}{4} = \frac{50 + 120 + 140 + 110 + 60}{4} = \frac{480}{4} = 115.00$$

**Seasonal Index Calculation (Additive):** Average the seasonal + irregular values for each quarter across all years, then adjust to sum to zero.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Relationship Modeling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Business Data X and Y] --> B1[Step 1 Data Cleaning and Validation]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2 Exploratory Analysis and Scatter Plot]
    C1 --> D1{Step 3 Is Correlation Significant?}
    D1 -- No --> E1[Step 3a Stop Reassess Variables]
    D1 -- Yes --> F1[Step 4 Fit OLS Regression Line]
    F1 --> G1[Step 5 Estimate Beta0 and Beta1]
    G1 --> H1[Step 6 Compute R2 and RMSE]
    H1 --> I1{Step 7 Is R2 above Threshold?}
    I1 -- No --> J1[Step 7a Try Multiple Regression or Non Linear]
    I1 -- Yes --> K1[Step 8 Residual Diagnostics]
    K1 --> L1{Step 9 Are Residuals Random?}
    L1 -- No --> M1[Step 9a Apply Box Cox or Log Transform]
    L1 -- Yes --> N1[Step 10 Deploy Model for Forecasting]
    N1 --> O1[Final Business Decision and ROI]
```

## 4.2 Time Series Decomposition Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Raw Time Series Yt] --> B2[Step 1 Identify Period m]
    B2 --> C2[Step 2 Apply Centered Moving Average of length m]
    C2 --> D2[Step 3 Extract Trend Component Tt]
    D2 --> E2{Step 4 Additive or Multiplicative Model}
    E2 -- Additive --> F2[Step 5a Compute Yt minus Tt to get St plus It]
    E2 -- Multiplicative --> G2[Step 5b Compute Yt divided by Tt to get St times It]
    F2 --> H2[Step 6 Average seasonal residuals by season to get St]
    G2 --> H2
    H2 --> I2[Step 7 Compute Irregular It as Residual]
    I2 --> J2[Step 8 Forecast Yt plus h using Tt plus h plus Sh]
    J2 --> K2[Final Business Forecast Output]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Business Question Type] --> B3{Are you predicting a number or a category}
    B3 -- Number --> C3{Do you have time indexed data}
    B3 -- Category --> D3[Step Logistic Regression or Classification Tree]
    C3 -- Yes --> E3{Is there strong seasonality}
    C3 -- No --> F3[Step Simple or Multiple Linear Regression]
    E3 -- Yes --> G3[Step Seasonal ARIMA or Holt Winters]
    E3 -- No --> H3[Step Exponential Smoothing or Trend Line]
    F3 --> I3{How many predictors k}
    I3 -- k equals 1 --> J3[Step Simple Linear Regression]
    I3 -- k greater than 1 --> K3[Step Multiple Linear Regression with OLS]
    D3 --> L3[Final Model Selection Complete]
    G3 --> L3
    H3 --> L3
    J3 --> L3
    K3 --> L3
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A4[Observed Data Point Yi Xi] --> B4[Vertical Projection onto Fitted Line]
    B4 --> C4[Predicted Value Yi_hat]
    A4 --> D4[Residual ei equals Yi minus Yi_hat]
    C4 --> E4[Centroid X_bar Y_bar]
    E4 --> F4[Slope Beta_1 = Rise per Run]
    F4 --> G4[Intercept Beta_0 = Y when X equals 0]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**"Differentiate between Correlation and Regression in the context of Business Analytics."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Correlation ($r$) | Regression ($Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Purpose** | Measures *strength* and *direction* of association | Models the *functional form* of the relationship |
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric: $r_{XY} = r_{YX}$ | Asymmetric: $Y$ on $X$ differs from $X$ on $Y$ |
| **Causation** | Does **not** imply causation | Can be used to test causal hypotheses (with assumptions) |
| **Output Range** | $-1 \leq r \leq 1$ (dimensionless) | $\hat{Y}$ in the units of the response variable |
| **Business Use** | Quick screening of variable pairs | Forecasting and scenario planning |

> **Awarding Marks:** Definition (1M), Tabular comparison (1M), One business example (1M).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**"Explain the four components of a classical time series decomposition model with one business example each."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The classical decomposition model expresses an observed series as:

$$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t \quad \text{(Additive)}$$

1. **Trend ($T_t$)** — The long-term directional movement. *Example:* 10-year growth of e-commerce penetration in India.
2. **Seasonality ($S_t$)** — Regular, calendar-driven patterns. *Example:* Ice-cream sales peaking every summer.
3. **Cyclical ($C_t$)** — Long, irregular oscillations tied to business cycles. *Example:* Recession-driven dips in luxury car sales.
4. **Irregular ($I_t$)** — Pure random noise. *Example:* A one-day flash sale causing a sales spike.

> **Awarding Marks:** Each component explained with example ≈ 0.75M × 4.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]

**A. (a)** Define the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method. Derive the expressions for the regression coefficients $\hat{\beta_0}$ and $\hat{\beta_1}$ using the calculus of minimization of the Residual Sum of Squares. **(7 Marks)**

**A. (b)** For the following data on advertising expenditure ($X$, in ₹ thousands) and sales ($Y$, in ₹ thousands), compute the regression equation of $Y$ on $X$. Also, find the coefficient of determination $R^2$ and predict sales when $X = 45$. **(7 Marks)**

| $X$ | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| $Y$ | 25 | 35 | 40 | 50 | 55 |

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**Definition (2 Marks):** The OLS method is a parameter estimation technique that finds the values of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ which minimize the sum of squared residuals, $RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2$. It is the most widely used estimation method in linear regression under the Gauss-Markov assumptions.

**Derivation (5 Marks):** Follow the exhaustive 12-step derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key checkpoints:
1. Write RSS as a function of $\beta_0, \beta_1$. (1M)
2. Partial derivative w.r.t. $\beta_0 = 0$ gives $\beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$. (1.5M)
3. Partial derivative w.r.t. $\beta_1 = 0$ and substitution yields the slope formula. (2M)
4. State the final closed-form. (0.5M)

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Pre-computation table:**

| $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $X_i^2$ | $Y_i^2$ | $X_i Y_i$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 10 | 25 | 100 | 625 | 250 |
| 15 | 35 | 225 | 1225 | 525 |
| 20 | 40 | 400 | 1600 | 800 |
| 25 | 50 | 625 | 2500 | 1250 |
| 30 | 55 | 900 | 3025 | 1650 |
| $\sum X = 100$ | $\sum Y = 205$ | $\sum X^2 = 2250$ | $\sum Y^2 = 8975$ | $\sum XY = 4475$ |

**Slope calculation:**

$$\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{5 \times 4475 - 100 \times 205}{5 \times 2250 - (100)^2} = \frac{22375 - 20500}{11250 - 10000} = \frac{1875}{1250} = 1.5$$

**Intercept calculation:**

$$\bar{X} = 20, \quad \bar{Y} = 41 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\beta_0} = 41 - 1.5 \times 20 = 41 - 30 = 11$$

**Regression Equation:**

$$\hat{Y} = 11 + 1.5 X$$

**Prediction at $X = 45$:**

$$\hat{Y} = 11 + 1.5 \times 45 = 11 + 67.5 = 78.5$$

**$R^2$ Calculation:**

| $X_i$ | $Y_i$ | $\hat{Y_i}$ | $(Y_i - \hat{Y_i})^2$ | $(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 10 | 25 | 26 | 1 | 256 |
| 15 | 35 | 33.5 | 2.25 | 36 |
| 20 | 40 | 41 | 1 | 1 |
| 25 | 50 | 48.5 | 2.25 | 81 |
| 30 | 55 | 56 | 1 | 196 |
| | | | **SSE = 7.5** | **SST = 570** |

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{7.5}{570} = 1 - 0.0132 = 0.9868 \approx 0.987$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **98.7%** of sales variation is explained by ad spend — an excellent model. Predicted sales at $X = 45$ is **₹78,500**.

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]

**B. (a)** Discuss the **classical multiplicative time series decomposition model**. Explain how the trend is extracted using a centered 4-quarter moving average. **(7 Marks)**

**B. (b)** Given the following quarterly sales data (₹ Lakhs), apply the multiplicative decomposition model and compute the seasonal indices for each quarter. **(7 Marks)**

| Quarter | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Q1 | 100 | 120 | 140 |
| Q2 | 150 | 170 | 195 |
| Q3 | 175 | 200 | 225 |
| Q4 | 125 | 145 | 170 |

#### Model Solution for B(a):

**Definition (2 Marks):** The multiplicative decomposition model expresses a time series as a product of four components:

$$Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$$

Unlike the additive model, it is used when the **amplitude of seasonal variation grows with the level of the trend** — common in business sales data.

**Trend Extraction using 4-Quarter Centered Moving Average (5 Marks):**

For quarterly data, the period is $m = 4$. A simple 4-quarter MA is not centered (it falls between Q2 and Q3). To center it, we average two consecutive 4-quarter MAs.

**Step 1:** Compute 4-quarter moving averages:
$$MA_1 = \frac{Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3 + Y_4}{4}, \quad MA_2 = \frac{Y_2 + Y_3 + Y_4 + Y_5}{4}, \dots$$

**Step 2:** Center the MAs by averaging $MA_t$ and $MA_{t+1}$:
$$T_t = \frac{MA_t + MA_{t+1}}{2} = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-2} + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1} + 0.5 Y_{t+2}}{4}$$

This works because placing weights 0.5, 1, 1, 1, 1, 0.5 over 6 consecutive points and dividing by 4 places equal effective weight on each of the 4 quarters, centering the average exactly on a quarter.

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Step 1: Compute the 4-quarter centered moving average (Trend $T_t$):**

$$T_3 = \frac{0.5(100) + 150 + 175 + 125 + 0.5(120)}{4} = \frac{50 + 150 + 175 + 125 + 60}{4} = \frac{560}{4} = 140$$

$$T_4 = \frac{0.5(150) + 175 + 125 + 120 + 0.5(170)}{4} = \frac{75 + 175 + 125 + 120 + 85}{4} = \frac{580}{4} = 145$$

$$T_5 = \frac{0.5(175) + 125 + 120 + 170 + 0.5(140)}{4} = \frac{87.5 + 125 + 120 + 170 + 70}{4} = \frac{572.5}{4} = 143.125$$

$$T_6 = \frac{0.5(125) + 120 + 170 + 145 + 0.5(195)}{4} = \frac{62.5 + 120 + 170 + 145 + 97.5}{4} = \frac{595}{4} = 148.75$$

$$T_7 = \frac{0.5(120) + 170 + 145 + 195 + 0.5(200)}{4} = \frac{60 + 170 + 145 + 195 + 100}{4} = \frac{670}{4} = 167.5$$

$$T_8 = \frac{0.5(170) + 145 + 195 + 200 + 0.5(225)}{4} = \frac{85 + 145 + 195 + 200 + 112.5}{4} = \frac{737.5}{4} = 184.375$$

$$T_9 = \frac{0.5(145) + 195 + 200 + 225 + 0.5(140)}{4} = \frac{72.5 + 195 + 200 + 225 + 70}{4} = \frac{762.5}{4} = 190.625$$

**Step 2: Compute Detrended Ratio $Y_t / T_t$ (multiplicative model):**

| Quarter | $Y_t$ | $T_t$ | $Y_t / T_t$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Q1-Y1 | 100 | — | — |
| Q2-Y1 | 150 | — | — |
| Q3-Y1 | 175 | 140.000 | 1.2500 |
| Q4-Y1 | 125 | 145.000 | 0.8621 |
| Q1-Y2 | 120 | 143.125 | 0.8384 |
| Q2-Y2 | 170 | 148.750 | 1.1429 |
| Q3-Y2 | 200 | 167.500 | 1.1940 |
| Q4-Y2 | 145 | 184.375 | 0.7864 |
| Q1-Y3 | 140 | 190.625 | 0.7344 |
| Q2-Y3 | 195 | — | — |
| Q3-Y3 | 225 | — | — |

**Step 3: Average the ratios by quarter (Seasonal Indices):**

$$SI_{Q1} = \frac{0.8384 + 0.7344}{2} = \frac{1.5728}{2} = 0.7864$$

$$SI_{Q2} = \frac{1.1429}{1} = 1.1429 \quad \text{(only one value available)}$$

$$SI_{Q3} = \frac{1.2500 + 1.1940}{2} = \frac{2.4440}{2} = 1.2220$$

$$SI_{Q4} = \frac{0.8621 + 0.7864}{2} = \frac{1.6485}{2} = 0.8243$$

**Step 4: Normalize to sum = 4 (since $m = 4$):**

Current sum = $0.7864 + 1.1429 + 1.2220 + 0.8243 = 3.9756$
Adjustment factor = $4 / 3.9756 = 1.00614$

$$SI_{Q1} = 0.7864 \times 1.00614 = 0.7912$$

$$SI_{Q2} = 1.1429 \times 1.00614 = 1.1499$$

$$SI_{Q3} = 1.2220 \times 1.00614 = 1.2295$$

$$SI_{Q4} = 0.8243 \times 1.00614 = 0.8294$$

**Final Seasonal Indices:**

| Quarter | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Seasonal Index | **0.7912** | **1.1499** | **1.2295** | **0.8294** |

**Interpretation:** Q3 is the **strongest** sales quarter (index 1.23 → 23% above average), and Q1 is the **weakest** (index 0.79 → 21% below average). A Kerala-based business should plan inventory and staffing around this pattern.

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers)**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** seasonal indices so they sum to $m$. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing additive vs. multiplicative** — the choice depends on whether seasonal amplitude grows with trend. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Skipping the centering** in 4-quarter MA — placing the average at the wrong time index. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not interpreting** the final $R^2$ or seasonal index in business terms. (–1 mark)
> 5. **In regression**, forgetting the centroid property check: $\bar{X}$ and $\bar{Y}$ should lie on the regression line. (–1 mark)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [ ] **Correlation** is *symmetric* and bounded; **regression** is *asymmetric* and unbounded.
- [ ] Always check **Correlation before Regression** — never regress uncorrelated variables.
- [ ] The OLS slope formula in raw-sum form is: $\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{n \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$.
- [ ] The OLS slope formula in centered form is: $\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{\sum (X - \bar{X})(Y - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X - \bar{X})^2}$.
- [ ] The intercept formula is always: $\hat{\beta_0} = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta_1} \bar{X}$.
- [ ] The regression line **always passes through the centroid** $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$.
- [ ] $R^2$ measures the **proportion of variance explained** — closer to 1 is better.
- [ ] For business models, an $R^2 \geq 0.7$ is generally considered a good fit.
- [ ] **Additive decomposition** is used when seasonal amplitude is **constant**; **Multiplicative** when seasonal amplitude **grows** with the trend.
- [ ] The 4-quarter centered MA uses weights $0.5, 1, 1, 1, 0.5$ to center the trend on a quarter.
- [ ] **Seasonal indices** in a multiplicative model should sum to $m$ (the number of seasons).
- [ ] The four components of a time series are: **T**rend, **S**easonality, **C**yclical, **I**rregular.
- [ ] **Pearson correlation** assumes linearity, normality, and homoscedasticity.
- [ ] **Forecasting accuracy metrics**: lower MAE and RMSE indicate better forecasts.
- [ ] **GAUSS-MARKOV theorem**: OLS is BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) under standard assumptions.
- [ ] Always validate a model on a **holdout/test set** — never evaluate on training data alone.
- [ ] **Coefficient of Correlation $r$** and **Coefficient of Determination $R^2$** are related by $R^2 = r^2$ in simple linear regression.
- [ ] In KTU exams, **show every formula** before substitution — partial credit depends on it.
- [ ] The **t-statistic** for the slope: $t = \frac{\hat{\beta_1}}{SE(\hat{\beta_1})}$. At $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value is $\pm 1.96$ for large samples.
- [ ] **Multiple regression** extends the simple model to $k$ predictors: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_k X_k + \varepsilon$.
- [ ] **Multicollinearity** (high correlation between predictors) is a key pitfall in multiple regression — check using VIF.
- [ ] The **adjusted $R^2$** penalizes adding irrelevant predictors: $\bar{R^2} = 1 - (1 - R^2) \frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models Involving Uncertainty - What-If Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Models Involving Uncertainty - What-If Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**What-If Analysis** is a systematic, data-driven modeling technique used in Business Analytics to evaluate the potential outcomes of a decision, model, or system by systematically changing one or more input variables (assumptions) and observing the corresponding effect on the output variable(s). It is the foundational pillar of **predictive modeling under uncertainty**, enabling decision-makers to comprehend the sensitivity, robustness, and risk profile of their analytical models before committing to a course of action.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECBT755 – Business Analytics, Module 4)**, What-If Analysis is defined as:

> *"A class of computational procedures wherein the analyst perturbs the controllable and uncontrollable inputs of a deterministic or stochastic business model, observes the propagation of these changes through the model logic, and quantifies the variance in the resulting Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to support risk-adjusted decision-making."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The official KTU PECBT755 Module 4 mandates coverage of **(a) What-If Analysis, (b) Types (Scenario Analysis, Sensitivity Analysis, Data Tables), (c) Goal Seek, (d) Monte Carlo Simulation, and (e) Decision Trees under uncertainty.** All five components are examinable as **14-mark Part B questions**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecast" Mental Model

Imagine you are planning an **outdoor wedding** in Kochi for December 15, 2026. You cannot control the weather, but you *can* model your decisions around it.

- **Fixed inputs (Assumptions):** Venue cost (₹2,00,000), Guest count (300), Catering cost/head (₹800).
- **Uncertain inputs (Variables):** Rain probability, Number of guests who will actually attend, Catering inflation.
- **The Question (What-If):** *What if it rains on the wedding day? What if only 250 guests attend? What if catering costs rise by 15%?*

A **What-If Analysis** would create a "financial safety net" by:
1. Building a model once.
2. Changing one input at a time (or several) to see what happens.
3. Discovering which assumption is the **tornado** (the lever that moves the total cost the most).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners love linking the *technical mechanism* to a *business scenario*. If you can explain that "Sensitivity Analysis finds the biggest lever" and "Scenario Analysis tells a story of best/expected/worst outcomes," you score full marks on definition questions.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (Board-Exam Ready)

| Term | Standard Definition (KTU-approved) |
|---|---|
| **Input Cell** | The spreadsheet/model cell whose value is the *driver* of uncertainty. |
| **Output Cell** | The dependent cell (KPI like NPV, Profit, Cost) whose value is observed. |
| **Decision Variable** | A controllable input (e.g., price, quantity). |
| **Uncertainty Variable** | A stochastic input described by a probability distribution (e.g., demand). |
| **Scenario** | A coherent, named set of input values (e.g., "Recession Scenario," "Boom Scenario"). |
| **Tornado Chart** | A bar chart ranking input variables by the magnitude of their effect on an output. |
| **Iteration** | One complete pass of the model computation for a given set of inputs. |

> [!TIP]
> **Constant to remember:** The number of scenarios in a full-factorial What-If Analysis with $k$ variables and $n$ levels per variable is $n^k$. For a Monte Carlo simulation with $N$ trials, the Law of Large Numbers guarantees convergence as $N \to \infty$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of What-If Analysis (KTU Module 4 Core)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following components. Each is a *different lens* on the same underlying question: *"What might happen?"*

### Pillar 1 — Manual What-If Analysis
- **Mechanism:** The analyst manually types different values into input cells and records the output.
- **Use Case:** Quick "back-of-envelope" testing with 1–2 variables.
- **Limitation:** Non-scalable; cannot handle >3 variables.

### Pillar 2 — Data Tables
- **Mechanism:** A built-in spreadsheet tool that runs the model once per row (1-input table) or column (2-input table) automatically.
- **One-Input Data Table:** Tests how one input affects one output across many values.
- **Two-Input Data Table:** Tests how two inputs simultaneously affect one output.
- **Limit:** Maximum of 2 variable inputs.

### Pillar 3 — Scenario Manager
- **Mechanism:** Stores and names multiple discrete sets of inputs ("Best," "Expected," "Worst").
- **Tool:** Excel *Data → What-If Analysis → Scenario Manager*.
- **Output:** A **Scenario Summary Report** (Pivot-style) comparing KPIs across scenarios.

### Pillar 4 — Goal Seek
- **Mechanism:** *Inverts* the model. Instead of "Given inputs → find output," it solves "**Given a desired output → find the required input**."
- **Algorithm:** Iterative numerical root-finding (essentially the **Secant Method** or **Newton-Raphson**).
- **Limit:** Single input, single output.

### Pillar 5 — Sensitivity Analysis
- **Mechanism:** Quantifies *how much* the output changes for a *unit change* in each input.
- **Types:**
  - *Local Sensitivity:* Partial derivative at a point: $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$.
  - *Global Sensitivity:* Sobol indices, variance-based; considers the full input range.
- **Visualization:** **Tornado Chart**, **Spider Chart**.

### Pillar 6 — Monte Carlo Simulation
- **Mechanism:** Replaces fixed inputs with **probability distributions**, then samples them $N$ times to build a *distribution of outcomes*.
- **Key Idea:** Uses **pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs)** to draw from input distributions.
- **Output:** Histogram, Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF), Confidence Intervals (CI), Value at Risk (VaR).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Linear What-If Model | $Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i X_i + \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$ = error term |
| Local Sensitivity Index | $S_i = \frac{\partial Y}{\partial X_i} \cdot \frac{X_i}{Y}$ | Elasticity (dimensionless) |
| Global Variance Sensitivity (Sobol 1st-order) | $S_i = \frac{V(E(Y \mid X_i))}{V(Y)}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| Total Sobol Index (including interactions) | $S_{T_i} = \frac{E(V(Y \mid X_{\sim i}))}{V(Y)}$ | $S_{T_i} \geq S_i$ |
| Mean of Monte Carlo Output | $\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} Y_j$ | $N$ = no. of trials |
| Standard Error of Mean | $SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Decreases as $\sqrt{N}$ |
| 95% Confidence Interval | $CI_{95\%} = \bar{Y} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE$ | For large $N$ |
| Value at Risk (VaR) | $VaR_\alpha = \inf\{y : P(Y \leq y) \geq 1-\alpha\}$ | Threshold at confidence $\alpha$ |
| Goal Seek Iteration | $X_{n+1} = X_n - \frac{f(X_n) - f^*}{f'(X_n)}$ | Newton-Raphson form |
| Full-Factorial Scenario Count | $N_{scen} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ | $n_i$ = levels for var $i$ |
| Expected Monetary Value (EMV) | $EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot V_i$ | $p_i$ = prob, $V_i$ = payoff |
| Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI) | $EVPI = EMV_{w/info} - EMV_{w/o\,info}$ | Maximum amount to pay for info |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX SAFEGUARD:** All absolute value / norm expressions inside the table above use the symbol $\vert$ deliberately to avoid breaking markdown table pipes. In your written exam, write $\lvert x \rvert$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

| Industry | What-If Application | Specific KPI Studied |
|---|---|---|
| **Finance / Banking** | Portfolio risk modelling | VaR, CVaR, Sharpe Ratio |
| **Supply Chain / Operations** | Demand forecasting under disruption | Safety Stock, Reorder Point |
| **Manufacturing** | Process capability under tolerance variation | Cpk, Defect Rate |
| **Marketing** | Campaign ROI under different spend levels | Conversion Rate, CAC |
| **Healthcare** | Drug efficacy under patient variability | QALY, ICER |
| **Energy Sector** | Wind farm output under varying wind speeds | LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) |
| **Civil Engineering** | Structural load under seismic uncertainty | Probability of Failure ($P_f$) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering connection:** For a KTU Civil/Mech student, "What-If" is the bridge between *deterministic design* (single safety factor) and *reliability-based design* (target $P_f \leq 10^{-6}$). Monte Carlo Simulation is the gold standard for solving the multidimensional integral of failure probability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Monte Carlo Convergence Theorem

Monte Carlo Simulation estimates a deterministic quantity (e.g., $\mu = E[Y]$) via sampling. The justification is the **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)**.

### Step 1 — Set up the integral representation
The expected value of any output $Y$ is, by definition of expectation:

$$E[Y] = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} y \cdot f_Y(y) \, dy$$

where $f_Y(y)$ is the PDF of the output. If $Y = g(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k)$ where $X_i$ are random inputs, then $f_Y$ is unknown analytically.

### Step 2 — Replace the integral with a sample mean
Draw $N$ independent samples $\{(X_1^{(j)}, X_2^{(j)}, \ldots, X_k^{(j)})\}_{j=1}^{N}$, compute $Y^{(j)} = g(X_1^{(j)}, \ldots, X_k^{(j)})$, and form:

$$\bar{Y}_N = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} Y^{(j)} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} g\left(X_1^{(j)}, \ldots, X_k^{(j)}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Strong Law of Large Numbers
For i.i.d. samples with finite variance $\sigma^2 < \infty$:

$$\bar{Y}_N \xrightarrow{\text{a.s.}} E[Y] \quad \text{as } N \to \infty$$

**Logic:** Each $Y^{(j)}$ is an unbiased estimator of $E[Y]$ (since $E[Y^{(j)}] = E[g(X)] = E[Y]$). The sample mean of i.i.d. unbiased estimators is itself unbiased and converges almost surely to the true mean.

### Step 4 — Quantify the error via the Central Limit Theorem
The CLT tells us that for finite $N$:

$$\bar{Y}_N \sim \mathcal{N}\left(E[Y],\ \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\right)$$

Hence, the **95% Confidence Interval** for the true mean is:

$$CI_{95\%} = \bar{Y}_N \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{\hat{\sigma}}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Engineering insight:** To halve the CI width, you need **4×** the simulations. This is the *quadratic* cost of Monte Carlo precision.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Goal Seek as Newton-Raphson Root Finding

Suppose you have a model $Y = f(X)$ and you want to find the input $X^*$ such that $Y = Y_{\text{target}}$. This is the root of:

$$h(X) = f(X) - Y_{\text{target}} = 0$$

### Step 1 — Initialize
Pick an initial guess $X_0$.

### Step 2 — Iterate
Newton's method updates:

$$X_{n+1} = X_n - \frac{h(X_n)}{h'(X_n)} = X_n - \frac{f(X_n) - Y_{\text{target}}}{f'(X_n)}$$

### Step 3 — Convergence
For a well-behaved (Lipschitz-continuous) $f$ and a good initial guess:

$$\lvert X_{n+1} - X^* \rvert \leq C \cdot \lvert X_n - X^* \rvert^2$$

**Quadratic convergence:** the number of correct digits doubles each iteration. Excel's *Goal Seek* typically converges in 5–15 iterations.

### Worked Numerical Example
Suppose $Y = 500 \cdot X^2 - 1000 \cdot X + 600$ and you want $Y = 400$.

- Step 1: Try $X_0 = 1$. Then $Y_0 = 500 - 1000 + 600 = 100$. Need to *increase* $X$.
- Step 2: Try $X_1 = 1.5$. Then $Y_1 = 1125 - 1500 + 600 = 225$. Still low.
- Step 3: Try $X_2 = 1.8$. Then $Y_2 = 1620 - 1800 + 600 = 420$. Close!
- Step 4: Try $X_3 = 1.78$. Then $Y_3 = 1584.2 - 1780 + 600 = 404.2$. Excellent.
- Converged: $X^* \approx 1.779$. Goal Seek would output this in milliseconds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sobol Sensitivity Index from First Principles

For a model $Y = g(X_1, \ldots, X_k)$ with independent inputs, decompose the total variance $V(Y)$ using the **Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)** representation:

$$V(Y) = \sum_{i} V_i + \sum_{i<j} V_{ij} + \sum_{i<j<l} V_{ijl} + \cdots + V_{12\ldots k}$$

where $V_i = V(E[Y \mid X_i])$ and $V_{ij} = V(E[Y \mid X_i, X_j]) - V_i - V_j$.

### First-Order Sobol Index
The fraction of output variance caused by $X_i$ alone:

$$S_i = \frac{V_i}{V(Y)} = \frac{V(E[Y \mid X_i])}{V(Y)}$$

### Total Sobol Index
Includes all interaction terms involving $X_i$:

$$S_{T_i} = \frac{E(V(Y \mid X_{\sim i}))}{V(Y)} = 1 - \frac{V(E[Y \mid X_{\sim i}])}{V(Y)}$$

### Properties (exam-favorite)
- $\sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i \leq 1$ (equality holds only if there are no interactions).
- $S_{T_i} \geq S_i$ always.
- $S_{T_i} - S_i$ measures the *interaction effect* of $X_i$ with other variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU loves this derivation:** It directly links What-If Analysis to variance-based attribution, which is the mathematical core of modern risk analytics (used in IPCC climate models, pharma trials, and credit scoring).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right What-If Tool

| Criterion | Manual / Data Table | Scenario Manager | Goal Seek | Sensitivity | Monte Carlo |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **# of variable inputs** | 1–2 | Up to 32 | 1 | 1 (local) / many (global) | Unlimited |
| **Probability distributions** | No | No | No | Optional | Yes (core) |
| **Output type** | Point estimates | Side-by-side KPIs | Inverse single value | Slopes/elasticities | Full distribution |
| **Computational cost** | Very low | Low | Low | Low–medium | High ($N$ trials) |
| **Captures interactions** | Only 2-input table | No | No | Global version: yes | Yes |
| **Best use** | Teaching, quick test | Boardroom "what-if" | Break-even / target | Variable ranking | Risk modelling |
| **Excel tool** | `Data Table` | `Scenario Manager` | `Goal Seek` | Manual or `Data → Solver` | `@RISK` add-in or VBA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The What-If Analysis Master Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Business Problem]) --> nodeB[Identify KPIs Output Cells]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Input Variables]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Classify Inputs}
    nodeD -->|Controllable| nodeE[Decision Variables]
    nodeD -->|Uncontrollable| nodeF[Uncertainty Variables]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Build the Base Model]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Choose What-If Tool]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Manual Data Table]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Scenario Manager]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Goal Seek]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Sensitivity Analysis]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Monte Carlo Simulation]
    nodeI --> nodeN[Generate Output Distribution]
    nodeJ --> nodeN
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Interpret Results]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Build Tornado Chart]
    nodeO --> nodeQ[Compute VaR and CI]
    nodeO --> nodeR[Make Risk Adjusted Decision]
    nodeP --> nodeS([End: Informed Decision])
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeR --> nodeS
```

## 4.2 Monte Carlo Simulation Internal Loop (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input Distributions] --> nodeB[Generate Random Numbers]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sample X1 X2 ... Xk]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Evaluate Model Y equals g of X]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Store Y in Results Array]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Trials less than N}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeB
    nodeF -->|No| nodeG[Compute Mean Variance Percentiles]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Build Histogram and CDF]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Report VaR and CI]
```

## 4.3 The "Tool Selection" Decision Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start Selection Logic] --> nodeB{How many uncertain inputs}
    nodeB -->|1| nodeC{What is the question}
    nodeC -->|Find required input for target output| nodeD[Use Goal Seek]
    nodeC -->|See how output changes with input| nodeE[Use Data Table 1 Input]
    nodeB -->|2 to 5| nodeF{Need to compare discrete stories}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Use Scenario Manager]
    nodeF -->|No need to rank variable influence| nodeH[Use Sensitivity Analysis]
    nodeB -->|More than 5 or probabilistic| nodeI[Use Monte Carlo Simulation]
    nodeD --> nodeJ([End])
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

## 4.4 Risk Communication Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Simulation Output] --> nodeB[Compute Statistics]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Mean and Median]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Standard Deviation]
    nodeB --> nodeE[5th and 95th Percentiles]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Value at Risk VaR]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Tornado Chart Variable Ranking]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH[Risk Profile Report]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI([Boardroom Decision])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define What-If Analysis. List its major types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** What-If Analysis is a systematic technique of changing one or more input variables in a business model to observe the effect on output variables, in order to understand model behavior and risk.
- **Types (1 mark):** Manual What-If, Data Tables, Scenario Analysis, Goal Seek, Sensitivity Analysis, and Monte Carlo Simulation.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between Sensitivity Analysis and Scenario Analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Sensitivity Analysis** studies the *magnitude* of change in output for a unit change in one input, *keeping others constant*. It identifies the most influential variable. **Output:** Slopes, elasticities, tornado chart.
- **Scenario Analysis** studies a *discrete, coherent set* of input values representing a "story" (e.g., recession, boom). **Output:** Side-by-side comparison of KPIs across named scenarios.
- **Key difference:** Sensitivity is *granular* (one input at a time), Scenario is *holistic* (many inputs together).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Choose A or B)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Modified]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the methodology of **Monte Carlo Simulation** in business analytics. How does it differ from deterministic What-If Analysis?

> **(b) [7 Marks)** A retail company is evaluating a new product launch. The **selling price** is uncertain, uniformly distributed between ₹400 and ₹600. The **monthly demand** is normally distributed with mean 1500 units and standard deviation 200 units. The **unit cost** is fixed at ₹300. Using **10,000 Monte Carlo trials**, compute the expected monthly profit and the Value at Risk at the 5% level.

#### Model Solution to A(a)

Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) is a stochastic modelling technique that propagates probability distributions of inputs through a mathematical model to obtain a probability distribution of the output.

**Methodology — 5 Steps:**

1. **Define the model** in mathematical form. For profit: $P = (Price - Cost) \times Demand$.
2. **Assign probability distributions** to each uncertain input. Price $\sim U(400, 600)$; Demand $\sim N(1500, 200)$.
3. **Generate random samples** from each input distribution using a Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG).
4. **Run the model** once per sample to produce one output value. Repeat $N$ times.
5. **Aggregate the outputs** statistically — mean, variance, percentiles, histogram.

**Difference from Deterministic What-If:**

| Aspect | Deterministic What-If | Monte Carlo |
|---|---|---|
| Input representation | Point estimates | Probability distributions |
| Number of runs | As many as scenarios (often 3–5) | 1,000 – 1,00,000+ |
| Output | Discrete KPI values | Full probability distribution |
| Captures uncertainty | No | Yes (explicitly) |
| Tool complexity | Low | High |

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Define the profit model:**

$$P = (Price - Cost) \times Demand = (Price - 300) \times Demand$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical expectations (using random variable theory):**

For **Price** $\sim U(400, 600)$:
$$E[Price] = \frac{400 + 600}{2} = 500, \quad Var[Price] = \frac{(600-400)^2}{12} = 3333.33$$

For **Demand** $\sim N(1500, 200^2)$:
$$E[Demand] = 1500, \quad Var[Demand] = 40000$$

**Step 3 — Expected profit (assuming independence):**

$$E[P] = E[Price - 300] \times E[Demand] = (500 - 300) \times 1500 = 200 \times 1500 = \text{₹ } 3,00,000$$

**Step 4 — Variance of profit (independent inputs, using second-moment formula):**

$$Var[P] = E[Demand^2] \cdot E[(Price - 300)^2] - (E[P])^2$$

$E[Demand^2] = Var[Demand] + (E[Demand])^2 = 40000 + 1500^2 = 22,90,000$

$E[(Price-300)^2] = Var[Price] + (E[Price]-300)^2 = 3333.33 + 200^2 = 43,333.33$

$$Var[P] = 2290000 \times 43333.33 - (300000)^2 = 99,23,33,33,000 - 90,00,00,00,000 = 9,23,33,33,000$$

$$\sigma_P = \sqrt{9,23,33,33,000} \approx \text{₹ } 3,03,873$$

**Step 5 — Value at Risk at 5% level (worst 5% of outcomes):**

$$VaR_{5\%} = \mu_P - 1.645 \cdot \sigma_P = 3,00,000 - 1.645 \times 3,03,873 \approx 3,00,000 - 4,99,871 \approx \text{₹ } -1,99,871$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** The 5% VaR is approximately **−₹1,99,871**, meaning that in 5% of simulated months, the company will **lose at least ₹2 lakhs**. This is a critical risk signal — the company should keep at least this much as a contingency reserve per month.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Modified]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Goal Seek** and **Scenario Manager** in spreadsheet-based What-If Analysis. Illustrate with a business example.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A company must choose between two projects. Project A yields ₹50 lakh with probability 0.7 and ₹20 lakh with probability 0.3. Project B yields ₹60 lakh with probability 0.5, ₹40 lakh with probability 0.3, and ₹10 lakh with probability 0.2. Compute the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** of each, the **standard deviation of payoffs**, and recommend using the **coefficient of variation (CV)**.

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Goal Seek:**
- **Definition:** A forward-inverse numerical tool that finds the input value required to make a formula output equal to a specified target.
- **Algorithm:** Iterative Newton-Raphson root-finding.
- **Example:** A firm wants a profit of ₹10 lakh. Current model: $P = 500Q - 200000$ where $Q$ is quantity. *Goal Seek* solves $500Q - 200000 = 1000000 \Rightarrow Q^* = 2400$ units.

**Scenario Manager:**
- **Definition:** A tool that stores and names multiple discrete sets of input values (scenarios) and produces a side-by-side summary report.
- **Example:** A FMCG firm defines three demand scenarios:
  - *Optimistic:* Price ₹50, Demand 10,000 units, Cost ₹30.
  - *Realistic:* Price ₹45, Demand 7,500 units, Cost ₹30.
  - *Pessimistic:* Price ₹40, Demand 5,000 units, Cost ₹32.
- The Scenario Summary Report shows Profit, ROI, and Cash Flow for each named scenario in a single table.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Goal Seek | Scenario Manager |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Inverse (output → input) | Forward (inputs → output) |
| # of inputs | 1 | Up to 32 |
| # of outputs | 1 | All in the model |
| Use | Break-even / target | Strategic "story-telling" |

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV):**

For **Project A**:
$$EMV_A = (0.7 \times 50) + (0.3 \times 20) = 35 + 6 = \text{₹ 41 lakh}$$

For **Project B**:
$$EMV_B = (0.5 \times 60) + (0.3 \times 40) + (0.2 \times 10) = 30 + 12 + 2 = \text{₹ 44 lakh}$$

**Step 2 — Variance and Standard Deviation:**

For **Project A**:
$$E[V_A^2] = (0.7 \times 50^2) + (0.3 \times 20^2) = 1750 + 120 = 1870$$
$$Var_A = 1870 - 41^2 = 1870 - 1681 = 189$$
$$\sigma_A = \sqrt{189} \approx 13.75 \text{ lakh}$$

For **Project B**:
$$E[V_B^2] = (0.5 \times 3600) + (0.3 \times 1600) + (0.2 \times 100) = 1800 + 480 + 20 = 2300$$
$$Var_B = 2300 - 44^2 = 2300 - 1936 = 364$$
$$\sigma_B = \sqrt{364} \approx 19.08 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Variation (CV = σ/μ):**

$$CV_A = \frac{13.75}{41} \approx 0.335$$

$$CV_B = \frac{19.08}{44} \approx 0.434$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

| Metric | Project A | Project B | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| EMV | ₹41 lakh | ₹44 lakh | **B** (higher expected return) |
| Std Dev (Risk) | ₹13.75 lakh | ₹19.08 lakh | **A** (lower risk) |
| CV (Risk/Mu) | 0.335 | 0.434 | **A** (better risk-adjusted) |

**Recommendation (1 mark):** If the company is **risk-averse**, choose **Project A** (lower CV means better return per unit of risk). If the company is **risk-neutral** and chasing the highest expected payoff, choose **Project B**. A typical KTU answer would say: *"Project A is preferred on a risk-adjusted basis (lower CV), even though Project B has a higher expected value."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Never confuse EMV with "most likely" outcome.** EMV is a probability-weighted average, not the mode.
> 2. **Always compute CV when comparing risk across projects of different scales.** Comparing σ alone is misleading (a ₹1000 crore project will always have higher σ than a ₹10 crore project).
> 3. **In Monte Carlo questions, do NOT forget to mention the Central Limit Theorem and the standard error formula** $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this.
> 4. **For Goal Seek, naming the algorithm (Newton-Raphson) earns a bonus mark.** Most students just write "it iterates" — that is incomplete.
> 5. **For Scenario Manager, always distinguish "scenarios" from "sensitivity runs."** Scenarios are *named and coherent*; sensitivity sweeps are *mechanical*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **What-If Analysis = changing inputs → observing outputs** to study model behavior under uncertainty.
- [ ] **Six pillars (in syllabus order):** Manual, Data Tables, Scenario Manager, Goal Seek, Sensitivity, Monte Carlo.
- [ ] **Data Table limit:** Maximum **2 variable inputs** (1-input or 2-input table only).
- [ ] **Goal Seek algorithm:** Newton-Raphson root-finding. Single input, single output. Quadratic convergence.
- [ ] **Scenario Manager:** Stores *named, discrete* sets of inputs. Generates a Scenario Summary Report.
- [ ] **Sensitivity Analysis** output is a **Tornado Chart**; variable with the *longest bar* is the most influential.
- [ ] **Local sensitivity** = partial derivative at a point. **Global sensitivity** = Sobol index $S_i \in [0,1]$.
- [ ] **Sobol rule:** $\sum S_i \leq 1$; equality holds only when there are **no interactions** among inputs.
- [ ] **Monte Carlo Simulation:** Replace fixed inputs with **probability distributions**, sample $N$ times, build a distribution of output.
- [ ] **Monte Carlo accuracy** improves as $\sqrt{N}$. To halve CI, need 4× trials.
- [ ] **95% CI for the mean** = $\bar{Y} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{N}$.
- [ ] **Value at Risk (VaR):** The threshold loss that is exceeded only with probability $\alpha$. Computed from the CDF of the simulation output.
- [ ] **EMV** = $\sum p_i V_i$ — used to compare discrete-outcome projects.
- [ ] **CV = σ/μ** — used to compare *risk per unit of expected return* across projects of different scales.
- [ ] **Decision rule:** Lowest CV = best risk-adjusted choice. Highest EMV = best expected-value choice (only for risk-neutral decision-makers).
- [ ] **KTU exam mantra:** "Always link the *tool* to the *business context*." A textbook definition without a business scenario loses 1 mark.
- [ ] **Full-factorial scenarios** for $k$ variables with $n$ levels each = $n^k$ scenarios. Mention this if a question asks about computational complexity.
- [ ] **Pseudo-Random Number Generators (PRNGs)** like the Mersenne Twister are the engine inside every Monte Carlo simulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scenario Manager
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scenario Manager

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario Manager** is a built-in *What-If Analysis* tool in spreadsheet applications (Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets, LibreOffice Calc) that enables analysts to create, save, substitute, and compare multiple sets of input values (called **scenarios**) for the same set of formulas, in order to observe how different business decisions affect model outcomes.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme — PECBT755 Business Analytics**, Scenario Manager falls under *Predictive and Prescriptive Analytics* and is classified as a **deterministic sensitivity-analysis instrument** used for **decision support under multiple plausible futures**.

Mathematically, a scenario is a *vector of input assumptions* applied to a deterministic model $f(\mathbf{x})$ to produce an outcome $y_i$:

$$y_i = f(\mathbf{x}_i) = f(x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}, \dots, x_n^{(i)})$$

where $\mathbf{x}_i$ is the input vector for scenario $i$, and $y_i$ is the corresponding model output.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Profit Planner"

Imagine you are the owner of a small restaurant. You want to know **how your monthly profit changes** if:
- Scenario A: Rent rises by **10%** and footfall drops by **15%**
- Scenario B: Menu prices increase by **5%** and ingredient costs fall by **8%**
- Scenario C: A pandemic shuts dine-in completely

You cannot run three separate spreadsheets — your menu, staff cost, and overhead formulas stay the **same**. What changes are the **input assumptions**. Scenario Manager is exactly this: a *single spreadsheet* holding *multiple parallel sets of inputs*, each tagged with a name, that you can flip between with one click to instantly see how your profit formula reacts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Scenario Manager is explicitly listed under *What-If Analysis Tools* alongside **Goal Seek**, **Data Tables**, and **Solver**. Students must clearly distinguish it from these — Scenario Manager handles **multiple discrete input sets**, Goal Seek handles **one variable to reach one target**, and Data Tables handle **one or two variables in tabular form**.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in Business Analytics

Modern enterprises face **deep uncertainty** — fluctuating raw-material costs, FX rates, demand shifts, regulatory changes. Scenario Manager empowers managers to:

- Stress-test business plans against **best-case, base-case, and worst-case** states
- Quantify **range of outcomes** rather than relying on a single point estimate
- Communicate risks visually using **Scenario Summary Reports**
- Make decisions aligned with **KTU Course Outcome CO4**: *Apply appropriate analytical models and tools for managerial decision-making*

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Industrial Applications:** Budget forecasting in finance, demand-supply planning in supply chains, hiring projections in HR analytics, portfolio risk assessment in banking, capacity planning in manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components of Scenario Manager

Scenario Manager operates on **four architectural pillars**. Mastering these is essential for KTU exam answers.

1. **Changing Cells (Input Variables):**
   The cells whose values change between scenarios.
   - Excel limit: up to **32 changing cells** per scenario
   - Number of scenarios: **unlimited** (constrained only by memory)
   - These cells may be either directly typed values or named ranges

2. **Result Cells (Output Variables):**
   The cells whose behaviour the analyst wishes to observe.
   - Must contain **formulas** that reference the changing cells
   - A scenario may have up to **32 result cells** (or 63 in newer Excel versions)
   - These are NOT specified during scenario creation — they are added later during the **Scenario Summary Report** generation

3. **Scenario Name & Description:**
   - Each scenario must have a **unique name** (max 255 characters)
   - Optional description helps future reviewers understand assumptions

4. **Protection Controls:**
   - **Protect Changes** option prevents users from editing existing scenarios
   - **Hide** option conceals scenarios from other users

### 2.2 Operational Workflow — Six-Stage Process

The Scenario Manager workflow follows a deterministic six-stage pipeline:

| Stage | Action | KTU Exam Tip |
|-------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | Identify the analytical problem and isolate input variables | Always state the **business question** first |
| 2 | Build the deterministic model on the spreadsheet using formulas | Draw the model on paper before opening Excel |
| 3 | Open *Data → What-If Analysis → Scenario Manager* | Mention the menu path explicitly in answers |
| 4 | Click **Add** → name the scenario → specify changing cells → enter values | Each scenario requires unique values for each changing cell |
| 5 | Create all required scenarios (Best / Base / Worst / etc.) | Typically a minimum of **3 scenarios** is expected |
| 6 | Generate a **Scenario Summary Report** for comparison | This is the key deliverable in KTU practical exams |

### 2.3 The Scenario Manager Analytical Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — Memorize the symbols and forms below; they appear routinely in 14-mark derivations.

| Symbol / Term | Mathematical Form | Interpretation | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scenario Output | $y_i = f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ | Result of model $f$ under scenario $i$ | Currency / Units |
| Expected Value (across scenarios) | $E[Y] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot y_i$ | Probability-weighted mean outcome | Same as $Y$ |
| Variance across scenarios | $\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i (y_i - E[Y])^2$ | Dispersion measure of risk | Squared units |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i (y_i - E[Y])^2}$ | Volatility / risk metric | Same as $Y$ |
| Range | $R = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | Worst-to-best spread | Same as $Y$ |
| Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \sigma / E[Y]$ | Risk-per-unit-return (dimensionless) | Ratio |
| Semi-Variance (downside risk) | $\sigma_-^2 = \sum_{i : y_i < E[Y]} p_i (E[Y] - y_i)^2$ | Loss-focused risk measure | Squared units |
| Probability-weighted utility | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot u(y_i)$ | Decision-maker preference aggregation | Utility units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy:** When a question says *"compare scenarios using an analytical metric"*, default to **Expected Value** if probabilities are given, or **Range / Best-Worst Comparison** if not. Always state the assumption about probabilities explicitly.

### 2.4 Classification of Scenarios in Business Analytics

Scenario Manager supports **four canonical scenario classes**, all of which may appear in KTU questions:

- **Optimistic (Best-Case) Scenario:** Favourable shifts in inputs — e.g., demand surge, cost drop
- **Pessimistic (Worst-Case) Scenario:** Adverse shifts — e.g., recession, supply-chain disruption
- **Most-Likely (Base-Case) Scenario:** Best estimate based on historical data and expert judgment
- **Stress / Shock Scenario:** Extreme but plausible conditions used for regulatory stress testing (Basel III, CCAR)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering and Management Utility

| Domain | Application of Scenario Manager |
|---|---|
| **Operations Management** | Production planning under demand uncertainty |
| **Finance** | NPV/IRR analysis under varying discount rates and revenue forecasts |
| **Marketing** | Campaign ROI under different conversion-rate assumptions |
| **Human Resources** | Headcount planning under varying attrition rates |
| **Supply Chain** | Inventory policy under lead-time and demand volatility |
| **Energy Sector** | Power-generation revenue under fuel-price and load scenarios |

> [!TIP]
> A **commonly-asked KTU question**: *"Distinguish between Scenario Manager and Solver."* Answer: *Scenario Manager is descriptive-comparative (compares outcomes of pre-defined input sets), whereas Solver is optimisation-oriented (searches for input values that maximise/minimise an objective subject to constraints).*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — ABC Manufacturing Profit Forecast

ABC Manufacturing produces a single product. The Profit model is:

$$P = (S - V) \cdot Q - F$$

where:
- $P$ = Monthly Profit (₹)
- $S$ = Selling Price per unit (₹)
- $V$ = Variable Cost per unit (₹)
- $Q$ = Quantity sold (units)
- $F$ = Fixed Cost per month (₹)

**Inputs considered for scenario analysis:** $S$, $V$, $Q$, $F$.

#### Step 1 — Define the Three Scenarios

| Scenario | Selling Price $S$ | Variable Cost $V$ | Quantity $Q$ | Fixed Cost $F$ | Business Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Optimistic | ₹120 | ₹40 | 5,000 | ₹200,000 | Festival demand boom |
| Base | ₹100 | ₹50 | 3,500 | ₹250,000 | Normal conditions |
| Pessimistic | ₹85 | ₹65 | 2,000 | ₹300,000 | Recession + inflation |

#### Step 2 — Compute Profit Under Each Scenario (Exhaustive Derivation)

**Scenario 1 — Optimistic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{opt}} &= (S_{\text{opt}} - V_{\text{opt}}) \cdot Q_{\text{opt}} - F_{\text{opt}} \\
&= (120 - 40) \cdot 5000 - 200000 \\
&= 80 \cdot 5000 - 200000 \\
&= 400000 - 200000 \\
&= \mathbf{₹\ 200{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Scenario 2 — Base:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{base}} &= (S_{\text{base}} - V_{\text{base}}) \cdot Q_{\text{base}} - F_{\text{base}} \\
&= (100 - 50) \cdot 3500 - 250000 \\
&= 50 \cdot 3500 - 250000 \\
&= 175000 - 250000 \\
&= \mathbf{-₹\ 75{,}000} \quad (\text{Loss})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Scenario 3 — Pessimistic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{pess}} &= (S_{\text{pess}} - V_{\text{pess}}) \cdot Q_{\text{pess}} - F_{\text{pess}} \\
&= (85 - 65) \cdot 2000 - 300000 \\
&= 20 \cdot 2000 - 300000 \\
&= 40000 - 300000 \\
&= \mathbf{-₹\ 260{,}000} \quad (\text{Heavy Loss})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Analytical Risk Metrics Across Scenarios

**Assumed probabilities:** $p_{\text{opt}} = 0.25$, $p_{\text{base}} = 0.50$, $p_{\text{pess}} = 0.25$.

**Expected Profit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[P] &= p_{\text{opt}} \cdot P_{\text{opt}} + p_{\text{base}} \cdot P_{\text{base}} + p_{\text{pess}} \cdot P_{\text{pess}} \\
&= 0.25 \cdot 200000 + 0.50 \cdot (-75000) + 0.25 \cdot (-260000) \\
&= 50000 - 37500 - 65000 \\
&= \mathbf{-₹\ 52{,}500}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \sum_{i} p_i (P_i - E[P])^2 \\
&= 0.25 \cdot (200000 - (-52500))^2 + 0.50 \cdot (-75000 - (-52500))^2 + 0.25 \cdot (-260000 - (-52500))^2 \\
&= 0.25 \cdot (252500)^2 + 0.50 \cdot (-22500)^2 + 0.25 \cdot (-207500)^2 \\
&= 0.25 \cdot 6.376 \times 10^{10} + 0.50 \cdot 5.0625 \times 10^{8} + 0.25 \cdot 4.306 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 1.594 \times 10^{10} + 2.531 \times 10^{8} + 1.0765 \times 10^{10} \\
&= \mathbf{2.696 \times 10^{10}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{2.696 \times 10^{10}} \approx \mathbf{₹\ 164{,}196}
$$

**Coefficient of Variation:**

$$
CV = \frac{\sigma}{E[P]} \approx \frac{164196}{-52500} \approx -3.13
$$

The negative expected profit combined with massive volatility (CV magnitude > 3) signals **critical business risk** — the model fails under base and pessimistic conditions.

#### Step 4 — Decision Matrix

| Metric | Optimistic | Base | Pessimistic | Aggregate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Profit (₹) | +200,000 | -75,000 | -260,000 | — |
| Probability | 0.25 | 0.50 | 0.25 | 1.00 |
| Probability-Weighted Profit | +50,000 | -37,500 | -65,000 | **-52,500** |

**Managerial Insight:** ABC Manufacturing must either cut fixed costs below ₹200,000, raise selling price above ₹115, or reduce variable cost below ₹45 per unit to make the base-case profitable.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Scenario Manager vs. Other What-If Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison matrix is a high-yield KTU exam topic.** Memorise the distinctions.

| Attribute | Scenario Manager | Goal Seek | Data Table | Solver |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Compare predefined scenarios | Find input to reach target | Tabulate effect of 1 or 2 inputs | Optimise objective with constraints |
| Number of Input Variables | Up to **32** | **1** | **1 or 2** | Up to **200** |
| Number of Output Variables | Up to **32** (report) | **1** | **1 or many** | **1** |
| Direction | Multi-input → multi-output | Reverse-lookup | Sensitivity grid | Constrained optimisation |
| Algorithm Type | Storage-and-substitute | Iterative (Newton-Raphson-like) | Recalculation-grid | Simplex / GRG / Evolutionary |
| Output Format | Summary Report (table) | Single cell value | Grid of values | Solution + Sensitivity Reports |
| Typical Use | Decision comparison | Target setting | Sensitivity visualisation | Resource allocation |

### 3.3 Derivation of the Decision Rule under Probability Assumption

When scenario probabilities are not supplied, the **Maximin / Maximax** decision criteria apply.

**Maximin** (pessimic, risk-averse): choose scenario with **maximum of the minimums**.

$$
\text{Decision} = \arg\max_i \min_j (P_{ij})
$$

**Maximax** (optimistic, risk-seeking): choose scenario with **maximum of the maximums**.

$$
\text{Decision} = \arg\max_i \max_j (P_{ij})
$$

**Hurwicz Criterion** (compromise with optimism index $\alpha \in [0,1]$):

$$
H_i = \alpha \cdot \max_j (P_{ij}) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \min_j (P_{ij})
$$

$$\text{Decision} = \arg\max_i H_i$$

**Minimax Regret** (Savage Criterion): for each state of nature, compute regret as missed opportunity, then minimise the maximum regret.

$$
R_{ij} = \max_k (P_{kj}) - P_{ij}
$$

$$\text{Decision} = \arg\min_i \max_j R_{ij}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Workflow Diagram — Scenario Manager Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Business Problem] --> B[Build Deterministic Model on Spreadsheet]
    B --> C[Open Data Tab - What-If Analysis - Scenario Manager]
    C --> D{Scenarios Exist?}
    D -- No --> E[Click Add - Define Scenario Name and Description]
    E --> F[Select Changing Cells - Up to 32]
    F --> G[Enter Values for Each Changing Cell]
    G --> H[Click OK to Save Scenario]
    H --> I{More Scenarios Needed?}
    I -- Yes --> E
    I -- No --> J[Click Summary to Generate Report]
    D -- Yes --> K[Select Scenario from List]
    K --> L[Click Show to Display Values]
    L --> M[Analyse Output Cells in Result Area]
    J --> N[Review Scenario Summary Report]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compare Across Scenarios using Metrics]
    O --> P[Make Data-Driven Business Decision]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Changing Cell 1]
        A2[Changing Cell 2]
        A3[Changing Cell N up to 32]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[SCENARIO MANAGER ENGINE]
        B1[Scenario 1 - Optimistic]
        B2[Scenario 2 - Base]
        B3[Scenario 3 - Pessimistic]

    end

    subgraph MODEL[DETERMINISTIC MODEL LAYER]

    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        D1[Scenario Summary Report Table]
        D2[Probability Weighted Metrics]
        D3[Decision Recommendation]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A3 --> B3

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3

    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Decision-Criteria Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Have Multiple Scenarios] --> B{Are Probabilities Known?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute Expected Value and Risk Metrics]
    C --> D[Use E Y and Sigma as Decision Criteria]
    B -- No --> E[Apply Decision Criteria]
    E --> F{Risk Appetite?}
    F -- Risk Averse --> G[Use Maximin Criterion]
    F -- Risk Seeking --> H[Use Maximax Criterion]

    F -- Loss Averse --> J[Use Minimax Regret Criterion]

    J --> K

```
