In KTU viva questions, students are often asked *"What exactly does the Scenario Manager do?"* — point to this node and explain that it **substitutes the changing-cell values** in the model and recalculates result cells.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Scenario Manager. List its two main components.**

**Model Answer:**

Scenario Manager is a *What-If Analysis* tool in spreadsheets that creates and compares multiple sets of input values (scenarios) for the same model.

The two main components are:
1. **Changing Cells** — input variables whose values differ across scenarios (max 32)
2. **Result Cells** — output cells whose values are observed after substitution

A third auxiliary component is the **Scenario Summary Report**, which presents all results in a side-by-side tabular format.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Scenario Manager and Goal Seek.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Scenario Manager | Goal Seek |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Compares outcomes of **multiple predefined input sets** | Finds the **single input value** that achieves a target output |
| Number of Inputs | Up to 32 | Exactly 1 |
| Direction | Forward substitution | Reverse calculation |
| Output | Comparative report across scenarios | Single numeric cell value |
| Example | "What if sales grow 10% vs. 20%?" | "What sales level yields ₹1 lakh profit?" |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** A retail company wants to evaluate its annual profit under three scenarios. The profit function is:

$$P = (P - C) \cdot U - E$$

where $P$ = price per unit, $C$ = cost per unit, $U$ = units sold, $E$ = annual expenses.

The three scenarios are:

| Scenario | Price $P$ | Cost $C$ | Units $U$ | Expense $E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best | ₹500 | ₹200 | 10,000 | ₹1,500,000 |
| Likely | ₹400 | ₹250 | 7,500 | ₹2,000,000 |
| Worst | ₹300 | ₹280 | 4,000 | ₹2,500,000 |

Calculate the profit under each scenario and identify the best and worst cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the probabilities of occurrence are 0.20 (Best), 0.60 (Likely), and 0.20 (Worst), compute the expected profit, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation. Comment on the business risk. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Profit under each scenario:**

**Best Case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{best}} &= (500 - 200) \cdot 10000 - 1500000 \\
&= 300 \cdot 10000 - 1500000 \\
&= 3000000 - 1500000 \\
&= \mathbf{₹\ 1{,}500{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Likely Case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{likely}} &= (400 - 250) \cdot 7500 - 2000000 \\
&= 150 \cdot 7500 - 2000000 \\
&= 1125000 - 2000000 \\
&= \mathbf{-₹\ 875{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worst Case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{worst}} &= (300 - 280) \cdot 4000 - 2500000 \\
&= 20 \cdot 4000 - 2500000 \\
&= 80000 - 2500000 \\
&= \mathbf{-₹\ 2{,}420{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Best Case yields ₹15 lakh profit; Likely and Worst cases produce losses. The **range of outcomes** is:

$$R = 1500000 - (-2420000) = \mathbf{₹\ 39{,}20{,}000}$$

indicating extreme volatility.

---

**(b) Statistical Analysis:**

**Expected Profit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[P] &= 0.20 \cdot 1500000 + 0.60 \cdot (-875000) + 0.20 \cdot (-2420000) \\
&= 300000 - 525000 - 484000 \\
&= \mathbf{-₹\ 7{,}09{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= 0.20 \cdot (1500000 - (-709000))^2 + 0.60 \cdot (-875000 - (-709000))^2 + 0.20 \cdot (-2420000 - (-709000))^2 \\
&= 0.20 \cdot (2209000)^2 + 0.60 \cdot (-166000)^2 + 0.20 \cdot (-1711000)^2 \\
&= 0.20 \cdot 4.880 \times 10^{12} + 0.60 \cdot 2.756 \times 10^{10} + 0.20 \cdot 2.928 \times 10^{12} \\
&= 9.759 \times 10^{11} + 1.653 \times 10^{10} + 5.855 \times 10^{11} \\
&= \mathbf{1.578 \times 10^{12}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{1.578 \times 10^{12}} \approx \mathbf{₹\ 12{,}56{,}184}
$$

**Coefficient of Variation:**

$$
CV = \frac{1256184}{-709000} \approx -1.77
$$

**Business Risk Commentary:**

The negative expected profit combined with extremely high standard deviation (~₹12.56 lakh) indicates **catastrophic business risk**. The company has a 80% probability of operating at a loss (combining Likely + Worst case probabilities). Strategic interventions — such as reducing fixed expenses by at least ₹10 lakh or increasing unit price by ₹60 — are essential to make the business model viable in the base case.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to create and compare scenarios using Microsoft Excel's Scenario Manager. Mention the menu path and key dialog boxes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company forecasts annual revenue under three fuel-price scenarios. Construct a decision matrix using the **Maximin, Maximax, Hurwicz (α = 0.6), and Minimax Regret** criteria.

| Decision / Fuel Price | Low (₹70/L) | Medium (₹85/L) | High (₹100/L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buy New Fleet | ₹50 lakh | ₹30 lakh | ₹10 lakh |
| Lease Fleet | ₹35 lakh | ₹32 lakh | ₹20 lakh |
| Outsource | ₹25 lakh | ₹25 lakh | ₹25 lakh |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a)** Procedure to use Scenario Manager:
1. Open the worksheet containing the model
2. Navigate: **Data tab → What-If Analysis → Scenario Manager**
3. Click **Add** → enter Scenario Name (e.g., "Best Case") and Description
4. Specify **Changing Cells** (Ctrl+click for non-contiguous)
5. Enter values for each changing cell → click OK
6. Repeat for all scenarios (Best, Base, Worst)
7. Click **Summary** → select Report Type → choose Result Cells → click OK

The resulting **Scenario Summary Report** presents a side-by-side comparison table.

**(b) Decision Criteria Computation:**

**Maximin (max of minimums):**
- Buy New: min = ₹10 lakh
- Lease: min = ₹20 lakh
- Outsource: min = ₹25 lakh
- **Decision: Outsource (₹25 lakh)**

**Maximax (max of maximums):**
- Buy New: max = ₹50 lakh
- Lease: max = ₹35 lakh
- Outsource: max = ₹25 lakh
- **Decision: Buy New (₹50 lakh)**

**Hurwicz (α = 0.6):**
- Buy New: $0.6 \cdot 50 + 0.4 \cdot 10 = 30 + 4 = 34$
- Lease: $0.6 \cdot 35 + 0.4 \cdot 20 = 21 + 8 = 29$
- Outsource: $0.6 \cdot 25 + 0.4 \cdot 25 = 15 + 10 = 25$
- **Decision: Buy New (₹34 lakh)**

**Minimax Regret:**

First, construct regret matrix (column max minus each value):

| Decision | Low Regret | Medium Regret | High Regret | Max Regret |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Buy New | 0 | 2 | 15 | 15 |
| Lease | 15 | 0 | 5 | 15 |
| Outsource | 25 | 7 | 0 | **25** |

Wait — minimum of maximums:
- Buy New: max = 15
- Lease: max = 15
- Outsource: max = 25
- **Decision: Buy New or Lease (tied at 15)**

**Final Insight:** Results diverge — risk-averse managers choose Outsource; growth-oriented managers choose Buy New. The Hurwicz compromise (α = 0.6) recommends Buy New, suggesting the company is moderately optimistic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Scenario Manager with Solver** — Marks deducted if you describe Scenario Manager as an *optimisation tool*. It is a *comparison tool*.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the menu path** — Always write: *Data → What-If Analysis → Scenario Manager → Add/Summary*. Examiners award 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Not labelling probabilities explicitly** — When computing Expected Value, the question *"how did you get those probabilities?"* will appear in the answer key. State assumptions.
> 4. **Mixing up changing cells with result cells** — Changing cells contain *raw inputs*; result cells contain *formulas that depend on changing cells*.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — Always write ₹, units, percentage, etc. A numeric answer without units loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in variance calculation** — Double-check squaring and subtraction signs; this is where most students slip.
> 7. **Not connecting back to the business decision** — The conclusion must address the original business question, not just present numbers.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Scenario Manager is a *What-If Analysis* tool for comparing outcomes under multiple discrete sets of input assumptions.
- **Menu Path:** `Data → What-If Analysis → Scenario Manager → Add / Show / Summary / Edit / Delete / Merge`.
- **Limits:** Up to **32 changing cells** per scenario; up to **32 result cells** in the summary report; unlimited number of scenarios.
- **Required Components:** Scenario Name, Changing Cells, Result Cells (added at report time).
- **Mandatory Workflow:** Identify problem → Build model → Open Scenario Manager → Add scenarios → Generate Summary Report → Analyse.
- **Key Analytical Metrics:** Expected Value $E[Y]$, Variance $\sigma^2$, Standard Deviation $\sigma$, Range, Coefficient of Variation $CV$.
- **Decision Criteria (when probabilities unknown):** Maximin, Maximax, Hurwicz, Minimax Regret.
- **Decision Criteria (when probabilities known):** Expected Value Maximisation, Expected Utility Maximisation, Risk-Adjusted Return.
- **Comparison Anchors:** *Scenario Manager = multiple inputs + descriptive comparison*; *Goal Seek = single input + target-seeking*; *Data Table = 1–2 inputs + grid sensitivity*; *Solver = many inputs + optimisation with constraints*.
- **Real-World Domains:** Finance forecasting, supply-chain planning, HR analytics, marketing ROI, energy-sector revenue modelling, manufacturing capacity planning.
- **Common Mistakes:** Confusing with Solver, omitting menu path, missing units, forgetting to state probability assumptions, mixing up changing and result cells.
- **Industrial Standards:** Aligned with Basel III stress testing, CCAR scenarios, and IFRS 9 expected-credit-loss modelling frameworks.
- **Visual Deliverable:** Always include a **Scenario Summary Report** (markdown table) in your KTU answer — it is the most mark-rich element.
- **Closing Line for Answers:** *Scenario Manager, therefore, empowers decision-makers to navigate uncertainty by quantifying the spread and probability-weighted outcomes across plausible futures.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goal-Seek
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Goal-Seek

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Goal-Seek** is a built-in *What-If Analysis* tool in Microsoft Excel that determines the input value required for a formula to achieve a specific target (goal) output. It is fundamentally a **single-variable root-finding (reverse-calculation) utility** that iteratively adjusts one independent (changing) cell to drive a dependent (result) formula cell to a user-defined numeric objective.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Business Analytics terminology, Goal-Seek belongs to the broader family of **prescriptive analytics tools** because it does not merely describe or predict data — it *prescribes* the exact input parameter needed to reach a business target.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight — Classification**
> - **Type of Analytics:** Prescriptive / What-If Analysis
> - **Domain:** Spreadsheet-based Decision Modeling
> - **Scope:** Single equation, single unknown
> - **Tools Ecosystem Member:** Goal-Seek → Scenario Manager → Solver (in increasing complexity)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Kochi to Delhi** (~2,500 km). You know the *target* (reach in 25 hours), and you know the *distance* is fixed, but you don't know the *average speed* required.

$$ \text{Speed} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} $$

You keep "guessing and adjusting" speeds until Time = 25 hours. This iterative back-calculation is exactly what **Goal-Seek** does — but instantly, inside Excel.

| Concept in Analogy | Excel Goal-Seek Component |
|---|---|
| Distance (fixed) | Constant input cell |
| Time (target = 25) | **Set cell** (the goal) |
| Speed (unknown) | **Changing cell** (what Goal-Seek solves for) |

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

> [!NOTE] **Default Convergence Settings in Excel Goal-Seek**
> - **Maximum Iterations:** **100**
> - **Maximum Change (Tolerance):** **0.001**
> - **Method:** Iterative numerical search (typically bisection / secant-like logic)
> - **Variables Allowed:** **Exactly 1** (one changing cell)
> - **Equations Solved:** **Exactly 1** (one set cell formula)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Goal-Seek

Goal-Seek is essentially a **numerical root-finder**. It treats your Excel formula as a function $f(x)$ and searches for the value of $x$ that satisfies:

$$ f(x) = T $$

where:
- $f(x)$ = the formula in the **Set Cell**
- $T$ = the **Target Value** (the goal)
- $x$ = the value in the **Changing Cell** (the unknown to be found)

Algebraically rearranged into root-finding form:

$$ g(x) = f(x) - T = 0 $$

Goal-Seek then iteratively evaluates $g(x)$ until $\vert g(x) \vert < \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is the convergence tolerance (**0.001** by default).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Mechanics

1. **Initial Probe:** Goal-Seek reads the current value of $x$ and computes $f(x)$.
2. **Error Calculation:** It computes the deviation $\Delta = f(x) - T$.
3. **Direction Detection:** It perturbs $x$ upward or downward to see which direction reduces $\vert \Delta \vert$.
4. **Bisection/Refinement:** It narrows the search interval around the suspected root.
5. **Convergence Check:** Iteration stops when $\vert \Delta \vert \leq 0.001$ **OR** when the iteration count reaches **100**.
6. **Reporting:** The final value of $x$ is written back into the Changing Cell.

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Limitations

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner Alert — Common Misconception**
> Goal-Seek is **NOT** the same as Solver. Do NOT interchange them in exam answers.

| Feature | Goal-Seek | Solver |
|---|---|---|
| Variables | 1 only | Many (up to 200) |
| Constraints | None | Yes (inequalities) |
| Algorithms | Iterative search | GRG, Simplex, Evolutionary |
| Use Case | Simple break-even / target | Optimization problems |
| Add-in Required | Built-in | Optional Add-in |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas below are **verifiable** and standard spreadsheet / numerical-methods identities.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Root-finding form | $g(x) = f(x) - T = 0$ | Rearranged equation to find root |
| 2 | Error at iteration $i$ | $E_i = f(x_i) - T$ | Deviation from target |
| 3 | Convergence criterion | $\vert E_i \vert \leq 0.001$ | Default Excel tolerance |
| 4 | Iteration limit | $i \leq 100$ | Maximum allowed loops |
| 5 | Break-Even (revenue) | $\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | Units to cover fixed cost |
| 6 | Target Profit Volume | $Q_{\text{target}} = \dfrac{F + \text{Profit}}{P - V}$ | Units for desired profit |
| 7 | Linear Demand | $P = a - bQ$ | Standard demand curve |
| 8 | Break-Even Price | $P_{\text{BE}} = V + \dfrac{F}{Q}$ | Minimum price to break even |
| 9 | CAGR | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Compound annual growth rate |
| 10 | PMT (Loan) | $\text{PMT} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}$ | Equated monthly installment |

> Where: $F$ = Fixed Cost, $P$ = Price/unit, $V$ = Variable Cost/unit, $Q$ = Quantity, $a$ = price-intercept, $b$ = slope.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Finance:** Finding the EMI required to repay a loan in a target tenure.
- **Marketing:** Determining the conversion rate needed to achieve a sales target.
- **Operations:** Back-calculating the production volume required for break-even.
- **HR Analytics:** Computing the headcount increase needed to hit revenue goals.
- **Engineering Economics:** Finding the rate of return that yields NPV = 0.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Quantity Using Goal-Seek Logic

**Business Problem:** A startup sells a product at a unit price of ₹500. Variable cost per unit is ₹300. Fixed monthly cost is ₹40,000. How many units must be sold to earn a profit of ₹60,000?

#### Step 1: Define the Profit Function

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = (P - V) \cdot Q - F $$

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = (500 - 300) \cdot Q - 40{,}000 $$

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = 200Q - 40{,}000 $$

#### Step 2: Set the Goal Condition

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = 60{,}000 $$

$$ 200Q - 40{,}000 = 60{,}000 $$

#### Step 3: Rearrange to Root-Finding Form

$$ g(Q) = 200Q - 40{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 0 $$

$$ g(Q) = 200Q - 100{,}000 = 0 $$

#### Step 4: Iterate (simulating Goal-Seek's internal search)

**Iteration 1** (initial guess $Q_0 = 100$):

$$ g(100) = 200(100) - 100{,}000 = 20{,}000 - 100{,}000 = -80{,}000 $$

Since $g < 0$, Profit is *below* target → need to **increase** $Q$.

**Iteration 2** (try $Q_1 = 400$):

$$ g(400) = 200(400) - 100{,}000 = 80{,}000 - 100{,}000 = -20{,}000 $$

Still negative → keep increasing $Q$.

**Iteration 3** (try $Q_2 = 500$):

$$ g(500) = 200(500) - 100{,}000 = 100{,}000 - 100{,}000 = 0 \;\;\checkmark $$

**Converged.** Root found at $Q = 500$ units.

#### Step 5: Verification

$$ \text{Profit}(500) = 200(500) - 40{,}000 = 100{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 60{,}000 \;\;\checkmark $$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Goal-Seek for Common Business Scenarios

| # | Business Goal | Set Cell Formula | Target Value | Changing Cell |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Break-even quantity | $\text{Profit}(Q)$ | 0 | Quantity $Q$ |
| 2 | Target profit volume | $\text{Profit}(Q)$ | Desired profit | Quantity $Q$ |
| 3 | Required selling price | Revenue $-$ Cost | 0 | Price $P$ |
| 4 | Loan EMI for a target tenure | PMT formula | Affordable EMI | Tenure $n$ |
| 5 | Conversion rate for revenue goal | Revenue formula | Target revenue | Conversion rate |
| 6 | Break-even price for fixed $Q$ | $\text{Profit}(P)$ | 0 | Price $P$ |
| 7 | Headcount for productivity target | Output per employee | Target output | Headcount |
| 8 | Discount % to move inventory | Net profit | Target profit | Discount rate |
| 9 | Required CAGR | Final value formula | Target value | Growth rate |
| 10 | Working capital cycle | Cash conversion | Target cash | Receivable days |

### 3.3 Derivation: Required Selling Price to Break Even

**Problem:** A company must sell 1,000 units. Fixed cost = ₹50,000. Variable cost = ₹40/unit. What is the minimum price to break even?

**Profit function:**

$$ \text{Profit}(P) = (P - 40)(1000) - 50{,}000 $$

**Goal condition:**

$$ (P - 40)(1000) - 50{,}000 = 0 $$

$$ 1000P - 40{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 0 $$

$$ 1000P = 90{,}000 $$

$$ P = 90 \;\;\text{(₹ per unit)} $$

**Interpretation:** Charge **at least ₹90** per unit to cover all costs. This is precisely the value Goal-Seek would write back into the Changing Cell (Price) in approximately 3–5 iterations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Goal-Seek

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Input Goal"] --> B["Set Target Value T"]
    B --> C["Identify Changing Cell x"]
    C --> D["Initial Guess x sub 0"]
    D --> E["Compute f x sub i"]
    E --> F{"Error less than 0.001"}
    F -- No --> G["Adjust x by Step"]
    G --> H{"Iteration less than 100"}
    H -- Yes --> E
    H -- No --> I["Display No Solution"]
    F -- Yes --> J["Write Final x to Cell"]
    J --> K["Output Result to User"]
    I --> K
    K --> L["End"]
```

### 4.2 Workflow Diagram — Using Goal-Seek in Excel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 Build Model with Formulas"] --> S2["Step 2 Place Cursor in Set Cell"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Data Menu"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 What If Analysis"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Goal Seek"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Enter Target Value"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7 Select Changing Cell"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8 Click OK"]
    S8 --> S9{"Converged?"}
    S9 -- Yes --> R1["Goal Seek Status Dialog"]
    R1 --> R2["Accept or Cancel Solution"]
    S9 -- No --> R3["Adjust Model and Retry"]
    R3 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic — When to Use Goal-Seek vs Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Need to Solve a What If Problem"] --> Q2{"How Many Unknowns?"}
    Q2 -- "Exactly 1" --> Q3{"Are There Constraints?"}
    Q2 -- "Two or More" --> P1["Use Solver"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> P2["Use Goal Seek"]
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> P3["Use Solver"]
    Q1 --> Q4{"Need Multiple Scenarios?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> P4["Use Scenario Manager"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> Q2
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation Tip for Examiners**
> When drawing Goal-Seek diagrams in the exam, always include the three named components: **Set Cell**, **Target Value**, and **Changing Cell** — these are mandatory terminology in KTU valuation keys.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Goal-Seek. List any two of its distinguishing features.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Goal-Seek is a What-If Analysis tool in Microsoft Excel that finds the input value required for a formula to reach a user-specified target. It is a single-variable reverse-calculation utility.

**Two Features (1 mark):**
1. It works with **only one changing cell** and **one set cell** at a time.
2. It uses an iterative search method with default tolerance of **0.001** and a maximum of **100 iterations**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Goal-Seek and Solver. Mention any three points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Goal-Seek | Solver |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solves for **1 variable** only | Solves for **multiple variables** |
| 2 | Does **not support constraints** | Supports **equality & inequality constraints** |
| 3 | Built-in to Excel by default | Requires loading the **Solver Add-in** |
| 4 | Simpler interface (3 fields) | Advanced interface with options & reports |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**A startup manufactures eco-friendly bottles. The selling price is ₹120/unit, variable cost is ₹70/unit, and total fixed cost is ₹50,000 per month.**

**(a)** Build the profit function and explain how Goal-Seek would find the **break-even quantity**. Show the step-by-step algebraic process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The startup now wants a **monthly profit of ₹1,00,000**. Using Goal-Seek logic, determine the quantity to be sold. Verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Construct the Profit Function (2 marks)**

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = (P - V)Q - F $$

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = (120 - 70)Q - 50{,}000 = 50Q - 50{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Define the Goal-Seek Setup (2 marks)**

- **Set Cell:** The cell containing $\text{Profit}(Q)$
- **Target Value:** $0$ (break-even point)
- **Changing Cell:** The cell containing $Q$

**Step 3: Apply Root-Finding Form (1 mark)**

$$ 50Q - 50{,}000 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q = \dfrac{50{,}000}{50} = 1{,}000 \text{ units} $$

**Step 4: Goal-Seek Iterative Logic Explanation (2 marks)**

Goal-Seek starts with an initial guess (say $Q = 500$), computes $\text{Profit} = -25{,}000$ (negative → under target), then increases $Q$ in successive iterations: 800 → 950 → 990 → 999 → 1000, until $\vert \text{Profit} - 0 \vert \leq 0.001$. The Changing Cell is finally set to **1,000 units**.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Modify the Goal (1 mark)**

$$ 50Q - 50{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Rearrange (1 mark)**

$$ 50Q = 1{,}50{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Solve (1 mark)**

$$ Q = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{50} = 3{,}000 \text{ units} $$

**Step 4: Verification (2 marks)**

$$ \text{Profit}(3000) = 50(3000) - 50{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \;\checkmark $$

**Step 5: Goal-Seek Reporting (2 marks)**

Upon convergence, the status dialog "Goal Seeking with Cell X found a solution" is displayed. The Changing Cell now shows **3,000**, and the Set Cell shows the target profit ₹1,00,000.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**A retail company wants to discount a product priced at ₹2,000 such that after a flat 10% discount, total sales of 100 units yield a revenue of ₹1,80,000. Variable cost is ₹800/unit and fixed cost is ₹20,000.**

**(a)** Formulate the model and explain how Goal-Seek can determine the **required discount percentage** to hit the revenue target. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Instead of changing the discount, the manager wants to find the **number of units to sell** (keeping discount = 10%) to achieve an **after-discount profit of ₹50,000**. Use Goal-Seek logic. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Build the Revenue Equation (2 marks)**

$$ \text{Revenue} = 2000 \cdot (1 - d) \cdot Q $$

$$ \text{Revenue} = 2000 \cdot (1 - d) \cdot 100 = 200{,}000(1 - d) $$

**Step 2: Set the Goal-Seek Target (1 mark)**

$$ 200{,}000(1 - d) = 1{,}80{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Solve Algebraically (2 marks)**

$$ 1 - d = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{200{,}000} = 0.9 $$

$$ d = 0.1 = 10\% \;\;\checkmark $$

**Step 4: Goal-Seek Setup Explanation (2 marks)**

- **Set Cell:** Revenue cell
- **Target Value:** 1,80,000
- **Changing Cell:** Discount % cell
- Goal-Seek iterates by reducing $d$ from 0% upward until Revenue = 1,80,000. The answer converges to **10%**.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Profit Function with Discount (2 marks)**

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = \big[2000(1 - 0.1) - 800\big] \cdot Q - 20{,}000 $$

$$ \text{Profit}(Q) = (1800 - 800)Q - 20{,}000 = 1000Q - 20{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Apply Goal Condition (1 mark)**

$$ 1000Q - 20{,}000 = 50{,}000 $$

**Step 3: Solve (1 mark)**

$$ 1000Q = 70{,}000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q = 70 \text{ units} $$

**Step 4: Verification (1 mark)**

$$ \text{Profit}(70) = 1000(70) - 20{,}000 = 70{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 50{,}000 \;\checkmark $$

**Step 5: Goal-Seek Iteration Insight (2 marks)**

Goal-Seek starts at $Q = 50$, profit = 30,000 (too low), then steps up: 60 → 65 → 68 → 69 → 70, until $\vert \text{Profit} - 50{,}000 \vert \leq 0.001$. Final output: **70 units**.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Wrong terminology:** Writing "Goal Seek" instead of "Goal-Seek" in definitions may cost **0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Set Cell and Changing Cell:** Always remember — the *Set Cell* contains the **target formula**, the *Changing Cell* contains the **unknown input**.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always append "₹" or "units" in the final answer. A number without units may lose **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping verification:** KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** explicitly for verification steps. Never skip them.
> 5. **Confusing Goal-Seek with Solver:** When asked for "single-variable" problems, Goal-Seek is the right answer — not Solver.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Goal-Seek**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Single-variable What-If tool that back-calculates an input to achieve a target output.
- ⭐ **Three Mandatory Components:**
  - **Set Cell** → contains the formula to be matched
  - **Target Value** → the desired numeric goal
  - **Changing Cell** → the input to be solved
- ⭐ **Default Settings:** Max iterations = **100**, Tolerance = **0.001**.
- ⭐ **Scope:** **1 equation, 1 unknown** only — no multiple variables, no constraints.
- ⭐ **Mathematical Form:** Converts to root-finding $g(x) = f(x) - T = 0$.
- ⭐ **Menu Path:** Data → What-If Analysis → Goal Seek.
- ⭐ **Common Use Cases:** Break-even analysis, target profit, EMI calculation, CAGR back-solving, required conversion rate.
- ⭐ **Key Difference from Solver:** Solver handles multi-variable optimization with constraints; Goal-Seek does not.
- ⭐ **Always verify** the algebraic answer by substituting back into the original profit/revenue equation.
- ⭐ **Examiner Loves:** Step-by-step iteration explanation + verification step + correct terminology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Other Business Areas - Analytics in Telecom and Location-Based Intelligence Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications in Other Business Areas - Analytics in Telecom and Location-Based Intelligence Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Analytics in the Telecommunications Industry

### 📌 Formal Definition
> **Telecom Analytics** is the systematic application of descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytical techniques on multi-structured telecom data (CDR — Call Detail Records, CRM logs, network sensor data, billing data, and social media chatter) to optimize network performance, maximize customer lifetime value, reduce churn, detect fraud, and improve revenue assurance.

It is a cross-functional discipline combining **Big Data Engineering**, **Statistical Modeling**, and **Telecom Domain Knowledge (TM Forum Frameworx)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Telecom Analytics is treated as a **vertical application** of business analytics — meaning the *same* descriptive/predictive/prescriptive stack is applied to a *specific industry data architecture*. Examiners expect students to map standard BA techniques (e.g., logistic regression, clustering, decision trees) onto telecom use-cases.

### 🍕 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **huge pizza restaurant chain (telecom operator)** with **10 crore daily customers**. Each customer calls, texts, browses, complains, switches plans, or leaves for another operator. The operator has:
- **CDRs** = the restaurant's order tickets (who ordered what, when, for how long).
- **CRM data** = loyalty card information.
- **Network logs** = the oven temperature and kitchen equipment health.

**Telecom Analytics is the kitchen manager who looks at all this data** to predict: *Which customer will stop coming next month? Which pizza is over-cooking the oven? Which delivery boy is delaying orders?* The manager acts *before* losses occur.

### 🎯 The Five Pillars of Telecom Analytics

| Pillar | Core Question Answered | Sample KPI |
|---|---|---|
| **Churn Analytics** | Who will leave? | Churn Rate %, Survival Probability |
| **CLV Analytics** | Who is most profitable long-term? | Customer Lifetime Value, ARPU |
| **Network Analytics** | Where is the network failing? | Drop Call Rate, Throughput Mbps |
| **Fraud Analytics** | Who is abusing the system? | Fraud Loss per 1000 subscribers |
| **Revenue Assurance** | Where is revenue leaking? | Revenue Leakage %, Margin Erosion |

> [!NOTE]
> **ARPU** = **Average Revenue Per User**. It is the single most-cited telecom KPI. Formula: $\text{ARPU} = \frac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Average Subscriber Count}}$ over a fixed period (typically monthly).

---

## 1.2 Location-Based Intelligence (LBI) Marketing

### 📌 Formal Definition
> **Location-Based Intelligence (LBI) Marketing** is the practice of collecting, integrating, and analyzing geospatial data (GPS coordinates, Wi-Fi fingerprints, Bluetooth beacons, cell-tower triangulation, IP geolocation) to derive actionable marketing insights — enabling micro-targeted promotions, hyper-local customer segmentation, proximity-based offers, and site selection decisions.

LBI is a sub-discipline of **Geomarketing** combined with **Real-Time Analytics**.

### 🍕 Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **mall with 200 shops**. The mall has CCTV, Wi-Fi hotspots, and entry cards. The mall manager (LBI analyst) can see:
- Which corridor you walk through (footfall heat maps).
- How long you stand at a specific shop window (dwell-time analytics).
- That you came from Office Park A, therefore likely an office-goer (contextual profiling).

The manager sends a **push notification with a 20% off coupon** to your phone the moment you pass near the coffee shop — *right when intent is highest*. This is **proximity marketing**, the operational face of LBI.

### 🧭 Sources of Location Data (Taxonomy)

| Source | Accuracy | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **GPS** | 3–10 m outdoors | Vehicle tracking, outdoor campaigns |
| **Wi-Fi Triangulation** | 5–15 m indoors | Mall/in-store analytics |
| **Bluetooth Beacons** | 1–3 m | Proximity push notifications |
| **Cell Tower (GSM/4G/5G)** | 50–3000 m | Coarse regional targeting |
| **IP Geolocation** | City-level | Web-content localization |
| **Geo-tagged Social Media** | Variable | Trend-spotting, event detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** Do NOT confuse **Geomarketing** (strategic, region-level planning) with **Proximity Marketing** (tactical, real-time push at a point-of-interest). Examiners award partial marks if these are used interchangeably.

### 🔗 The Linkage Between Telecom and LBI
Telecom operators are the **only entities** that possess the **carrier-grade location infrastructure** (cell-tower triangulation, 5G mmWave positioning, IMSI-catch ranges). Therefore, **LBI marketing frequently runs on telecom data pipes** — making these two topics naturally coupled in KTU Module 4.

---

## 🧠 Section 1 Recap
- Telecom Analytics = data-driven decisioning across churn, CLV, network, fraud, revenue.
- LBI Marketing = geospatial analytics for hyper-local targeting and micro-segmentation.
- The two domains converge because telcos own the location infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Telecom Analytics — Conceptual Framework

Telecom analytics operates on a **four-layer maturity pyramid** (aligned with the TM Forum Analytics Maturity Model):

### 🔺 Layer 1 — Descriptive Analytics (What happened?)
- Dashboards on call volumes, ARPU, data consumption, dropped calls.
- Tools: Tableau, Power BI, Qlik.
- Output: **Static, historical reports**.

### 🔺 Layer 2 — Diagnostic Analytics (Why did it happen?)
- Root-cause analysis of churn spikes, network outages.
- Techniques: **Drill-down, Correlation Analysis, Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagrams**.
- Output: **Causal narrative**.

### 🔺 Layer 3 — Predictive Analytics (What will happen?)
- Churn prediction using **Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, Survival Analysis (Cox Proportional Hazards)**.
- CLV forecasting using **BG/NBD (Beta-Geometric/NBD) + Gamma-Gamma** models.
- Output: **Probabilistic future-state scoring**.

### 🔺 Layer 4 — Prescriptive Analytics (What should we do?)
- Next-Best-Offer (NBO) engines.
- Network investment optimization.
- Dynamic pricing using **reinforcement learning**.
- Output: **Action recommendation with confidence scores**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Cognitive Mapping:** Descriptive & Diagnostic = **L2 (Understand)** questions; Predictive = **L3 (Apply)**; Prescriptive = **L4 (Analyze/Evaluate)**. Examiner's valuation expects you to clearly **state which layer** your example belongs to.

---

## 2.2 Key Use-Case Deep-Dive — Churn Analytics

### 🎯 Objective
Predict the probability that a subscriber will **voluntarily disconnect** within a defined time horizon (typically 30, 60, or 90 days).

### 🧮 Churn Rate Formula
$$\text{Churn Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Customers Lost During Period}}{\text{Number of Customers at Start of Period}} \times 100\%$$

### 🛡️ Survival Function (Kaplan-Meier)
The probability that a customer survives past time $t$:
$$S(t) = \prod_{i : t_i \le t} \left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)$$
where $d_i$ = number of churn events at time $t_i$, and $n_i$ = number of customers at risk just before $t_i$.

### 📈 Hazard Function (Cox Proportional Hazards)
$$h(t \vert \mathbf{X}) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p)$$
where $h_0(t)$ is the baseline hazard, and $\mathbf{X}$ = vector of covariates (tenure, ARPU, complaint count, data usage, plan type).

---

## 2.3 Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — The Core Telecom Metric

### 🧮 Standard Discrete CLV Formula
$$\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$$
where:
- $R_t$ = Revenue from customer in period $t$
- $C_t$ = Cost of serving customer in period $t$
- $r_t$ = Retention probability in period $t$ (from survival analysis)
- $d$ = Discount rate (typically WACC or risk-free rate)
- $T$ = Forecast horizon (usually 3–5 years)

### 📉 Simplified (Constant Margin) CLV
$$\text{CLV} = \text{Margin} \times \frac{r}{(1 + d) - r}$$
This is the closed-form **contractual + stochastic** CLV derived from the Pareto/NBD assumption.

### 🧮 ARPU (Recap)
$$\text{ARPU} = \frac{\text{Total Revenue in Period}}{\text{Average Subscriber Count}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked *"How does analytics improve ARPU?"*, the model answer should connect: **Predictive CLV → Segmented Offers → Targeted Upsell → ARPU ↑**. Examiners award marks for the **logical chain**, not isolated facts.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet (Telecom)

| Metric | Formula | Unit | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Churn Rate** | Lost Customers / Start Customers $\times 100$ | % | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ARPU** | Revenue / Subscribers | ₹/month | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **CLV (Discrete)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | ₹ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Survival Function** | $S(t) = \prod (1 - d_i/n_i)$ | Probability | ⭐⭐ |
| **Hazard (Cox)** | $h(t) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta^T X)$ | Rate | ⭐⭐ |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | \% Promoters $-$ \% Detractors | $-100$ to $+100$ | ⭐⭐ |
| **RFM Score** | Recency + Frequency + Monetary (scaled) | Composite index | ⭐⭐ |
| **Revenue Leakage** | Lost Revenue / Billed Revenue $\times 100$ | % | ⭐ |

> ⚠️ **Markdown Note:** All $\vert$ symbols in the table above are rendered using `\vert` (not `|`) to preserve table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Location-Based Intelligence — Theoretical Framework

### 🧭 The LBI Analytical Pipeline (Five-Stage)
1. **Data Acquisition** — GPS, Wi-Fi, beacons, CDR triangulation.
2. **Data Cleansing & Geocoding** — Convert addresses → lat/long.
3. **Spatial Analysis** — Buffer, overlay, proximity, clustering.
4. **Pattern Mining** — Hotspot detection, trajectory mining, dwell-time.
5. **Activation** — Push notifications, geofenced ads, store visit attribution.

### 📐 Haversine Formula — Distance Between Two Geo-Coordinates
$$d = 2r \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\phi_2 - \phi_1}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\lambda_2 - \lambda_1}{2}\right)}\right)$$
where:
- $r$ = Earth's radius (**6,371 km**)
- $\phi_1, \phi_2$ = latitudes (radians)
- $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ = longitudes (radians)

> [!NOTE]
> The Haversine is the **default distance metric** for great-circle problems on a sphere. It is preferred over Euclidean distance on geo-data because Earth is curved, not flat.

### 🟢 Geofencing Radius Concept
A **geofence** is a virtual perimeter around a real-world geographic area. The radius $R$ is a tunable parameter:
- **Retail Store** → $R = 100$ m
- **Shopping Mall** → $R = 500$ m
- **City Zone** → $R = 5{,}000$ m

**Conversion Efficiency** (LBI KPI):
$$\text{Conversion Rate}_{\text{LBI}} = \frac{\text{Ad Recipients Who Visited Store}}{\text{Total Ad Recipients}} \times 100\%$$

### 🧮 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet (LBI)

| Metric | Formula | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Haversine Distance** | $2r \arcsin(\sqrt{\ldots})$ | Nearest-store routing |
| **Dwell Time** | Exit Timestamp $-$ Entry Timestamp | Section interest scoring |
| **Footfall Index** | Visits / Hour | Store performance benchmarking |
| **Geofence Conversion Rate** | Visits / Recipients $\times 100$ | Campaign ROI |
| **Hotspot Z-Score** | $(X - \mu)/\sigma$ | Anomaly hotspot detection |
| **Walk-in Lift %** | $(\text{Test} - \text{Control})/\text{Control} \times 100$ | A/B test of LBI campaign |

---

## 2.6 Why These Formulas Matter in Practice
- **CLV** dictates **how much to spend on customer acquisition (CAC)** — the rule of thumb is CLV:CAC $\ge 3:1$.
- **Churn Rate** inversely drives valuation — telco multiples drop 1–2x for every 1% churn increase.
- **Haversine** underpins ride-sharing (Ola/Uber dispatch), food delivery (Swiggy/Zomato), and last-mile logistics.
- **Geofence Conversion** is the de-facto ROI proof for retail LBI campaigns and is the most-asked metric in B-school case studies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CLV Calculation for a Telecom Subscriber

### 📋 Given Data (KTU-style problem)
Airtel Kerala wants to compute the 3-year CLV of a postpaid subscriber.
- Average monthly revenue $R = \text{₹}799$
- Average monthly serving cost $C = \text{₹}280$
- Annual retention probability $= 0.85$ (i.e., 15% annual churn)
- Annual discount rate $d = 10\%$
- Forecast horizon $T = 3$ years

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Margin and Convert to Annual CLV Form**

$$R_{\text{annual}} = 799 \times 12 = 9588$$
$$C_{\text{annual}} = 280 \times 12 = 3360$$
$$M = R_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{annual}} = 9588 - 3360 = 6228$$

So annual margin $M = \text{₹}6{,}228$.

**Step 2 — Use the Simplified Constant-Margin CLV Formula**

$$\text{CLV} = M \cdot \frac{r}{1 + d - r}$$

Plug in $M = 6228$, $r = 0.85$, $d = 0.10$:

$$\text{CLV} = 6228 \cdot \frac{0.85}{1.10 - 0.85}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Denominator**

$$1.10 - 0.85 = 0.25$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Ratio**

$$\frac{0.85}{0.25} = 3.4$$

**Step 5 — Final CLV**

$$\text{CLV} = 6228 \times 3.4 = 21{,}475.2$$

$$\boxed{\text{CLV} \approx \text{₹}21{,}475}$$

### 🧠 Interpretation
- The customer is worth **₹21,475 over 3 years** in present value.
- Airtel can justify spending up to ₹7,158 on acquisition (CLV ÷ 3).
- If CLV < CAC, the company is **destroying value** with every new sign-up.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Churn Prediction Using Logistic Regression (Conceptual Walkthrough)

### 📋 Setup
A Jio prepaid customer has these features (normalized 0–1 scale):
- Tenure (months/60) = 0.50
- ARPU (₹/1000) = 0.30
- Complaint count (last 90 days) = 0.80
- Data usage drop (last 30 vs prior 30 days) = 0.70

The fitted logistic model is:
$$P(\text{Churn}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = -2.5 + 1.2(\text{Tenure}) - 1.8(\text{ARPU}) + 2.5(\text{Complaints}) + 1.1(\text{DataDrop})$$

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $z$**

$$z = -2.5 + 1.2(0.50) - 1.8(0.30) + 2.5(0.80) + 1.1(0.70)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate Each Term**

- $1.2 \times 0.50 = 0.60$
- $-1.8 \times 0.30 = -0.54$
- $2.5 \times 0.80 = 2.00$
- $1.1 \times 0.70 = 0.77$

**Step 3 — Sum**

$$z = -2.5 + 0.60 - 0.54 + 2.00 + 0.77 = 0.33$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Sigmoid**

$$P(\text{Churn}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.33}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.7189} = \frac{1}{1.7189} \approx 0.5817$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

$$\boxed{P(\text{Churn}) \approx 58.2\%}$$

This is a **HIGH-RISK subscriber** (threshold typically 0.50). Airtel/Jio's retention engine should:
1. Trigger a **win-back offer** (e.g., 10 GB bonus data).
2. Route to a **priority call center**.
3. Assign a **field relationship manager** for high-ARPU cases.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Haversine Distance for Nearest-Store

### 📋 Problem
A customer is at coordinates $(\phi_1, \lambda_1) = (9.9312°\text{ N}, 76.2673°\text{ E})$ — **Kochi city center**.
A Reliance Digital store is at $(\phi_2, \lambda_2) = (9.9700°\text{ N}, 76.2900°\text{ E})$.
Compute the great-circle distance.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert Degrees to Radians**

$$\phi_1 = 9.9312 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17336 \text{ rad}$$
$$\phi_2 = 9.9700 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17401 \text{ rad}$$
$$\lambda_1 = 76.2673 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33101 \text{ rad}$$
$$\lambda_2 = 76.2900 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33141 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Latitudinal and Longitudinal Differences**

$$\Delta\phi = 0.17401 - 0.17336 = 0.00065 \text{ rad}$$
$$\Delta\lambda = 1.33141 - 1.33101 = 0.00040 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Sine Half-Differences**

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = \sin(0.000325) \approx 0.000325$$
$$\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \approx 1.056 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) = \sin(0.00020) \approx 0.00020$$
$$\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4 — Cosine of Latitudes**

$$\cos(\phi_1) \approx \cos(0.17336) \approx 0.98503$$
$$\cos(\phi_2) \approx \cos(0.17401) \approx 0.98492$$

**Step 5 — Inner Square Root Term**

$$a = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$$

$$a = 1.056 \times 10^{-7} + (0.98503)(0.98492)(4.0 \times 10^{-8})$$

$$a = 1.056 \times 10^{-7} + 3.880 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$a = 1.444 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 6 — Outer Square Root**

$$\sqrt{a} = \sqrt{1.444 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 3.800 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Arcsin**

$$2 \arcsin(3.800 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 7.600 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 8 — Multiply by Earth's Radius (6371 km)**

$$d = 6371 \times 7.600 \times 10^{-4} \approx 4.842 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 4.84 \text{ km}}$$

### 🧠 Interpretation
- The store is **4.84 km away** from the customer.
- A **geofence radius of 5 km** would catch this customer for a promotion.
- Distance < 5 km → eligible for **"Store within 5 km" push notification**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Geofence Conversion Rate (LBI ROI Check)

### 📋 Problem
A BigBazaar store in Thrissur ran a **LBI campaign** for 30 days:
- Total push notifications sent = **20,000**
- Customers who **entered the geofence** = **3,200**
- Customers who **made a purchase** = **480**

Compute the LBI funnel metrics.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Geofence Entry Rate**

$$\text{Entry Rate} = \frac{3200}{20000} \times 100 = 16\%$$

**Step 2 — Visit-to-Purchase Conversion**

$$\text{Conversion Rate} = \frac{480}{3200} \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Step 3 — End-to-End Push-to-Purchase Rate**

$$\text{End-to-End Rate} = \frac{480}{20000} \times 100 = 2.4\%$$

**Step 4 — Walk-in Lift % (vs. Control Store with no campaign)**

If the control store had a 1.2% walk-in rate:
$$\text{Walk-in Lift} = \frac{2.4 - 1.2}{1.2} \times 100 = 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{The LBI campaign DOUBLED the walk-in rate.}}$$

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Mapping Techniques to Use-Cases

| Business Problem | Data Type | Analytical Technique | Tool Stack | Output Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Churn Prediction** | CDR + CRM | Logistic Reg., XGBoost, Survival | Python, SAS, SPSS | Risk Score |
| **CLV Forecasting** | Billing + Tenure | BG/NBD, Gamma-Gamma, Markov Chain | R (BTYD), Python | ₹ Value |
| **Network Outage RCA** | Sensor logs | Time-series, Root-Cause, CUSUM | Splunk, Kafka | Alert |
| **Fraud Detection** | CDR + Velocity | Anomaly Detection, Isolation Forest | Spark, Flink | Flag |
| **Hotspot Identification** | Geo-tagged transactions | DBSCAN, K-Means, KDE | QGIS, ArcGIS | Heatmap |
| **Site Selection** | Demographics + Traffic | Multi-Criteria Decision, Gravity Model | Tableau, Excel | Ranked List |
| **Proximity Marketing** | Real-time GPS | Geofencing, Rule Engine | Google Maps API, Firebase | Push Notification |
| **Customer Journey** | Wi-Fi + POS | Markov Chain, Path Analysis | Mixpanel, Adobe | Funnel Map |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
