# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Telecom Analytics — End-to-End Functional Architecture

> The diagram below maps the **data → analytics → business action** flow for a telecom operator's analytics stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CDR Data Source] --> B1[Data Ingestion Layer]
    A2[CRM and Billing Data] --> B1
    A3[Network Sensor Logs] --> B1
    A4[Social Media and NPS] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Data Lake and Data Warehouse]
    C1 --> D1[Descriptive Analytics]
    C1 --> D2[Diagnostic Analytics]
    C1 --> D3[Predictive Analytics]
    C1 --> D4[Prescriptive Analytics]
    D1 --> E1[Executive Dashboards]
    D2 --> E2[Root Cause Reports]
    D3 --> E3[Churn Risk Score]
    D3 --> E4[CLV Forecast]
    D4 --> E5[Next Best Offer Engine]
    D4 --> E6[Network Investment Optimizer]
    E3 --> F1[Retention Campaign]
    E4 --> F2[Customer Tiering and Loyalty]
    E5 --> F3[Personalized Marketing]
    E6 --> F4[Capex Allocation]
    F1 --> G1[Reduced Churn and Higher ARPU]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- **Layer A (Sources):** Heterogeneous telecom data (CDR, CRM, network, social).
- **Layer B (Ingestion):** Kafka, Flume, or cloud-native equivalents.
- **Layer C (Storage):** Data Lake (raw) + Data Warehouse (structured).
- **Layer D (Analytics):** The four-tier descriptive→prescriptive stack.
- **Layer E (Insights):** Domain-specific outputs (churn score, CLV, NBO).
- **Layer F (Action):** Operational interventions.
- **Layer G (Outcome):** Business KPIs (lower churn, higher ARPU).

---

## 4.2 Location-Based Intelligence Marketing — Functional Architecture

> The diagram below shows the **acquisition → enrichment → activation** pipeline for LBI marketing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[User Mobile Device] --> B1[GPS Sensor]
    A1 --> B2[WiFi and Bluetooth]
    A1 --> B3[Cell Tower Signal]
    A1 --> B4[App SDK Telemetry]
    B1 --> C1[Location Data Aggregator]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Geocoding and Reverse Lookup]
    D1 --> E1[Spatial Database and GIS Engine]
    E1 --> F1[Hotspot and Cluster Analysis]
    E1 --> F2[Geofencing Rule Engine]
    E1 --> F3[Dwell Time and Trajectory Mining]
    F1 --> G1[Customer Segmentation]
    F2 --> G2[Proximity Marketing Engine]
    F3 --> G3[Journey Analytics]
    G1 --> H1[Targeted Campaign]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H2[Store Layout Optimization]
    H1 --> I1[Push Notification and Coupon]
    H2 --> I2[Mall Analytics Dashboard]
    I1 --> J1[Higher Conversion and ROI]
    I2 --> J1
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- **A → B:** Device-level location signal capture.
- **C → D:** Data fusion and geocoding (lat/long → address & vice versa).
- **E:** GIS engine (PostGIS, ArcGIS, Google Maps Platform).
- **F:** Three parallel analytical processes.
- **G → H:** Insights converted to campaigns and store-design changes.
- **I → J:** Revenue and engagement uplift.

---

## 4.3 Churn Prediction Model — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Customer Data] --> B1[Feature Engineering]
    B1 --> C1{Train or Predict Mode}
    C1 -->|Train| D1[Label Historical Churners]
    D1 --> E1[Fit Logistic Regression and XGBoost]
    E1 --> F1[Validate on Holdout Set]
    F1 --> G1[Compute AUC and Lift]
    C1 -->|Predict| H1[Score Live Customers]
    G1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Apply Risk Threshold 0.5]
    I1 --> J1[Trigger Retention Action]
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- A single **feature engineering layer** feeds both training and scoring.
- Training produces a **validated model** (AUC ≥ 0.75 is industry acceptable).
- Live scoring applies a **business rule threshold** before triggering an action.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### 🔹 Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Customer Lifetime Value (CLV). State its significance in the telecom industry.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Customer Lifetime Value is the **total net present value of future cash flows** (revenue minus cost) expected from a customer over the entire duration of their relationship with the company, discounted to present value using a risk-adjusted discount rate.

$$\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) r_t}{(1+d)^t}$$

**Significance in Telecom (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) Justification:** A telco can spend up to ~33% of CLV to acquire a customer without destroying value.
2. **Segmentation & Loyalty Tiers:** High-CLV customers receive platinum retention treatment.
3. **Valuation:** Telecom equity valuations are directly linked to aggregate CLV.
4. **Profitability Targeting:** Marketing spend is optimized to high-CLV micro-segments.
5. **Strategic Planning:** Guides long-term network and product investment.

---

### 🔹 Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Location-Based Intelligence (LBI) marketing? List any four data sources used for LBI.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Location-Based Intelligence marketing is the use of **geospatial data (GPS, Wi-Fi, beacons, cell-tower triangulation) and analytical techniques** to deliver contextually relevant, geographically targeted marketing messages and to derive customer-behavior insights tied to physical locations.

**Four Data Sources (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **GPS** — Outdoor positioning, accuracy 3–10 m.
2. **Wi-Fi Triangulation** — Indoor positioning, accuracy 5–15 m.
3. **Bluetooth Beacons** — Sub-3 m proximity for retail stores.
4. **Cell Tower Triangulation** — Carrier-grade, 50–3000 m.
5. *(Alt: IP Geolocation, Geo-tagged Social Media Posts, RFID)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **(14 Marks)**

**"Telecom operators increasingly rely on analytics to combat churn and maximize Customer Lifetime Value." Discuss the role of analytics in the telecom industry. Explain the following with examples:**
**(a)** Churn prediction methodology and the **logistic regression churn model** with a suitable illustration. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Customer Lifetime Value computation, the **CLV formula**, and its **strategic use** in telecom marketing decisions. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction to Telecom Churn Analytics (2 Marks):**
- Telecom churn is the **percentage of subscribers who voluntarily disconnect** services in a given period.
- Industry benchmarks: India prepaid churn ≈ **3–5% monthly**, postpaid ≈ **1–2% monthly**.
- A **1% reduction in churn** can translate to **5–10% increase in EBITDA** for telcos.

**2. Churn Prediction Methodology (2 Marks):**
- **Step 1:** Collect CDR, CRM, billing, complaint, network-usage data.
- **Step 2:** Feature engineering — derive tenure, ARPU, complaint count, data-drop %, recharge gap.
- **Step 3:** Label historical churners (binary 0/1).
- **Step 4:** Train classifier (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost).
- **Step 5:** Score live customers → risk score.
- **Step 6:** Trigger retention action if score > threshold (typically 0.5).

**3. Logistic Regression Model (2 Marks):**
$$P(Y=1 \mid X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p)}}$$

**Illustration:** A subscriber with $\beta_0 = -2.5$ and weighted feature sum $z = 0.33$ yields $P(\text{Churn}) = 0.58$ → **HIGH RISK** → trigger win-back.

**4. Evaluation Metrics (1 Mark):**
- **AUC-ROC** (target ≥ 0.75)
- **Lift chart** (top decile lift > 3)
- **Confusion matrix** (precision, recall, F1)

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**1. CLV Definition & Formula (3 Marks):**
$$\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$$
The constant-margin closed form:
$$\text{CLV} = M \cdot \frac{r}{1 + d - r}$$

**Worked Example:** A subscriber with $M = \text{₹}6{,}228$, $r = 0.85$, $d = 0.10$ gives:
$$\text{CLV} = 6228 \times \frac{0.85}{0.25} = \text{₹}21{,}475$$

**2. Strategic Use in Telecom Marketing (4 Marks):**
- **a) Acquisition Spending (1 M):** CLV:CAC ≥ 3:1 rule. Max CAC = ₹7,158.
- **b) Tiered Loyalty (1 M):** Platinum (CLV > ₹50k), Gold (₹20k–50k), Silver (< ₹20k).
- **c) Offer Personalization (1 M):** Cross-sell (broadband to high-CLV mobile user).
- **d) Retention Priority (1 M):** High-CLV + high-churn-risk → VIP retention queue.

---

### 🅱️ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **(14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice)*

**"Location-Based Intelligence (LBI) is transforming the way marketers engage with customers." Explain this statement by addressing:**
**(a)** The **conceptual framework of LBI marketing** and its **data sources** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** The **Haversine formula** for computing distance between two geo-coordinates, with a **worked numerical example**, and explain **how LBI metrics measure campaign effectiveness**. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. LBI Conceptual Framework (3 Marks):**
LBI operates on a **five-stage pipeline:**
1. **Data Acquisition** — GPS, Wi-Fi, beacons, cell-tower, IP.
2. **Geocoding & Cleansing** — Lat/Long ↔ Address resolution.
3. **Spatial Analysis** — Buffers, overlays, proximity, clustering (DBSCAN, K-Means).
4. **Pattern Mining** — Heatmaps, trajectory, dwell-time.
5. **Activation** — Push, geofenced ads, store attribution.

**2. Data Sources (2 Marks):**
- **GPS (3–10 m)** — Outdoor, used by Uber/Swiggy for nearest-driver/store.
- **Bluetooth Beacons (1–3 m)** — Mall retail — push coupon on entry.
- **Wi-Fi Fingerprinting (5–15 m)** — In-store navigation in airports.
- **Cell Tower (50–3000 m)** — Regional, used by telecom for Airtel Thanks offers.

**3. Examples (2 Marks):**
- **Zomato** uses GPS to push "20% off" notifications to users within 1 km of a partner restaurant.
- **Reliance Trends** uses beacons to push a coupon when customer enters the trial room.

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**1. Haversine Formula (3 Marks):**
$$d = 2r \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\phi_2 - \phi_1}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\lambda_2 - \lambda_1}{2}\right)}\right)$$

where $r = 6371$ km (Earth's mean radius), $\phi$ = latitude, $\lambda$ = longitude (radians).

**2. Worked Example (2 Marks):**
For Kochi center $(9.9312°\text{N}, 76.2673°\text{E})$ and a store at $(9.9700°\text{N}, 76.2900°\text{E})$:
- Convert degrees → radians.
- Apply formula in 8 steps (as detailed in Section 3.3 above).
- Result: $d \approx 4.84$ km.

**3. LBI Campaign Effectiveness Metrics (2 Marks):**
- **Geofence Entry Rate** = (Entries / Recipients) × 100.
- **Visit-to-Purchase Conversion** = (Purchases / Entries) × 100.
- **Walk-in Lift %** = (Test − Control) / Control × 100 — the **gold standard** for LBI ROI proof.
- **Dwell Time** = Exit time − Entry time — proxy for engagement quality.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing Geomarketing with Proximity Marketing** (lose 1 mark if swapped). Geomarketing = strategic regional planning; Proximity = real-time push at a point-of-interest.
> 2. **Writing `|x|` in a markdown table** → this **breaks the table** and the formula becomes unreadable. Always write `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$`.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in the Haversine formula → entire calculation wrong (full 7 marks at risk in Part B).
> 4. **Confusing ARPU with CLV** — ARPU is a *period* metric (₹/month), CLV is a *lifetime* discounted value (₹).
> 5. **Omitting the discount rate `d`** in the CLV formula — examiners specifically test for **present-value awareness**.
> 6. **Writing Python code** in a business analytics answer — for KTU BA papers, **no code is expected**; the analytical logic and formulas earn marks, not the code.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Telecom Analytics** = 4-layer maturity pyramid: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive.
- ✅ **Five Pillars:** Churn, CLV, Network, Fraud, Revenue Assurance.
- ✅ **ARPU** = Total Revenue / Average Subscribers (₹/month).
- ✅ **Churn Rate** = Customers Lost / Customers at Start × 100.
- ✅ **CLV (discrete)** uses retention probability $r$ and discount rate $d$.
- ✅ **CLV (constant margin)** = $M \cdot r / (1+d-r)$.
- ✅ **CLV:CAC ≥ 3:1** is the universal SaaS/telco rule of thumb.
- ✅ **Cox Proportional Hazards** models time-to-churn with covariates.
- ✅ **LBI Marketing** uses geospatial data (GPS, Wi-Fi, beacons, cell-tower).
- ✅ **Haversine formula** uses $r = 6371$ km; **always convert degrees → radians**.
- ✅ **Geofence Conversion Rate** and **Walk-in Lift %** are the primary LBI ROI metrics.
- ✅ **Proximity Marketing** is tactical/real-time; **Geomarketing** is strategic/regional.
- ✅ **Telecom ↔ LBI linkage:** telcos own the carrier-grade location infrastructure.
- ✅ **Examiner-favorite buzzwords to embed in answers:** *Next-Best-Offer, Survival Analysis, Geofencing, ARPU, CLV, Dwell Time, RFM, AUC-ROC*.
- ✅ **Avoid these confusions:** ARPU ≠ CLV; Geomarketing ≠ Proximity Marketing; Diagnostic ≠ Predictive; Dwell Time ≠ Conversion Rate.
- ✅ **Final golden rule:** Always show the **mathematical formula + business interpretation** in every numerical answer — KTU examiners reward *both* the computation and its *strategic meaning*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytics in Consumer-Packaged Goods (CPG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Consumer-Packaged Goods (CPG)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📦 Analytics in Consumer-Packaged Goods (CPG)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> **Consumer-Packaged Goods (CPG) Analytics** is the systematic application of descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytical techniques to data generated across the value chain of fast-moving, high-volume, low-unit-cost goods (e.g., food, beverages, toiletries, household products). It integrates **point-of-sale (POS) data, syndicated retail panel data, loyalty data, supply-chain data, and digital/social signals** to optimize revenue growth management (RGM), trade promotion, assortment planning, demand forecasting, and customer/shopper segmentation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CPG analytics sits at the intersection of **Marketing Analytics**, **Supply Chain Analytics**, and **Retail Analytics**, forming a *commercial excellence* function that drives decisions for **manufacturers (FMCG companies)**, **retailers**, and **category management teams**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Café Chai Tapri" Analogy ☕

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you run a small **chai (tea) stall** outside a college. You sell:
> - **Packaged CPG items:** biscuits, bottled water, chips, toothpaste.
> - **Fast-moving goods:** customers buy them every day, low cost per unit, high frequency.

You notice that:
1. Every **Monday morning**, sales of biscuits spike (students returning from hostels).
2. When it rains, **bottled water** sales rise 30% — but you ran out last week!
3. A **new "Maggi" combo pack** placed at eye-level sold 3× faster than the older shelf position.
4. Your **loyalty card** customers (regulars) buy 4× more than walk-ins.

This is **CPG Analytics in action** — even at a small stall. In a real CPG company (e.g., HUL, Nestlé, ITC, P&G), the same decisions are made at **petabyte scale** using advanced analytics:
- Demand sensing (predicting the rain-driven water surge),
- Assortment & shelf optimization (placing Maggi at eye-level),
- Trade promotion effectiveness (the "combo offer"),
- Customer Lifetime Value (the loyal regulars).

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU Cares About CPG Analytics:** CPG is one of the **largest employers** of business analytics graduates in India (FMCG giants + retail chains like DMart, Reliance Retail, BigBasket). KTU frequently tests this topic because it combines **marketing + supply chain + finance** in a single industry context.

## 1.3 Scope of the CPG Industry

| Sub-Sector | Examples | Key Analytics Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Food & Beverages** | Biscuits, soft drinks, dairy | Demand forecasting, cold-chain analytics |
| **Personal Care** | Shampoo, soap, cosmetics | New product launch, sensory analytics |
| **Home Care** | Detergents, cleaners | Trade promotion ROI, refill rate prediction |
| **Tobacco & Cigarettes** | Cigarettes, matches | Shrinkage analytics, regulatory compliance |
| **OTC Pharma** | Antacids, cough syrups | Self-medication trend mining |

## 1.4 The CPG Data Ecosystem — 5 Core Data Sources

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight — Memorize These 5 Data Sources**
> 1. **POS / Store-level Data** — sales by SKU, store, day, hour.
> 2. **Syndicated Panel Data** — Nielsen, IRI, Kantar Worldpanel (household purchase behavior).
> 3. **Loyalty / First-Party Data** — registered customers, baskets, RFM.
> 4. **Supply Chain / ERP Data** — inventory, distributor sell-in, on-shelf availability.
> 5. **Digital & Social Signals** — reviews, e-commerce clicks, influencer chatter.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📊 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPG Analytics Value Chain — Structured Logic Flow

CPG analytics operates across **5 functional pillars**. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know the *function → data → technique → outcome* mapping for each.

### Pillar 1: Demand Forecasting & Sensing
- **Why it matters:** CPG = *high SKU count × low margin × high volatility*. A 1% forecast error at a ₹10,000 Cr company = ₹100 Cr working-capital loss.
- **How it works:**
  1. Capture daily POS from retailers.
  2. Layer in exogenous variables (weather, festivals, school calendar, cricket matches).
  3. Run **time-series models** (ARIMA, Prophet) for baseline demand.
  4. Apply **machine-learning models** (XGBoost, LSTM) for non-linear drivers.
  5. Output → SKU-store-day forecast with confidence intervals.
- **Outcome:** Optimized production, reduced stockouts, leaner inventory.

### Pillar 2: Revenue Growth Management (RGM)
RGM = **Price + Promo + Mix + Pack architecture**. The "4 Ps of revenue."
- **Price Elasticity Modeling:** How demand changes when price changes.
- **Promotional Lift:** Incremental volume from a discount vs. baseline.
- **Pack Architecture:** Deciding whether to launch a 50g, 100g, 200g, or family pack.
- **Mix Analytics:** Margin contribution of each SKU in a category.

### Pillar 3: Trade Promotion Analytics
CPG companies spend **15–25% of gross revenue** on trade promotions (schemes to retailers). Half of this is wasted. Analytics quantifies *true incremental ROI*.
- **Baseline vs. Lift** decomposition.
- **Pre vs. Post promotional dip** (consumer pantry-loading).
- **Forward-looking vs. backward-looking** evaluation.

### Pillar 4: Category Management & Assortment
- **Category Captaincy** — the manufacturer becomes the "trusted advisor" to the retailer.
- **Planogram Optimization** — which product on which shelf at what height.
- **Assortment Rationalization** — drop the bottom 10% SKUs that contribute < 2% revenue.

### Pillar 5: Shopper & Customer Analytics
- **Shopper segmentation** (mission: fill-up vs. top-up vs. treat).
- **Basket analysis** (Market Basket — which products are bought together).
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).**
- **Churn / Switching analytics** between brands.

## 2.2 Key CPG Metrics & Formulas (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Must Memorize**

### Table 2.A: Core CPG Metrics

| # | Metric | Formula | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\% \Delta Q}{\% \Delta P} = \dfrac{(Q_2 - Q_1)/(Q_2 + Q_1)}{(P_2 - P_1)/(P_2 + P_1)}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Most tested. Negative value. |
| 2 | **Promotional Lift (PL)** | $PL = \dfrac{V_{promo} - V_{base}}{V_{base}} \times 100$ | Tests trade promotion effectiveness. |
| 3 | **Incremental Volume** | $IV = V_{promo} - V_{base} \times (1 - Halo - Cannibalization)$ | Separates true lift from substitution. |
| 4 | **Return on Trade Investment (ROTI)** | $ROTI = \dfrac{\text{Net Incremental Margin}}{\text{Total Trade Spend}}$ | CPG profitability lens. |
| 5 | **Category Development Index (CDI)** | $CDI = \dfrac{\% \text{ Category Sales in Region}}{\% \text{ Population in Region}} \times 100$ | Geographic potential. |
| 6 | **Brand Development Index (BDI)** | $BDI = \dfrac{\% \text{ Brand Sales in Region}}{\% \text{ Category Sales in Region}} \times 100$ | Brand strength. |
| 7 | **RFM Score** | $RFM = R \times W_R + F \times W_F + M \times W_M$ | Customer segmentation. |
| 8 | **On-Shelf Availability (OSA)** | $OSA = \dfrac{\text{Stores with product available}}{\text{Total stores audited}} \times 100$ | Supply chain KPI. |
| 9 | **Market Basket — Support** | $\text{Support}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Baskets containing A and B}}{\text{Total baskets}}$ | Association rule mining. |
| 10 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \times r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | Loyalty program ROI. |

### Table 2.B: Demand Forecasting Models — KTU Comparison

| Model Type | Best For | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Moving Average** | Stable demand | Simple | Lags trends |
| **Exponential Smoothing (ETS)** | Trend + seasonality | Robust | No causal drivers |
| **ARIMA / SARIMA** | Stationary series with seasonality | Strong statistical base | Needs data prep |
| **Prophet (Facebook)** | Festival-driven CPG | Handles Indian holidays out-of-box | Less granular |
| **XGBoost / Random Forest** | Multi-driver forecasting | Captures non-linearity | Black-box |
| **LSTM / Deep Learning** | Long-sequence POS | High accuracy | Data-hungry |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Skills Land You**
> - **FMCG Giants:** HUL, ITC, Nestlé, P&G, Dabur, Marico, Britannia.
> - **Retail & E-commerce:** DMart, Reliance Retail, BigBasket, Blinkit, Zepto.
> - **Analytics Vendors:** NielsenIQ, Kantar, IRI, Affine, Tiger Analytics, Fractal.
> - **Job Roles:** Category Manager, Trade Marketing Analyst, RGM Lead, Demand Planner, Insights Manager.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!WARNING]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate Active** — Every algebraic step is shown in full. No skipping. No "therefore follows." Each step is followed by an English explanation of *why* that step happens.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) for a CPG SKU

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Style Problem — 7 Mark Standard**

**Problem Statement:**
A leading CPG company in Kerala sells **"Premium Tea 500g"** packs. Before a price change, the unit price was **₹200** and weekly sales were **5,000 units**. The company raised the price to **₹220**, and weekly sales dropped to **4,400 units**. Compute the **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)** using the **arc elasticity (mid-point) formula** and interpret the result for the CPG manager.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Variables

We list the two price-quantity observations:

$$
P_1 = 200 \quad , \quad Q_1 = 5{,}000
$$

$$
P_2 = 220 \quad , \quad Q_2 = 4{,}400
$$

**Explanation:** $P_1, Q_1$ represent the *pre-promotion* or *original* state. $P_2, Q_2$ represent the *post-change* state. KTU expects both points to be clearly stated.

### Step 2 — Recall the Arc Elasticity Formula

For pedagogical clarity (and KTU's preference), we use the **mid-point (arc) elasticity** formula rather than point elasticity. This avoids the bias of choosing a base point:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{(Q_2 + Q_1)/2}}{\dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{(P_2 + P_1)/2}}
$$

**Explanation:** The numerator is the *percentage change in quantity* expressed as a fraction of the *average* quantity. The denominator is the *percentage change in price* expressed as a fraction of the *average* price. This symmetric form is the KTU standard.

### Step 3 — Compute the Numerator (Change in Quantity over Average Quantity)

$$
\Delta Q = Q_2 - Q_1 = 4{,}400 - 5{,}000 = -600
$$

$$
\bar{Q} = \dfrac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2} = \dfrac{5{,}000 + 4{,}400}{2} = 4{,}700
$$

$$
\% \Delta Q = \dfrac{-600}{4{,}700} = -0.12766
$$

**Explanation:** Quantity *fell* by 600 units. Average quantity is 4,700. The percentage drop is approximately −12.77%. The negative sign indicates the *direction* of change, not a problem.

### Step 4 — Compute the Denominator (Change in Price over Average Price)

$$
\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = 220 - 200 = 20
$$

$$
\bar{P} = \dfrac{P_1 + P_2}{2} = \dfrac{200 + 220}{2} = 210
$$

$$
\% \Delta P = \dfrac{20}{210} = 0.09524
$$

**Explanation:** Price *rose* by ₹20. Average price is ₹210. The percentage rise is approximately +9.52%.

### Step 5 — Divide to Get Elasticity

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{-0.12766}{0.09524} = -1.3403
$$

**Explanation:** A 1% increase in price led to a 1.34% decrease in quantity demanded. The negative sign confirms the **law of demand** (price ↑, quantity ↓).

### Step 6 — Take the Absolute Value & Interpret (KTU Critical Step)

$$
\vert \varepsilon \vert = 1.34
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Interpretation Rule**
> - $\vert \varepsilon \vert > 1$ → **Elastic** → consumers are *sensitive* to price. **Avoid price hikes**; use pack-size/format innovation instead.
> - $\vert \varepsilon \vert = 1$ → **Unit elastic** → revenue unchanged.
> - $\vert \varepsilon \vert < 1$ → **Inelastic** → safe to raise price for revenue gain.

**Managerial Recommendation for KTU answer:**
Since $|\varepsilon| = 1.34 > 1$, the **Premium Tea 500g** category is **price-elastic**. The 10% price hike caused a 12.77% volume loss, which is **revenue-destroying**. The CPG manager should **revert the price**, explore **pack-size changes** (e.g., launch 400g at ₹180), or invest the saved margin in **trade promotion** rather than a permanent price increase.

### Step 7 — Revenue Sanity Check

$$
\text{Revenue}_1 = 200 \times 5{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \ \text{(₹10 lakh)}
$$

$$
\text{Revenue}_2 = 220 \times 4{,}400 = 9{,}68{,}000 \ \text{(₹9.68 lakh)}
$$

**Explanation:** Despite the higher unit price, **total revenue fell by ₹32,000 (−3.2%)**, confirming the elasticity verdict.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Market Basket Analysis (Apriori Logic)

**Problem Statement:**
A small kirana store in Kochi recorded the following **5 transactions** in an hour. Apply the **Apriori algorithm** logic to find product association rules with **minimum support = 40%** and **minimum confidence = 60%**.

| Txn ID | Items Bought |
|---|---|
| T1 | {Bread, Butter, Jam} |
| T2 | {Bread, Butter} |
| T3 | {Butter, Jam, Milk} |
| T4 | {Bread, Jam, Milk} |
| T5 | {Bread, Butter, Jam, Milk} |

### Step 1 — Compute Support of All 1-Itemsets

$$
\text{Support}(X) = \dfrac{\text{Txns containing X}}{5}
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\text{Bread}) = 4/5 = 80\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\text{Butter}) = 4/5 = 80\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\text{Jam}) = 4/5 = 80\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\text{Milk}) = 3/5 = 60\%
$$

**Explanation:** All 1-itemsets pass the 40% support threshold. KTU note: only *frequent* itemsets progress to the next level.

### Step 2 — Generate Candidate 2-Itemsets and Compute Support

$$
\text{Support}(\{B,Bu\}) = 3/5 = 60\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{B,J\}) = 3/5 = 60\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{B,M\}) = 2/5 = 40\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{Bu,J\}) = 3/5 = 60\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{Bu,M\}) = 2/5 = 40\%
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{J,M\}) = 3/5 = 60\%
$$

**Explanation:** All 2-itemsets pass. (B = Bread, Bu = Butter, J = Jam, M = Milk.)

### Step 3 — Generate Candidate 3-Itemsets (Apriori Pruning)

$$
\text{Support}(\{B,Bu,J\}) = 2/5 = 40\% \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{B,Bu,M\}) = 1/5 = 20\% \quad \times \quad (\text{Pruned})
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{B,J,M\}) = 2/5 = 40\% \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Support}(\{Bu,J,M\}) = 2/5 = 40\% \quad \checkmark
$$

**Explanation:** Apriori prunes any 3-itemset if any of its 2-item subsets fails. $\{B, Bu, M\}$ has $\{B,M\}$ subset, which has 40% (passes), so Apriori would not auto-prune — but its *own* support (20%) is below threshold, so it is removed.

### Step 4 — Generate Association Rules & Compute Confidence

For each frequent itemset, generate rules $X \Rightarrow Y$ and compute:

$$
\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X)}
$$

$$
\text{Conf}(\text{Bread,Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Jam}) = \dfrac{2/5}{3/5} = 66.67\%
$$

$$
\text{Conf}(\text{Bread,Jam} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) = \dfrac{2/5}{3/5} = 66.67\%
$$

$$
\text{Conf}(\text{Butter,Jam} \Rightarrow \text{Milk}) = \dfrac{2/5}{3/5} = 66.67\%
$$

**Explanation:** All three rules exceed the 60% confidence threshold.

### Step 5 — Compute Lift to Measure Strength Beyond Random

$$
\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \times \text{Support}(Y)}
$$

$$
\text{Lift}(\{B,Bu\} \Rightarrow J) = \dfrac{0.40}{0.60 \times 0.80} = 0.833
$$

**Explanation:** Lift < 1 → the rule is *weaker* than random chance. KTU warning: don't blindly trust high-confidence rules. **Lift > 1** is the gold standard for actionable rules.

### Step 6 — Managerial Insight (KTU Demand)

> [!TIP]
> **CPG Actionable Output:** Place Bread and Butter at opposite ends of the aisle to **force shopper traversal** past Jam and impulse items. The rule $\{B, Bu\} \Rightarrow J$ confirms **cross-sell potential**, but a better rule would require Lift > 1.5 for store-actionable confidence.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) Simplified

**Problem Statement:**
A D2C CPG startup (homemade pickle brand) acquires customers at **₹300 CAC**. Each customer places **3 orders/year**, average order value = **₹600**, gross margin = **40%**. Average customer lifespan = **4 years**, discount rate = **10%**. Compute the **CLV** and decide if CAC is justified.

### Step 1 — Compute Annual Gross Margin per Customer

$$
\text{Annual Margin} = 3 \times 600 \times 0.40 = 720
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Discounted CLV Formula

$$
CLV = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{M_t}{(1+d)^t} = \sum_{t=1}^{4} \dfrac{720}{(1.10)^t}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Geometric Series

$$
CLV = 720 \times \left[\dfrac{1}{1.10} + \dfrac{1}{1.21} + \dfrac{1}{1.331} + \dfrac{1}{1.4641}\right]
$$

$$
CLV = 720 \times [0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830] = 720 \times 3.1698
$$

$$
\boxed{CLV = 2{,}282.3 \ \text{rupees}}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision

$$
CLV : CAC = 2{,}282 : 300 = 7.6 : 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** A healthy SaaS/CPG business has a **CLV:CAC ratio > 3:1**. Here 7.6:1 → the startup is **under-investing in customer acquisition** and should **scale marketing spend aggressively** to capture more customers at this profitable unit economics.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which CPG Analytics Technique

| Business Question | Recommended Technique | Data Source | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| How much to produce next month? | SARIMA / Prophet | POS + Calendar | SKU-Store-Day forecast |
| Will sales rise if we hike price? | Price Elasticity Model | POS + Price log | $\varepsilon$ per SKU |
| Is our trade promo profitable? | Promotional ROI / Lift | POS + Promo calendar | Net Incremental Margin |
| Which products to bundle? | Market Basket / Apriori | Transactional | Association rules |
| Where to launch next store? | CDI vs BDI analysis | Geo + Census | Geographic heatmap |
| Who are the high-value shoppers? | RFM Segmentation | Loyalty | Clustered personas |
| Will a customer churn? | Logistic / XGBoost | Loyalty + Behavior | Churn probability score |
| Which shelf layout is best? | Planogram Optimization | Store audits + POS | Optimal planogram |

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CPG Analytics Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[POS Store Data] --> pipeA[Data Ingestion Layer]
    srcB[Syndicated Panel Data Nielsen Kantar] --> pipeA
    srcC[Loyalty and First Party Data] --> pipeA
    srcD[ERP and Supply Chain Data] --> pipeA
    srcE[Digital and Social Signals] --> pipeA
    pipeA --> lake[Unified Data Lake on Cloud]
    lake --> martA[Sales Mart]
    lake --> martB[Customer Mart]
    lake --> martC[Supply Mart]
    martA --> mdl1[Demand Forecasting Models]
    martA --> mdl2[Price Elasticity Engine]
    martA --> mdl3[Promotional ROI Models]
    martB --> mdl4[RFM and CLV Models]
    martB --> mdl5[Churn Prediction Models]
    martC --> mdl6[Inventory and OSA Optimizer]
    mdl1 --> dash[Executive Decision Dashboard]
    mdl2 --> dash
    mdl3 --> dash
    mdl4 --> dash
    mdl5 --> dash
    mdl6 --> dash
    dash --> act1[Category and Trade Marketing]
    dash --> act2[Revenue Growth Management]
    dash --> act3[Supply Chain Planning]
    dash --> act4[Personalized Marketing]
```

**Explanation:** The architecture follows the standard **Ingestion → Lake → Mart → Model → Dashboard → Action** pipeline used by CPG companies. Each *mart* serves a specific business function. KTU students should note that *data engineering* (left side) is as important as *modeling* (middle).

## 4.2 The RGM (Revenue Growth Management) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[CPG Revenue Decline Diagnosed] --> q1{Is Volume Down?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is Distribution Down?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Is Realized Price Down?}
    q2 -- Yes --> fix1[Fix Distribution and OSA]
    q2 -- No --> q4{Is Promotion Ineffective?}
    q4 -- Yes --> fix2[Redesign Trade Promo Plan]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Is Mix Shifting to Low Value Packs?}
    q5 -- Yes --> fix3[Pack Architecture Innovation]
    q5 -- No --> fix4[Demand Sensing and Forecasting Fix]
    q3 -- Yes --> q6{Is Elasticity Favorable?}
    q6 -- Yes --> fix5[Strategic Price Hike]
    q6 -- No --> fix7[Hold Price Invest in Value Adds]
    q3 -- No --> fix6[Mix and Pack Optimization]
```

**Explanation:** This decision tree mirrors the **RGM diagnostic playbook** taught at leading B-schools and used by McKinsey/BCG in CPG engagements. KTU often frames a 7-mark question around this flow.

## 4.3 The Customer Segmentation — RFM Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cus[All CPG Customers] --> rfm[Compute R F M Scores]
    rfm --> seg1[Champions - High R High F High M]
    rfm --> seg2[Loyal Customers - High F High M]
    rfm --> seg3[At Risk - Low R High F High M]
    rfm --> seg4[New Customers - High R Low F]
    rfm --> seg5[Hibernating - Low R Low F Low M]
    seg1 --> act1[Reward and Retain with Premium Loyalty]
    seg2 --> act2[Upsell and Cross Sell]
    seg3 --> act3[Win Back Campaigns]
    seg4 --> act4[Onboarding and Activation]
    seg5 --> act5[Drip Reactivation or Suppress]
```

**Explanation:** RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) is a *foundational* KTU topic. Each segment maps to a distinct marketing action — this is the heart of **personalization at scale**.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Define CPG analytics. List any THREE data sources used in CPG analytics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** CPG Analytics is the application of statistical, predictive, and prescriptive analytical techniques on data generated across the consumer-packaged goods value chain (manufacturing → distribution → retail → consumption) to optimize decisions on demand forecasting, pricing, trade promotion, assortment, and customer engagement.
>
> **Three Data Sources (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **POS / Store-level Data** — daily transactional data captured at checkout.
> 2. **Syndicated Panel Data** — household purchase data from agencies like Nielsen, Kantar.
> 3. **Loyalty / First-party Data** — registered customer transactions and demographics.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)? Why is it more relevant than one-time sales for CPG firms?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** CLV is the **discounted total future net margin** a customer is expected to generate over their entire relationship with a brand. Formula: $CLV = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{M_t}{(1+d)^t}$
>
> **Relevance (1.5 marks):** CPG products are **repeat-purchase / habitual categories** (soap, shampoo, snacks). A customer retained for 5 years is worth **5× more** than a one-time buyer. CLV justifies **loyalty programs, acquisition spend (CAC), and retention budgets**, which are critical because CPG gross margins (15–40%) are too thin to survive without repeat purchase.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **5 functional pillars of CPG analytics** with one industry example for each pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A detergent brand launches a **"Buy 1 kg, get 1 kg free"** trade promotion. Baseline weekly sales (without promotion) average **8,000 units**. During the promotion, sales spike to **22,000 units**. However, the **post-promotion week** sales collapse to **5,000 units** (vs. baseline 8,000). The unit margin is **₹40** and the cost of the free unit is **₹35**.
**Compute:**
- (i) Promotional Lift.
- (ii) Incremental Volume (assuming 20% of the lift is halo/cannibalization, 10% is forward-buying).
- (iii) Net Incremental Margin.
- (iv) ROTI (Return on Trade Investment), assuming total trade spend = ₹7,00,000.
**Interpret whether the promotion was profitable.** **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer Structure: 5 Pillars × (1.4 marks each)**

**1. Demand Forecasting & Sensing (1.4 marks):** Uses historical POS + exogenous data (weather, festivals) to predict SKU-store-day demand. *Example:* Hindustan Unilever (HUL) uses machine-learning demand sensing to forecast Lifebuoy soap demand in monsoon-affected regions, reducing stockouts by ~15%.

**2. Revenue Growth Management – RGM (1.4 marks):** Combines price, promotion, mix, and pack decisions to maximize sustainable revenue. *Example:* Nestlé India uses RGM to determine when to launch a 90g Maggi pack (price-elastic segment) vs. a 4-pack family pack (convenience segment).

**3. Trade Promotion Analytics (1.4 marks):** Quantifies true incremental ROI of retailer-facing schemes. *Example:* ITC's trade marketing team uses post-event analysis to identify that 40% of "Buy 2 Get 1" offers drive no incremental volume — only forward-buying.

**4. Category Management & Assortment (1.4 marks):** Decides shelf layout, planogram, and SKU rationalization. *Example:* BigBasket uses algorithmic planograms to place impulse items (chips, cold drinks) at checkout based on shopper mission data.

**5. Shopper & Customer Analytics (1.4 marks):** RFM, CLV, churn, basket analysis. *Example:* Mamaearth uses loyalty data and RFM to target "Loyal Champions" with premium D2C bundles, lifting repeat-purchase rate by 22%.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Full Numerical Working)

**Given:**

$$
V_{base} = 8{,}000, \quad V_{promo} = 22{,}000, \quad V_{post} = 5{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Margin} = ₹40, \quad \text{Free unit cost} = ₹35, \quad \text{Trade Spend} = ₹7{,}00{,}000
$$

**(i) Promotional Lift:**

$$
PL = \dfrac{V_{promo} - V_{base}}{V_{base}} \times 100 = \dfrac{22{,}000 - 8{,}000}{8{,}000} \times 100 = \dfrac{14{,}000}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 175\%
$$

> *Sales rose 175% during the promotion.*

**(ii) Incremental Volume (Net of Halo, Cannibalization & Forward-Buying):**

First compute the *raw* incremental volume:

$$
IV_{raw} = V_{promo} - V_{base} = 14{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

Halo/cannibalization adjustment (20%) means 20% of this lift was due to **shift from other brands/SKUs within the company** — not new consumption:

$$
IV_{after\_halo} = 14{,}000 \times (1 - 0.20) = 11{,}200
$$

**Post-promotion dip correction (forward-buying) (10%):**
The post-promotion drop of 3,000 units (8,000 → 5,000) is consumers **stockpiling** during promo, then *not buying* later. This 3,000-unit dip is *borrowed* future volume. We must net it:

$$
IV_{net} = 11{,}200 - 3{,}000 = 8{,}200 \text{ units}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to deduct the **post-promotion dip** (forward-buying). Always use the post-promo data to correct for pantry-loading.

**(iii) Net Incremental Margin:**

Each incremental unit yields the unit margin (₹40) but incurs the free-unit cost (₹35), so net margin per incremental unit:

$$
\text{Net Margin/unit} = 40 - 35 = ₹5
$$

$$
\text{Net Incremental Margin} = 8{,}200 \times 5 = ₹41{,}000
$$

> Note: This is the **net contribution** from the promotion itself.

**(iv) ROTI Calculation:**

If we consider only the promotion-period trade spend of ₹7,00,000 against the net incremental margin of ₹41,000:

$$
ROTI = \dfrac{41{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} = 0.0586 = 5.86\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> *Students commonly make these mistakes:*
> - **Forgetting to subtract free-unit cost (₹35)** — they treat margin as full ₹40. (-1 mark)
> - **Not adjusting for the post-promo dip** — they take raw 14,000 as incremental. (-1 mark)
> - **Forgetting to subtract 20% halo/cannibalization.** (-1 mark)
> - **Misinterpreting ROTI** — A ROTI < 100% is a *failed promotion* even if the lift % looks high.
> - **Concluding "promotion was good because lift = 175%"** — KTU specifically tests whether you understand *gross lift ≠ true value*.

**Managerial Verdict:**
> The promotion generated a **gross lift of 175%** but only **8,200 truly incremental units** after adjustments. The net incremental margin (₹41,000) is **far below the trade spend (₹7,00,000)**. **ROTI = 5.86%** — the promotion is **financially unprofitable** and should be redesigned (e.g., reduce discount depth, target high-value loyalty customers only, shorten duration to prevent pantry-loading).

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Price Elasticity of Demand** concept in CPG. How is arc elasticity different from point elasticity? A beverage SKU at **₹30** sells **10,000 units/week**. The price is raised to **₹35** and sales fall to **8,000 units/week**.
**Compute the arc elasticity and advise the CPG manager.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **RFM model** for CPG customer segmentation. Construct an **RFM matrix with a worked example** of 5 customers and explain the action plan for the "**Champions**" and "**At Risk**" segments. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) measures the **responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price**. In CPG, it is foundational to **pricing strategy, pack architecture, and promotional depth decisions**.

**Arc vs. Point Elasticity (1 mark):**
- **Point elasticity** uses a *single base point* and is sensitive to the direction of change (asymmetric).
- **Arc elasticity** uses the *mid-point* (average) of the two price-quantity pairs, giving a *symmetric* result preferred by KTU boards.

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

Given: $P_1 = 30, Q_1 = 10{,}000; \quad P_2 = 35, Q_2 = 8{,}000$.

$$
\Delta Q = 8{,}000 - 10{,}000 = -2{,}000
$$

$$
\bar{Q} = (10{,}000 + 8{,}000)/2 = 9{,}000
$$

$$
\Delta P = 35 - 30 = 5
$$

$$
\bar{P} = (30 + 35)/2 = 32.5
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{-2{,}000/9{,}000}{5/32.5} = \dfrac{-0.2222}{0.1538} = -1.444
$$

$$
\vert \varepsilon \vert = 1.44 > 1 \Rightarrow \textbf{Elastic demand}
$$

**Managerial Advice (closing the answer):** A 1% price hike caused a 1.44% drop in volume. Revenue: $30 \times 10{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000$ vs. $35 \times 8{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000$ — **revenue fell by ₹20,000**. The manager should **revert the price** or **introduce a smaller pack (e.g., 200ml at ₹18)** to serve price-sensitive consumers without cannibalizing the 1L SKU.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**RFM Concept (2 marks):**
**R**ecency (how recent the last purchase), **F**requency (how often they buy), **M**onetary (how much they spend). Each customer is scored 1–5 on each dimension; the 3-digit RFM score drives segmentation.

**Worked Example (3 marks):**

| Customer | Last Purchase (Days Ago) | Orders/Year | Annual Spend (₹) | R-Score | F-Score | M-Score | RFM Cell |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 18 | 12,000 | 5 | 5 | 5 | **Champions** |
| B | 25 | 12 | 8,000 | 4 | 4 | 4 | **Loyal** |
| C | 10 | 4 | 6,000 | 5 | 2 | 3 | **New Customers** |
| D | 90 | 14 | 9,500 | 2 | 4 | 4 | **At Risk** |
| E | 180 | 2 | 1,500 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Hibernating** |

**Action Plans (2 marks):**

> [!TIP]
> **Champions (Customer A) — Action Plan:**
> - Enroll in premium **tier-1 loyalty program** (e.g., free same-day delivery, early access to new SKUs).
> - Offer **referral incentives** — they are the best brand advocates.
> - **Do NOT** discount — they buy regardless. Discounting destroys margin.

> **At Risk (Customer D) — Action Plan:**
> - Trigger a **win-back campaign**: personalized "We miss you" email with a 20% coupon valid for 14 days.
> - Survey them for **churn reasons** (price, stockout, competitor switch).
> - **Limit** aggressive spend — they may not return; cap retention budget at projected CLV recovery.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 4: CPG Analytics — Key Points to Remember**

- **CPG = Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG)** — high volume, low unit cost, frequent repeat purchase.
- **5 Data Sources** — POS, Syndicated Panel, Loyalty, ERP/Supply Chain, Digital/Social.
- **5 Functional Pillars** — Demand Forecasting, RGM, Trade Promotion, Category Management, Shopper Analytics.
- **Arc Elasticity formula** is the KTU-preferred elasticity form (use mid-point, not base-point).
- **$\vert \varepsilon \vert > 1$** → Elastic (avoid price hikes); **$\vert \varepsilon \vert < 1$** → Inelastic (safe to raise price).
- **Trade Promotion ROI** must net out: free-unit cost, halo/cannibalization, and **post-promotion dip (forward-buying)**. Gross lift % is **not enough**.
- **Apriori Algorithm** uses **Support, Confidence, Lift** — only **Lift > 1** indicates truly actionable rules.
- **CLV = discounted sum of future margins**; healthy **CLV : CAC ratio > 3 : 1**.
- **RFM = Recency × Frequency × Monetary** — Champions (5-5-5) get *retention*, At Risk (low R, high F/M) get *win-back*.
- **CDI vs BDI** — Category Development Index measures *category potential* in a region; Brand Development Index measures *brand strength* in that region.
- **Key Indian Context:** Nielsen, Kantar Worldpanel, and IRI are the dominant syndicated data providers. FMCG majors (HUL, ITC, Nestlé, Dabur, Marico, Britannia) are the primary employers of CPG analysts.
- **Common KTU Traps:** Forgetting free-unit cost, ignoring post-promo dip, misinterpreting Lift < 1, treating gross lift as profit, not applying arc elasticity formula correctly.

topics.title: Analytics in Utilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Utilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Utility Analytics** is the systematic application of statistical, mathematical, and computational techniques (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics) to data generated by utility service providers — **electricity, water, gas, and waste management** — to optimize operations, improve customer experience, enhance asset performance, and ensure regulatory compliance.

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics context, the utility sector represents a **data-rich, infrastructure-heavy** industry where every kilowatt-hour, gallon, or cubic meter can be measured, transmitted, and analyzed in near real-time. The sector is broadly classified into four verticals:

| Vertical | Core Product | Key Data Sources |
|----------|--------------|------------------|
| **Electric Power** | Electricity (kWh) | Smart meters, SCADA, AMI, PMUs |
| **Water & Wastewater** | Potable water (m³) | Flow sensors, SCADA, AMR |
| **Gas Distribution** | Natural gas (therms) | Smart gas meters, pressure sensors |
| **Renewable / Solar** | Clean energy (MWh) | Inverter telemetry, weather APIs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The emphasis is on understanding *how* analytics transforms a traditionally low-tech, public-good sector into a **smart, demand-responsive, customer-centric** ecosystem using IoT, Big Data, AI/ML, and cloud computing.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **🍎 Real-World Analogy — "The Doctor for a Power Grid"**

Imagine a city's power grid is a **human body**:
- **Smart meters** are like **pulse-rate sensors** attached to every house (customer).
- **SCADA systems** are like the **central nervous system**, carrying signals from every transformer and substation.
- **Analytics** is the **cardiologist** who reads these signals, predicts a heart attack (blackout) before it happens, prescribes a bypass (load shifting), and continuously monitors recovery.

Without analytics, the utility is a doctor using only a stethoscope from 1980. With analytics, it gets a **real-time MRI, ECG, and AI-assisted diagnosis** — making the difference between a power cut and a preemptive reroute.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Utilities Need Analytics (Stat-of-the-day):** Globally, utility companies lose approximately **$96 billion annually** to non-technical losses (theft, leaks, billing errors, unmetered consumption). Analytics-driven revenue protection programs can recover **20–40%** of these losses — making analytics not just a luxury, but a survival tool.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & KPIs in Utility Analytics

The following **bolded** metrics form the **core measurement vocabulary** of any utility analytics discussion:

- **SAIDI** (System Average Interruption Duration Index) — measured in **minutes/customer/year**.
- **SAIFI** (System Average Interruption Frequency Index) — measured in **interruptions/customer/year**.
- **CAIDI** (Customer Average Interruption Duration Index) — minutes/interruption.
- **AT&C Losses** (Aggregate Technical & Commercial losses) — **% of input energy**.
- **Peak Demand** — **MW (Megawatts)**.
- **Load Factor** — **dimensionless ratio (0 to 1)**.
- **Per Capita Consumption** — **kWh/person/year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** A world-class utility operates at **SAIDI < 60 min/yr** and **AT&C losses < 6%**. Indian state DISCOMs average **SAIDI ~ 12 hours/yr** and **AT&C losses ~ 22%** — leaving massive analytics-driven improvement potential.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Utility Analytics

Utility analytics is structured along the **Gartner Analytics Maturity Model**, which KTU examiners frequently test. Each level answers a specific business question:

| Level | Analytics Type | Core Question Answered | Utility Example |
|-------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **L1** | **Descriptive** | *"What happened?"* | Monthly energy consumption dashboards |
| **L2** | **Diagnostic** | *"Why did it happen?"* | Root-cause analysis of a transformer failure |
| **L3** | **Predictive** | *"What will happen?"* | 24-hour ahead load forecasting |
| **L4** | **Prescriptive** | *"What should we do?"* | Optimal DG dispatch to minimize ₹/MWh |

---

### 2.2 Key Application Areas (KTU High-Yield)

#### A. Smart Meter Analytics (AMI — Advanced Metering Infrastructure)
- **What:** Each customer is fitted with a smart meter that transmits consumption data every **15 minutes** (some even every **1 minute**).
- **Why:** Enables **time-of-use (ToU) billing**, **demand response**, and **theft detection**.
- **How:** Clustering algorithms (k-means) identify consumption profiles (e.g., residential vs. industrial).

#### B. Load & Demand Forecasting
- **What:** Predicting future electricity/water demand.
- **Time horizons:** **Short-term (hour-ahead to week-ahead)**, **Medium-term (month to 1 year)**, **Long-term (5–20 years)** for capacity planning.
- **Techniques:** ARIMA, LSTM neural networks, regression with weather variables.

#### C. Outage & Asset Management
- **What:** Predictive maintenance of transformers, pumps, and pipelines.
- **Why:** A single transformer failure can cause **lakhs of ₹ in losses** plus cascading outages.
- **Technique:** Survival analysis, anomaly detection on vibration/temperature sensors.

#### D. Revenue Protection & Theft Analytics
- **What:** Detecting non-technical losses (illegal connections, meter tampering, billing fraud).
- **Technique:** Energy balance analysis — comparing input energy at feeder to sum of billed consumption.

#### E. Customer Segmentation & Churn
- **What:** Identifying high-value vs. low-engagement customers.
- **Technique:** RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) analysis adapted for utilities.

#### F. Renewable Integration & Grid Balancing
- **What:** Managing intermittent solar/wind generation.
- **Technique:** Stochastic optimization for storage dispatch.

---

### 2.3 The Operational Logic Flow

1. **Data Acquisition** → Smart meters, IoT sensors, weather feeds, billing systems.
2. **Data Ingestion** → Real-time streaming (Apache Kafka) + batch ETL.
3. **Data Storage** → Time-series databases (InfluxDB), data lakes (AWS S3).
4. **Analytics Engine** → ML models running descriptive → predictive → prescriptive pipelines.
5. **Visualization & Action** → Operator dashboards, automated control signals to SCADA.
6. **Feedback Loop** → Continuous model retraining with new data.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the exact formulas examiners expect in a 14-mark derivation. Memorize these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definition | Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Load Factor** | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | Smoother = more efficient grid | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Diversity Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Coincident Max Demand}}$ | Always $\geq 1$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | **SAIDI** | $SAIDI = \dfrac{\sum U_i \cdot N_i}{N_{total}}$ | $U_i$ = outage duration; $N_i$ = customers affected | min/cust/yr |
| 4 | **SAIFI** | $SAIFI = \dfrac{\sum N_i}{N_{total}}$ | $N_i$ = customers interrupted per event | int/cust/yr |
| 5 | **CAIDI** | $CAIDI = \dfrac{SAIDI}{SAIFI}$ | Average restoration time per event | min/int |
| 6 | **AT&C Losses (%)** | $AT\&C = 1 - \dfrac{Billing\ Efficiency \times Collection\ Efficiency}{1} \times 100$ | Composite loss measure | % |
| 7 | **MAPE (Forecast Error)** | $MAPE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left \vert \dfrac{A_t - F_t}{A_t} \right \vert \times 100$ | $A_t$ = actual, $F_t$ = forecast | % |
| 8 | **RMSE (Forecast Error)** | $RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(A_t - F_t)^2}$ | Penalizes large errors | same as A |
| 9 | **Demand Response Savings** | $DR\_Savings = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{base,i} - P_{DR,i}) \times \Delta t \times C_k$ | $C_k$ = ₹/kWh | ₹ |
| 10 | **Energy Balance Loss** | $Loss_{feeder} = E_{in} - \sum_{j=1}^{m} E_{consumed,j} - E_{unbilled}$ | Non-technical + technical | kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip:** KTU examiners often ask *"What is the difference between SAIDI and SAIFI?"* Always answer in terms of **DURATION vs. FREQUENCY**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic — they are used in:
- **Power system planning** (CEA, India) — for granting generation licenses.
- **Regulatory commissions** (CERC, SERCs) — for tariff approval and penalty decisions.
- **Utility operations centers** — daily dashboards.
- **Smart city projects** — for water & electricity benchmarking.
- **Insurance & risk modeling** — for outage-related business interruption claims.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Smart Meter Data Anomaly Detection using Energy Balance

**Problem Statement:**
A distribution feeder in Kochi supplies **10,000 kWh** of energy in a day. There are **500 customers** with smart meters. The total billed consumption is **7,800 kWh**. Technical losses (line losses) are estimated at **3%** of input energy. Find the **non-technical (commercial) loss percentage** and identify whether analytics should flag this feeder for revenue protection investigation.

---

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{in} &= 10{,}000\ \text{kWh} \\
E_{billed} &= 7{,}800\ \text{kWh} \\
\text{Technical Loss Rate} &= 3\% \\
N_{customers} &= 500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Loss Percentages into Absolute kWh Values**

First, compute the technical (inherent) loss in kWh:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{tech} &= 3\% \times 10{,}000 \\
&= 0.03 \times 10{,}000 \\
&= 300\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Expected Loss**

Total energy that "disappears" from the system (technical + non-technical) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total\ loss} &= E_{in} - E_{billed} \\
&= 10{,}000 - 7{,}800 \\
&= 2{,}200\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate Non-Technical Loss**

Non-technical loss = Total loss − Technical loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{non\text{-}tech} &= E_{total\ loss} - E_{tech} \\
&= 2{,}200 - 300 \\
&= 1{,}900\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express Non-Technical Loss as a Percentage of Input**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NTL\%} &= \dfrac{1{,}900}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 19\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute AT&C Loss Composite**

Billing Efficiency (BE) and Collection Efficiency (CE):

$$
\begin{aligned}
BE &= \dfrac{7{,}800}{10{,}000} = 0.78 \\
AT\&C\ Loss\% &= \left(1 - BE\right) \times 100 \\
&= 22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(Coincidentally, assuming 100% collection efficiency for simplicity.)

**Step 7 — Interpret the Result**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** A 19% non-technical loss is **more than 3× the acceptable benchmark (<6%)**. Analytics would automatically **red-flag** this feeder, trigger a field investigation team, and apply clustering algorithms to identify the top-10 anomalous meters for physical inspection.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: 24-Hour Load Forecasting using a Simple Linear Model

**Problem Statement:**
A utility wants to forecast tomorrow's peak load (in MW) using the relationship: $\hat{L} = a + b \cdot T_{max}$, where $T_{max}$ is the maximum temperature in °C. From historical data, the regression coefficients are $a = 200$ and $b = 5$. The weather forecast predicts $T_{max} = 38°C$. Also, last week the actual peak was **400 MW** and the forecast was **380 MW**. Calculate the predicted peak, the MAPE for the past week, and advise on capacity planning.

---

**Step 1 — Forecast Tomorrow's Peak Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{L}_{tomorrow} &= a + b \cdot T_{max} \\
&= 200 + 5 \times 38 \\
&= 200 + 190 \\
&= 390\ \text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Last Week's Forecast Error**

Actual = **400 MW**, Forecast = **380 MW**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error} &= A_t - F_t = 400 - 380 = 20\ \text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Absolute Percentage Error (Single Point)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
APE &= \left \vert \dfrac{A_t - F_t}{A_t} \right \vert \times 100 \\
&= \left \vert \dfrac{20}{400} \right \vert \times 100 \\
&= 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For one data point, $MAPE = APE = 5\%$.

**Step 4 — Capacity Planning Interpretation**

If a 5% error is typical, the utility must keep a **spinning reserve of ~5%**, i.e., ensure the installed capacity is at least:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reserve} &= 5\% \times 390 = 19.5\ \text{MW} \\
\text{Required Capacity} &= 390 + 19.5 = 409.5\ \text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** A lower MAPE directly reduces reserve requirements, which directly saves fuel cost — making accurate forecasting worth **crores of ₹ annually** for large utilities.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3: Computing Reliability Indices (SAIDI/SAIFI) for a Substation

**Problem Statement:**
A substation serves **5,000 customers**. In one year, **3 outage events** occurred:

| Event | Duration (min) | Customers Affected |
|-------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | 60 | 2,000 |
| 2 | 120 | 1,500 |
| 3 | 45 | 3,000 |

Compute **SAIDI**, **SAIFI**, and **CAIDI**.

---

**Step 1 — Compute SAIDI (Customer-Minutes of Interruption / Total Customers)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (U_i \cdot N_i) &= (60 \times 2{,}000) + (120 \times 1{,}500) + (45 \times 3{,}000) \\
&= 120{,}000 + 180{,}000 + 135{,}000 \\
&= 435{,}000\ \text{customer-minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
SAIDI &= \dfrac{435{,}000}{5{,}000} \\
&= 87\ \text{minutes/customer/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SAIFI (Total Interruptions / Total Customers)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i &= 2{,}000 + 1{,}500 + 3{,}000 = 6{,}500\ \text{interruptions}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
SAIFI &= \dfrac{6{,}500}{5{,}000} \\
&= 1.3\ \text{interruptions/customer/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CAIDI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CAIDI &= \dfrac{SAIDI}{SAIFI} \\
&= \dfrac{87}{1.3} \\
&= 66.92\ \text{minutes/interruption}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for Board Exams:** This substation performs at a **moderate reliability tier**. Best-in-class utilities achieve SAIDI < 60 min/yr. Improving CAIDI (faster restoration) is more cost-effective than reducing SAIFI (preventing outages), and analytics helps identify the *optimal crew dispatch* to minimize CAIDI.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
