## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Utility Analytics. List any four key application areas of analytics in the utility sector.**

**Model Answer:**

Utility Analytics is the application of statistical, computational, and machine-learning techniques to data generated by utility service providers (electricity, water, gas, waste) to optimize operations, predict demand, detect losses, and enhance customer experience.

**Four Key Application Areas:**

1. **Smart Meter Analytics** – Time-of-use profiling, demand response, theft detection.
2. **Load and Demand Forecasting** – Short-term and long-term capacity planning.
3. **Predictive Asset Maintenance** – Failure prediction for transformers, pumps, and pipelines.
4. **Revenue Protection Analytics** – Identifying non-technical losses and billing irregularities.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between SAIDI and SAIFI with a one-line definition of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Index | Definition | Unit |
|-------|------------|------|
| **SAIDI** | System Average Interruption **Duration** Index — total customer-minutes of interruption divided by total customers served. | **minutes/customer/year** |
| **SAIFI** | System Average Interruption **Frequency** Index — total number of customer interruptions divided by total customers served. | **interruptions/customer/year** |

SAIDI measures *how long* outages last, while SAIFI measures *how often* they occur. SAIDI ÷ SAIFI = **CAIDI** (average restoration time).

*(3 marks: 1 each for definitions, 1 for relationship.)*

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Option A*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **four levels of analytics maturity** (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) with **two utility-specific examples** for each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distribution transformer in a substation has the following sensor data for the last 6 months:

| Month | Avg Load (kW) | Peak Load (kW) | Ambient Temp (°C) | Failures in Region |
|-------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Jan | 320 | 480 | 22 | 0 |
| Feb | 340 | 510 | 25 | 0 |
| Mar | 380 | 555 | 30 | 1 |
| Apr | 420 | 600 | 33 | 1 |
| May | 450 | 640 | 36 | 2 |
| Jun | 470 | 680 | 38 | 3 |

Compute the **Load Factor** for each month. Identify the **trend** and advise on **predictive maintenance** scheduling. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (Question A)

#### Part (a) — Four Levels of Analytics

**1. Descriptive Analytics (What happened?)**
- *Example 1:* Monthly dashboards showing total kWh billed, peak demand, and revenue collection per circle.
- *Example 2:* SAIDI/SAIFI reports submitted annually to the Electricity Regulatory Commission.

**2. Diagnostic Analytics (Why did it happen?)**
- *Example 1:* Root-cause analysis of a transformer failure — linking it to overloading during a heatwave.
- *Example 2:* Energy-balance drill-down showing 25% non-technical loss in a specific feeder.

**3. Predictive Analytics (What will happen?)**
- *Example 1:* LSTM model forecasting next week's load curve using weather + calendar features.
- *Example 2:* Survival analysis predicting probability of a 33/11 kV transformer failure in the next 30 days.

**4. Prescriptive Analytics (What should we do?)**
- *Example 1:* Linear programming optimizer that schedules DG (diesel generator) and battery dispatch to minimize cost.
- *Example 2:* Reinforcement learning agent that automates demand-response signaling to top-100 industrial customers.

*(7 marks: 4 × 1.5 = 6 marks for explanations + 1 mark for example quality.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Load Factor Calculation

**Formula:**

$$
LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}
$$

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

For **January**:

$$
LF_{Jan} = \dfrac{320}{480} = 0.667
$$

Computing all months:

| Month | Avg (kW) | Peak (kW) | Load Factor |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Jan | 320 | 480 | 0.667 |
| Feb | 340 | 510 | 0.667 |
| Mar | 380 | 555 | 0.685 |
| Apr | 420 | 600 | 0.700 |
| May | 450 | 640 | 0.703 |
| Jun | 470 | 680 | 0.691 |

**Trend Analysis:**

- The **Load Factor is increasing** from Jan (0.667) to May (0.703), indicating the transformer's average utilization is rising relative to its peak.
- However, **absolute peak load is rising faster** (480 → 680 kW, a 41.6% increase), while the average has only risen from 320 to 470 kW (46.9%).
- **Failures correlate strongly with peak load crossing 555 kW** and ambient temperature >30°C.

**Predictive Maintenance Recommendation:**

1. **Thermal Imaging Inspection** of the transformer every 30 days during Apr–Jun.
2. **Oil Quality Testing (DGA)** every 60 days to detect incipient faults.
3. **Load Redistribution** — transfer ~50 kW to adjacent feeder if peak exceeds 600 kW.
4. **Proactive Replacement** scheduled for end of Q3 if failure count >2 in any month.

*(7 marks: 2 for table computation, 2 for trend insight, 3 for maintenance strategy.)*

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Option B*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Smart Meters and AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure)** in enabling utility analytics. Mention any **four technical benefits** and **two regulatory challenges**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A feeder in a Kerala DISCOM receives **8,500 kWh/day** at the input end. There are **400 customers**. Total billed consumption is **6,200 kWh/day**. Technical line loss is estimated at **2.5%**. Compute the **non-technical loss (kWh and %)**, the **billing efficiency**, and the **AT&C loss percentage** (assume 100% collection efficiency). Should analytics flag this feeder for investigation? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (Question B)

#### Part (a) — Smart Meters & AMI in Utility Analytics

**Role:** AMI is the **digital nervous system** of the modern utility. Smart meters record consumption at 15-minute intervals and transmit it via RF/GPRS/PLC to a central head-end system, which feeds the analytics engine.

**Four Technical Benefits:**

1. **Granular Data Resolution:** Enables time-of-use (ToU) tariffs and demand-response programs.
2. **Two-Way Communication:** Utility can send price signals or outage notifications; customer can sell rooftop solar back to grid.
3. **Remote Connect/Disconnect:** Eliminates truck-rolls for new connections and disconnections.
4. **Real-Time Outage Detection:** Pinpoints outage location within minutes, not hours.

**Two Regulatory Challenges:**

1. **Data Privacy:** High-resolution consumption data can reveal household routines — requires DPDP Act 2023 compliance in India.
2. **Tariff Structure:** ToU tariffs are politically sensitive; regulators must balance revenue recovery with consumer protection.

*(7 marks: 3 for role, 2 for benefits, 2 for challenges.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Loss Computation

**Step 1 — Compute Technical Loss (kWh)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{tech} &= 2.5\% \times 8{,}500 \\
&= 0.025 \times 8{,}500 \\
&= 212.5\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Loss**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total\ loss} &= 8{,}500 - 6{,}200 \\
&= 2{,}300\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Non-Technical Loss**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{non\text{-}tech} &= 2{,}300 - 212.5 \\
&= 2{,}087.5\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Non-Technical Loss Percentage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NTL\% &= \dfrac{2{,}087.5}{8{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= 24.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Billing Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BE &= \dfrac{6{,}200}{8{,}500} \\
&= 0.7294\ \text{or}\ 72.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — AT&C Loss (assuming 100% collection)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AT\&C\% &= (1 - BE) \times 100 \\
&= 27.06\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification for Flagging:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision: YES — Flag this feeder for investigation.**

**Reasoning:**
- NTL of **24.56%** is more than **4× the benchmark** of <6%.
- AT&C loss of **27%** is in the "red zone" used by the Ministry of Power's UDAY scheme.
- Per-customer daily average loss = 2,087.5 / 400 = **5.22 kWh/customer/day** unaccounted.
- Analytics should auto-generate a **top-10 anomalous meter list** for field inspection, prioritizing meters whose consumption is **<50%** of the area median.

*(7 marks: 2 for loss calc, 1 for BE, 1 for AT&C, 3 for flag justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up SAIDI and SAIFI** — always clarify DURATION vs. FREQUENCY.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert loss percentages to absolute kWh** before subtracting.
> 3. **Not stating units** (kWh, MW, min/yr) — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 4. **Conflating "technical loss" with "transmission loss"** — at the **distribution feeder level**, "technical loss" = line + transformer loss.
> 5. **MAPE interpretation pitfall:** If actual = 0, MAPE is undefined — mention this in viva.
> 6. **Smart Meter vs. AMI confusion:** Smart meter = device; AMI = entire ecosystem (meters + comms + head-end + analytics).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Utility Analytics applies **descriptive → diagnostic → predictive → prescriptive** techniques to electricity, water, gas, and waste sectors.
- ✅ The **four pillars of utility analytics** are: **Smart Metering, Load Forecasting, Outage/Asset Management, and Revenue Protection**.
- ✅ **SAIDI** = duration of outages; **SAIFI** = frequency; **CAIDI** = SAIDI / SAIFI.
- ✅ **AT&C Loss** = (1 − Billing Efficiency) × Collection Efficiency — the most-watched metric by Indian regulators.
- ✅ **Load Factor = Avg Load / Peak Load** — closer to 1 means better asset utilization.
- ✅ **Non-Technical Loss (NTL)** is detected by **energy balance analysis** — input − billed − technical loss.
- ✅ **MAPE** and **RMSE** are the two canonical **forecast accuracy** metrics; MAPE < 5% is industry gold standard.
- ✅ **AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure)** = smart meters + communication + head-end + analytics.
- ✅ Analytics maturity: **Descriptive (L1) → Diagnostic (L2) → Predictive (L3) → Prescriptive (L4)**.
- ✅ **Demand Response** uses analytics to shift load from peak to off-peak hours, saving crores in capacity cost.
- ✅ The **smart grid** is essentially the convergence of **IoT + Big Data + ML + Cloud** in the power sector.
- ✅ Always quote **units** and **standard benchmarks** (e.g., world-class SAIDI < 60 min/yr) in your KTU answers.
- ✅ A **single transformer** serves ~500–2,000 customers — failure causes cascading outages; analytics enables **predictive replacement**.
- ✅ **Renewable integration** analytics = weather forecasting + storage optimization + grid balancing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytics in Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Healthcare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Healthcare Analytics** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative analysis of historical and real-time clinical, operational, financial, and behavioural data within the healthcare ecosystem — encompassing hospitals, insurance providers, pharmaceutical firms, public-health agencies, and wearable-device manufacturers — using statistical modelling, machine learning, data mining, and visualisation techniques to derive actionable insights that improve patient outcomes, optimise resource allocation, reduce costs, and enable evidence-based clinical and administrative decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Board Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> *"Healthcare Analytics is the science of analysing healthcare data to make informed decisions regarding patient care, hospital management, disease prediction, and public-health policy using descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytical techniques."*

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Healthcare Analytics

| Pillar | Core Question Answered | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Descriptive Analytics** | *"What happened?"* | Monthly patient admission reports |
| **Diagnostic Analytics** | *"Why did it happen?"* | Root-cause analysis of hospital readmissions |
| **Predictive Analytics** | *"What is likely to happen?"* | Forecasting ICU bed demand |
| **Prescriptive Analytics** | *"What should we do?"* | Recommending optimal staff rosters |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuations, the mnemonic **"DDPP"** (Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive) is the highest-weight descriptive content worth memorising for the 14-mark question.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital as a Factory

Think of a hospital as a **highly complex factory** where the raw material is a *sick patient*, the production line is the *clinical pathway*, the quality-control unit is the *doctor*, and the finished product is a *recovered patient*. Just as a modern factory uses sensors, SCADA systems, and predictive maintenance to avoid breakdowns, healthcare analytics uses:

- **EHRs (Electronic Health Records)** → factory's raw-material database
- **Predictive models** → SCADA's failure-prediction algorithms
- **Dashboards** → real-time monitoring screens on the shop floor
- **AI-driven clinical decision support** → automated robotic arms on an assembly line

The **goal** is identical: maximise *throughput* (patients healed), minimise *waste* (misdiagnosis, bed-blockers), and guarantee *quality* (positive outcomes).

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants in Healthcare Analytics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $Se$ | $TP \div (TP + FN)$ |
| **Specificity** | $Sp$ | $TN \div (TN + FP)$ |
| **Positive Predictive Value** | $PPV$ | $TP \div (TP + FP)$ |
| **Negative Predictive Value** | $NPV$ | $TN \div (TN + FN)$ |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1$ | $2 \cdot (PPV \cdot Se) \div (PPV + Se)$ |
| **AUC-ROC** | $A$ | $0.5$ (random) → $1.0$ (perfect) |
| **Average Hospital Stay** | $ALOS$ | $\text{Inpatient Days} \div \text{Discharges}$ |
| **Bed Occupancy Rate** | $BOR$ | $\text{Occupied Bed-Days} \div \text{Available Bed-Days}$ |
| **30-Day Readmission Rate** | $RR_{30}$ | Readmissions within 30 days ÷ Total discharges |
| **HIPAA Fine Cap (2024)** | $F$ | Up to **USD 1.5 million** per violation category/year |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Healthcare Analytics Value Chain — Structured Logic Flow

The complete lifecycle of a healthcare-analytics project follows **seven sequential phases**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer**
   - *Sources*: EHRs (Cerner, Epic), HL7/FHIR messages, IoT/wearables, insurance claims (837/835 files), genomics databases, public registries (CDC, WHO).
   - *Challenge*: Heterogeneous, multi-structured (text, image, time-series, genomic sequences).

2. **Data Warehousing & Integration Layer**
   - Architectures: **EDW (Enterprise Data Warehouse)**, **Data Lake**, **Lakehouse (Delta Lake)**.
   - Schema: Star Schema with *Patient* as the fact table and *Diagnosis*, *Procedure*, *Provider* as dimensions.

3. **Data Quality & Governance Layer**
   - Standards: **HIPAA (Privacy)**, **HL7 FHIR (Interoperability)**, **ICD-10 (Diagnosis Coding)**, **SNOMED CT (Clinical Terminology)**.

4. **Analytical Modelling Layer**
   - Statistical: Logistic Regression, Cox Proportional-Hazards.
   - Machine-Learning: Random Forest, XGBoost for readmission prediction.
   - Deep-Learning: CNNs for radiology, RNNs/LSTMs for time-series vitals.

5. **Insight Generation & Visualisation Layer**
   - Tools: **Tableau, Power BI, Qlik**.
   - Visuals: Heat-maps of disease spread, Sankey diagrams of patient flow, control charts of infection rates.

6. **Decision & Action Layer**
   - Output: Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) alerts, optimised OT schedules, formulary substitutions.

7. **Feedback & Continuous Improvement**
   - A/B testing of new protocols, model retraining pipelines (MLOps).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** For a 14-mark analytical question, you are expected to write at least **3 of the following formulas** with their variables. Memorise the column headers and units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity (True Positive Rate) | $Se = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$; Higher is better |
| 2 | Specificity (True Negative Rate) | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$; Higher is better |
| 3 | Positive Predictive Value | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Depends on disease prevalence |
| 4 | Negative Predictive Value | $NPV = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FN}$ | Depends on disease prevalence |
| 5 | Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| 6 | F1-Score (Harmonic Mean) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot Se}{PPV + Se}$ | Best for class-imbalance |
| 7 | Prevalence (prior) | $Prev = \dfrac{TP+FN}{Total}$ | Population-level metric |
| 8 | Bayes' Theorem (Post-test) | $P(D+\vert T+) = \dfrac{Se \cdot Prev}{Se \cdot Prev + (1-Sp)(1-Prev)}$ | Foundation of diagnostic analytics |
| 9 | Average Length of Stay | $ALOS = \dfrac{\text{Inpatient Days}}{\text{Discharges}}$ | Industry benchmark: **4–5 days** |
| 10 | Bed Occupancy Rate | $BOR = \dfrac{\text{Occupied Bed-Days}}{\text{Available Bed-Days}} \times 100\%$ | Ideal: **80–85 %** |
| 11 | Cost Savings from Analytics | $\Delta C = C_{\text{baseline}} - C_{\text{analytics}}$ | Positive $\Delta C$ = ROI achieved |
| 12 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{\Delta C + \text{Quality Gains} - \text{Analytics Cost}}{\text{Analytics Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as % |
| 13 | Cox Hazard (Survival) | $h(t) = h_0(t) \cdot e^{(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots)}$ | Used in survival analytics |
| 14 | Number Needed to Treat | $NNT = \dfrac{1}{ARR}$ where $ARR = CER - EER$ | Lower NNT = more effective therapy |
| 15 | Youden's J Statistic | $J = Se + Sp - 1$ | Range: $[0, 1]$; optimal threshold |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Management Utility

- **Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS)** reduce diagnostic errors by **15–20 %** (source: JAMA Internal Medicine meta-analyses).
- **Predictive readmission models** save US hospitals an estimated **USD 26 billion/year** (NEJM study).
- **Operational analytics** reduces Average Length of Stay by 0.3–0.8 days, freeing capacity equivalent to building a 25-bed hospital.
- **Genomic analytics** powers personalised medicine: pharmacogenomics cuts adverse drug reactions by **30 %**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Bayes' Theorem in Diagnostic Testing (Board-Favourite)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A new rapid COVID-19 test has Sensitivity $Se = 0.95$ and Specificity $Sp = 0.90$. In a district where the current disease prevalence is $Prev = 0.02$ (i.e., 2 % of the population is infected), a randomly selected person tests **positive**. What is the probability that this person is *actually* infected?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (Bayes' Theorem for diagnostic tests).**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) = \frac{Se \cdot Prev}{Se \cdot Prev + (1 - Sp)(1 - Prev)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) = \frac{(0.95)(0.02)}{(0.95)(0.02) + (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.02)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.95 \times 0.02 = 0.019
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the first term of the denominator.**

$$
0.95 \times 0.02 = 0.019
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the second term of the denominator.**

$$
(1 - 0.90) \times (1 - 0.02) = 0.10 \times 0.98 = 0.098
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the denominator terms.**

$$
0.019 + 0.098 = 0.117
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the final posterior probability.**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) = \frac{0.019}{0.117} \approx 0.1624
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to percentage and interpret.**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) \approx 16.24 \,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with a "positive" test, the patient's *actual* probability of being infected is only **16.24 %** because the disease is rare (low prevalence). This is why a **second confirmatory test** is recommended — the *positive predictive value* plummets at low prevalence.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: ROI Calculation for an Analytics Project

**Problem Statement:**
A 500-bed hospital implements a predictive analytics system to reduce 30-day readmissions.
- Baseline annual readmission cost: $C_{\text{baseline}}$ = INR **12 crore**.
- Post-implementation annual cost: $C_{\text{analytics}}$ = INR **9.5 crore**.
- Quality gains (avoided patient complications) monetised at: INR **1.2 crore/year**.
- Analytics project cost (software + staff): INR **1.8 crore/year**.
Compute the **Return on Investment (ROI)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Cost savings from analytics.**

$$
\Delta C = C_{\text{baseline}} - C_{\text{analytics}} = 12 - 9.5 = 2.5 \;\text{crore}
$$

**Step 2 — Net monetary benefit (savings + quality gains − project cost).**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = \Delta C + \text{Quality Gains} - \text{Project Cost}
$$

$$
= 2.5 + 1.2 - 1.8 = 1.9 \;\text{crore}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ROI formula.**

$$
ROI = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
ROI = \frac{1.9}{1.8} \times 100\% = 1.0556 \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$
ROI \approx 105.56 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An ROI of over **100 %** means the project recovers its cost *and* delivers an equivalent amount of additional value within one year — a strongly board-defensible decision.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Confusion-Matrix & Performance Metrics

A hospital's sepsis-prediction model is evaluated on 1,000 patients. The results are:

| | Actually Septic (D+) | Actually Non-Septic (D−) |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Septic (T+)** | $TP = 180$ | $FP = 40$ |
| **Predicted Non-Septic (T−)** | $FN = 20$ | $TN = 760$ |

**Compute Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, Accuracy, and F1-Score.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Sensitivity.**

$$
Se = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{180}{180+20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90
$$

**Step 2 — Specificity.**

$$
Sp = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{760}{760+40} = \frac{760}{800} = 0.95
$$

**Step 3 — Positive Predictive Value (Precision).**

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{180}{180+40} = \frac{180}{220} \approx 0.8182
$$

**Step 4 — Negative Predictive Value.**

$$
NPV = \frac{TN}{TN+FN} = \frac{760}{760+20} = \frac{760}{780} \approx 0.9744
$$

**Step 5 — Accuracy.**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{180+760}{1000} = \frac{940}{1000} = 0.94
$$

**Step 6 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of $Se$ and $PPV$).**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot Se}{PPV + Se} = \frac{2 \times 0.8182 \times 0.90}{0.8182 + 0.90}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.4727}{1.7182} \approx 0.8571
$$

**Final Performance Summary.**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $Se$ | **0.90** | Catches 9 of 10 true sepsis cases |
| $Sp$ | **0.95** | Correctly clears 95 % of non-septic patients |
| $PPV$ | **0.8182** | Of alarms raised, ~82 % are real |
| $NPV$ | **0.9744** | A "non-septic" alert is right ~97 % of the time |
| $Acc$ | **0.94** | Overall 94 % correct |
| $F_1$ | **0.8571** | Strong balance of precision and recall |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Healthcare Analytics — End-to-End Architecture Flow

The following **block-level functional architecture** illustrates how raw clinical, operational, financial, and genomic data is collected, processed, modelled, and finally presented to clinicians, administrators, and policy-makers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1[Hospital EHR Systems]:::layer1 --> ing
    src2[IoT Wearables and Sensors]:::layer1 --> ing
    src3[Insurance Claim Files]:::layer1 --> ing
    src4[Genomic and Lab Data]:::layer1 --> ing
    src5[Public Health Registries]:::layer1 --> ing

    ing[Data Ingestion and Integration Layer]:::layer2 --> wh
    wh[Enterprise Data Warehouse and Data Lake]:::layer2 --> qa
    qa[Data Quality and Governance HIPAA FHIR ICD10]:::layer2 --> mod

    mod[Analytical Modelling Layer]:::layer3 --> des
    mod --> dia
    mod --> pre
    mod --> prs
    des[Descriptive Models Reports]:::layer4 --> viz
    dia[Diagnostic Models Root Cause]:::layer4 --> viz
    pre[Predictive Models ML and DL]:::layer4 --> viz
    prs[Prescriptive Models Optimisation]:::layer4 --> viz

    viz[Insight Visualisation Dashboards]:::layer5 --> cd
    viz --> ad
    viz --> ph
    cd[Clinical Decision Support CDSS]:::layer6
    ad[Administrative and Operational Decisions]:::layer6
    ph[Public Health Policy Makers]:::layer6

    cd --> fb
    ad --> fb
    ph --> fb
    fb[Feedback Loop and Continuous Improvement MLOps]:::layer7 --> mod

    classDef layer1 fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#2e8b57,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef layer5 fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#fff
    classDef layer6 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef layer7 fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#595959,color:#000
```

### Legend of the Architecture

| Block (Layer) | Function |
|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Data Sources** | EHR, wearables, claims, genomics, registries |
| **Layer 2 — Integration & Governance** | Ingestion, EDW, HIPAA/FHIR compliance |
| **Layer 3 — Modelling Engine** | Runs statistical and ML models |
| **Layer 4 — Four Pillars** | Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive outputs |
| **Layer 5 — Visualisation** | Dashboards and reports for human consumption |
| **Layer 6 — Decision Consumers** | CDSS, administrators, public-health officials |
| **Layer 7 — Feedback** | MLOps retraining, KPI tracking, A/B testing |

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Analytic Pillar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Healthcare Business Question] --> q1{Is the data historical or real-time?}
    q1 -- Historical --> d[Use Descriptive Analytics]
    q1 -- Real-time --> q2{Do you know the root cause?}
    q2 -- No --> dia[Use Diagnostic Analytics Drill Down]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Do you need future forecast?}
    q3 -- No --> q4{Do you need recommended action?}
    q4 -- No --> desc2[Stick with Diagnostic]
    q4 -- Yes --> prs[Use Prescriptive Analytics]
    q3 -- Yes --> pred[Use Predictive Analytics ML Model]
    d --> outcome[Generate Reports and KPI Dashboards]
    dia --> outcome
    pred --> outcome
    prs --> outcome
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Memory Hook:** Always draw the four pillars (Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive) in **left-to-right chronological order** during an exam — examiners award 2–3 bonus marks for visual clarity in a 14-mark question.

---

## 4.3 KTU-Recommended Use-Case Matrix Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uc1[Predicting Patient Readmission]:::app --> a1[Reduces 30-day RR by 20 percent]
    uc2[Medical Imaging with CNN]:::app --> a2[Detects tumours 95 percent accurate]
    uc3[Drug Discovery and Genomics]:::app --> a3[Shortens trial phase by 18 months]
    uc4[Hospital Operations]:::app --> a4[Saves 12 percent on staffing]
    uc5[Population Health Surveillance]:::app --> a5[Real-time outbreak detection]
    uc6[Fraud Detection in Claims]:::app --> a6[Saves 5 percent of claim value]
    uc7[Personalised Treatment Plans]:::app --> a7[Improves adherence by 35 percent]
    uc8[Remote Patient Monitoring]:::app --> a8[Reduces ICU admissions by 25 percent]

    classDef app fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Healthcare Analytics. List its four types with one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Healthcare Analytics is the systematic analysis of clinical, operational, financial, and behavioural data to derive actionable insights and improve decision-making in healthcare organisations.
The **four types** are:
- **Descriptive** – e.g., monthly dashboard of in-patient admissions.
- **Diagnostic** – e.g., root-cause analysis of a hospital-acquired infection spike.
- **Predictive** – e.g., ML model forecasting 30-day readmission risk.
- **Prescriptive** – e.g., optimisation model suggesting ICU nurse rosters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** For full 3 marks, examiners expect: (1 mark) definition, (1 mark) listing the four types, (1 mark) one example for each type.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of Electronic Health Records (EHR) in enabling healthcare analytics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EHR systems (such as **Epic, Cerner, Allscripts**) are digital repositories of patient information including demographics, diagnoses, medications, lab results, vitals, and clinical notes. They enable healthcare analytics by:
1. Providing **structured data** (ICD-10 codes, LOINC lab values) ready for statistical modelling.
2. Offering **real-time access** for predictive algorithms (e.g., sepsis early-warning scores).
3. Supporting **interoperability** via HL7/FHIR standards, allowing aggregation across hospitals.
4. Acting as a **longitudinal record** essential for survival analysis and cohort studies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) Discuss the four levels of healthcare analytics (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**(b) A diagnostic test for a particular cancer has Sensitivity = 0.92 and Specificity = 0.88. In a screening camp, the prevalence of the disease is 4 %. A patient tests positive. Using Bayes' theorem, compute the probability that the patient actually has the disease. Comment on the result. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Four Levels of Analytics (7 marks)

The four levels, often called the **"Maturity Pyramid of Analytics"**, are arranged from basic to most advanced.

**1. Descriptive Analytics — "What happened?" (2 marks)**
- Uses historical, aggregated data to produce dashboards, KPI reports, and statistical summaries.
- *Tools:* Tableau, Power BI, Excel.
- *Example:* A monthly MIS report of *Out-Patient Department (OPD) footfall*, average length of stay (ALOS), bed occupancy rate (BOR), and surgery counts.

**2. Diagnostic Analytics — "Why did it happen?" (2 marks)**
- Uses drill-down, correlation, and root-cause analysis to find reasons behind past events.
- *Techniques:* Pareto charts, fishbone diagrams, regression analysis.
- *Example:* A hospital notices a 30 % spike in catheter-associated UTIs in the ICU. Diagnostic analytics reveals the cause — a change in catheter brand with a higher infection rate.

**3. Predictive Analytics — "What is likely to happen?" (1.5 marks)**
- Uses ML, statistical modelling, and time-series forecasting to predict future events.
- *Algorithms:* Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM.
- *Example:* A readmission-prediction model trained on 50,000 discharge summaries flags high-risk patients 48 hours before discharge.

**4. Prescriptive Analytics — "What should we do?" (1.5 marks)**
- Uses optimisation and simulation to recommend best actions.
- *Techniques:* Linear/Integer Programming, Reinforcement Learning.
- *Example:* A scheduling engine recommends the optimal mix of full-time and on-call surgeons to minimise waiting-list time within a budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Always end your answer by mentioning **"DDPP"** as a mnemonic and stating that prescriptive analytics is the *most valuable and most difficult* to implement.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Bayes' Theorem Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $Se = 0.92$, $Sp = 0.88$, $Prev = 0.04$ (i.e., 4 %).

**Step 1 — Write the Bayes' formula for diagnostic tests.**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) = \frac{Se \cdot Prev}{Se \cdot Prev + (1 - Sp)(1 - Prev)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) = \frac{(0.92)(0.04)}{(0.92)(0.04) + (1 - 0.88)(1 - 0.04)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
0.92 \times 0.04 = 0.0368
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the second denominator term.**

$$
(1 - 0.88) \times (1 - 0.04) = 0.12 \times 0.96 = 0.1152
$$

**Step 5 — Add the denominator terms.**

$$
0.0368 + 0.1152 = 0.1520
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the posterior probability.**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) = \frac{0.0368}{0.1520} \approx 0.2421
$$

**Step 7 — Convert and comment.**

$$
P(D+ \mid T+) \approx 24.21 \,\%
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
Even after testing positive, the patient has only a **24.21 %** chance of having the disease. This is because the **pre-test probability (prevalence) is very low (4 %)**. In low-prevalence settings, *false positives* outnumber *true positives*. Hence, **a second confirmatory test is mandatory** before initiating treatment.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) Explain the major applications of analytics in healthcare with at least six real-world use cases. (7 marks)**

**(b) A hospital invests INR 2 crore in a predictive analytics project. It reduces annual operational wastage from INR 14 crore to INR 11 crore. Additional quality improvements are valued at INR 1.5 crore per year. Calculate the ROI and comment on the project's viability. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Major Applications of Analytics in Healthcare (7 marks)

| # | Application | Real-World Use-Case | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Predictive Clinical Analytics** | Forecasting 30-day readmission for heart-failure patients using XGBoost. | Reduces readmission rate by **20 %**. |
| 2 | **Medical Imaging & Deep Learning** | CNN-based detection of diabetic retinopathy from retinal fundus images (Google Health). | Achieves **95 % accuracy** matching ophthalmologists. |
| 3 | **Drug Discovery & Genomics** | AI-driven molecule screening for COVID-19 protease inhibitors. | Cuts pre-clinical phase by **18 months**. |
| 4 | **Hospital Operations Analytics** | Real-time bed-occupancy dashboard and OT scheduling. | Saves **12 %** on staffing costs. |
| 5 | **Population Health & Epidemiology** | Google Flu Trends-style syndromic surveillance of search & pharmacy data. | Outbreak detection **7–10 days earlier** than traditional methods. |
| 6 | **Fraud, Waste & Abuse (FWA) Detection** | Anomaly detection on insurance claim patterns using isolation forests. | Saves **5 %** of claim value annually. |
| 7 | **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)** | IoT wearables (Apple Watch, Fitbit) streaming vitals to cloud. | Reduces ICU admissions by **25 %** for chronic patients. |
| 8 | **Personalised Treatment (Pharmacogenomics)** | Genomic-based drug dosage for warfarin. | Reduces adverse drug reactions by **30 %**. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Analytics transforms healthcare from a *reactive* system to a *proactive, predictive, and personalised* system — the **"3P Medicine"**.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — ROI Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Project Cost = INR **2 crore/year**
- $C_{\text{baseline}}$ = INR **14 crore**
- $C_{\text{analytics}}$ = INR **11 crore**
- Quality Gains = INR **1.5 crore/year**

**Step 1 — Compute cost savings.**

$$
\Delta C = C_{\text{baseline}} - C_{\text{analytics}} = 14 - 11 = 3 \;\text{crore}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute net monetary benefit.**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = \Delta C + \text{Quality Gains} - \text{Project Cost}
$$

$$
= 3 + 1.5 - 2 = 2.5 \;\text{crore}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ROI formula.**

$$
ROI = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
ROI = \frac{2.5}{2} \times 100\% = 1.25 \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Final result and comment.**

$$
ROI = 125 \,\%
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
An ROI of **125 %** means the project recovers its entire cost and generates an *additional 1.25 times the cost* in value within one year. This is **highly viable** and easily justifies continued investment and scaling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract Project Cost** from Net Benefit — students often stop at $\Delta C = 3$ crore and incorrectly claim "300 % ROI". This is a recurring **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Mixing up Sensitivity and Specificity** in Bayes' theorem — always re-read whether the question asks for $P(D+ \mid T+)$ or $P(D- \mid T-)$.
> 3. **Skipping the "Comment" line** in numerical answers — KTU valuations reserve **1 mark** explicitly for managerial interpretation.
> 4. **Writing $|$ inside markdown tables** for absolute value — this breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX instead.
> 5. **Not labelling the four analytics types in order** DDPP — partial marks lost for mis-ordering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Block — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition (1-liner):** Healthcare Analytics = quantitative analysis of clinical, operational, financial & behavioural data to improve outcomes.
- **Four Pillars Mnemonic:** **DDPP** — Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive.
- **Data Sources (5):** EHR, IoT/wearables, claims, genomics, public registries.
- **Standards (4):** HIPAA, HL7 FHIR, ICD-10, SNOMED CT.
- **Key Formulae to Memorise:** Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, Accuracy, F1, Bayes' Theorem, ALOS, BOR, ROI.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** Low prevalence → low PPV → always confirm with a second test.
- **Cox Proportional Hazards Model:** Used for *survival analytics* (time-to-event data).
- **Number Needed to Treat (NNT):** $NNT = 1 / ARR$; lower NNT = better therapy.
- **HIPAA Fine Cap:** Up to **USD 1.5 million** per violation category per year.
- **Three P's of Modern Medicine:** *Predictive, Preventive, Personalised* — analytics powers all three.
- **High-Value Use-Cases to Memorise:** Readmission prediction, sepsis early-warning, CNN-based radiology, drug discovery, RPM, fraud detection, pharmacogenomics, OT scheduling.
- **Confusion-Matrix Trap:** High accuracy on imbalanced data (e.g., rare disease) is *misleading* — always report **F1-score** or **AUC-ROC**.
- **Typical Industry Benchmarks:** ALOS = 4–5 days, BOR = 80–85 %, 30-day readmission < 15 %, sepsis prediction AUC > 0.85.
- **One-Liner for the Examiner:** *"Analytics converts data from the *EHR* into **decisions at the bedside, the boardroom, and the policy table**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytics in Online Retail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Online Retail

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Online Retail Analytics** is the systematic application of data collection, statistical analysis, predictive modeling, and visualization techniques to digital commerce data (clicks, views, transactions, reviews, returns) in order to optimize revenue, customer experience, inventory, marketing spend, and overall business performance in e-commerce platforms.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under **Prescriptive and Predictive Analytics** applied to the **B2C (Business-to-Consumer) e-commerce** vertical. It integrates four pillars:

1. **Descriptive Analytics** – What happened? (Sales dashboards, traffic reports)
2. **Diagnostic Analytics** – Why did it happen? (Funnel drop-off, cart abandonment diagnosis)
3. **Predictive Analytics** – What will happen? (Demand forecasting, churn prediction)
4. **Prescriptive Analytics** – What should we do? (Dynamic pricing, recommendation engines)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mall Manager's Brain — Reimagined for the Digital World**

Imagine a traditional mall manager standing at the center of a shopping complex. In a physical mall, the manager observes:
- Which aisles customers walk down
- Which products they touch but don't buy
- Where they stop, where they rush
- How long they queue at billing counters

The mall manager uses these *visual cues* to rearrange shelves, place offers, and design store layouts. However, the data is **incomplete** — the manager cannot track every visitor, and many customers leave without purchasing.

Now, **Online Retail Analytics** is the equivalent of giving the digital mall manager **God-mode vision**:
- Every click, scroll, hover, and pause is recorded (millisecond precision)
- Every customer has a **digital fingerprint** (session ID, cookies, device ID)
- Every transaction is timestamped and linked to behavior
- The manager can A/B test two store layouts *simultaneously* and statistically determine the winner

In short, **Online Retail Analytics transforms every digital touchpoint into a measurable, analyzable data event that fuels business decisions**.

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Online Retail Analytics

| Pillar | Purpose | Typical Business Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Analytics** | Segment & understand buyers | Who are our most valuable customers? |
| **Product Analytics** | Optimize catalog & pricing | Which products should we promote? |
| **Marketing Analytics** | Maximize ROI on campaigns | Which channel gives best ROAS? |
| **Operations Analytics** | Streamline supply chain | How much stock to reorder? |
| **Fraud Analytics** | Detect anomalous transactions | Is this a legitimate purchase? |

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary (KTU Standardized)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Online Retail Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Conversion Rate (CVR)** — Bold standard target: **2%–5%** for e-commerce
> - **Average Order Value (AOV)** — Average revenue per transaction
> - **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** — Cost to acquire one new paying customer
> - **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV / LTV)** — Total predicted revenue from a customer
> - **Cart Abandonment Rate** — Industry average: **69.99%** (Baymard Institute)
> - **Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)** — Revenue per rupee spent on advertising
> - **Churn Rate** — % of customers who stop buying in a given period

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Online Retail Analytics Lifecycle

The end-to-end analytics workflow in an e-commerce setting follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Data Collection Layer**
   - Web logs, clickstream data, server logs
   - Transactional databases (orders, payments, refunds)
   - Third-party data (social media, CRM, ad platforms)
   - IoT / sensor data (warehouses, delivery tracking)

2. **Data Storage Layer**
   - Data Lake (raw, schema-less) — e.g., Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake
   - Data Warehouse (structured, OLAP) — e.g., Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift
   - Operational databases (real-time) — e.g., MongoDB, PostgreSQL

3. **Data Processing Layer**
   - ETL/ELT pipelines (Extract, Transform, Load)
   - Real-time stream processing (Apache Kafka, Apache Flink)
   - Batch processing (Apache Spark, Hadoop)

4. **Analytics & Modeling Layer**
   - Descriptive: Dashboards, KPI tracking
   - Predictive: ML models (classification, regression, time-series)
   - Prescriptive: Optimization, reinforcement learning

5. **Action & Feedback Layer**
   - Personalized recommendations
   - Dynamic pricing engines
   - Targeted marketing campaigns
   - Inventory reallocation decisions

## 2.2 Deep Dive: RFM Analysis (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RFM is the most frequently tested online retail analytics topic in KTU exams.** Master it thoroughly.

**RFM** is a customer segmentation technique that scores each customer on three dimensions:

- **R (Recency)**: How recently did the customer make a purchase?
  - Lower recency value (in days) = better customer
- **F (Frequency)**: How often do they purchase?
  - Higher frequency = more loyal customer
- **M (Monetary)**: How much do they spend?
  - Higher monetary value = more valuable customer

Each dimension is typically scored on a **1–5 scale** (quintile segmentation), producing a 3-digit RFM score (e.g., `5-1-5` = recent, infrequent, high-spender).

**Customer Segments Derived from RFM:**

| Segment | RFM Pattern | Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Champions** | 5-5-5 | Reward, early access, loyalty programs |
| **Loyal Customers** | 4-4-4 to 5-4-5 | Upsell, cross-sell premium products |
| **Potential Loyalists** | 4-1-4 to 5-3-4 | Onboarding, personalized offers |
| **At-Risk Customers** | 2-4-4 to 3-4-5 | Win-back campaigns, special discounts |
| **Hibernating** | 1-1-1 to 2-2-3 | Reactivation or low-cost remarketing |
| **Lost** | 1-1-1 | Ignore or low-priority campaigns |

## 2.3 The Conversion Funnel (AIDA + E-commerce)

Online retail analytics heavily relies on **funnel analysis** to identify drop-off points:

1. **Awareness** (Ad impression, site visit) → 100%
2. **Interest** (Product page view, search) → ~40%
3. **Desire** (Add to cart, add to wishlist) → ~10%
4. **Action** (Checkout completed, payment success) → ~2–5%

The drop-off between **Desire → Action** is the famous **Cart Abandonment** problem, which is the single biggest analytical challenge in e-commerce.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following formulas — they are the most frequently tested in KTU examinations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CVR} = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Total Visitors}} \times 100\%$ | Conversion Rate | % (typical 2–5%) |
| 2 | $\text{AOV} = \dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Number of Orders}}$ | Average Order Value | Currency |
| 3 | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers Acquired}}$ | Customer Acquisition Cost | Currency |
| 4 | $\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan}$ | Customer Lifetime Value | Currency |
| 5 | $\text{CLTV:CAC} = \dfrac{\text{CLV}}{\text{CAC}}$ | Ideal ratio | $\geq 3:1$ |
| 6 | $\text{Cart Abandonment \%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Completed Checkouts}}{\text{Carts Created}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Cart Abandonment | % (typical ~70%) |
| 7 | $\text{Churn Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Lost}}{\text{Customers at Start}} \times 100\%$ | Churn Rate | % |
| 8 | $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}}$ | Return on Ad Spend | Ratio |
| 9 | $\text{Bounce Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Single Page Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}} \times 100\%$ | Bounce Rate | % |
| 10 | $\text{Repeat Purchase Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Customers}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100\%$ | Repeat Purchase | % |
| 11 | $\text{Market Basket Support}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Transactions containing both A and B}}{\text{Total Transactions}}$ | Association Rule | 0 to 1 |
| 12 | $\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Support}(A \cup B)}{\text{Support}(A)}$ | Association Rule | 0 to 1 |
| 13 | $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B)}{\text{Support}(B)}$ | Association Strength | $\vert \text{Lift} \vert > 1$ means positive correlation |
| 14 | $\text{Inventory Turnover} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Inventory Turnover | Ratio |
| 15 | $\text{Gross Margin \%} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue} - \text{COGS}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100\%$ | Gross Margin | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** For absolute values in formulas, we use $\vert x \vert$ syntax (LaTeX `\vert`) to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax with the pipe character.

## 2.5 Market Basket Analysis (Association Rule Mining)

> [!NOTE]
> **Market Basket Analysis is a direct application of association rule mining to online retail transaction data. The Apriori Algorithm and FP-Growth are the two most common algorithms.**

**Three Key Metrics:**

1. **Support** — How frequently does the itemset appear?
$$ \text{Support}(X) = \frac{\text{Number of transactions containing } X}{\text{Total number of transactions}} $$

2. **Confidence** — How likely is Y to be bought when X is bought?
$$ \text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X)} $$

3. **Lift** — How much more likely is Y to be bought when X is bought, compared to Y being bought independently?
$$ \text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{Support}(Y)} = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \cdot \text{Support}(Y)} $$

**Interpretation of Lift:**

- $\text{Lift} > 1$ → Positive correlation (X and Y are bought together more than random)
- $\text{Lift} = 1$ → No correlation (independent)
- $\text{Lift} < 1$ → Negative correlation (X and Y are substitutes)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Online Retail Analytics |
|---|---|
| **E-commerce Platforms (Amazon, Flipkart)** | Personalized homepage, dynamic pricing, recommendations |
| **Quick Commerce (Blinkit, Zepto)** | Real-time inventory, hyperlocal demand forecasting |
| **Travel (MakeMyTrip, Booking.com)** | Dynamic pricing of flights/hotels, personalized offers |
| **Streaming (Netflix, Spotify)** | Content recommendation, viewing behavior analysis |
| **Fintech (Paytm, PhonePe)** | Fraud detection, credit risk scoring |
| **D2C Brands (Boat, Mamaearth)** | Cohort analysis, retention optimization, ROAS optimization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: RFM Scoring and Segmentation

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a classic 14-mark KTU-style problem.** The methodology below is what examiners expect.

**Problem Statement:**
An online retailer has the following data for 5 customers. Compute the RFM scores (on 1–5 scale, 5 = best) and identify the segment for each.

| Customer | Last Purchase (days ago) | Total Orders | Total Spend (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 25 | 80,000 |
| B | 60 | 8 | 15,000 |
| C | 10 | 30 | 1,20,000 |
| D | 90 | 2 | 5,000 |
| E | 3 | 15 | 50,000 |

**Step 1: Rank customers on each dimension.**

For **Recency (R)** — Lower days = Higher score:

| Customer | Days | Rank | R Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| E | 3 | 1 (lowest days) | 5 |
| A | 5 | 2 | 5 |
| C | 10 | 3 | 4 |
| B | 60 | 4 | 2 |
| D | 90 | 5 (highest days) | 1 |

For **Frequency (F)** — Higher orders = Higher score:

| Customer | Orders | Rank | F Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| C | 30 | 1 | 5 |
| A | 25 | 2 | 5 |
| E | 15 | 3 | 4 |
| B | 8 | 4 | 3 |
| D | 2 | 5 | 1 |

For **Monetary (M)** — Higher spend = Higher score:

| Customer | Spend | Rank | M Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| C | 1,20,000 | 1 | 5 |
| A | 80,000 | 2 | 4 |
| E | 50,000 | 3 | 4 |
| B | 15,000 | 4 | 2 |
| D | 5,000 | 5 | 1 |

**Step 2: Combine RFM Scores.**

| Customer | R | F | M | RFM Score | Segment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 5 | 4 | 5-5-4 | **Champion** |
| B | 2 | 3 | 2 | 2-3-2 | **Hibernating / At-Risk** |
| C | 4 | 5 | 5 | 4-5-5 | **Champion** |
| D | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1-1-1 | **Lost** |
| E | 5 | 4 | 4 | 5-4-4 | **Loyal Customer** |

**Step 3: Marketing Action Plan.**

- **A & C (Champions)** → Loyalty rewards, early access, referral programs
- **E (Loyal)** → Cross-sell premium products
- **B (Hibernating)** → Win-back campaign with 20% discount
- **D (Lost)** → Reactivation email or remove from active campaigns

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Market Basket Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A retail store has 5 transactions. Find association rules using Support ≥ 0.4 and Confidence ≥ 0.6.

| TID | Items Bought |
|---|---|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Jam |
| T2 | Bread, Butter |
| T3 | Butter, Jam, Milk |
| T4 | Bread, Jam, Milk |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk |

**Step 1: Calculate item frequencies (1-itemset support).**

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Bread}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Butter}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Jam}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Milk}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 $$

All items have support $\geq 0.4$, so all are frequent.

**Step 2: Calculate 2-itemset support.**

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Bread}, \text{Butter}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Bread}, \text{Jam}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Bread}, \text{Milk}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Butter}, \text{Jam}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Butter}, \text{Milk}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 $$

$$ \text{Support}(\text{Jam}, \text{Milk}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 $$

Frequent 2-itemsets (support $\geq 0.4$): All six pairs qualify.

**Step 3: Calculate Confidence for candidate rules.**

$$ \text{Confidence}(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) = \frac{\text{Support}(\text{Bread} \cup \text{Butter})}{\text{Support}(\text{Bread})} = \frac{0.6}{0.8} = 0.75 $$

$$ \text{Confidence}(\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Bread}) = \frac{0.6}{0.8} = 0.75 $$

$$ \text{Confidence}(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Jam}) = \frac{0.4}{0.8} = 0.50 $$

$$ \text{Confidence}(\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Jam}) = \frac{0.4}{0.8} = 0.50 $$

**Step 4: Filter rules by Confidence ≥ 0.6.**

Strong rules:
- $\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}$ (Confidence = 0.75) ✓
- $\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Bread}$ (Confidence = 0.75) ✓

**Step 5: Calculate Lift for the strong rules.**

$$ \text{Lift}(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) = \frac{0.75}{0.8} = 0.9375 $$

$$ \text{Lift}(\text{Butter} \Rightarrow \text{Bread}) = \frac{0.75}{0.8} = 0.9375 $$

**Step 6: Interpret results.**

$$ \text{Lift} = 0.9375 < 1 $$

This indicates a **negative (or weak) correlation** between Bread and Butter — they are NOT bought together more than random chance. The high confidence is misleading because both items are independently very popular.

**Business Insight:** Do not place Bread and Butter as a "buy together" suggestion. Instead, pair Bread with Milk or Jam for higher conversion.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A coffee subscription service has the following metrics:
- Average Order Value (AOV) = ₹500
- Purchase Frequency = 4 orders/year
- Average Customer Lifespan = 3 years
- Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) = ₹800

**Step 1: Calculate CLV.**

$$ \text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan} $$

$$ \text{CLV} = 500 \times 4 \times 3 = ₹6{,}000 $$

**Step 2: Calculate CLV:CAC Ratio.**

$$ \text{CLTV:CAC} = \frac{6{,}000}{800} = 7.5 $$

**Step 3: Interpret.**

$$ \text{CLTV:CAC} = 7.5 \gg 3 $$

The ratio of **7.5** is **excellent** (ideal benchmark is 3:1). The business is highly profitable. It can afford to spend more on customer acquisition.

**Step 4: Calculate Payback Period.**

$$ \text{Payback Period (months)} = \frac{\text{CAC}}{\text{AOV} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \div 12} $$

$$ \text{Payback Period} = \frac{800}{500 \times 4 \div 12} = \frac{800}{166.67} \approx 4.8 \text{ months} $$

**Step 5: Implication.**

A payback period under 12 months indicates a **healthy unit economics** model suitable for aggressive scaling.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Churn Prediction Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Logistic Regression is the most common classifier for churn prediction in online retail.**

The probability of churn is modeled as:

$$ P(\text{Churn} = 1 \mid X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_n X_n)}} $$

Where:
- $X_1$ = Recency (days since last purchase)
- $X_2$ = Frequency (orders per month)
- $X_3$ = Monetary (total spend)
- $X_4$ = Number of complaints
- $X_5$ = Average session duration

If $P(\text{Churn}) > 0.5$ → Customer flagged for **retention campaign**.

**Key churn indicators in online retail:**
- Decreasing session frequency
- Lower cart additions
- Increased bounce rate on product pages
- Reduced email open rates

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Online Retail Analytics Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Web Server Logs] --> B1[Data Lake]
    A2[Mobile App Events] --> B1
    A3[Transaction DB] --> B1
    A4[CRM System] --> B1
    A5[Ad Platforms] --> B1
    A6[Social Media APIs] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[ETL Pipeline]
    C1 --> D1[Data Warehouse]
    C1 --> D2[Stream Processor]
    D1 --> E1[Descriptive Analytics]
    D1 --> E2[Diagnostic Analytics]
    D2 --> E3[Predictive Analytics]
    D2 --> E4[Prescriptive Analytics]
    E1 --> F1[KPI Dashboards]
    E2 --> F2[Funnel Reports]
    E3 --> F3[Churn Models]
    E3 --> F4[Demand Forecast]
    E4 --> F5[Recommendation Engine]
    E4 --> F6[Dynamic Pricing]
    F1 --> G1[Business Decisions]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    F5 --> G1
    F6 --> G1
```

## 4.2 RFM Segmentation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Transaction Data] --> B[Calculate Recency]
    A --> C[Calculate Frequency]
    A --> D[Calculate Monetary]
    B --> E[Quintile Score 1 to 5]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[RFM Matrix 5x5x5]
    F --> G1[Champions]
    F --> G2[Loyal Customers]
    F --> G3[Potential Loyalists]
    F --> G4[At Risk]
    F --> G5[Hibernating]
    F --> G6[Lost]
    G1 --> H1[Reward Program]
    G2 --> H2[Upsell Strategy]
    G3 --> H3[Onboarding Offers]
    G4 --> H4[Win Back Campaign]
    G5 --> H5[Reactivation Email]
    G6 --> H6[Low Cost Remarketing]
```

## 4.3 Conversion Funnel with Analytics Interventions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage1 Awareness] --> S2[Stage2 Interest]
    S2 --> S3[Stage3 Desire]
    S3 --> S4[Stage4 Action]
    S1 -->|Drop off analysis| A1[Track impressions and reach]
    S2 -->|Clickstream analysis| A2[Heatmaps and scroll depth]
    S3 -->|Cart analysis| A3[Abandonment reasons]
    S4 -->|Payment analysis| A4[Gateway failures]
    A1 --> I1[Optimize ad targeting]
    A2 --> I2[Improve UX and content]
    A3 --> I3[Trigger recovery email]
    A4 --> I4[Streamline checkout]
```

## 4.4 Recommendation Engine Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer [Input Data]
        I1[User Browsing History]
        I2[Purchase History]
        I3[Product Catalog]
        I4[Demographics]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer [Processing Engine]
        P1[Collaborative Filtering]
        P2[Content Based Filtering]
        P3[Hybrid Model]
        P4[Association Rules]
    end

        O1[Top N Recommendations]
        O2[Personalized Homepage]
        O3[Email Campaigns]
        O4[Push Notifications]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    I4 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    I1 --> P4
    I2 --> P4
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P3 --> O4
```

## 4.5 CLV Optimization Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acquire Customers] --> B[Track Purchase Behavior]
    B --> C[Compute RFM and CLV]
    C --> D{Segment Value}

    D -->|Medium CLV| F[Upsell and Cross Sell]
    D -->|Low CLV| G[Reactivation or Drop]

```
