# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define Marketing Analytics. List any FOUR key performance indicators (KPIs) used to measure digital marketing effectiveness.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Marketing Analytics is the practice of measuring, managing, and analyzing marketing data to maximize the effectiveness of marketing initiatives and optimize ROI. It involves capturing data from all marketing channels and consolidating it to derive predictive insights.
- **Four KPIs (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** — Cost to acquire one new customer.
  2. **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** — Total revenue a customer generates over their lifetime.
  3. **Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI)** — Revenue generated per rupee spent.
  4. **Conversion Rate (CVR)** — Percentage of visitors who complete a desired action.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Explain the RACE framework in digital marketing. Mention the role of analytics in each stage.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The **RACE framework** is a 4-stage digital marketing model: **Reach, Act, Convert, Engage (1 Mark).**
- **Reach (0.5 Marks):** Build awareness using SEO, display ads, social media. Analytics role → *Impressions, Reach, CPM, Brand Lift.*
- **Act (0.5 Marks):** Drive on-site interaction. Analytics role → *CTR, Bounce Rate, Dwell Time, Page Views.*
- **Convert (0.5 Marks):** Turn interactions into sales. Analytics role → *CVR, AOV, Cart Abandonment, CAC.*
- **Engage (0.5 Marks):** Foster loyalty. Analytics role → *CLV, Repeat Purchase Rate, NPS, Email Open Rate.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**
> *A leading FMCG company is facing declining ROI from traditional mass-media advertising. As a Business Analytics consultant, propose a comprehensive digital marketing analytics strategy for the company.*
> *Sub-parts: (a) Discuss the data infrastructure and KPI framework required. (b) Illustrate the attribution model selection with a decision matrix and discuss the role of Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM).*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Data Infrastructure & KPI Framework [7 Marks]**

1. **Data Infrastructure Stack (3.5 Marks):**
   - **Customer Data Platform (CDP)** — Stitches 1st-party identifiers (email, phone, app ID, loyalty card) into a unified profile.
   - **Tag Management System** — Google Tag Manager Server-Side for cookieless tracking.
   - **Data Warehouse** — BigQuery / Snowflake / Redshift for unified storage.
   - **BI Tool** — Power BI / Tableau / Looker for dashboards.
   - **Ad Platforms** — Meta Ads, Google Ads, DV360 with API-level integration.
   - **Consent Management Platform (CMP)** — DPDP Act (India) 2023 / GDPR compliance.

2. **KPI Framework (3.5 Marks):**
   - **Funnel-aligned KPI tree** (Awareness → Consideration → Conversion → Retention).
   - Stage-wise KPIs: *Reach, CTR, CVR, CAC, CLV, ROMI, NPS, AOV, Market Basket Share.*
   - Set **SMART targets** with monthly variance bands of $\pm 5\%$.
   - Implement a **North Star Metric** (e.g., Weekly Active Buyers × AOV × Purchase Frequency).

---

**Part (b) — Attribution Models & MMM [7 Marks]**

1. **Attribution Model Decision Matrix (3.5 Marks):**
   - Present the decision tree from Section 4.3.
   - For an FMCG with **low monthly conversions** (typically $\leq 5000$ per channel) → recommend a **hybrid approach** using **last-click for short-cycle campaigns** and **MMM for long-term brand health**.
   - For a company scaling to $\geq 15{,}000$ conversions/month → move to **Data-Driven Attribution (Shapley Value)**.

2. **Marketing Mix Modeling — Detailed Treatment (3.5 Marks):**
   - Define MMM as a top-down econometric model using historical weekly data over 2–3 years.
   - Equation:

$$
\text{Sales}_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{c=1}^{C} \beta_c \cdot \text{Adstock}(\text{Spend}_{c,t}) + \beta_{P} \cdot \text{Price}_t + \beta_{S} \cdot \text{Seasonality}_t + \epsilon_t
$$

   - The **adstock transformation** is:

$$
\text{Adstock}_t = \text{Spend}_t + \lambda \cdot \text{Adstock}_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \lambda < 1
$$

   - **Interpretation for the case:** If $\beta_{\text{digital}} = 0.42$ and $\lambda_{\text{digital}} = 0.55$, it implies digital spend has *moderate immediate impact* and *strong carryover*, justifying an **18% budget reallocation from TV to digital**.

---

> **Q3(B). Alternative 14-Mark Question**
> *Explain with a suitable case illustration how a consumer goods company uses Marketing Analytics to drive its Digital Marketing strategies. Discuss the role of (a) customer segmentation and propensity modeling, and (b) campaign optimization and closed-loop reporting.*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Segmentation & Propensity Modeling [7 Marks]**
- Use the **RFM model** with a 5-point scale and the **Logistic Regression propensity equation** from Section 3.3.
- Discuss **behavioral segmentation** (heavy users, switchers, lapsed) and **needs-based segmentation** (price-sensitive, premium, eco-conscious).
- Build **personas** (e.g., "Value-Seeker Vinaya," "Premium Priya") and map them to channel-and-message combinations.

**Part (b) — Campaign Optimization & Closed-Loop Reporting [7 Marks]**
- Use the **real-time bid formula** from Section 3.4.
- Explain **A/B testing protocol** → Hypothesis → Variants → Sample size → Significance test ($p < 0.05$).
- Describe the **closed-loop architecture** from Section 4.4 where every campaign feeds conversion data back into the CDP, refining future models.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks (Read Before Exam!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Marketing Analytics" with "Web Analytics":** Web Analytics is only ONE component (digital channels). Marketing Analytics spans offline + online + product + pricing.
> 2. **Writing definitions without equations:** KTU examiners award marks for *formulas with units*. Always write $\text{ROMI} = \cdots$ not just "ROMI measures return."
> 3. **Ignoring boundary conditions:** A statement like "CLV : CAC = 3 : 1 is healthy" should also mention that ratios **above 5 : 1 indicate under-investment** in growth.
> 4. **Forgetting Indian regulatory context:** India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023** mandates explicit consent. Always mention **data privacy compliance** in any digital analytics strategy.
> 5. **One-sided case study answers:** Don't describe *only* the analytics. Always map it to the **business outcome** (revenue, margin, share).
> 6. **Skipping MMM vs MTA distinction:** MMM = strategic (top-down, weekly data, 2–3 years). MTA = tactical (bottom-up, user-level, real-time). Examiners love this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final Revision — 60-Second Glance**

- ✅ Marketing Analytics = **Measure + Manage + Analyze + Interpret** marketing data for **ROI optimization**.
- ✅ **4 Types:** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (ascending cognitive value).
- ✅ **Digital Marketing Funnel:** Awareness → Interest → Consideration → Intent → Purchase → Loyalty.
- ✅ **RACE Framework:** Reach → Act → Convert → Engage.
- ✅ **Master KPIs:** CAC, CLV, CLV : CAC ratio, ROMI, CVR, CTR, CPM, CPC, AOV, NPS, MBS.
- ✅ **Healthy CLV : CAC ratio = 3 : 1**; below 1:1 is a value-destruction red flag.
- ✅ **Attribution Models:** Last-Click, Linear, Time-Decay, Position-Based, Data-Driven (Shapley).
- ✅ **MMM equation** uses **adstock transformation** to model carryover effects ($0 < \lambda < 1$).
- ✅ **MTA** is tactical & user-level; **MMM** is strategic & aggregate — *reconcile both monthly*.
- ✅ **Propensity-to-Buy** is modeled using **Logistic Regression** outputting a probability score.
- ✅ **RFM** uses Recency, Frequency, Monetary — each scored 1–5.
- ✅ **Closed-Loop Architecture:** Data → CDP → Analytics → Activation → Customer Event → **back to Data**.
- ✅ **FMCG-specific metrics:** Market Basket Share, Distribution Numeric, Brand Health Index.
- ✅ **Regulatory compliance:** India's **DPDP Act 2023** + global **GDPR/CCPA** require Consent Management Platforms (CMP).
- ✅ **Final Outcome of a Successful Analytics Program:** Higher ROMI, lower CAC, higher CLV : CAC, higher Marketing Contribution %.
- ✅ **Key Phrases Examiners Love:** *closed-loop attribution*, *incrementality testing*, *geo-lift analysis*, *propensity scoring*, *marketing contribution %*, *adstock decay*, *Shapley value attribution*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Retail Company That Uses Predictive Analytics for Pricing, Inventory Management, and Customer Behavior Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT755 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Retail Company That Uses Predictive Analytics for Pricing, Inventory Management, and Customer Behavior Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Predictive Analytics in a Retail Context?

**Predictive Analytics (PA)** is the branch of advanced analytics that uses historical data, statistical algorithms, machine learning models, and mathematical optimization techniques to forecast future outcomes with a quantified probability. In retail, PA transforms reactive decision-making (what already happened?) into **proactive decision-making** (what will happen, and what should we do about it?).

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, predictive analytics in retail is applied to three interconnected pillars:

1. **Pricing** — forecasting the *optimal price* a customer is willing to pay at a given moment.
2. **Inventory Management** — forecasting *future demand* so the right product is in the right store at the right time.
3. **Customer Behavior Prediction** — forecasting *next-best-action* per customer (buy, churn, browse, return).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *Predictive analytics in retail is the systematic use of historical sales, transactional, behavioral, and external data combined with statistical and machine learning models to generate forward-looking decisions on price, stock, and customer action, thereby maximizing revenue, margin, and customer lifetime value.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shopkeeper Who Never Sleeps"

Imagine a 70-year-old kirana shop owner who, after 50 years, has memorized everything:

- He knows that **prices of onions drop on Sundays** and **rice sells more in the first week of every month**.
- He knows **Mr. Rajan always buys Atta on the 28th** and **Mrs. Priya swaps to organic milk when her kid's exam is near**.
- He knows **stocking 40 kg of sugar in summer is wasteful** but **40 kg in Onam season is too little**.

Predictive analytics is essentially **turning this 50-year human intuition into a scalable, automated, mathematical model** that can serve thousands of stores and millions of customers — like cloning the wise shopkeeper a million times. That is what companies like Amazon, Walmart, Target, Reliance Retail, and DMart do at scale.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Dynamic Pricing (Price Elasticity + Competitor + Demand):**
> Adjusting the price of a SKU (Stock Keeping Unit) in near real-time based on demand signals, competitor price, inventory level, and customer willingness to pay.
>
> **Pillar 2 — Demand Forecasting & Inventory Optimization:**
> Using time-series and ML models to predict SKU-store-day demand, then deriving optimal replenishment quantities using Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) and safety stock logic.
>
> **Pillar 3 — Customer Behavior Prediction:**
> Predicting per-customer outcomes such as **propensity to buy**, **propensity to churn**, **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**, and **next-product-to-buy** using classification, regression, and RFM segmentation models.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Across All Three Pillars

| Metric | Symbol | Used In | Target / Industry Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Absolute Percentage Error | **MAPE** | Demand Forecasting | **< 15%** for stable SKUs |
| Root Mean Square Error | **RMSE** | Forecasting & Pricing | Minimize per SKU |
| Price Elasticity of Demand | **E** | Pricing | $\vert E \vert > 1$ = elastic |
| Customer Lifetime Value | **CLV** | Customer Behavior | Maximize over 3–5 yr horizon |
| Inventory Turnover Ratio | **ITR** | Inventory | **8–12x** for FMCG retail |
| Service Level (Fill Rate) | **α** | Inventory | **95–98%** for retail |
| AUC-ROC | **AUC** | Churn Propensity | **> 0.80** is good |
| Forecast Bias | **FB** | Forecast | Close to **0%** (unbiased) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar 1 — Dynamic Pricing: How It Works

Dynamic pricing is **not** the same as price gouging. In retail, it is a controlled, model-driven adjustment of prices within pre-defined guardrails (e.g., never below cost, never above MRP).

**Step-by-step logic of a retail dynamic pricing engine:**

- **Step 1 — Data Ingestion:** Collect (a) own historical sales at price $P_i$, (b) competitor prices $P_{c,t}$, (c) inventory on hand $I_t$, (d) calendar features (festivals, weekday, pay-day), (e) weather and macro data.
- **Step 2 — Demand Function Estimation:** Estimate $\hat{Q}(P)$ using log-log regression (constant elasticity model) or ML-based demand models.
- **Step 3 — Price Elasticity Computation:** Calculate elasticity $E$ to know whether a price cut will increase or decrease revenue.
- **Step 4 — Optimization:** Solve the revenue-maximization problem: $\max_{P} \; P \cdot \hat{Q}(P)$ subject to guardrails.
- **Step 5 — Deployment & A/B Testing:** Push prices to stores/sites, measure uplift using controlled experiments.
- **Step 6 — Continuous Learning:** Re-train models weekly/daily as new sales data arrives.

**Why it works — the economic "Why":**
- **Law of Demand** says as price falls, quantity demanded rises. But **how much** it rises depends on elasticity, which differs per product, per customer, per season.
- Retailers with elasticity intelligence can **lower price on elastic SKUs to grow volume**, and **raise price on inelastic SKUs to grow margin** — a win-win in revenue and profit.

### 2.2 Pillar 2 — Inventory & Demand Forecasting

The **fundamental inventory equation** that retailers try to satisfy every day is:

$$
\text{Stock Available} = \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Incoming Replenishment} - \text{Actual Demand} - \text{Shrinkage}
$$

If *Actual Demand > Stock Available*, the retailer suffers a **stockout** (lost sales + customer dissatisfaction). If demand is much lower, the retailer suffers **overstock** (markdowns, holding cost, obsolescence).

Predictive analytics attacks this by:
- **Forecasting demand** $\hat{D}_t$ for each SKU-store-day using time-series (ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing) or ML (XGBoost, LSTM) models.
- **Deriving Safety Stock** to absorb forecast error and lead-time variability.
- **Computing Reorder Point (ROP)** and **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** to decide *when* and *how much* to order.

### 2.3 Pillar 3 — Customer Behavior Prediction

Customers leave a digital trail at every touchpoint: browse, search, add-to-cart, abandon, purchase, return, review, call-support. Predictive analytics mines this trail to predict:

- **Propensity to Buy (PTB):** P(purchase $\mid$ features) — used for personalized offers.
- **Propensity to Churn (PTC):** P(churn in next 30/60/90 days) — used for retention campaigns.
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV):** Total future revenue from a customer minus cost-to-serve.
- **Next Best Product (NBP):** Recommendation engine output.
- **Customer Segmentation:** RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) clustering to find high-value, at-risk, dormant, and loyal cohorts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU board question on Module 4 Business Analytics will be solved using one or more of these equations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Demand Function | $Q = a - bP$ | $a,b$ = constants | Basic pricing |
| 2 | Constant-Elasticity Demand | $Q = kP^{E}$ | $k$ = scale, $E$ = elasticity | Realistic retail |
| 3 | Price Elasticity (Point) | $E = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$ | — | Pricing decision |
| 4 | Revenue Function | $R(P) = P \cdot Q(P)$ | — | Maximize revenue |
| 5 | Optimal Price (Linear Q) | $P^* = \dfrac{a}{2b}$ | From $Q = a - bP$ | Single-product monopoly |
| 6 | Optimal Price (Constant E) | $P^* = \dfrac{MC}{1 + \frac{1}{E}}$ | $MC$ = marginal cost | Real-world pricing |
| 7 | Economic Order Quantity | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$=annual demand, $S$=order cost, $H$=holding cost/unit/yr | Replenishment |
| 8 | Reorder Point | $\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_L$ | $\bar{d}$=avg daily demand, $L$=lead time days, $z$=service z-score | When to order |
| 9 | Safety Stock | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_L$ | — | Buffer stock |
| 10 | Inventory Turnover | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg Inventory}}$ | — | Efficiency KPI |
| 11 | MAPE | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{A_t - F_t}{A_t} \right\vert$ | $A_t$ actual, $F_t$ forecast | Forecast accuracy |
| 12 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (A_t - F_t)^2}$ | — | Forecast error |
| 13 | CLV (Discounted) | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+d)^t}$ | $R_t$=revenue, $C_t$=cost, $d$=discount rate | Customer value |
| 14 | RFM Score | $\text{RFM} = w_R R + w_F F + w_M M$ | Weights $w$ sum to 1 | Segmentation |
| 15 | Confusion Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | — | Churn model |
| 16 | Precision-Recall | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}, \; R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | — | Propensity models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility in engineering & management:** These equations are used daily by data scientists at Amazon (pricing), Flipkart (recommendation), Uber (surge), Walmart (inventory), and Zomato (churn). For a KTU B.Tech student, mastering these is the difference between a 60% and a 90% answer script.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Case Study Setup — "VegaMart": A Fictional Omnichannel Retailer

**Company:** VegaMart (omnichannel grocery + electronics retailer, 240 stores + website)
**Problem (Year 0):** 9.2% stockout rate, 14% markdown wastage, 3.1% customer churn/quarter, gross margin 18%.
**Solution (Year 1):** Implemented a Predictive Analytics Platform built on Python/SQL, deployed on cloud, integrated with POS, ERP, CRM, and website clickstream.

**Three workstreams:**

| Workstream | Model Deployed | Outcome (Year 1) |
|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Pricing | Log-log demand regression + price elasticity | Gross margin: 18% → 22.5% |
| Inventory Forecasting | SARIMA + XGBoost hybrid | Stockouts: 9.2% → 3.4% |
| Customer Behavior | XGBoost churn + RFM + CLV | Churn: 3.1% → 1.6% |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Deriving the Optimal Price (Pillar 1)

VegaMart observes that the weekly demand for a 1 kg basmati rice SKU follows a linear demand curve:

$$
Q(P) = 1200 - 80P
$$

where $P$ is the price in ₹ and $Q$ is the weekly demand in kg.

**Step 1 — Write the Revenue Function:**

Revenue is price times quantity. We substitute $Q(P)$ into $R = P \cdot Q$:

$$
R(P) = P \cdot (1200 - 80P)
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Expression:**

We distribute $P$ across both terms inside the parenthesis:

$$
R(P) = 1200P - 80P^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate Revenue with Respect to Price:**

To find the maximum, we take the first derivative and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{dR}{dP} = 1200 - 160P = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Linear Equation for P:**

$$
160P = 1200 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P^* = \frac{1200}{160} = 7.5
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the Second Derivative Test:**

$$
\frac{d^{2}R}{dP^{2}} = -160 \;\; (\text{negative} \Rightarrow \text{maximum confirmed})
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Optimal Quantity and Revenue:**

$$
Q^* = 1200 - 80(7.5) = 1200 - 600 = 600 \text{ kg/week}
$$

$$
R^* = 7.5 \times 600 = 4500 \text{ ₹/week}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation (Board Examiner Loves This):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The optimal price is **₹7.50**, the optimum weekly quantity is **600 kg**, and the maximum weekly revenue is **₹4,500**. Any price above or below ₹7.50 will reduce revenue. This is the textbook *profit-maximizing monopoly price* derivation — KTU board exams expect exactly this 7-step structure.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Price Elasticity (Pillar 1)

Using the same VegaMart basmati rice: at price $P_1$ = ₹10, weekly demand $Q_1$ = 400 kg. The retailer cuts price to $P_2$ = ₹8, and demand rises to $Q_2$ = 560 kg.

**Step 1 — Use the Midpoint (Arc) Elasticity Formula:**

$$
E_d = \frac{(Q_2 - Q_1) / [(Q_1+Q_2)/2]}{(P_2 - P_1) / [(P_1+P_2)/2]}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values:**

$$
E_d = \frac{(560 - 400) / [(400+560)/2]}{(8 - 10) / [(10+8)/2]}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Denominators:**

$$
\text{Avg } Q = \frac{960}{2} = 480, \quad \text{Avg } P = \frac{18}{2} = 9
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Percentages:**

$$
\% \Delta Q = \frac{160}{480} = 0.3333, \quad \% \Delta P = \frac{-2}{9} = -0.2222
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Ratio:**

$$
E_d = \frac{0.3333}{-0.2222} = -1.50
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Magnitude:**

> [!NOTE]
> $\vert E_d \vert = 1.5 > 1$ means rice is **elastic** — a 1% price cut yields a 1.5% rise in quantity, so revenue **increases** with a price cut here. VegaMart's analytics team will recommend further price experimentation downward, but never below the floor set by the optimal-price formula (Pillar 1 integration).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — EOQ and Reorder Point (Pillar 2)

VegaMart's inventory data for a popular shampoo SKU:

- Annual demand: $D = 24{,}000$ units
- Ordering cost per order: $S = ₹500$
- Holding cost per unit per year: $H = ₹20$
- Lead time: $L = 5$ days
- Daily demand std dev: $\sigma_d = 8$ units
- Desired service level: 95% → $z = 1.65$

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ Using the Formula:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24000 \times 500}{20}}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator and Denominator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 24{,}000{,}000, \quad \text{Denominator} = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Division and Square Root:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} \approx 1095.45 \text{ units per order}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Daily Demand:**

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{D}{365} = \frac{24000}{365} \approx 65.75 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Lead-Time Demand and Its Std Dev:**

$$
\bar{d}_L = 65.75 \times 5 = 328.75 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\sigma_L = \sigma_d \sqrt{L} = 8 \times \sqrt{5} \approx 17.89 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6 — Safety Stock and ROP:**

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_L = 1.65 \times 17.89 \approx 29.5 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{ROP} = \bar{d}_L + SS = 328.75 + 29.5 \approx 358.25 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 7 — Managerial Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> VegaMart should place a new order of approximately **1,095 units** whenever stock falls to about **358 units**. This balances ordering cost vs holding cost (EOQ) and protects against lead-time uncertainty (safety stock). KTU expects you to state this managerial insight at the end of every quantitative answer — it is a 2-mark value-add.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Customer Lifetime Value (Pillar 3)

VegaMart has a customer "Anita" who, on average:
- Spent ₹4,800/year
- Cost-to-serve ₹800/year
- Will remain a customer for 3 more years
- Discount rate $d = 10\%$

**Step 1 — Net Annual Cash Flow:**

$$
R_t - C_t = 4800 - 800 = 4000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Discounted CLV Formula:**

$$
\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{4000}{(1+0.10)^{t}}
$$

**Step 3 — Year-by-Year Present Values:**

$$
\text{PV}_1 = \frac{4000}{1.10} = 3636.36
$$

$$
\text{PV}_2 = \frac{4000}{1.21} = 3305.79
$$

$$
\text{PV}_3 = \frac{4000}{1.331} = 3005.26
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Present Values:**

$$
\text{CLV} = 3636.36 + 3305.79 + 3005.26 = 9947.41 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Use:**

> [!NOTE]
> VegaMart's marketing team can now spend up to **₹9,947** on Anita over 3 years to retain and grow her (acquisition cost, retention offers, loyalty perks). If the cost-to-retain is less than CLV, the customer is profitable. This single insight justifies VegaMart's ₹2 Cr analytics investment in the first 18 months itself.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — How the Three Pillars Connect

| Pillar | Predictive Question | Input Data | Model | Business Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pricing | "What is the *willingness to pay* today?" | Sales, competitor, calendar, weather | Log-log regression, ML | Optimal price per SKU |
| Inventory | "What will *demand* be next week/store?" | POS, promo, seasonality, lead time | SARIMA, XGBoost, Prophet | EOQ, ROP, Safety Stock |
| Customer | "Will this customer *buy/churn*?" | Transactions, clicks, RFM, demographics | XGBoost, RFM, k-means | Offer, retention, segment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three pillars are **not independent** in production. A pricing decision must consider inventory (don't discount slow-moving stock to zero profit), and a customer offer must consider elasticity (high-CLV customers deserve personalized coupons, low-CLV get generic promotions). KTU expects you to mention this *integration* in long answers for full marks.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define predictive analytics. List any three retail business problems that can be solved using it.*

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):**
Predictive analytics is the use of historical data, statistical algorithms, and machine learning models to forecast future outcomes. Three retail problems solved by it are:
- **Pricing** — forecasting optimal price per SKU to maximize revenue/margin.
- **Inventory** — forecasting SKU-store-day demand to prevent stockouts and overstock.
- **Customer Behavior** — forecasting churn, propensity-to-buy, and CLV to enable personalized marketing.

> 3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for three problems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
*What is the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)? State its formula and explain each term.*

**Model Answer:**
EOQ is the order quantity that minimizes the **total inventory cost** (ordering cost + holding cost) for a SKU. Formula:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = annual demand (units)
- $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹)
- $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹)

> 3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for formula + 1 for term explanation.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget the managerial interpretation** at the end of a long answer. KTU 2024 scheme awards 2 marks for "managerial insight / business implication" — never skip it.
> 2. **Units must be consistent** in EOQ/ROP calculations. Mixing monthly and annual values is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 3. In elasticity questions, **always state whether $\vert E \vert > 1$ or $\vert E \vert < 1$** and link it to the pricing decision. Writing the value alone fetches only half marks.
> 4. For CLV, **forget the discount rate** is the most common error — CLV without discounting overstates value by 10–30%.
> 5. In case-study questions, examiners expect **3 pillars mentioned + a connecting paragraph on integration**. Missing the integration point costs 2 marks.

---

#### QUESTION A — (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**Case:** A retail chain "VegaMart" sells a 1 Litre packaged water bottle. The demand function is $Q = 1500 - 100P$ where $P$ is in ₹ and $Q$ is bottles per week. The marginal cost per bottle is ₹4.

**(a)** Find the **revenue-maximizing price**, the **quantity sold** at that price, and the **weekly revenue**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **price elasticity of demand** at the optimum price. Is the product elastic or inelastic at optimum? What pricing recommendation would you give the retailer? (7 marks)

---

**Solution A:**

**(a) — Revenue Maximization (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Write the revenue function $R(P) = P \cdot Q(P)$:

$$
R(P) = P(1500 - 100P) = 1500P - 100P^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the first derivative:

$$
\frac{dR}{dP} = 1500 - 200P
$$

**Step 3:** Set derivative to zero and solve:

$$
1500 - 200P = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P^* = \frac{1500}{200} = 7.5 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4:** Second derivative test:

$$
\frac{d^{2}R}{dP^{2}} = -200 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Maximum confirmed}
$$

**Step 5:** Optimum quantity:

$$
Q^* = 1500 - 100(7.5) = 1500 - 750 = 750 \text{ bottles/week}
$$

**Step 6:** Optimum weekly revenue:

$$
R^* = 7.5 \times 750 = 5625 \text{ ₹/week}
$$

**Step 7 — Managerial Insight (1 mark reserved):** VegaMart should price the 1L water bottle at **₹7.50**, expect **750 units/week** in sales, and target **₹5,625 weekly revenue**. Since MC = ₹4, profit per unit = ₹3.50 and weekly profit = ₹2,625.

---

**(b) — Price Elasticity at Optimum (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute $\frac{dQ}{dP}$ from the demand function $Q = 1500 - 100P$:

$$
\frac{dQ}{dP} = -100
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the point elasticity formula at $P^* = 7.5, Q^* = 750$:

$$
E_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q} = (-100) \cdot \frac{7.5}{750}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the value:

$$
E_d = -100 \times 0.01 = -1.0
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the magnitude:

$$
\vert E_d \vert = 1.0 \quad \text{(Unit Elastic)}
$$

**Step 5:** Managerial Recommendation (2 marks):
Since demand is **unit elastic** at the optimum, the retailer is already at the sweet spot. Any further price increase will reduce revenue (and a cut will also reduce it). VegaMart should:
- **Hold the price at ₹7.50** as the baseline.
- **Test small variations (±5%)** to find a marginally higher-revenue point if elasticity shifts.
- Use this SKU's elasticity as a benchmark to detect elasticity changes due to weather, festivals, or competitor moves.

---

#### QUESTION B — (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**Case:** A retail company "NovaRetail" wants to optimize inventory for a popular detergent SKU. Annual demand $D = 30{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S = ₹600$ per order, holding cost $H = ₹30$ per unit per year, lead time $L = 7$ days, daily demand standard deviation $\sigma_d = 10$ units, desired service level = 95% (z = 1.65), store operates 365 days/year.

**(a)** Compute the **EOQ**, the **number of orders per year**, and the **time between orders** (in days). (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **Safety Stock** and the **Reorder Point (ROP)**. Briefly explain how NovaRetail can use the ROP in its ERP system to automate replenishment. (7 marks)

---

**Solution B:**

**(a) — EOQ Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Apply the EOQ formula:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 30000 \times 600}{30}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator and denominator:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 36{,}000{,}000, \quad \text{Denominator} = 30
$$

**Step 3:** Divide and take square root:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} \approx 1095.45 \text{ units per order}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute number of orders per year:

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{30000}{1095.45} \approx 27.39 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute time between orders (cycle time):

$$
T = \frac{365}{N} = \frac{365}{27.39} \approx 13.33 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 6:** Verify total cost (optional but earns a value-add mark):

$$
\text{Ordering Cost} = N \times S = 27.39 \times 600 \approx 16434 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{Q^*}{2} \times H = 547.7 \times 30 \approx 16431 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

> Note: Ordering and holding costs are nearly equal — this is the **mathematical proof that EOQ is optimal** (they intersect at minimum total cost). (1 mark value-add)

**Step 7 — Managerial Insight:** NovaRetail should order **1,095 units** every **~13 days** to minimize inventory cost.

---

**(b) — Safety Stock, ROP, and ERP Automation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute average daily demand:

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{D}{365} = \frac{30000}{365} \approx 82.19 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute lead-time demand:

$$
\bar{d}_L = 82.19 \times 7 = 575.34 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute lead-time standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_L = \sigma_d \sqrt{L} = 10 \times \sqrt{7} \approx 10 \times 2.6458 = 26.46 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Safety Stock:

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_L = 1.65 \times 26.46 \approx 43.66 \text{ units} \;\; (\text{round up to } 44)
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Reorder Point:

$$
\text{ROP} = \bar{d}_L + SS = 575.34 + 43.66 \approx 618.99 \;\; (\text{round up to } 619)
$$

**Step 6 — ERP Automation Logic (2 marks):**
NovaRetail's ERP should be configured with the following rule:

> **IF (On-hand Inventory + In-transit Inventory) $\leq$ 619 units THEN auto-generate a Purchase Order of 1,095 units to the approved vendor.**

This is called a **"Min/Max" or "Reorder Point" inventory policy** in ERP terminology (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics all support this natively).

**Step 7 — Managerial Insight:**
The safety stock of **44 units** acts as a buffer against demand spikes or supplier delays. The 95% service level means NovaRetail will avoid stockouts in 95 out of every 100 order cycles — a benchmark expected in modern retail. If higher service is needed (e.g., 99%), $z$ rises to 2.33 and SS rises proportionally.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **10 High-Yield Takeaways for KTU Board Exam (Module 4 — Predictive Analytics Case Study):**
>
> 1. **Predictive analytics = past data → future decisions.** Always start an answer with this definition.
> 2. **Three pillars in retail:** Pricing, Inventory, Customer Behavior. KTU expects all three named in long answers.
> 3. **Optimal price (linear demand) is $P^* = a/2b$.** Always verify with second derivative $< 0$.
> 4. **Optimal price (constant elasticity) is $P^* = MC / (1 + 1/E)$** — a powerful real-world result.
> 5. **Price elasticity $\vert E \vert > 1$ = elastic (cut price to grow revenue); $\vert E \vert < 1$ = inelastic (raise price to grow margin).** This is the single most-tested concept in pricing questions.
> 6. **EOQ = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$** — minimize *joint* ordering + holding cost.
> 7. **Reorder Point = Lead-time demand + Safety Stock**; Safety Stock = $z \cdot \sigma_L$ where $z$ comes from service level (95% → 1.65, 99% → 2.33).
> 8. **MAPE < 15%** is the universal benchmark for a "good" demand forecast. Always quote this in answers.
> 9. **CLV = Sum of discounted (Revenue − Cost) over customer lifespan.** It is the *upper bound on what a retailer can spend to acquire/keep a customer.*
> 10. **Always end with a managerial/business insight** (2 marks reserved) — this is the *single biggest differentiator* between an 80% answer and a 95% answer in KTU 2024 scheme.
